title: Batteries (Management and Handling) Rules, 2001: What Every Manufacturer, Importer, and Bulk Consumer Must Know
summary: The Batteries (Management and Handling) Rules, 2001, notified under the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986, establish a comprehensive Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework for lead acid batteries in India — placing binding obligations on every link in the battery value chain, from manufac

sections.t: Executive Summary The Batteries (Management and Handling) Rules, 2001, notified under the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986, establish a comprehensive Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework for lead acid batteries in India — placing binding obligations on every link in the battery value chain, from manufacturers and importers to dealers, recyclers, and bulk consumers. Companies that manufacture, import, assemble, recondition, sell, or use lead acid batteries in significant volumes are legally required to collect used batteries, channel them exclusively to registered recyclers, and file half-yearly compliance returns with State Pollution Control Boards. Non-compliance carries regulatory consequences including registration cancellation and environmental liability — risks that are increasingly scrutinised by investors and global supply chain partners.

What Changed Originally notified on 16 May 2001 and subsequently amended — most significantly by S.O. 1002(E) dated 4 May 2010 — the Batteries (Management and Handling) Rules, 2001 created India's first structured take-back and recycling regime specifically for lead acid batteries. Lead acid batteries are classified as hazardous waste because they contain lead metal, a potent neurotoxin. Unregulated disposal and informal recycling of these batteries cause serious soil and groundwater contamination, as well as occupational health crises in recycling communities. The 2010 amendment tightened the framework in three important ways: it redefined 'bulk consumers' to explicitly include government departments such as Railways, Defence, Telecom, and Posts and Telegraph that purchase 100 or more batteries per year; it mandated that new batteries may only be sold to registered dealers; and it introduced a formal registration regime for importers with the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB), including a five-year registration cycle and clear grounds for cancellation. Together, these rules create a closed-loop accountability system — every battery sold must be traceable back to a collection and recycling pathway. In plain terms: if your organisation touches lead acid batteries at any point — making them, importing them, selling them, or using large quantities of them — you have legal obligations under this framework. Ignorance of the rules is not a defence, and the regulatory net covers entities across the public and private sectors alike.

Who Is Affected Manufacturers of lead acid batteries and their components (factories and their CEOs/control persons) Importers of new lead acid batteries or lead-containing components — must register with CPCB for a five-year period Assemblers — entities that manufacture batteries by assembling components (partially exempt from certain obligations such as setting up collection centres) Reconditioners — entities that repair and resell used lead acid batteries (similarly partially exempt) Dealers — sellers and receivers of lead acid batteries on behalf of manufacturers, importers, assemblers, and reconditioners; must be registered to legally receive new batteries Recyclers and registered recyclers — entities processing used batteries for lead recovery; must hold registration with MoEF or its designated agency Auctioneers — entities conducting bulk sale of used batteries through tender, auction, or negotiation Bulk consumers — any Central or State Government department, public sector undertaking, board, agency, or private company purchasing 100 or more batteries per annum (Railways, Defence, Telecom, Power utilities, large manufacturing plants, data centres, and telecom tower operators are prime examples) Individual consumers — persons using lead acid batteries, subject to return obligations to dealers or designated collection centres Original Equipment Manufacturers (OEMs) — manufacturers of equipment or products that use lead acid batteries as components (partially excluded from buy-back schedules)

Key Obligations & Deadlines COLLECTION BUY-BACK TARGETS: Manufacturers, importers, assemblers, and reconditioners must collect used batteries back from the market in accordance with the prescribed Schedule — matched to the volume and type of new batteries sold (OEM and bulk consumer sales are excluded from this calculation). REGISTERED RECYCLERS ONLY: All used batteries collected must be sent exclusively to recyclers registered with MoEF or its designated agency. Sending used batteries to unregistered informal recyclers is a direct regulatory violation. HALF-YEARLY RETURNS — 30 JUNE & 31 DECEMBER: Manufacturers, importers, assemblers, and reconditioners must file half-yearly returns in Form-I with the relevant State Pollution Control Board (SPCB), reporting sales volumes and buy-back performance. Deadlines are 30 June (for the first half of the year) and 31 December (for the second half) annually. IMPORTER REGISTRATION WITH CPCB: All importers must register with the Central Pollution Control Board using Form-I for a five-year renewable period. Registration is contingent on compliance with collection targets and timely return filing. Non-compliance can lead to suspension or cancellation of registration. CUSTOMS CLEARANCE CONDITION: Import of new lead acid batteries is contingent upon valid registration (with RBI and CPCB as applicable) — importers must ensure registration is current before initiating shipments. COLLECTION CENTRES: Manufacturers, importers, and reconditioners must establish or jointly establish designated collection centres at various locations to receive used batteries from consumers and dealers. SAFE TRANSPORTATION: Entities in the chain must ensure arrangements are in place with dealers for safe, environmentally sound transportation of used batteries from collection centres to registered recyclers — preventing environmental damage in transit. SELL ONLY TO REGISTERED DEALERS: As of the 2010 amendment, new batteries may only be sold to registered dealers. Manufacturers and importers must verify dealer registration status before invoicing. BUY RECYCLED LEAD FROM REGISTERED SOURCES ONLY: Manufacturers must source recycled lead exclusively from registered recyclers — closing the loop and preventing laundering of informally recycled material into the supply chain. PUBLIC AWARENESS OBLIGATION: Manufacturers, importers, assemblers, and reconditioners must conduct public awareness campaigns — through advertisements, publications, or posters — covering: (a) the hazards of lead; (b) consumer return obligations; and (c) addresses of collection centres and dealers. INTERNATIONAL RECYCLING SYMBOL: Batteries must carry the internationally recognised recycling symbol. VIOLATION REPORTING: Manufacturers and importers must report dealer violations to the relevant SPCB or MoEF. APPEALS: Appeals against CPCB orders on registration suspension or cancellation must be filed in writing within 30 days of the order, accompanied by a copy of the order being challenged.

Climate Transition Angle Lead acid batteries sit at a critical intersection of India's industrial base and its energy transition. Despite the rapid growth of lithium-ion technology, lead acid batteries remain the dominant chemistry for automotive starter batteries, telecom tower backup, industrial UPS systems, and rural electrification storage — and will continue to be for the foreseeable future. As India scales up renewable energy deployment, the demand for battery-based storage — including lead acid — is set to increase significantly before the transition to advanced chemistries becomes economically universal. From a physical risk perspective, unregulated lead acid battery disposal creates severe soil and groundwater contamination — particularly in peri-urban and industrial clusters. Companies with manufacturing or warehousing operations in regions with high informal recycling activity face direct physical and reputational risks, including contaminated water sources affecting operations and communities. Supply chain due diligence under emerging global frameworks is now scrutinising these upstream environmental impacts. Globally, the Batteries (Management and Handling) Rules mirror the direction of travel set by the European Union's Battery Regulation (2023), which mandates EPR schemes, carbon footprints, recycled content targets, and due diligence for battery supply chains — and is already affecting Indian exporters supplying components or finished products to European markets. India's domestic framework, while older and less granular on carbon metrics, establishes the foundational traceability and accountability infrastructure that ISSB (IFRS S2) and CSRD-aligned disclosures will increasingly require companies to evidence. Investors conducting ESG due diligence on Indian industrials, utilities, and auto sector companies are beginning to treat battery waste compliance as a proxy for broader environmental governance maturity. For companies on a net-zero trajectory, formalising battery waste management is not merely a compliance exercise — it is a prerequisite for credible circular economy claims, Scope 3 emissions accounting accuracy, and supply chain resilience in a resource-constrained world.

What To Do Now CONDUCT A VALUE CHAIN AUDIT: Map every entity in your battery supply chain — suppliers, dealers, recyclers — and verify their registration status with CPCB/MoEF. Immediately suspend transactions with unregistered dealers or recyclers and document your corrective actions. ESTABLISH OR JOIN A COLLECTION NETWORK: If you are a manufacturer, importer, or reconditioner without a functioning collection centre network, explore joint collection centre arrangements with industry peers or authorised PRO (Producer Responsibility Organisation) networks to meet your take-back obligations cost-effectively. SET UP A COMPLIANCE CALENDAR FOR HALF-YEARLY RETURNS: Assign ownership within your ESG or legal compliance team for Form-I return filing. Automate data collection on battery sales volumes and buy-back quantities to ensure accurate, timely submissions to the SPCB by 30 June and 31 December each year. INTEGRATE BATTERY COMPLIANCE INTO ESG DISCLOSURES: If your organisation reports under BRSR (Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting), GRI, or is preparing for ISSB-aligned reporting, document your battery take-back rates, recycled lead sourcing percentages, and collection centre network coverage as part of your circular economy and waste management disclosures. TRAIN PROCUREMENT AND OPERATIONS TEAMS: Ensure that procurement officers (especially in bulk consumer organisations — utilities, telecom, defence contractors, large manufacturers) understand their obligations under the Rules, including the requirement to return used batteries and the prohibition on disposing of batteries through informal channels. Embed battery waste handling into your HSE (Health, Safety, and Environment) standard operating procedures.

Our Take The Batteries (Management and Handling) Rules, 2001 represent India's foundational EPR architecture for hazardous battery waste — and while the framework is over two decades old, enforcement focus and stakeholder scrutiny are intensifying rather than diminishing. The rules are increasingly relevant in the context of India's battery storage ambitions, the PM-eBus Sadan programme, and the National Programme on Advanced Chemistry Cell Battery Storage, all of which will dramatically expand the battery economy and, consequently, the volume of end-of-life batteries requiring responsible management. Companies that have treated these rules as a low-priority compliance checkbox are exposed to both regulatory risk — registration cancellations, SPCB notices, and environmental liability — and reputational risk as global customers and investors apply tighter ESG supply chain standards. We expect the Central Pollution Control Board to continue tightening EPR targets and enforcement under the broader trajectory set by the Battery Waste Management Rules, 2022, which has already updated the framework for other battery chemistries. Organisations that build robust, auditable battery take-back and recycling systems now will be better positioned to absorb future regulatory tightening, meet global customer requirements, and make credible circular economy claims — turning a compliance obligation into a competitive and reputational advantage.

title: Battery Waste Management (Amendment) Rules, 2023: What India's Producers, Importers, and Recyclers Must Do Now
summary: India's Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change has significantly tightened the Battery Waste Management Rules by issuing the Battery Waste Management (Amendment) Rules, 2023, effective October 25, 2023. The amendments strengthen Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) obligations, overhau

sections.t: Executive Summary India's Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change has significantly tightened the Battery Waste Management Rules by issuing the Battery Waste Management (Amendment) Rules, 2023, effective October 25, 2023. The amendments strengthen Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) obligations, overhaul the EPR Certificate trading framework, mandate stricter registration and annual reporting requirements, and introduce a price-band mechanism for EPR Certificates — bringing India's battery waste governance closer to global best practice. All battery producers, importers, recyclers, and refurbishers operating in India must urgently review and update their compliance programmes to align with the revised rules.

What Changed The Battery Waste Management Rules, 2022 provided the foundational framework for EPR in India's battery sector. The October 2023 amendment notification (S.O. 4669(E)) makes targeted but consequential changes across multiple rules. Here is what has materially changed: First, the definition of 'battery' has been broadened to explicitly include both disposable (primary) and rechargeable (secondary) batteries along with battery packs — closing definitional gaps that previously created compliance ambiguity for complex battery systems used in electric vehicles (EVs) and energy storage. Second, the scope of 'producer' has been expanded to include entities that import batteries or devices incorporating batteries, and those that manufacture or assemble batteries for sale under another producer's brand — ensuring that the EPR net captures contract manufacturers and importers who previously operated in a grey zone. Third, EPR obligations now explicitly extend to batteries kept for a producer's own use (captive consumption), not just batteries placed on the market. This has significant implications for industrial and telecom players operating large captive battery fleets. Fourth, the EPR Certificate (EPRC) framework has been substantially reformed: certificates will now be issued based on the weight of waste batteries processed or refurbished and the weight of battery material recovered, rather than a simpler unit-based count. Category-wise cross-use of EPRCs is prohibited — a certificate earned in one battery category cannot offset obligations in another. Critically, no EPRCs will be generated for recycling or refurbishing imported waste batteries under the Hazardous Waste Rules, 2016, and such activity must be separately accounted for and reported. Fifth, a regulated EPRC trading platform mechanism has been introduced. One or more electronic trading platforms may be established — through accredited agencies and with Central Government approval — governed by guidelines issued by the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB). The CPCB will set minimum and maximum prices for EPRCs every six months, or more frequently as needed, factoring in collection costs and environmental compensation. Sixth, producers must now file an annual return in Form 1(C) with CPCB by June 30 each year for batteries manufactured, assembled, or imported in the preceding financial year. Recyclers and refurbishers must upload quarterly data on processed waste battery weights to the CPCB portal. Producers must also file Form 3 annual returns for pre-consumer waste batteries. Seventh, a sustainability production mandate has been introduced: producers must take measures for sustainable production of batteries or battery packs in accordance with guidelines to be issued by CPCB. Finally, producers that cease operations are not absolved of EPR obligations — they must continue to fulfil their EPR duties for batteries already placed on the market until operations fully wind down.

Who Is Affected Battery manufacturers and assemblers — including those producing under third-party or OEM arrangements for other brands Battery importers — including companies importing finished batteries, battery packs, or devices with embedded batteries (mobile phones, laptops, EVs, industrial equipment) Electric vehicle (EV) manufacturers and EV fleet operators with captive battery use Consumer electronics companies (smartphones, laptops, wearables, power tools) Telecom tower operators and data centre operators with large captive UPS/backup battery fleets Industrial equipment manufacturers using lead-acid or lithium-ion batteries Energy storage system (ESS) developers and grid-scale battery storage project owners Battery recyclers and refurbishers registered under the BWM Rules Importers of waste batteries or scrap batteries under the Hazardous Waste Management Rules, 2016 EPR compliance intermediaries, Producer Responsibility Organisations (PROs), and EPRC traders

Key Obligations & Deadlines IMMEDIATE (from October 25, 2023): The amended rules are effective from the date of Gazette publication. All producers, importers, recyclers, and refurbishers must treat the revised definitions, EPR scope, and EPRC rules as binding from this date. REGISTRATION: Every producer (including importers and contract manufacturers newly brought within scope) must obtain EPR registration from CPCB through the centralised online portal using Form 1(A). CPCB will issue a registration certificate in Form 1(B) that remains valid until cancelled or withdrawn. Producers already registered must update their registration details to reflect any changes in business operations. ANNUAL RETURN — BATTERIES PLACED ON MARKET: File Form 1(C) with CPCB by June 30 each year, covering batteries manufactured, assembled, or imported in the preceding financial year. The first filing under the amended rules covers FY 2023–24, due by June 30, 2024. ANNUAL RETURN — PRE-CONSUMER WASTE: File Form 3 annually for pre-consumer waste batteries generated during manufacturing, assembly, or import of batteries or battery packs in the preceding financial year. COLLECTION & RECYCLING/REFURBISHING TARGETS: Meet the collection, recycling, and refurbishing targets as specified in Schedule II of the BWM Rules, 2022, now extended to batteries kept for own use in addition to batteries placed on the market. QUARTERLY DATA UPLOAD — RECYCLERS & REFURBISHERS: Upload total weight of waste batteries processed on a quarterly basis to the CPCB portal for EPRC generation. This is a continuous obligation from the date of notification. EPRC USAGE RESTRICTION: Ensure EPR Certificates are used only for off-setting, carrying forward, or trading within the same battery category in which they were generated. Update internal compliance tracking systems to enforce category-wise EPRC accounting. IMPORTED WASTE BATTERY ACCOUNTING: Maintain separate accounts and reporting for recycling or refurbishing of imported waste batteries (under Hazardous Waste Rules). No EPRCs will be generated for this activity. SUSTAINABILITY PRODUCTION MEASURES: Monitor CPCB for forthcoming guidelines on sustainable battery production and prepare to implement measures upon notification. OPERATIONAL CLOSURE OBLIGATION: If planning to wind down operations, ensure EPR obligations for batteries already on the market are fulfilled through the closure date — factor this into any M&A, restructuring, or exit planning.

Climate Transition Angle The Battery Waste Management (Amendment) Rules, 2023 are not merely a compliance housekeeping exercise — they sit at the intersection of India's clean energy transition, circular economy ambitions, and growing global ESG disclosure expectations. India's EV and energy storage transition is accelerating rapidly. The FAME-II scheme, Production Linked Incentive (PLI) schemes for advanced chemistry cells, and state-level EV policies are collectively driving exponential growth in battery deployment. Without a robust end-of-life management system, this transition risks creating a new waste crisis — leaching toxic heavy metals (lead, cadmium, cobalt, lithium) into soil and groundwater, and releasing greenhouse gases from uncontrolled battery disposal. The 2023 amendment directly addresses this risk by tightening the EPR net and improving the traceability of waste battery flows. From a physical climate risk perspective, battery waste mismanagement has documented links to water contamination and biodiversity loss — both physical risks that are increasingly material to supply chain resilience and corporate liability. Companies with operations in flood-prone or water-stressed regions (increasingly common as climate impacts intensify) face amplified risks from improper battery storage and disposal. On global standards alignment, the International Sustainability Standards Board's IFRS S2 (Climate-related Disclosures) and the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) — both of which are shaping expectations for Indian companies listed or operating globally — require disclosure of circular economy practices and waste management as part of value chain sustainability reporting. The EU's own Battery Regulation (Regulation 2023/1542), which mandates recycled content requirements, carbon footprints, and digital battery passports, is already affecting Indian battery exporters and suppliers to European OEMs. India's strengthened BWM Rules create a domestic regulatory floor that helps Indian companies build credibility in these global value chains. For investors applying ESG screens, the amended rules raise the bar on what constitutes adequate battery waste governance. Companies that cannot demonstrate EPRC compliance, recycling target achievement, and sustainable production practices will face increasing scrutiny from domestic and foreign institutional investors, particularly as SEBI's Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting (BRSR) framework continues to evolve.

What To Do Now CONDUCT AN EPR SCOPE AUDIT: Map all battery-related activities across your business — manufacturing, assembly, importation of batteries or battery-embedded devices, captive battery use, and pre-consumer waste generation. Determine whether your current EPR registration and obligations reflect the expanded definitions and scope under the amended rules. Pay particular attention to contract manufacturing arrangements and captive battery fleets that may now be newly in scope. UPDATE REGISTRATION AND COMPLIANCE SYSTEMS: Log in to the CPCB centralised EPR portal to verify your registration is current, update any changed business information, and ensure your annual return filing system is configured to meet the June 30, 2024 deadline for Form 1(C). If you are a newly covered entity under the amended producer definition, initiate registration immediately. RESTRUCTURE YOUR EPRC TRACKING AND TRADING STRATEGY: Review your existing EPRC inventory and trading arrangements in light of the new category-wise restriction — EPRCs can no longer be used across battery categories. Engage your compliance team and any PROs or intermediaries to re-engineer your EPRC procurement and offsetting strategy. Monitor CPCB for the announcement of authorised EPRC trading platforms and the first EPRC price band notification. ENGAGE YOUR SUPPLY CHAIN: If you are a downstream user of batteries (EV manufacturer, electronics brand, industrial OEM), engage your battery suppliers to understand their EPR registration status and recycling target performance. Supplier EPR non-compliance creates reputational and legal exposure for your brand, and is increasingly a disclosure risk under BRSR and ISSB-aligned reporting frameworks. PREPARE FOR SUSTAINABILITY PRODUCTION GUIDELINES: Proactively assess your battery or battery pack production processes against emerging sustainability criteria — energy efficiency, recycled material content, hazardous substance reduction. Engage with CPCB's consultation processes when guidelines are drafted, and begin internal capability-building so you are not caught flat-footed when the guidelines become mandatory.

Our Take The Battery Waste Management (Amendment) Rules, 2023 represent a meaningful step-change in India's EPR governance maturity. The introduction of a regulated, price-banded EPRC trading mechanism — modelled loosely on the renewable energy certificate market — is particularly significant: it transforms EPR compliance from a pure administrative exercise into a market-based instrument that will, over time, incentivise investment in collection infrastructure and recycling capacity. The prohibition on cross-category EPRC use closes a loophole that had the potential to undermine the environmental integrity of the system. What to watch next: CPCB's implementation of the trading platform guidelines and the first EPRC price band will be critical stress tests of whether the market mechanism delivers real-world waste recovery improvements or remains a paper compliance system. We also expect CPCB to issue the sustainable production guidelines in 2024, which could introduce binding design-for-recyclability requirements that reshape product development timelines for battery manufacturers. Companies that treat these amendments as a minimum compliance threshold — rather than a ceiling — and invest early in closed-loop battery supply chains will be best positioned as India's battery economy scales toward the terawatt-hour era.

title: Battery Waste Management (Amendment) Rules, 2024: What India's Battery Industry Must Know Now
summary: India's Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change has amended the Battery Waste Management Rules, 2022 with effect from March 14, 2024, tightening the Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework for batteries. The amendments introduce price floors and ceilings on EPR certificates, mand

sections.t: Executive Summary India's Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change has amended the Battery Waste Management Rules, 2022 with effect from March 14, 2024, tightening the Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework for batteries. The amendments introduce price floors and ceilings on EPR certificates, mandate structured Environmental Compensation guidelines, and significantly liberalise carry-forward provisions for unmet collection targets — replacing volume-averaged calculations with a simpler 'remaining quantity' basis, uniformly capped at 60%. Producers, importers, refurbishers, and recyclers of all battery types must immediately reassess their EPR compliance strategies and portal registrations to align with the revised rules.

What Changed The Battery Waste Management (Amendment) Rules, 2024 make four substantive changes to the parent Battery Waste Management Rules, 2022. First, Rule 10 is amended to establish a price band for EPR certificates traded on the CPCB portal: the highest permissible price is set at 100% of the Environmental Compensation (EC) payable for non-fulfilment of EPR obligations, and the lowest permissible price is set at 30% of that EC value. This creates a regulated market corridor — EPR certificates can no longer be traded below 30% of the penalty equivalent, preventing a race to the bottom in certificate pricing. Second, a new sub-rule (18) is inserted to clarify that all certificate exchange prices between registered entities must fall within this regulated band, ensuring market integrity on the CPCB portal. Third, Rule 13(3) is rewritten to require the CPCB to formally prepare, consult on, and recommend to MoEF&CC a comprehensive guideline for the imposition and collection of Environmental Compensation from producers, refurbishers, and recyclers who fail to comply. This replaces a more open-ended provision and introduces a consultative process via the Rule 15 Implementation Committee before guidelines are finalised. Fourth, and perhaps most practically impactful, Schedule II carry-forward provisions are uniformly revised across multiple battery categories (including automotive, industrial, electric vehicle, and portable batteries under clauses vi through xii). The old language calculated carry-forward as a percentage of the average annual quantity placed in the market over multi-year cycles (which varied — 60% for some, 20% for others). The new, uniform language allows carry-forward of up to 60% of the remaining unmet quantity from the applicable compliance cycle. This shift from an average-based to a residual-based calculation is more equitable for companies whose market volumes fluctuate year to year, and it standardises the carry-forward cap at 60% across all major battery categories.

Who Is Affected Battery Producers and Manufacturers: All entities that manufacture batteries in India — including EV batteries (lithium-ion), automotive lead-acid batteries, industrial batteries, and portable consumer batteries — and are registered on the CPCB EPR portal. Battery Importers: Companies importing batteries or battery-containing products (EVs, electronics, UPS systems, power tools) that have EPR obligations under the 2022 Rules. Electric Vehicle (EV) OEMs: Automobile manufacturers selling EVs in India, who bear producer responsibility for the batteries placed in the market through their vehicles. Battery Recyclers and Refurbishers: Entities engaged in collection, refurbishment, or recycling of waste batteries that participate in the EPR certificate ecosystem or are subject to Environmental Compensation for non-compliance. EPR Certificate Traders and Intermediaries: Registered entities buying and selling EPR certificates on the CPCB portal, who must now operate within the newly defined price band. Consumer Electronics and IT Hardware Companies: Brands placing portable battery-containing devices in the Indian market with battery EPR obligations. Telecom Companies and Data Centre Operators: Large users of industrial and stationary batteries with take-back and EPR responsibilities.

Key Obligations & Deadlines Immediate (effective March 14, 2024): All EPR certificate trades on the CPCB portal must fall within the newly defined price band — minimum 30% and maximum 100% of the Environmental Compensation value applicable to the obligated entity. Any trades structured outside this band are non-compliant from the date of notification. Ongoing — EPR Target Fulfilment: Producers must continue meeting annual collection and recycling targets as specified in Schedule II. However, for the current and future compliance cycles, the carry-forward entitlement is now calculated as 60% of the remaining (unmet) battery quantity from the applicable cycle — not as a percentage of the annual average placed in the market. Companies must recalculate their carry-forward positions using the new methodology. Ongoing — Environmental Compensation Readiness: Await CPCB's new guidelines on Environmental Compensation imposition and collection, which the Board is now mandated to prepare in consultation with the Rule 15 Implementation Committee and submit to MoEF&CC. Once approved, these guidelines will govern penalties for non-compliance by producers, refurbishers, and recyclers. Portal Compliance: Ensure all EPR certificate transactions are executed exclusively through registered entities on the CPCB portal at prices within the regulated band. Review existing contractual arrangements with recyclers and aggregators to ensure price terms remain compliant. Registration Verification: Confirm that all entities in your battery waste value chain — recyclers, collection agents, refurbishers — are registered on the CPCB portal, as the price band provisions apply specifically to portal-registered entity transactions. Documentation and Audit Trail: Update internal EPR compliance records to reflect the new carry-forward calculation methodology. Ensure your compliance management system is recalibrated before the next annual EPR reporting cycle.

Climate Transition Angle Batteries are the material backbone of India's energy transition. The country's stated target of 500 GW of renewable energy by 2030, its rapidly scaling EV ecosystem, and the electrification of industrial and telecom infrastructure all depend on a massive and growing battery supply chain. The waste that this transition generates — lithium, cobalt, nickel, lead, and other critical minerals — represents both an environmental liability and a significant circular economy opportunity. The Battery Waste Management Rules are India's primary regulatory instrument for ensuring that the climate solution does not become a waste crisis. The amendments signal regulatory maturation. By pricing EPR certificates within a defined band, the government is building a functional secondary market for battery recycling compliance — analogous to Renewable Energy Certificates (RECs) in the power sector. A credible price floor (30% of the penalty equivalent) ensures recyclers and collectors receive economically viable compensation; a ceiling (100% of penalty) prevents producers from simply buying their way out of physical collection obligations at any cost. From a global standards perspective, this framework is increasingly relevant to companies reporting under the International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) IFRS S2 framework or preparing for alignment with the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD). Both frameworks require disclosure of material resource and waste risks. For companies with significant battery supply chains or battery-intensive products, the cost of EPR compliance, exposure to Environmental Compensation, and the quality of take-back infrastructure are all financially material disclosures. Additionally, the EU Battery Regulation (2023), which mandates recycled content thresholds and carbon footprint declarations for batteries sold in Europe, means that Indian battery manufacturers and EV exporters face convergent regulatory pressure from both domestic and international directions. A robust domestic EPR compliance posture directly supports the due diligence documentation these exporters will need. On physical climate risk: battery recycling facilities and collection infrastructure in India face growing exposure to extreme heat, flooding, and water stress — all of which can disrupt waste battery logistics and processing. Companies building EPR compliance programmes should embed climate resilience into their collection network design, particularly in climate-vulnerable geographies.

What To Do Now Recalculate Your Carry-Forward Position Today: If you are in an active compliance cycle with unmet EPR targets, urgently recalculate your permissible carry-forward under the new 'remaining quantity' methodology (up to 60% of unmet quantity, not 60% of annual average). This will change your liability exposure and your EPR certificate procurement strategy for the remainder of the cycle. Engage your EPR compliance consultant or legal team to model both old and new calculations. Audit All EPR Certificate Contracts and Portal Transactions: Review all existing and pipeline agreements for purchase or sale of EPR certificates. Any price terms that fall below 30% or above 100% of the applicable Environmental Compensation value are now non-compliant. Renegotiate contracts as necessary and brief your procurement and finance teams on the new price band before the next transaction. Engage Your Recycler and Refurbisher Network: The new Rule 13(3) will bring recyclers and refurbishers under the Environmental Compensation regime, not just producers. Conduct a compliance health check with your downstream partners — are they portal-registered? Are they meeting processing standards? Their non-compliance creates reputational and supply chain risk for your EPR programme. Monitor CPCB for Environmental Compensation Guidelines: The CPCB is now mandated to draft and recommend EC guidelines to MoEF&CC. Track CPCB notifications closely, as these guidelines will define the financial penalty structure for non-compliance. Once released, model the financial exposure under various EPR shortfall scenarios and factor this into your sustainability budget and risk register. Integrate EPR Compliance into ESG Reporting and Transition Planning: If your company reports under BRSR (Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting), GRI, or is preparing for ISSB/CSRD alignment, formally incorporate battery EPR compliance status, EC risk exposure, and recycled material recovery rates into your material topics assessment. This positions your organisation ahead of mandatory ESG disclosure requirements and supports investor-grade climate transition disclosures.

Our Take The Battery Waste Management (Amendment) Rules, 2024 represent a quiet but consequential tightening of India's circular economy architecture for batteries. The introduction of a price band for EPR certificates is particularly significant — it transforms what was a compliance mechanism into an embryonic environmental commodity market with real price discovery, which is exactly what India needs to fund a scalable battery recycling industry. The uniform carry-forward cap of 60% on residual quantities is a pragmatic concession to industry volatility, but make no mistake: the direction of travel is toward stricter targets and higher accountability as India's EV and renewable energy markets scale exponentially. Companies that treat EPR as a box-ticking exercise — buying the cheapest certificates available and minimising engagement with actual waste streams — will find themselves exposed as the CPCB's Environmental Compensation guidelines sharpen and as international customers and investors demand verifiable circular economy credentials. The smart move is to use this regulatory moment to build genuine take-back infrastructure, lock in credible recycling partnerships, and embed battery lifecycle management into your broader climate transition strategy. Those who do will find compliance costs manageable and competitive advantage real; those who do not will face escalating penalties and reputational risk in an increasingly scrutinised sector.

title: Battery Waste Management Amendment Rules, 2025: What Producers Must Do Now on EPR Labelling and Hazardous Metal Marking
summary: The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) notified the Battery Waste Management Amendment Rules, 2025 on 24 February 2025, introducing three targeted but operationally significant changes to Schedule I of the 2022 Rules. Producers of batteries and battery-integrated equipment m

sections.t: Executive Summary The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) notified the Battery Waste Management Amendment Rules, 2025 on 24 February 2025, introducing three targeted but operationally significant changes to Schedule I of the 2022 Rules. Producers of batteries and battery-integrated equipment must now print their Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) registration number as a barcode or QR code on products, packaging, or accompanying brochures — and must notify the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) in writing before doing so. Simultaneously, the amendment clarifies that mandatory hazardous-metal labelling for Cadmium ('Cd') and Lead ('Pb') does not apply where concentrations fall below defined trace thresholds, reducing compliance burden for manufacturers of modern low-hazard battery chemistries.

What Changed The Battery Waste Management Rules, 2022 established India's Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework for batteries — requiring producers, importers, and brand owners to register with CPCB, meet collection and recycling targets, and label their batteries with specific information. The 2025 Amendment, the fifth modification to the 2022 Rules, makes three precise changes to Schedule I, Paragraph 2. First, an exemption has been carved out for labelling obligations under Clause (ia): packaging that is already regulated under Rule 26 of the Legal Metrology (Packaged Commodities) Rules, 2011 — which governs mandatory declarations on pre-packaged goods — will not need to duplicate those requirements under the Battery Waste Rules. This eliminates regulatory overlap for consumer-facing packaged battery products. Second, a new Clause (ib) has been inserted, offering producers a flexible pathway to fulfil EPR labelling requirements. Instead of a single mandated format, producers may now embed their EPR registration number into a barcode or QR code and place it on any one of five specified locations: the battery or battery pack itself, the equipment containing the battery, the battery's packaging, the equipment's packaging, or bulk packaging for wholesale (non-retail) batteries. Alternatively, the EPR registration number may be printed on the product information brochure. The critical procedural requirement: producers must inform CPCB in writing before opting for this alternative route. CPCB will in turn publish and quarterly-update a consolidated public list of producers who have done so on its centralised online portal. Third, the amendment brings welcome clarity on hazardous metal marking. Previously, all batteries containing Cadmium or Lead were required to display the chemical symbols 'Cd' or 'Pb'. The new proviso under Clause (v) specifies that this marking is not required when Cadmium concentration is at or below 0.002% by weight (20 parts per million) or Lead concentration is at or below 0.004% by weight (40 parts per million). This threshold-based exemption aligns India's approach more closely with international practice and reflects the reality that trace amounts of these metals in modern battery chemistries pose a categorically different risk profile than batteries where these metals are a primary component.

Who Is Affected Battery producers and importers: All entities registered or required to register under the EPR framework for batteries — including manufacturers of portable, automotive, industrial, EV traction, and stationary storage batteries. Brand owners and OEMs: Companies that sell equipment with built-in batteries (consumer electronics, power tools, electric vehicles, UPS systems) under their own brand name and hold EPR obligations. Bulk/wholesale battery suppliers: Entities supplying batteries or battery packs in bulk for industrial or commercial use (not for direct retail sale) must comply with QR/barcode labelling on bulk packaging. Battery recyclers and extended supply chain: While the direct obligations are on producers, recyclers and downstream handlers benefit from improved traceability enabled by mandatory EPR registration codes on products. Compliance and legal teams in the EV sector: Given India's rapidly scaling electric vehicle ecosystem, EV OEMs and their battery suppliers face the most material operational impact given high production volumes and complex battery pack configurations. Consumer electronics manufacturers: Companies producing smartphones, laptops, wearables, and other devices with integrated or replaceable batteries must assess labelling on both the battery and the device/packaging. Legal Metrology-regulated packagers: Companies whose packaging already complies with Legal Metrology (Packaged Commodities) Rules, 2011, Rule 26 gain direct relief from duplicative battery labelling requirements.

Key Obligations & Deadlines IMMEDIATE (effective 24 February 2025): The amendment came into force on the date of publication in the Official Gazette. All new obligations and exemptions are legally operative from this date. EPR QR/Barcode Labelling — Prior Written Notice to CPCB: Before a producer adopts the barcode/QR code route under new Clause (ib), they must submit written intimation to the Central Pollution Control Board. This is a precondition, not a post-facto filing. Producers must not implement this labelling approach without completing this step. EPR Registration Number Placement: Producers must print a barcode or QR code containing their CPCB EPR registration number on at least one of the following: (a) the battery or battery pack; (b) the equipment containing the battery; (c) packaging of the battery/battery pack; (d) packaging of the equipment; or (e) bulk packaging of batteries not for retail sale. Alternatively, the EPR registration number must appear on the product information brochure. Quarterly Portal Updates: Producers who opt for the QR/barcode route must keep their details current on CPCB's centralised online portal, as CPCB will publish a consolidated and quarterly-updated list of such producers. Non-updation may result in removal from the published list, creating compliance visibility risk. Cadmium and Lead Marking Exemption — Internal Chemical Testing: Producers seeking to avail the 'Cd'/'Pb' marking exemption must verify and document that their battery's Cadmium concentration is ≤ 0.002% by weight (20 ppm) and/or Lead concentration is ≤ 0.004% by weight (40 ppm). Internal test records must be maintained as evidence for potential regulatory scrutiny. Legal Metrology Overlap Check: Producers whose packaging falls under Rule 26 of the Legal Metrology (Packaged Commodities) Rules, 2011 should formally document that determination to substantiate their exemption from the overlapping Battery Waste Rules labelling requirement under Clause (ia). Review Existing EPR Registrations: Ensure your CPCB EPR registration number is current, valid, and accurately reflected in all labelling systems and product information materials before deploying QR/barcode-based compliance.

Climate Transition Angle At first glance, labelling and marking amendments may appear to be administrative housekeeping. In the context of India's climate transition, however, they carry deeper strategic significance. Batteries are the foundational technology enabling the shift from fossil fuels to clean energy — in electric vehicles, in grid-scale storage balancing intermittent solar and wind generation, and in distributed energy systems for homes and businesses. The integrity of the battery value chain — from raw material sourcing through manufacturing, use, collection, and recycling — is a critical climate infrastructure question. The QR/barcode EPR registration requirement is a traceability mechanism. When every battery in the market carries a machine-readable link to its producer's EPR registration, it becomes far easier to enforce collection and recycling targets, close leakages in the reverse logistics chain, and generate auditable data on battery material flows. This kind of product-level digital traceability is precisely what global frameworks are beginning to mandate: the European Union's Battery Regulation (EU) 2023/1542, for instance, requires a 'battery passport' — a digital record of a battery's composition, carbon footprint, supply chain provenance, and end-of-life data — for EV, industrial, and LMT batteries by 2027. India's EPR QR code requirement is an embryonic version of this concept. Companies that treat it as a checkbox exercise will find themselves unprepared when global buyers, institutional investors, and export market regulators ask for richer data. Companies that build robust digital product identity systems now — linked to bill-of-materials data, carbon footprint per cell, and recycled content claims — will be positioned to meet both domestic EPR obligations and emerging international disclosure requirements simultaneously. On physical risk: the amendment's trace-threshold exemptions for Cadmium and Lead reflect the shift in dominant battery chemistries — Lithium Iron Phosphate (LFP), Nickel Manganese Cobalt (NMC), and solid-state batteries — away from historically hazardous chemistries. This chemistry transition is itself climate-driven, as energy density, cycle life, and thermal safety improvements accelerate electrification. Compliance teams should track chemical composition as a live variable, not a static product specification, given how rapidly battery technology is evolving. From a supply chain disclosure perspective, SEBI's Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting (BRSR) Core framework — mandatory for the top 150 listed companies from FY 2023-24 — requires value chain disclosures on environmental metrics. Battery producers and their upstream mineral suppliers (Lithium, Cobalt, Manganese) face growing pressure to disclose Scope 3 emissions and hazardous waste management practices. ISSB's IFRS S2 Climate Disclosures and the EU's CSRD (applicable to Indian subsidiaries of European companies and Indian companies accessing EU capital markets) both require disclosure of climate-related physical and transition risks that are directly relevant to battery manufacturers operating in a rapidly decarbonising economy.

What To Do Now Audit your current EPR registration status and labelling: Immediately verify that your CPCB EPR registration number is valid and active. Map all current product labels, packaging designs, and brochures to identify where the EPR number currently appears — and whether a barcode/QR code integration is feasible in your next production cycle. Submit written intimation to CPCB before switching to QR/barcode labelling: If your organisation chooses to adopt the new QR/barcode pathway under Clause (ib), draft and dispatch formal written notice to CPCB before any product bearing the new label format reaches the market. Work with your legal and regulatory affairs team to ensure this notification is filed correctly and acknowledged. Conduct battery chemistry testing and documentation for Cd/Pb exemption: Commission or review existing test reports for Cadmium and Lead concentration in your battery products. If concentrations are at or below the defined thresholds (Cd ≤ 20 ppm, Pb ≤ 40 ppm), formally document this finding and instruct your labelling team to apply the exemption — while retaining test evidence for at least the duration of your regulatory record-keeping obligations. Establish a quarterly CPCB portal update process: Assign ownership within your compliance or regulatory function to ensure your producer details on CPCB's centralised portal are reviewed and updated every quarter. Treat this as a recurring calendar obligation, not a one-time registration activity, to remain on CPCB's published consolidated producer list. Begin building a battery digital traceability roadmap: Use this amendment as the trigger to initiate a broader conversation internally about digital product identity for batteries. Evaluate whether your QR code can be enriched over time to carry carbon footprint data, recycled content claims, and end-of-life instructions — positioning your organisation ahead of the EU Battery Regulation passport requirements and future BRSR value chain disclosure expectations.

Our Take The Battery Waste Management Amendment Rules, 2025 are incremental in legal scope but directionally significant. India's battery EPR framework is still relatively young, and each amendment is systematically closing gaps — reducing regulatory duplication with Legal Metrology rules, providing operational flexibility on labelling formats, and calibrating hazardous-substance marking to reflect the chemistry realities of modern batteries. What stands out is the emphasis on digital traceability: mandating EPR registration numbers in machine-readable QR/barcode format is a meaningful step toward a data-driven circular economy for batteries. For compliance teams, the immediate priorities are clear — CPCB notification before label changes, chemistry testing for metal thresholds, and portal hygiene. But the strategic message is larger: the global battery regulatory environment is tightening rapidly, driven by the EU Battery Regulation, ISSB climate disclosure standards, and the supply chain scrutiny of institutional investors. Indian battery producers and OEMs that invest now in robust product identity, traceability infrastructure, and chemistry transparency will not merely be compliant — they will be competitive in the markets and supply chains that matter most over the next decade.

title: Battery Waste Management Rules, 2022: What India's Producers, Importers, and Recyclers Must Know
summary: India's Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) notified the Battery Waste Management Rules, 2022 on August 24, 2022, superseding the older Battery (Management and Handling) Rules, 2001. The new framework introduces a comprehensive Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) regime co

sections.t: Executive Summary India's Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) notified the Battery Waste Management Rules, 2022 on August 24, 2022, superseding the older Battery (Management and Handling) Rules, 2001. The new framework introduces a comprehensive Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) regime covering all battery types — from portable and automotive to industrial and electric vehicle (EV) batteries — making producers, importers, and recyclers directly accountable for end-of-life battery collection, refurbishment, and recycling. For businesses across India's rapidly growing EV, consumer electronics, energy storage, and manufacturing sectors, compliance is no longer optional: it is a legal obligation with measurable targets and a centralized EPR registration system managed by the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB).

What Changed The Battery Waste Management Rules, 2022 replace the two-decade-old Battery (Management and Handling) Rules, 2001, which were widely regarded as inadequate for the scale and complexity of India's modern battery economy. The old rules had limited scope and weak enforcement mechanisms. The 2022 Rules mark a fundamental shift in philosophy: instead of placing the burden of battery waste management on consumers and local bodies, the new framework holds producers responsible through Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR). EPR, in plain terms, means that the company which manufactures, sells, or imports a battery must also take responsibility for what happens to that battery at the end of its useful life. Producers are required to register on the CPCB's centralized EPR portal, set collection and recycling targets, and demonstrate compliance annually. The rules cover all battery chemistries and sizes — including lithium-ion, nickel, cobalt, and lead-acid variants — and apply irrespective of whether the battery is sold standalone or embedded within a device or vehicle. The rules define four key battery categories: (1) Portable Batteries — sealed, under 5 kg, not for industrial or EV use; (2) Automotive Batteries — used for starting, lighting, or ignition in motor vehicles; (3) Industrial Batteries — designed for industrial use, excluding portable, EV, and automotive types; and (4) Electric Vehicle (EV) Batteries — specifically designed to power hybrid and electric road vehicles. Each category carries distinct EPR collection and recycling obligations as specified in Schedule II of the Rules. Additionally, the framework recognizes refurbishers and recyclers as formal entities that must register and operate within an environmentally sound management framework.

Who Is Affected Battery Producers: Any entity manufacturing batteries (or devices containing batteries) under its own brand, or selling batteries manufactured by third parties under its brand name Battery Importers: Companies importing batteries — standalone or embedded in equipment such as laptops, smartphones, power tools, EVs, and energy storage systems Electric Vehicle (EV) Manufacturers and OEMs: Companies manufacturing or selling EVs, which carry specific EV battery EPR obligations given the high material content (lithium, cobalt, nickel) and scale of deployment Consumer Electronics Companies: Manufacturers and importers of smartphones, laptops, wearables, and other portable electronics with embedded rechargeable batteries Industrial Equipment Manufacturers: Companies making UPS systems, telecom infrastructure, solar storage, and heavy industrial machinery that use sealed or unsealed industrial batteries Battery Recyclers and Refurbishers: Entities engaged in dismantling, processing, reconditioning, or recycling waste batteries — all of whom must register with CPCB Retailers and Bulk Consumers: Dealers and large-volume consumers (such as telecom towers, data centers, and utilities) who have obligations to facilitate collection and return of waste batteries Public Waste Management Authorities: Gram Panchayats, Municipal Corporations, and their service agencies who interact with battery waste streams at the local level

Key Obligations & Deadlines EPR Registration (Immediate / Ongoing): All producers (manufacturers and importers) must register on the CPCB's centralized EPR portal before placing batteries on the market. Registration is mandatory — operating without it constitutes a violation of the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986 Collection Targets: Producers must meet phased collection targets for waste batteries as specified in Schedule II of the Rules. Targets are defined as a percentage of batteries placed on the market in prior years, and ramp up progressively over time to encourage rapid scale-up of collection infrastructure Recycling and Refurbishment Targets: Collected waste batteries must meet minimum recycling and/or refurbishment targets as specified in Schedule II. Recyclers and refurbishers must also be CPCB-registered, and producers are responsible for ensuring only registered entities handle their waste batteries EPR Credit Mechanism: The Rules introduce a tradeable EPR credit system — producers who exceed their targets can generate credits, which can be purchased by those falling short. This market-based mechanism is managed through the CPCB portal and mirrors global EPR best practices Annual Reporting: Producers, recyclers, and refurbishers must submit annual returns to CPCB through the EPR portal, detailing quantities of batteries sold, collected, recycled, and refurbished. Non-submission or misreporting attracts penalties under the Environment (Protection) Act Environmentally Sound Management (ESM): All waste batteries must be handled, stored, transported, and processed in accordance with ESM standards. Hazardous battery materials — including lead, cadmium, lithium, and cobalt compounds — must not be disposed of in landfills or through open burning Take-Back and Deposit-Refund Systems: Producers of portable batteries are encouraged (and in some cases required) to implement take-back schemes, potentially including deposit-refund mechanisms, to incentivize consumer return of spent batteries Labelling Requirements: Batteries placed on the market must carry prescribed labelling, including EPR registration numbers and symbols indicating that the battery should not be disposed of as general municipal waste Effective Date: The Rules came into force on the date of publication in the Official Gazette — August 24, 2022. Producers who have not yet registered or put EPR systems in place are already in a state of non-compliance

Climate Transition Angle The Battery Waste Management Rules, 2022 are far more than a housekeeping regulation — they sit at the intersection of India's climate transition ambitions, global supply chain scrutiny, and the emerging international ESG disclosure landscape. Understanding these connections is essential for companies that see themselves as part of the clean energy economy. India's National Electric Mobility Mission and its target of 30% EV penetration by 2030 will place millions of lithium-ion battery packs into circulation over the next decade. Without a robust end-of-life management system, this transition risks creating a toxic waste crisis that undermines the very environmental credentials of clean transportation. The 2022 Rules are, in effect, the regulatory infrastructure that makes the EV transition environmentally credible — not just economically viable. From a physical risk perspective, improper battery disposal — particularly of lead-acid and lithium-ion chemistries — poses significant soil and groundwater contamination risks in industrial corridors and urban areas. Companies with supply chains running through regions with poor waste management infrastructure face reputational and liability exposure if their end-of-life batteries are traced back to irresponsible handling. This is a material risk that institutional investors and ESG rating agencies are increasingly factoring into company assessments. On the global standards front, the European Union's new Battery Regulation (2023/1542) — one of the most ambitious battery regulations in the world — introduces mandatory carbon footprint declarations, minimum recycled content requirements, and digital battery passports for batteries sold in the EU. Indian exporters supplying batteries or battery-embedded products to European markets will need to align their EPR compliance data and recycled content tracking with EU requirements. The ISSB's IFRS S2 climate disclosure standard and the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) both require companies to disclose material risks and opportunities related to the circular economy and resource transition — of which battery waste is a growing component. Indian companies with international investors or listed subsidiaries in global markets should treat CPCB EPR compliance as the foundation of a broader ESG data architecture, not a standalone regulatory tick-box.

What To Do Now Register on the CPCB EPR Portal Immediately: If your company manufactures, imports, or sells batteries or battery-embedded products in India and has not yet obtained EPR registration, this is your most urgent action. Assign a dedicated compliance officer to complete registration, upload the required documentation, and maintain the account on an ongoing basis Conduct a Full Battery Inventory and Market Placement Audit: Map every SKU or product line that contains a battery — including embedded batteries in devices — and quantify the volumes placed on the Indian market in each of the past three financial years. This data forms the baseline for calculating your EPR collection and recycling targets under Schedule II Establish Verified Collection and Recycling Partnerships: Identify and contract with CPCB-registered recyclers and refurbishers. Do not rely on informal or unregistered collection channels — doing so will not count toward your EPR targets and exposes your company to liability. Consider joining or forming a Producer Responsibility Organisation (PRO) to pool collection infrastructure costs with other producers Build an EPR Credit Tracking and Reporting System: Implement internal systems (or integrate with CPCB's portal) to track battery collection, handover to recyclers, and EPR credit generation in real time. Annual reporting errors or gaps are a common cause of regulatory notices — invest in data accuracy from the outset Align Internal ESG Disclosures with EPR Data: If your company publishes a Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report (BRSR), sustainability report, or responds to ESG questionnaires from investors or customers, incorporate your EPR targets, actual collection rates, and recycled material content data. This positions your company ahead of the curve as global buyers and investors increasingly demand circular economy metrics aligned with ISSB and EU standards

Our Take The Battery Waste Management Rules, 2022 represent one of the most significant upgrades to India's extended producer responsibility framework in recent years, and their importance will only grow as the country's EV and energy storage sectors scale rapidly. What makes this regulation particularly consequential is its timing: it arrives just as global supply chains for critical battery minerals — lithium, cobalt, nickel, and manganese — are coming under intense regulatory and investor scrutiny, and as India positions itself as both a major EV market and a potential battery manufacturing hub under the PLI (Production Linked Incentive) scheme. Companies that treat EPR compliance as a mere administrative formality will find themselves poorly positioned when global customers and investors start demanding auditable end-of-life battery data as a condition of doing business. Those who invest now in building robust collection infrastructure, verified recycling partnerships, and integrated ESG reporting systems will not only stay ahead of domestic enforcement but will also be better placed to access export markets under the EU Battery Regulation and to satisfy the disclosure expectations of ISSB-aligned investors. The next frontier — which CPCB is likely to address in amendments — will be mandatory minimum recycled content requirements and battery passport-style traceability, mirroring the EU trajectory. The time to build the data and operational foundations for that future is now.

title: Behind the Rules: How ISSB Sets IFRS Sustainability Standards — And Why Indian Companies Must Pay Attention
summary: The International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB), operating under the IFRS Foundation, follows a rigorous, multi-stage due process to develop globally consistent sustainability disclosure standards — most notably IFRS S1 (General Requirements) and IFRS S2 (Climate-Related Disclosures). Unders

sections.t: Executive Summary The International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB), operating under the IFRS Foundation, follows a rigorous, multi-stage due process to develop globally consistent sustainability disclosure standards — most notably IFRS S1 (General Requirements) and IFRS S2 (Climate-Related Disclosures). Understanding how these standards are created is not merely academic: it gives Indian companies early warning of what is coming, allows them to engage in shaping the rules, and enables proactive alignment with frameworks that Indian regulators — including SEBI — are increasingly mirroring. Companies that track the ISSB's pipeline today will avoid costly compliance scrambles tomorrow.

What Changed The IFRS Foundation, the independent not-for-profit body that oversees global accounting and sustainability reporting standards, has published a transparent account of how IFRS Standards — including those issued by the ISSB — are developed and maintained. The standard-setting process is structured, consultative, and iterative, designed to ensure that standards reflect the needs of investors, preparers, regulators, and the public across jurisdictions. At a high level, the process moves through several defined stages: agenda consultation (identifying priority topics), research and discussion papers (exploring potential approaches), exposure drafts (formal proposals open for public comment), standard finalisation (incorporating feedback and issuing the standard), and post-implementation review (assessing real-world effectiveness). Each stage includes mandatory public consultation periods, typically 90–120 days, during which any stakeholder — including Indian companies and regulators — can submit formal comments. For sustainability specifically, the ISSB has signalled that its near-term agenda beyond IFRS S1 and S2 will focus on biodiversity, human capital, and human rights disclosures. These are not distant abstractions — they are the next wave of mandatory reporting obligations that will cascade into Indian regulatory frameworks, supply chain contracts, and investor expectations within the next three to five years.

Who Is Affected Listed companies on Indian stock exchanges (BSE/NSE), particularly the top 1,000 by market capitalisation already subject to SEBI's Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report (BRSR) framework Indian subsidiaries and joint ventures of multinational corporations headquartered in jurisdictions adopting ISSB standards (EU, UK, Japan, Singapore, Australia, and others) Export-oriented manufacturers and suppliers integrated into global value chains where buyers are subject to CSRD or ISSB-aligned reporting obligations Financial institutions — banks, asset managers, and insurers — increasingly required to disclose portfolio-level climate and sustainability risks CFOs, Chief Sustainability Officers, and Investor Relations teams responsible for sustainability reporting strategy and investor communication Audit and assurance firms advising on sustainability disclosure readiness and limited/reasonable assurance engagements

Key Obligations & Deadlines IFRS S1 & S2 (already issued, June 2023): Companies in jurisdictions that have adopted or endorsed these standards must disclose material sustainability-related risks and opportunities (S1) and climate-specific information including Scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions (S2) — early adopters began reporting in FY2024 SEBI BRSR Core (effective FY2023–24 onwards): India's BRSR Core framework is explicitly designed to be interoperable with ISSB standards; top 150 listed companies must provide assured BRSR Core disclosures — expand to top 250 by FY2024–25 and top 500 by FY2025–26 ISSB Agenda Consultation (ongoing): The ISSB periodically releases agenda consultations — monitor the IFRS Foundation website and submit responses to shape standards on biodiversity (TNFD-aligned), human capital, and just transition topics before they are finalised Exposure Draft Comment Periods: When the ISSB releases exposure drafts (expected on biodiversity and human capital disclosures in 2025–2026), Indian companies have a 90–120 day window to submit formal comments — missing this window means losing the opportunity to influence rules that will affect you ISSB Jurisdictional Adoption Tracker: Continuously monitor which of your key trading partners and investor-domicile countries have adopted ISSB standards, as contractual and investor-driven disclosure requirements will flow down to Indian entities regardless of domestic mandates

Climate Transition Angle The ISSB's standard-setting process is the architectural backbone of the global climate disclosure infrastructure. IFRS S2 is explicitly designed to be consistent with the Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures (TCFD) recommendations and to interoperate with the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and its European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS). This means that a standard shaped in Basel or London today becomes a supply chain requirement, investor screening criterion, or trade barrier for Indian companies within two to three years. From a physical risk perspective, IFRS S2 requires companies to disclose their exposure to acute events (cyclones, floods, heatwaves) and chronic shifts (sea-level rise, changes in precipitation) — risks that are acutely relevant to Indian industries from agriculture and textiles to infrastructure and financial services. The Ganges basin, coastal manufacturing clusters, and rain-fed agricultural supply chains all carry material physical climate risk that investors will soon demand to be quantified and disclosed under ISSB-aligned frameworks. The transition risk dimension is equally significant. As the ISSB refines requirements around carbon pricing exposure, stranded asset risk, and climate scenario analysis (using 1.5°C and 2°C pathways), Indian companies in carbon-intensive sectors — steel, cement, chemicals, thermal power — must build the internal data and modelling capabilities now to meet these disclosures credibly. Companies that treat ISSB alignment as a future problem will find themselves at a competitive and capital-access disadvantage against peers who have invested early.

What To Do Now Map your regulatory exposure: Identify which jurisdictions your investors, lenders, customers, and partners are based in, and determine whether those jurisdictions have adopted or are adopting ISSB standards — this maps your de facto compliance timeline even before Indian mandates expand Subscribe to the ISSB's due process pipeline: Register on the IFRS Foundation website to receive notifications on new discussion papers and exposure drafts; assign a team member to review and respond to consultations on upcoming topics (biodiversity, human capital) — your response shapes standards that will govern your disclosures Conduct a gap assessment against IFRS S1 and S2: Even if not yet mandated domestically, benchmark your current BRSR and ESG disclosures against IFRS S1 and S2 requirements to identify data gaps, particularly around Scope 3 emissions, climate scenario analysis, and governance disclosures Invest in Scope 3 data infrastructure now: IFRS S2's Scope 3 requirements are the most technically demanding element — begin supplier engagement, primary data collection, and spend-based estimation methodologies immediately, as building this capability takes 18–24 months minimum Brief your board and audit committee: Present the ISSB standard-setting timeline and its interoperability with SEBI BRSR Core to your board; ensure that sustainability disclosure is treated as a governance and financial reporting matter — not just a CSR or communications exercise

Our Take The ISSB's transparent, consultative standard-setting process is both a technical mechanism and a strategic opportunity — and most Indian companies are not yet using it as one. The window to influence rules on biodiversity disclosure, human capital metrics, and just transition reporting is open right now, through formal comment submissions and participation in ISSB roundtables and outreach events. Indian industry associations, the Institute of Chartered Accountants of India (ICAI), and SEBI itself have begun engaging with ISSB processes, but corporate-level participation remains thin. This is a missed opportunity. Beyond participation, the deeper significance of understanding how ISSB standards are made is this: the pipeline is predictable. Companies that track the research agenda today can build systems, train teams, and engage supply chains in advance — converting a compliance burden into a competitive advantage. The question for Indian business leaders is no longer whether ISSB-aligned disclosure will be required; it is whether you will be ready when it is.

title: Beta Framework Testing: What India's Business Leaders Need to Know About TNFD's Nature Disclosure Roadmap
summary: The Taskforce on Nature-related Financial Disclosures (TNFD) released its beta framework for market testing, inviting companies and financial institutions to pilot nature-related risk assessment and disclosure practices ahead of the finalised framework. For Indian businesses — particularly those in

sections.t: Executive Summary The Taskforce on Nature-related Financial Disclosures (TNFD) released its beta framework for market testing, inviting companies and financial institutions to pilot nature-related risk assessment and disclosure practices ahead of the finalised framework. For Indian businesses — particularly those in agriculture, textiles, mining, pharmaceuticals, and financial services — early engagement with the TNFD beta process is not optional preparation; it is a strategic imperative. As global capital markets increasingly price nature-related risks alongside climate risks, companies that build disclosure capabilities now will hold a decisive advantage when mandatory or investor-driven reporting requirements arrive.

What Changed The TNFD (Taskforce on Nature-related Financial Disclosures) is a global, market-led initiative that has developed a framework to help organisations identify, assess, manage, and disclose their dependencies and impacts on nature — covering biodiversity, freshwater, land, and ocean systems. Modelled structurally on the Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures (TCFD), the TNFD framework was released in iterative beta versions (v0.1 through v0.4) between 2022 and 2023, inviting businesses worldwide to stress-test the methodology in real operating environments before the final framework was published in September 2023. The beta testing phase was significant because it moved nature-related financial risk from a theoretical concern into a practical, structured disclosure discipline. Companies that participated in beta testing worked through the LEAP approach — Locate your interface with nature, Evaluate dependencies and impacts, Assess material risks and opportunities, and Prepare to respond and report. This iterative, open-testing model allowed the TNFD to refine sector-specific guidance, data requirements, and metrics based on real-world feedback from over 500 pilot organisations globally.

Who Is Affected Agribusiness, food & beverage companies with land-use footprints and supply chain dependencies on ecosystems Textile and apparel manufacturers reliant on water-intensive cotton and natural fibres Mining and extractives companies with operations in or near biodiversity-sensitive zones Pharmaceutical and biotechnology companies dependent on natural compounds and genetic resources Infrastructure and real estate developers whose projects intersect with natural habitats Banks, NBFCs, and institutional investors financing sectors with significant nature dependencies Export-oriented businesses supplying to EU, UK, and US markets where nature disclosure is becoming a procurement and regulatory requirement Large listed companies likely to face SEBI BRSR (Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting) evolution toward nature metrics

Key Obligations & Deadlines Familiarise leadership and sustainability teams with the final TNFD framework (published September 2023) and the sector-specific supplemental guidance available at tnfd.global Conduct an initial LEAP assessment: map where your operations and supply chains interface with nature-sensitive areas using tools such as the TNFD's recommended IBAT (Integrated Biodiversity Assessment Tool) and WWF's STAR metric Review SEBI's BRSR Core disclosures (mandatory for top 150 listed companies from FY2023-24) — nature-adjacent metrics on water, waste, and biodiversity are already embedded and will likely expand Monitor the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS E4 — Biodiversity & Ecosystems), which will affect Indian companies with EU operations or large EU customer bases Engage with pilot cohorts and industry associations (such as CII, FICCI sustainability councils) running TNFD familiarisation workshops to benchmark against Indian peers Begin building internal data infrastructure for nature-related metrics — species impact, freshwater consumption by watershed, land-use change — as these will underpin future disclosures No single statutory deadline in India as of now, but EU CSRD obligations for large non-EU subsidiaries begin phasing in from 2026; proactive alignment is advised immediately
