Climate Transition Angle Nature and climate are inseparable systems, and the TNFD framework explicitly recognises this interdependence. Physical climate risks — extreme heat, erratic monsoons, flooding, and groundwater depletion — are simultaneously nature risks for Indian businesses. A textile manufacturer in Gujarat facing water scarcity is experiencing both a climate physical risk and a nature dependency risk. The TNFD framework provides the analytical architecture to assess these compounding risks together, rather than in silos. Globally, the TNFD aligns with and complements the International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) framework — particularly IFRS S2 on climate — and is being integrated into CSRD's ESRS standards. Indian companies reporting under ISSB-aligned standards (which SEBI is expected to progressively adopt) will find that nature disclosure is the logical next layer after climate disclosure. The Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework, agreed at COP15 in 2022, set a target of 30% protected land and oceans by 2030 and requires financial flows to be aligned with biodiversity goals — creating regulatory and reputational pressure that will flow through to corporate supply chains and investment mandates. Early TNFD adoption positions Indian businesses ahead of this tightening landscape.

What To Do Now ASSIGN OWNERSHIP: Designate a cross-functional TNFD working group including sustainability, finance, risk, and procurement leads — nature risk is not solely a CSR function; it requires CFO and board-level visibility RUN A MATERIALITY SCREENING: Use the TNFD's LEAP approach to conduct a rapid, high-level screening of your top three to five value chain nodes for nature dependencies and impacts — this scoping exercise can be completed in 6 to 8 weeks with existing data AUDIT YOUR DATA GAPS: Identify which nature-related metrics (water withdrawal by source, land-use change, supplier geographic coordinates near protected areas) you currently do not capture, and initiate data collection protocols in your next reporting cycle ENGAGE YOUR SUPPLY CHAIN: Nature risks are overwhelmingly concentrated in upstream supply chains — begin supplier engagement and questionnaires on land use, water stewardship, and proximity to Key Biodiversity Areas (KBAs) ALIGN WITH BRSR AND PLAN AHEAD: Map TNFD disclosure requirements against your current BRSR Core submissions to identify overlap, efficiency opportunities, and gaps — use this as your roadmap for the next two to three annual reporting cycles

Our Take The TNFD beta framework testing phase was a quiet but consequential moment in the evolution of sustainable finance — it signalled that nature-related financial risk is transitioning from voluntary best practice to the foundational layer of corporate disclosure, sitting alongside climate as a non-negotiable lens for investors, lenders, and regulators. For Indian businesses, the urgency is real but the window to act proactively is still open. India's economy is among the most nature-dependent in the world: agriculture employs over 40% of the workforce, water stress affects major industrial corridors, and biodiversity loss threatens supply chains from pharmaceuticals to food. Companies that treat TNFD preparation as a compliance checkbox are missing the strategic point — those that genuinely integrate nature risk into capital allocation, procurement decisions, and long-term strategy will be better positioned for resilience, investor confidence, and access to green finance. The question is no longer whether nature disclosure will become mandatory for significant Indian enterprises; it is how quickly, and whether your organisation will be ready.

title: Carbon Tracker's CA100+ Climate Accounting Audit: What Indian CFOs and Audit Committees Must Understand About the New Baseline for Climate Financial Reporting
summary: Carbon Tracker's January 2024 Climate Accounting and Audit Assessment (CAAA) for Climate Action 100+ companies — covering oil & gas and electric utilities — has established that the overwhelming majority of large listed emitters continue to produce financial statements that are materially inconsiste

sections.t: Executive Summary Carbon Tracker's January 2024 Climate Accounting and Audit Assessment (CAAA) for Climate Action 100+ companies — covering oil & gas and electric utilities — has established that the overwhelming majority of large listed emitters continue to produce financial statements that are materially inconsistent with their own stated climate commitments, violating the internal coherence test that auditors are now being pressed to apply. For Indian CFOs and audit committees at BSE 500 companies, this is not a distant benchmark: the same logical inconsistency — net zero pledges sitting entirely outside the financial statements — now sits at the centre of SEBI's BRSR Core assurance framework, ISSB IFRS S2 adoption discussions at MCA, and the expectations of every major foreign institutional investor with a CA100+ or Net Zero mandate voting at Indian AGMs.

What Changed Reporting by Carbon Tracker (https://carbontracker.org/reports/climate-accounting-and-audit-2023-assessments-to-climate-action-100/), published 26 January 2024, presents the third annual iteration of its CAAA methodology applied to CA100+ focus companies across oil & gas and electric utilities. The core finding — consistent with their prior 'Still Flying Blind' report — is that companies continue to use climate assumptions in their financial statements (asset useful life, impairment discount rates, capital expenditure schedules, reserve valuations) that are incompatible with a 1.5°C or even 2°C scenario. Auditors are signing off on accounts where a company simultaneously pledges net zero by 2050 in its sustainability report and then uses a business-as-usual fossil fuel demand curve to justify full asset carrying values under IAS 36. Carbon Tracker's methodology tests precisely this internal contradiction — it is not asking companies to adopt SBTi targets; it is asking whether the numbers in the financial statements are consistent with the narrative in the same annual report. This has direct and escalating relevance to Indian-listed companies for three reasons. First, SEBI's BRSR Core framework (mandatory for the top 150 listed companies by market cap from FY2023-24, expanding to top 250 from FY2024-25) now requires 'reasonable assurance' on nine KPIs including Scope 1 and Scope 2 GHG emissions — this is the first time an Indian regulator has mandated external assurance on a climate metric that feeds directly into financial risk assessment. Second, ISSB IFRS S1 and S2 have been endorsed as the global baseline by IOSCO, of which SEBI is a member, and MCA's Company Law Committee is actively considering their adoption into Indian accounting standards — once that happens, the same 'climate-financial consistency' test that Carbon Tracker applies will become an audit obligation under Indian GAAP and Ind AS. Third, Reliance Industries, ONGC, NTPC, Adani Ports, JSW Steel, and Tata Steel are all CA100+ focus companies; their climate accounting practices are being scrutinised by the same institutional investors who hold significant FII positions on Indian exchanges.

Who Is Affected Oil & gas producers and refiners: ONGC, Reliance Industries, Oil India, BPCL, HPCL — all holding long-dated fossil fuel assets whose carrying values depend on demand and price assumptions that must now be stress-tested against IEA NZE scenarios Power utilities and coal-dependent generators: NTPC, Adani Power, Tata Power, NHPC — particularly those with coal plant assets whose useful life assumptions embedded in depreciation schedules may not survive a 1.5°C demand pathway Statutory auditors and Big Four audit partners signing off accounts of BSE 500 energy and heavy industry companies — the CAAA methodology is now being cited by audit regulators globally as the standard for what 'climate-aware audit' looks like CFOs and Group Finance Controllers at companies with net zero pledges or science-based targets — they carry personal accountability under Ind AS 36 (impairment) and Schedule III Companies Act disclosures if climate assumptions in accounts diverge materially from stated commitments ESG heads and Chief Sustainability Officers preparing BRSR Core submissions who are not yet coordinating with their CFO and statutory auditor on the financial statement implications of their climate narratives Foreign institutional investors with CA100+ or Net Zero Asset Managers Initiative (NZAMI) mandates — they are required by their own commitments to vote against auditor reappointment and against accounts where this inconsistency persists Credit rating agencies and lenders under RBI's draft climate risk disclosure framework — the same asset impairment logic applies to collateral valuations and covenant assessments in green and sustainability-linked bonds

Key Obligations & Deadlines BRSR Core — Reasonable Assurance on GHG emissions (Scope 1 & 2): Mandatory for top 150 listed companies from FY2023-24 disclosures (filed by September 2024); expanding to top 250 from FY2024-25 — CFOs must ensure GHG figures are audit-ready and consistent with climate assumptions in financial statements SEBI Circular SEBI/HO/CFD/CFD-SEC-2/P/CIR/2023/122 (July 2023): Mandates BRSR Core KPI assurance and introduces the 'glide path' for broader ESG disclosure — companies must align narrative climate commitments with financial reporting assumptions or face investor challenge at AGM Ind AS 36 (Impairment of Assets): Already in force — requires companies to use assumptions consistent with their best estimate of future economic conditions; climate transition scenarios now constitute a quantifiable input; auditors who ignore this are exposed to NFRA scrutiny IEA Net Zero Emissions by 2050 Scenario: Adopted as the reference scenario by CA100+ benchmark — Indian CA100+ focus companies must be able to demonstrate their capex plans, asset lives, and reserve valuations survive a stress test against this pathway or disclose why they do not MCA / ICAI Ind AS convergence with ISSB IFRS S2: No formal deadline yet, but ICAI's Sustainability Reporting Standards Board (SRSB) is actively mapping ISSB to Indian context — CFOs should plan for adoption readiness within 24-36 months CA100+ Net Zero Company Benchmark Phase 2: Assessment reports published January 2024 — Indian companies named as focus companies should review their individual company profiles (behind login at carbontracker.org) and prepare investor response briefs before the next proxy season (April-July 2024 AGMs)

Climate Transition Angle The Carbon Tracker CAAA framework operationalises what regulators have been signalling for three years: climate transition risk is not a sustainability narrative — it is a balance sheet question. For Indian companies, the transition pathway is particularly acute because India has committed to net zero by 2070 under its NDC, and the BEE PAT scheme is already forcing energy intensity reductions that affect the productive life assumptions of industrial assets. NTPC's coal fleet, for example, carries assets with accounting lives extending to 2045-2060; if India's coal phase-down trajectory accelerates under pressure from the Global Stocktake or bilateral climate finance agreements, those asset lives — and the depreciation and impairment treatment flowing from them — become materially misstated. The same logic applies to ONGC's upstream reserve valuations under Ind AS 106 (Exploration for and Evaluation of Mineral Resources), where proved developed reserves are discounted at rates that embed an oil demand curve inconsistent with even a 2°C scenario. ISSB IFRS S2's requirement to disclose the financial effects of climate-related risks — including transition risks on asset values, revenue assumptions, and capex plans — will, once adopted in India, make this inconsistency an explicit audit finding rather than an activist campaign point. The capital market consequence is already playing out: FIIs with NZAMI commitments are required to escalate to voting action against companies that fail the climate accounting consistency test, and proxy advisors IiAS and SES are beginning to incorporate climate accounting quality into their audit committee assessments.

What To Do Now CFO and statutory auditor (immediate — before FY2024 accounts sign-off): Conduct an internal 'climate accounting consistency review' — map every climate commitment made in the sustainability/BRSR report against the assumptions embedded in IAS 36 impairment models, asset useful life schedules, and capex authorisation papers; document where divergences exist and whether they are material ESG/Sustainability team and CFO (within 60 days): Cross-reference your company's CA100+ company profile on carbontracker.org — if your company is a CA100+ focus company, your climate accounting score is visible to every signatory investor; prepare a formal response brief for the investor relations team before the AGM season Audit Committee (next scheduled meeting): Place 'climate assumptions in financial statements' as a standing agenda item; request that the statutory auditor brief the committee on how they are applying Ind AS 36 in the context of the company's stated net zero or emission reduction targets — document this in the audit committee report Company Secretary and Board (before next AGM): Review proxy advisor guidelines from IiAS and SES on climate-related voting — anticipate shareholder resolutions or vote-against recommendations on auditor reappointment if the climate accounting gap is visible and unaddressed Sustainability Reporting team (within 90 days): Align BRSR Core GHG emission figures with the same emission trajectory implied by your net zero or SBTi commitment — if Scope 1 emissions in your BRSR are growing while your sustainability narrative claims a 2030 peak, that inconsistency is now audit-relevant under BRSR Core reasonable assurance Treasury and Capital Markets team: If your company has issued green bonds or sustainability-linked bonds referencing climate targets, assess whether the financial statement assumptions are consistent with the KPI targets in the bond framework — misalignment creates covenant and reputational risk with international bond investors

Our Take Carbon Tracker's CAAA work is quietly becoming the most consequential ESG analytical framework that most Indian CFOs have never read — and that is a board governance failure. The shift from 'disclose your climate risk' to 'prove your financial statements are internally consistent with your climate commitments' is the single most important transition happening in global sustainability regulation right now, and India is not insulated from it. NFRA, India's audit regulator, has been steadily raising the bar on audit quality and auditor independence since 2018; it is only a matter of time before a climate accounting inconsistency at a major listed company becomes the subject of an NFRA inspection finding or enforcement action — particularly as ISSB standards get embedded into Indian accounting and audit standards through the ICAI-SRSB process. The CA100+ universe is also expanding its India footprint: as more Indian companies enter global sustainability indices and attract NZAMI-mandated capital, the number of Indian companies subject to this level of climate accounting scrutiny will grow from the current handful to several dozen within three to five years. Boards should understand that the 'climate accounting gap' — the distance between the net zero narrative and the financial statement assumptions — is no longer a communications problem or an ESG ratings problem; it is an audit opinion problem, a director liability problem, and, in the context of SEBI's expanding BRSR assurance requirements, a regulatory compliance problem. The companies that get ahead of this — by genuinely aligning capex plans, asset life assumptions, and impairment models with their stated transition pathways — will not only reduce audit exposure but will find it materially easier to access green capital, retain FII ownership, and command a lower cost of debt on sustainability-linked instruments. The ones that do not will face an increasingly uncomfortable AGM season from 2025 onwards.

title: Carbon Tracker's CA100+ Methodology Update: What India's Oil, Gas, Utilities, and Auto Sector Boards Must Now Audit Against
summary: Carbon Tracker has published its updated analytical methodologies underpinning the Climate Action 100+ (CA100+) Net Zero Company Benchmark — the most consequential investor-led climate engagement framework globally, covering 170+ systemically significant emitters and over $65 trillion in assets unde

sections.t: Executive Summary Carbon Tracker has published its updated analytical methodologies underpinning the Climate Action 100+ (CA100+) Net Zero Company Benchmark — the most consequential investor-led climate engagement framework globally, covering 170+ systemically significant emitters and over $65 trillion in assets under management. The methodologies, published on 4 June 2025 and accessible at https://carbontracker.org/reports/carbon-tracker-methodologies/, assess companies across two dimensions: adequacy of climate disclosure and Paris alignment of actual corporate actions. For Indian boards, this is not an abstract global development — CA100+ signatories include LIC, SBI Mutual Fund's global counterparts, and every major foreign institutional investor (FII) holding positions in ONGC, NTPC, Coal India, Tata Power, Reliance Industries, and Adani Enterprises. The methodologies now in effect define the scoring template these investors will use to vote, engage, and ultimately reallocate capital. The update lands at a critical juncture for India: SEBI's BRSR Core framework is now mandatory for the top 150 listed entities, ONGC and NTPC are enrolled in CA100+ engagement, and India's carbon credit trading scheme under the Energy Conservation (Amendment) Act 2022 is moving toward operationalisation. The Carbon Tracker framework effectively sets a higher bar than BRSR Core on alignment indicators — creating a dual compliance burden and a valuation risk for any Indian large-cap that scores poorly on Paris alignment metrics.

What Changed Reporting by Carbon Tracker Initiative (carbontracker.org) confirms that the updated CA100+ methodologies now operationalise two distinct but interlocking assessment lenses. First, Disclosure Framework Indicators evaluate whether companies are disclosing against the vocabulary of ISSB IFRS S2, TCFD, and GHG Protocol — covering Scope 1, 2, and material Scope 3 emissions, climate scenario analysis, and board-level climate governance. Second, and more commercially significant, Alignment Assessments evaluate whether company capex plans, production trajectories, and lobbying positions are actually consistent with a 1.5°C pathway as defined by IEA's Net Zero Emissions by 2050 scenario. The sector-specific methodologies for Oil & Gas and Electric Utilities are the most granular — they score companies on whether upstream capex approvals are consistent with no new fossil fuel development post-2021 (IEA NZE), whether power generation portfolios are decarbonising at IEA-compatible rates, and whether climate accounting in financial statements reflects stranded asset risk. The related finding published by Carbon Tracker in the same reporting cycle — that oil companies systematically under-report carbon intensity through automotive investment structures — expands the methodology's reach into the auto and industrial sectors. This directly implicates Indian companies: Tata Motors, Mahindra & Mahindra, and Maruti Suzuki parent Suzuki Motor Corp all carry legacy ICE investment profiles that Carbon Tracker's new 'Oil Companies in Disguise' lens would flag for under-reporting. The Climate Accounting and Audit methodology pillar is particularly significant for Indian audit committees: it requires that impairment tests, asset useful life assumptions, and capital allocation disclosures in audited financial statements be stress-tested against 1.5°C scenarios — a standard that India's current audit practice under SA 570 (Going Concern) and Ind AS 36 (Impairment) does not yet explicitly require, but which institutional investors are beginning to demand via AGM resolutions.

Who Is Affected Oil & Gas sector: ONGC, Oil India, Reliance Industries, GAIL — directly enrolled or peer-benchmarked in CA100+ Oil & Gas methodology; capex and production plans scored against IEA NZE Electric Utilities: NTPC, Adani Green, Tata Power, Power Grid Corporation — assessed on generation mix decarbonisation trajectory, coal retirement timelines, and renewable capex commitments Auto OEMs and component makers: Tata Motors, Mahindra & Mahindra, Bharat Forge — exposed to Carbon Tracker's new under-reporting methodology on ICE investment carbon intensity Top 150 BRSR Core companies: all entities subject to SEBI's mandatory BRSR Core assurance obligations face a methodology gap between BRSR Core's disclosure requirements and CA100+'s Paris alignment assessments CFOs and Audit Committees at BSE 500 companies: climate accounting and audit methodology creates direct exposure to investor challenge on impairment assumptions, asset life, and stranded asset provisions in Ind AS financials Chief Sustainability Officers at CA100+ target companies: responsible for translating CA100+ benchmark scores into board-level engagement responses and IR disclosures Foreign Institutional Investors and ESG-screened funds holding Indian large-caps: CA100+ scores directly influence voting decisions, engagement escalation, and portfolio exclusion triggers Indian domestic institutional investors — LIC, SBI MF, HDFC AMC — increasingly co-filing climate resolutions at AGMs; CA100+ methodology provides the scoring basis for such resolutions

Key Obligations & Deadlines Immediate — CA100+ enrolled companies (ONGC, NTPC): review current CA100+ benchmark scores against updated Carbon Tracker methodology indicators; identify gaps in Scope 3 disclosure and Paris alignment evidence before next investor engagement cycle (typically Q3 annual engagement window) FY2025-26 Annual Reports: BRSR Core mandatory assurance (top 150 listed entities per SEBI circular SEBI/HO/CFD/CFD-SEC-2/P/CIR/2023/122) must now be read alongside CA100+ disclosure indicators — any material gap between BRSR Core disclosures and CA100+ disclosure framework indicators is an investor relations exposure Audit Committees — FY2026 audit planning: incorporate Carbon Tracker's Climate Accounting and Audit methodology questions into Ind AS 36 impairment review briefings; auditors should be asked whether oil, gas, and coal asset carrying values have been stress-tested against IEA NZE scenario India Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) — once MoEFCC operationalises the CCTS registry under the Energy Conservation (Amendment) Act 2022: CA100+ alignment indicators will require that carbon credit reliance be clearly distinguished from absolute emissions reduction in corporate disclosures Ongoing stewardship obligation: CA100+ signatory investors are required to escalate engagement to voting action if companies do not demonstrate credible alignment progress — Indian companies must treat each AGM cycle as a live scoring event against Carbon Tracker's published methodology SBTi target-setting: CA100+ alignment assessments cross-reference SBTi approval status; Indian companies without approved SBTi targets in Oil & Gas and Utilities sectors will score adversely on Paris alignment — SBTi submission window is open but Oil & Gas FLAG sector guidance applies Board-level: Climate governance indicators under CA100+ require evidence of board-level climate competency and explicit linkage of executive remuneration to climate KPIs — Indian listed companies subject to Regulation 17 of SEBI LODR must assess whether current board skills matrix and remuneration committee mandates satisfy this threshold

Climate Transition Angle The Carbon Tracker CA100+ methodology update crystallises a structural tension at the heart of India's energy transition: the country's largest listed emitters — ONGC, Coal India, NTPC — are simultaneously instruments of energy security policy and targets of global investor decarbonisation pressure. The methodology's Paris alignment indicators are calibrated to the IEA NZE 2050 scenario, which calls for no new fossil fuel development approvals from 2021. India's National Energy Policy, however, explicitly supports continued upstream oil and gas development and coal expansion through the 2030s to meet baseload and energy access imperatives. This creates a structural mis-score for Indian SOEs under CA100+ metrics that is not resolvable through disclosure improvement alone — it requires India to develop a credible Just Transition narrative that explains divergence from IEA NZE in terms of developmental equity, which BRSR's BRSR Core Principle 6 (responsible business on environment) begins to address but does not fully resolve. For private sector Indian companies, the transition angle is more actionable. Tata Power's renewable build-out, Adani Green's solar pipeline, and Reliance's green hydrogen ambitions are exactly the type of alignment evidence that CA100+ Alignment Assessments reward — but only if accompanied by credible coal and fossil fuel exit timelines and verified Scope 3 accounting. The ISSB IFRS S2 standard, which SEBI has signalled it will incorporate into future BRSR evolution, uses the same conceptual architecture as CA100+'s disclosure framework indicators. Indian companies investing now in ISSB S2-quality disclosure infrastructure are simultaneously building CA100+ compliance readiness and future-proofing against the next BRSR Core revision expected before FY2027-28.

What To Do Now Sustainability / ESG team (within 30 days): download and map Carbon Tracker's sector-specific methodology documents for Oil & Gas, Electric Utilities, and Climate Accounting against your current BRSR Core disclosure outputs — identify every CA100+ indicator that BRSR Core does not currently capture, particularly Scope 3 category-level reporting and Paris-aligned capex narrative CFO and Finance team (before FY2026 audit sign-off): brief your statutory auditor and audit committee on Carbon Tracker's Climate Accounting and Audit methodology; request that Ind AS 36 impairment workpapers for oil, gas, coal, and thermal power assets include at minimum a qualitative 1.5°C scenario stress-test note — this pre-empts investor challenge and reduces Going Concern disclosure risk under SA 570 Investor Relations (ahead of Q2 FY26 earnings season): proactively disclose your current CA100+ benchmark score and engagement status in investor presentations; institutional investors using CA100+ data are already forming views — silence is scored as non-disclosure, not neutrality Board Risk Committee (next quarterly meeting): add CA100+ methodology alignment gap as a standing agenda item; commission a board-level climate governance gap analysis against CA100+ governance indicators — assess whether current board skills matrix, audit committee mandate, and executive remuneration KPIs satisfy Carbon Tracker's climate governance thresholds under SEBI LODR Regulation 17 Legal and Compliance (60-day horizon): assess lobbying and trade association positions against CA100+'s policy alignment indicators — Carbon Tracker explicitly scores whether company lobbying is consistent or inconsistent with Paris goals; membership of coal or oil industry associations that lobby against climate policy creates an adverse alignment score that must be disclosed or mitigated SBTi submission prioritisation (for Oil & Gas and Utilities): companies without approved SBTi near-term and long-term targets should initiate SBTi commitment letters now; CA100+ 2025-cycle scoring will penalise absence of SBTi approval as an alignment gap, directly affecting FII voting decisions at FY26 AGMs

Our Take The Carbon Tracker CA100+ methodology publication is a quiet but structurally significant event for Indian capital markets. Here is what boards and investors should expect over the next 12 to 36 months. First, the methodology's Climate Accounting and Audit pillar will migrate from investor expectation to audit standard — within 24 months, we expect ICAI's Auditing and Assurance Standards Board to face pressure from SEBI and MCA to incorporate climate scenario testing into Ind AS 36 guidance notes, driven by the same institutional investor lobby that uses CA100+ scoring. Companies that wait for regulatory mandate will face a compressed audit remediation window. Second, the under-reporting methodology Carbon Tracker has applied to automakers — finding that ICE investment carbon intensity is being systematically obscured through holding structures — is a direct warning to Indian conglomerates with diversified fossil and clean energy portfolios: Adani Group, Reliance, and Tata Group all carry structural complexity that could generate adverse CA100+-style scores if Scope 3 boundary-setting and intra-group capital flow disclosures are not tightened. Third, and most consequentially for Indian sovereign and quasi-sovereign borrowers, the CA100+ benchmark is increasingly referenced in green bond and sustainability-linked bond (SLB) covenant frameworks used by offshore investors. ONGC and NTPC are active in international debt markets — a deteriorating CA100+ score directly affects the coupon step-up risk on SLBs and the eligibility of future green bond issuances under ICMA Green Bond Principles. The Indian government's own Sovereign Green Bond programme, now in its third issuance cycle, will face credibility questions if CA100+ anchor companies in the public sector score poorly on Paris alignment. The regulatory and commercial logic is converging: Indian companies that treat CA100+ as a distant global investor concern rather than a proximate capital cost risk are making a material misjudgement.

title: Carbon Tracker's Enagas Report: Why European Gas Network Stranded Asset Risk Is a Live Governance and Portfolio Question for Indian Investors and Utilities
summary: Carbon Tracker's February 2024 report on Enagas — the third in a trilogy covering European gas network operators Snam, Italgas, and Enagas — delivers a clear capital markets warning: the energy transition is structurally undermining future cash flows from gas transport infrastructure, and institutio

sections.t: Executive Summary Carbon Tracker's February 2024 report on Enagas — the third in a trilogy covering European gas network operators Snam, Italgas, and Enagas — delivers a clear capital markets warning: the energy transition is structurally undermining future cash flows from gas transport infrastructure, and institutional investors holding equity and debt in these companies face material, under-disclosed stranded asset risk. For Indian CFOs, CSOs, and ESG investors, this is not a distant European problem. It is a direct read-across to India's own gas pipeline expansion ambitions, the stranded asset exposure embedded in Indian utility and infrastructure portfolios, and the rapidly tightening disclosure obligations under SEBI's BRSR Core and ISSB IFRS S2 that will require Indian companies to quantify exactly this kind of transition risk. The report's core finding — that Paris alignment, hydrogen realism, asset life assumptions, and regulatory incentives are all misaligned at major European gas networks — is a template that Indian regulators, investors, and boards should apply urgently to domestic gas infrastructure, where capital allocation decisions made today carry 30-40 year asset life implications.

What Changed Reporting by Carbon Tracker (https://carbontracker.org/reports/decarbonising-gas-networks-part-2-enagas/), published 8 February 2024, completes a three-part institutional research series examining how Snam (Italy), Italgas (Italy), and Enagas (Spain) are — or are not — aligning their business models with net zero pathways. Senior Corporate Research Analyst Chris Moore assesses each company across four critical dimensions: Paris alignment of operations and capex, the realism of hydrogen as a decarbonisation pathway for gas networks, whether asset economic lives reflect lower future gas demand, and whether national regulatory frameworks are incentivising faster decarbonisation or entrenching fossil fuel infrastructure lock-in. The reports represent a significant escalation in how financial think tanks are engaging with infrastructure transition risk — moving beyond carbon footprinting to challenge the regulatory compact itself, arguing that gas network regulators in Italy and Spain are not sufficiently focused on decarbonisation. This is the ISSB IFRS S2 'transition risk' disclosure thesis made operational: Carbon Tracker is doing the asset-level analysis that companies themselves are expected to disclose under IFRS S2 para 10(b), which requires disclosure of transition risks that could reasonably be expected to affect the entity's cash flows, access to finance, or cost of capital over the short, medium, and long term. The fact that an independent think tank is producing this analysis — rather than the companies themselves — is itself the disclosure gap. For India, the direct regulatory connection is SEBI's BRSR Core framework, mandated for the top 150 listed entities from FY2023-24 and expanding to the top 250 from FY2024-25. BRSR Core's Principle 6 Essential Indicators require Scope 1, Scope 2, and — critically — Scope 3 emissions disclosure, along with transition risk assessment. Indian gas pipeline operators (GAIL, Gujarat Gas, Mahanagar Gas, Indraprastha Gas, Petronet LNG) and power utilities with gas-fired generation assets (NTPC, Torrent Power, Adani Power) are carrying structurally similar long-duration asset risk to Snam and Enagas — and are currently disclosing far less about it.

Who Is Affected Indian gas distribution and transmission companies: GAIL (India) Ltd, Gujarat Gas Ltd, Mahanagar Gas Ltd, Indraprastha Gas Ltd, Petronet LNG — all carrying long-duration gas infrastructure whose economic life assumptions must now be stress-tested against 1.5°C and 2°C scenarios Power utilities with gas-fired generation exposure: NTPC Ltd, Torrent Power, Adani Power, and state DISCOMs with gas PPAs — facing equivalent stranded asset risk as electrification accelerates and renewable generation undercuts gas economics Indian institutional investors and asset managers — including LIC, SBI Mutual Fund, HDFC AMC, Nippon India AMC — with significant holdings in infrastructure and utility stocks/bonds that carry undisclosed transition risk on gas assets Foreign institutional investors (FIIs) and ESG-labelled funds investing in Indian infrastructure: already applying SFDR Article 8/9 standards, they will require Paris-aligned asset life disclosures that Indian investee companies cannot currently produce CFOs and treasury teams at BSE 500 companies with gas-linked capex programmes: capital allocation decisions made without scenario analysis are now an audit committee and board governance risk under ISSB IFRS S2 CSOs and sustainability heads at Indian energy companies preparing BRSR Core submissions: the Carbon Tracker framework — Paris alignment, hydrogen realism, asset lives, regulatory adequacy — is precisely the transition risk disclosure structure they must populate PNGRB (Petroleum and Natural Gas Regulatory Board) and MoPNG policy teams: the Carbon Tracker critique of Spain's and Italy's regulators for failing to incentivise decarbonisation is a direct governance warning for India's own gas network regulatory compact

Key Obligations & Deadlines SEBI BRSR Core — Scope 1, 2, and 3 GHG emissions disclosure: mandatory for top 150 listed entities from FY2023-24 (i.e., already in force); top 250 from FY2024-25 — gas and power companies must quantify emissions from all operated assets including pipelines and generation fleet SEBI BRSR Core — transition risk disclosure: companies must disclose risks and opportunities arising from energy transition; gas infrastructure operators must now assess asset stranding probability under IEA NZE 2050 and IPCC 1.5°C scenarios — no explicit deadline but FY2024-25 BRSR filings will be the first major test ISSB IFRS S2 (Climate-related Disclosures) — while not yet mandated by SEBI, the Ministry of Corporate Affairs' (MCA) National Roadmap on Sustainability Reporting (2023) signals convergence; companies proactively applying IFRS S2 must disclose transition risks including asset impairment risk under scenario analysis per para 10(b) and 13 GHG Protocol — Scope 3 Category 11 (use of sold products): gas distribution companies selling fossil gas to end consumers must account for downstream combustion emissions; this is the most material and most under-reported category for Indian gas utilities SBTi Net Zero Standard — companies with SBTi commitments (TATA group entities, Mahindra, Infosys among early Indian adopters) must ensure capital allocation to gas assets does not conflict with validated near-term and long-term targets; SBTi's Oil & Gas sector guidance explicitly restricts new fossil fuel infrastructure capex EU CSRD/ESRS E1 — Indian exporters supplying into European markets or Indian subsidiaries of European groups: ESRS E1 requires disclosure of GHG emissions across the value chain and Paris-aligned transition plans; gas-dependent supply chains face scrutiny from European parent entity sustainability reporting obligations effective from FY2024 for large EU companies

Climate Transition Angle The Carbon Tracker Enagas report crystallises the central transition risk calculus that Indian gas infrastructure boards have not yet confronted at the required level of granularity. India's government has committed to 500 GW of non-fossil fuel capacity by 2030 and net zero by 2070 under its NDC and LT-LEDS. The IEA's India Energy Outlook 2021 projects natural gas demand peaking before 2040 under its Sustainable Development Scenario. Yet India continues to expand its City Gas Distribution (CGD) network — PNGRB has authorised CGD operations across 307 Geographical Areas covering approximately 98% of India's population — with capex decisions being made on asset lives of 25-40 years that extend well beyond any credible Paris-aligned gas demand curve. The three specific decarbonisation failures Carbon Tracker identifies at Enagas — insufficient gas leak reduction (fugitive methane emissions represent a Scope 1 disclosure obligation under BRSR Core and are subject to GHG Protocol's Scope 1 accounting rules), unrealistic hydrogen transition assumptions (India's National Hydrogen Mission's blending timeline of 2025 remains technically and commercially unproven at scale), and asset lives that fail to reflect declining gas demand — are not European peculiarities. They are structural features of how gas networks everywhere are avoiding the financial write-down that Paris alignment mathematically implies. For Indian gas utilities, this creates two specific capital market risks: first, impairment charges that have not been provisioned will eventually surface in audit processes, particularly as ICAI begins to align Indian accounting standards with IASB's evolving guidance on climate-related uncertainties in financial statements; second, international ESG-labelled bond issuances by Indian gas utilities (green bonds, sustainability-linked bonds) are increasingly vulnerable to greenwashing challenges if asset life assumptions embedded in bond prospectuses are not Paris-stress-tested. The RBI's 2023 Discussion Paper on Climate Risk and Sustainable Finance specifically flags transition risk in fossil fuel infrastructure as a systemic banking sector concern, meaning lenders to Indian gas networks face parallel regulatory pressure to require scenario-tested disclosures.

What To Do Now CFO and Treasury (immediate, within 60 days): commission an independent asset impairment stress test on all gas infrastructure assets using IEA NZE 2050, IEA SDS, and IPCC 1.5°C scenarios; document assumptions on asset economic lives and compare against Paris-aligned gas demand projections — this is both an IFRS accounting risk and an ISSB IFRS S2 scenario analysis obligation CSO and Sustainability team (FY2024-25 BRSR Core filing): structure transition risk disclosure using the Carbon Tracker four-pillar framework as a quality benchmark — Paris alignment of operations and capex, hydrogen realism, asset life appropriateness, and regulatory adequacy; this goes substantially beyond minimum BRSR Core requirements and will materially differentiate disclosure quality for ESG-rated investors Board Risk Committee (next scheduled meeting): add 'gas infrastructure stranded asset risk' as a standing agenda item; request management to present scenario analysis outputs and confirm that capex approval processes incorporate 1.5°C transition risk gating — this is a direct board governance obligation under SEBI's Corporate Governance framework (LODR Regulation 17) and ISSB IFRS S2's governance disclosure requirements Investor Relations team (before next earnings call): proactively brief institutional investors — particularly FIIs applying SFDR and domestic ESG funds — on the company's position on gas asset transition risk; failure to do so creates the same credibility gap that Carbon Tracker is now publicly identifying at Enagas and Snam Legal and Compliance (within 90 days): audit all outstanding green bond or SLB documentation for gas-linked assets to identify Paris-alignment representations that may not be supportable under ISSB IFRS S2 scenario analysis; coordinate with auditors on ICAI's emerging guidance on climate uncertainties in financial statement disclosures Government Affairs and Regulatory Engagement: monitor PNGRB's regulatory asset base (RAB) framework and engage proactively on accelerated depreciation or asset life revision mechanisms — Carbon Tracker's finding that European regulators are insufficiently focused on decarbonisation is a warning that India needs to get ahead of this before stranded asset write-downs become a regulatory and capital markets crisis

Our Take Carbon Tracker's Enagas report is, on the surface, a European utilities story. Its real significance for Indian boards and investors is as a preview of the institutional investor engagement that India's gas sector has not yet experienced but will. The pattern is consistent and accelerating: independent financial analysis identifies the gap between a company's disclosed assumptions (asset lives, hydrogen upside, regulatory support) and what Paris alignment actually requires; institutional investors use that analysis to engage or divest; regulatory scrutiny follows; and eventually, accounting and audit standards catch up to force the write-downs that markets had already priced in. European gas utilities are at stage three of that sequence. Indian gas infrastructure is at stage one — but the distance between stage one and stage three is compressing rapidly, for three reasons. First, SEBI's BRSR Core framework is generating standardised, auditable Scope 1 and transition risk data for the first time, creating the evidence base for exactly the kind of Carbon Tracker-style analysis to be applied to GAIL, Gujarat Gas, or IGL. Second, India's green bond market — now operating under SEBI's Green Bond Framework (2023) and subject to ICMA Green Bond Principles — is creating a financial accountability mechanism: if gas network bonds are marketed as green or sustainability-linked, the Paris-alignment gap becomes a legal and reputational liability, not just an ESG rating issue. Third, global capital allocators are rapidly losing patience with 'future hydrogen' narratives as a justification for continued gas infrastructure investment; the IEA's World Energy Outlook 2023 confirmed that hydrogen blending in gas networks will have negligible impact on emissions trajectories at any credible scale before 2035. Indian boards that continue to rely on hydrogen optionality to justify 30-year gas network capex should expect Carbon Tracker, IEEFA, or CREA to produce the Indian equivalent of this Enagas report within 18-24 months. The question for Indian CFOs is whether they will be disclosing ahead of that analysis — or responding to it under pressure.

title: Carbon Tracker's Italgas Decarbonisation Report: What European Gas Network Stranded Asset Risk Means for Indian Gas Utilities, BRSR Disclosures, and Infrastructure Investors
summary: Carbon Tracker's February 2024 report on Italgas — the third in a trilogy covering European gas network operators Snam, Italgas, and Enagas — delivers a stark warning to institutional investors: the energy transition is materially undermining the future cash flow certainty of gas transport and distr

sections.t: Executive Summary Carbon Tracker's February 2024 report on Italgas — the third in a trilogy covering European gas network operators Snam, Italgas, and Enagas — delivers a stark warning to institutional investors: the energy transition is materially undermining the future cash flow certainty of gas transport and distribution assets, and decarbonisation credibility is now a direct determinant of equity and debt returns. For Indian CFOs and CSOs, this is not a European story. It is a direct preview of the stranded asset, regulatory, and capital market dynamics now accelerating toward India's own listed gas distribution companies — GAIL, Gujarat Gas, Indraprastha Gas, Mahanagar Gas, and Gujarat State Petronet — all of which face analogous structural questions about asset life, hydrogen viability, and Paris alignment that their European peers are being forced to answer today. The India-specific urgency is sharpened by SEBI's BRSR Core framework, which mandates climate-related financial disclosures aligned with ISSB IFRS S2 principles for the top 150 listed entities from FY2024-25, with expansion to the top 1,000 imminent. Gas network operators and city gas distribution (CGD) licensees with long-dated pipeline infrastructure now face a convergence of regulatory disclosure pressure, investor engagement escalation, and asset impairment risk that Carbon Tracker's Italgas analysis maps in precise, replicable detail.

What Changed Reporting by Carbon Tracker (https://carbontracker.org/reports/decarbonising-gas-networks-part-3-italgas/), authored by Senior Corporate Research Analyst Chris Moore and published 8 February 2024, provides institutional investors with a structured analytical framework to assess whether Italgas — and by extension all European gas network operators — is Paris-aligned, whether hydrogen decarbonisation pathways are financially realistic, whether asset economic lives have been appropriately shortened in response to lower long-term gas demand, and whether national regulators are creating sufficient incentive structures for accelerated decarbonisation. The report explicitly calls on investors to engage with investee companies, regulators, and policymakers to push three concrete actions: further reduction of methane and gas leaks, shortening of gas network asset economic lives on balance sheets, and improvement of national regulatory frameworks to incentivise faster decarbonisation. What this report overturns — or more precisely, challenges — is the long-standing investment thesis that gas network infrastructure is a 'regulated asset base' business with predictable, bond-like cash flows protected by national regulators who treat pipelines as strategic assets. Carbon Tracker's analysis shows that this thesis is eroding rapidly: declining gas throughput, accelerating electrification, uncertain hydrogen economics, and the physics of methane leakage combine to create a fundamental question about whether current asset lives embedded in regulated returns are financially honest. For Indian listed gas companies and their investors, this framework is immediately applicable. India's CGD network — now covering over 90% of India's geographical area across 307 GAs (Geographical Areas) licensed by PNGRB — was built on a similar regulated-return model. As India's own net zero 2070 pathway and its Nationally Determined Contribution targets translate into declining long-term gas demand scenarios, the same asset life, stranded cost, and hydrogen viability questions will arrive at PNGRB, MoPNG, and the boards of India's listed gas distributors. The question is not if — it is when, and whether Indian boards are getting ahead of it.

Who Is Affected Listed Indian city gas distribution companies: Indraprastha Gas Ltd (IGL), Mahanagar Gas Ltd (MGL), Gujarat Gas Ltd, GAIL (India) Ltd, Gujarat State Petronet Ltd (GSPL) — all carrying long-dated pipeline and distribution infrastructure on their balance sheets Infrastructure-focused institutional investors and debt capital market participants with exposure to Indian gas sector bonds, NCDs, or green/sustainability-linked bonds issued by gas utilities BRSR Core-reporting entities (top 150 listed companies by market cap from FY2024-25) in the energy sector, where ISSB IFRS S2-aligned climate scenario analysis and transition risk disclosure is now a compliance requirement ESG analysts and fund managers at domestic mutual funds (SBI MF, HDFC MF, Nippon India MF) and FPIs with significant India infrastructure or energy holdings who use Carbon Tracker-style frameworks for engagement CFOs and treasury teams at gas sector companies preparing sustainability-linked financing, where lenders are increasingly requiring Paris-alignment evidence and asset-life review as covenant conditions Chief Sustainability Officers and BRSR reporting teams at gas utilities, who must now address Scope 1 methane emissions, transition risk scenarios, and asset impairment disclosures with far greater specificity than in prior voluntary reports PNGRB (Petroleum and Natural Gas Regulatory Board) and MoPNG officials, whose tariff-setting and asset-life assumptions are directly analogous to the European regulatory frameworks Carbon Tracker critiques Board risk committees at gas sector companies, who are now exposed to investor engagement on Paris alignment and stranded asset risk in terms directly mirroring the Italgas, Snam, and Enagas engagement campaigns Carbon Tracker is driving

Key Obligations & Deadlines BRSR Core — FY2024-25 mandatory: Top 150 NSE/BSE-listed entities (by market capitalisation) must provide BRSR Core disclosures including climate-related financial risk indicators aligned with ISSB IFRS S2; gas utilities in this cohort must disclose transition risks, Scope 1 and Scope 2 GHG emissions, and climate scenario analysis — Carbon Tracker's three-point framework (Paris alignment, asset lives, hydrogen viability) maps directly onto these required disclosure categories ISSB IFRS S2 (effective globally January 2024): Requires disclosure of climate-related risks and opportunities, including physical and transition risks, scenario analysis using 1.5°C and 2°C pathways, and industry-specific metrics — gas network operators must assess whether their asset base is consistent with each scenario; Indian companies voluntarily adopting ISSB or facing ISSB-aligned BRSR requirements must begin this work now GHG Protocol / Scope 1 methane emissions: Carbon Tracker's explicit call for 'further reduction of gas leaks' maps to Scope 1 fugitive emissions reporting — BRSR Core requires Scope 1 and Scope 2 GHG data with third-party assurance for top 150 listed entities from FY2024-25; Indian gas companies must now ensure methane leak quantification methodology meets GHG Protocol standards, not just CPCB general air quality reporting SBTi Net-Zero Standard: As European gas peers face SBTi validation pressure, Indian gas utilities seeking to issue green or sustainability-linked bonds will face lender and rating agency scrutiny on whether their capex plans and asset retirement schedules are SBTi-consistent; no hard deadline, but capital market access is the effective enforcement mechanism PNGRB Tariff Review Cycles: Asset economic lives are embedded in PNGRB's tariff determination methodology; Carbon Tracker's call for 'shortening of gas network economic asset lives' is a direct signal that Indian regulators will face similar pressure from investors and civil society to revisit 25-30 year asset life assumptions in CGD tariff models — boards should anticipate this as a 3-5 year regulatory risk horizon EU CBAM (Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism) — Phase-in 2024-2026, full enforcement 2026: While primarily affecting steel, cement, and fertiliser exports, CBAM signals the direction of EU trade policy; Indian gas-intensive manufacturing exporters supplying EU markets must understand that Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions intensity is now a trade cost, not just a disclosure item

Climate Transition Angle The Carbon Tracker Italgas report crystallises a transition risk dynamic that is structurally identical to the challenge facing India's gas infrastructure sector, even if the regulatory and market timeline differs by 5-10 years. The core mechanism is this: gas networks are capital-intensive, long-lived assets whose economic viability depends on throughput volume and regulated returns over 20-30 year time horizons. Under any credible 1.5°C or even 2°C Paris-aligned scenario — including India's own NDC commitment to achieve net zero by 2070 and reduce emissions intensity of GDP by 45% from 2005 levels by 2030 — long-term gas demand in distribution networks will peak and decline. When that happens, the regulated asset base supporting current tariffs and investor returns becomes partially stranded. The timing and magnitude of this stranding depends on three variables Carbon Tracker explicitly tests: how fast electrification displaces gas in end-use sectors (cooking, heating, industrial process heat), how viable hydrogen repurposing of gas networks proves to be at commercial scale, and how quickly national regulators adjust asset life assumptions in tariff frameworks. In the Indian context, the hydrogen question is particularly acute. India's National Green Hydrogen Mission (NGHM), launched in January 2023 with a target of 5 MMT of green hydrogen production by 2030, explicitly contemplates blending hydrogen into existing gas networks and repurposing CGD infrastructure. This is precisely the decarbonisation pathway Carbon Tracker interrogates for Italgas — and finds wanting at current cost and readiness levels. Indian gas companies and their investors should treat this finding as a direct caution: the 'hydrogen will save the gas network' narrative, which is already appearing in Indian company sustainability reports and investor presentations, needs to be tested against the same rigorous Paris-alignment and financial viability scrutiny that Carbon Tracker applies to European peers. BRSR Core's climate scenario analysis requirement creates the disclosure architecture to force this testing into the public domain. Investors who read Carbon Tracker's Italgas report and then look at the BRSR disclosures of IGL, MGL, or Gujarat Gas in FY2025 will be asking exactly these questions — and the quality of the answers will increasingly determine cost of capital. The methane leakage dimension adds a further layer of urgency specific to India's context. India's gas distribution network, particularly older city distribution infrastructure in Tier 1 cities, has significant methane fugitive emission exposure. Methane's global warming potential over 20 years (GWP-20) is 80 times that of CO2, and the IPCC's AR6 report has reinforced that rapid methane reduction is one of the highest-leverage near-term climate interventions available. BRSR Core's Scope 1 emissions disclosure requirement, combined with growing investor expectations for GHG Protocol-compliant methane quantification, means that Indian gas utilities can no longer treat fugitive emissions as an operational footnote. Carbon Tracker's call for 'further reduction of gas leaks' as a primary investor engagement priority for European gas networks will translate directly into Indian investor engagement agendas within 24-36 months.

What To Do Now CFO / Treasury (immediate — Q1 FY2025-26): Commission an independent asset life review of all gas pipeline and distribution infrastructure on your balance sheet, stress-tested against 1.5°C and 2°C IEA NZE scenarios; understand the impairment exposure before your auditors or investors surface it in annual accounts or engagement letters Chief Sustainability Officer / BRSR Reporting Team (by FY2024-25 annual report filing): Ensure BRSR Core climate disclosures explicitly address transition risk scenarios for gas demand decline, including quantified Scope 1 fugitive/methane emissions using GHG Protocol methodology with third-party assurance — do not rely on CPCB air quality data as a proxy; align disclosure language with ISSB IFRS S2 industry-specific guidance for oil and gas Board Risk Committee (next board cycle): Place 'gas network stranded asset and Paris alignment risk' formally on the board risk register, referencing Carbon Tracker's analytical framework as the investor engagement template now being applied to European peers; brief the board that BRSR Core disclosures will expose these risks to public investor scrutiny from FY2025 onward Strategy / Regulatory Affairs (6-12 months): Engage proactively with PNGRB on the trajectory of asset life assumptions in tariff determinations and the regulatory treatment of stranded costs under accelerated energy transition scenarios; Carbon Tracker's critique of European regulators for being 'insufficiently focused on decarbonisation' is a direct signal that PNGRB will face similar pressure — better to shape that conversation than react to it Investor Relations (before next earnings cycle): Prepare an explicit Paris-alignment narrative for analyst and investor briefings that addresses the hydrogen viability question with financial rigour, not aspirational language; FPIs and ESG-focused domestic funds are reading Carbon Tracker reports and will ask whether your hydrogen transition strategy meets the same standard applied to Italgas and Snam Procurement / Operations (12-18 months): Initiate a gas leak detection and repair (LDAR) programme benchmarked to international best practice (EPA Method 21 or OGI camera standards) and capable of generating GHG Protocol-compliant methane emissions data; this is both a Scope 1 emissions reduction opportunity and a BRSR Core assurance requirement

Our Take Carbon Tracker's Italgas report is not primarily about an Italian gas utility — it is a methodological template for how institutional investors will engage with gas infrastructure companies globally as the energy transition accelerates. The three-part series on Snam, Italgas, and Enagas should be read by every board member and CFO of a listed Indian gas company as a preview of the investor engagement letters, analyst downgrades, and regulatory challenge they will face within the next 3-7 years. The European gas network sector is approximately one regulatory cycle ahead of India: CSRD-mandated double materiality assessments, ISSB-aligned climate scenario analysis, and SBTi validation pressure are already forcing European utilities to confront the questions Carbon Tracker raises. India's BRSR Core framework, while less prescriptive than CSRD, is converging rapidly on the same disclosure architecture — and SEBI has made clear that the top 1,000 listed companies will be within its scope within the next reporting cycle. The deeper signal here is about the future of regulated infrastructure as an asset class. For decades, gas networks have been prized by institutional investors — pension funds, infrastructure funds, insurance companies — for their bond-like, inflation-linked returns underpinned by regulatory compacts. Carbon Tracker's analysis challenges whether that regulatory compact can survive the energy transition intact, or whether the stranded cost risk is being systematically underpriced in both equity valuations and bond spreads. In India, this question will arrive at PNGRB and the MoPNG with increasing force as India's NDC-aligned policies, the BEE's energy efficiency standards, and the emerging national carbon market (under the Carbon Credit Trading Scheme notified in 2023) create explicit financial penalties for high-carbon operating models. Indian gas utilities that get ahead of this — by shortening asset lives in their financial models, quantifying methane exposure with rigour, and building credible Paris-aligned transition plans — will have a structural advantage in capital market access, sustainability-linked financing terms, and regulatory goodwill. Those that do not will find themselves facing the same investor engagement pressure, credit rating scrutiny, and potential impairment charges that Carbon Tracker is now systematically building the case for across European gas networks. The window to act on Indian terms, rather than react to external pressure, is open — but it is not open indefinitely.

title: Carbon Tracker's 'Recalibrating Climate Risk': Why India's Boards and Institutional Investors Are Sitting on Mispriced Physical Climate Exposure
summary: A major new report from Carbon Tracker and the University of Exeter's Green Futures Solutions, published 5 February 2026 and drawing on structured expert judgement from climate scientists across 12 countries, establishes with rigour what many Indian risk officers have quietly suspected: the economic

sections.t: Executive Summary A major new report from Carbon Tracker and the University of Exeter's Green Futures Solutions, published 5 February 2026 and drawing on structured expert judgement from climate scientists across 12 countries, establishes with rigour what many Indian risk officers have quietly suspected: the economic damage models underpinning climate scenario analysis used by governments, central banks, and institutional investors are systematically understating physical climate risk as warming approaches 2°C — and the gap is widening, not closing. For Indian listed companies, this is not an abstract modelling debate. India is one of the world's highest-exposure geographies to physical climate impacts — heatwaves, monsoon disruption, coastal flooding, and agricultural stress — and the SEBI BRSR Core framework, ISSB IFRS S2, and the Reserve Bank of India's emerging climate risk supervisory guidance all require scenario-based physical risk disclosure. If the scenarios being used are structurally flawed, so is the disclosure, the capital allocation, and the board's risk assurance. The central finding is that economic damage functions used in integrated assessment models (IAMs) — including those embedded in the NGFS climate scenarios widely used by Indian banks and SEBI-regulated entities for BRSR and IFRS S2 reporting — undercount losses from mortality, ecosystem degradation, inequality, and regional extremes, while GDP-reconstruction effects can mask welfare destruction after disasters. For Indian CFOs and audit committees, this means the climate scenario analysis they are filing or preparing is not just incomplete — it may be creating a false sense of quantified certainty that will expose them to regulatory and investor challenge as physical impacts materialise.

What Changed Reporting by Carbon Tracker (https://carbontracker.org/reports/recalibrating-climate-risk/), the report 'Recalibrating Climate Risk' published 5 February 2026, represents a significant escalation in the credibility of the critique against standard climate economic modelling. This is not a fringe academic argument — it builds on Carbon Tracker's 'Loading the DICE Against Pensions' (2023) and the Institute and Faculty of Actuaries/University of Exeter 'Emperor's New Climate Scenarios' (2023), and now has structured expert input from climate scientists across 12 countries. The University of Exeter's Green Futures Solutions — whose researchers have directly shaped UK, EU, and UN climate risk thinking — leads the analysis. The report's four structural findings each have direct Indian disclosure and investment implications. First, physical climate damages are structural and compounding: at higher warming levels, cascade effects across sectors and geographies undermine stable economic growth. India's economic geography makes it acutely vulnerable to this dynamic — a severe monsoon failure simultaneously stresses agriculture (22% of employment), hydropower generation, inland logistics, and rural consumption. Single-variable IAM models miss this compounding entirely. Second, economic damage models focus on global mean temperature, but India's actual risk is driven by regional and local extremes — the wet bulb temperature thresholds that determine outdoor labour productivity loss in Rajasthan and Odisha, the specific flood return periods in Mumbai's coastal industrial zones, the drought frequency in Vidarbha's cotton belt. BRSR Core's principal adverse impact disclosures and IFRS S2's physical risk quantification requirements both implicitly demand regional-level granularity that current models do not provide. Third, GDP metrics mask welfare losses: reconstruction spending after cyclones in Odisha or Andhra Pradesh can show as GDP-positive while communities and ecosystems suffer multi-year productivity losses. Fourth, and most critically for risk governance: as warming approaches 2°C, tail risks and tipping points become dominant features of the risk landscape, yet models present smooth, precise-looking point estimates — exactly the kind of output that audit committees sign off on as 'robust' climate scenario analysis. Carbon Tracker's CEO Mark Campanale is direct: 'financial institutions will continue to chronically under-price climate risks — meaning that pension funds and taxpayers will remain dangerously exposed.'

Who Is Affected BSE 500 and NSE-listed companies in the top 1,000 by market capitalisation subject to SEBI BRSR Core mandatory disclosure — particularly those with material physical climate exposure: agriculture-linked FMCG, cement, steel, textiles, chemicals, real estate, and infrastructure Indian scheduled commercial banks and NBFCs preparing for RBI's climate risk and scenario analysis supervisory guidelines, whose NGFS scenario-based stress tests embed the same flawed damage functions this report critiques Indian insurance and reinsurance companies — GIC Re, New India Assurance, private sector general insurers — whose catastrophe modelling and premium pricing face material mis-calibration if physical damage functions understate tail risks Domestic institutional investors: LIC, EPFO, NPS Trust, and mutual funds with long-duration equity and infrastructure exposure in climate-vulnerable sectors and geographies across India CFOs and Chief Risk Officers at Indian infrastructure developers — ports, highways, renewable energy, urban water utilities — where project finance covenants increasingly require ISSB S2-aligned physical risk disclosure ESG analysts and stewardship teams at foreign institutional investors (FIIs) with India equity allocations, who use scenario analysis to underpin engagement letters and proxy voting on climate risk management Company secretaries and audit committee chairs at BRSR Core-filing entities who are responsible for assuring the integrity of climate-related financial disclosures under SEBI's Listing Obligations and Disclosure Requirements (LODR)

Key Obligations & Deadlines BRSR Core — SEBI mandatory disclosure for top 150 listed entities (FY2023-24 onwards) and top 250 (FY2024-25 onwards): Principle 6 and Leadership Indicators require disclosure of physical and transition risk exposure and scenario analysis. The Carbon Tracker findings directly challenge whether NGFS-based scenarios used in these filings meet the 'decision-useful' standard SEBI expects ISSB IFRS S2 (effective for Indian companies voluntarily adopting or required by lenders/investors): Paragraphs 10-12 require disclosure of physical risk scenario analysis using recognised scenarios — but IFRS S2 does not mandate specific scenarios, leaving companies exposed to challenge if chosen scenarios systematically understate damages RBI Draft Guidelines on Climate Risk and Scenario Analysis (expected to be finalised in 2025-26 cycle): Indian banks using NGFS scenarios for supervisory stress tests must assess whether damage functions in those scenarios are fit for India's regional climate exposure profile SEBI ESG Rating Providers (ERPs) Regulations (2023): ERPs rating Indian companies on physical risk are implicitly calibrated against the same damage models — a systemic mis-calibration of the underlying science flows directly into ESG ratings and index inclusion/exclusion decisions Companies Act 2013 / LODR — Board fiduciary duty: Directors have an ongoing duty to ensure material risk disclosures are accurate. If physical climate risk is structurally mispriced in scenario analysis, audit committees need to assess whether current disclosures satisfy the materiality standard under Schedule III amendments requiring climate-related financial information CDP 2025 disclosure cycle (questionnaire responses due mid-2025): Physical risk scoring in CDP's Climate Change questionnaire C2 requires scenario analysis; companies selecting flawed scenarios risk lower scores and investor confidence gaps

Climate Transition Angle The Carbon Tracker report lands at a moment when India's physical climate risk exposure is moving from theoretical to balance-sheet real. The 2024 monsoon saw deficits in key agricultural states, the 2023 Manipur floods disrupted supply chains across the Northeast, and summer 2024 heatwaves in Rajasthan and UP resulted in documented labour productivity losses in construction and agriculture — the sectors that structurally underpin Indian GDP growth. Yet when Indian companies file BRSR Core disclosures citing NGFS 'Orderly 2°C' or 'Hot House World' scenarios, the damage functions embedded in those scenarios — derived from DICE, PAGE, or FUND model variants — will, per this report's expert-consensus findings, systematically undercount exactly the regional extreme-weather losses that are already appearing in Indian P&L statements. The transition finance dimension is equally sharp. India's sovereign green bond programme, the green infrastructure pipeline under the National Infrastructure Pipeline, and the nascent domestic carbon market under the Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) all depend on investor confidence in climate risk pricing. If the physical risk anchor is miscalibrated, the risk-adjusted return calculations underpinning green bond pricing and infrastructure equity valuations are wrong. This matters for India's ability to mobilise the USD 2.5 trillion in climate finance it needs to 2030 under its Nationally Determined Contribution. The Paris Agreement's Article 2.1(c) goal of making financial flows consistent with a low-carbon, climate-resilient pathway requires not just transition finance but accurate physical risk pricing — and this report says the latter is broken. For Indian issuers of sustainability-linked bonds and green loans under SEBI's green bond framework, lenders are beginning to ask harder questions about physical risk scenario quality, and the Carbon Tracker findings give them specific grounds to do so.

What To Do Now CFO / Chief Risk Officer — within 90 days: Commission a gap assessment of the climate scenarios currently used in BRSR Core and IFRS S2 disclosures against the specific model limitations identified in the Carbon Tracker report — particularly whether your scenario source (NGFS, IEA, IPCC AR6 RCPs) uses global mean temperature damage functions or regional extreme-event calibrations relevant to your Indian asset footprint Sustainability / ESG team — within 60 days: Engage your scenario analysis provider or internal modelling team to identify whether physical risk quantification covers the four specific gaps flagged: cascade effects, regional extremes, non-GDP welfare losses (mortality, morbidity, ecosystem), and tail risk/tipping point representation. Document this assessment for audit committee review Audit Committee — at next quarterly meeting: Add a standing agenda item: 'Integrity of Climate Scenario Assumptions.' Request management to confirm which economic damage models underpin physical risk disclosures, and seek external expert assurance on model fitness-for-purpose in line with SEBI LODR Regulation 17 (board risk oversight) and Schedule III climate disclosure requirements Investor Relations / Board — before next AGM: Prepare an enhanced narrative in the Annual Report and BRSR that explicitly acknowledges the limitations of current physical risk models and describes the precautionary and robustness measures the company is taking in its risk management — this positions ahead of investor questions and demonstrates the disclosure sophistication that FIIs and proxy advisors like Institutional Shareholder Services (ISS) and Glass Lewis increasingly reward Treasury / Green Finance team — linked to next debt issuance: If issuing green bonds or sustainability-linked instruments under SEBI's green bond circular, proactively engage lead arrangers and rating agencies on the physical risk scenario methodology question — document your approach to model uncertainty, as international bond investors post this report will treat NGFS-only scenario reliance as a disclosure risk flag Board Risk Committee — 12-month horizon: Commission scenario planning that goes beyond NGFS standard scenarios to incorporate India-specific regional physical risk pathways, using IPCC AR6 Working Group II regional chapters on South Asia as the scientific anchor, and stress-test key assets (manufacturing sites, supply chain nodes, water-intensive operations) against 1.5°C, 2°C, and 3°C regional warming trajectories with cascade and tail risk overlays

Our Take The Carbon Tracker 'Recalibrating Climate Risk' report is, in our reading, a watershed document — not because its critique is new, but because it has now reached the threshold of structured, multi-country expert consensus that regulators, central banks, and institutional investors can no longer dismiss as minority scientific opinion. The trajectory from here is clear: within 24-36 months, NGFS will be compelled to update its scenario damage functions, ISSB will face pressure to specify minimum standards for physical risk model quality in IFRS S2 implementation guidance, and the EU's European Supervisory Authorities will likely require banks and insurers under CSRD and Pillar 3 to disclose the specific damage model assumptions underlying their physical risk quantification. India will not be insulated from this wave. SEBI, whose BRSR Core framework explicitly references ISSB alignment, will need to respond — and we expect SEBI's sustainability disclosure advisory committee to take up the model integrity question in its 2026-27 review cycle. For Indian companies, the risk is a two-stage exposure: first, the scenario analysis they are currently filing will be retrospectively seen as having understated physical risk — creating reputational and potential legal exposure as climate impacts materialise; second, the recalibration of damage models will, when it comes, produce a step-change upward in disclosed physical risk that will re-price insurance premiums, infrastructure valuations, and credit spreads for Indian climate-exposed assets. Boards that have treated climate scenario analysis as a compliance checkbox — exactly the 'tick-box disclosure exercise' Campanale names — will find themselves managing a sudden credibility gap with investors, lenders, and regulators simultaneously. The companies that invest now in model quality, regional granularity, and honest uncertainty communication will be structurally better positioned for that reckoning. This is not a 2050 problem. The physical damages are accruing now, the model gap is documented, and the regulatory response clock is running.

title: Carbon Tracker's Snam Report: Why European Gas Network Stranded Asset Risk Should Alarm Indian Infrastructure Investors, Gas Utilities, and BRSR Disclosers Now
summary: Carbon Tracker's February 2024 report on Snam — Europe's largest gas transmission infrastructure operator — delivers a systemic warning that energy transition is rendering future cash flows from gas transport and distribution structurally uncertain, with asset life assumptions, hydrogen decarbonisat

sections.t: Executive Summary Carbon Tracker's February 2024 report on Snam — Europe's largest gas transmission infrastructure operator — delivers a systemic warning that energy transition is rendering future cash flows from gas transport and distribution structurally uncertain, with asset life assumptions, hydrogen decarbonisation narratives, and regulatory frameworks failing to adequately reflect Paris-aligned scenarios. For Indian stakeholders, this is not a European story: it is a direct preview of the stranded asset and disclosure risk trajectory facing India's gas infrastructure sector — GAIL, Gujarat Gas, Indraprastha Gas, Mahanagar Gas, Petronet LNG — and the institutional investors who hold their equity and bonds. With SEBI's BRSR Core mandating climate-related financial disclosures aligned to ISSB IFRS S2 for India's top 150 listed companies from FY2024-25, the transition risk valuation methodology Carbon Tracker applies to Snam is the same analytical lens that global asset managers and proxy advisors will shortly apply to Indian gas utilities.

What Changed Reporting by Carbon Tracker (https://carbontracker.org/reports/decarbonising-gas-networks-part-1-snam/), published 08 February 2024, forms Part 1 of a three-report series covering Snam, Italgas, and Enagas — the three major European listed gas network companies whose stocks and bonds are embedded in European infrastructure and utility fund portfolios. The analytical framework Carbon Tracker deploys is instructive: it assesses Paris alignment of each operator's decarbonisation pathway, the credibility of hydrogen retrofit narratives as a transition strategy, the appropriateness of existing asset economic lives given declining gas demand projections, and whether national regulators are building sufficient decarbonisation incentives into the tariff and concession frameworks that underpin these companies' regulated asset base (RAB) valuations. The core finding — that gas network decarbonisation is activity-dependent (transmission versus distribution) and geography-dependent, and that hydrogen as a like-for-like gas substitute is increasingly challenged as a mass network decarbonisation pathway — has direct structural parallels in India. India's City Gas Distribution (CGD) sector, regulated under PNGRB, is mid-build across 295 Geographical Areas (GAs) under the 9th and 10th CGD bidding rounds. The network economic life assumptions embedded in PNGRB's tariff authorisations and the capital work-in-progress (CWIP) on the balance sheets of IGL, MGL, Gujarat Gas and their peers reflect a demand trajectory that has not been stress-tested against India's own net zero by 2070 commitment or the IEA's Net Zero Emissions by 2050 scenario.
