Carbon Tracker's methodology — shortening asset economic lives, stress-testing RAB against demand scenarios, and requiring quantified methane leak reduction commitments — is precisely the analytical lens that ISSB IFRS S2 scenario analysis (paragraph 18, disclosure of climate-related risks over short, medium, and long-term horizons) will require Indian gas utilities to apply and disclose under BRSR Core. The report also highlights that investor engagement with gas network operators must extend beyond company-level action to regulatory advocacy — companies must actively push national regulators to reform frameworks to incentivise decarbonisation. In India's context, this means GAIL, Gujarat Gas, and Petronet LNG boards should be preparing to engage PNGRB and MoPNG on whether the CGD regulatory framework — currently designed to incentivise network rollout, not network managed decline or hydrogen transition — is fit for a 2070 net zero pathway. This is a board governance question, not merely a technical one.

Who Is Affected Indian City Gas Distribution companies: Indraprastha Gas (IGL), Mahanagar Gas (MGL), Gujarat Gas, Adani Total Gas — mid-rollout on 9th and 10th CGD round networks, with long asset life assumptions embedded in balance sheets and tariff orders Gas transmission and processing majors: GAIL India, Petronet LNG, GSPL — with pipeline RAB, LNG terminal capex, and long-term offtake commitments exposed to demand scenario risk Institutional investors and debt holders: Infrastructure debt funds, insurance companies (LIC, GIC Re), and pension funds with India CGD equity or NCD exposure; ESG-screened funds benchmarking against MSCI ESG or FTSE4Good indices where stranded asset methodology is being tightened CFOs and treasury teams at listed gas utilities preparing BRSR Core disclosures for FY2024-25 under SEBI's mandatory ISSB-aligned climate scenario analysis requirements Chief Sustainability Officers at BSE 500 energy companies where Scope 1 methane emissions from gas infrastructure will come under increasing scrutiny from CDP, SBTi Oil & Gas pathway, and proxy advisors (ISS, Glass Lewis) Board risk committees at Indian gas sector companies where the intersection of regulated asset base valuation, Paris-aligned scenario analysis, and audit committee sign-off on climate-related financial disclosures creates a new governance exposure Export-facing Indian energy companies and project developers where EU Taxonomy alignment and CBAM-adjacent green finance criteria require demonstration that gas assets are transition-credible or time-limited

Key Obligations & Deadlines SEBI BRSR Core — FY2024-25 (mandatory for top 150 listed companies by market cap): Climate-related financial disclosures including Scope 1, 2, 3 GHG emissions, transition risk assessment, and scenario analysis aligned to ISSB IFRS S2 — gas utilities must now disclose the financial materiality of demand scenario risk on asset valuations ISSB IFRS S2 (effective for reporting periods beginning on or after 01 January 2024): Paragraph 18 requires disclosure of climate-related risks and opportunities over short, medium, and long-term horizons — Indian companies voluntarily adopting or compelled through BRSR Core alignment must stress-test gas network asset lives against 1.5°C and 2°C scenarios GHG Protocol / CDP disclosure: Methane fugitive emissions from gas transmission and distribution must be quantified and disclosed under Scope 1 — CPCB's draft methane monitoring guidelines and India's updated UNFCCC NDC both reference improved methane accounting; CDP Oil & Gas questionnaire 2024 explicitly asks for methane intensity targets SBTi Oil & Gas pathway (applicable to GAIL, ONGC, and gas-sector companies with SBTi commitments or under investor pressure to commit): Requires absolute Scope 1+2 reduction targets and methane intensity targets aligned to IEA NZE scenario — asset life and demand assumptions are implicit in any credible SBTi submission PNGRB CGD network regulatory review: While no formal deadline exists, the Carbon Tracker methodology signals that RAB-based valuations and concession period assumptions in CGD GAs must be reviewed for Paris alignment — boards should proactively engage PNGRB before investor pressure forces reactive disclosure EU Green Bond Standard and SFDR Article 9 fund eligibility: Indian gas infrastructure bonds or green bonds sold into European markets will face increased scrutiny on Paris alignment, asset life assumptions, and hydrogen transition credibility — CFOs planning ECB or Masala Bond issuance in 2024-25 must pre-empt this

Climate Transition Angle Carbon Tracker's Snam analysis crystallises the central tension in gas infrastructure globally: network operators are stranded between the physical reality of continued gas demand in the near term and the financial reality that long-duration gas network assets — pipelines with 40-60 year economic lives, LNG regasification terminals with 25-30 year amortisation schedules, CGD network steel embedded in urban geographies — are being valued on demand assumptions that are inconsistent with Paris-aligned scenarios. In India, this tension is acutely visible: the government's Gas Vision 2030 targets raising natural gas's share of the primary energy mix from approximately 6% to 15%, while simultaneously India's NDC commits to achieving net zero by 2070 and 50% non-fossil electricity capacity by 2030. These two policy vectors are not currently reconciled in any publicly disclosed scenario analysis by any major Indian gas utility. The hydrogen dimension is particularly important for India. Snam has heavily promoted hydrogen blending and hydrogen-ready infrastructure as a decarbonisation narrative — Carbon Tracker's analysis interrogates whether this is Paris-credible or a form of transition risk obfuscation. Indian gas majors and the government's National Green Hydrogen Mission (NGHM), which targets 5 MMTPA of green hydrogen by 2030, face the same credibility test. If green hydrogen at scale for gas network blending remains technically and commercially constrained (as Carbon Tracker argues for Europe), then the transition narrative underpinning Indian CGD network expansion capex requires much harder scrutiny — both from audit committees signing off on impairment assessments and from investors evaluating BRSR Core climate disclosures. For India's capital markets, the stranded asset risk embedded in gas network balance sheets connects directly to the RBI's climate risk framework (RBI Discussion Paper on Climate Risk, July 2022) and the expected incorporation of climate scenario analysis into bank credit risk assessments. Lenders to GAIL, IGL, Gujarat Gas, and Petronet LNG — SBI, HDFC Bank, Axis Bank — will increasingly be required to assess whether loan books to gas infrastructure are consistent with 1.5°C or 2°C transition pathways. The Carbon Tracker methodology is the international template for that assessment.

What To Do Now CFOs at IGL, MGL, Gujarat Gas, GAIL, Petronet LNG: Commission an independent asset life stress-test of your gas network RAB and CWIP against IEA NZE 2050 and IEA STEPS scenarios — this is both an ISSB IFRS S2 compliance requirement under BRSR Core FY2024-25 and a proactive defence against activist investor or proxy advisor challenge; timeline: Q1 FY2025-26 at latest Chief Sustainability Officers at BSE 500 gas and energy utilities: Map your Scope 1 methane fugitive emissions against GHG Protocol Oil and Gas Sector guidance and CDP 2024 Oil & Gas questionnaire requirements — quantify methane intensity ratios and set time-bound reduction targets before your next AGM season; connect this to SBTi Oil & Gas pathway if your company is under investor pressure to commit Board Risk Committees at gas infrastructure companies: Add 'Paris alignment of gas network asset life and demand assumptions' as a standing agenda item under climate-related financial risk — require management to present scenario analysis results and compare against Carbon Tracker's Snam methodology; this is a governance best practice that proxy advisors (ISS, Glass Lewis) will increasingly score Investor Relations teams at listed gas utilities: Proactively engage your top 10 institutional shareholders — particularly any ESG-screened funds or European asset managers with SFDR Article 8/9 mandates — to brief them on your climate scenario analysis work and your regulatory engagement strategy with PNGRB and MoPNG; do not wait for a Carbon Tracker-style report on your company to force reactive disclosure Treasury and finance teams planning green bond or ECB issuance: Engage your international legal counsel and ESG rating advisors now to assess whether your gas infrastructure assets meet EU Green Bond Standard or Climate Bonds Initiative (CBI) certification thresholds — the Carbon Tracker methodology is being used by EU fund managers to challenge gas infrastructure green bond eligibility Legal and compliance teams: Review impairment testing assumptions for gas network assets in your next annual accounts — auditors (Big Four, in particular) are beginning to challenge whether climate scenario analysis is adequately reflected in IND AS 36 impairment assessments; the Carbon Tracker report creates a public analytical benchmark that auditors can cite

Our Take Carbon Tracker's Snam report is not primarily about Snam. It is the opening salvo in what will become a systematic, methodology-driven reassessment of gas infrastructure equity and debt valuations globally — and India's listed gas sector is squarely in the trajectory. The analytical tools Carbon Tracker deploys — Paris alignment scoring, asset life stress-testing, hydrogen transition credibility assessment, regulatory framework adequacy review — are the same tools that ISSB IFRS S2, BRSR Core, and increasingly RBI's climate risk guidelines are building into mandatory disclosure and credit assessment architecture. What is voluntary institutional investor engagement in Europe today becomes mandatory disclosure in India within 12-24 months. The boards of India's gas utilities face a compound governance challenge that is not yet fully visible in their boardrooms: they are mid-cycle on massive capex rollout (CGD rounds 9 and 10, GAIL's pipeline expansion, Petronet's LNG terminal capacity additions) based on demand assumptions and asset life conventions that have not been publicly stress-tested against India's own NDC commitments, let alone a 1.5°C scenario. When Indian institutional investors — LIC, EPFO, NPS trust funds — are required to incorporate climate risk into their asset allocation and stewardship frameworks (which RBI and SEBI are progressively mandating), the gas sector will face a repricing event unless companies have pre-emptively built credible, Paris-aligned transition narratives backed by quantified commitments. Watch for three developments in the next 24-36 months: first, a Carbon Tracker or IEEFA equivalent analysis targeting Indian CGD and gas transmission companies by name — GAIL's pipeline RAB and IGL's network asset life assumptions are analytically vulnerable on the same dimensions as Snam's; second, SEBI tightening BRSR Core to explicitly require asset-level climate scenario analysis for energy infrastructure companies, mirroring ISSB IFRS S2's sector-specific requirements; and third, EU CBAM-adjacent green finance criteria and supply chain due diligence requirements (CSDDD) creating a de facto Paris alignment test for any Indian gas sector company seeking European capital or operating in European supply chains. Companies that treat this as a future compliance problem rather than a present capital strategy problem will find themselves on the wrong side of a valuation gap that, once visible to markets, is very difficult to close.

title: CAT Bonn 2026 Briefing: Energy Crisis Responses Under the Microscope — What Indian Policymakers and Listed Companies Must Watch
summary: The Climate Action Tracker (CAT) team — led by Niklas Höhne, Bill Hare, and Sofia Gonzales-Zuñiga — will present a critical briefing at the Bonn Climate Change Conference on 11 June 2026, examining whether national responses to the global energy crisis are accelerating or derailing the clean energy

sections.t: Executive Summary The Climate Action Tracker (CAT) team — led by Niklas Höhne, Bill Hare, and Sofia Gonzales-Zuñiga — will present a critical briefing at the Bonn Climate Change Conference on 11 June 2026, examining whether national responses to the global energy crisis are accelerating or derailing the clean energy transition. For India, this is not an abstract diplomatic moment: CAT's assessments directly feed into sovereign climate credibility scores used by ESG-oriented global investors, export market gatekeepers under the EU's CBAM and CSRD supply chain rules, and multilateral lenders calibrating concessional green finance allocations. Indian CFOs and CSOs at BSE 500 companies — particularly in energy-intensive sectors — need to understand that a CAT downgrade of India's climate trajectory, or a finding that India's energy security response has locked in fossil fuel capacity, will have measurable consequences for cost of capital and investor engagement cycles within 12 to 24 months.

What Changed Reporting by Climate Action Tracker (climateactiontracker.org) confirms a press conference scheduled for 11 June 2026, 15:00–15:30 CEST, at the Bonn Climate Change Conference (Nairobi 4 press room, with webcast available). The CAT briefing will interrogate a question that sits at the heart of the current NDC cycle: have governments used the post-2022 global energy crisis — triggered by the Russia-Ukraine war, supply chain fractures, and LNG market volatility — as a launchpad for faster renewable deployment, or have they used it as political cover to entrench new fossil fuel infrastructure? Source article: https://climateactiontracker.org/blog/press-conference-bonn-sessions-2026/ For India, the stakes are unusually high at this particular Bonn session. India submitted its updated NDC in 2022, committing to 500 GW of non-fossil electricity capacity by 2030 and a 45% reduction in emissions intensity of GDP versus 2005 levels. However, since 2022, India has also approved significant expansions of domestic coal production under the energy security rationale — including Coal India capacity additions, new mine openings under the commercial coal mining programme, and continued investment in coal-based thermal capacity in states where renewable intermittency remains a grid management challenge. CAT's briefing is expected to examine precisely this tension: whether India's energy crisis response is consistent with a credible 1.5°C-aligned pathway or whether it represents a structural detour. CAT has historically rated India's overall climate action as 'Highly Insufficient' at the global level, though it has acknowledged India's renewable energy progress. Any update to this rating — upward or downward — carries immediate signalling value for ESG investors running ISSB IFRS S2-aligned portfolio risk assessments. The Bonn sessions sit within the broader UNFCCC inter-sessional calendar and serve as the technical preparation ground for COP31, scheduled for November 2026 in Brazil. Countries are expected to submit enhanced NDCs ahead of COP31 under the Paris Agreement's ratchet mechanism. CAT's briefing will therefore function as an early-warning signal to negotiators, investors, and corporates about which countries are on track and which are not — with direct implications for India's positioning heading into the final NDC submission window.

Who Is Affected Energy-intensive Indian industries: steel, cement, aluminium, fertilisers, and petrochemicals — sectors where CAT's assessment of India's fossil fuel lock-in risk directly affects CBAM liability calculations and EU export market access BSE 500 listed companies with BRSR Core disclosure obligations, particularly those reporting Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions under the mandatory intensity and absolute metrics introduced in SEBI's BRSR framework Indian renewable energy developers, IPPs, and power sector utilities navigating the tension between coal capacity approvals and green finance eligibility under multilateral development bank frameworks CFOs and Treasurers at companies with international bond issuances, sustainability-linked loans, or green bond frameworks — where covenant compliance is tied to India's sovereign climate trajectory Chief Sustainability Officers at companies undergoing SBTi validation or CDP disclosure cycles, for whom CAT's country-level assessment forms part of the contextual baseline ESG analysts and portfolio managers at foreign institutional investors (FIIs) and domestic mutual funds running climate-screened or Paris-aligned strategies with Indian equity exposure India's Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) and Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE) officials preparing India's pre-COP31 NDC enhancement submission

Key Obligations & Deadlines 11 June 2026: CAT press briefing at Bonn — watch the webcast and obtain CAT's updated country assessment for India; CSO and IR teams should prepare a response brief within 5 business days Pre-COP31 NDC window (Q3 2026): India is expected to submit an enhanced NDC ahead of COP31 Brazil; companies should engage with MoEFCC's industry consultation process and ensure their sector-level decarbonisation data feeds into India's national submission BRSR Core FY2025-26 disclosure cycle: Top 150 listed entities by market cap must report BRSR Core metrics including Scope 1, Scope 2, and relevant Scope 3 emissions — CAT's updated assessment of India's energy transition credibility will be referenced by ESG analysts reviewing these disclosures ISSB IFRS S2 alignment: Companies with international capital market exposure should ensure their climate-related financial disclosures address scenario analysis consistent with India's NDC trajectory — a CAT downgrade materially affects the 'policy scenario' inputs in transition risk modelling EU CBAM Phase 2 preparation: Indian exporters of steel, cement, aluminium, and fertilisers must track whether India's energy crisis response is classified by CAT as fossil-entrenching — this affects the carbon price liability calculations under CBAM's 2026-onward full implementation CDP 2026 disclosure season (responses due July 2026): Companies reporting on climate should explicitly address how India's national energy policy context — including any CAT-flagged fossil fuel expansions — affects their own Scope 2 procurement strategy and transition risk exposure

Climate Transition Angle The central analytical question CAT is posing — did the energy crisis accelerate or entrench fossil fuels — maps directly onto one of the most consequential strategic decisions Indian boards are currently navigating. India's short-term energy security calculus, which has justified continued coal investment, sits in structural tension with the long-term capital market reality: global investors managing over USD 130 trillion in assets under the Net Zero Asset Managers initiative are progressively tightening their India portfolio screens based on sovereign and sectoral climate trajectory. If CAT's June 2026 briefing concludes that India's post-crisis energy response has materially weakened the country's 2030 NDC credibility, this will propagate through Bloomberg ESG scores, MSCI climate ratings, and Sustainalytics country risk assessments — all of which are inputs into FII investment mandates and cost of capital calculations for Indian corporates issuing in international markets. For Indian companies with SBTi-validated targets or those in the validation pipeline, a CAT finding that India's grid emissions factor is not declining on the expected trajectory will directly affect the credibility of Scope 2 market-based emission reduction claims. Under the GHG Protocol's Scope 2 Guidance and SBTi's power sector criteria, the residual mix emissions factor for India is already among the highest in Asia — any signal that coal capacity additions are outpacing renewable deployment will further complicate Scope 2 reduction pathways. This is not a theoretical risk: SBTi's 2025-26 review of near-term target validation criteria has already tightened requirements for power procurement claims in high-carbon grid geographies, and India sits squarely in that category. Boards should treat CAT's Bonn briefing as an early-read on the evidence base that will inform SBTi, ISSB, and CSRD-aligned transition risk assessments for the next 18 months.

What To Do Now Sustainability / ESG team — by 13 June 2026: Download and analyse CAT's full briefing document once released post-press conference; map any India-specific findings against your company's BRSR Core emission intensity disclosures and identify disclosure gaps or narrative inconsistencies CFO / Investor Relations — within 2 weeks of CAT release: Prepare a one-page climate positioning note for international investors that contextualises your company's own decarbonisation trajectory against India's national energy policy environment; do not wait for investors to raise the question Legal / Compliance — Q3 2026: Cross-reference CAT's assessment of India's fossil fuel policy with your EU CBAM liability modelling; if CAT flags that India's carbon pricing or energy transition policy is below CBAM equivalence thresholds, initiate immediate review of export pricing and CBAM certificate provisioning Board Risk Committee — next scheduled meeting: Add India's NDC credibility and CAT rating trajectory as a standing agenda item under climate-related financial risk; this is a BRSR Core principal adverse impact disclosure obligation and an ISSB IFRS S2 scenario analysis input Government Affairs / Policy Engagement — June–September 2026: Engage with MoEFCC and MNRE's pre-COP31 NDC preparation consultations; provide sector-level data on renewable energy uptake, energy efficiency improvements (BEE PAT Scheme cycle performance), and emission intensity reductions to strengthen India's national submission Treasury / Green Finance team — Q3 2026: Review sustainability-linked bond or loan covenant compliance in light of CAT's updated India assessment; where KPIs are indexed to India's renewable energy share or national emission intensity metrics, stress-test covenant breach scenarios

Our Take CAT's Bonn 2026 briefing is arriving at a pivotal inflection point in the global climate architecture. The 2026 NDC cycle is the first genuine test of whether the Paris Agreement's ratchet mechanism has teeth — and India's response will be watched with unusual intensity, not just by climate advocates but by the capital markets infrastructure that has quietly hardwired climate credibility into sovereign and corporate risk pricing. My read of the trajectory is this: India is caught in a structural credibility trap that will become increasingly costly to navigate. The energy security justification for continued coal investment was defensible in 2022-23; by 2026, with battery storage costs having collapsed further and India's own RE auction pipeline demonstrating sub-Rs 2.50/unit tariffs, the economic case for new coal capacity is weakening even as the political case persists. CAT's briefing will likely surface this gap explicitly. For Indian boards and CFOs, the 12-to-36-month consequence is clear: if India enters COP31 without a materially enhanced NDC — one that credibly addresses the coal capacity question and strengthens the 2030 renewable target — the repricing of India's sovereign climate risk in global ESG indices will accelerate. This will show up first in FII portfolio rebalancing, then in green bond spread widening for Indian issuers, and eventually in tighter covenants from multilateral lenders on new infrastructure finance. The companies best positioned to weather this are those that have already decoupled their own decarbonisation narrative from India's national grid trajectory — through Power Purchase Agreements for renewable energy, on-site generation, and credible Scope 3 engagement programmes that can be verified under ISSB S2 and BRSR Core standards. Boards that treat CAT's June assessment as a diplomatic footnote rather than a capital market signal will find themselves behind the curve when the repricing arrives.

title: CAT's Power Sector Decarbonisation Scorecard: What India's NDC 2.0, RE Tripling Commitment, and Grid Transition Mean for Listed Companies and Investors
summary: The Climate Action Tracker's May 2025 virtual event — 'Plugging into Progress: Evaluating Power Sector Decarbonisation for 1.5°C' — delivered a pointed verdict: the COP28 pledge to triple global renewables capacity by 2030 is structurally at risk because most countries' new NDCs are not yet translat

sections.t: Executive Summary The Climate Action Tracker's May 2025 virtual event — 'Plugging into Progress: Evaluating Power Sector Decarbonisation for 1.5°C' — delivered a pointed verdict: the COP28 pledge to triple global renewables capacity by 2030 is structurally at risk because most countries' new NDCs are not yet translating political commitment into credible, quantified power sector targets. For India, this is not an abstract global concern — India's own NDC 2.0, submitted in 2022, targets 50% cumulative electric power installed capacity from non-fossil sources by 2030, and the pace of grid-scale solar, wind, and storage deployment will determine whether India is ranked among CAT's 'gathering speed' performers or flagged as a laggard. That determination directly affects sovereign climate credibility, green bond pricing, export supply chain eligibility under EU CBAM and CSRD, and the ESG ratings of every Indian listed company with a reported Scope 2 footprint. CFOs and CSOs at BSE 500 companies should read this as an early warning: the credibility of India's national power decarbonisation trajectory is now a material input into company-level ESG assessments, BRSR Core disclosures, and SBTi target validation — and the gap between NDC ambition and actual grid reality is where investor scrutiny is intensifying.

What Changed Reporting by the Climate Action Tracker (climateactiontracker.org — https://climateactiontracker.org/blog/virtual-event-pulling-into-progress-power-decarbonisation/) on their 21 May 2025 webinar reveals a new, structured evaluation framework for country-level power sector decarbonisation, specifically designed to assess whether national NDCs — which governments are currently finalising and submitting in 2025 ahead of COP30 in Belém, Brazil — are consistent with the 1.5°C pathway and the COP28 Global Renewables and Energy Efficiency Pledge. That pledge, signed by 130+ countries including India, commits to tripling global renewable energy capacity to at least 11,000 GW by 2030 and doubling the rate of energy efficiency improvement. What is new and consequential is the sectoral granularity of CAT's evaluation: rather than assessing only national economy-wide emissions, the framework now disaggregates by sector — beginning with power — and evaluates whether the specific policies, grid investment plans, and capacity auction pipelines within that sector are sufficient to meet the NDC and 1.5°C alignment thresholds. This shift from economy-wide to sector-specific NDC accountability is exactly the direction that ISSB IFRS S2, the GHG Protocol's Scope 2 guidance, and SBTi's sector-specific pathways (including its Power Sector Science-Based Target methodology) are also moving. For India specifically, the CAT framework will evaluate whether India's 500 GW non-fossil capacity target by 2030 — the headline figure in India's updated NDC — is backed by a credible policy and investment stack. As of mid-2025, India has approximately 220 GW of installed renewable capacity, meaning it must add roughly 280 GW in five years, a pace that requires not just auction announcements but grid infrastructure, storage mandates, land clearance reform, and transmission investment that current policy execution has not consistently delivered at scale. The second-order implication for Indian companies is this: CAT's country-level power sector ratings feed directly into frameworks that institutional investors use to assess sovereign climate risk — including Climate Policy Initiative analyses, MSCI sovereign ESG scores, and the inputs used by foreign portfolio investors to set country-level ESG overweights or underweights. A CAT finding that India's power sector NDC is 'insufficient' or 'critically insufficient' relative to 1.5°C does not stay in a policy document — it migrates into FPI risk models, green bond spread calculations, and the Scope 2 emissions factors that companies must use under the GHG Protocol's market-based and location-based accounting methods.

Who Is Affected Power and utilities sector: NTPC, Adani Green, Tata Power, CESC, Torrent Power, NHPC — companies whose entire business model is the subject of this decarbonisation evaluation and whose capital expenditure plans are now scrutinised against 1.5°C sector pathways Energy-intensive industries with large Scope 2 footprints: steel (JSW, Tata Steel), aluminium (Hindalco, Vedanta), cement (UltraTech, Shree Cement), chemicals, and data centres — all of whom depend on the pace of grid decarbonisation to reduce their Scope 2 emissions and meet SBTi or BRSR Core intensity targets Indian exporters to the EU subject to CBAM (carbon border adjustment) and CSRD supply chain disclosure requirements — for whom the carbon intensity of India's grid is a direct cost variable starting with CBAM's full enforcement from January 2026 CFOs and treasury teams at listed companies issuing or planning green bonds, sustainability-linked bonds, or accessing climate-labelled credit facilities — where India's grid decarbonisation credibility is a pricing input Chief Sustainability Officers and BRSR preparers at SEBI-regulated top 1,000 listed entities — who must disclose Scope 2 emissions under BRSR Core and will face investor questions about grid emission factor trajectories ESG analysts and fund managers at domestic and foreign institutional investors — including pension funds, sovereign wealth funds, and ESG-dedicated AUM — who are incorporating CAT country scores into portfolio climate risk assessments SEBI, MoEFCC, and BEE policy teams — who will need to respond to CAT's evaluation if India's power sector is rated as misaligned, particularly as India prepares its position for COP30 in November 2025

Key Obligations & Deadlines India's NDC 2.0 target — 50% cumulative installed electric power capacity from non-fossil sources by 2030 — is a sovereign commitment under the Paris Agreement; Indian listed companies' Scope 2 reduction targets are implicitly dependent on the government delivering this; CSOs should formally track and disclose this dependency in BRSR Core Principle 6 responses SEBI BRSR Core (mandatory for top 150 listed entities from FY2023-24, top 250 from FY2024-25): Scope 1 and Scope 2 GHG emissions must be disclosed with assurance; the grid emission factor used for Scope 2 calculation must be disclosed and justified — CAT's evaluation of India's grid trajectory is directly relevant to the credibility of these disclosures EU CBAM full enforcement from January 2026: Indian exporters of steel, aluminium, cement, fertilisers, and electricity must report and pay for embedded carbon using verified emissions data; the carbon intensity of India's grid directly determines the Scope 2 component of embedded emissions in manufactured exports COP30, Belém, Brazil — November 2025: India's revised or confirmed NDC and power sector commitments will be assessed in real time by CAT and other trackers; this is the credibility checkpoint that institutional investors will watch for sovereign and company-level ESG rating implications SBTi Power Sector pathway: Indian power companies seeking SBTi validation must demonstrate alignment with a sector-specific decarbonisation trajectory consistent with 1.5°C; CAT's new evaluation framework uses compatible methodology and will serve as a cross-reference point for SBTi validators BEE's Perform Achieve and Trade (PAT) Scheme Cycle 7 targets and the proposed Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) under MoEFCC's January 2023 notification — both require power sector benchmarking against efficiency and emissions intensity metrics that will be stress-tested against CAT's sector evaluation

Climate Transition Angle The CAT power sector evaluation sits at the precise intersection of physical climate risk and transition risk for Indian business. On the transition side: the speed of India's grid decarbonisation determines the trajectory of every Indian company's Scope 2 emissions — which under ISSB IFRS S2 (adopted voluntarily by several Indian companies and referenced in SEBI's BRSR Core design) must be disclosed alongside climate-related financial risks. If India's grid decarbonisation falls behind the 1.5°C pathway, Indian companies face a structural Scope 2 emissions problem that no amount of renewable energy certificates (RECs) or captive solar can fully offset at scale — particularly for heavy industries where grid dependence is unavoidable for baseload operations. This is not a 2035 problem; it is showing up right now in SBTi validation rejections, green finance term sheet conditions, and EU CSRD value chain disclosure obligations. On the physical risk side, India's power sector is acutely exposed to climate-driven disruptions: hydropower output volatility due to monsoon variability (a documented risk to NHPC and other hydro operators), thermal plant efficiency losses during extreme heat events (materially affecting coal plant capacity factors), and transmission infrastructure stress during peak summer demand. CAT's evaluation, by focusing on the 1.5°C-aligned decarbonisation pathway, implicitly highlights the cost of inaction: a grid that remains coal-heavy into the 2030s is simultaneously a high-transition-risk grid and a physically vulnerable one. For boards applying TCFD or ISSB S2 scenario analysis, both the 1.5°C transition scenario (rapid coal phase-down, stranded asset risk for coal-linked companies) and the 3°C physical risk scenario (grid reliability failure, cooling load surge) must be modelled — and CAT's sectoral framework provides the benchmark trajectories for the transition scenario. Indian companies with coal-linked power purchase agreements, coal mine captive plants, or significant coal sector equity exposure should treat a CAT 'insufficient' rating on India's power sector as a trigger to update their stranded asset provisioning and transition plan disclosures under BRSR Core.

What To Do Now Sustainability/ESG team (immediate — Q2 FY2026): Download and review CAT's power sector evaluation slides and methodology; map India's current CAT rating against your company's Scope 2 baseline and SBTi or internal net zero target trajectory; identify the grid emission factor assumption embedded in your BRSR Core Scope 2 disclosure and stress-test it against a scenario where India's grid decarbonises 20% slower than NDC implies CFO/Treasury (within 90 days): For any green bond, SLB, or climate-labelled facility with KPIs linked to Scope 2 intensity reductions, assess whether the KPI design is robust to grid decarbonisation delay; renegotiate or disclose material dependency on national grid trajectory in sustainability-linked financing documentation Procurement/Supply Chain Head (ahead of CBAM full enforcement, January 2026): Quantify the Scope 2 component of embedded carbon in EU-bound exports using India's current grid emission factor (CEA's latest: approximately 0.716 tCO2/MWh for FY2022-23); model the financial impact of CBAM carbon pricing at €50–€80/tCO2 on export margins; initiate captive renewable or open-access RE procurement to reduce grid dependence for export-linked manufacturing Board Risk Committee (next scheduled meeting): Commission a formal scenario analysis presentation covering India's power sector decarbonisation risk under 1.5°C and 2.5°C pathways, using CAT's framework as the reference; ensure this is reflected in the company's TCFD/ISSB S2 climate risk disclosure in the next annual report Investor Relations (before COP30, October 2025): Prepare a proactive ESG investor briefing note that addresses India's NDC power sector commitments, your company's Scope 2 reduction roadmap, and your independence from or exposure to grid carbon intensity — international ESG investors will be asking this question more pointedly as COP30 approaches Government Affairs/Policy Engagement: Engage through industry bodies (CII, FICCI, IEEMA) to advocate for accelerated transmission infrastructure investment, storage mandate implementation, and RPO (Renewable Purchase Obligation) enforcement — these are the policy levers that determine whether India lands on CAT's 'gathering speed' or 'not delivering' list, and that determination has direct capital market consequences for Indian industry

Our Take CAT's move to sector-specific NDC evaluation — starting with power — is not a research exercise. It is the architecture for the next generation of climate accountability, and it is arriving at exactly the moment when India is finalising its COP30 positioning, when ISSB S2 sector-specific guidance is being embedded into national disclosure frameworks, and when EU CBAM is shifting from transition to enforcement phase. The power sector is the right place to start because it is the foundation of every other sector's decarbonisation: you cannot decarbonise Indian steel, cement, chemicals, or data centres without decarbonising the grid. India's 500 GW non-fossil target by 2030 is credible in headline terms but fragile in execution terms — transmission buildout, storage procurement, land acquisition, and domestic manufacturing of solar modules all face well-documented bottlenecks. If CAT formally rates India's power sector as 'insufficient' against 1.5°C in its published tracker (a real possibility given current execution gaps), the consequences will not be confined to diplomatic embarrassment at COP30. They will flow into MSCI and Sustainalytics country-level ESG adjustments, into green bond spread pricing for Indian sovereign and corporate issuers, and into the SBTi validation pipeline for Indian companies whose Scope 2 trajectories are implicitly underwritten by the NDC grid target. The 12–36 month horizon I am watching: SEBI's anticipated update to BRSR to incorporate ISSB S2-aligned climate scenario disclosure requirements; MoEFCC's rollout of the Carbon Credit Trading Scheme and its intersection with PAT cycle outcomes in the power sector; and the emergence of a grid-level carbon accounting standard for India that reconciles CEA's annual emission factors with the real-time, marginal emission factors that sophisticated Scope 2 accounting and CBAM compliance will eventually require. Boards that treat power sector decarbonisation as someone else's problem — a utility or government issue — are mispricing the transition risk sitting inside their own Scope 2 number.

title: CAT's Power Sector Decarbonisation Scorecard: What India's NDC Update and Renewable Energy Trajectory Mean for Capital Markets and BRSR Disclosure
summary: The Climate Action Tracker's May 2025 virtual event — 'Plugging into Progress: Evaluating Power Sector Decarbonisation for 1.5°C' — has released a structured evaluation of whether countries' renewable energy rollouts are on track to meet the COP28 commitment to triple global renewables capacity by 2

sections.t: Executive Summary The Climate Action Tracker's May 2025 virtual event — 'Plugging into Progress: Evaluating Power Sector Decarbonisation for 1.5°C' — has released a structured evaluation of whether countries' renewable energy rollouts are on track to meet the COP28 commitment to triple global renewables capacity by 2030. For India, this is not an abstract global scorecard: India's updated NDC — due in 2025 as part of the Paris Agreement cycle — will be benchmarked against exactly this framework, and its credibility gap or progress story will directly influence sovereign climate risk ratings, green bond demand, and the ESG narrative Indian listed companies can credibly tell institutional investors. The CAT analysis, sourced from climateactiontracker.org, makes clear that NDC ambition without sectoral power decarbonisation evidence is no longer sufficient for 1.5°C alignment. For Indian CFOs and CSOs at BSE 500 companies, this event signals an accelerating convergence: global investors and supply chain counterparties will increasingly triangulate India's national power grid trajectory against company-level Scope 2 disclosures under BRSR Core and ISSB IFRS S2, making grid decarbonisation both a country-level diplomatic issue and an enterprise-level financial materiality question.

What Changed Reporting by Climate Action Tracker (https://climateactiontracker.org/blog/virtual-event-pulling-into-progress-power-decarbonisation/) documents a formal structured evaluation of national power sector decarbonisation trajectories against the 1.5°C pathway, with particular focus on whether the COP28 tripling-renewables pledge — signed by over 130 countries including India — is being translated into credible, measurable NDC commitments in 2025. CAT's framework goes beyond headline renewable capacity targets and evaluates the pace of rollout, policy sufficiency, and alignment with Paris Agreement carbon budgets. The key analytical shift: CAT is now treating power sector decarbonisation as a leading indicator of NDC credibility, not just a sub-component. For India, this matters acutely. India submitted its updated NDC in 2022, committing to 500 GW non-fossil capacity by 2030 and a 45% reduction in emissions intensity of GDP. The 2025 NDC revision cycle — which all Paris signatories are expected to complete ahead of COP30 in Belém, Brazil, in November 2025 — will require India to either deepen these commitments or explain why not. CAT's evaluation framework, now publicly available, will be used by investors, ratings agencies (including those informing MSCI ESG Ratings, S&P Global ESG Scores, and Sustainalytics), and EU counterparties assessing India under CBAM and CSRD value chain due diligence to judge whether India's power sector transition is real or rhetorical. Critically, India's current renewable addition rate — while impressive in absolute terms, with over 200 GW of installed non-fossil capacity as of early 2025 — still faces structural challenges: grid integration bottlenecks, land acquisition delays, state DISCOM financial distress, and a coal capacity pipeline that remains active. CAT's methodology distinguishes between installed capacity and actual clean energy share in generation mix, a distinction that will increasingly matter to Scope 2 emissions calculations that Indian companies must disclose under BRSR Core (mandatory for top 150 listed entities by market cap from FY2023-24) and that institutional investors will scrutinise under ISSB IFRS S2 climate-related disclosure frameworks.

Who Is Affected Power generation and utilities sector: NTPC, Adani Green, Tata Power, JSW Energy, ReNew Power — directly rated on generation mix trajectory and capex alignment with 1.5°C pathways Energy-intensive industries with high Scope 2 exposure: Steel (Tata Steel, JSW Steel, SAIL), Aluminium (Hindalco, Vedanta), Cement (UltraTech, ACC, Ambuja), and Data Centres — whose decarbonisation credibility is structurally dependent on grid emission factor trajectory Top 150 BSE-listed companies by market cap: mandatory BRSR Core disclosures require Scope 1 and 2 GHG reporting; grid emission factor assumptions used in Scope 2 calculations will face investor scrutiny against CAT's power sector ratings Export-oriented manufacturers: companies supplying to EU buyers face CSRD value chain disclosure requirements and CBAM carbon pricing on steel, aluminium, cement, and fertilisers — all sectors where Scope 2 grid intensity is a direct cost and compliance variable CFOs and Treasury teams at companies with green bonds or sustainability-linked loans: covenants tied to renewable energy procurement or Scope 2 reduction targets are exposed if India's grid decarbonisation pace slips ESG analysts and portfolio managers at FIIs, domestic mutual funds with ESG mandates, and development finance institutions (IFC, ADB, AIIB) financing Indian energy transition assets India's Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE) and MoEFCC officials preparing India's 2025 NDC revision — CAT's evaluation will be a reference document at COP30 negotiations

Key Obligations & Deadlines India's updated NDC submission: Due ahead of COP30 (November 2025, Belém) — India must present revised 2035 targets; CAT's power sector evaluation will benchmark the credibility of any renewable energy commitment made BRSR Core — Scope 2 GHG disclosure: Mandatory for top 150 listed companies by market cap from FY2023-24 reporting (published in FY2024-25 annual reports); companies must use a defensible grid emission factor — currently CEA's CO2 baseline database — and disclose methodology BRSR Core — Energy intensity and renewable energy consumption: Leadership indicators require disclosure of percentage of renewable energy consumed; this directly links to grid mix trajectory and on-site/off-site renewable procurement strategies COP28 Tripling Renewables Pledge: India is a signatory; 2025 NDC must demonstrate a credible pathway to contribution — failure to do so will be flagged by CAT and picked up by sovereign ESG rating agencies EU CBAM Phase-In: Full CBAM obligations begin January 2026 for steel, cement, aluminium, fertilisers, hydrogen, and electricity; Indian exporters in these sectors must report embedded carbon including Scope 2 from grid electricity — grid decarbonisation pace directly affects CBAM liability SBTi Corporate Net-Zero Standard: Companies with validated science-based targets must align Scope 2 reduction trajectories with a 1.5°C pathway; CAT's assessment of India's grid trajectory will inform SBTi's country-level grid decarbonisation assumptions used in target validation ISSB IFRS S2 adoption: While not yet mandated in India, SEBI's stated intent to align BRSR with ISSB means companies should begin preparing climate-related financial disclosures including transition risk scenarios tied to power sector decarbonisation pace

Climate Transition Angle The CAT power sector evaluation is, at its core, a transition risk stress test applied at the country level — and for Indian businesses, it directly shapes the financial materiality landscape. India's NDC credibility gap, if identified by CAT, will flow into sovereign climate risk assessments used by Moody's, S&P, and Fitch, raising the cost of green sovereign bonds and infrastructure financing. At the corporate level, the most immediate transition risk is Scope 2 emissions intensity: if India's grid emission factor — currently approximately 0.71 tCO2/MWh on the CEA baseline — does not decline at a pace consistent with 1.5°C, then energy-intensive Indian manufacturers face a structural competitive disadvantage in EU export markets under CBAM, and a credibility deficit in meeting SBTi-validated Scope 2 reduction targets. The BRSR Core framework requires listed Indian companies to disclose energy consumption from grid versus renewable sources, and to report total Scope 2 emissions. As institutional investors — particularly those aligned with TCFD, ISSB IFRS S2, and the Net Zero Asset Managers Initiative — increase their scrutiny of Scope 2 trajectories, a stagnant or slow-decarbonising Indian grid becomes a portfolio-level risk flag. Indian companies that have not invested in captive renewable energy, Power Purchase Agreements (PPAs) for RE100-quality certificates, or RECs will find their Scope 2 disclosures increasingly difficult to defend against 1.5°C alignment benchmarks that CAT's methodology now makes publicly accessible and comparable. The physical risk dimension is equally present: India's power sector is already experiencing grid stress from extreme heat events — a trend that the IPCC AR6 WG2 report identifies as accelerating under 1.5°C and beyond, creating both reliability risk for industrial consumers and stranded asset risk for coal-heavy generation portfolios.

What To Do Now Sustainability and Strategy teams (immediate, Q2-Q3 FY2025-26): Download CAT's power sector evaluation slides and methodology; map India's rating against your company's Scope 2 emission factor assumptions in BRSR Core disclosures — identify whether your current grid emission factor is defensible or needs updating to CEA's latest CO2 baseline CFOs and Treasury (within 90 days): Review green bond frameworks, sustainability-linked loan covenants, and ESG-linked financing terms for any Scope 2 or renewable energy KPIs; assess exposure if India's grid decarbonisation pace causes covenant breach or target miss — engage lenders proactively with a grid trajectory narrative Procurement and Energy teams (FY2025-26 planning cycle): Accelerate evaluation of long-term PPAs, open access renewable energy, and captive solar/wind projects to structurally decouple Scope 2 intensity from grid trajectory — this is now both a compliance necessity (BRSR Core, SBTi) and a CBAM cost-management strategy for exporters Board Risk Committee (next board meeting): Add 'India NDC revision and power sector decarbonisation trajectory' as a standing agenda item under climate-related financial risk; commission a scenario analysis under ISSB IFRS S2 Appendix B climate scenarios showing the impact of slow vs. fast grid decarbonisation on Scope 2 costs and export competitiveness Investor Relations (ahead of AGM season FY2025-26): Prepare a briefing note for institutional investors on your company's Scope 2 strategy relative to India's grid trajectory, referencing CAT's framework; proactively address how you are managing the gap between current grid emission factor and your net-zero or SBTi pathway — investors aligned with NZAM and PRI will ask this question Legal and Compliance (Q3 FY2025-26): Map CBAM embedded carbon reporting requirements for any steel, aluminium, cement, or fertiliser exports to the EU against current Scope 2 grid emission factor; engage with EU CBAM authorised declarant process and ensure carbon data chain of custody is audit-ready by January 2026

Our Take CAT's structured power sector evaluation is a signal of a much larger shift: the era of NDC-level ambiguity is ending. By 2026-2027, sovereign climate performance on power decarbonisation will be a hard input into capital market pricing — not a soft ESG narrative. India is at a pivotal juncture. Its renewable energy capacity addition story is genuinely impressive, but CAT's methodology will expose the gap between installed capacity headlines and actual grid carbon intensity, and that gap has direct financial consequences for Indian listed companies across three vectors: Scope 2 disclosure credibility under BRSR Core and ISSB IFRS S2, CBAM cost exposure for industrial exporters, and SBTi target validation integrity. What boards and investors should watch over the next 12-36 months: First, India's 2025 NDC revision will be a defining moment — if it fails to include a credible 2035 power sector decarbonisation trajectory with quantified coal phase-down milestones, expect CAT and allied research bodies to publish critical assessments that will be cited in sovereign ESG ratings downgrades. Second, SEBI is moving toward mandatory ISSB IFRS S2 alignment — the BRSR evolution roadmap telegraphed in SEBI's 2023 consultation suggests that Scope 2 market-based disclosures and transition plan requirements are coming within the next 2-3 years, which will make RE100-quality procurement and grid factor methodology a board-level governance question, not just a sustainability team exercise. Third, the EU's CSRD value chain rules will force large Indian suppliers to multinational companies to provide power sector carbon data that is externally assured — companies that have not invested in renewable energy procurement infrastructure by 2026 will face supply chain disqualification risk, not just reputational exposure. India's power sector transition is no longer a background condition for corporate ESG strategy — it is the single most important structural variable determining whether Indian companies can credibly claim 1.5°C alignment, and the capital market consequences of getting this wrong are now measurable, proximate, and accelerating.

title: CPCB Directs Customs Authorities to Release Imported Consignments for 106 EEE Item Producers: What EPR-Registered Companies Must Know
summary: On June 30, 2023, the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) issued a letter to Customs Authorities directing the release of imported consignments belonging to producers of 106 categories of Electrical and Electronic Equipment (EEE) covered under India's Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framew

sections.t: Executive Summary On June 30, 2023, the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) issued a letter to Customs Authorities directing the release of imported consignments belonging to producers of 106 categories of Electrical and Electronic Equipment (EEE) covered under India's Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework. This intervention signals that CPCB is actively coordinating with border agencies to ensure that EPR-compliant producers are not unduly penalised at the import stage. Companies importing EEE products or components must ensure their EPR registrations and obligations are current — or risk consignment holds that can disrupt supply chains and operations.

What Changed Under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, producers of Electrical and Electronic Equipment listed across 106 item categories are required to register on the CPCB EPR portal and meet annual e-waste collection and channelisation targets. Customs Authorities, as part of India's broader regulatory enforcement apparatus, can flag or hold imported consignments of EEE goods when producers are not demonstrably compliant with EPR obligations. The June 30, 2023 letter from CPCB to Customs represents a significant procedural clarification: CPCB has communicated directly to port and customs officials, likely providing a list of registered and compliant producers whose consignments should be released without undue delay. This is a dual-edged action — it rewards compliance by facilitating trade, while implicitly putting non-compliant or unregistered producers on notice that their imports may face scrutiny or detention. In plain terms, if your company imports EEE goods into India and is registered on the CPCB EPR portal with obligations in good standing, this letter works in your favour. If you are not registered or have pending EPR targets, this coordination between CPCB and Customs increases your exposure to import delays and regulatory action.

Who Is Affected Producers, importers, and brand owners of any of the 106 categories of Electrical and Electronic Equipment (EEE) listed under Schedule I of the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 Consumer electronics companies importing finished goods such as laptops, mobile phones, televisions, refrigerators, washing machines, and lighting equipment Industrial electronics importers dealing with medical devices, monitoring and control instruments, and IT and telecommunications equipment Component importers whose products are classified as EEE sub-assemblies under the applicable HSN codes Foreign Original Equipment Manufacturers (OEMs) selling into the Indian market through distribution arrangements, who hold producer liability under EPR rules Logistics and customs clearing agents acting on behalf of EEE producers, who need to ensure their clients' EPR documentation is in order before filing bills of entry

Key Obligations & Deadlines EPR Registration (Immediate): All producers of the 106 EEE categories must be registered on the CPCB EPR Portal (https://eprewaste.cpcb.gov.in). Non-registered entities importing EEE goods risk consignment detention at customs. Annual EPR Target Filing: Producers must submit their annual EPR targets for e-waste collection and channelisation for FY 2023-24 on the CPCB portal. Targets are based on sales data from previous years. EPR Certificate Procurement: Producers who cannot collect e-waste directly must procure EPR certificates from CPCB-registered recyclers or dismantlers to offset their annual obligations — ensure certificates are current and cover applicable volumes. Customs Documentation Readiness (Immediate): Ensure that your EPR registration certificate and compliance status documents are available to your customs clearing agent for submission at the port of entry, aligned with the CPCB's communication to Customs Authorities. Quarterly Reporting: Producers are required to file quarterly reports on e-waste collected, channelised, and recycled on the CPCB portal — lapses in reporting can flag a company as non-compliant, jeopardising future import clearances. Importer of Record Review: Companies where the importer of record differs from the EPR-registered entity must resolve this discrepancy immediately to avoid mismatch issues at Customs.

Climate Transition Angle India's EPR framework for e-waste is far more than a domestic compliance checkbox — it sits at the intersection of circular economy policy, climate risk management, and global supply chain sustainability standards that international investors and trading partners increasingly scrutinise. E-waste contains significant quantities of hazardous materials (lead, mercury, cadmium) as well as critical minerals (cobalt, lithium, rare earth elements) that are essential to the clean energy transition — solar panels, EV batteries, and wind turbines all depend on supply chains that intersect with EEE manufacturing. Improper e-waste disposal not only creates toxic environmental liabilities but also represents a systemic loss of materials needed for the low-carbon economy. India's EPR framework, by mandating formal recycling, directly supports the recovery of these critical resources. From a global standards perspective, companies operating in India that also report under the International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) framework — specifically IFRS S1 and S2 — or are subject to the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) through their European parent entities or supply chain relationships, will find that e-waste EPR compliance feeds directly into their Scope 3 value chain disclosures and product stewardship narratives. The EU's Ecodesign for Sustainable Products Regulation (ESPR) is also placing circular economy obligations on electronics sold in Europe, creating regulatory convergence that Indian EEE exporters cannot ignore. Getting EPR compliance right in India is not just about avoiding a customs hold — it is about building the operational and data infrastructure to meet multi-jurisdictional sustainability disclosure requirements.

What To Do Now Audit Your EPR Registration Status Immediately: Log into the CPCB EPR portal and verify that your producer registration is active, that your product categories are correctly listed, and that there are no outstanding compliance flags that could trigger a customs hold on incoming shipments. Brief Your Customs Clearing Agents: Share a copy of your CPCB EPR registration certificate and current compliance status with all customs clearing agents and freight forwarders handling your EEE import consignments. Instruct them to reference EPR compliance proactively when filing bills of entry for covered goods. Reconcile Your EPR Targets vs. Actuals: For FY 2022-23, confirm that your e-waste collection and channelisation actuals match or exceed your declared EPR targets. If there is a shortfall, immediately engage a CPCB-registered recycler to procure the necessary EPR certificates before the compliance window closes. Map All 106 EEE Categories to Your Product Portfolio: Conduct an internal product classification exercise to ensure every imported SKU is correctly mapped to the relevant EEE schedule category. Misclassification is a common source of compliance gaps and regulatory exposure. Establish a Cross-Functional EPR Compliance Team: Bring together your sustainability, legal, supply chain, and finance teams to build a quarterly EPR reporting calendar, assign ownership, and integrate EPR data into your broader ESG disclosure and BRSR (Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report) processes.

Our Take The CPCB's decision to issue a direct communication to Customs Authorities on behalf of compliant EEE producers is a landmark moment in India's EPR enforcement architecture. For the first time, we are seeing explicit coordination between the country's apex pollution control regulator and its trade border agencies — a sign that EPR is graduating from a self-declaration exercise to a hard-edged trade compliance requirement. Companies that have treated EPR registration as a paperwork formality should treat this notice as a wake-up call: the cost of non-compliance is no longer just a penalty on paper but a very real, very disruptive halt to your import operations. Looking ahead, we expect CPCB to expand this coordination model to other EPR streams — plastic packaging, batteries, and tyres — as those frameworks mature. The companies that invest now in robust EPR compliance systems, real-time portal monitoring, and supply chain traceability will be the ones that avoid costly disruptions and build the credibility that ESG-conscious investors and global customers increasingly demand.

title: CPCB Directs Customs Release for 106 EEE Items: What EPR-Registered Producers Must Know
summary: On 31 August 2023, the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) issued a formal letter to Customs Authorities facilitating the release of imported consignments belonging to producers of 106 categories of Electrical and Electronic Equipment (EEE) listed under India's E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022. T

sections.t: Executive Summary On 31 August 2023, the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) issued a formal letter to Customs Authorities facilitating the release of imported consignments belonging to producers of 106 categories of Electrical and Electronic Equipment (EEE) listed under India's E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022. This intervention signals that EPR registration and compliance status are now actively linked to import clearance — a significant escalation in regulatory enforcement. Producers who are not registered on the CPCB EPR portal or who have lapsed obligations risk customs-level disruption to their supply chains.

What Changed Under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, producers of Electrical and Electronic Equipment (EEE) are legally required to register with CPCB, set annual EPR targets, and ensure end-of-life collection and recycling of their products through authorised dismantlers and recyclers. The 106 EEE item categories covered span a wide range — from consumer electronics and IT equipment to large household appliances and medical devices. The CPCB letter dated 31 August 2023 directed Customs Authorities to release imported consignments specifically for producers who are compliant with EPR obligations. In plain terms: CPCB has formally communicated to Customs that EPR registration and compliance is a condition that must be verified at the point of import. Consignments from non-compliant or unregistered producers face the risk of being held at ports. This move effectively transforms EPR compliance from a back-office regulatory requirement into a real-time, supply-chain-critical obligation.

Who Is Affected Producers and brand owners of any of the 106 EEE categories who import finished goods, components, or sub-assemblies into India Importers acting as 'producers' under the E-Waste Rules — including subsidiaries of multinational electronics, appliance, and technology companies Consumer electronics companies (smartphones, laptops, TVs, audio equipment) White goods manufacturers and importers (refrigerators, washing machines, air conditioners) IT and office equipment producers (printers, servers, networking equipment) Medical device companies whose products fall under the notified EEE schedule Lighting equipment producers and importers (including LED systems) Customs brokers and freight forwarders handling EEE consignments on behalf of producers

Key Obligations & Deadlines IMMEDIATE: Verify that your organisation is registered as a Producer on the CPCB EPR Portal (https://eprnewportal.cpcb.gov.in) for all applicable EEE product categories IMMEDIATE: Confirm that your EPR Certificate is current, valid, and reflects accurate product categories matching your import consignments BY NEXT IMPORT CYCLE: Ensure your Customs Broker has a copy of your valid CPCB EPR registration/certificate to present at the time of import clearance ONGOING (Annual): Meet your EPR targets for e-waste collection and channelisation through CPCB-authorised recyclers — non-fulfilment of targets can trigger non-compliance status affecting future imports ONGOING: File quarterly and annual returns on the CPCB EPR portal accurately and on time; gaps in filings can trigger compliance flags REVIEW: If your consignment was recently held or delayed at customs, engage with CPCB's regional office immediately to resolve compliance status and obtain clearance documentation AUDIT: Conduct an internal review of all product SKUs imported against the 106 EEE categories to ensure no category is inadvertently unregistered

Climate Transition Angle While EPR for e-waste is primarily framed as a waste management regulation, its climate dimensions are significant and increasingly recognised in global sustainability frameworks. E-waste is one of the fastest-growing solid waste streams globally, and improper disposal leads to toxic emissions, soil contamination, and loss of critical minerals — including lithium, cobalt, and rare earth elements — that are essential for the clean energy transition (EV batteries, solar panels, wind turbines). From a climate transition strategy perspective, robust EPR compliance directly supports circular economy goals — a key pillar of India's Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) and the National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC). Recovering critical minerals through formal e-waste recycling reduces dependence on primary mining, which is both carbon-intensive and geopolitically risky. Internationally, frameworks such as the ISSB's IFRS S2 (climate-related disclosures) and the EU's CSRD increasingly require companies to account for Scope 3 emissions and circular economy performance across their value chains. Indian subsidiaries of global companies reporting under CSRD or preparing for BRSR Core disclosures will find that EPR compliance data — collection rates, recycling volumes, certified recycler partnerships — feeds directly into resource use and circular economy metrics demanded by these standards. Non-compliance is not just a regulatory risk; it is an ESG disclosure gap.

What To Do Now REGISTER OR RENEW: Log into the CPCB EPR Portal today and confirm your Producer registration is active, covers all 106 applicable EEE categories you manufacture or import, and that your EPR certificate has not lapsed BRIEF YOUR CUSTOMS TEAM: Share your current EPR compliance documentation (registration certificate, target fulfilment records) with your customs broker and import/logistics team — make this a standard document in your import checklist MAP YOUR EPR TARGETS: Work with your sustainability or compliance team to calculate your mandatory collection targets under the 2022 Rules and verify you have sufficient agreements in place with CPCB-authorised PROs (Producer Responsibility Organisations) or recyclers to meet them INTEGRATE EPR INTO SUPPLY CHAIN RISK MANAGEMENT: Flag EPR compliance status as a supply chain risk indicator — a lapsed registration can now cause port-level delays, directly impacting revenue and customer commitments ALIGN WITH ESG REPORTING: Begin capturing EPR performance data (tonnes collected, recycling certificates, PRO agreements) in a format compatible with BRSR Core and ISSB/CSRD disclosures to future-proof your sustainability reporting

Our Take This CPCB letter is a watershed moment in India's EPR enforcement journey. For years, EPR for e-waste existed largely as a compliance-on-paper obligation — producers registered, filed returns, and moved on with limited real-world consequence for non-fulfilment. By formally looping in Customs Authorities, CPCB has introduced a hard, operational consequence: your goods don't move if your EPR house is not in order. This is exactly the kind of multi-agency enforcement coordination that gives regulatory teeth to environmental rules, and it mirrors how the EU has long linked market access to environmental compliance. We expect this approach to be extended further — to plastics EPR, battery EPR, and tyre EPR — as CPCB builds on this precedent. For business leaders, the message is clear: EPR compliance is no longer a sustainability team checkbox. It is a supply chain continuity issue that belongs on the CFO's and COO's radar today.

title: CPCB Directs Customs Release for 106 EEE Items: What Importers and EPR-Registered Producers Must Know
summary: On 31 August 2023, the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) issued a formal letter to Customs Authorities directing the release of imported consignments belonging to producers of 106 specified Electrical and Electronic Equipment (EEE) items. This intervention signals that CPCB is actively coordina

sections.t: Executive Summary On 31 August 2023, the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) issued a formal letter to Customs Authorities directing the release of imported consignments belonging to producers of 106 specified Electrical and Electronic Equipment (EEE) items. This intervention signals that CPCB is actively coordinating with border agencies to enforce EPR compliance as a pre-condition for market access. Companies importing or manufacturing EEE products must treat valid EPR registration — and timely target fulfilment — as a non-negotiable gateway to trade, not merely a back-office reporting obligation.

What Changed Under India's E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, producers, importers, and brand owners of Electrical and Electronic Equipment are required to register on the CPCB EPR portal and meet annual e-waste collection and channelisation targets. When a producer fails to demonstrate valid EPR registration or compliance status, Customs Authorities can — and increasingly do — hold consignments at the port of entry. The CPCB letter dated 31 August 2023 represents a specific directive issued on behalf of 106 EEE producers, instructing Customs to release their consignments. This type of letter is typically issued when a producer can demonstrate active EPR registration and/or partial or full target fulfilment, resolving a hold that had been placed on their imports. The significance lies in the precedent: CPCB is functioning as an active gatekeeper between the EPR registry and physical trade flows, meaning lapses in EPR compliance can now directly disrupt supply chains at the border.

Who Is Affected Producers and importers of any of the 106 EEE categories listed under Schedule I of the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 — including IT and telecom equipment, consumer electronics, large and small household appliances, lighting equipment, and solar panels. Indian subsidiaries and branches of multinational electronics brands that import finished goods or semi-finished assemblies into India. Original Equipment Manufacturers (OEMs) sourcing components internationally, where the importing entity qualifies as a 'producer' under the Rules. E-commerce platforms and marketplace sellers who qualify as producers under the Rules by virtue of selling EEE products under their own brand or through private labels. Logistics and customs clearance agents acting on behalf of EEE producers — they must be aware of EPR compliance status before filing Bills of Entry. Compliance and legal teams at mid-to-large electronics companies who may not have connected their customs clearance workflows to EPR registration status.

Key Obligations & Deadlines EPR Registration (Immediate / Ongoing): All producers and importers of EEE items must hold a valid, active EPR registration on the CPCB centralised portal (https://eprewaste.cpcb.gov.in) before importing any consignment. Registration must be renewed and kept current. Annual EPR Targets (Financial Year Basis): Producers must meet e-waste collection targets as specified in their EPR plan. Targets are calculated as a percentage of the quantity of EEE placed on the market in prior years — non-fulfilment triggers hold notices at Customs. EPR Certificate Submission to Customs: At the time of import, producers or their agents should be prepared to furnish EPR registration certificates and compliance status documents to Customs Authorities as proof of adherence. Extended Producer Responsibility Plan Filing: Producers must file an annual EPR plan with CPCB at the start of each financial year, detailing projected sales volumes and corresponding collection commitments. Consignment Release Letters (As Needed): If a consignment is held, producers must proactively engage CPCB with evidence of registration and target compliance to obtain a release letter — a process that can take days to weeks, causing costly demurrage and supply chain disruption. Quarterly / Annual Reporting: EPR-registered producers must submit periodic reports on e-waste collected, channelised to authorised dismantlers/recyclers, and certificates obtained — failures here can trigger future holds. Deadline Reference — 31 August 2023: The CPCB letter was issued on this date for 106 specific producers. Companies not covered by this letter should treat this as a warning signal and self-audit their EPR status immediately.

Climate Transition Angle E-waste is one of the fastest-growing waste streams globally, and its proper management sits at the intersection of the circular economy, toxic material control, and climate-related physical risk. Electronic devices contain critical minerals — lithium, cobalt, rare earth elements — whose extraction carries significant carbon and biodiversity costs. By enforcing EPR at the import stage, India is embedding circular economy principles directly into trade infrastructure, ensuring that the end-of-life cost of electronics is internalised by those who profit from their sale. From a global standards perspective, the International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) under IFRS S2 requires companies to disclose material climate-related risks across their value chains, including those arising from regulatory non-compliance. A supply chain disruption caused by a Customs hold due to EPR non-compliance is precisely the kind of transition risk — regulatory and operational — that investors and lenders are beginning to scrutinise. Similarly, the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and its underlying European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS) require companies with EU market exposure to report on circular economy metrics, including e-waste management practices in global operations. For Indian EEE producers with export ambitions or foreign investment, demonstrating robust domestic EPR compliance is increasingly a credential, not just a legal obligation. It signals governance maturity, supply chain resilience, and readiness for the global regulatory environment that is rapidly converging on extended producer responsibility as a baseline requirement.

What To Do Now Conduct an immediate EPR compliance audit: Verify that your company's EPR registration on the CPCB portal is active, reflects current product categories and sales volumes, and that your EPR plan for FY 2023-24 has been filed. If you have not yet registered, initiate the process without delay — Customs holds are operational, not theoretical. Integrate EPR status into customs clearance workflows: Brief your customs agents, freight forwarders, and import-export teams that EPR registration certificates must be validated before filing Bills of Entry for EEE goods. Create a pre-shipment checklist that includes EPR compliance as a mandatory clearance criterion. Verify and document e-waste target fulfilment: Confirm with your authorised e-waste dismantlers and recyclers that collection certificates (EPR certificates) have been issued and uploaded to the CPCB portal for all quantities claimed. Gaps here are the most common trigger for Customs holds. Establish a CPCB liaison protocol: Designate a senior compliance officer or engage a regulatory consultant with direct CPCB contact experience. In the event of a Customs hold, the ability to respond within 24-48 hours with the correct documentation can mean the difference between a minor delay and weeks of costly demurrage. Map your product portfolio against the 106 EEE categories: Ensure that every SKU you import or manufacture is correctly categorised under the E-Waste Rules schedules and that corresponding EPR targets are reflected in your plan. Miscategorisation is a common compliance gap that surfaces only at the point of enforcement.

Our Take The CPCB's August 2023 letter to Customs Authorities is a small but telling document. It confirms what many compliance teams have been slow to accept: EPR in India has moved beyond a registration exercise into an enforcement regime with real teeth at the border. The fact that 106 producers needed a release letter on a single date suggests both that Customs holds are being applied at scale and that CPCB is managing a high volume of compliance-linked trade interventions. Looking ahead, we expect this coordination between CPCB and Customs to deepen — potentially including automated data-sharing between the EPR portal and the ICEGATE customs system, which would make real-time compliance status checks at the point of import a reality. Companies that treat EPR as a strategic compliance priority today — rather than a paperwork afterthought — will have a material operational advantage as enforcement intensifies. For sustainability and ESG officers, this is also a disclosure moment: supply chain disruptions caused by regulatory non-compliance in environmental obligations are precisely the kind of governance failures that institutional investors are flagging in ESG due diligence. Get ahead of it now.

title: CPCB Extends Deadline for E-Waste EPR Annual Return Filing: What Producers, Importers, and Recyclers Must Do Now
summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent notice extending the timeline for filing Annual Returns on the E-Waste EPR Portal, providing temporary relief to producers, importers, brand owners, and recyclers registered under the E-Waste Management Rules, 2022. While the extension

sections.t: Executive Summary The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent notice extending the timeline for filing Annual Returns on the E-Waste EPR Portal, providing temporary relief to producers, importers, brand owners, and recyclers registered under the E-Waste Management Rules, 2022. While the extension eases immediate compliance pressure, it does not reduce the underlying obligations — entities must use this additional time to reconcile collection data, verify recycler certificates, and close any EPR credit gaps.
