Companies that treat this as a simple deadline shift rather than a compliance checkpoint risk penalties, portal de-registration, and reputational exposure as India's EPR enforcement posture tightens.

What Changed The CPCB has officially extended the deadline for submitting Annual Returns on the centralised E-Waste EPR Portal — the digital platform through which all registered entities under the E-Waste Management Rules, 2022 must report their production volumes, collection targets, actual collection achieved, recycling/refurbishment quantities, and EPR credit reconciliation for the relevant financial year. Annual Return filing is not a formality. It is the primary mechanism through which CPCB verifies whether a Producer Responsibility Organisation (PRO), producer, importer, or recycler has met its mandatory collection and channelisation targets. Failure to file accurately — or at all — can trigger demand notices, financial penalties, and suspension of EPR registration, all of which carry serious operational and reputational consequences. The extension follows what appears to be widespread difficulty among registered entities in completing data reconciliation on the portal — a pattern seen in prior EPR cycles across plastic and battery waste streams as well. The revised deadline has been communicated via official CPCB notice, and entities should monitor the CPCB website and their registered email for the specific new date.

Who Is Affected Producers of Electrical and Electronic Equipment (EEE) — including manufacturers of IT equipment, consumer electronics, large and small household appliances, lighting equipment, medical devices, and telecom products listed under Schedule I of the E-Waste Management Rules, 2022 Importers of EEE products sold in India, regardless of whether the parent company is foreign or domestic Brand Owners whose products are manufactured by third parties but sold under their brand name in India Producer Responsibility Organisations (PROs) managing EPR obligations on behalf of multiple producers Registered E-Waste Recyclers and Refurbishers who must report quantities received, processed, and certificate details on the portal Dismantlers operating within the formal e-waste value chain and registered on the CPCB EPR portal Any entity that has been issued EPR registration certificates and EPR credits under the 2022 Rules framework

Key Obligations & Deadlines IMMEDIATE: Visit the official CPCB website (cpcb.nic.in) and the E-Waste EPR Portal to confirm the exact revised deadline communicated in the notice — do not rely on third-party sources alone Compile and reconcile all production/import quantity data for the reporting financial year against collection and channelisation records before the extended deadline Ensure all EPR credits received from registered recyclers are uploaded, verified, and matched against your collection targets on the portal — unmatched credits will not count toward target fulfilment Producers and importers must confirm that recyclers they engaged with hold valid CPCB registration certificates for the relevant processing categories and that certificate numbers are correctly entered in the portal PROs must consolidate Annual Return data across all producer clients they represent and submit unified or individual returns as required by the portal's filing structure Recyclers must ensure that all inward e-waste receipts and processing certificates issued to producers/importers are accurately reflected in their own portal submissions to avoid discrepancies during CPCB reconciliation Document all internal data sources used for the Annual Return — sales data, collection MIS, third-party recycler invoices, and PRO statements — as CPCB may seek supporting evidence during scrutiny or audit Post-filing: Retain copies of filed returns, acknowledgement receipts, and all supporting documents for a minimum of five years in line with regulatory record-keeping norms

Climate Transition Angle E-waste EPR compliance is not merely a domestic regulatory checkbox — it sits at the intersection of circular economy imperatives, supply chain decarbonisation, and global ESG disclosure standards that Indian companies are increasingly subject to. From a physical risk perspective, the informal e-waste sector — which still processes a significant share of India's 1.6+ million metric tonnes of annual e-waste generation — releases toxic materials including lead, mercury, and cadmium into soil and water systems, compounding climate-related health and ecological vulnerabilities in urban and peri-urban regions. Robust EPR compliance that channels e-waste into the formal sector directly mitigates these risks. On the transition side, critical minerals such as cobalt, lithium, copper, and rare earth elements embedded in electronic equipment are essential for the clean energy transition — in batteries, solar panels, and EV motors. Formal e-waste recycling is increasingly recognised globally as a strategic secondary source of these materials, reducing dependence on primary mining and its associated carbon intensity. Companies with strong e-waste EPR track records are better positioned to demonstrate supply chain circularity to global buyers and investors. For companies reporting under BRSR Core (India's mandatory ESG framework for the top 150 listed companies), the ISSB's IFRS S2 climate standard, or the EU's CSRD (relevant for Indian subsidiaries of European companies or Indian exporters into the EU), e-waste management data feeds directly into resource efficiency, waste metrics, and value chain responsibility disclosures. Non-compliance with EPR is increasingly flagged in ESG due diligence by investors and rating agencies as a governance risk indicator.

What To Do Now CONFIRM THE NEW DEADLINE TODAY: Assign a compliance team member to access the CPCB E-Waste EPR Portal and the official notice immediately, log the revised date, and set internal milestone reminders at 30, 15, and 5 days before the deadline CONDUCT A DATA GAP AUDIT: Pull your production/import volumes for the reporting year, cross-reference with collection MIS data from PROs, recyclers, and collection point operators, and identify any shortfalls in EPR credit coverage that need to be addressed before filing ENGAGE YOUR PRO OR RECYCLING PARTNERS NOW: If you work through a PRO, formally request a status update on credit issuance and portal entries. If you work directly with recyclers, verify that all processing certificates have been uploaded to the portal on their end — delays on their side will affect your return ALIGN WITH YOUR ESG/SUSTAINABILITY TEAM: Ensure that the data being submitted to CPCB is consistent with what your company is reporting in BRSR disclosures, sustainability reports, or responses to ESG rating questionnaires — discrepancies create regulatory and reputational risk PREPARE FOR ENHANCED SCRUTINY IN FUTURE CYCLES: Use this filing cycle as an opportunity to build a more robust, automated data pipeline for EPR reporting. CPCB is progressively digitising enforcement, and manual, spreadsheet-based reconciliation will become increasingly inadequate as portal functionality and third-party audits expand

Our Take The CPCB's decision to extend the Annual Return filing deadline for E-Waste EPR is pragmatic — the portal ecosystem, while significantly improved since the 2022 Rules came into force, still presents reconciliation challenges, particularly for companies managing complex multi-product portfolios or working with large networks of collection and recycling partners. However, companies should read this extension as a signal, not a safety net. India's EPR enforcement architecture is maturing rapidly: CPCB has already demonstrated a willingness to issue penalty notices and de-register non-compliant entities across plastic and battery EPR streams, and e-waste is next in line for intensified scrutiny. More importantly, as global sustainability disclosure requirements cascade down supply chains — through CSRD, ISSB, and evolving SEBI mandates — EPR compliance data is becoming a hard input into ESG ratings, investor due diligence, and export market access. The companies that build robust, auditable EPR data systems now will not just avoid penalties; they will gain a meaningful competitive and reputational advantage as the circular economy becomes central to India's industrial and trade policy over the next decade.

title: CPCB Extends E-Waste EPR Return Filing Deadline: What Producers, Importers & Brand Owners Must Do Now
summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent notice extending the timeline for filing returns on the E-Waste EPR Portal, providing temporary relief to producers, importers, and brand owners (PIBOs) struggling with compliance deadlines. While the extension offers breathing room, it

sections.t: Executive Summary The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent notice extending the timeline for filing returns on the E-Waste EPR Portal, providing temporary relief to producers, importers, and brand owners (PIBOs) struggling with compliance deadlines. While the extension offers breathing room, it does not reduce the underlying obligation to register, report, and meet Extended Producer Responsibility targets under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022. Companies should treat this window as an opportunity to get their EPR documentation, channel partner agreements, and recycler certifications in order — not as a signal that enforcement pressure is easing.

What Changed The CPCB has formally notified stakeholders through its official portal of an extension to the deadline for filing annual or periodic returns under the E-Waste EPR Portal. This portal, launched as part of the revamped E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, is the centralised digital platform through which all registered Producers, Importers, and Brand Owners (PIBOs) of electrical and electronic equipment (EEE) must report the quantity of e-waste generated, collected, channelised, and recycled against their EPR obligations. The extension is consistent with a pattern seen across India's EPR frameworks — including plastic and battery EPR — where the CPCB has periodically granted additional time in response to industry requests, portal technical issues, or low compliance rates. The notice signals that CPCB remains serious about enforcing return filing but is providing a structured grace period to allow stragglers to come into compliance before penalties are triggered. Importantly, the extension applies specifically to the return filing process — the submission of data on e-waste collected and recycled. It does not extend the underlying EPR targets themselves, nor does it provide amnesty from scrutiny of past shortfalls.

Who Is Affected Producers of Electrical and Electronic Equipment (EEE) — including manufacturers of consumer electronics, IT equipment, telecom devices, medical devices, and large/small household appliances listed under Schedules I and II of the E-Waste Rules, 2022 Importers of EEE products who place equipment into the Indian market and are independently liable for EPR obligations proportional to their import volumes Brand Owners who sell EEE under their own label, regardless of whether manufacturing is outsourced domestically or abroad E-Waste Producer Responsibility Organisations (PROs) that facilitate collective EPR compliance on behalf of PIBOs and must consolidate and report aggregated data Refurbishers and dismantlers registered under the portal who have reporting obligations tied to the volumes they receive and process Large enterprises with diverse product portfolios spanning multiple EEE categories — particularly those in consumer electronics, IT/ITES hardware, telecom, and white goods sectors Foreign Original Equipment Manufacturers (OEMs) with Indian subsidiaries or authorised importers who may be unaware of localised EPR reporting requirements

Key Obligations & Deadlines REGISTER on the CPCB E-Waste EPR Portal (https://ewasteauth.cpcb.gov.in) if not already done — registration is a prerequisite for return filing and cannot be completed retroactively without scrutiny FILE ANNUAL RETURNS detailing: (a) quantity of EEE placed on the market in the preceding year, (b) EPR targets applicable (calculated as a percentage of market placement), (c) e-waste collected through authorised channels, and (d) e-waste sent to registered recyclers/dismantlers — within the extended window notified by CPCB UPLOAD SUPPORTING EVIDENCE including recycler certificates, channel partner agreements with Collection Points or PROs, and invoices/gate passes for e-waste handed over to authorised facilities RECONCILE EPR TARGET vs. ACTUAL PERFORMANCE — any shortfall must be addressed either through additional collection drives before the window closes or by documenting carry-forward obligations as permissible under the rules MONITOR THE CPCB PORTAL regularly for the specific extended deadline date, as the notice does not always specify a fixed calendar date upfront — assign a compliance officer to track portal announcements weekly AVOID ASSUMING AUTO-EXTENSION — the extension applies to the filing of returns, not to EPR target fulfilment itself; companies that have not met collection targets must still account for the shortfall NON-COMPLIANCE CONSEQUENCES: Failure to file returns within the extended timeline may result in Environmental Compensation levied under Rule 22 of the E-Waste Rules, 2022, potential suspension of EPR certificates, and public disclosure of non-compliant entities on the CPCB website

Climate Transition Angle E-waste EPR is not merely a domestic compliance checkbox — it sits at the intersection of climate risk, resource efficiency, and global supply chain accountability. The extraction of virgin critical minerals (lithium, cobalt, copper, rare earth elements) embedded in electronic equipment is a significant driver of carbon emissions and ecological disruption. India's EPR framework, by mandating formalised recycling and channelisation, is designed to build a circular economy that reduces this extraction pressure and keeps valuable materials in productive use. From a climate transition perspective, companies that systematically track and recover e-waste are simultaneously building the data infrastructure needed to meet emerging global disclosure standards. The International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) under IFRS S2, and Europe's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD), both require disclosure of Scope 3 value chain emissions — which includes the end-of-life treatment of products sold. A company with robust EPR return data is far better positioned to quantify and disclose product-lifecycle emissions than one that has no visibility into where its products end up. Additionally, as global customers and institutional investors increasingly apply ESG due diligence to Indian suppliers, documented EPR compliance becomes a competitive differentiator. A supplier that cannot demonstrate responsible product end-of-life management faces growing risk of being de-listed from international value chains, particularly those serving European or North American markets subject to stricter producer responsibility laws. India's EPR framework, while still maturing, is directionally aligned with these global standards — making early, rigorous compliance a strategic investment, not just a regulatory burden.

What To Do Now AUDIT YOUR EPR STATUS IMMEDIATELY: Assign your sustainability or legal compliance team to verify registration status on the CPCB E-Waste EPR Portal, check whether prior year returns have been filed, and identify any outstanding target shortfalls — do this within the next 5 business days, not at the end of the extended window ENGAGE YOUR PRO OR CHANNEL PARTNERS: If you rely on a Producer Responsibility Organisation or authorised collection partners, contact them immediately to obtain updated recycling certificates, tonnage receipts, and any documentation needed for return filing — delays in gathering third-party documents are the most common cause of last-minute non-compliance CALCULATE YOUR EPR TARGETS PRECISELY: Use your sales/import data for the relevant compliance year to compute your mandatory collection targets under the E-Waste Rules, 2022 (targets are phased, starting at lower percentages and escalating annually) — incorrect target calculation is a common audit trigger BUILD AN INTERNAL EPR COMPLIANCE CALENDAR: Use this extension notice as a catalyst to establish a standing compliance calendar for all EPR obligations (e-waste, plastic, battery) with designated owners, milestone dates, and escalation protocols — reactive compliance is increasingly untenable as enforcement intensifies CONNECT EPR DATA TO ESG REPORTING: Begin integrating your e-waste collection and recycling data into your annual ESG or Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report (BRSR) disclosures — SEBI's BRSR framework already references product lifecycle stewardship, and this data will become increasingly material to investor-grade sustainability reporting

Our Take This extension is a pragmatic acknowledgement by CPCB that India's EPR ecosystem — while architecturally sound — is still building the operational capacity needed for universal compliance. Portal technical issues, low awareness among smaller importers, and the sheer complexity of multi-category target calculations have created genuine barriers that the regulator is, for now, accommodating. However, the direction of travel is unmistakable: enforcement is tightening, Environmental Compensation mechanisms are active, and CPCB's public disclosure of non-compliant entities has real reputational consequences. Companies that treat every extension as an opportunity to genuinely strengthen their EPR systems — rather than simply delay the inevitable paperwork — will be the ones best positioned as India's circular economy regulations mature. The e-waste framework is also a bellwether: what CPCB is building here in terms of digital tracking, producer accountability, and lifecycle data will almost certainly inform how India approaches broader climate-related supply chain disclosure requirements in the years ahead. Get compliant now, and get ahead of what is coming next.

title: CPCB Extends E-Waste EPR Return Filing Deadline: What Producers, Importers, and Recyclers Must Do Now
summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent notice extending the timeline for filing annual returns on the E-Waste EPR Portal, providing temporary relief to producers, importers, brand owners, and recyclers struggling with compliance deadlines. While the extension offers breathin

sections.t: Executive Summary The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent notice extending the timeline for filing annual returns on the E-Waste EPR Portal, providing temporary relief to producers, importers, brand owners, and recyclers struggling with compliance deadlines. While the extension offers breathing room, it does not reduce the underlying obligations — companies must use this window to ensure accurate data collection, reconcile EPR certificates, and close any compliance gaps. Failure to file within the revised timeline will attract regulatory action under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022.

What Changed The CPCB has formally notified stakeholders registered on the E-Waste EPR Portal that the deadline for submitting annual returns has been extended beyond the originally prescribed date. This notice, classified as urgent, signals that the regulator acknowledged practical difficulties faced by obligated entities — likely including technical issues on the portal, delays in EPR certificate transactions, or challenges in aggregating collection and recycling data across complex supply chains. Under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, all Producers of Electrical and Electronic Equipment (PEEEs), importers, and authorised recyclers/refurbishers are required to file annual returns detailing quantities of e-waste generated, collected, channelised, and recycled. These returns are filed through the centralised CPCB E-Waste EPR Portal, which also tracks EPR certificate generation and redemption — the core mechanism by which companies demonstrate they have met their Extended Producer Responsibility targets. The extension modifies the filing deadline but does not alter the scope of what must be reported. All data covering the relevant compliance year — production volumes, e-waste collection, recycler linkages, and EPR certificate balances — must still be submitted in full. Stakeholders should monitor the CPCB portal and official communications closely for the revised specific date, as the extracted notice did not confirm a singular new deadline at the time of publication.

Who Is Affected Producers of Electrical and Electronic Equipment (PEEEs) — including manufacturers of consumer electronics, IT equipment, telecom devices, lighting equipment, and large/small household appliances Importers of EEE products sold in India, regardless of company size or origin country Brand Owners whose products are manufactured under licence or on a contract basis in India Authorised E-Waste Recyclers and Refurbishers registered on the CPCB EPR Portal Bulk consumers (large corporates, government bodies) who generate e-waste and have reporting obligations under the Rules Dealers and refurbishers who have taken on EPR obligations by agreement with producers Any entity that has already registered on the E-Waste EPR Portal and received a unique EPR Registration Number

Key Obligations & Deadlines FILE ANNUAL RETURNS: All obligated entities must submit annual returns on the CPCB E-Waste EPR Portal covering production/import volumes, e-waste collected, channelised, recycled, and EPR certificates purchased and redeemed — within the newly extended timeline announced by CPCB MONITOR PORTAL FOR REVISED DATE: The specific extended deadline must be confirmed directly on the CPCB E-Waste EPR Portal (https://eprnew.cpcbccr.com) or via CPCB official circulars — do not rely on third-party sources alone RECONCILE EPR CERTIFICATES: Before filing, ensure all EPR certificates procured from registered recyclers are uploaded, linked, and reconciled against your annual EPR target obligations to avoid shortfall penalties MEET EPR TARGETS: Producers must demonstrate they have met the prescribed EPR collection targets (as per Schedule I of the E-Waste Rules, 2022) — extension of the filing window does not extend the period for actually meeting collection targets AVOID LAPSE IN REGISTRATION: EPR registrations must remain active; companies that have not renewed or updated their registration details should do so immediately alongside filing returns DOCUMENT RECYCLER LINKAGES: Maintain auditable records of agreements with CPCB-authorised recyclers, collection agencies, and PROs (Producer Responsibility Organisations) — these may be called for during scrutiny PENALTY RISK: Non-compliance with return filing obligations can attract suspension of EPR registration, financial penalties, and reputational risk under the Environment Protection Act, 1986 and E-Waste Rules, 2022

Climate Transition Angle E-waste EPR compliance is not merely a regulatory checkbox — it sits at the intersection of India's circular economy ambitions and the global climate transition agenda. Electronic and electrical equipment manufacturing is resource- and energy-intensive, and the improper disposal of e-waste releases toxic substances (lead, mercury, cadmium, brominated flame retardants) that contaminate soil and water systems, compounding physical climate risks in already stressed ecosystems. From a supply chain perspective, the recovery and recycling of critical minerals — lithium, cobalt, copper, rare earth elements — embedded in e-waste is increasingly central to India's strategy for energy transition. The batteries, solar inverters, and power electronics that underpin India's renewable energy buildout rely heavily on these same materials. A robust EPR ecosystem for e-waste is therefore a prerequisite for closing the loop on clean energy supply chains. Globally, frameworks such as the ISSB's IFRS S2 (climate-related disclosures) and the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) are pushing companies to report on resource use, circularity, and waste across their value chains. Indian companies exporting to EU markets or seeking foreign investment must increasingly demonstrate that their e-waste management practices are aligned with international expectations — making CPCB EPR compliance a de facto element of ESG due diligence. The Securities and Exchange Board of India's (SEBI) Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report (BRSR) framework also requires disclosure of hazardous waste including e-waste for listed companies, creating a direct regulatory link between EPR compliance and capital market disclosures.

What To Do Now CONFIRM THE NEW DEADLINE IMMEDIATELY: Assign a compliance officer to check the CPCB E-Waste EPR Portal and the official CPCB website today. Subscribe to portal notifications to receive real-time updates on the revised filing date and any further extensions or technical advisories CONDUCT AN INTERNAL EPR DATA AUDIT: Pull together production/import data, e-waste collection records, recycler certificates, and PRO agreements for the relevant compliance year. Identify and close any data gaps before the extended deadline — last-minute scrambles lead to inaccurate filings and potential penalties VERIFY RECYCLER AUTHORISATIONS: Confirm that all recyclers and refurbishers you have transacted with are currently authorised by CPCB and that the EPR certificates they have issued to you are valid and uploaded on the portal. Certificates from de-registered recyclers will not count toward your EPR target ALIGN BRSR AND EPR DISCLOSURES: If your company is a listed entity, use the EPR filing process as an opportunity to reconcile e-waste data with your BRSR disclosures (Principle 2 — sustainable and safe products). Inconsistencies between regulatory filings and public disclosures create reputational and governance risks ENGAGE YOUR PRO OR COMPLIANCE PARTNER: If you work with a Producer Responsibility Organisation (PRO) or third-party EPR compliance partner, convene an urgent call to review filing readiness, certificate balances, and any portal technical issues that need to be escalated to CPCB before the deadline

Our Take CPCB's decision to extend the e-waste EPR return filing timeline reflects a recurring tension in India's EPR ecosystem: regulatory ambition outpacing operational readiness, particularly the technological and administrative capacity of the centralised portal to handle hundreds of thousands of registered entities simultaneously. While extensions are a practical necessity, they also risk normalising non-compliance for entities that treat each extension as an indefinite delay rather than a firm new boundary. The more important signal here is directional — CPCB is actively enforcing EPR return filing requirements, and the window for voluntary regularisation is finite. We expect the regulator to tighten enforcement significantly in the coming compliance cycles, with greater scrutiny on EPR certificate authenticity (a known area of fraud), recycler authorisation status, and the alignment between declared production volumes and actual collection targets. Companies that invest now in robust EPR data systems, credible recycler partnerships, and integrated sustainability reporting will not only avoid regulatory risk — they will be better positioned as India moves toward a more comprehensive circular economy policy framework, including potential lifecycle assessment requirements and e-waste targets linked to critical mineral recovery.

title: CPCB Issues Directive to Customs Authorities for Release of Imported E-Waste Items: What EPR-Registered Producers Must Know
summary: On 30 June 2023, the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) issued a formal letter to Customs Authorities directing the release of imported consignments belonging to producers of 106 Electrical and Electronic Equipment (EEE) items covered under India's Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framewor

sections.t: Executive Summary On 30 June 2023, the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) issued a formal letter to Customs Authorities directing the release of imported consignments belonging to producers of 106 Electrical and Electronic Equipment (EEE) items covered under India's Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework. This directive signals that CPCB is actively coordinating with border agencies to ensure EPR-compliant producers face no undue trade disruption, while simultaneously reinforcing that EPR registration and compliance are non-negotiable prerequisites for importing covered EEE goods. Companies operating in the electronics and electrical equipment space must treat this as a clear signal that EPR compliance is now a supply chain and customs-clearance issue — not merely a regulatory checkbox.

What Changed The CPCB's letter to Customs Authorities on 30 June 2023 is a critical operational intervention under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, which mandate Extended Producer Responsibility for producers, importers, and brand owners (PIBOs) of 106 categories of Electrical and Electronic Equipment listed in Schedule I of the Rules. Under EPR, producers are legally obligated to register on the CPCB EPR portal, obtain EPR certificates, and meet annual e-waste collection and channelisation targets. The significance of this letter lies at the intersection of trade and environmental regulation: Customs Authorities at Indian ports and airports serve as a practical enforcement point for EPR compliance. When consignments of covered EEE items are held at the border — either because the importer lacks EPR registration or because there is ambiguity about their compliance status — it creates immediate business disruption. CPCB's directive to facilitate the release of consignments for producers who are registered and compliant is both a relief measure and a compliance signal. It confirms that Customs is now an active enforcement partner in the EPR ecosystem, and that unregistered or non-compliant importers risk having their goods held at the border indefinitely. In plain terms: if you import any of the 106 covered EEE items and you are not registered on the CPCB EPR portal with a valid EPR authorisation, your shipments are at risk of being detained at customs. This letter resolves the immediate bottleneck for compliant producers — but it draws a sharper line for those who have not yet registered.

Who Is Affected Producers and importers of any of the 106 EEE item categories listed under Schedule I of the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 — including consumer electronics, IT and telecom equipment, lighting equipment, electrical and electronic tools, and large/small household appliances. Brand owners who place EEE products on the Indian market under their own label, even if manufactured by a third party domestically or abroad. Foreign Original Equipment Manufacturers (OEMs) with Indian subsidiaries or authorised importers who bring in covered electronic goods. Customs brokers and logistics providers handling EEE shipments at Indian ports, ICDs (Inland Container Depots), and airports — as they must now verify EPR compliance status before clearance. Small and Medium Enterprises (SMEs) in the electronics import and distribution segment who may have assumed EPR registration was only applicable to large corporations — this notice dispels that assumption. E-commerce companies and marketplace operators importing electronics inventory directly.

Key Obligations & Deadlines EPR Registration (Immediate): All producers, importers, and brand owners of covered EEE items must register on the CPCB EPR Portal (https://eprnewportal.cpcb.gov.in) and obtain a valid EPR authorisation before importing any covered goods. This is a prerequisite — not an optional step. Annual EPR Target Compliance: Registered PIBOs must meet annual e-waste collection targets as prescribed under the E-Waste Rules, 2022. Targets are set as a percentage of quantity of EEE placed on the market in previous years (Phase I: 60% for Year 1, scaling upward). EPR Certificate Procurement: Producers must procure EPR certificates from CPCB-registered recyclers and dismantlers to demonstrate that e-waste equivalent to their obligation has been responsibly processed. These certificates must be uploaded on the CPCB portal. Customs Documentation: With immediate effect following this notice, companies should ensure that EPR authorisation letters and registration numbers are included in import documentation packages presented to Customs Authorities to facilitate smooth clearance. Annual Filing: PIBOs are required to file annual returns on the CPCB portal detailing quantities of EEE placed on the market and e-waste collected/recycled, within prescribed timelines under the Rules. Deadline for FY 2022-23 Compliance: Producers should have already met their EPR targets for the financial year ending 31 March 2023. Any shortfall must be addressed through extended EPR certificate procurement and reported to CPCB promptly to avoid penalties. Penalty Exposure: Non-compliance with EPR obligations can attract Environmental Compensation (EC) as prescribed under the Rules, in addition to potential detention of imported consignments at Customs.

Climate Transition Angle India's EPR framework for e-waste is not merely a domestic compliance exercise — it sits squarely within the global push for circular economy principles that are central to credible climate transition strategies. The 106 EEE categories covered under these Rules encompass products whose manufacturing is highly resource- and energy-intensive, involving rare earth metals, hazardous materials, and long, complex global supply chains. By mandating that producers take end-of-life responsibility for these products, India is embedding circular economy logic into its industrial policy in a manner increasingly aligned with international frameworks. From a physical risk perspective, informal e-waste recycling — which EPR is designed to displace — contributes directly to soil and water contamination, creating localised environmental degradation that affects community health and, over time, erodes the social licence of companies operating in affected areas. EPR-compliant, formal recycling reduces these risks and builds supply chain resilience by enabling recovery of critical minerals (cobalt, lithium, copper, rare earths) that are essential for the energy transition itself — including batteries for electric vehicles and renewable energy storage. Global ESG reporting standards are increasingly demanding supply chain transparency in this domain. The International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) under IFRS S2 requires companies to disclose material climate-related risks across their value chains. Extended producer responsibility obligations — and the risk of supply chain disruption from customs detention — are precisely the kind of regulatory and operational risks that must be disclosed under these standards. Similarly, the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and its sector-specific standards are expanding obligations for European companies with Indian suppliers or subsidiaries. Indian exporters of electronics components who are not EPR-compliant may find themselves unable to meet the due diligence requirements of their European buyers. This customs-level enforcement by CPCB is, therefore, a preview of the kind of supply chain accountability that global standards will require comprehensively within this decade.

What To Do Now Audit Your Product Portfolio Immediately: Map all EEE products you produce, import, or brand against the 106 categories in Schedule I of the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022. Identify which products are covered and quantify the volumes placed on the Indian market in the preceding financial years — this forms the basis for your EPR obligation calculation. Register or Verify Registration on the CPCB EPR Portal: If your organisation is not yet registered, initiate the registration process on the CPCB EPR portal without delay. If already registered, verify that your authorisation is current, your annual targets are correctly filed, and your EPR certificates are uploaded. Assign a dedicated compliance officer with accountability for this portal. Brief Your Customs and Logistics Teams: Share the CPCB Customs Authority letter reference with your customs brokers, freight forwarders, and import teams. Instruct them to include EPR authorisation documentation in all import clearance packages for covered EEE consignments to avoid detention at ports and ICDs. Engage CPCB-Registered Recyclers and Dismantlers: Establish or review agreements with authorised e-waste processors to ensure you can generate sufficient EPR certificates to meet your annual obligations. Build this into procurement and vendor management processes — do not treat it as an afterthought at year-end. Integrate EPR Compliance into ESG Reporting and Due Diligence: Include your EPR registration status, annual targets, and achievement rates in your BRSR (Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report) disclosures and any voluntary ESG reporting frameworks you follow. Proactively communicate compliance status to investors, buyers, and global partners who are increasingly scrutinising supply chain sustainability credentials.

Our Take CPCB's decision to issue a formal letter to Customs Authorities is a strategic escalation that should not be read narrowly as an administrative facilitation measure. It is, in effect, the regulator making trade access conditional on EPR compliance — a powerful lever that transforms what many companies treated as a back-office environmental obligation into a front-line business continuity issue. The companies that will be most affected are not the large, well-resourced electronics majors who have already built EPR compliance into their operations, but the mid-tier importers, distributors, and brand owners who have delayed registration assuming enforcement would remain lax. That assumption is now definitively invalidated. Looking ahead, we expect CPCB to deepen its coordination with Customs, the Directorate General of Foreign Trade (DGFT), and potentially the Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) to create a multi-agency enforcement ecosystem around covered EEE imports. Companies that invest now in robust EPR compliance infrastructure — including digital tracking of product placement volumes, formal agreements with certified recyclers, and integration of EPR data into sustainability reporting — will not only avoid costly supply chain disruptions but will build the kind of ESG credibility that increasingly influences both market access and capital allocation decisions in a transitioning global economy.

title: CPCB Issues Interim EPR Credit Transfer Framework for Tyre Waste Recyclers: What You Need to Know
summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent interim notice establishing a framework for the generation and transfer of EPR end-product credits (certificates) by tyre waste recyclers in India. This move signals the regulator's intent to operationalise the tyre waste EPR ecosystem

sections.t: Executive Summary The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent interim notice establishing a framework for the generation and transfer of EPR end-product credits (certificates) by tyre waste recyclers in India. This move signals the regulator's intent to operationalise the tyre waste EPR ecosystem more rigorously, creating both compliance obligations and market opportunities for producers, importers, and recyclers. Companies in the tyre manufacturing, automotive, and waste management sectors must act swiftly to align their EPR compliance systems with the updated credit transfer mechanism.

What Changed India's EPR framework for waste tyres — governed under the Hazardous and Other Wastes (Management and Transboundary Movement) Rules and operationalised through CPCB's EPR portal — requires tyre producers and importers to meet annual recycling and recovery targets. To facilitate this, registered tyre waste recyclers generate EPR certificates (end-product credits) that producers can purchase to offset their obligations. The CPCB's interim notice addresses a critical operational gap: the mechanism by which these credits are generated and transferred between recyclers and producers on the centralised EPR portal. The interim arrangement likely introduces a standardised, time-bound process for credit issuance, validation, and transfer — providing regulatory clarity while a permanent system is finalised. This is consistent with CPCB's broader pattern of issuing interim protocols across plastic, battery, and tyre EPR streams as the portal infrastructure matures. In plain terms: if you are a tyre recycler, you now have updated instructions on how to log your recycling output and issue tradeable credits. If you are a tyre producer or importer, you need to ensure your compliance team is purchasing credits through the updated mechanism to avoid shortfalls against your EPR targets.

Who Is Affected Tyre manufacturers (domestic producers) registered under the EPR framework for waste tyres Importers of new tyres and vehicles with tyres, who carry EPR obligations under Indian rules Registered tyre waste recyclers and retreaders who generate EPR end-product credits on the CPCB portal Authorised collection agents and aggregators operating in the waste tyre supply chain Automotive OEMs with take-back or EPR obligations linked to end-of-life tyres EPR compliance consultants and intermediaries facilitating credit transactions on behalf of producers State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs) responsible for oversight of recycler registration and compliance

Key Obligations & Deadlines Tyre waste recyclers must follow the interim procedure outlined in the CPCB notice to generate EPR end-product credits on the centralised portal — non-compliance may result in credits being deemed invalid Recyclers should verify their registration status on the CPCB EPR portal is active and up to date before initiating any credit generation under the new interim arrangement Producers and importers must confirm that EPR credits being purchased or transferred to their accounts are generated under the updated interim mechanism to ensure these credits are recognised for annual target compliance All parties should monitor the CPCB website (cpcb.nic.in) and EPR portal for the transition from interim to permanent arrangements, as timelines for finalisation have not been publicly confirmed Companies should reconcile their EPR obligation targets for the current financial year against available verified credits under the new framework before the annual compliance reporting deadline Recyclers should maintain documentary evidence of all recycling quantities, process outputs, and portal submissions in line with the interim arrangement to withstand SPCB or CPCB audits Deadline for compliance with interim arrangement: Not explicitly stated in available notice information — companies should treat this as immediately effective and seek clarification from CPCB or their SPCB without delay

Climate Transition Angle At first glance, tyre waste EPR may appear to be a narrow regulatory compliance issue. In the broader context of India's climate transition and circular economy ambitions, however, it is anything but. End-of-life tyres are a significant source of carbon emissions and toxic pollutants when incinerated or landfilled illegally. A well-functioning EPR credit market incentivises formal, low-emission recycling — including pyrolysis-derived fuel oil, reclaimed carbon black, and steel wire recovery — directly displacing virgin, carbon-intensive raw materials. From a supply chain perspective, global automotive and tyre manufacturers face mounting pressure under frameworks such as the ISSB's IFRS S2 (climate-related disclosures) and the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) to disclose and reduce Scope 3 emissions, including those associated with product end-of-life. A credible, auditable EPR credit system in India — exactly what CPCB is working to build — provides the data infrastructure needed to substantiate these disclosures. Furthermore, as India advances its National Action Plan on Climate Change and circular economy policy, robust EPR enforcement for waste streams like tyres is a prerequisite for attracting green finance, meeting Extended Producer Responsibility commitments under international trade agreements, and demonstrating regulatory equivalence with EU and US sustainability standards that multinational supply chain partners increasingly require.

What To Do Now Download and review the full CPCB interim notice immediately: Access the official PDF at cpcb.nic.in and circulate it to your EPR compliance lead, legal team, and any registered recycling partners — do not rely on secondary summaries for a regulatory notice of this nature Audit your EPR portal account: Both producers and recyclers should log into the CPCB EPR portal, verify account details, and identify any pending credit generation or transfer transactions that may need to be reprocessed under the interim arrangement Engage your recycler network proactively: Producers who depend on third-party recyclers to generate credits on their behalf should communicate the new interim requirements immediately and confirm that recyclers are compliant — a recycler's non-compliance directly creates a gap in your own EPR obligations Map your annual EPR target against verified credit availability: With the financial year progressing, conduct a gap analysis now — how many tonnes of tyre waste must you account for, how many valid credits do you currently hold, and what additional procurement is needed under the updated mechanism? Build a regulatory monitoring cadence: CPCB's EPR frameworks are evolving rapidly across all waste streams. Assign a team member or engage an external ESG/compliance advisor to track CPCB portal updates, circulars, and notices on a weekly basis to avoid being caught off guard by future interim or permanent changes

Our Take This interim notice is a microcosm of a larger story unfolding across India's EPR landscape: the regulator is serious about making EPR credit markets functional, transparent, and enforceable, but the infrastructure is still catching up to the policy ambition. The fact that CPCB is issuing interim arrangements — rather than waiting for a perfect, permanent solution — reflects pragmatic urgency and should be read as a signal that enforcement pressure on producers and recyclers will only intensify. Companies that treat EPR compliance as a box-ticking exercise, purchasing credits at the last minute without robust documentation, are accumulating regulatory risk. The smarter play is to embed EPR credit management into your supply chain governance now, build direct relationships with verified, high-quality recyclers, and position your organisation to benefit from what will eventually become a liquid, price-transparent tyre waste credit market — one that sophisticated investors will scrutinise as part of broader ESG due diligence.

title: CPCB Issues Urgent Notice to Producers on E-Waste Management Rules, 2022: What You Must Do Now
summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent notice to producers — including manufacturers, importers, and brand owners of electrical and electronic equipment — directing them to comply with their obligations under the E-Waste Management Rules, 2022. Companies that have not yet re

sections.t: Executive Summary The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent notice to producers — including manufacturers, importers, and brand owners of electrical and electronic equipment — directing them to comply with their obligations under the E-Waste Management Rules, 2022. Companies that have not yet registered on the EPR portal, set collection targets, or filed returns are now at heightened regulatory risk. Non-compliance can attract penalties, production stoppages, and reputational damage under India's strengthening extended producer responsibility (EPR) framework.

What Changed The E-Waste Management Rules, 2022 replaced the earlier 2016 rules and introduced a significantly more structured Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework for electrical and electronic equipment (EEE). Under EPR, producers — defined broadly to include manufacturers, importers, and brand owners selling EEE in India — are legally required to take back and channel end-of-life products through registered recyclers and dismantlers. The 2022 rules also introduced EPR certificates as a market-based compliance instrument, allowing producers to meet targets by purchasing certificates from registered recyclers. The CPCB's urgent notice signals that the regulator has identified a significant gap in compliance — many producers have either not registered on the centralised EPR portal, have not set or met their annual collection targets, or have failed to submit the mandatory annual returns. This notice is a formal warning that enforcement action is imminent for defaulting entities. It also reflects CPCB's broader push to operationalise EPR across waste streams (plastic, battery, tyre, and e-waste) as a cornerstone of India's circular economy agenda.

Who Is Affected Manufacturers of electrical and electronic equipment (EEE) with operations or sales in India — including consumer electronics, IT and telecom equipment, large and small household appliances, lighting equipment, and medical devices covered under Schedule I of the Rules Importers of EEE — any company bringing finished electronic goods or components into India for sale Brand owners — companies that place EEE products in the Indian market under their own brand, even if manufactured by a third party E-commerce platforms and producers selling directly to consumers online who may have obligations as brand owners or importers Producer Responsibility Organisations (PROs) managing EPR compliance on behalf of producers Small and medium enterprises (SMEs) in the electronics sector — the Rules apply regardless of company size if products fall under the notified categories

Key Obligations & Deadlines REGISTRATION (Immediate): All producers, importers, and brand owners must register on the CPCB's centralised EPR portal (https://eprewaste.cpcb.gov.in). Unregistered entities are in direct violation of Rule 5 of the E-Waste Management Rules, 2022. EPR TARGETS: Producers must set and fulfil annual EPR targets for e-waste collection, channelisation, and recycling as prescribed under Schedule II of the Rules. Targets are based on sales volumes from previous years. EPR CERTIFICATE PROCUREMENT: Where producers cannot meet collection targets through their own systems, they must procure EPR certificates from CPCB-registered recyclers or dismantlers within the compliance year. ANNUAL RETURNS: Producers are required to file annual returns on the EPR portal detailing quantities of EEE placed on the market, e-waste collected, and EPR certificates held. Returns for the previous financial year must be filed promptly — check the portal for the current deadline. TAKE-BACK SYSTEMS: Producers must establish or participate in a system for collecting e-waste from consumers, including through collection centres, reverse logistics, or PRO partnerships. Evidence of operational take-back mechanisms must be maintained. RECORD-KEEPING: Maintain records of all e-waste generated, collected, transported, and channelised to authorised dismantlers/recyclers for a minimum of 5 years and make these available to CPCB/State PCBs on demand. NON-COMPLIANCE RISK: CPCB has indicated enforcement action including cancellation of EPR registration, financial penalties under the Environment Protection Act, 1986, and potential suspension of production or import licences for persistent defaulters.

Climate Transition Angle E-waste compliance is not merely a regulatory checkbox — it sits at the intersection of climate risk, supply chain resilience, and global sustainability standards. The extraction of virgin raw materials for electronics (rare earth metals, cobalt, lithium, copper) is energy-intensive and carbon-heavy. A well-functioning EPR system for e-waste creates the infrastructure for a domestic circular economy, reducing dependence on imported virgin materials and cutting embodied carbon in the electronics value chain. For companies aligned with or preparing for global frameworks — including the ISSB's IFRS S2 (climate-related disclosures), the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) for entities with European exposure, and India's own BRSR Core mandatory disclosures for listed companies — e-waste management is increasingly a material topic. Supply chain Scope 3 emissions, responsible sourcing, and circular economy practices are now standard expectations from institutional investors and multinational buyers. Physical climate risks also intersect here: flooding, heat stress, and extreme weather events disrupt e-waste collection logistics and informal recycling clusters concentrated in cities like Delhi, Mumbai, and Bengaluru. Producers who build robust, formalised take-back systems now are better insulated against supply chain shocks as climate volatility intensifies. Furthermore, the EU's Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive (CS3D) and similar regulations will soon require Indian exporters and suppliers to demonstrate circular economy compliance — making CPCB EPR compliance a de facto export-readiness requirement.

What To Do Now AUDIT YOUR EPR STATUS IMMEDIATELY: Conduct an internal compliance audit to confirm your registration status on the CPCB EPR portal, verify that your product categories and sales volumes are correctly declared, and assess whether your EPR targets for the current financial year are on track to be met. APPOINT OR REVIEW YOUR PRO ARRANGEMENT: If you are using a Producer Responsibility Organisation (PRO) to manage EPR obligations, obtain a written status report confirming collection activities, recycler partnerships, and certificate balances. If you do not have a PRO, engage one or build an in-house take-back system without delay. PROCURE EPR CERTIFICATES TO BRIDGE GAPS: If your collection targets cannot be met through physical take-back before the year-end, immediately engage CPCB-registered recyclers to procure EPR certificates. Delays in certificate procurement are a common compliance gap — act before the financial year closes. INTEGRATE E-WASTE KPIs INTO BRSR AND SUSTAINABILITY REPORTING: Map your e-waste collection and recycling data to BRSR Core Principle 2 (Sustainable and Safe Products) disclosures and to ISSB/TCFD-aligned climate reporting on circular economy and Scope 3 value chain impacts — this strengthens investor and buyer confidence. ESTABLISH A REGULATORY MONITORING PROCESS: Assign a dedicated compliance owner to track CPCB portal notifications, EPR rule amendments, and State PCB directives. Given the pace of regulatory activity across EPR categories (plastic, battery, tyre, e-waste), a fragmented monitoring approach creates avoidable risk.

Our Take CPCB's urgent notice is a clear signal that India's EPR enforcement era has well and truly begun. For years, e-waste rules existed largely on paper, with low registration rates and minimal enforcement action. That era is ending. The CPCB has invested significantly in its digital EPR portal infrastructure, and the issuance of formal urgent notices to producers — with implied threats of production and import licence consequences — marks a qualitative shift in regulatory intent. We expect this to be followed by show-cause notices to specific defaulters, and potentially publicised enforcement actions, as CPCB seeks to establish credibility ahead of EPR frameworks for additional waste categories. For business leaders, the message is unambiguous: e-waste EPR compliance is now a board-level risk topic, not an operational afterthought. Companies that treat this as a compliance-first, strategy-second opportunity — building circular supply chains, engaging certified recyclers, and transparently reporting performance — will be better positioned for the regulatory, investor, and customer scrutiny that is coming at pace.

title: CPCB Notice to E-Waste Recyclers and Producers: GST-Linked E-Invoicing Now Mandatory Under EPR Rules 2022
summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued a binding notice requiring all e-waste recyclers to generate EPR Certificates exclusively through GST-linked e-invoices, and mandating that producers accept only such GST-authenticated invoices as valid proof of recycling. This tightens the trace

sections.t: Executive Summary The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued a binding notice requiring all e-waste recyclers to generate EPR Certificates exclusively through GST-linked e-invoices, and mandating that producers accept only such GST-authenticated invoices as valid proof of recycling. This tightens the traceability and accountability framework under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, closing a significant loophole where unverified paper records could be used to fulfil Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) obligations. Companies that fail to align their invoicing and record-keeping systems with this requirement risk invalidation of their EPR credits and potential non-compliance penalties.

What Changed The E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, notified by the Ministry of Environment, Forest & Climate Change on November 2, 2022 (G.S.R 801(E)), established a digital EPR Portal through which recyclers must generate EPR Certificates — the documents that prove to producers that a certain quantity of e-waste has been responsibly recycled. Until now, the specific format and authentication standard for the underlying invoices was not explicitly enforced. The CPCB's notice dated June 15, 2023, issued by the WM-III Division, closes this gap with two clear mandates: First, recyclers must now maintain records of all sales and purchases — covering both recovered end-products (such as metals, plastics) and raw e-waste inputs — using e-invoices generated through the GST Portal. Second, producers must only accept GST-linked invoices from recyclers as proof of recycling when claiming EPR compliance. In essence, the EPR ecosystem is now anchored to India's GST infrastructure, making every transaction digitally traceable and cross-verifiable by tax and environmental authorities simultaneously.

Who Is Affected Registered E-Waste Recyclers: All recyclers registered on the CPCB EPR Portal who process e-waste and sell recovered materials — regardless of scale or geography. Producers of Electrical and Electronic Equipment (EEE): All companies that manufacture or import EEE covered under the E-Waste Rules and are obligated to meet annual EPR targets for e-waste collection and recycling. Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) Compliance Teams: In-house sustainability, legal, and finance teams at electronics OEMs, IT hardware companies, consumer electronics brands, and white goods manufacturers. EPR Compliance Service Providers & PROs: Producer Responsibility Organisations (PROs) that aggregate EPR obligations and work with recyclers on behalf of producers. CFOs and Finance Teams at Recycling Units: Since GST e-invoicing requirements may mandate a minimum turnover threshold compliance under GST law, finance teams must assess their e-invoicing eligibility and readiness. Importers and Brand Licence Holders: Companies importing EEE into India and holding EPR obligations under the Rules.

Key Obligations & Deadlines IMMEDIATE — Recyclers must generate GST-linked e-invoices for ALL sales and purchases of recovered materials (end-products) and raw e-waste through the GST Portal, with effect from the notice date of June 15, 2023. IMMEDIATE — Recyclers must link these GST e-invoices to their EPR Certificate generation process on the CPCB EPR Portal. EPR Certificates generated without this documentation linkage may be considered invalid. IMMEDIATE — Producers must update their internal procurement and EPR compliance verification protocols to only accept GST-authenticated invoices from recyclers as proof of recycling. Paper invoices or non-GST records will no longer suffice. ONGOING — Recyclers must maintain auditable records of all GST e-invoices related to e-waste transactions and make these available for CPCB inspection or EPR audit purposes. ONGOING — Producers must reconcile EPR credits claimed on the portal against GST-verified recycler invoices, ensuring no discrepancy between reported recycling quantities and authenticated transaction records. REVIEW — Finance and compliance teams at recycler units must verify whether they meet the GST e-invoicing turnover threshold (currently ₹5 crore and above as per GST law) and take steps to comply with GST e-invoicing regulations if not already doing so.

Climate Transition Angle This regulatory tightening is far more than an administrative housekeeping measure — it sits at the intersection of India's circular economy ambitions and its commitments under global climate frameworks. E-waste is one of the fastest-growing waste streams globally, and the responsible recovery of critical minerals — lithium, cobalt, copper, rare earth elements — from e-waste is increasingly recognised as essential to building a resilient, low-carbon economy. Without credible traceability in recycling chains, the circular economy narrative risks becoming 'greenwashing by default.' From a supply chain risk perspective, global buyers and investors are increasingly demanding proof of responsible material sourcing. The EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and the ISSB's IFRS S2 climate disclosure standards both require companies to report on supply chain Scope 3 emissions and resource circularity. Indian exporters and suppliers to multinational corporations will face growing pressure to demonstrate that their EPR compliance is backed by verifiable data — exactly what GST-linked e-invoicing enables. Physical climate risks are also relevant here: extreme heat, flooding, and erratic monsoons disrupt informal e-waste collection and recycling operations disproportionately. As India's formal recycling sector grows under regulatory pressure, companies investing in formalised, GST-compliant recycling partners are simultaneously building supply chain resilience against these climate-driven disruptions. The CPCB's move to anchor EPR to GST infrastructure is, in this sense, a foundational step toward climate-aligned material traceability.

What To Do Now Audit Your Recycler Network Immediately: Producers should contact all empanelled recyclers and PROs to confirm they are GST-registered, e-invoice compliant, and have updated their processes on the CPCB EPR Portal. Any recycler unable to provide GST e-invoices should be flagged as a compliance risk. Update Internal EPR Verification Protocols: Revise your EPR proof-of-recycling acceptance policy in writing — legal and compliance teams should formally document that only GST-linked e-invoices will be accepted, and communicate this to procurement and sustainability teams. Integrate GST and EPR Portal Workflows (Recyclers): If you are a recycler, work with your IT or ERP team to ensure that e-invoices generated on the GST Portal can be referenced directly when uploading EPR Certificate data on the CPCB portal. Avoid manual data re-entry, which increases error and audit risk. Conduct a Gap Assessment on Historical EPR Credits: Review EPR credits already claimed or pending for FY 2022-23 and assess whether the underlying recycler invoices meet the new GST e-invoice standard. Where gaps exist, consult legal counsel on remediation steps before the next compliance cycle. Engage Your CFO and Tax Team: Since this requirement bridges environmental and tax compliance, ensure your CFO and GST/tax team are aware of the obligation. For recyclers near or below the e-invoicing turnover threshold, proactively assess whether voluntary GST e-invoicing adoption is needed to remain a credible EPR partner.

Our Take This notice from CPCB is deceptively significant. On the surface, it looks like a procedural update about invoicing formats. In reality, it represents a structural shift in how India's EPR ecosystem will function: by tethering environmental compliance to the GST backbone, regulators are creating a dual-verification system where tax authorities and environmental enforcement agencies can cross-reference data simultaneously. This makes fraudulent EPR certificate generation — a known problem in the e-waste sector — exponentially harder. For responsible producers and recyclers, this is welcome news; it levels the playing field and protects the integrity of the EPR market. For laggards still relying on informal or paper-based recycling arrangements, the window to get compliant is closing rapidly. We expect CPCB to follow this notice with enforcement actions targeting EPR certificates that cannot be backed by GST-verified transactions, and we anticipate further integration of EPR compliance data with MCA and SEBI disclosure requirements as India's ESG regulatory architecture matures. Companies should treat this not as a one-time compliance task, but as a signal to invest in robust, digitally traceable EPR supply chains now.

title: CPCB Notice to E-Waste Recyclers and Producers: GST-Linked E-Invoicing Now Mandatory for EPR Compliance
summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) issued a critical notice on June 15, 2023, mandating that e-waste recyclers must now use GST-linked e-invoices for all sales and purchases of recovered materials and e-waste in order to generate valid EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility) Certificates on t

sections.t: Executive Summary The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) issued a critical notice on June 15, 2023, mandating that e-waste recyclers must now use GST-linked e-invoices for all sales and purchases of recovered materials and e-waste in order to generate valid EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility) Certificates on the EPR Portal. Producers are simultaneously required to accept only GST-linked invoices from recyclers as legally recognised proof of recycling. This effectively closes a documentation loophole and tightens the audit trail across India's formal e-waste recycling chain.

What Changed The E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, notified by the Ministry of Environment, Forest & Climate Change on November 2, 2022, established a digital EPR Portal through which recyclers generate EPR Certificates — the instruments that allow producers to demonstrate they have met their mandatory e-waste collection and recycling targets. Until this notice, the documentation standards for transactions on the EPR Portal were not explicitly harmonised with India's GST e-invoicing framework. The CPCB's June 15, 2023 directive now makes GST-linked e-invoices the sole acceptable documentation for: (1) recyclers recording the purchase of e-waste and the sale of recovered/end-product materials on the EPR Portal, and (2) producers accepting proof of recycling from those recyclers. In plain terms: if a recycler cannot show a GST e-invoice for their e-waste transactions, they cannot generate a legitimate EPR Certificate. And if a producer accepts non-GST documentation from a recycler, that recycling activity will not count toward the producer's EPR obligation.

Who Is Affected Registered E-Waste Recyclers: All recyclers authorised under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 who process and recover materials from electrical and electronic equipment (EEE). Producers of Electrical and Electronic Equipment (EEE): Any company — domestic or multinational — that manufactures, imports, or sells EEE products listed in Schedule I of the 2022 Rules (covering consumer electronics, IT equipment, lighting, medical devices, etc.). Producer Responsibility Organisations (PROs): Entities that facilitate EPR compliance on behalf of multiple producers and coordinate with recyclers — their documentation flows are directly impacted. Bulk Consumers and Collection Agents: While the notice is addressed to recyclers and producers, bulk consumers who channel e-waste to recyclers should ensure their own transaction records are GST-compliant to maintain a clean chain of custody. Large and Mid-Sized Manufacturers: Companies with significant annual production volumes and correspondingly large EPR targets face the highest financial exposure if recycling claims are invalidated due to non-compliant invoicing. SME Recyclers: Smaller recycling units that may not yet be fully integrated into the GST e-invoicing system face the greatest operational adjustment and risk of EPR Certificate invalidation.

Key Obligations & Deadlines IMMEDIATE — Recyclers must transition all sales and purchase transactions related to e-waste and recovered/end-product materials to GST e-invoices generated through the GST Portal. Non-GST or manual invoices will no longer be valid for EPR Portal submissions. IMMEDIATE — Recyclers must link these GST e-invoices to their records on the CPCB EPR Portal as the basis for generating EPR Certificates. Ensure your EPR Portal account is configured to upload or reference GST invoice data. IMMEDIATE — Producers must update their internal procurement and compliance verification processes to accept only GST-linked sales invoices from e-waste recyclers as valid proof of recycling toward EPR targets. ONGOING — Producers and PROs must audit existing recycler agreements and contracts to include a contractual requirement for GST e-invoice compliance, creating a legal basis for rejecting non-compliant documentation. COMPLIANCE REVIEW — Companies should conduct an internal audit of all EPR Certificates already generated and recycling claims already submitted in FY 2022-23 to assess whether documentation meets the new GST-linked standard, and proactively engage CPCB if gaps are identified. REGISTRATION CHECK — Recyclers not yet registered for GST e-invoicing (applicable to businesses with annual turnover above the notified threshold) must complete registration immediately. Those below the threshold should seek legal clarity on applicability. DEADLINE CONTEXT — While no grace period is specified in the notice (effective June 15, 2023), EPR targets for FY 2023-24 will be assessed against this standard; non-compliant recycling transactions risk being excluded from target calculations.

Climate Transition Angle E-waste is one of the fastest-growing waste streams globally, and the recovery of critical minerals — lithium, cobalt, copper, rare earth elements — from end-of-life electronics is increasingly central to the climate transition. India's ability to build a circular economy for these materials directly supports domestic manufacturing of EVs, solar panels, and energy storage systems, reducing dependence on virgin mineral extraction and the associated carbon footprint. This CPCB notice, while procedural in nature, is part of a broader global tightening of traceability standards in recycling and resource recovery. The European Union's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and the ISSB's IFRS S2 climate disclosure standards both require companies to account for material risks and circularity in their supply chains. For multinational producers selling into India, failure to maintain rigorous, auditable EPR compliance records creates a supply chain transparency gap that will increasingly be scrutinised by global investors and regulators. From a physical risk perspective, India's dependence on imported critical minerals makes domestic recycling infrastructure a strategic climate resilience asset. Strengthening the formal recycling sector through enforceable documentation standards — as this notice does — is a foundational step toward reducing that import dependency and building supply chain resilience against resource scarcity scenarios projected under 2°C and higher warming pathways. Companies with Science-Based Targets (SBTs) or net-zero commitments should also note that scope 3 emissions accounting increasingly includes the end-of-life treatment of sold products. A credible, GST-verified EPR compliance record provides the documentation backbone for such scope 3 disclosures.

What To Do Now Recyclers: Register or verify your GST e-invoicing capability immediately — contact your GST Suvidha Provider (GSP) or the GST Portal helpdesk. Ensure your billing software or ERP system is configured to generate e-invoices for all e-waste purchase and recovered material sale transactions, and that invoice reference numbers can be uploaded to the CPCB EPR Portal. Producers & PROs: Issue a formal compliance advisory to all your registered recycling partners requiring GST e-invoice submission with immediate effect. Update vendor onboarding checklists and recycler empanelment criteria to make GST e-invoice compliance a mandatory qualification. Legal & Compliance Teams: Amend recycler contracts and PRO agreements to include a clause explicitly requiring GST e-invoice documentation for all EPR-related transactions, with a right to reject non-compliant claims. Specify that the producer/PRO bears no liability for EPR shortfalls arising from a recycler's failure to comply. Sustainability & Finance Teams: Cross-check your FY 2022-23 EPR Certificate portfolio against the new documentation standard. If any certificates were generated without GST-linked invoices, consult with your legal team and consider voluntary disclosure to CPCB to avoid penalties during the next compliance assessment cycle. IT & Data Teams: Build a data linkage between your GST invoice repository and your EPR Portal submissions to enable real-time verification and streamline annual EPR reporting. This integration will also support future ESG and sustainability disclosures that require auditable recycling data.

Our Take This notice may read like a technical clarification, but its significance should not be underestimated. By anchoring EPR Certificate generation to the GST e-invoicing infrastructure, CPCB is effectively bringing the weight of India's tax enforcement architecture to bear on e-waste compliance — a smart and overdue move. The informal recycling sector has long been a weak link in India's e-waste chain, with a substantial portion of e-waste being processed outside the formal system. Requiring GST e-invoices creates a hard filter: recyclers operating informally or without proper GST registration simply cannot generate valid EPR Certificates, which in turn means producers cannot use their output to meet EPR targets. For producers, this raises the stakes considerably — your EPR compliance is now only as strong as your recyclers' GST compliance. We expect CPCB to follow this notice with stricter scrutiny of EPR Portal submissions and potentially enhanced penalties for producers found to have claimed recycling credits based on non-GST documentation. Companies that proactively audit their recycler networks and upgrade their documentation systems now will be well-positioned — those that wait risk EPR shortfalls, penalties, and reputational exposure as India's ESG regulatory environment continues to harden.

title: CPCB Puts Recyclers on Notice: What the E-Waste Management Rules 2022 Compliance Crackdown Means for Your Business
summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent notice to registered e-waste recyclers across India, signalling heightened regulatory scrutiny over compliance with the E-Waste Management Rules, 2022. Recyclers who have not met their Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) obligations

sections.t: Executive Summary The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent notice to registered e-waste recyclers across India, signalling heightened regulatory scrutiny over compliance with the E-Waste Management Rules, 2022. Recyclers who have not met their Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) obligations — including collection targets, channelisation records, and certificate issuance — now face potential suspension or cancellation of registration. This notice is a clear signal that India's e-waste enforcement regime is tightening, and the entire EPR value chain — from producers to dismantlers to recyclers — must treat compliance as a board-level priority.

What Changed The E-Waste Management Rules, 2022, which came into force replacing the earlier 2016 rules, introduced a significantly more structured EPR framework. Under this framework, Producers, Importers, and Brand Owners (PIBOs) are required to meet annual e-waste collection and recycling targets, and they must do so by sourcing EPR certificates from CPCB-registered recyclers and dismantlers. The entire system is operationalised through a centralised EPR portal managed by CPCB. The CPCB's latest urgent notice targets registered recyclers who appear to be falling short of their obligations under these rules. Non-compliances likely include failure to upload accurate processing data on the EPR portal, inability to issue valid EPR certificates to producers, inadequate record-keeping of material channelisation, and operating below permitted capacities. By issuing this notice, CPCB is formally putting recyclers on record — non-response or continued non-compliance could trigger enforcement action including show-cause notices, financial penalties, and deregistration. This is not a routine advisory. An 'Urgent Notice' classification from CPCB indicates that the regulator has already identified specific gaps and is demanding corrective action within a defined timeframe. Recyclers receiving this notice must treat it as a legal document requiring an immediate, documented response.

Who Is Affected CPCB-registered e-waste recyclers and dismantlers across all states, particularly those with pending or incomplete EPR portal submissions Producers, Importers, and Brand Owners (PIBOs) of Electrical and Electronic Equipment (EEE) — including consumer electronics, IT equipment, white goods, and telecom hardware — who rely on recyclers to fulfil their own EPR targets E-waste aggregators and collection point operators whose downstream recycling partners are affected Companies in the electronics manufacturing sector — including contract manufacturers and component suppliers — with EPR obligations under Schedule I of the 2022 Rules Investors and lenders with exposure to e-waste recycling businesses, where regulatory non-compliance creates material financial and reputational risk Sustainability and ESG teams at large corporates whose Scope 3 or product stewardship disclosures depend on verified e-waste recycling data
