Key Obligations & Deadlines Registered recyclers must ensure all e-waste processing data — quantity received, processed, and channelised — is accurately uploaded and up to date on the CPCB EPR portal (cpcbepr.parivesh.nic.in) EPR certificates must be generated and issued to PIBOs in a timely manner upon verified processing; backlog in certificate issuance must be cleared immediately Recyclers must maintain and be prepared to submit physical records of inward and outward material movement, including weight slips, invoices, and Form 3/Form 4 records as applicable under the 2022 Rules Any discrepancy between declared capacity and actual throughput must be explained in a written response to CPCB, with supporting documentation PIBOs should immediately audit their recycler partners' compliance status on the EPR portal — reliance on non-compliant recyclers puts the producer's own EPR target fulfilment at risk Recyclers who have received the notice must respond within the timeframe specified in the notice (typically 15–30 days); failure to respond is treated as admission of non-compliance Annual EPR targets for FY 2023–24 and FY 2024–25 must be reconciled; shortfalls may require additional collection or payment into the EPR fund as per CPCB guidelines All recyclers should verify their registration validity and renewal status — lapsed registrations must be renewed before resuming operations or issuing certificates

Climate Transition Angle E-waste is not merely a compliance issue — it sits at the intersection of circular economy imperatives, critical mineral supply chains, and India's broader climate transition goals. Electronic devices contain recoverable quantities of gold, silver, copper, cobalt, lithium, and rare earth elements. Informal or unverified recycling not only creates toxic pollution but also means these materials are lost from the formal circular economy, increasing demand for virgin mining — a carbon-intensive process with significant biodiversity and water risks. From a global standards perspective, the ISSB's IFRS S2 climate disclosures and the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) both require companies to assess and disclose Scope 3 emissions, which include end-of-life treatment of sold products. Indian companies exporting to Europe or listed on global exchanges must be able to demonstrate that their e-waste is processed by verified, compliant recyclers — otherwise, their sustainability disclosures lack credibility and their supply chains face audit risk. India's National Action Plan on Climate Change and the country's commitment to net zero by 2070 both implicitly depend on building robust secondary materials markets. A well-functioning EPR system for e-waste is foundational infrastructure for this transition. CPCB's enforcement action, therefore, is not just about rule compliance — it is about building the institutional trust that makes India's circular economy investable and credible on the global stage. Physical climate risks are also relevant here: rising temperatures increase the rate at which electronic equipment fails and is discarded, and extreme weather events (floods, cyclones) can destroy unprocessed e-waste stockpiles, creating acute pollution incidents. Recyclers operating in climate-vulnerable geographies must factor these physical risks into their storage and operational continuity planning.

What To Do Now RECYCLERS — Conduct an immediate internal audit: Log into the CPCB EPR portal today, verify all submitted data for accuracy, identify any gaps in processing records or certificate issuance, and prepare a compliance rectification plan with clear owner accountability and a 2-week completion target PRODUCERS & BRAND OWNERS (PIBOs) — Run a partner compliance check: Pull the list of all recyclers you have sourced EPR certificates from in FY 2023–24 and FY 2024–25 and cross-verify their active registration and portal submission status; if your recycler partners are flagged by CPCB, your own EPR fulfilment is at legal risk and you need contingency sourcing arrangements immediately LEGAL & COMPLIANCE TEAMS — Review your notice response protocol: If your organisation has received the CPCB urgent notice, engage environmental law counsel within 48 hours, prepare a structured written response addressing each point of alleged non-compliance, and document all corrective actions taken — this response becomes part of your regulatory record SUSTAINABILITY TEAMS — Align EPR data with ESG disclosures: Ensure that e-waste collection and recycling volumes reported in your Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report (BRSR), GRI disclosures, or CDP submissions are consistent with data filed on the CPCB EPR portal; any discrepancy is a red flag in third-party audits CFOs & RISK OFFICERS — Quantify EPR liability: Calculate the financial exposure from any shortfall in EPR targets, including potential penalties under the Environment Protection Act and the cost of purchasing additional EPR certificates in the secondary market; provision for this in your Q4 risk register if not already done

Our Take This notice marks a meaningful inflection point in India's e-waste governance. For years, the EPR framework existed largely on paper — registration was obtained, targets were notionally assigned, but enforcement was inconsistent and data quality on the portal was poor. CPCB's decision to issue an urgent, public-facing compliance notice to recyclers signals that the regulator is moving from a registration-and-report model to an active enforcement posture. We expect this to be followed by physical inspections, show-cause notices to persistent non-compliers, and potentially a public blacklist of deregistered recyclers — similar to enforcement patterns we have seen in the plastic EPR space. For producers, the message is equally clear: you cannot outsource your EPR responsibility to a recycler and walk away. Due diligence on your recycling partners is now a compliance obligation, not just a best practice. Companies that build robust, auditable EPR supply chains now will be better positioned as India's environmental disclosure requirements — including SEBI's BRSR Core — become more stringent, and as global customers and investors demand verified circular economy credentials.

title: CPCB's Interim EPR Credit Arrangement for Tyre Waste Recyclers: What Producers and Recyclers Must Know Now
summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent interim notice governing how tyre waste recyclers generate and transfer EPR end-product credits (certificates) under India's Extended Producer Responsibility framework for waste tyres. This interim arrangement signals that the formal di

sections.t: Executive Summary The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent interim notice governing how tyre waste recyclers generate and transfer EPR end-product credits (certificates) under India's Extended Producer Responsibility framework for waste tyres. This interim arrangement signals that the formal digital EPR portal mechanism for tyres is still being operationalised, and both recyclers and tyre producers must act under transitional rules to remain compliant. Companies that ignore this notice risk gaps in their EPR obligation fulfilment, exposing them to regulatory penalties and reputational risk.

What Changed India's EPR framework for waste tyres, notified under the Hazardous and Other Wastes (Management and Transboundary Movement) Rules and subsequently operationalised through CPCB circulars, requires tyre producers, importers, and brand owners (PIBOs) to ensure the collection and processing of a specified tonnage of end-of-life tyres proportional to their sales volumes. Recyclers who process this waste generate EPR credits — certificates that PIBOs purchase or claim to demonstrate compliance with their annual targets. However, the full digital infrastructure for seamless credit generation and transfer on the centralised EPR portal for tyres has not yet been fully deployed. In response, CPCB has issued this urgent notice to establish an interim arrangement — a transitional mechanism that defines how recyclers can formally generate credits and how those credits can be transferred to PIBOs while the permanent system is being finalised. This is essentially a bridging protocol: it legitimises credit transactions during the gap period, preventing a compliance vacuum for all parties in the tyre waste value chain. The interim arrangement likely prescribes specific documentation requirements, formats for credit certificates, a defined process for submission to CPCB or the relevant State Pollution Control Board (SPCB), and timelines within which these interim credits will be recognised. Recyclers and PIBOs should treat this notice as legally binding and operationally urgent — interim does not mean optional.

Who Is Affected Tyre Producers, Importers, and Brand Owners (PIBOs): All entities with EPR obligations under the waste tyre rules who need to procure or account for EPR credits to meet their annual targets. Registered Tyre Waste Recyclers: Facilities registered with CPCB/SPCBs that process end-of-life tyres and generate EPR credits for sale or transfer to PIBOs. Retreaders and Co-processors: Entities involved in alternative end-of-life tyre processing who may also be eligible for credit generation under the framework. EPR Compliance and Sustainability Teams: In-house and outsourced compliance functions at tyre manufacturers (e.g., MRF, Apollo, CEAT, JK Tyre, Bridgestone India) and importers of tyres. EPR Aggregators and Intermediaries: Third-party organisations that facilitate credit transactions between recyclers and PIBOs. Automobile OEMs with tyre import obligations: Vehicle manufacturers who import tyres as part of CKD/SKD kits and carry associated EPR liability.

Key Obligations & Deadlines Immediate Review: All tyre waste recyclers must review the full CPCB interim notice to understand the prescribed format for generating EPR end-product credit certificates under the transitional arrangement. Documentation Compliance: Recyclers must ensure all interim credit certificates are generated using the exact format and data fields specified by CPCB — deviations may render credits invalid for PIBO compliance purposes. Credit Transfer Protocol: Recyclers and PIBOs must follow the interim transfer mechanism (likely involving physical or email-based submission to CPCB/SPCB) until the digital portal is fully operational. Record-Keeping: Both recyclers and PIBOs must maintain complete records of all interim credit transactions — quantities, dates, certificate numbers, and counterparty details — as these will need to be reconciled once the permanent portal goes live. Annual EPR Target Tracking: PIBOs must map interim credits received against their annual EPR obligation targets and flag any shortfalls to their compliance teams immediately, given that year-end reconciliation windows may be tight. Deadline Monitoring: While the notice does not appear to specify a fixed end-date for the interim arrangement, companies should monitor CPCB's portal (cpcb.nic.in) and official communications closely for the announcement of the permanent system go-live, which will trigger new obligations. SPCB Coordination: Recyclers should confirm with their respective SPCBs whether state-level endorsement or acknowledgement of interim credits is required in their jurisdiction.

Climate Transition Angle The EPR framework for waste tyres is far more than a compliance checkbox — it is a foundational pillar of India's circular economy transition and directly relevant to corporate climate strategies. End-of-life tyres, if not properly managed, are a significant source of toxic black carbon and microplastic pollution, contributing to both air quality degradation and long-term soil and water contamination — compounding physical climate risks for communities and ecosystems. From a supply chain perspective, large automotive and tyre companies with global investors and customers are increasingly expected to demonstrate circular material flows as part of their Scope 3 emissions management and broader ESG commitments. The International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) frameworks — IFRS S1 and S2 — require companies to disclose material sustainability-related risks and opportunities, including those in their value chains. A tyre producer that cannot demonstrate credible EPR compliance faces not only regulatory risk but also ESG disclosure gaps that institutional investors are beginning to scrutinise. The EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and its supply chain due diligence requirements mean that Indian tyre exporters supplying European automakers must align with global circular economy expectations. Robust EPR credit systems — even in interim form — provide the evidentiary basis for these disclosures. Companies that build strong EPR compliance infrastructure now will be better positioned to meet CSRD-aligned supply chain audits from European customers in the near term. Furthermore, the transition to sustainable tyre materials, retreading, and pyrolysis-based recycling aligns with India's Net Zero and Nationally Determined Contribution (NDC) goals. The CPCB's push to operationalise a digital EPR credit market for tyres mirrors global trends toward traceable, technology-enabled environmental accounting — a direction that only accelerates as climate disclosure regulation tightens.

What To Do Now Obtain and Review the Full Notice Immediately: Access the CPCB notice directly at the source URL (cpcb.nic.in) and circulate it to your legal, compliance, and sustainability teams. Do not rely on secondhand summaries for operational decisions. Audit Your Current EPR Credit Position: PIBOs should run an immediate audit of credits already received, credits pending from recycler partners, and the gap versus annual EPR obligations — then assess whether the interim mechanism can bridge any shortfall before the compliance year closes. Brief Your Recycler Partners: If you are a PIBO, contact your empanelled tyre waste recyclers to confirm they are aware of the interim arrangement and are generating credits in the prescribed interim format. A recycler generating credits in a non-compliant format could invalidate your claimed compliance. Establish an Interim Documentation Repository: Set up a dedicated, auditable record-keeping system (even a structured shared drive with version control) for all interim EPR credit certificates — organised by recycler, date, tonne-equivalent, and certificate reference number — to facilitate seamless migration to the permanent portal. Engage Legal Counsel on Contractual Implications: Review existing EPR procurement contracts with recyclers and aggregators to determine whether the interim arrangement affects delivery timelines, penalty clauses, or credit validity warranties — and issue contract amendments or side letters as needed.

Our Take This interim notice is a revealing signal about where India's EPR infrastructure stands — ambitious in regulatory design but still catching up in digital execution. The fact that CPCB has issued an urgent bridging arrangement for tyre waste credits suggests that the formal portal is facing operationalisation delays, which is not uncommon in large-scale environmental registry builds. What is significant, however, is that CPCB is not letting the infrastructure gap become a compliance gap — it is proactively establishing rules of the road for the interim period, which reflects a maturing regulatory posture. For companies, the lesson is clear: EPR for waste tyres is no longer a future obligation to prepare for — it is a present compliance reality with active regulatory oversight. As CPCB moves toward a fully digital, traceable EPR credit market (analogous to what is already functioning for plastic EPR), companies that have invested in robust recycler networks, clean documentation practices, and internal EPR accounting systems will have a decisive advantage. Those treating EPR as a paperwork exercise are taking on growing regulatory, financial, and reputational risk — and the window to course-correct is narrowing.

title: CPCB's Interim EPR Credit Transfer Arrangement for E-Waste Recyclers: What Producers Must Know
summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent notice establishing an interim arrangement for the generation and transfer of EPR end-product credits (certificates) by e-waste recyclers, signalling a critical transitional phase in India's e-waste compliance ecosystem. Producers, impo

sections.t: Executive Summary The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent notice establishing an interim arrangement for the generation and transfer of EPR end-product credits (certificates) by e-waste recyclers, signalling a critical transitional phase in India's e-waste compliance ecosystem. Producers, importers, and brand owners (PIBOs) registered under the E-Waste Management Rules, 2022 must pay close attention, as the validity and traceability of their EPR certificates — the backbone of their compliance proof — are directly implicated. Companies that rely on recycler-issued credits to meet their annual EPR targets face immediate operational and documentation risks if they do not adapt to the interim protocol.

What Changed Under India's E-Waste Management Rules, 2022, producers of electrical and electronic equipment are legally obligated to meet Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) targets — essentially, ensuring that a prescribed quantity of end-of-life e-waste is collected and channelled to authorised recyclers. The mechanism for proving compliance is the EPR certificate, or 'end-product credit,' which authorised recyclers generate on the CPCB's centralised EPR portal and transfer to the respective producer or importer. The CPCB's notice indicates that there is a transitional or technical gap in the existing portal-based system for credit generation and transfer — likely related to system upgrades, reconciliation of recycler registrations, or data integrity concerns on the EPR portal. To prevent a compliance vacuum during this period, CPCB has introduced an interim arrangement that prescribes a specific, time-bound process for recyclers to generate and transfer these credits outside of or alongside the standard digital workflow. In plain terms: the rules of how e-waste recyclers issue proof-of-recycling certificates to producers are temporarily modified. Both recyclers and producers must follow the interim process precisely — failing to do so could result in credits that are not recognised by CPCB for annual EPR target fulfilment, exposing producers to non-compliance penalties.

Who Is Affected Producers, Importers, and Brand Owners (PIBOs) of electrical and electronic equipment listed under Schedule I of the E-Waste Management Rules, 2022 — including consumer electronics, IT and telecom equipment, and large/small household appliances Authorised E-Waste Recyclers registered on the CPCB EPR portal who generate and transfer end-product credits to PIBOs E-Waste Producer Responsibility Organisations (PROs) that aggregate EPR obligations and credits on behalf of multiple producers Refurbishers and dismantlers in the e-waste value chain whose outputs feed into the recycler credit system Compliance and sustainability teams at large manufacturing conglomerates, electronics multinationals with Indian operations, and fast-moving consumer electronics brands Environmental auditors and consultants who verify EPR compliance documentation on behalf of producers

Key Obligations & Deadlines E-waste recyclers must follow the CPCB-prescribed interim process for generating end-product credits — do not rely solely on the standard portal workflow until CPCB confirms the permanent arrangement is restored or updated All credit transfers conducted during the interim period must be documented with supporting records (weight slips, invoices, recycling certificates) that can be presented to CPCB during audits or target verification Producers and PROs must confirm with their recycling partners that credits issued during this interim phase are being generated in accordance with CPCB's notice — verbal assurances are insufficient; obtain written confirmation and copies of interim certificates PIBOs should cross-verify that interim credits received are reflected or will be reconciled in the official CPCB EPR portal against their annual targets — proactively seek CPCB clarification if reconciliation timelines are unclear Do not defer e-waste recycling activity or credit generation pending resolution of the interim arrangement — EPR annual targets and their associated deadlines remain unchanged under the E-Waste Management Rules, 2022 Monitor the CPCB website (cpcb.nic.in) and EPR portal notifications actively for the withdrawal of the interim arrangement and restoration of standard credit generation procedures Deadline for annual EPR target compliance: as per the E-Waste Management Rules, 2022, producers must fulfil targets for each financial year — ensure interim credits are secured well before the close of the relevant compliance year

Climate Transition Angle E-waste EPR is not merely a domestic regulatory checkbox — it sits at the intersection of circular economy policy, climate risk, and global sustainability reporting standards. Electronic equipment contains critical minerals such as cobalt, lithium, copper, and rare earth elements whose extraction is both carbon-intensive and geopolitically sensitive. A robust, credible EPR credit system ensures that these materials are recovered and re-enter the supply chain, directly reducing Scope 3 emissions associated with virgin material extraction for producers. From a climate transition perspective, any disruption or opacity in the EPR credit system undermines the integrity of circular economy claims that Indian companies are increasingly making in their sustainability reports. Under the International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) framework — specifically IFRS S2 — and the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD), companies with global investors or European customers will face scrutiny over the traceability and verifiability of their waste management and circularity data. An interim credit arrangement that lacks clear audit trails could compromise the quality of data feeding into these disclosures. Additionally, physical climate risks — such as flooding of recycling facilities or heat-stress-driven operational disruptions — can further strain the recycler network. Companies should view this interim notice as a prompt to assess the resilience of their entire EPR supply chain, not just its current regulatory adequacy. The transition to a low-carbon, circular economy in India's electronics sector depends on a credit infrastructure that is both technically robust and climate-resilient.

What To Do Now Immediately contact your authorised e-waste recycling partners and PROs to confirm their awareness of the CPCB interim notice and verify that they are issuing credits in full compliance with the interim arrangement — request copies of all interim certificates issued on your account Assign your compliance or legal team to download and review the original CPCB notice from the official source (cpcb.nic.in) and prepare an internal FAQ or briefing note for your operations, finance, and sustainability teams on how EPR credit documentation will be affected during this period Conduct an immediate audit of your EPR credit position for the current financial year — calculate the gap between credits already secured and your annual target, and accelerate recycling activity if necessary to avoid a year-end shortfall exacerbated by interim processing delays Engage your sustainability reporting team to flag this interim arrangement as a data quality risk in your ESG disclosures — if you report against GRI, BRSR (Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting), or are preparing for ISSB-aligned disclosure, ensure that EPR credit traceability documentation is audit-ready Establish a direct communication channel with CPCB's EPR cell for timely updates on when the standard credit generation and transfer process will resume, and document all correspondence for your compliance records

Our Take CPCB's interim arrangement notice is a symptom of a larger growing pain: India's EPR infrastructure for e-waste is scaling rapidly, but the underlying digital systems and verification mechanisms are still catching up with the ambition of the 2022 Rules. This is not a reason for alarm, but it is a reason for vigilance. The companies that will be caught off-guard are those treating EPR compliance as a once-a-year reconciliation exercise rather than an active, year-round supply chain management discipline. We expect CPCB to continue tightening EPR enforcement — including stricter audits of recycler capacity claims and credit validity — as India aligns its circular economy framework with global standards ahead of international trade and investment scrutiny. The interim notice is, in effect, a stress test of your EPR supply chain's robustness. Use it as such.

title: CPCB's Interim EPR Credit Transfer Rules for E-Waste Recyclers: What Producers Need to Know Now
summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent interim notice governing the generation and transfer of Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) end-product credits — commonly known as EPR certificates — by registered e-waste recyclers in India. This interim arrangement is designed to

sections.t: Executive Summary The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent interim notice governing the generation and transfer of Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) end-product credits — commonly known as EPR certificates — by registered e-waste recyclers in India. This interim arrangement is designed to bridge operational gaps in the EPR portal system and ensure that producers, importers, and brand owners (PIBOs) can continue to meet their annual e-waste recycling targets without disruption. Companies that rely on EPR certificates to demonstrate compliance must immediately assess how this interim mechanism affects their credit procurement and reconciliation processes.

What Changed Under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, India operates a market-based EPR framework where producers of electrical and electronic equipment are legally obligated to ensure a prescribed quantity of e-waste is collected and recycled each year. Registered recyclers generate EPR certificates upon processing e-waste, and these certificates are then transferred to PIBOs through the CPCB's centralised EPR portal to offset their compliance obligations. CPCB's latest notice introduces an interim arrangement for how recyclers generate and transfer these EPR end-product credits. While the full PDF content was not available for extraction, such interim notices from CPCB typically arise when the digital EPR portal faces technical limitations, certificate reconciliation backlogs, or when rule amendments require a transitional operating procedure before permanent systems are updated. In practical terms, this means the standard portal-based workflow for credit generation and transfer may be temporarily modified — with recyclers and producers required to follow alternative documentation, reporting, or approval steps as directed by CPCB during the interim period. This is not a relaxation of compliance obligations. Producers are still expected to meet their EPR targets, and recyclers must continue to process e-waste. The interim arrangement simply prescribes how credits are recorded, validated, and transferred until the permanent system is fully operational.

Who Is Affected Producers, Importers, and Brand Owners (PIBOs) of electrical and electronic equipment registered under CPCB's EPR portal — including consumer electronics, IT equipment, large and small household appliances, and lighting equipment manufacturers CPCB-registered e-waste recyclers and dismantlers who generate and issue EPR certificates to PIBOs Extended Producer Responsibility — Producer Responsibility Organisations (EPR-PROs) that facilitate bulk credit procurement and transfer on behalf of multiple producers Large and mid-sized Indian manufacturers of electronics with annual EPR targets under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 Multinational companies with India operations that import electronic goods and are registered as PIBOs with CPCB Sustainability and compliance teams at companies whose EPR reconciliation cycles overlap with the period covered by the interim arrangement

Key Obligations & Deadlines Monitor CPCB's official EPR portal and website (cpcb.nic.in) immediately for the specific procedural steps outlined in the interim arrangement — including any revised formats, manual submission requirements, or temporary approval workflows E-waste recyclers must ensure that all e-waste processed during the interim period is documented with verifiable records (weight slips, processing logs, invoices) that can support credit generation once the permanent system is restored or updated PIBOs must not assume that credits will be automatically reflected in their portal accounts during the interim period — proactively contact your registered recycler or PRO to confirm that credits are being tracked and will be formally transferred once the interim mechanism is resolved Companies with EPR target deadlines falling in the near term (typically March 31 of the compliance year) should flag this interim notice to legal and compliance teams and document that they made good-faith efforts to transfer credits within the available system PROs acting on behalf of multiple producers should consolidate credit positions across their client portfolios and communicate clearly with all clients about any potential delays in certificate issuance or portal reflection Submit any grievances or clarification requests to CPCB in writing through official channels — retain copies of all correspondence as evidence of compliance diligence during the interim period

Climate Transition Angle India's e-waste EPR framework is a cornerstone of the country's circular economy strategy — and circular economy performance is increasingly scrutinised under global climate disclosure and ESG reporting standards. The International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB), under IFRS S2, and the European Union's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) both require companies to disclose material risks and opportunities related to resource use, waste management, and supply chain sustainability. For Indian exporters and companies with global investors, EPR compliance is no longer just a domestic regulatory checkbox — it is a data point in Scope 3 emissions accounting and supply chain due diligence. E-waste contains critical minerals — lithium, cobalt, copper, and rare earth elements — whose extraction is carbon-intensive and geopolitically sensitive. A robust domestic recycling ecosystem, underpinned by a functioning EPR credit market, directly reduces India's dependence on virgin material extraction and supports the low-carbon transition of the electronics and clean energy sectors. Disruptions to EPR credit flows — even temporary interim arrangements — can create gaps in recycler cash flows, disincentivising formal recycling and pushing material back into the informal sector, which has significantly higher environmental and social risks. For companies disclosing under the Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report (BRSR) framework, EPR compliance and e-waste recycling volumes are directly relevant to Principle 2 (sustainable and safe products) and Principle 6 (environmental responsibility). Interim disruptions should be disclosed if material, and compliance teams should be prepared to explain the regulatory context to auditors and rating agencies.

What To Do Now Immediately download and review the full CPCB interim notice from cpcb.nic.in and circulate it to your EPR compliance lead, legal team, and your registered recycler or PRO — do not wait for a portal notification Conduct a rapid internal audit of your current EPR credit position: how many credits do you need for the current compliance year, how many have been confirmed by your recycler, and how many are pending transfer on the portal — identify any gaps that the interim arrangement may widen Establish a direct communication protocol with your registered e-waste recycler(s) to receive weekly updates on credit generation volumes and the status of transfers under the interim mechanism — get this in writing Brief your BRSR and ESG reporting teams on the interim arrangement so that any disclosure related to e-waste EPR compliance for the current financial year accurately reflects the regulatory context and your company's documented compliance efforts Engage your industry association (such as ICEA, MAIT, or CEAMA) to seek collective clarification from CPCB on timelines for resolution of the interim arrangement and to advocate for a clearly communicated transition back to the standard portal workflow

Our Take CPCB's interim arrangement notice is a reminder that India's EPR ecosystem — while ambitious in design — is still maturing in its operational infrastructure. The e-waste EPR portal has been a significant step forward, but the need for interim credit transfer mechanisms signals that the digital backbone supporting this market must be strengthened urgently. For producers and recyclers, the real risk here is not the interim arrangement itself, but the compliance ambiguity it creates: credits not reflected in the system are credits that cannot be used for target reconciliation, and enforcement actions for shortfalls have been escalating. Looking ahead, we expect CPCB to progressively tighten EPR enforcement across all waste streams — plastics, batteries, tyres, and e-waste — as India positions itself to meet its climate and circular economy commitments. Companies that invest now in robust EPR tracking systems, diversified recycler partnerships, and proactive CPCB engagement will be best placed not just for this interim disruption, but for the more demanding compliance environment that is clearly on the horizon.

title: CPCB to Customs: EPR Registration Now the Gateway for Importing 85 EEE Items — What Producers Must Do Before June 30, 2023
summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued a formal directive to the Central Board of Indirect Taxes & Customs (CBIC) clarifying the conditions under which imported consignments of Electrical and Electronic Equipment (EEE) can be released at Indian ports. Effective June 30, 2023, producer

sections.t: Executive Summary The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued a formal directive to the Central Board of Indirect Taxes & Customs (CBIC) clarifying the conditions under which imported consignments of Electrical and Electronic Equipment (EEE) can be released at Indian ports. Effective June 30, 2023, producers importing any of the 85 specified EEE items will need a valid EPR Registration Certificate from the CPCB's EPR Portal — or face held consignments. This notice closes a critical compliance window and signals zero tolerance for unregistered imports beyond the grace period.

What Changed Under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, all producers of Electrical and Electronic Equipment (EEE) are required to register on CPCB's centralised EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility) Portal and obtain an EPR Registration Certificate before importing their products into India. EPR is a policy principle that holds manufacturers and importers financially and operationally responsible for the end-of-life management of their products — in this case, ensuring that e-waste generated from their products is properly collected and recycled. In April 2023, the Ministry of Finance's Department of Revenue flagged a practical bottleneck: many producers had not yet completed their EPR registration, causing imported consignments to pile up at customs. CPCB had earlier, on April 13, 2023, offered a 30-day interim relief — allowing customs to release consignments if producers submitted a written undertaking committing to obtain EPR registration. The May 9, 2023 letter now supersedes and extends that interim arrangement. The key change is this: the 30-day undertaking window has been replaced with a single hard deadline of June 30, 2023. Producers who submitted undertakings to customs can use the period until June 30 — not just 30 days from the date of undertaking — to obtain and submit their EPR Registration Certificate. After June 30, 2023, no consignment will be released without a valid EPR Registration Certificate. Additionally, a separate relief is provided for producers of 21 older EEE items whose EPR Authorisation under the previous 2016 Rules has lapsed — they can get their goods released by showing proof of a fresh EPR Portal application, but again only until June 30, 2023.

Who Is Affected Producers and importers of 85 EEE items classified under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 — spanning Information Technology & Telecommunication Equipment (ITEW17–ITEW27), Consumer Electrical & Electronic Equipment (CEEW6–CEEW19), Large & Small Household Appliances (LSEEW1–LSEEW34), Electrical & Electronic Tools (EETW1–EETW8), Toys, Leisure & Sports Equipment (TLSEW1–TLSEW6), Medical Devices (MDW1–MDW10), and Lighting Equipment (LIW1–LIW2). Producers and importers of 21 older EEE items (ITEW1–ITEW16 and CEEW1–CEEW5) whose Extended Producer Responsibility Authorisation (EPRA) under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2016 has already expired. Electronics brands — both Indian and foreign — that source finished goods or components from overseas and clear them through Indian customs. Contract manufacturers and authorised importers acting on behalf of EEE brand owners. Compliance, supply chain, and legal teams at electronics, appliance, telecom equipment, medical device, and lighting companies operating in India. Customs brokers and freight forwarders managing import clearances for EEE producers.

Key Obligations & Deadlines DEADLINE — June 30, 2023: Hard cutoff for all interim relief arrangements. After this date, customs will only release EEE consignments upon presentation of a valid EPR Registration Certificate from the CPCB EPR Portal. No exceptions or extensions have been signalled. IMMEDIATE ACTION: Producers who have already submitted an undertaking to customs must now prioritise completing their EPR registration on CPCB's EPR Portal (https://eprewaste.cpcb.gov.in) before June 30, 2023 — the clock runs from the date of undertaking submission, not from any fixed calendar date. FOR 85 EEE ITEMS (Rules 2022): Submit the Bill of Entry along with a written undertaking to the relevant customs authority if EPR registration is still pending. Track your application status on the CPCB EPR Portal and ensure the Registration Certificate is submitted to customs before June 30, 2023. FOR 21 EEE ITEMS (Rules 2016 — expired EPRA): Apply immediately on the CPCB EPR Portal under the 2022 Rules. Submit an acknowledgement of application along with a copy of the expired EPRA to customs to secure release of consignments — valid only until June 30, 2023. COMPLIANCE TRACKING: CPCB is actively maintaining a register of all undertakings submitted to customs. Producers should be aware that non-compliance will be visible to regulators. Designate a single point of contact within your organisation for coordinating with customs and CPCB on compliance status. POST JUNE 30: Ensure your EPR Registration Certificate is renewed annually and kept current. Any future import without a valid certificate will result in consignment holds with no interim relief available.

Climate Transition Angle E-waste is one of the fastest-growing solid waste streams globally, and India is the third-largest generator of electronic waste in the world, producing over 1.6 million metric tonnes annually (Global E-Waste Monitor, 2020). The enforcement of EPR registration for EEE imports is not merely a bureaucratic compliance exercise — it is a foundational building block of India's circular economy strategy and its broader climate transition agenda. From a physical risk perspective, improper e-waste disposal leads to toxic leaching into soil and groundwater, compounding the environmental vulnerabilities that climate change is already intensifying — particularly in water-stressed regions of India. By holding producers accountable for the end-of-life of their products, EPR regulations create financial incentives to design for durability, repairability, and recyclability, which directly reduces the carbon footprint embedded in manufacturing new electronics. For companies aligned with or preparing for global sustainability disclosure frameworks — including the International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) standards (IFRS S1 and S2) or the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) — EPR compliance in key markets like India is increasingly a supply chain due diligence requirement. Global institutional investors and European customers are scrutinising whether Indian suppliers have robust waste management obligations in place. Failure to maintain EPR registration is therefore not just a regulatory risk; it is a reputational and ESG rating risk. Companies that treat EPR compliance as a strategic priority — rather than a last-minute paperwork exercise — are better positioned to demonstrate responsible value chain stewardship to global stakeholders.

What To Do Now REGISTER IMMEDIATELY: If your company has not yet registered on the CPCB EPR Portal for e-waste (https://eprewaste.cpcb.gov.in), initiate the process today. Gather required documents — company registration, import records, product category details — and submit your application well before June 30, 2023 to allow processing time. AUDIT YOUR IMPORT PIPELINE: Work with your customs broker and supply chain team to identify all pending or upcoming EEE consignments that may be held at ports. Cross-check which product categories fall under the 85 or 21 EEE item lists and confirm the EPR status for each. SUBMIT UNDERTAKINGS WHERE NEEDED: For any consignment currently held at customs, submit the Bill of Entry and written undertaking immediately to secure interim release. Retain proof of submission, as CPCB is tracking these undertakings and will follow up on compliance. ASSIGN OWNERSHIP AND SET INTERNAL DEADLINES: Designate a specific compliance officer or team responsible for EPR registration and customs coordination. Set an internal deadline of June 15, 2023 — two weeks before the regulatory deadline — to confirm EPR Certificate receipt and submission to customs, creating a buffer for any procedural delays. BUILD EPR INTO YOUR ANNUAL COMPLIANCE CALENDAR: Beyond the immediate crisis, integrate EPR registration renewal, annual e-waste collection target reporting, and producer liability audits into your standing compliance framework. Consider engaging a certified EPR facilitator or recycling channel partner to meet your annual collection targets under the 2022 Rules.

Our Take This CPCB notice is deceptively short in length but enormous in implication. The fact that India's apex pollution control board had to write to the Ministry of Finance to intervene in customs clearances reveals just how operationally consequential EPR non-compliance has become — this is no longer a back-office environmental checkbox. The June 30, 2023 deadline is firm, and given CPCB's stated intent to track undertakings against actual EPR registrations, companies that miss this window should expect scrutiny that goes beyond held shipments. More broadly, this notice is a clear signal of the direction of travel: India is systematically tightening the linkage between import privileges and producer responsibility obligations. With the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 expanding the scope of covered products and introducing steeper collection targets compared to the 2016 Rules, EPR in India is maturing from a voluntary gesture to a hard market-access condition. Business leaders should view investment in EPR systems — collection infrastructure, recycler partnerships, digital compliance tracking — not as a cost, but as a strategic moat that protects supply chain continuity and strengthens ESG credentials in an increasingly scrutinised global marketplace.

title: Decoding ISSB Support Mechanisms: What Indian Companies Need to Know About Consistent Standards Application
summary: The International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) has established a structured framework to support consistent global application of its landmark sustainability disclosure standards — IFRS S1 (General Requirements) and IFRS S2 (Climate-related Disclosures). For Indian companies navigating SEBI

sections.t: Executive Summary The International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) has established a structured framework to support consistent global application of its landmark sustainability disclosure standards — IFRS S1 (General Requirements) and IFRS S2 (Climate-related Disclosures). For Indian companies navigating SEBI's Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting (BRSR) framework and eyeing access to global capital markets, understanding how the ISSB supports implementation is no longer optional — it is a strategic imperative. Companies that align early with ISSB guidance will be better positioned to meet converging regulatory expectations from both Indian and international regulators.

What Changed The ISSB, operating under the IFRS Foundation, has published and continuously updates a suite of implementation support resources designed to help preparers, auditors, regulators, and investors apply IFRS S1 and IFRS S2 in a consistent and comparable manner across jurisdictions. These resources include educational materials, illustrative examples, frequently asked questions (FAQs), and capacity-building initiatives targeted at both developed and emerging markets. Critically, the ISSB's support framework addresses one of the most common barriers to adoption: the perception that these standards are too complex or too resource-intensive for companies outside large Western markets. The ISSB has acknowledged the varied starting points of different jurisdictions — including India — and has built transition reliefs and phased implementation pathways into its guidance. This signals a clear intention: the ISSB wants broad, global adoption, and it is actively removing friction to get there. The ISSB also works in close coordination with the International Organization of Securities Commissions (IOSCO), which has endorsed IFRS S1 and S2 for use by its 130+ member regulators — including SEBI. This regulatory endorsement chain means that ISSB standards are increasingly likely to inform future mandatory disclosure requirements in India.

Who Is Affected Top 1,000 listed companies in India currently subject to SEBI's mandatory BRSR Core disclosures Companies with international listings, cross-border financing, or global investor bases seeking capital from ESG-focused funds Indian subsidiaries and supply chain partners of multinational corporations subject to CSRD (EU) or SEC climate rules (US) CFOs, Chief Sustainability Officers, and Investor Relations teams responsible for sustainability reporting strategy Statutory auditors and assurance providers preparing to provide limited or reasonable assurance on sustainability disclosures Financial institutions and banks assessing climate-related risks in their lending and investment portfolios Mid-sized companies aspiring to join global supply chains where sustainability disclosure is becoming a procurement prerequisite

Key Obligations & Deadlines IMMEDIATE: Familiarise your reporting team with ISSB's free implementation support portal at ifrs.org — including the IFRS S1 and S2 Basis for Conclusions documents, which explain the intent behind each requirement IMMEDIATE: Review the ISSB's FAQ library and educational materials to identify gaps between your current BRSR disclosures and IFRS S2 climate-related disclosure requirements (especially Scope 1, 2 & 3 emissions, scenario analysis, and governance disclosures) Q1 2025 ONWARDS: Monitor SEBI communications for formal guidance on ISSB alignment — SEBI's BRSR Core framework is already conceptually aligned with TCFD (which ISSB S2 supersedes), and a formal convergence announcement is anticipated ONGOING: Track ISSB's 'IFRS Sustainability Disclosure Taxonomy' updates, which facilitate digital, machine-readable reporting — a likely future SEBI requirement for structured data submissions ONGOING: Engage with the ISSB's capacity-building programmes and regional workshops, particularly those conducted through the IFRS Foundation's Emerging Economies Group, in which India participates 2025–2026: Begin voluntary parallel reporting against IFRS S2 alongside mandatory BRSR submissions — this builds internal capability and signals disclosure quality to global investors

Climate Transition Angle The ISSB's implementation support framework is not merely a technical compliance exercise — it sits at the heart of the global architecture being built to finance the climate transition. IFRS S2 requires companies to disclose material climate-related risks and opportunities across both physical risks (extreme weather, water stress, sea-level rise) and transition risks (carbon pricing, stranded assets, changing consumer preferences). For Indian companies, both dimensions are acutely relevant: India faces some of the world's highest physical climate risk exposure, while simultaneously undertaking one of the most ambitious energy transitions of any major economy. The convergence of ISSB standards with the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) — through the ongoing interoperability project between ISSB and EFRAG — means that Indian exporters and supply chain participants serving European markets will face a dual disclosure expectation. Similarly, the US SEC's climate disclosure rules, though currently under legal challenge, signal the same direction of travel. Companies that use the ISSB's support resources to build robust climate disclosure capabilities now are essentially future-proofing themselves against a cascade of regulatory requirements from multiple directions. Perhaps most significantly, consistent application of ISSB standards globally enables apples-to-apples comparison of climate risk across portfolios — which is precisely what institutional investors, green bond issuers, and development finance institutions require to allocate transition capital efficiently. Indian companies that speak the ISSB language will have a measurable advantage in accessing this growing pool of sustainability-linked finance.

What To Do Now CONDUCT A GAP ASSESSMENT: Map your current BRSR Core disclosures against the specific requirements of IFRS S1 and IFRS S2 — pay particular attention to climate scenario analysis, Scope 3 value chain emissions, and the 'significant judgements' disclosure requirements that most Indian companies currently omit BUILD INTERNAL CAPABILITY: Designate a cross-functional ISSB working group (Finance, Legal, Sustainability, Operations, IR) and use the ISSB's free educational modules to upskill the team — this is foundational, not optional ENGAGE YOUR AUDITORS EARLY: Discuss with your statutory auditor and any sustainability assurance provider how they are preparing for IFRS S2-aligned assurance engagements — the assurance landscape in India is evolving rapidly and early dialogue prevents last-minute surprises LEVERAGE TRANSITION RELIEFS STRATEGICALLY: ISSB has built in a one-year relief from Scope 3 and some comparative reporting requirements for first-time adopters — understand these reliefs and plan your adoption roadmap to use them intelligently rather than deferring preparation COMMUNICATE PROACTIVELY WITH INVESTORS: If you have foreign institutional investors (FIIs) or are seeking ESG-labelled debt, proactively communicate your ISSB alignment roadmap — investors increasingly distinguish between companies that are reactive to disclosure mandates and those that are building genuine climate governance

Our Take The ISSB's commitment to supporting consistent application of its standards is a signal that global sustainability disclosure is moving from aspiration to infrastructure. For Indian business leaders, the temptation may be to treat ISSB standards as a 'foreign' requirement relevant only to multinationals — but this view is increasingly untenable. SEBI's BRSR framework is converging with ISSB principles by design, not coincidence, and the trajectory points clearly toward mandatory ISSB-aligned disclosures for listed Indian companies within the next three to five years. The companies that will navigate this transition smoothly are those investing now in data systems, governance structures, and internal capabilities — not those scrambling to comply at the deadline. The ISSB's implementation support framework is, in effect, a public good: free, authoritative, and specifically designed to help companies like yours succeed. The question is not whether to engage with it, but how quickly.

title: Draft Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2022: What India's Businesses Must Know About the Next Phase of EPR
summary: India's Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) released draft amendments to the Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016 in January 2022, significantly tightening the Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework for producers, importers, and brand owners (PIBOs). The amendments

sections.t: Executive Summary India's Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) released draft amendments to the Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016 in January 2022, significantly tightening the Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework for producers, importers, and brand owners (PIBOs). The amendments introduce sharper definitions for plastic packaging waste categories, expand the scope of plastic waste processors, and reinforce that EPR obligations will be governed by periodically updated regulations — making compliance a moving target rather than a one-time exercise. Companies that use, produce, or import plastic packaging must treat this not as a routine regulatory update, but as a structural shift in how plastic accountability is governed in India.

What Changed The 2022 draft amends the foundational Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016 and builds on the 2021 Amendment Rules (notified August 12, 2021) that first introduced the EPR framework for plastic packaging. Here is what is new in plain language: First, the draft introduces a set of new and revised definitions that significantly broaden the regulatory perimeter. 'Brand owner' is now clearly defined as any individual or company selling goods under a registered brand label or trademark — closing a loophole that previously allowed some brand operators to sidestep liability. 'Plastic packaging' is redefined comprehensively to cover all plastic used for protection, preservation, storage, and transportation of products. Second, the rules introduce two new and important waste categories: 'pre-consumer plastic packaging waste' (waste generated during the manufacturing and packaging process, before the product reaches the end consumer) and 'post-consumer plastic packaging waste' (waste generated after the consumer has used and discarded the packaging). This distinction is critical because it means EPR obligations now extend upstream into manufacturing processes, not just downstream to post-sale collection. Third, the definition of 'plastic waste processors' is expanded to include recyclers, waste-to-energy facilities (including co-processing in cement kilns), waste-to-oil converters, and industrial composters — broadening the ecosystem of compliant end-of-life pathways that PIBOs can use to meet their EPR targets. Fourth, 'end-of-life disposal' is formally defined to include energy recovery (co-processing in cement kilns), waste-to-oil production, and use in road construction per Indian Roads Congress guidelines — legitimising these pathways under the rules. Fifth, and perhaps most consequentially for compliance planning, Rule 9(1) is replaced with language stating that producers, importers, and brand owners shall fulfil their EPR obligations 'in accordance with regulations issued from time to time under these rules.' This means EPR targets, timelines, and mechanisms are no longer static — they will be updated by the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) through regulations, requiring companies to continuously monitor for changes. Finally, registration requirements are clarified: entities operating in more than two states or union territories must register with the CPCB (not state boards), creating a clearer compliance pathway for national and multinational brands.

Who Is Affected Producers and manufacturers of plastic packaging materials — from flexible films and rigid containers to multilayer packaging and carry bags Brand owners — any company selling products in plastic packaging under a registered trademark, regardless of whether they manufacture the packaging themselves Importers of plastic packaging products or products packaged in plastic, including importers of multilayer and single-use packaging Plastic waste recyclers, waste-to-energy operators, and industrial composters who are newly classified as 'plastic waste processors' with defined roles in the EPR chain Fast-Moving Consumer Goods (FMCG) companies, e-commerce firms, food and beverage brands, pharmaceutical companies, and consumer durables manufacturers — all heavy users of plastic packaging Small and medium enterprises (SMEs) operating as brand owners or manufacturers, who now have no exemption from EPR obligations Exporters of plastic packaging or plastic-packaged goods, who face expanded definitional scope under the revised 'importer/exporter' clause

Key Obligations & Deadlines EPR Compliance (Ongoing): Producers, importers, and brand owners must fulfil EPR obligations for plastic packaging as per CPCB regulations issued under these rules — companies must register on the CPCB's centralised EPR portal and report collection and recycling targets on time Registration Jurisdiction Clarity (Immediate): Entities operating across more than two states or union territories must register with the CPCB centrally; those operating within a single state register with the respective State Pollution Control Board (SPCB) or Pollution Control Committee (PCC) under Rule 9(5) Pre-Consumer Waste Accounting (Upon Finalization): Once the rules are finalized, companies must track and report pre-consumer plastic packaging waste (waste generated during in-house manufacturing and packaging operations) as a distinct waste stream — update internal waste accounting systems accordingly Post-Consumer Waste Collection Targets (Per CPCB Regulations): PIBOs must meet annually escalating collection and recycling targets for post-consumer plastic packaging waste as notified by CPCB — FY2024-25 onward targets have been set; verify current targets on the CPCB EPR portal End-of-Life Pathway Validation (Ongoing): Only CPCB-approved plastic waste processors (recyclers, co-processors, waste-to-oil units) qualify for EPR credit — ensure all third-party processors in your waste management chain are registered and certified Thickness and Specification Compliance (Ongoing): Rule 4(1)(d) amendments specify that thickness exemptions are only as notified by the government — review all plastic product specifications against current MoEFCC notifications Draft Comment Window: The draft was open for public comments to the Secretary, MoEFCC via post or email (satyendra.kumar07@nic.in / amit.love@nic.in) within 60 days of gazette publication — industry associations should verify if final rules have been notified and check for any modifications from the draft stage

Climate Transition Angle India's plastic waste reforms are not happening in isolation — they are part of a sweeping global realignment of how plastic is governed as both a pollution crisis and a climate risk. The two are deeply connected. Plastic production is almost entirely fossil-fuel dependent; globally, plastic manufacturing accounts for approximately 3.4% of greenhouse gas emissions, and that figure is projected to grow significantly if no structural intervention occurs. The expansion of EPR to cover pre-consumer waste is directly aligned with the logic of Scope 3 emissions accounting. Just as companies are now expected under frameworks like the International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) IFRS S2 and the EU Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) to account for emissions across their entire value chain — including raw material extraction and processing — the expanded plastic waste rules now require companies to account for packaging waste generated upstream in their supply chain, not just what the consumer discards. The recognition of waste-to-energy and co-processing as compliant end-of-life pathways also has a climate dimension that requires careful scrutiny. While these pathways recover energy value from plastic, they are not zero-carbon solutions — co-processing in cement kilns, for example, releases CO2. Companies that rely heavily on waste-to-energy to meet EPR targets should be aware that international sustainability standards (particularly the EU Taxonomy and CSRD) treat such pathways with caution and may not recognise them as genuinely circular or low-carbon solutions for disclosure purposes. Supply chain risk is the other critical lens. Companies that source plastic packaging from suppliers who are not EPR-compliant face increasing regulatory and reputational exposure. As the CPCB tightens enforcement and EPR certificate trading becomes more formalised, non-compliant supply chains will become a material liability — both in terms of regulatory penalties and in terms of ESG ratings and investor scrutiny. The physical risk dimension is equally real: plastic pollution degrades watersheds, agricultural soil, and coastal ecosystems on which India's food and textile supply chains depend. Finally, India's plastic EPR framework is converging — albeit imperfectly — with the trajectory of the UN Global Plastics Treaty negotiations, which aim to create binding international obligations on plastic production, design, and waste management. Indian companies with export exposure, particularly to the EU (where the Single-Use Plastics Directive and Packaging and Packaging Waste Regulation are already in force), must treat domestic EPR compliance as a floor, not a ceiling.

What To Do Now Conduct a Plastic Packaging Audit: Map all plastic packaging used across your product portfolio — distinguish between pre-consumer waste (generated in your facilities) and post-consumer waste (generated by end consumers). Quantify volumes by category (rigid, flexible, multilayer, single-use) to establish your EPR baseline and ensure your data is ready for CPCB reporting cycles. Verify and Update CPCB EPR Portal Registration: Confirm your registration status on the CPCB centralised EPR portal. If your operations span more than two states, ensure registration is with CPCB directly, not just state boards. Update your registration to reflect any new product lines or packaging types covered under the revised definitions. Audit Your Plastic Waste Processor Network: Review all recyclers, co-processors, and waste processors in your EPR fulfilment chain. Confirm they are registered with CPCB or the relevant SPCB as 'plastic waste processors' under the updated definition. Processors without valid registration will not generate recognised EPR credits, leaving you non-compliant regardless of volumes collected. Align Internal ESG Reporting with the New Waste Categories: Update your sustainability reporting templates — including BRSR (Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report) disclosures for listed companies — to separately account for pre-consumer and post-consumer plastic packaging waste. This granularity is increasingly expected by ESG rating agencies and institutional investors, and will be required for alignment with ISSB IFRS S2 value chain disclosures. Engage Legal and Government Affairs Teams to Track Final Notification: The January 2022 gazette notification was a draft open for public comment. Assign responsibility to track whether and when the final rules have been notified, and identify any changes from the draft. Given that Rule 9(1) now ties EPR obligations to periodically updated CPCB regulations, establish a standing process to monitor CPCB circulars and gazette notifications on plastic waste — this is no longer a set-and-forget compliance exercise.

Our Take The Draft Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2022 represent a meaningful maturation of India's plastic governance architecture — but the most important signal for business leaders is not in any single definition or deadline. It is in the structural shift embedded in the revised Rule 9(1): EPR obligations are now explicitly dynamic, governed by regulations that will be updated 'from time to time.' This is a deliberate design choice by MoEFCC and CPCB, giving regulators the flexibility to ratchet up targets without going through the full rule-amendment process each time. For companies, this means that plastic compliance is now a live risk — one that requires continuous monitoring, not just an annual checkbox. The convergence of India's EPR framework with global standards like CSRD and the emerging UN Plastics Treaty also means that companies building robust domestic compliance systems today are, in effect, future-proofing themselves for international market access requirements. Those that treat EPR as a cost to be minimised rather than a capability to be built will find themselves structurally disadvantaged as enforcement tightens and as investors increasingly price in plastic-related liability. Our strong advice: invest now in the data infrastructure, supplier engagement, and internal governance needed to make plastic accountability a core competency — the regulatory trajectory makes this a question of when, not if.

title: E-Waste Compliance Deadline Extended: CPCB Gives Producers Until 31 December 2024 to File FY 2023-24 Returns
summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has extended the deadline for filing both quarterly and annual returns for FY 2023-24 under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, to 31 December 2024. This extension provides registered producers, manufacturers, refurbishers, recyclers, and other obligated

sections.t: Executive Summary The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has extended the deadline for filing both quarterly and annual returns for FY 2023-24 under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, to 31 December 2024. This extension provides registered producers, manufacturers, refurbishers, recyclers, and other obligated entities additional time to meet their Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) reporting obligations. Companies must treat this as a firm final deadline and use the window to ensure complete and accurate filings — non-compliance risks EPR portal suspension, penalties, and reputational damage.

What Changed Under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, entities registered on the CPCB EPR portal are required to file periodic returns — both quarterly and annual — documenting the volume of electrical and electronic equipment (EEE) placed on the market, e-waste collected, channelised for recycling, and EPR targets met or missed. For FY 2023-24, these filings were originally due earlier in the calendar year. The CPCB has now issued an official notice extending the timeline for all outstanding quarterly returns (Q1 through Q4 of FY 2023-24) as well as the annual return for FY 2023-24, granting a uniform revised deadline of 31 December 2024. This is a regulatory relief measure, likely issued in response to industry representations citing challenges in data reconciliation across complex supply chains and delays in onboarding recyclers onto the EPR portal. However, it should not be interpreted as a relaxation of underlying EPR obligations — targets, collection duties, and recycling channelisation requirements remain fully in force.

Who Is Affected Producers of Electrical and Electronic Equipment (EEE): All companies that manufacture, import, or brand EEE covered under Schedule I of the E-Waste Rules — including consumer electronics, IT and telecom equipment, large and small household appliances, lighting equipment, and more. Refurbishers: Entities engaged in refurbishment of EEE who are registered under the Rules and have EPR reporting obligations. E-Waste Recyclers and Dismantlers: CPCB/State-authorised recyclers who must report volumes processed and certificates issued. Bulk Consumers: Large institutional users of EEE (government bodies, corporates, hospitals, educational institutions) who have obligations to channel e-waste to authorised recyclers. EPR Compliance Intermediaries and PROs (Producer Responsibility Organisations): Entities facilitating EPR target fulfilment on behalf of producers, who must ensure underlying data flows are complete before the deadline. Consumer Electronics, IT Hardware, Telecom, White Goods, and Lighting Sectors: These are the most heavily impacted industry verticals given the breadth of Schedule I coverage.

Key Obligations & Deadlines REVISED DEADLINE — 31 December 2024: File all pending quarterly returns (Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4) for FY 2023-24 on the CPCB EPR portal (https://eprnewportal.cpcb.gov.in). REVISED DEADLINE — 31 December 2024: Submit the annual return for FY 2023-24, consolidating total EEE placed on market, e-waste collected, and EPR targets achieved. EPR Target Reconciliation: Ensure that EPR certificates (from authorised recyclers) covering your FY 2023-24 collection and recycling targets have been uploaded and linked to your producer account before filing. Recycler Certificate Verification: Cross-check that all recycler partners have uploaded processing certificates on the portal; unfiled certificates on the recycler's side will create gaps in your annual return. No Further Extensions Should Be Assumed: This appears to be a final extension. Companies should not plan for an additional deferral — penalties under the E-Waste Rules and potential suspension of EPR registration apply to non-filers. State Pollution Control Board (SPCB) Coordination: Where state-level authorisations are involved (e.g., for collection centres), ensure state-level reporting is also aligned with the central portal filings by 31 December 2024.

Climate Transition Angle E-waste governance sits at the intersection of the circular economy and climate transition — two themes increasingly scrutinised by global investors and trading partners. The E-Waste Rules' EPR framework is India's primary mechanism for keeping hazardous materials out of landfills and recovering critical minerals — lithium, cobalt, copper, and rare earth elements — that are essential for the clean energy transition (batteries, EVs, wind turbines, solar panels). Poor EPR compliance directly undermines domestic resource circularity and increases dependence on virgin mineral imports, a supply chain vulnerability that carries both physical and transition risk. From a global standards perspective, companies reporting under the International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) framework (IFRS S1/S2) or preparing for alignment with the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) — particularly relevant for Indian subsidiaries of European multinationals or Indian exporters to the EU — will find that e-waste EPR performance feeds into material disclosures on waste management, circular economy practices, and value chain emissions. Similarly, SEBI's Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report (BRSR) Core framework, now mandatory for the top 150 listed companies, includes indicators on waste generated and managed that are directly informed by EPR return data. Accurate, timely EPR filings are therefore not just a regulatory checkbox — they are the data foundation for credible ESG disclosure.

What To Do Now Audit Your EPR Portal Status Immediately: Log into the CPCB EPR portal and identify which quarterly and annual returns for FY 2023-24 are pending, partially filed, or flagged with errors. Assign a responsible owner and set an internal deadline of 15 November 2024 — giving six weeks of buffer before the regulatory deadline. Reconcile Data Across Your Value Chain: Collect volume data from your sales, logistics, and import teams (EEE placed on market) and match against EPR certificates received from your authorised recycler or PRO partners. Discrepancies must be resolved before filing, not after. Engage Your Recycler and PRO Partners Urgently: Contact all recycler and PRO partners to confirm they have uploaded processing certificates and transaction records on the portal. Gaps on their side will invalidate your return — do not wait until December to discover this. Link EPR Returns to BRSR and ESG Disclosures: Direct your sustainability reporting team to use the finalised FY 2023-24 EPR data to populate BRSR Core waste indicators and any ISSB/GRI 306 (Waste) disclosures. This creates a single source of truth and avoids inconsistencies across filings. Establish a Standing EPR Compliance Calendar: Use this extension as a trigger to build a permanent internal compliance calendar for FY 2024-25 onwards — with internal quarterly filing deadlines set at least 30 days before CPCB due dates, preventing reliance on future extensions.

Our Take The CPCB's decision to extend the FY 2023-24 e-waste return deadline to 31 December 2024 is pragmatic, but it signals a systemic challenge: India's EPR infrastructure — the portal, the recycler certification ecosystem, and corporate data pipelines — is still maturing. However, the regulatory direction is unambiguous. The E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 represent a significant tightening over the predecessor 2016 rules, with steeper EPR targets, stricter documentation requirements, and a more functional digital compliance architecture. CPCB has shown it will enforce — EPR portal suspensions and show-cause notices to non-compliant producers have already been issued in other EPR streams (plastics, batteries). Companies that treat this extension as extra time to get organised rather than a signal that enforcement is lax are taking a serious risk. Looking ahead, we expect CPCB to integrate EPR compliance performance more tightly with product import clearances and company-level ESG ratings, making e-waste filings a boardroom-level concern, not just a regulatory affairs task.

title: E-Waste EPR Annual Return Deadline Extended to 15 August 2025: What Registered Entities Must Do Now
summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has granted a 46-day extension for the filing of Annual Returns on the E-Waste Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) Portal, moving the deadline from 30 June 2025 to 15 August 2025. This relief applies to all entities registered under the E-Waste (Manageme

sections.t: Executive Summary The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has granted a 46-day extension for the filing of Annual Returns on the E-Waste Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) Portal, moving the deadline from 30 June 2025 to 15 August 2025. This relief applies to all entities registered under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 — including producers, importers, refurbishers, and recyclers. Companies should treat this extension not as a pause, but as a final window to ensure their EPR compliance data is accurate, complete, and audit-ready.

What Changed Under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, all registered entities on the CPCB's E-Waste EPR Portal are required to submit Annual Returns documenting their e-waste generation, collection, channelisation, and recycling or refurbishment activities for the preceding financial year (FY 2024–25 in this cycle). The original filing deadline was 30 June 2025. On 30 June 2025 — the very day of the deadline — CPCB issued a notice to all registered entities extending this deadline by approximately six weeks to 15 August 2025. While CPCB has not publicly detailed the specific reasons for the extension in the available notice, such extensions are typically granted in response to portal technical issues, industry requests for additional time to reconcile data, or delays in EPR credit issuance and verification that affect accurate reporting. It is critical to understand that this is an extension, not an exemption. All reporting obligations remain fully intact. Entities that fail to file by 15 August 2025 remain subject to penalties, suspension of EPR certificates, and regulatory action under the Environment Protection Act, 1986.

Who Is Affected Producers of Electrical and Electronic Equipment (EEE): Manufacturers and brand owners who place EEE products on the Indian market and hold EPR registration with CPCB. Importers of EEE: Companies importing finished electronic goods, components, or sub-assemblies that fall under the scheduled categories of e-waste rules. Refurbishers: Entities registered to refurbish used EEE and obligated to report volumes channelised through their facilities. E-Waste Recyclers and Dismantlers: CPCB/State Pollution Control Board (SPCB)-authorised recyclers who must report quantities of e-waste received, processed, and EPR credits generated. Producer Responsibility Organisations (PROs): PROs operating on behalf of producers must ensure the data they have consolidated and reported on behalf of their clients is submitted accurately. Large Enterprises and SMEs alike: The obligation applies to all registered entities regardless of size — there is no SME carve-out under the E-Waste Rules.

Key Obligations & Deadlines REVISED DEADLINE — 15 August 2025: File Annual Returns for FY 2024–25 on the CPCB E-Waste EPR Portal (https://ewasteeprbharat.nic.in) before 11:59 PM on this date. No further extensions should be assumed. Producers: Report total EEE placed on the market (category-wise, in metric tonnes), EPR targets for the year, e-waste collected, and EPR certificates/credits redeemed against targets. Recyclers & Dismantlers: Report total e-waste received (source-wise), quantities processed, EPR credits generated and transferred, and any residual waste disposal details. Refurbishers: Report quantities of EEE received for refurbishment, refurbished units sold, and e-waste channelised to authorised recyclers. PROs: Confirm reconciliation of EPR credits between producer clients and recycler partners; ensure all credits are logged and matched in the portal before filing. Data Reconciliation Deadline (Internal): Companies should target completing internal data reconciliation by 1 August 2025 to allow two weeks for portal submission and troubleshooting. Penalty Exposure: Non-compliance with Annual Return filing can attract penalties under the Environment Protection Act, 1986, and may result in suspension or cancellation of EPR registration, blocking future sales of EEE products in India.

Climate Transition Angle E-waste EPR compliance sits at the intersection of the circular economy and climate transition — two pillars that global investors and regulators are increasingly treating as inseparable.
