Electronics manufacturing is a resource-intensive sector with significant embedded carbon; responsible end-of-life management through authorised recycling directly reduces the need for virgin material extraction, cutting lifecycle emissions. From a physical risk perspective, the informal e-waste recycling sector — which EPR frameworks aim to displace — is concentrated in climate-vulnerable urban and peri-urban areas. Formalising the recycling chain through EPR not only reduces toxic exposure but builds supply chain resilience by securing a domestic source of recovered critical minerals (cobalt, lithium, copper, rare earths) essential for India's clean energy transition — particularly for EV batteries and solar components. On the disclosure front, international frameworks are rapidly converging on supply chain material traceability. The ISSB's IFRS S2 standard requires companies to disclose climate-related risks across their value chains, and the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) — which affects Indian subsidiaries and exporters to Europe — demands disclosure of circular economy metrics including waste and material flows. Robust EPR Annual Return data is the foundational layer for building these disclosures. Companies that invest now in clean, auditable EPR data will have a significant advantage when SEBI's Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report (BRSR) Core disclosures on resource use and waste are further tightened, as is widely expected.

What To Do Now Audit your EPR portal data immediately: Log in to the CPCB E-Waste EPR Portal today and verify that all FY 2024–25 transactions — EEE sales volumes, e-waste collection records, EPR credit purchases, and recycler certificates — are accurately entered and match your internal ERP or inventory records. Discrepancies now are far easier to resolve than post-filing. Engage your PRO or recycler partners without delay: If you rely on a PRO or directly contracted recyclers to generate and transfer EPR credits, confirm with them in writing by 10 July 2025 that all credits for FY 2024–25 have been generated, verified, and transferred to your account on the portal. Credit shortfalls must be addressed before filing. Assign a dedicated compliance owner and set an internal deadline of 1 August 2025: Do not wait until 14 August. Assign a named individual (typically the Head of EHS or Corporate Compliance) as accountable for the filing. Set an internal deadline of 1 August 2025 for a complete draft return to allow buffer time for corrections and approvals. Document everything for future BRSR and ESG disclosures: As you compile Annual Return data, simultaneously extract the metrics you will need for BRSR Core reporting — total e-waste generated, percentage channelised to authorised recyclers, EPR target fulfilment rate. Build this into a standing ESG data management process, not a one-off compliance exercise. Monitor CPCB portal advisories actively: Given that this extension was announced on the deadline date itself, ensure your compliance team has subscribed to CPCB notifications and checks the E-Waste EPR Portal notice board regularly. Any further guidance on filing formats, technical issues, or clarifications will appear there first.

Our Take The last-minute extension of the E-Waste EPR Annual Return deadline is a practical relief, but it should not obscure the larger regulatory direction of travel. India's EPR framework for e-waste is maturing rapidly — CPCB has been steadily tightening enforcement, increasing scrutiny of EPR credit authenticity, and expanding the list of obligated product categories. The days of treating EPR compliance as a checkbox exercise are numbered. For companies with operations or exports touching the EU, the stakes are even higher: the European Battery Regulation and CSRD supply chain provisions are creating hard commercial incentives for traceable, certified end-of-life management. Indian producers and recyclers that build robust compliance infrastructure now — clean data, verified credits, auditable recycling chains — will be positioned not just to satisfy CPCB, but to meet the growing due diligence demands of global customers and institutional investors. Use the six weeks wisely.

title: E-Waste First Amendment Rules, 2023: What Solar and MedTech Companies Must Know Now
summary: The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) notified the E-Waste (Management) Amendment Rules, 2023 on 30 January 2023, effective 1 April 2023. Two critical changes stand out: solar photovoltaic (PV) panels and medical devices have been formally brought under the hazardous substa

sections.t: Executive Summary The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) notified the E-Waste (Management) Amendment Rules, 2023 on 30 January 2023, effective 1 April 2023. Two critical changes stand out: solar photovoltaic (PV) panels and medical devices have been formally brought under the hazardous substance restrictions of the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, and the trigger for hazardous substance disclosure has been shifted from a fixed product-documentation requirement to an on-demand basis as required by the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB). Companies operating in the solar energy and healthcare equipment sectors must immediately review their supply chains, product compositions, and Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) frameworks to ensure compliance.

What Changed The E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 — India's primary regulatory framework governing the lifecycle management of electrical and electronic equipment waste — have been amended in two specific but consequential ways through this notification. First, Rule 16(5), which governs how manufacturers must disclose information about restricted hazardous substances present in their products, has been modified. Previously, this disclosure was required to appear 'in the product user documentation' — meaning it had to be proactively included in manuals, spec sheets, or packaging materials. The amendment replaces this with a requirement to disclose 'as and when required by the Central Pollution Control Board.' In practical terms, this shifts the obligation from automatic, always-on disclosure to a responsive, regulator-triggered disclosure model. Companies must now be ready to produce detailed hazardous substance data on CPCB's request, rather than embedding it in every product document. Second, and more significantly from an industry scope perspective, Schedule II of the Rules — which lists restricted hazardous substances and their applications — has been expanded. Two new entries have been added: Serial No. 36 covers cadmium and lead in solar panels, solar cells, and solar photovoltaic (PV) panels, cells, and modules; and Serial No. 37 covers lead in medical devices, with an explicit exclusion for implanted and infected products. This means that for the first time, solar PV manufacturers and medical device companies are formally subject to the substance restriction and EPR obligations under the E-Waste Rules for these specific hazardous materials.

Who Is Affected Solar PV manufacturers, assemblers, and importers — including those producing or sourcing solar panels, solar cells, and photovoltaic modules for rooftop, utility-scale, or off-grid applications Medical device manufacturers and importers — particularly those whose products contain lead-based components such as solders, radiation shielding elements, or electronic sub-assemblies (excluding implanted and infected products) Original Equipment Manufacturers (OEMs) and contract manufacturers supplying components to solar and medtech companies, who may need to provide substance declarations Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) compliance teams and Producer Responsibility Organisations (PROs) operating across these two newly covered sectors Importers and brand owners of foreign-manufactured solar PV equipment and medical devices who are the deemed 'producers' under India's E-Waste Rules Environmental compliance officers and legal teams at conglomerates with diversified portfolios spanning energy, electronics, and healthcare

Key Obligations & Deadlines Effective Date — 1 April 2023: All obligations under the Amendment Rules came into force. Companies in the solar PV and medical device sectors should treat this date as the baseline for compliance assessment. Substance Restriction Compliance: Manufacturers and importers of solar panels, PV modules, and medical devices (non-implanted) must ensure that cadmium and lead content in covered products complies with the threshold limits specified in Schedule II of the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022. Review existing product specifications against permitted concentration values. EPR Registration and Target-Setting: Producers newly brought under Schedule II coverage (solar PV and medical devices) must register on the CPCB EPR portal if not already registered, and set EPR targets for collection and recycling of end-of-life products containing the restricted substances. CPCB Disclosure Readiness: Under the amended Rule 16(5), companies must maintain up-to-date, accessible records of hazardous substance content across their product lines — ready to be submitted to CPCB upon request. This replaces the earlier static documentation requirement but demands a more rigorous internal data management system. Supply Chain Due Diligence: Engage tier-1 and tier-2 suppliers to obtain material declarations (preferably aligned with IEC 62474 or equivalent standards) confirming the presence or absence of cadmium and lead in components used in covered product categories. Existing Inventory Review: Audit stock and products manufactured before 1 April 2023 to determine whether they contain restricted substances above threshold limits and assess how these will be managed, disclosed, or phased out. Legal and Documentation Update: Update product compliance declarations, technical files, and import documentation to reflect the new Schedule II entries. Ensure that EPR annual returns filed with CPCB account for the newly regulated product categories.

Climate Transition Angle At first glance, this amendment may appear to be a narrow regulatory housekeeping exercise. In reality, it sits at the intersection of two of the most important climate transition trends affecting Indian industry: the explosive growth of renewable energy infrastructure and the rapid expansion of the domestic medical technology sector. India's solar capacity ambitions are staggering — the government targets 500 GW of renewable energy by 2030, with solar PV forming the backbone of that expansion. However, the lifecycle environmental cost of solar panels, particularly the cadmium in thin-film technologies and the lead in solders and junction boxes, has historically been under-regulated in India. By formally bringing these substances under the E-Waste Rules, the government is signalling that the clean energy transition must itself be clean — that the environmental liability of end-of-life solar panels cannot be externalised onto communities or landfills. For companies, this means that EPR obligations for solar PV will become increasingly material as the installed base ages and the first wave of large-scale decommissioning begins within the next decade. From a global standards perspective, this move aligns India more closely with the European Union's RoHS (Restriction of Hazardous Substances) Directive, which has long restricted cadmium and lead in electrical and electronic equipment, and with the EU's emerging Solar PV End-of-Life framework under the WEEE Directive. For Indian solar manufacturers exporting to Europe or seeking European investment, this domestic regulatory alignment reduces the compliance gap and strengthens the case for ESG-grade supply chain credentials. The ISSB's IFRS S2 climate disclosure standard and the EU's CSRD both require companies to assess and disclose material climate-related risks, including physical risks (such as extreme weather damage to solar assets) and transition risks (such as regulatory changes affecting product design and end-of-life costs). The inclusion of solar PV in India's E-Waste Rules is precisely the kind of transition risk that sustainability officers must now factor into product lifecycle cost models, capital expenditure planning, and investor disclosures. Companies that have not begun integrating e-waste EPR costs into their solar project financial models are materially underestimating their long-term liability exposure.

What To Do Now Conduct an immediate product and substance audit: Map all solar PV and medical device product lines against Schedule II entries to determine which products contain cadmium or lead above permissible thresholds. Engage your materials engineering or EHS team to produce a substance inventory aligned with IEC 62474 or RoHS declaration formats. Register or update your CPCB EPR profile: If your company manufactures, imports, or brands solar PV panels or relevant medical devices and is not yet registered under the EPR framework, initiate registration on the CPCB centralised portal immediately. Existing registrants should update their product category listings to include the newly covered items. Build a CPCB-ready disclosure system: Since Rule 16(5) now requires hazardous substance information 'as and when required by CPCB,' establish an internal data management process — ideally a Product Compliance Management System — that can generate substance disclosure reports on demand, with version control and audit trails. Engage your supply chain proactively: Issue supplier questionnaires or formal Material Declaration Requests to all tier-1 component suppliers for solar and medtech product lines. Specify that declarations must cover cadmium and lead content and be updated whenever materials or processes change. Build contractual obligations for substance compliance into new supplier agreements. Integrate EPR costs into financial and ESG planning: Work with your CFO and sustainability team to model the long-term cost of EPR targets for solar panels — including collection, dismantling, and recycling — and incorporate these into project IRR calculations, product pricing strategies, and BRSR (Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting) disclosures to investors.

Our Take The E-Waste First Amendment Rules, 2023 are deceptively significant. On the surface, two new Schedule II entries and a tweak to a disclosure clause may seem like minor administrative updates. But read in the context of India's climate ambitions and the global regulatory direction of travel, they represent a clear and irreversible policy signal: the solar energy sector will be held accountable for the full environmental lifecycle of its products, not just the clean kilowatt-hours they generate. The modification to Rule 16(5) also deserves attention — by making disclosure CPCB-triggered rather than document-embedded, the regulator has given itself a powerful supervisory tool while simultaneously raising the stakes for companies that lack robust internal substance data systems. We expect the CPCB to use this authority actively as enforcement capacity improves. Looking ahead, companies should anticipate further expansions of Schedule II to cover additional solar PV materials (such as per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances used in panel coatings) and a potential tightening of EPR collection targets for solar as India's decommissioning cycle accelerates post-2030. Those who build compliance infrastructure now — rather than scrambling when enforcement begins — will be best positioned to compete for institutional capital and long-term project contracts in a market where ESG credentials are increasingly non-negotiable.

title: E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022: What Indian Businesses Must Know Before April 2023 and Beyond
summary: India's E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, notified by the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change on November 2, 2022 and effective from April 1, 2023, replace the 2016 framework with a significantly expanded and more rigorous regime for managing end-of-life electrical and electronic equi

sections.t: Executive Summary India's E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, notified by the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change on November 2, 2022 and effective from April 1, 2023, replace the 2016 framework with a significantly expanded and more rigorous regime for managing end-of-life electrical and electronic equipment. The new rules extend obligations across the entire value chain — from manufacturers, producers, and importers to refurbishers, dismantlers, and recyclers — and introduce mandatory Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) targets tied to verified recycling outcomes. For Indian businesses, this is not merely a compliance checkbox: it signals a structural shift toward circular economy principles that will reshape procurement, product design, and supply chain accountability for years to come.

What Changed The E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 supersede the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2016, which had been criticised for weak enforcement, poor collection infrastructure, and an informal recycling sector that processed over 90% of India's e-waste without adequate environmental safeguards. The 2022 rules were drafted following public consultations initiated in May 2022 and came into force on April 1, 2023. The most consequential change is the formalisation and tightening of Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR). Producers — defined broadly to include any entity that manufactures, assembles, or imports electrical and electronic equipment (EEE) listed in Schedule I — are now legally accountable for ensuring their products are recycled through registered recyclers in accordance with annual EPR targets set out in Schedules III and IV. These targets escalate year-on-year, meaning the compliance burden grows over time. The rules also introduce the concept of 'large consumers' — entities that have used at least 1,000 units of any Schedule I EEE in a financial year, including e-retailers — bringing bulk corporate buyers into the compliance net for the first time. Solar photovoltaic modules, panels, and cells are now explicitly included within the definition of e-waste, a landmark inclusion given India's rapidly growing solar capacity. The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) is designated as the nodal authority and will operate a dedicated online portal for registration, EPR target tracking, and compliance reporting. Importantly, the rules carve out exemptions for waste batteries covered under Battery Waste Management Rules 2022, plastic packaging under Plastic Waste Management Rules 2016, micro enterprises (service sector) as defined under the MSME Development Act 2006, and radioactive waste under the Atomic Energy Act 1962 — ensuring regulatory clarity and avoiding double-counting across overlapping frameworks.

Who Is Affected Manufacturers of EEE: Any company or factory producing electrical and electronic equipment listed in Schedule I — covering categories ranging from IT and telecom equipment to consumer electronics, solar PV modules, and large household appliances. Producers (Brand Owners & Importers): Entities that sell EEE under their own brand, including those who assemble products from third-party components or import finished EEE or its sub-assemblies for sale in India. E-Retailers: Online marketplaces and direct-to-consumer digital sellers of Schedule I EEE are explicitly covered, including platforms operating via internet, social media, or telephone networks. Large Consumers: Corporates, institutions, government bodies, and data centres that have procured and used at least 1,000 units of any single Schedule I product in a financial year. Refurbishers: Entities engaged in the repair, restoration, or upgrading of used EEE for resale. Dismantlers: Entities that dismantle used EEE into components, authorised by the relevant State Pollution Control Board (SPCB) or Pollution Control Committee (PCC) per CPCB guidelines. Recyclers: Facilities that process e-waste to recover materials, required to be registered with CPCB to be eligible to fulfil producers' EPR obligations. Sectors most impacted: Consumer electronics, IT hardware and software, telecommunications, renewable energy (solar), defence electronics, healthcare devices, white goods and home appliances, and automotive electronics.

Key Obligations & Deadlines Effective Date — April 1, 2023: All obligations under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 came into force. Companies that have not yet registered or aligned internal processes are already operating in a compliance deficit. Registration on CPCB Portal: All producers, manufacturers, refurbishers, dismantlers, and recyclers must register on the CPCB's online EPR portal. Registration is a prerequisite for discharging EPR obligations and must be completed before commencing or continuing operations. EPR Target Compliance (Schedules III & IV): Producers must ensure that the quantity of e-waste collected and recycled through CPCB-registered recyclers meets the annual EPR targets specified in Schedule III (for existing producers) and Schedule IV (for new producers). Targets are set as a percentage of EEE placed on the market in prior years and escalate annually — failure to meet targets attracts financial penalties. EPR Certificate Procurement: Producers that cannot directly manage collection and recycling must purchase EPR certificates from registered recyclers through the CPCB portal, providing a market-based compliance mechanism similar to Plastic Waste EPR. Annual Reporting: Producers, large consumers, and recyclers are required to submit annual returns on the CPCB portal detailing quantities of EEE sold, e-waste generated, collected, and recycled. Timelines for annual returns follow the financial year cycle (April–March). Solar PV Module Compliance: Companies deploying or decommissioning solar PV modules must now account for end-of-life management of panels within the e-waste framework — a new obligation with no precedent under the 2016 rules. EPR targets for solar PV producers will be phased in as the technology reaches end-of-life at scale. Large Consumer Obligations: Corporate entities meeting the 1,000-unit threshold must ensure their discarded EEE is channelled to authorised collection centres or registered dismantlers/recyclers, and must maintain records of e-waste disposal for audit purposes. Refurbisher Registration and Reporting: Refurbishers must register with CPCB, maintain records of EEE received for refurbishment, and ensure that non-refurbishable residual waste is handed over to authorised dismantlers or recyclers. Orphan Products (Unbranded/Defunct-Brand EEE): The rules address 'ownerless products' — unbranded or aggregated EEE, or products from companies that have ceased operations. CPCB and SPCBs are tasked with managing historical e-waste, but industry associations and recyclers should engage proactively on implementation guidance.

Climate Transition Angle The E-Waste Rules 2022 sit at the intersection of India's domestic regulatory evolution and global sustainability megatrends. Understanding this broader context is essential for companies positioning themselves for long-term resilience. Circular Economy as a Carbon Strategy: E-waste recycling is not just a waste management issue — it is a carbon management issue. Manufacturing new electronic components from virgin raw materials is extraordinarily energy-intensive. Recovering metals such as gold, copper, palladium, lithium, and rare earth elements from e-waste through formal recycling channels reduces the need for energy-intensive primary extraction and processing, directly lowering scope 3 emissions for EEE manufacturers and their supply chains. Companies that build closed-loop material recovery into their operations will have a measurable advantage in life-cycle carbon accounting. Solar PV End-of-Life Risk: India's solar capacity has grown explosively, and the first large wave of module decommissioning is approaching. The International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA) estimates that by 2050, cumulative global solar PV waste could reach 78 million tonnes. India, as one of the world's largest solar markets, faces a material physical and regulatory risk if end-of-life infrastructure is not built now. The inclusion of solar PV in the 2022 rules is therefore both timely and strategically significant — companies in the renewable energy sector must begin planning EPR compliance for modules being commissioned today. Supply Chain Traceability and Global Standards Alignment: Major global frameworks — including the International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) under IFRS S2, the European Union's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD), and the EU's Ecodesign for Sustainable Products Regulation — are driving demand for product-level material traceability and circular economy disclosures. Indian exporters and listed companies with international investors are increasingly expected to demonstrate responsible end-of-life product management. The E-Waste Rules 2022 provide the domestic regulatory baseline; companies should map their compliance posture against ISSB and CSRD expectations to avoid a two-speed compliance burden. BRSR Core and ESG Disclosure: SEBI's Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting (BRSR) Core framework, mandatory for the top 150 listed companies from FY2023-24, includes product stewardship and waste management as key disclosure indicators. E-waste compliance data — volumes recycled, EPR certificates held, recycler audit outcomes — will feed directly into BRSR disclosures and investor ESG assessments. Non-compliance is therefore a reputational and investor-relations risk, not only a regulatory one.

What To Do Now Conduct an Immediate EPR Liability Assessment: Map all Schedule I EEE products your company manufactures, imports, sells, or procures above the 1,000-unit threshold. Quantify your EPR obligation for the current financial year using the target percentages in Schedules III and IV. If you have not yet registered on the CPCB portal, treat this as a critical compliance gap requiring immediate resolution — you are already operating in arrears. Audit Your Recycler and Dismantler Partnerships: EPR certificates are only valid if sourced from CPCB-registered recyclers. Verify the registration status of all e-waste handling partners. Conduct due diligence on their environmental and safety practices — engaging with informal or unregistered operators not only invalidates your EPR compliance but creates significant liability and reputational risk. Integrate Solar PV End-of-Life Planning into Project Lifecycles: If your company is a solar EPC developer, IPP, or corporate with captive solar installations, begin modelling e-waste volumes from modules approaching end-of-life or likely to be replaced during repowering cycles. Engage with CPCB on sector-specific EPR guidance for solar PV and build decommissioning costs and compliance obligations into project financial models now. Align E-Waste Data with BRSR and ESG Reporting Systems: Designate a data owner for e-waste metrics — volumes generated, collected, recycled, and EPR certificates obtained. Ensure this data flows into your BRSR Core disclosures and any ISSB/CSRD-aligned sustainability reports. Gaps in e-waste data are increasingly flagged by ESG rating agencies and institutional investors conducting supply chain due diligence. Engage Legal and Sustainability Counsel on Escalating Targets: The EPR targets under Schedules III and IV are designed to escalate annually. Build a multi-year compliance roadmap — not just a point-in-time fix — that anticipates rising obligations. Consider engaging with industry associations (MAIT, ICEA, FICCI) to contribute to evolving CPCB guidance on implementation, particularly for complex product categories and orphan product management.

Our Take The E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 represent India's most ambitious attempt yet to formalise a sector that has historically operated in the shadows of the informal economy. By expanding EPR to cover e-retailers and large corporate consumers, mandating CPCB-portal-based transparency, including solar PV modules, and linking compliance to verifiable recycling certificates, the framework is structurally more robust than its 2016 predecessor. However, the true test will be enforcement: India generates over 1.6 million metric tonnes of e-waste annually, yet formal recycling capacity remains a fraction of that volume. We expect CPCB to intensify inspections and penalty proceedings against non-compliant producers as the EPR certificate market matures — and companies that have not yet registered or established recycler partnerships will face both financial penalties and the reputational cost of being publicly identified as defaulters. Looking ahead, the convergence of e-waste EPR with SEBI's BRSR disclosures, ISSB climate standards, and the EU's incoming digital product passport requirements means that e-waste compliance is fast becoming a boardroom issue, not just an environmental affairs one. Companies that treat the 2022 rules as a strategic opportunity to build circular economy capabilities — not merely as a compliance burden — will be better positioned to access international capital, win global supply chain contracts, and contribute meaningfully to India's net-zero transition.

title: E-Waste Second Amendment Rules, 2023: What India's Electronics and Refrigeration Industries Must Do Now
summary: India's Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) notified the E-Waste (Management) Second Amendment Rules, 2023 on July 25, 2023, amending the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022. The amendment introduces three critical changes: mandatory safe management of refrigerants generated dur

sections.t: Executive Summary India's Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) notified the E-Waste (Management) Second Amendment Rules, 2023 on July 25, 2023, amending the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022. The amendment introduces three critical changes: mandatory safe management of refrigerants generated during the manufacture and dismantling of air-conditioning and cooling equipment; revised timelines for phasing out hazardous substances (RoHS compliance) across newly classified equipment schedules; and new conversion factors for Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) certificates covering multiple recycling end-products. Companies across electronics manufacturing, HVAC, healthcare equipment, and IT hardware sectors must immediately assess their compliance posture against these updated obligations.

What Changed The E-Waste (Management) Second Amendment Rules, 2023 amend the parent E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 in four substantive areas, effective from the date of gazette notification (July 25, 2023). First, refrigerant management obligations have been inserted into three separate rules — Rule 5 (manufacturers), Rule 7 (dealers/refurbishers), and Rule 9 (dismantlers/recyclers) — requiring that refrigerants generated during the manufacturing process as well as during the dismantling of old air-conditioning and cooling equipment be managed in a safe, leak-proof, and sustainable manner, using only CPCB-approved destruction technologies and following CPCB-issued guidelines. Second, the hazardous substance (RoHS) phase-out timeline has been restructured. Equipment placed on the market before May 1, 2014 (listed under the new Schedule II-B) remains exempt from RoHS restrictions where compliant spare parts are unavailable. Critically, a new Schedule II-C has been created for a broader category of electrical and electronic equipment (EEE) — including advanced telecom, IT, and consumer electronics — placed on the market on or before April 1, 2025, with a further exemption for components and spare parts required for this equipment extending until April 1, 2028. Third, for recyclers generating multiple end-products from a single waste stream, conversion factors will now be determined by CPCB (with Steering Committee approval) to enable proportionate EPR certificate generation across all or some end-products — removing a previous ambiguity that disadvantaged recyclers handling complex material streams. Fourth, Schedule II-A has been introduced to carve out specific exemptions for medical and laboratory monitoring and control equipment, listing 24 categories of applications where lead, cadmium, and mercury are permitted in tightly defined technical uses — such as X-ray tubes, MRI superconductors, pH electrodes, and ultrasonic transducers — aligning India's approach with international RoHS exemption frameworks.

Who Is Affected HVAC and Refrigeration Equipment Manufacturers: Companies manufacturing air conditioners, chillers, cold-storage units, and refrigeration systems — now obligated to manage refrigerants generated during production using CPCB-approved destruction technologies. E-Waste Dismantlers and Recyclers: Facilities dismantling end-of-life air-conditioning and cooling equipment must ensure refrigerant recovery and safe disposal as per CPCB guidelines; recyclers generating multiple end-products gain clarity on EPR certificate conversion factors. Consumer Electronics and IT Hardware Manufacturers and Importers: Companies placing desktops, laptops, notebooks, notepads, printers, copiers, telephones, and televisions on the Indian market — particularly those in the transition window to Schedule II-C — must track their RoHS compliance timelines. Telecom Equipment Manufacturers and Importers: Products transmitting voice, image, or data via telecommunications (newly classified under Schedule II-C, Code ITEW17) face the April 1, 2025 market-placement threshold for RoHS exemption. Medical Device and Laboratory Equipment Manufacturers: Companies producing X-ray systems, MRI machines, ultrasonic diagnostic equipment, pH meters, and portable defibrillators benefit from the new Schedule II-A exemptions but must document and justify their use of restricted substances against the listed categories. Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) Portal Registrants: All producers registered under the E-Waste Management Rules, 2022 need to understand how the new conversion factor provisions affect their EPR certificate calculations and annual compliance filings. Large Format Retailers and E-Commerce Platforms: As facilitators of EEE sales, they must ensure producer partners are compliant with the revised schedules applicable to products listed on their platforms.

Key Obligations & Deadlines Effective Immediately (July 25, 2023): Manufacturers of refrigeration and air-conditioning equipment must ensure that refrigerants generated during the manufacturing process are managed safely, in a leak-proof and sustainable manner, using CPCB-approved destruction technologies — as per new Rule 5(4). Effective Immediately (July 25, 2023): Dismantlers and recyclers of old refrigeration and air-conditioning equipment must comply with new Rule 7(5) and Rule 9(11), mandating safe, leak-proof, and sustainable management of refrigerants from end-of-life equipment using CPCB-approved processes. Effective Immediately (July 25, 2023): Recyclers handling e-waste that generates multiple end-products must engage with CPCB to understand the new conversion factor framework under Rule 14(1)(ii)(c) and adjust their EPR certificate generation methodology accordingly. By April 1, 2025: Manufacturers and importers of Schedule II-C EEE (including advanced telecom and IT equipment) must note that products placed on the market after this date will no longer benefit from the RoHS exemption applicable to Schedule II-C equipment — full RoHS compliance will be required. By April 1, 2028: Even for Schedule II-C equipment placed on market on or before April 1, 2025, all components, consumables, and spare parts must meet RoHS hazardous substance restrictions by this date, unless compliant alternatives remain unavailable. Ongoing: Medical and laboratory equipment manufacturers must maintain documentation demonstrating that use of restricted substances (lead, cadmium, mercury) falls within the specific Schedule II-A exemption categories — this is not a blanket exemption and each application must be individually justified. Ongoing: All regulated entities must monitor CPCB for issuance of specific guidelines on approved refrigerant destruction technologies, as the rules reference CPCB guidelines that are yet to be formally published in their final form.

Climate Transition Angle The refrigerant management provisions in this amendment are far more consequential for India's climate commitments than they might first appear. Refrigerants used in air-conditioning and cooling equipment — particularly hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs) — are among the most potent greenhouse gases, with global warming potentials (GWPs) hundreds to thousands of times higher than CO2. India is a signatory to the Kigali Amendment to the Montreal Protocol, which mandates a phased reduction of HFC consumption. The amendment's requirement for safe, leak-proof, and sustainable refrigerant management directly supports India's Kigali compliance obligations and its Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) under the Paris Agreement. From a physical risk perspective, India's rapidly expanding cooling demand — driven by rising temperatures, urbanisation, and income growth — means that refrigerant leakage during manufacturing and end-of-life dismantling represents a growing emissions source. The International Energy Agency estimates that the cooling sector could account for 45% of India's electricity demand by 2050. Poorly managed refrigerant recovery today creates a significant and measurable Scope 1 emissions liability for manufacturers, and a Scope 3 liability for companies throughout the cooling equipment value chain. On the disclosure front, global reporting frameworks are converging on refrigerant management as a material climate issue. The ISSB's IFRS S2 (Climate-related Disclosures) requires companies to disclose material Scope 1 emissions, which include direct refrigerant releases. The EU's CSRD, which Indian exporters and subsidiaries of European multinationals must increasingly navigate, requires detailed value-chain emissions reporting under ESRS E1. India's own SEBI BRSR Core framework for top-listed companies is evolving in the same direction. Companies that treat this amendment merely as a waste management compliance issue — rather than as a climate and ESG disclosure issue — will find themselves underprepared for the next wave of mandatory climate reporting. The RoHS schedule restructuring also has supply chain implications. As India integrates more deeply into global electronics supply chains — particularly under the Production Linked Incentive (PLI) schemes — adherence to hazardous substance restrictions is a baseline market access requirement for exports to the EU, UK, and increasingly Southeast Asian markets. The new Schedule II-C timelines give Indian manufacturers a transition window, but global buyers and EPR scheme administrators in export markets will not extend the same flexibility.

What To Do Now Conduct a Refrigerant Management Audit: HVAC and refrigeration manufacturers and dismantlers should immediately audit their current refrigerant handling procedures during both production and end-of-life processing. Map all points of refrigerant release, quantify volumes, and assess whether current recovery and destruction methods align with CPCB's forthcoming guidelines. Engage a certified environmental consultant to identify gaps and implement a corrective action plan. Review and Reclassify Your Product Portfolio Against Updated Schedules: Legal, compliance, and product management teams should jointly review all EEE product lines against the revised Schedule II-B and the new Schedule II-C. Identify which products fall under which schedule, note the applicable RoHS exemption windows (April 1, 2025 and April 1, 2028), and create a product-level compliance roadmap to eliminate restricted substances from components and spare parts before deadlines lapse. Update EPR Registrations and Certificate Calculations: Recyclers and producers registered on the CPCB EPR portal should review the implications of the new conversion factor provisions under Rule 14(1)(ii)(c). Engage with CPCB to clarify how conversion factors will be determined for your specific waste streams and end-products, and update your EPR compliance model accordingly to avoid certificate shortfalls or over-reporting. Build Refrigerant Management into Climate and ESG Disclosures: Sustainability and CFO teams should begin quantifying refrigerant-related GHG emissions (particularly HFC releases) as part of their Scope 1 inventory, in preparation for BRSR Core disclosures and any voluntary ISSB/TCFD-aligned reporting. This positions the company ahead of mandatory climate disclosure requirements rather than scrambling to comply retrospectively. Monitor CPCB for Implementing Guidelines: The amendment makes several provisions conditional on CPCB issuing specific guidelines — particularly on approved refrigerant destruction technologies. Assign a regulatory affairs team member to actively track CPCB notifications and circulars, and ensure that once guidelines are published, internal SOPs are updated within 60 days.

Our Take The E-Waste Second Amendment Rules, 2023 are deceptively significant. On the surface, they look like a technical amendment — tweaking schedules, inserting sub-rules, and adjusting deadlines. But taken together, these changes signal that India's regulatory architecture around electronic and electrical waste is maturing rapidly, and is beginning to align with both the Kigali Protocol's refrigerant phase-down agenda and the global convergence on RoHS-equivalent hazardous substance restrictions. The refrigerant management clauses in particular deserve board-level attention in the HVAC and cooling sector — not because they impose immediate penalties, but because they establish the legal foundation for what will almost certainly become a more stringent, monitored, and reported regime as India's climate commitments deepen. The companies that treat these provisions as an opportunity to build best-in-class refrigerant recovery infrastructure today will gain a durable competitive advantage in both domestic and export markets. Those that wait for CPCB enforcement action will find themselves managing a compliance crisis and a reputational risk simultaneously. The window to get ahead of this is open — but it is narrowing.

title: E-Waste Third Amendment Rules, 2024: What India's Electronics Industry Must Know About EPR Certificate Trading and New Compliance Flexibilities
summary: India's Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change notified the E-Waste (Management) Amendment Rules, 2024 on March 8, 2024, introducing three significant changes to the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022: a clarified definition of 'dismantler,' a formal government power to extend filing deadl

sections.t: Executive Summary India's Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change notified the E-Waste (Management) Amendment Rules, 2024 on March 8, 2024, introducing three significant changes to the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022: a clarified definition of 'dismantler,' a formal government power to extend filing deadlines by up to nine months, and — most consequentially — a structured marketplace for trading Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) certificates, complete with price floors and ceilings set by the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB). For manufacturers, producers, importers, refurbishers, and recyclers of electrical and electronic equipment (EEE), these amendments create both new compliance pathways and new strategic opportunities. Companies that move quickly to understand the EPR certificate trading mechanism will be better positioned to manage their obligations cost-effectively while staying ahead of tightening enforcement.

What Changed The E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 established India's current Extended Producer Responsibility framework for electronics, requiring producers and manufacturers to ensure that a defined percentage of the e-waste generated by their products is collected and recycled each year. The Third Amendment, effective from March 8, 2024, modifies three specific provisions of those rules. First, the definition of 'dismantler' (Rule 3, clause f) has been tightened. A dismantler is now explicitly defined as any person or entity engaged in dismantling used electrical and electronic equipment and components in accordance with CPCB guidelines. This alignment with CPCB standards raises the bar for who qualifies as a legitimate dismantler in the EPR ecosystem — a critical point for producers who source dismantling services to fulfil their obligations. Second, a new Rule 9A grants the Central Government the power to relax, by order, any return or report filing deadline under the rules for manufacturers, producers, refurbishers, or recyclers — for a further period not exceeding nine months. This is a practical relief mechanism, acknowledging that the EPR portal and compliance infrastructure sometimes face operational challenges, and providing a formal legal basis for deadline extensions rather than ad hoc administrative orders. Third, and most strategically significant, sub-rules (7) through (10) have been inserted into Rule 15, creating the legal architecture for a formal EPR Certificate Exchange Platform. The Central Government may establish one or more platforms for the exchange or transfer of EPR certificates, operated under CPCB-recommended guidelines. Critically, the CPCB will set price bands for certificate trading: the ceiling price will equal 100% of the environmental compensation (penalty) for non-fulfilment of EPR obligations under Rule 22, and the floor price will equal 30% of that same environmental compensation. All trades between registered entities must occur through the portal and within these price bands.

Who Is Affected Producers and manufacturers of electrical and electronic equipment (EEE) covered under Schedule I of the E-Waste Rules — including consumer electronics, IT and telecom equipment, large and small household appliances, lighting equipment, and medical devices Importers of EEE who are classified as 'producers' under the rules and carry EPR obligations for imported products placed on the Indian market Refurbishers of used EEE who are required to file annual returns and meet EPR targets Recyclers and dismantlers authorised under the CPCB framework, whose eligibility to generate EPR certificates depends on meeting the revised CPCB guideline-aligned definition of 'dismantler' Extended Producer Responsibility Authorisation (EPRA) holders — both large multinational electronics brands and Indian manufacturers — who buy or sell EPR certificates to meet annual collection and recycling targets Compliance and sustainability teams at fast-moving consumer electronics companies, white goods manufacturers, laptop and mobile device brands, and EV component producers (where applicable under EEE schedules) EPR aggregators and intermediaries who facilitate certificate transactions on behalf of smaller producers

Key Obligations & Deadlines Effective immediately (March 8, 2024): The amended definition of 'dismantler' is in force — producers must verify that their dismantling partners operate strictly in accordance with CPCB guidelines; contracts or empanelment agreements with dismantlers should be reviewed and updated accordingly Effective immediately (March 8, 2024): The Rule 9A relaxation mechanism is available — however, deadline extensions are not automatic; they require a Central Government order, so companies should not assume extensions will be granted and must continue filing returns on time unless a specific order is issued Upcoming (timeline to be notified): Watch for Central Government orders establishing the EPR Certificate Exchange Platform under Rule 15(7) — the legal basis now exists, but the platform's operational launch depends on CPCB issuing guidelines and the government notifying the platform; companies should monitor the CPCB portal and MoEFCC notifications closely Upcoming (to be fixed by CPCB): The CPCB will publish the highest and lowest price bands for EPR certificate trading — the ceiling is pegged at 100% of the Rule 22 environmental compensation and the floor at 30%; once notified, these prices will govern all certificate transactions Ongoing: Producers must continue to meet annual EPR targets for e-waste collection and recycling as per their EPRA; the new trading platform, once live, will provide an additional compliance route but does not replace the obligation to achieve targets Ongoing: All EPR certificate exchanges must occur through the designated government portal between registered entities only — off-portal or informal certificate transactions will not be recognised for compliance purposes

Climate Transition Angle The introduction of a regulated EPR certificate trading market for e-waste is far more than a procedural compliance update — it is a structural step toward embedding circular economy principles into India's electronics value chain, with direct relevance to climate transition planning. E-waste is one of the fastest-growing waste streams globally, and its improper management releases hazardous substances — including persistent organic pollutants and heavy metals — that degrade ecosystems and contribute to soil and water contamination, compounding physical climate risks already facing vulnerable communities. By formalising a price-signalling mechanism through certificate trading, the amendment creates a financial incentive for producers to invest in legitimate, high-quality recycling infrastructure rather than relying on the informal sector. From a global standards perspective, this development sits squarely within the scope of what the International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) — through IFRS S1 and S2 — and the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) require companies to disclose: namely, how they manage their waste obligations, circular economy commitments, and transition risks in their supply chains. For Indian subsidiaries of global electronics companies already reporting under CSRD or preparing for ISSB-aligned disclosures, the EPR certificate trading mechanism will become a material data point — demonstrating that India's regulatory framework is converging with global circular economy expectations. Supply chain implications are equally significant. The tightened dismantler definition aligns with the principle that only verified, standard-compliant facilities should handle hazardous e-waste — a concern directly connected to Scope 3 value chain emissions and responsible sourcing commitments. As buyers of EPR certificates, producers are effectively co-financing India's formal recycling ecosystem, which supports recovery of critical minerals (lithium, cobalt, rare earths) essential for the energy transition. Building robust e-waste recycling infrastructure today is an investment in domestic critical mineral security tomorrow.

What To Do Now Audit your dismantling partners immediately: Cross-check all empanelled dismantlers against current CPCB guidelines to confirm they meet the revised Rule 3(f) definition; any dismantler not operating in strict accordance with CPCB standards could invalidate the EPR credits generated through their activities — update vendor agreements and conduct physical or documentation-based audits as needed Model your EPR certificate position and price exposure: Using the Rule 22 environmental compensation as the benchmark, calculate the implied ceiling and floor prices for EPR certificates that the CPCB is likely to set; stress-test your annual EPR budget against a scenario where you need to purchase certificates at the ceiling price — this will inform procurement strategy once the exchange platform launches Engage with the CPCB portal and industry associations proactively: The EPR Certificate Exchange Platform is coming; register your interest, ensure your EPRA and portal credentials are current, and participate in any CPCB consultation processes on platform guidelines — early registered entities will have an advantage in navigating the new market Integrate EPR compliance data into your sustainability reporting framework: If your organisation reports under BRSR (Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report), GRI, or is preparing for ISSB/CSRD-aligned disclosures, begin capturing EPR certificate generation, purchase, and surrender data as a formal metric — this positions your company ahead of mandatory ESG disclosure requirements and demonstrates circular economy accountability to investors Brief your legal and compliance teams on Rule 9A — but do not rely on it: The nine-month extension power is a safety valve, not a routine tool; treat filing deadlines as firm, document your compliance calendar, and only engage the Rule 9A mechanism through proper channels if a genuine operational barrier arises — proactive compliance is always preferable to seeking ex-post relief

Our Take The E-Waste Third Amendment Rules, 2024 may appear modest in length — just four clauses — but they signal a maturing of India's EPR governance architecture that deserves serious strategic attention. The introduction of a price-banded EPR certificate exchange is India's most explicit move yet toward a market-based environmental compliance mechanism in the waste sector, drawing conceptually from models seen in carbon markets and plastic credit systems. The design — with a ceiling anchored to the penalty for non-compliance and a floor set at 30% of that penalty — is pragmatic: it prevents both market cornering at artificially high prices and a race to the bottom that would undermine the incentive to actually collect and recycle e-waste. What comes next will be decisive: the quality of CPCB's operational guidelines for the platform, the robustness of the portal's verification infrastructure, and the government's willingness to enforce against off-portal trades will determine whether this becomes a genuine driver of formal recycling capacity or another compliance checkbox. For forward-thinking companies, the message is clear — build your EPR strategy now, invest in verified recycling partnerships, and position yourself as a net seller of certificates rather than a distressed buyer. The companies that treat EPR as a supply chain and circular economy opportunity, rather than a regulatory cost, will be best placed as India's environmental compliance landscape continues its rapid convergence with global standards.

title: EFRAG & EU CSRD: What Indian Companies Exporting to Europe Must Know Now
summary: The European Union's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD), underpinned by standards developed by EFRAG (European Financial Reporting Advisory Group), is rapidly becoming the most consequential sustainability disclosure regime in the world — and its reach extends well beyond European b

sections.t: Executive Summary The European Union's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD), underpinned by standards developed by EFRAG (European Financial Reporting Advisory Group), is rapidly becoming the most consequential sustainability disclosure regime in the world — and its reach extends well beyond European borders. Indian companies supplying goods or services to EU-based corporations, or those listed on EU-regulated markets, are already within scope of its value chain disclosure requirements. Companies that treat CSRD as a purely European problem do so at significant commercial risk.

What Changed The CSRD replaced the earlier Non-Financial Reporting Directive (NFRD) and dramatically expanded both the scope of companies required to report and the depth of information they must disclose. The European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS), developed by EFRAG and adopted by the European Commission, form the technical backbone of CSRD reporting. These standards cover a sweeping range of topics — climate change (physical and transition risks), biodiversity, water, pollution, workforce conditions, supply chain due diligence, and governance. Crucially, ESRS E1 (Climate) requires large EU companies to disclose their full value chain emissions — Scope 1, 2, and 3 — along with transition plans, scenario analyses, and climate-related financial risks. This means EU buyers will increasingly demand verified emissions data, sustainability performance metrics, and evidence of credible decarbonisation commitments from their Indian suppliers. EFRAG continues to refine sector-specific standards and guidance for Small and Medium Enterprises (SMEs), signalling that the regulatory framework is still evolving and tightening.

Who Is Affected Large Indian exporters supplying directly to EU-listed or EU-headquartered companies in sectors such as textiles, pharmaceuticals, automotive components, IT services, chemicals, and agri-commodities Indian subsidiaries or joint ventures of European parent companies, which may be required to provide consolidated sustainability data Indian companies with securities listed on EU-regulated markets or those raising capital from European institutional investors Mid-sized Indian manufacturers embedded in EU company supply chains — even if not directly reporting under CSRD, they will face contractual data requests from EU customers Indian IT and professional services firms providing ESG data management, assurance, or consulting services to European clients Indian banks and financial institutions with significant exposure to EU-linked corporates or green bond markets

Key Obligations & Deadlines January 2025 (FY2024 data): Large EU public-interest entities (>500 employees, already under NFRD) must publish first CSRD-compliant reports — their Scope 3 data requests to Indian suppliers begin immediately January 2026 (FY2025 data): All large EU companies (meeting two of three thresholds: >250 employees, >€40M turnover, >€20M total assets) must report under CSRD — significantly expanding the pool of EU buyers requesting value chain ESG data from Indian partners January 2027 (FY2026 data): Listed SMEs on EU-regulated markets come into scope — relevant for Indian companies cross-listed in Europe Ongoing — EFRAG Sector Standards: Sector-specific ESRS (covering oil & gas, mining, textiles, food & agriculture, and others) are under development; Indian companies in these sectors should monitor EFRAG's publication calendar closely Ongoing — Double Materiality Assessments: EU companies must conduct 'double materiality' assessments identifying both how sustainability issues affect the business AND how the business impacts society and environment — Indian suppliers will be asked to contribute data to these assessments Immediate — Contractual ESG clauses: EU buyers are already embedding CSRD-aligned data-sharing requirements into supplier contracts and RFP processes; Indian companies should review new and renewal contracts carefully

Climate Transition Angle CSRD and the EFRAG standards are not merely a reporting exercise — they are a structural mechanism to align European corporate behaviour with the EU's 2050 net-zero target and the Paris Agreement's 1.5°C pathway. For Indian companies, this creates a dual imperative. First, physical climate risk: ESRS E1 requires EU companies to assess and disclose climate-related physical risks across their supply chains. Indian manufacturing hubs — particularly in coastal regions, river basins, and heat-stressed geographies — face material physical risks from extreme weather, water stress, and sea-level rise. EU buyers will scrutinise whether their Indian suppliers have assessed and are managing these risks, as supply chain disruptions become a financial disclosure item for European companies. Second, transition risk and global standard convergence: CSRD's architecture closely mirrors the International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) framework — specifically IFRS S2 on climate — and has significant overlap with the Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures (TCFD). India's own SEBI Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report (BRSR) Core framework, and RBI's emerging climate risk guidance, are also converging toward these global standards. Indian companies that build CSRD-compatible reporting capabilities will simultaneously be better positioned for domestic BRSR compliance and ISSB-aligned disclosures as India's regulatory expectations rise.

What To Do Now Map your EU exposure immediately: Identify all EU-domiciled customers, investors, and business partners. Determine which of them fall under CSRD's first and second waves of reporting — these entities will begin requesting supplier ESG data in 2025 and 2026 respectively Establish a Scope 3 emissions baseline: Begin measuring your Greenhouse Gas (GHG) emissions across all three scopes using the GHG Protocol. EU buyers will require credible, ideally third-party verified emissions data. Engage a reputable assurance provider familiar with both GHG Protocol and ESRS requirements Conduct a gap assessment against ESRS E1 and ESRS S1/S2: Review your current sustainability disclosures against the European Sustainability Reporting Standards — particularly on climate (E1), your own workforce (S1), and workers in your value chain (S2). Identify material gaps in data collection, governance, and reporting infrastructure Engage legal and procurement teams on contract review: Ensure your legal and procurement teams are reviewing new EU customer contracts for sustainability data-sharing clauses, audit rights, and representations. Negotiate reasonable timelines and data specifications before signing Align internal reporting systems with ISSB/ESRS architecture: Invest in ESG data management systems that can produce structured, auditable sustainability data aligned with both ESRS and ISSB IFRS S1/S2. This dual-standard compatibility will future-proof your reporting for both EU market access and domestic SEBI BRSR Core requirements

Our Take CSRD is not on the horizon for Indian businesses — it is already at the door. The first wave of EU companies began reporting on FY2024 data in early 2025, meaning their Scope 3 supply chain data requests are live today. What makes CSRD particularly significant compared to previous sustainability initiatives is its legal enforceability: non-compliant EU companies face penalties, and they will pass that compliance pressure directly down their supply chains through contractual requirements, procurement scoring, and supplier audits. For Indian companies, the window to treat sustainability as a voluntary communications exercise has closed. Those who move now to build credible, data-driven sustainability reporting capabilities will gain a durable competitive advantage in EU markets — and will find themselves well-prepared for the tightening domestic regulatory environment that SEBI, RBI, and MCA are clearly signalling. Those who wait will face contract losses, market access barriers, and a far steeper — and more expensive — remediation curve.

title: EFRAG & EU CSRD: What Indian Companies Must Know About Europe's Sustainability Reporting Revolution
summary: The European Union's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD), developed in close collaboration with EFRAG (European Financial Reporting Advisory Group), is rapidly becoming the world's most demanding sustainability disclosure framework — and its reach extends well beyond European borders

sections.t: Executive Summary The European Union's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD), developed in close collaboration with EFRAG (European Financial Reporting Advisory Group), is rapidly becoming the world's most demanding sustainability disclosure framework — and its reach extends well beyond European borders. Indian companies that supply to, invest in, or operate within EU markets are increasingly expected to provide granular ESG data to their European counterparts. Boards and sustainability officers who treat CSRD as a 'European problem' do so at significant commercial and reputational risk.

What Changed The CSRD replaced the older Non-Financial Reporting Directive (NFRD) and dramatically expanded the scope, depth, and assurance requirements for sustainability disclosures across the EU. EFRAG, as the technical architect of the European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS), has developed a comprehensive set of standards covering climate, biodiversity, water, social factors, and governance — all underpinned by the concept of 'double materiality,' meaning companies must report both on how sustainability issues affect their business AND how their business affects the world. What makes this directly relevant to India is the 'value chain' provision. EU-based companies subject to CSRD are required to collect and disclose sustainability data from their entire value chain — including non-EU suppliers, joint venture partners, and investee companies. This means that even if an Indian company is not itself subject to CSRD, it will face mounting requests from its EU customers and investors for ESRS-aligned ESG disclosures. EFRAG continues to publish implementation guidance, Q&A documents, and sector-specific standards that shape exactly what data will be requested.

Who Is Affected Large Indian exporters to the EU across sectors including textiles, pharmaceuticals, automotive components, chemicals, IT services, and agri-commodities Indian subsidiaries or joint ventures of EU-headquartered companies already subject to CSRD reporting obligations Indian companies listed on EU exchanges or with significant EU institutional investor bases SMEs in the supply chain of large EU corporates, who face indirect disclosure requests even if not formally regulated Indian financial institutions and asset managers with EU clients or co-investment structures Companies preparing for BRSR Core alignment, as CSRD/ESRS represents the global benchmark those frameworks are converging toward

Key Obligations & Deadlines Wave 1 (FY2024, reporting in 2025): ~11,500 large EU public-interest entities already under NFRD — their value chain data requests to Indian suppliers are live NOW Wave 2 (FY2025, reporting in 2026): All large EU companies (500+ employees OR €40M+ turnover OR €20M+ balance sheet, meeting 2 of 3 criteria) — significantly expands the number of EU buyers requesting Indian supplier ESG data Wave 3 (FY2026, reporting in 2027): Listed EU SMEs — broadens the net further into mid-market supply chains Non-EU parent companies with EU-listed securities or €150M+ EU net turnover and at least one large EU subsidiary or branch: subject to CSRD from FY2028 — directly capturing large Indian multinationals with EU presence EFRAG sector-specific ESRS standards (covering oil & gas, mining, retail, food & beverages, etc.) expected to be finalised progressively — Indian exporters in these sectors should monitor EFRAG's publication calendar closely Limited assurance on CSRD disclosures required from Wave 1; reasonable assurance expected by 2028 — internal data quality must improve now, not at deadline

Climate Transition Angle CSRD and the ESRS are not merely a compliance exercise — they are the EU's primary mechanism for redirecting capital toward companies credibly managing climate transition risks. ESRS E1 (Climate Change) is the most detailed and non-waivable standard, requiring companies to disclose Scope 1, 2, and 3 greenhouse gas emissions, transition plans aligned with the Paris Agreement's 1.5°C pathway, physical climate risk assessments, and carbon price assumptions embedded in financial planning. For Indian companies, this creates an urgent intersection with physical climate risk. India is among the world's most climate-vulnerable economies — extreme heat, monsoon variability, and water stress directly affect operations across manufacturing, agriculture, and infrastructure. EU buyers and investors now need to understand these physical risks within their own supply chain disclosures. Indian companies that can quantify and communicate their climate resilience will be commercially advantaged over those that cannot. The CSRD's ESRS framework was deliberately designed to interoperate with the ISSB's IFRS S1 and S2 standards, which India's SEBI and ICAI are actively considering for integration into the BRSR (Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting) framework. Companies investing in CSRD-readiness are simultaneously building the foundation for next-generation domestic compliance. The direction of travel is unambiguous: granular, assured, comparable sustainability data is becoming a non-negotiable condition of market participation globally.

What To Do Now Conduct a CSRD Exposure Mapping: Identify all EU-based customers, investors, and partners and assess which are already subject to CSRD Wave 1 or Wave 2. Prioritise those relationships where ESG data requests are imminent or already arriving — this is your most urgent compliance trigger. Perform a Double Materiality Assessment (DMA): Even if not formally required under Indian law, conducting a DMA aligned with ESRS methodology will future-proof your reporting, satisfy EU value chain queries, and align with emerging BRSR Core expectations. Engage a qualified ESG advisor to facilitate the process. Build Scope 3 Emissions Capability: ESRS E1 requires EU companies to disclose Scope 3 emissions — which means your emissions become their emissions. Begin collecting activity data across your own supply chain, establish emission factors for key materials and logistics, and invest in credible carbon accounting software or methodology. Review and Strengthen ESG Data Governance: CSRD requires limited (and eventually reasonable) third-party assurance. Audit your current data collection processes for GHG emissions, energy, water, waste, and social indicators. Identify gaps, assign data ownership, and implement controls as if assurance is already required — because for your EU customers, it effectively is. Engage Legal and Commercial Teams on Contractual Implications: EU companies may begin embedding CSRD data-sharing obligations into supplier contracts. Ensure your legal, procurement, and sustainability teams are aligned on what data you can provide, at what frequency, and under what confidentiality terms — before you are negotiating from a position of weakness.

Our Take CSRD and EFRAG's ESRS represent a structural shift, not a regulatory cycle. The EU has effectively established sustainability disclosure as a condition of market access, and through value chain provisions, that condition now applies to Indian companies whether or not they ever set foot in Brussels. What we are witnessing is the gradual emergence of a global ESG disclosure floor — shaped by CSRD in Europe, ISSB internationally, and progressively by SEBI's BRSR Core domestically. Indian companies that move early — investing in emissions accounting, double materiality assessments, and data governance — will not only avoid the commercial disruption of being unable to answer EU buyer questionnaires, but will build a genuine competitive advantage as sustainability transparency becomes a procurement and investment criterion. The question is no longer whether Indian businesses will need to report to this standard, but whether they will be ready when their largest customers demand it.

title: Environmental Compensation (EC) Guidelines Under E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022: What Producers, Importers & Brand Owners Must Do Now
summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued Environmental Compensation (EC) guidelines under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, establishing a structured penalty framework for entities that fail to meet their Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) obligations for electronic waste. Compa

sections.t: Executive Summary The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued Environmental Compensation (EC) guidelines under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, establishing a structured penalty framework for entities that fail to meet their Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) obligations for electronic waste. Companies — including producers, importers, and brand owners of electrical and electronic equipment — now face quantified financial consequences for non-compliance with EPR targets. This development signals a decisive shift from voluntary adherence to enforceable accountability in India's e-waste governance regime.

What Changed India's E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 placed the onus of responsible end-of-life management of electronic products squarely on those who manufacture, import, or sell them — a framework known as Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR). Under EPR, companies are required to ensure that a prescribed quantity of e-waste is collected and channelled to authorised recyclers each year, with targets set and tracked through the CPCB's centralised EPR portal. While the 2022 Rules established this obligation, there was previously limited regulatory clarity on the exact financial consequences of falling short of EPR targets. The newly issued Environmental Compensation (EC) guidelines fill this gap. They define how Environmental Compensation — effectively a penalty or levy — is calculated when a producer, importer, or brand owner (collectively referred to as 'obligated entities') fails to fulfil their annual EPR collection and recycling obligations within the stipulated timeframe. In plain terms: if your company has not met its e-waste EPR targets, CPCB now has a clear, published formula to determine how much you owe as Environmental Compensation. This amount is intended to reflect the environmental cost of the e-waste that was not properly managed, and the funds collected are earmarked for remediation and strengthening the formal e-waste recycling ecosystem.

Who Is Affected Producers of Electrical and Electronic Equipment (EEE): Manufacturers of laptops, mobile phones, televisions, refrigerators, washing machines, and all Schedule I equipment under the Rules. Importers: Companies importing EEE or components into India, including those importing for captive use. Brand Owners: Indian companies selling EEE under their own brand, even if manufacturing is outsourced domestically or abroad. Refurbishers: Entities engaged in refurbishment of used EEE with EPR obligations under the 2022 Rules. Large Enterprises & SMEs alike: EPR registration is mandatory regardless of company size; EC liability scales with the volume of shortfall, meaning no entity is exempt. EPR Compliance Managers, Legal & Finance Teams: Those responsible for portal submissions, target reconciliation, and financial provisioning for regulatory risk. Investors & Lenders: Financial institutions with exposure to consumer electronics, IT hardware, and appliance sectors face heightened ESG risk from portfolio companies with unresolved EPR gaps.

Key Obligations & Deadlines EPR Target Fulfilment: All registered producers, importers, and brand owners must meet their annual EPR collection and recycling targets as assigned on the CPCB EPR portal. Shortfalls trigger EC liability. Environmental Compensation Calculation: EC is computed based on the quantity of e-waste (in metric tonnes) by which the entity fell short of its EPR target, multiplied by a prescribed rate reflecting the environmental cost of unmanaged e-waste. Obligated entities should immediately audit their portal accounts to quantify any shortfall. Mandatory Disclosure on EPR Portal: All EPR activities — collection, recycling certificates (RCs) from authorised recyclers/dismantlers — must be logged and reconciled on the CPCB EPR portal. Incomplete or inaccurate portal records are themselves a compliance risk. Payment of EC Where Applicable: Entities identified as having unmet obligations must deposit Environmental Compensation as directed by CPCB. Delayed payment may attract additional interest or escalation to enforcement action under Environment Protection Act, 1986. Recycling Certificate (RC) Procurement: EPR compliance is only recognised through valid RCs issued by CPCB-registered recyclers. Companies must ensure their recycling partners are currently registered and that RCs are properly transferred and recorded. Ongoing Annual Compliance Cycle: EPR targets are set annually. Companies must build internal processes to monitor target progress throughout the year — not just at year-end — to avoid shortfalls. Deadline Note: Specific EC deposit deadlines will be communicated by CPCB on a case-by-case basis following target assessment. Companies should proactively engage with CPCB and their EPR portal accounts without waiting for individual notices.

Climate Transition Angle E-waste is not merely a compliance issue — it sits at the intersection of climate risk, circular economy transition, and global supply chain sustainability. Electronic waste is one of the fastest-growing solid waste streams globally, and improper management releases hazardous substances (lead, mercury, cadmium) while also squandering the embedded energy and critical minerals — including lithium, cobalt, and rare earth elements — that are essential for the clean energy transition itself. Batteries, solar panels, and EV components all rely on materials that, if recovered through formal recycling, reduce dependence on virgin mining and its associated carbon footprint. From a physical risk perspective, unmanaged e-waste in informal recycling clusters creates soil and groundwater contamination that compounds climate-related water stress, particularly relevant in water-scarce regions of India. Companies with supply chains reliant on such regions face compounding risks. On the global standards front, both the ISSB's IFRS S2 (climate-related disclosures) and the EU's CSRD increasingly require companies to account for Scope 3 emissions and circular economy practices — including end-of-life product management. Indian companies exporting to the EU or listed on global exchanges will find that robust EPR compliance and EC avoidance are not just domestic regulatory matters but material ESG data points for international investors and customers. The EU's Ecodesign for Sustainable Products Regulation (ESPR) and Digital Product Passport requirements further reinforce that responsible e-waste management will be a market-access issue, not just a regulatory one, within this decade.

What To Do Now Conduct an Immediate EPR Gap Audit: Log into the CPCB EPR portal today and reconcile your registered EPR targets against actual Recycling Certificates (RCs) procured and uploaded for the current and previous compliance years. Quantify any shortfall in metric tonnes to estimate potential EC liability before CPCB does it for you. Engage a CPCB-Registered Recycler Urgently: If you have a target shortfall, approach authorised e-waste recyclers or PROs (Producer Responsibility Organisations) to procure additional RCs where permissible. Ensure all recycling partners hold valid, current CPCB registration — invalid RCs do not count toward EPR targets. Provision for EC Liability in Financial Accounts: Finance and legal teams should assess and provision for potential Environmental Compensation liability as a contingent obligation in current financial statements.
