Unquantified regulatory penalties are a financial reporting risk, particularly for listed companies under SEBI oversight. Build a Year-Round EPR Monitoring Process: Move away from year-end scrambles. Establish quarterly internal reviews of EPR target progress, RC procurement pipelines, and portal data accuracy. Assign clear ownership — whether in-house or through a compliance partner — for continuous EPR monitoring. Align EPR Disclosure with ESG Reporting: Integrate e-waste EPR performance metrics — targets set, targets achieved, RCs procured, EC paid (if any) — into your Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report (BRSR) and any voluntary ESG disclosures. This demonstrates accountability to investors and positions the company ahead of expected SEBI and global disclosure requirements on circular economy metrics.

Our Take The issuance of Environmental Compensation guidelines under the E-Waste Rules, 2022 is a watershed moment for EPR enforcement in India. For years, the gap between ambitious EPR targets and actual compliance on the ground was an open secret — with many obligated entities either under-registering, procuring questionable RCs from informal operators, or simply not engaging with the system at all. By publishing a clear EC formula, CPCB has effectively put a price on non-compliance, transforming EPR from a checkbox exercise into a quantifiable financial risk. We expect this to be followed by increased scrutiny of RC validity, cross-verification of recycler registrations, and potentially sector-specific enforcement drives targeting high-volume EEE categories. Companies that have been passive about EPR should treat this notice as a final warning before penalties arrive. More broadly, this move aligns India's e-waste governance with the direction of travel globally — toward mandatory, measurable, and financially consequential producer responsibility — and companies that build genuine circular economy capabilities now will be far better positioned as both domestic enforcement and international market expectations tighten over the next three to five years.

title: Environmental Compensation (EC) Guidelines Under E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022: What Producers, Importers & Brand Owners Must Do Now
summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued Environmental Compensation (EC) guidelines under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, introducing a structured penalty framework for entities that fail to meet their Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) obligations for electronic waste. Compan

sections.t: Executive Summary The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued Environmental Compensation (EC) guidelines under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, introducing a structured penalty framework for entities that fail to meet their Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) obligations for electronic waste. Companies that miss their annual e-waste collection and recycling targets now face quantified financial penalties — making compliance not just an environmental imperative but a direct financial risk. Producers, importers, and brand owners of electrical and electronic equipment must immediately audit their EPR status and close any shortfalls before regulatory action is initiated.

What Changed India's E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 overhauled the country's approach to managing end-of-life electronics by placing clear Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) obligations on producers, importers, and brand owners (PIBOs) of electrical and electronic equipment. Under EPR, these entities are required to ensure that a defined quantity of e-waste — proportional to the volume of products they place in the market — is collected and channelled to authorised recyclers or refurbishers each financial year. The latest CPCB notice formalises the Environmental Compensation mechanism: a financial liability triggered when a registered entity fails to fulfil its annual EPR target. Think of EC as the regulatory 'polluter pays' instrument for e-waste — if you put electronics into the Indian market and do not ensure equivalent e-waste is responsibly recycled, you pay a penalty calculated on the shortfall. This moves e-waste compliance from a largely procedural exercise to one with hard financial consequences, bringing India's framework closer to global best practices seen in the EU's WEEE Directive.

Who Is Affected Producers of electrical and electronic equipment (EEE) — including manufacturers of IT equipment, consumer electronics, telecom devices, medical devices, and large/small household appliances Importers of EEE — companies bringing finished electronic goods or components into India for sale or distribution Brand Owners (BOOs) — entities whose brand name appears on EEE products sold in India, even if manufacturing is outsourced EPR-registered entities on the CPCB centralised portal who have filed (or should have filed) annual EPR returns Bulk consumers and dismantlers/recyclers, to the extent they are linked to EPR credit fulfilment chains Startups and MSMEs in the electronics sector are not exempt — registration and target thresholds apply based on volume of equipment placed in the market

Key Obligations & Deadlines EPR Target Fulfilment: PIBOs must collect and recycle e-waste equivalent to their prescribed annual EPR targets; shortfalls in any financial year trigger Environmental Compensation liability EC Calculation: Environmental Compensation is computed on the quantity of e-waste not recycled against the mandated target — companies should obtain the precise rate schedule from the CPCB EC guidelines document and model their potential liability immediately Annual EPR Returns: Entities must file annual returns on the CPCB EPR portal disclosing quantities of EEE placed on market and e-waste collected/recycled; non-filing compounds EC risk EPR Credit Reconciliation: Ensure that EPR certificates/credits purchased from authorised recyclers are valid, traceable, and reconciled on the CPCB portal — invalid or duplicate credits do not count toward target fulfilment Shortfall Remediation Window: Companies with existing shortfalls should proactively engage with CPCB and authorised recyclers to close gaps before formal EC notices are issued — voluntary compliance is typically treated more favourably than post-notice action New Registrations: Entities placing EEE in the Indian market that have not yet registered on the CPCB EPR portal must do so immediately to avoid compounded penalties for non-registration plus EC liability Stay updated: Bookmark the CPCB EPR portal (eprnewportal.cpcb.gov.in) and the official gazette for any revision to EC rates or target schedules for FY 2024-25 and beyond

Climate Transition Angle E-waste is far more than a compliance checkbox — it sits at the intersection of the circular economy, supply chain resilience, and climate transition strategy. The extraction of virgin metals (cobalt, lithium, copper, rare earth elements) used in electronics is energy-intensive and carbon-heavy. When e-waste is responsibly recycled, these critical materials are recovered and re-enter supply chains, reducing the demand for new mining and the associated Scope 3 emissions for electronics manufacturers. For companies aligning with global climate disclosure standards — particularly the ISSB's IFRS S2 (climate-related disclosures) and the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) for those with European operations or investors — e-waste management is increasingly visible in Scope 3 value chain emissions accounting and in broader biodiversity and resource stewardship disclosures. Investors conducting ESG due diligence will scrutinise EPR compliance rates as a proxy for operational discipline and regulatory risk management. India's EC framework also signals a broader regulatory trajectory: the government is methodically building enforcement teeth into its extended producer responsibility ecosystem — covering not just e-waste but plastics, batteries, and tyres. Companies that treat EPR as a strategic priority today will be structurally better positioned as this regulatory architecture tightens over the next three to five years.

What To Do Now Conduct an immediate EPR gap analysis: Pull your CPCB portal data for the current and prior financial years, compare EPR targets against credits reconciled, and quantify any shortfall — this is your potential EC liability exposure Engage authorised recyclers urgently: If you have a shortfall, contract with CPCB-authorised e-waste recyclers or aggregators immediately to collect and recycle the required volumes; ensure EPR certificates are issued and uploaded to the portal correctly Review and validate your EPR credit chain: Audit the provenance of all EPR credits on your account — credits from non-authorised or deregistered recyclers will not be accepted; replace invalid credits before they are flagged by CPCB Assign internal ownership and set up monitoring: Designate a compliance owner for e-waste EPR, create a monthly dashboard tracking EPR targets vs. actuals, and integrate EPR compliance into your broader ESG and regulatory reporting calendar Integrate e-waste EPR into ESG disclosures: If you report under SEBI's Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report (BRSR) framework or prepare disclosures for international investors, ensure your EPR compliance status, e-waste collected metrics, and recycling rates are accurately reflected and disclosed

Our Take The formalisation of Environmental Compensation guidelines under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 is a significant escalation in India's environmental enforcement posture — and frankly, it has been long overdue. For years, EPR for e-waste existed more on paper than in practice, with registration rates low and recycling targets routinely missed without consequence. The EC mechanism changes that calculus fundamentally: non-compliance now has a quantifiable price tag. We expect CPCB to become progressively more assertive in issuing EC notices, particularly to large-volume producers and importers who have registered but underperformed on targets. Companies that have been treating EPR as a back-office compliance task need to elevate it to a board-level ESG risk — because environmental compensation liabilities, if they accumulate, will be material. More broadly, this is a preview of the India that is coming: a regulatory environment where the circular economy is enforced, not merely aspirational. The companies that build robust EPR systems now will not only avoid penalties but will gain genuine competitive advantage as supply chain traceability and responsible material stewardship become non-negotiable expectations from global customers and investors alike.

title: The EPR Certificate Market in India (2026): Prices, Trading and How to Hit Your Targets
summary: India's Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) market has matured from a paperwork obligation into a real, priced market for recycling certificates — worth roughly $1.5 billion in 2025 and projected to reach $5 billion by 2030. This guide explains how the certificate market works across plastic, e-waste, battery and tyre waste, what credits cost today, what changed under the 2026 rules, and exactly how to calculate and meet your obligation using Green Curve's free EPR Market tools .

sections.t: What "the EPR market" actually means Extended Producer Responsibility makes producers, importers and brand owners (PIBOs) responsible for recycling the packaging and products they put on the market. You discharge that responsibility by obtaining EPR certificates — digital proof, issued by CPCB-registered recyclers, that a given quantity of waste was collected and recycled. Where you cannot recycle enough yourself, you buy certificates from recyclers who have a surplus. That buying and selling is the EPR market. Three features define it. First, certificates are category-specific — a rigid-plastic certificate cannot satisfy a multilayer obligation, and a lead-acid battery credit cannot cover lithium-ion. Second, prices sit inside a CPCB price band set at 30%–100% of the environmental-compensation rate, to stop a race to the bottom. Third, the market is now genuinely liquid: roughly 3.7 million tonnes of plastic certificates changed hands in 2023, traded on the CPCB EPR portals and private exchanges.

sections.h: How EPR credits are generated and traded
sections.t: How EPR credits are generated and traded The flow is consistent across all four waste streams: Collection — waste is gathered from the market by producers, aggregators or recyclers. Processing — a CPCB-registered recycler processes it and the system issues category-specific EPR certificates. Verification — CPCB/SPCB cross-checks invoices, GST records and third-party reports. GST-compliant e-invoicing has cut verification from about 30 days to 7–10 days. Trading — certificates are listed and bought on CPCB portals and private platforms; credits remain valid into the following year within their category. Reconciliation — PIBOs file annual returns matching certificates held against the obligation. A word of caution: CPCB flagged over six lakh fake certificates in 2023. Traceability and a clean audit trail — invoices, GST records, recycler registration numbers — are now as important as the price you pay.

What EPR certificates cost in 2026 (indicative) Prices vary by stream, category, volume and counterparty. The ranges below are indicative mid-2025 trading levels, useful for budgeting rather than as quotes: Stream Indicative ₹/kg Note Plastic — rigid (Cat I) ₹0.5 – 1.1 Lowest plastic band Plastic — flexible (Cat II) ₹0.8 – 1.5 — Plastic — multilayer (Cat III) ₹1.0 – 2.0 Highest plastic band E-waste ₹10 – 30 Floor price ₹22/kg since Mar 2024 Battery — lead-acid ₹5 – 20 — Battery — lithium-ion ₹35 – 50 Scarcity keeps rates high Tyre ₹0.3 – 0.8 Lowest of all streams Watch the calendar. Demand concentrates near the annual-return deadline, so certificate prices typically climb sharply between January and March. PIBOs that procure early in the year routinely pay materially less than last-minute buyers. See a live view across all four streams in the EPR Certificate Price Tracker →

What changed in 2026 The Plastic Waste Management (Amendment) Rules, 2026, notified on 19 January 2026, tightened the regime in three important ways: End-of-Life (EOL) certificates withdrawn — companies can no longer use EOL disposal certificates to meet recycling targets. Real recycling, not disposal, now counts. Mandatory recycled content — rigid plastic (Category I) must contain at least 30% recycled material in FY2025-26, rising to 60% by FY2028-29 . This is a supply-side obligation that needs early engagement with recyclers. Limited carry-forward — FY2025-26 shortfalls can be carried forward up to three years, provided at least one-third of the deficit is cleared each year. Plan the recycled tonnage you'll need year by year with the Recycled-Content Planner →

sections.h: How to calculate and meet your obligation
sections.t: How to calculate and meet your obligation Your obligation is simple in principle: the recycling target percentage applied to the quantity you place on the market. For plastic that target is around 80% of packaging placed on market; for lead-acid batteries it is as high as 90%. The cost is the shortfall (in tonnes) multiplied by the certificate price. The reason to act early is economics, not just compliance. Environmental Compensation (EC) — what CPCB levies on the unmet portion — is deliberately set higher than the cost of genuine compliance. Buying certificates is almost always cheaper than defaulting, and far cheaper than a blocked import consignment at the port. Estimate your target, certificates needed, procurement cost and EC exposure with the free EPR Obligation & Cost Calculator → — or, if you are not yet registered, start with the EPR Registration guide .

sections.h: EPR feeds your BRSR — treat it as one pipeline
sections.t: EPR feeds your BRSR — treat it as one pipeline The numbers you generate for EPR are the same numbers SEBI's Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report (BRSR) asks for. Plastic placed on market and product responsibility map to Principle 2; recycled quantities, certificates and waste circularity map to Principle 6; and any Environmental Compensation paid is material to your penalties disclosure. Companies that run EPR and BRSR as one data pipeline avoid duplicate effort and the embarrassment of inconsistent figures across filings. Carry your EPR data straight into a SEBI-ready report with the BRSR builder , and benchmark your EPR exposure against 1,200+ listed companies in the ESG Quotient .

Our take EPR in India has crossed the line from compliance chore to managed cost — a line item with a price curve, a seasonality pattern, and real penalties for getting it wrong. The 2026 amendments push it further still, replacing easy end-of-life shortcuts with genuine recycled-content obligations that ramp every year. The PIBOs who come out ahead will be the ones who quantify their obligation early, procure certificates before the Q4 price spike, lock in recycler relationships for recycled content, and wire the resulting data straight into their BRSR. The tools to do all four are now free and in your browser — start with the EPR Market hub .

sources: Sources: CPCB EPR portals ( epr.cpcb.gov.in ); Plastic Waste Management (Amendment) Rules, 2026; public EPR market price disclosures and industry analyses, 2025. Figures are indicative — verify against the latest CPCB / MoEFCC notifications before acting.

title: ESG & Climate Compliance Digest — 20 May 2026: E-Waste EPR Portal in Focus as Regulators Tighten the Screws
summary: Today's compliance landscape is dominated by a cluster of eight urgent notices from the CPCB relating to the E-Waste Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) Portal — covering filing extensions, Environmental Compensation guidelines, recycler obligations, and the framework for EPR certificate generati

sections.t: Executive Summary Today's compliance landscape is dominated by a cluster of eight urgent notices from the CPCB relating to the E-Waste Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) Portal — covering filing extensions, Environmental Compensation guidelines, recycler obligations, and the framework for EPR certificate generation. Taken together, these notices signal that India's e-waste regulatory machinery is undergoing a significant operational tightening, with no more 'soft landing' for producers, importers, or recyclers who have deferred compliance. Companies with any footprint in electronics manufacturing, import, retail, or recycling must treat today's digest as a call to action — deadlines are real, penalties are codified, and the portal is live. The convergence of these notices in a short window is not coincidental: it reflects CPCB's intent to close the gap between EPR registration and actual verified compliance before the next annual reporting cycle begins.

What Changed TODAY'S HEADLINE UPDATE — Annual Return Deadline Extended to 15 June (from 30 June 2025 baseline): CPCB has issued an urgent notice extending the deadline for filing annual returns on the E-Waste EPR Portal. While the original deadline stood at 30 June 2025, a further extension has been granted, with the operative cut-off now running through 15 June 2026 for the current cycle. This is the last extension signal being communicated — entities that miss this window face Environmental Compensation (EC) proceedings. EXTENSION FOR QUARTERLY AND ANNUAL RETURNS FOR FY 2023-24 (till 31 December 2024 — now lapsed, action required): An earlier notice had extended both quarterly and annual returns for FY 2023-24 to 31 December 2024. This deadline has now lapsed. Any entity that did not file within that window is in arrears and must proactively engage with CPCB's Environmental Compensation framework to regularise their status. Continued non-filing will attract escalating EC charges. ENVIRONMENTAL COMPENSATION (EC) GUIDELINES UNDER E-WASTE (MANAGEMENT) RULES, 2022: CPCB has formalised and published the Environmental Compensation guidelines applicable to entities that fail to meet their EPR obligations under the 2022 Rules. EC is not a fine in the traditional sense — it is a quantified liability calculated on the basis of the volume of e-waste for which the obligation was unmet, multiplied by prescribed rates. The guidelines clarify the methodology, the appeal mechanism, and the timelines for EC recovery. This is a materially important development: it transforms non-compliance from an administrative risk into a balance-sheet liability. GUIDELINES FOR DETERMINATION OF PROCESSING CAPACITY OF E-WASTE RECYCLING FACILITIES BY SPCBs/PCCs: CPCB has issued standardised guidelines for State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs) and Pollution Control Committees (PCCs) to uniformly assess and certify the processing capacity of e-waste recycling facilities. This is significant because EPR certificate generation — the mechanism by which producers get credit for e-waste channelled to recyclers — is directly tied to verified recycler capacity. Inflated or unverified capacity claims have been a persistent loophole; these guidelines close it at the source. FRAMEWORK FOR GENERATION OF EPR CERTIFICATES UNDER E-WASTE (MANAGEMENT) RULES, 2022: A dedicated framework document has been issued governing how EPR Certificates are generated, validated, transferred, and retired on the portal. Producers and importers use EPR certificates as proof that they have met their collection and recycling targets. The framework introduces stricter traceability requirements — certificates must now be linked to verified recycler capacity (per the capacity determination guidelines above), actual weight of e-waste processed, and time-stamped portal entries. Bulk or retrospective certificate generation without underlying transaction evidence will no longer be accepted. NOTICE TO REGISTERED RECYCLERS UNDER E-WASTE (M) RULES, 2022: A targeted notice has been sent to all registered recyclers on the portal. The notice reinforces that recyclers must update their processing capacity data, ensure their facility certifications are current with the relevant SPCB/PCC, and that any EPR certificates they generate must strictly correspond to actual processing records. Recyclers found generating certificates beyond their verified capacity face suspension of registration. NOTICE REGARDING EXTENSION OF TIMELINE FOR FILING RETURNS (general portal notice): A broader portal-level notice reiterates the extended timelines and urges all registered entities — producers, importers, recyclers, refurbishers, and dismantlers — to log in, verify their data, and complete pending return filings. CPCB has indicated that the portal will flag non-compliant entities automatically after the extended deadline passes, triggering the EC process.

Who Is Affected Electronics Manufacturers (OEMs) — Any company manufacturing electrical and electronic equipment (EEE) listed in Schedule I of the E-Waste Rules, 2022, including consumer electronics, IT equipment, telecom devices, lighting equipment, and large/small household appliances. Importers of EEE — Companies importing any Schedule I equipment into India, regardless of whether they are the brand owner or a trading entity. E-Commerce Platforms — Platforms facilitating the sale of EEE are increasingly being examined for EPR liability; those with marketplace models should review their exposure under the 2022 Rules. Authorised E-Waste Recyclers — All CPCB/SPCB-registered recycling facilities are directly named in today's notices and face capacity verification requirements. Refurbishers and Dismantlers — Registered entities in the refurbishment and dismantling segments must file returns and ensure their portal data is current. Large Industrial Consumers — Companies operating large data centres, manufacturing plants, or IT infrastructure with significant end-of-life EEE volumes may have producer-equivalent obligations depending on the nature of their operations. Supply Chain & Procurement Teams — Companies sourcing electronics from vendors who are non-compliant EPR registrants face reputational and supply chain disruption risk. ESG / Sustainability Reporting Teams — E-waste EPR compliance is increasingly a disclosure item under SEBI's BRSR (Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting) framework for listed companies.

Key Obligations & Deadlines DEADLINE — 15 June 2026: File Annual Returns on the E-Waste EPR Portal for the current reporting cycle. This is the final extended deadline. No further extensions are expected. IMMEDIATE ACTION: Any entity that missed the FY 2023-24 annual return deadline of 31 December 2024 must proactively assess their Environmental Compensation liability using the newly published EC guidelines and engage with CPCB before the portal auto-flags them. ONGOING — Recyclers: Update and resubmit processing capacity data to your respective SPCB/PCC in accordance with the new capacity determination guidelines. EPR certificate generation will be gated against verified capacity from this point forward. ONGOING — Producers/Importers: Review all EPR certificates in your account. Ensure each certificate is backed by verified recycler processing records. Any certificates generated under the old framework that cannot be substantiated may need to be retired or reconciled. ONGOING — All Registered Entities: Log into the E-Waste EPR Portal, verify that your registration details, contact information, and category classifications are current. Stale data is a common reason for filing failures and penalty triggers. REVIEW NOW: Cross-check your EPR targets (in metric tonnes) for the current financial year against actual collection and channelisation volumes. If you are tracking below target, initiate engagement with authorised recyclers immediately — last-minute certificate procurement without underlying transactions will not be accepted under the new framework. GOVERNANCE: Ensure your Board/CSR/ESG Committee is briefed on the Environmental Compensation guidelines — EC liabilities must be provisioned for in financial statements where applicable.

Climate Transition Angle E-waste regulation sits at a critical intersection of the circular economy and India's broader climate transition strategy. The production and disposal of electronic equipment is one of the fastest-growing waste streams globally, and the carbon footprint of linear 'take-make-dispose' electronics consumption is substantial — both in embodied energy during manufacturing and in the toxic and greenhouse gas emissions from informal disposal. India generates approximately 1.6 million metric tonnes of e-waste annually, with formal recycling rates still well below 30%. Today's regulatory actions are therefore not merely administrative — they are structural interventions to redirect material flows toward formal, lower-carbon processing pathways. From a global standards alignment perspective, the EPR certificate framework and the capacity verification guidelines directly support the kind of supply chain traceability that is now required under the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and the ISSB's IFRS S2 climate disclosures. CSRD's supply chain due diligence requirements (under CSDDD) and ISSB's S2 both require companies to account for Scope 3 emissions — which include the end-of-life treatment of products sold. An Indian electronics manufacturer or importer that cannot demonstrate verified e-waste recycling is increasingly exposed not just to domestic regulatory risk, but to trade and procurement barriers in European and other global markets. The formalisation of Environmental Compensation as a quantified, balance-sheet-relevant liability also mirrors the direction of travel in carbon pricing globally. Just as carbon credits must be verified and retired, EPR certificates must now be traceable to real recycling activity. This convergence of 'circular economy finance' with the logic of verified environmental markets is a signal that India's environmental regulatory architecture is maturing rapidly — and companies that invest in robust EPR compliance today are building the data infrastructure they will need for BRSR Core disclosures, ISSB reporting, and eventual carbon accounting requirements tomorrow. Physical climate risk is also a factor: climate-stressed supply chains, extreme heat events disrupting informal recycling clusters, and flood risk to e-waste storage sites are all material concerns that make formal, resilient recycling infrastructure a climate adaptation priority — not just a compliance checkbox.

What To Do Now COMPLIANCE AUDIT (This Week): Assign a responsible owner — your Environment, Health & Safety (EHS) lead or ESG officer — to conduct an immediate audit of your E-Waste EPR Portal account. Confirm registration status, check pending returns, and document any gaps against the 15 June 2026 filing deadline. EC LIABILITY ASSESSMENT (This Week): If any annual or quarterly returns for FY 2023-24 or FY 2024-25 were missed, use the newly published Environmental Compensation guidelines to calculate your potential EC exposure. Share this assessment with your CFO and legal team for provisioning and disclosure decisions. RECYCLER DUE DILIGENCE (Within 2 Weeks): Contact your authorised e-waste recycling partners and request confirmation that (a) their SPCB/PCC capacity certification is current and compliant with the new capacity determination guidelines, and (b) that EPR certificates they will generate on your behalf are supported by actual processing records. Do not accept certificates from recyclers whose capacity status is unverified. EPR CERTIFICATE RECONCILIATION (Before 15 June 2026): Reconcile your EPR certificate holdings against your EPR targets for the current FY. Identify any shortfall and procure additional verified certificates from compliant recyclers before the annual return filing deadline. Build in at least a two-week buffer for portal processing delays. BRSR & BOARD REPORTING (Next Governance Cycle): Incorporate today's regulatory developments into your BRSR disclosure narrative. Flag the Environmental Compensation framework as a new material regulatory risk in your next Board ESG/Risk Committee update, and align your e-waste KPIs with ISSB S2 and GRI 306 (Waste) disclosures for international investor audiences.

Our Take Today's cluster of eight CPCB notices is one of the most significant single-day regulatory signals in India's e-waste compliance history — not because any one notice is unprecedented, but because of what they represent collectively: the closing of the implementation gap. For years, India's EPR framework for e-waste has had the architecture of accountability — registration portals, certificate systems, return filings — but the enforcement infrastructure was patchy and extensions were routine. What today's notices communicate, reading between the lines, is that the era of 'extend and pretend' is ending. The Environmental Compensation guidelines give CPCB a financially potent enforcement tool; the capacity verification guidelines eliminate the most widely used compliance workaround; and the EPR certificate traceability framework removes the ambiguity that allowed paper compliance without physical recycling. For companies that have been treating EPR as a low-priority administrative task, the risk profile has changed materially — and it has changed today. Our strong recommendation: treat the 15 June 2026 deadline as hard, invest in recycler due diligence as a strategic supplier relationship rather than a transactional procurement exercise, and start building the data trails now that will be demanded not just by CPCB, but by global ESG disclosure frameworks within the next two to three reporting cycles.

title: ESG & Climate Compliance Digest — 22 May 2026: EPR Enforcement Intensifies Across E-Waste, Plastics & Batteries
summary: Today's digest captures a significant regulatory surge across India's Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) landscape — with urgent enforcement notices targeting e-waste recyclers and producers, a second amendment to Plastic Waste Management Rules, updated EPR guidelines for plastic packaging, two

sections.t: Executive Summary Today's digest captures a significant regulatory surge across India's Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) landscape — with urgent enforcement notices targeting e-waste recyclers and producers, a second amendment to Plastic Waste Management Rules, updated EPR guidelines for plastic packaging, two rounds of e-waste rule amendments, and back-to-back Battery Waste Management Rules in 2024 and 2025. The cumulative signal is unmistakable: India's Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) and the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) are closing compliance gaps and dramatically raising the cost of non-conformance. Producers, importers, brand owners, and waste processors operating in electronics, FMCG, consumer goods, EV/energy storage, and packaging-intensive sectors must treat EPR obligations as board-level risk — not a back-office checkbox.

What Changed TODAY'S HEADLINE — EPR ENFORCEMENT NOTICES (E-WASTE): Two urgent notices issued by CPCB mark the sharpest escalation in e-waste enforcement to date. The first targets registered recyclers and refurbishers under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, flagging non-submission or under-reporting of processing data and threatening suspension or cancellation of EPR certificates. The second is directed at producers — including manufacturers and importers of electrical and electronic equipment (EEE) — for failure to meet annual EPR targets, file returns, or maintain valid EPR authorisations. Both notices signal a shift from advisory to punitive enforcement. PLASTIC WASTE — 2ND AMENDMENT & EPR GUIDELINES: The 2nd Amendment to the Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2022 tightens definitions around single-use plastics, revises the categorisation of plastic packaging for EPR purposes, and introduces stricter producer accountability for multilayer plastics. Simultaneously, CPCB has released revised Guidelines on EPR for Plastic Packaging, which update the EPR certificate framework, clarify the role of PROs (Producer Responsibility Organisations), and set revised recycling/end-of-life targets by plastic category. Together, these updates close several loopholes that companies had used to reduce reported plastic footprints. E-WASTE RULE AMENDMENTS — 2023 & 2024: The E-Waste Second Amendment Rules (2023) introduced revised collection targets phased by producer turnover category and tightened the dismantling-to-recycling handoff documentation requirements. The Third Amendment Rules (2024) went further — introducing mandatory deposit-refund scheme pilots for select EEE categories, stricter penalties for non-compliant recyclers, and a new requirement for producers to publish annual EPR performance reports on their websites. Read together, these amendments represent a near-complete overhaul of the 2022 framework within just two years. BATTERY WASTE MANAGEMENT RULES — 2024 & 2025: The Battery Waste Management Rules, 2024 established India's first comprehensive EPR framework specifically for batteries — covering portable, automotive, industrial, and electric vehicle batteries. The 2025 update accelerates collection and recycling targets, introduces extended producer liability for battery-embedded products (including EVs and consumer electronics), and mandates traceability through a centralised Battery Waste Management Portal. This is particularly consequential for EV OEMs, energy storage system providers, and consumer electronics brands, all of whom now carry full lifecycle responsibility for battery waste.

Who Is Affected Electronics & IT Hardware Manufacturers (laptops, phones, white goods, industrial EEE) — directly under e-waste producer EPR obligations Importers & Brand Owners of EEE — including global brands selling through Indian subsidiaries or e-commerce channels E-Waste Recyclers & Refurbishers — facing direct CPCB scrutiny on processing records and certificate validity FMCG, Retail & Consumer Goods companies — under plastic packaging EPR rules, especially those using multilayer or non-recyclable packaging EV Original Equipment Manufacturers (OEMs) and Battery Manufacturers/Importers — battery waste EPR obligations now fully operational Energy Storage System (ESS) Providers — industrial battery EPR targets now applicable Producer Responsibility Organisations (PROs) — compliance and reporting obligations tightened across all three waste streams E-commerce platforms acting as marketplace operators for EEE or packaged goods — increasingly in regulatory scope as deemed producers SMEs and MSMEs in manufacturing with annual turnover above threshold limits — phased targets now apply

Key Obligations & Deadlines IMMEDIATE (within 30 days of notice): E-waste producers and recyclers served with CPCB urgent notices must respond with compliance documentation, EPR certificate status, and corrective action plans — failure risks certificate suspension Q1 FY2026-27 (by 30 June 2026): Annual EPR return filing deadline for e-waste producers for FY2025-26 on the CPCB EPR Portal — non-filing triggers automatic penalty proceedings 30 June 2026: Plastic packaging EPR targets for FY2025-26 must be met and reported; revised guidelines require updated PRO agreements aligned to new recycling categories 31 July 2026: Battery waste EPR targets (2025 Rules) — annual collection and recycling obligations for FY2025-26 must be submitted via the Battery Waste Management Portal Ongoing — FY2026-27 Targets: E-waste Third Amendment (2024) requires producers to publish EPR performance reports on corporate websites by 30 September 2026 for the previous financial year Ongoing: Recyclers must maintain real-time digital records of inward and outward material flows; CPCB has indicated surprise audits will increase in H2 2026 Ongoing: Multilayer plastic producers must demonstrate transition roadmaps to mono-material or recyclable alternatives under the revised PWM Amendment — no hard deadline yet, but regulatory guidance expected by Q3 2026 EV OEMs: Battery traceability registration on the Battery Waste Management Portal is now mandatory for all new vehicle models; retrospective registration for existing models required by 31 December 2026

Climate Transition Angle India's escalating EPR enforcement is not occurring in isolation — it is structurally aligned with the global shift toward circular economy principles embedded in major international frameworks. The ISSB's IFRS S2 climate disclosure standard requires companies to assess physical and transition risks across their full value chain, which now unambiguously includes end-of-life waste liabilities. Companies with unresolved EPR non-compliance are, in effect, carrying unquantified transition risk on their balance sheets. The EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and its accompanying European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS) require disclosure on circular economy practices, waste generation, and resource use — meaning Indian subsidiaries or supply chain partners of EU-headquartered companies will face double-barrelled pressure: Indian regulatory compliance AND international disclosure obligations. The battery waste framework, in particular, connects directly to physical climate risk. As India's EV penetration grows — driven partly by climate policy and partly by energy security — the volume of lithium-ion battery waste will grow exponentially. Poorly managed battery waste creates both toxic contamination risk (a physical risk to communities and supply chains) and a critical minerals recovery opportunity. Companies that build closed-loop battery recovery systems now will have a strategic advantage in securing recycled lithium, cobalt, and nickel as virgin material costs rise. Finally, the tightening of plastic waste rules signals regulatory alignment with India's commitments under the UN Global Plastics Treaty negotiations. Companies that treat EPR as a minimum compliance floor rather than a strategic input risk being caught flat-footed when treaty obligations translate into even stricter domestic law — likely within the next 24–36 months.

What To Do Now AUDIT EPR REGISTRATION & CERTIFICATE STATUS TODAY: Cross-check all EPR certificates (e-waste, plastic, battery) for validity, target achievement status, and filing deadlines on the CPCB portal. Identify any gaps before CPCB does — proactive disclosure is treated more favourably than reactive compliance. ISSUE AN INTERNAL COMPLIANCE ALERT TO ALL BUSINESS UNITS: Given the multi-stream nature of today's updates (e-waste + plastics + batteries), sustainability and legal teams should immediately brief product, procurement, and operations heads on applicable obligations. Many EPR failures originate in product design and procurement decisions made without compliance input. REVIEW PRO AND RECYCLER CONTRACTS: Validate that your registered PROs and recycling partners have valid CPCB authorisations and are meeting their processing obligations on your behalf. An invalid or suspended recycler certificate directly exposes the producer — conduct due diligence now, not at year-end. MAP YOUR BATTERY AND PLASTIC FOOTPRINT TO THE NEW RULES: For companies in EV, consumer electronics, or FMCG sectors, conduct a rapid materiality assessment against the updated Battery Waste Rules (2025) and revised plastic EPR guidelines. Quantify your FY2026-27 EPR targets and identify whether current collection infrastructure is sufficient. PREPARE FOR DISCLOSURE — ALIGN EPR DATA WITH ISSB/BRSR REPORTING: Begin structuring EPR performance data (collection volumes, recycling rates, EPR certificate spend) in a format compatible with Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting (BRSR) Core disclosures and ISSB S2 value chain metrics. Investors and lenders are increasingly requesting this data in ESG due diligence.

Our Take Today's cluster of regulatory updates tells a single, coherent story: India is done with the 'awareness and registration' phase of EPR and has entered the 'enforce and penalise' phase with intent. The CPCB urgent notices — issued simultaneously against both recyclers and producers — are a deliberate signal that the regulator is tightening the full value chain at once, preventing companies from offloading liability onto downstream processors. The back-to-back amendments to e-waste and battery rules, layered over a revised plastic EPR framework, reflect a regulatory architecture that is becoming more sophisticated and more difficult to navigate through partial compliance. What's coming next? Expect CPCB to operationalise the deposit-refund scheme for select EEE categories by late FY2027, mandatory third-party EPR audits for Tier 1 producers, and the integration of EPR performance data into the BRSR Core framework for listed companies. For sustainability officers, the message is clear: EPR is no longer a peripheral compliance function — it is a material ESG risk that requires the same rigour, resourcing, and board visibility as carbon reporting.

title: ESG & Climate Compliance Digest — 26 May 2026: India's EPR Enforcement Wave Arrives
summary: Today's digest carries a high-urgency regulatory signal: India's Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) is actively enforcing its Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) ecosystem across e-waste, battery waste, and plastic waste simultaneously — a coordinated compliance pressure that affects thousand

sections.t: Executive Summary Today's digest carries a high-urgency regulatory signal: India's Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) is actively enforcing its Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) ecosystem across e-waste, battery waste, and plastic waste simultaneously — a coordinated compliance pressure that affects thousands of producers, importers, brand owners, and recyclers. The issuance of urgent notices to e-waste recyclers alongside interim credit-transfer arrangements signals that the CPCB is closing loopholes in the EPR certificate ecosystem before the financial year-end compliance cycle. Companies that have treated EPR as a paperwork exercise are now facing real enforcement risk, and those without audited recycler partnerships or verified credit trails should treat this week as a red-alert moment.

What Changed TODAY'S HEADLINE: CPCB Issues Dual Urgent Notices on E-Waste EPR — The most consequential development today is the CPCB's release of two simultaneous urgent notices targeting the e-waste recycling chain. The first notice puts recyclers on formal notice regarding compliance obligations under the E-Waste Management Rules, 2022 — signalling that CPCB is auditing whether registered recyclers are meeting their processing targets, maintaining required documentation, and operating within approved capacities. Non-compliant recyclers risk de-registration, which would immediately invalidate EPR credits purchased by producers linked to them. The second urgent notice addresses an interim arrangement for the generation and transfer of EPR End Product Credits (certificates) by e-waste recyclers. This is technically significant: it means the CPCB has identified gaps or disputes in how recyclers generate and transfer certificates on the EPR portal, and has issued a temporary procedural fix. For producers and brand owners, this creates a short-term uncertainty window — credits already purchased or in transit may need re-verification under the new interim process. Legal and compliance teams should immediately check the status of pending certificate transfers on the CPCB EPR portal. PLASTIC WASTE: Draft Rules and EPR Notification Recirculated — The Draft Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2022 and the accompanying Draft Notification on Extended Producer Responsibility for plastics have re-entered the regulatory spotlight today, likely ahead of a final notification or amendment cycle. These drafts establish the architecture for plastic EPR obligations — covering producers, importers, and brand owners (PIBOs) dealing in plastic packaging across four categories. The recirculation suggests a revision or stakeholder consultation round may be imminent. Companies in FMCG, retail, e-commerce, and food & beverage must treat this as a preparatory signal to audit their plastic packaging inventory and EPR registration status. E-WASTE RULES CONSOLIDATED: 2022 Base Rules + 2023 First Amendment — The E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 form the foundational legal framework, establishing EPR obligations for producers of Electrical and Electronic Equipment (EEE), targets for collection and recycling, and a digital certificate-based compliance mechanism. The E-Waste First Amendment Rules, 2023 refined these obligations — notably adjusting EPR targets, refining definitions of covered equipment categories, and strengthening the role of the CPCB-operated EPR portal. Together, these rules create a binding, target-driven system where producers must demonstrate verified recycling of a percentage of the EEE they placed on the market in prior years. BATTERY WASTE: Dual-Year Framework Now Fully Active — The Battery Waste Management Rules, 2022 and their 2023 successor rules establish India's first comprehensive EPR framework for batteries — covering portable, automotive, industrial, and EV battery packs. The 2023 rules increased ambition: higher collection targets, mandatory extended producer responsibility registration, and specific provisions for lithium-ion batteries that reflect the explosive growth of the EV sector. With EV adoption accelerating in India, the battery waste rules are transitioning from future-concern to present-obligation for automotive OEMs, EV manufacturers, battery importers, and consumer electronics brands.

Who Is Affected E-Waste EPR Notices: All CPCB-registered e-waste recyclers; producers and importers of electrical and electronic equipment (laptops, phones, appliances, IT equipment, medical devices); brand owners who have purchased EPR certificates from recyclers now under CPCB scrutiny Plastic Waste EPR: Producers, importers, and brand owners (PIBOs) dealing in plastic packaging — specifically FMCG companies, e-commerce platforms, food & beverage manufacturers, retail chains, and packaging converters E-Waste Rules (2022 + 2023 Amendment): Any company manufacturing or importing EEE covered under Schedule I of the rules — including consumer electronics, IT and telecom equipment, large and small household appliances, lighting equipment, power tools, and medical devices Battery Waste Rules (2022 + 2023): Automotive OEMs (including EV manufacturers), two-wheeler and three-wheeler producers, battery importers, consumer electronics brands with battery-embedded products, industrial equipment manufacturers, and energy storage system providers Recyclers and Dismantlers: All entities registered with CPCB under any of these three waste streams — e-waste, plastic, or battery — face direct compliance obligations and are subject to the enforcement actions signalled today Investors and Lenders: Financial institutions with exposure to consumer goods, electronics, automotive, and packaging sectors face material ESG risk from portfolio companies with unresolved EPR liabilities

Key Obligations & Deadlines IMMEDIATE (This Week): E-waste producers and brand owners must log into the CPCB EPR portal and verify the status of all pending EPR End Product Credit transfers — the interim arrangement notice may require re-submission or re-validation of credits generated by recyclers now under scrutiny IMMEDIATE: If your EPR certificates were sourced from recyclers named in or potentially covered by the CPCB urgent compliance notice, engage your legal and compliance team today to assess certificate validity and explore alternative verified recycler partnerships BY 31 MAY 2026: Cross-check your EPR certificate inventory against FY2025–26 collection and recycling targets for e-waste. Any shortfall must be addressed before annual return filing windows open — penalties under the rules include financial liability and potential suspension of EPR registration JUNE 2026 (Anticipated): Watch for CPCB's finalisation of the interim credit-transfer arrangement — the current notice is explicitly temporary, and a revised process is expected within weeks. Assign a portal monitoring responsibility within your team now ONGOING — Plastic Waste: Ensure your company is registered on the CPCB EPR portal for plastic packaging if annual turnover exceeds the threshold. Begin compiling plastic packaging data by category (rigid, flexible, multi-layer, compostable) ahead of any final rule notification ONGOING — Battery Waste: EV manufacturers and battery importers must confirm registration under Battery Waste Management Rules, 2023 and verify that collection targets for FY2025–26 are being met through registered recycling partners. Lithium-ion battery EPR targets are non-negotiable given CPCB's stated enforcement focus ANNUAL RETURN FILING: E-waste and battery waste annual returns for FY2025–26 are expected to fall due in the July–September 2026 window — begin data consolidation now to avoid last-minute scrambles with recycler documentation

Climate Transition Angle Today's regulatory cluster is not just a compliance story — it is a material signal about where India's industrial economy is heading. The simultaneous enforcement of EPR frameworks across three waste streams (e-waste, plastic, battery) represents India operationalising the circular economy as climate policy. Extended Producer Responsibility, when functioning as intended, internalises the end-of-life cost of products into business models — exactly what the global transition to sustainable production requires. The battery waste framework deserves special attention from a climate lens. India's EV transition is accelerating, driven by government targets, falling battery costs, and rising fuel prices. The Battery Waste Management Rules, 2023 are the regulatory guardrail ensuring that the climate benefits of EVs are not undermined by an unmanaged waste stream. Companies in the EV value chain — OEMs, battery manufacturers, charging infrastructure providers — face a dual transition obligation: decarbonise their products AND close the loop on the materials that make decarbonisation possible. From a global standards perspective, the EPR enforcement wave aligns directly with what the International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) under IFRS S2, and the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD), are demanding from multinational companies: disclosure of circular economy risks, supply chain material flows, and waste management liabilities. Indian subsidiaries of global companies, and Indian exporters supplying EU or UK markets, face a convergence of domestic EPR enforcement and international disclosure expectations. Failure to maintain clean EPR compliance records will increasingly translate into supply chain disqualification, investor scrutiny, and reputational risk at the global level. The physical risk dimension is equally relevant: India's informal recycling sector — which the EPR framework is designed to formalise — operates in conditions of high climate vulnerability. Extreme heat events, flooding in industrial clusters, and water stress affect recycler operations and throughput capacity. Companies relying on a thin network of recycling partners should stress-test their EPR compliance chains against climate disruption scenarios — a supply chain resilience question that is rapidly becoming a board-level concern.

What To Do Now AUDIT YOUR EPR CERTIFICATE CHAIN TODAY: Pull a full report of all e-waste EPR certificates purchased or pending for FY2025–26. Cross-reference the issuing recyclers against any CPCB compliance notice lists. If any certificates originate from recyclers under enforcement action, flag this immediately to your General Counsel and explore procurement from alternative CPCB-verified recyclers to fill potential gaps ASSIGN A CPCB PORTAL MONITOR: Designate a specific team member — within EHS, Legal, or Sustainability — as the daily monitor for the CPCB EPR portal across all three waste streams your company is registered under. The interim arrangement for e-waste credit transfers will be updated, and missing the process change could invalidate compliance filings CONDUCT A THREE-STREAM EPR READINESS REVIEW: Map your company's obligations under all three frameworks — e-waste, plastic waste, and battery waste — against current registration status, annual targets, collected quantities, and verified recycler contracts. This is the week to find gaps before enforcement pressure intensifies BRIEF YOUR BOARD AND INVESTORS: The CPCB's coordinated enforcement action is a material ESG development. Sustainability officers should prepare a short brief for the board or ESG committee covering current EPR compliance status, any certificate validity risks, and the financial exposure from potential non-compliance — including penalties, reputational risk, and supply chain disruption PREPARE FOR PLASTIC WASTE RULE FINALISATION: If your company uses plastic packaging and has not yet built an internal plastic packaging data system (volumes by category, supplier data, recyclability classification), start now. The recirculation of draft rules today suggests finalisation is approaching — companies caught without data infrastructure will face acute compliance pressure at short notice

Our Take Today's digest represents something qualitatively different from the routine regulatory updates we track: it signals that India's EPR enforcement machinery has shifted from framework-building to active accountability. The CPCB's decision to issue two urgent notices on e-waste in a single day — one targeting recyclers, one addressing systemic gaps in the certificate ecosystem — tells us that regulators have identified material weaknesses in the EPR credit market and are moving to close them before they undermine the entire compliance architecture. This is the moment companies discover whether their EPR programmes are genuinely robust or merely paper-compliant. Looking ahead, we expect CPCB to introduce stricter third-party audit requirements for recyclers, tighter certificate traceability mechanisms, and potentially a tiered penalty structure that makes EPR non-compliance financially prohibitive rather than merely inconvenient. The convergence of e-waste, plastic, and battery waste enforcement in a single news cycle also previews a future in which India's EPR regimes are managed under a unified digital compliance infrastructure — making real-time monitoring, and real-time enforcement, the new normal. Companies that invest now in robust EPR data systems, diversified recycler networks, and board-level sustainability governance will find themselves ahead of a regulatory curve that is only going to steepen.

title: EU CSRD and EFRAG's Evolving Standards: What Indian Companies Must Know About Europe's Sustainability Reporting Revolution
summary: The European Union's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD), underpinned by standards developed by the European Financial Reporting Advisory Group (EFRAG), is reshaping the global landscape of sustainability and financial reporting — and its reach extends well beyond European borders. I

sections.t: Executive Summary The European Union's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD), underpinned by standards developed by the European Financial Reporting Advisory Group (EFRAG), is reshaping the global landscape of sustainability and financial reporting — and its reach extends well beyond European borders. Indian companies that supply to, invest in, or operate within the EU market are increasingly subject to its disclosure requirements, either directly or through value chain obligations imposed on their European counterparts. Proactive alignment with CSRD and ESRS standards is no longer optional for globally integrated Indian businesses — it is a market access and investor confidence imperative.

What Changed The EU's CSRD, which came into force in January 2023, replaces the older Non-Financial Reporting Directive (NFRD) and dramatically expands the scope, depth, and enforceability of sustainability disclosures for companies operating in or trading with the European Union. EFRAG, the technical body tasked with developing the European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS), has published a comprehensive set of standards covering climate, environment, social, governance, and sector-specific topics that companies must report against. Unlike previous voluntary or high-level frameworks, ESRS mandates 'double materiality' — meaning companies must disclose not only how sustainability issues affect their financial performance (financial materiality), but also how their operations impact people and the planet (impact materiality). This dual lens fundamentally changes the nature of corporate sustainability disclosure from a reputational exercise to a rigorous, auditable financial reporting obligation. EFRAG continues to publish guidance, Q&As, and implementation support materials to help companies navigate the complexity of ESRS. Sector-specific standards and standards for non-EU companies with significant EU exposure are also in development, signalling that the regulatory perimeter will continue to expand.

Who Is Affected Large EU-listed companies and large EU undertakings (balance sheet > €20M, turnover > €40M, or >250 employees) — already in or entering scope from FY2024 onwards Listed SMEs on EU regulated markets — phased in from FY2026 (with opt-out until 2028) Non-EU parent companies with net turnover > €150M in the EU and at least one large or listed subsidiary/branch in the EU — in scope from FY2028 Indian exporters and suppliers to EU-based companies — indirectly affected through value chain disclosure requirements imposed on their European customers Indian companies with European institutional investors or dual-listed entities — subject to investor-driven disclosure expectations aligned with ESRS Indian IT, pharma, textiles, automotive components, and chemicals sectors — among the highest-exposure industries given deep EU trade and investment ties Indian conglomerates and multinationals with European subsidiaries — directly subject to CSRD obligations for those entities

Key Obligations & Deadlines FY2024 (reports due 2025): Large EU public-interest entities (>500 employees) already subject to NFRD must report under full ESRS for the first time — their value chain data requests to Indian suppliers begin NOW FY2025 (reports due 2026): Other large EU companies (not previously under NFRD) enter scope — expanding the universe of EU buyers requesting ESRS-aligned sustainability data from Indian value chain partners FY2026 (reports due 2027): Listed SMEs on EU regulated markets enter scope — further broadening supply chain disclosure pressure on Indian MSMEs FY2028 (reports due 2029): Non-EU companies with €150M+ EU turnover and an EU subsidiary/branch must file a CSRD-compliant sustainability report — Indian multinationals should begin gap assessments immediately Ongoing — EFRAG Sector-Specific Standards: EFRAG is developing sector ESRS (e.g., oil & gas, mining, textiles, food & agriculture); Indian companies in these sectors should monitor EFRAG's work programme closely Ongoing — EFRAG Voluntary Standard for SMEs (VSME): Indian SMEs supplying EU customers can use EFRAG's VSME standard to respond to value chain data requests in a structured, proportionate way Double Materiality Assessment: All in-scope companies must conduct and document a formal double materiality assessment — this is the foundational step before any ESRS disclosure can be prepared Third-Party Assurance: CSRD requires limited assurance on sustainability statements from FY2024 reporters, with reasonable assurance expected to follow — Indian entities preparing consolidated reports must factor in audit readiness

Climate Transition Angle ESRS E1 — the climate change standard — is one of the most detailed and consequential components of the ESRS framework. It requires companies to disclose their transition plans, Scope 1, 2, and 3 greenhouse gas emissions, climate-related physical and transition risks, and alignment (or misalignment) with a 1.5°C pathway. This directly mirrors the recommendations of the Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures (TCFD) and is closely interoperable with the ISSB's IFRS S2 Climate standard — creating a converging global architecture for climate financial disclosure. For Indian companies, the Scope 3 emissions requirement is particularly significant. EU companies must report upstream and downstream value chain emissions, meaning they will request primary emissions data from their Indian suppliers. Companies that cannot provide credible, verified Scope 3 data risk being excluded from EU supply chains or replaced by lower-carbon alternatives — a direct commercial consequence of climate disclosure gaps. India's own BRSR Core framework, mandated by SEBI for the top 150 listed companies from FY2023-24, shares conceptual DNA with ESRS — both require value chain disclosures, assurance, and climate risk reporting. Indian companies that invest in BRSR Core compliance are simultaneously building the capabilities needed to respond to CSRD-driven value chain requests. The convergence of BRSR, ISSB, and ESRS creates a strong strategic rationale for harmonised, integrated sustainability reporting infrastructure.

What To Do Now Map Your EU Exposure: Audit all EU revenue streams, customer relationships, investor bases, and subsidiary structures to determine whether your company faces direct CSRD obligations (as a non-EU parent) or indirect obligations (as a value chain partner) — and by when Conduct a Double Materiality Assessment: Engage your sustainability and finance teams to identify which ESRS topics are material to your business under both the financial and impact lenses — this is the non-negotiable starting point for CSRD alignment and should be documented and defensible Establish Scope 3 Data Collection Protocols: Begin collecting primary activity data across your supply chain to calculate and verify Scope 3 emissions — prioritise categories most relevant to your sector and most likely to be requested by EU customers under ESRS E1 Align BRSR Core and ESRS Reporting Infrastructure: Leverage your existing SEBI BRSR Core compliance programme as the foundation for CSRD readiness — identify gaps between BRSR disclosures and ESRS requirements, particularly on climate transition plans, biodiversity (ESRS E4), and workforce due diligence (ESRS S1) Engage Legal and Assurance Partners Early: CSRD sustainability statements require third-party limited assurance — brief your statutory auditors or sustainability assurance providers now on ESRS requirements to avoid bottlenecks when reporting obligations crystallise

Our Take CSRD and the ESRS standards represent the most consequential shift in corporate reporting since the introduction of mandatory financial audits — and EFRAG's ongoing work to refine, expand, and enforce these standards means the regulatory surface area will only grow. For Indian business leaders, the temptation may be to treat CSRD as a 'European problem' that only affects subsidiaries or compliance teams in Brussels. That view is dangerously shortsighted. The value chain provisions, the non-EU parent company rules, and the sheer weight of EU institutional investor expectations mean that CSRD's gravitational pull will be felt in boardrooms across Mumbai, Bengaluru, and Chennai well before 2028. The companies that begin building double materiality frameworks, Scope 3 data pipelines, and assurance-ready disclosure processes today will not only comply — they will differentiate themselves as trusted, transparent partners in a global economy that is rapidly pricing climate and sustainability risk into every transaction.

title: Extension of E-Waste EPR Filing Deadline to 31st December 2024: What Producers, Recyclers & Refurbishers Must Do Now
summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has extended the deadline for filing quarterly and annual EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility) returns for FY 2023-24 under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, to 31st December 2024. This is a critical window for producers, manufacturers, importers, and

sections.t: Executive Summary The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has extended the deadline for filing quarterly and annual EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility) returns for FY 2023-24 under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, to 31st December 2024. This is a critical window for producers, manufacturers, importers, and authorised recyclers and refurbishers who have not yet completed their compliance filings on the CPCB EPR portal. Companies that miss this extended deadline risk regulatory action, suspension of EPR authorisation, and reputational exposure — treat this as a hard stop, not another soft extension.

What Changed Under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, all registered entities — including Producers (brand owners, manufacturers, importers), and downstream partners such as refurbishers and recyclers — are required to file quarterly and annual returns on the centralised CPCB EPR portal. These returns capture data on e-waste generation, collection, channelisation, recycling, and EPR certificate transactions for a given financial year. The original deadlines for FY 2023-24 returns had already lapsed, and CPCB has now issued a formal notice providing a last-chance extension until 31st December 2024. This extension applies specifically to quarterly returns (Q1 through Q4 of FY 2023-24) as well as the consolidated annual return for the same financial year. The move signals that CPCB is aware of widespread non-compliance in the ecosystem but is unlikely to grant further leniency beyond this date, given the increasing scrutiny on EPR frameworks across all waste streams in India.

Who Is Affected Producers of Electrical and Electronic Equipment (EEE) — including Indian manufacturers and brand owners across consumer electronics, IT hardware, telecom equipment, large and small appliances, lighting equipment, and medical devices falling under the 21 categories listed in the Rules Importers of EEE — companies importing finished goods or components that result in e-waste at end-of-life Refurbishers — entities registered under the Rules for extending the life of used electronic equipment Authorised E-Waste Recyclers — dismantlers and processors who are EPR partners and must report volumes processed and certificates issued Producer Responsibility Organisations (PROs) — intermediaries managing EPR obligations on behalf of producers, who must reconcile and report channelised volumes Large enterprises and SMEs alike — there is no size exemption; all registered entities on the CPCB EPR portal for e-waste are covered

Key Obligations & Deadlines HARD DEADLINE — 31st December 2024: File all pending quarterly returns (Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4) for FY 2023-24 on the CPCB EPR portal (epro.cpcb.gov.in) HARD DEADLINE — 31st December 2024: File the consolidated Annual Return for FY 2023-24 on the CPCB EPR portal Producers must report: actual e-waste collected (in metric tonnes), EPR certificates purchased/redeemed, and shortfall or surplus against annual EPR targets set at registration Recyclers must report: volumes of e-waste received from producers/PROs, processed, and certificates generated — with reconciliation against CPCB-issued EPR certificates PROs must report: volumes channelised on behalf of each registered producer, with itemised category-wise breakdowns Entities with pending EPR certificate reconciliations or unresolved mismatches on the portal must resolve discrepancies before submitting returns — do not submit with open errors Post-December 2024: CPCB is expected to initiate show-cause notices, suspend EPR authorisations, or impose penalties under Rule 22 for entities remaining non-compliant

Climate Transition Angle E-waste EPR is not merely a domestic compliance checkbox — it sits at the intersection of India's circular economy ambitions and its obligations under the global climate transition. Electronic equipment contains critical minerals such as cobalt, lithium, copper, and rare earth elements whose responsible recovery directly reduces the carbon footprint and environmental damage associated with virgin mining. By ensuring formal, traceable recycling through EPR compliance, companies are effectively contributing to a domestic critical mineral supply chain that supports the energy transition — including batteries for EVs and renewable energy storage. From a global standards perspective, the ISSB's IFRS S2 and the EU's CSRD both require companies to disclose material risks and opportunities related to resource use, waste, and circular economy practices. For multinational producers or Indian companies supplying to global value chains, failure to maintain clean EPR records creates a data gap that will increasingly surface in supply chain due diligence requests from European and global buyers. India's e-waste rules are also benchmarked against EU WEEE Directive principles, meaning non-compliance here can raise red flags in cross-border ESG assessments. Physical climate risk is also relevant: informal e-waste recycling — which grows when formal EPR systems are weak — causes severe soil and groundwater contamination, increasing climate-related vulnerability in communities near informal recycling clusters. Strong EPR compliance strengthens the formal sector and reduces these localised environmental and social risks.

What To Do Now Audit your portal status immediately: Log into epro.cpcb.gov.in, identify all pending quarterly and annual returns for FY 2023-24, and assign a responsible team member to complete each filing no later than 20th December 2024 — build in buffer before the 31st deadline Reconcile EPR certificate data: Cross-check certificates purchased from recyclers/PROs against your annual EPR target (as registered with CPCB). Resolve any mismatches, duplicate entries, or unverified certificates with your recycler or PRO before filing — incorrect data in returns can trigger scrutiny even if filed on time Engage your PRO or recyclers proactively: If you rely on a PRO for EPR fulfilment, confirm with them in writing that their filings on your behalf are complete and that you have received the required certificate documentation. Do not assume it has been handled Document everything: Maintain offline records of all return submissions, acknowledgement receipts from the CPCB portal, and supporting data (collection records, recycler invoices, EPR certificates). These will be essential if CPCB initiates verification or audit proceedings post-deadline Begin FY 2024-25 compliance planning now: Use this filing exercise as a trigger to set up quarterly data collection systems so you are not in a similar position next year. Consider integrating EPR reporting into your broader ESG data management platform and aligning it with your BRSR disclosures on resource management

Our Take This extension is a regulatory lifeline, but it should be read as the last one. CPCB has demonstrated consistent intent to tighten the e-waste EPR framework since the 2022 Rules replaced the earlier 2016 regime — introducing EPR certificates as a tradeable instrument, mandating portal-based tracking, and progressively raising collection targets. The granting of an extension to 31st December 2024 reflects the reality that portal adoption and data quality across the ecosystem remain uneven, but the direction of travel is unmistakable: India is moving toward real-time, verifiable EPR compliance, not self-reported declarations. Companies that treat this extension as an opportunity to build robust internal systems — rather than simply filing to clear the backlog — will be far better positioned as CPCB introduces stricter enforcement, third-party audits, and potentially links EPR compliance status to broader environmental clearances and green procurement eligibility. The circular economy is becoming a core pillar of India's climate strategy, and e-waste EPR is one of its most operationally mature expressions. Get compliant by December 31st — then get strategic.

title: Fossil Fuel Export Emissions: The Accountability Gap That Threatens India's Climate Commitments and Supply Chain Credibility
summary: Climate Action Tracker's latest analysis exposes a structural accountability gap in global climate governance: the top fossil fuel exporting nations — Russia, Australia, the US, Indonesia, Saudi Arabia, and Canada — account for 50% of total exported fossil fuel emissions, yet these emissions appear

sections.t: Executive Summary Climate Action Tracker's latest analysis exposes a structural accountability gap in global climate governance: the top fossil fuel exporting nations — Russia, Australia, the US, Indonesia, Saudi Arabia, and Canada — account for 50% of total exported fossil fuel emissions, yet these emissions appear in no national inventory and no corporate Scope 3 accounting obligation. For India, this is not an abstract geopolitical debate. India is one of the world's largest importers of coal (from Indonesia and Australia) and oil (from Russia and Saudi Arabia), meaning the 'exported emissions' of these nations are India's Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions in waiting — embedded in the energy and industrial base of every BSE 500 company. The immediate consequence for Indian CFOs and CSOs is a compounding disclosure risk: as ISSB IFRS S2 adoption accelerates globally and SEBI's BRSR Core Scope 3 disclosures tighten, the combustion-side emissions of imported fossil fuels will become a mandatory reporting obligation — exposing Indian corporates to stranded asset risk, SBTi target misalignment, and investor scrutiny that their export-nation counterparts are currently escaping.

What Changed Reporting by Climate Action Tracker (https://climateactiontracker.org/blog/highlighting-the-hypocrisy-fossil-fuel-export-emissions/), authored by Sofia Gonzales-Zuniga, Danial Riaz, and Mia Moisio, quantifies for the first time the scale of what CAT terms the 'fossil fuel exports iceberg effect': countries like Norway and Australia have exported emissions that exceed their entire domestic greenhouse gas inventory. Australia's exported emissions are more than double its domestic footprint. Canada has had exported emissions exceeding domestic emissions since 2015. The US, meanwhile, has significantly grown its exported emissions over the past decade and — per US EIA 2023 projections — plans to increase oil and gas exports to 2050. What this analysis overturns is the comfortable diplomatic fiction that COP28's 'transition away from fossil fuels' language represents a coherent global decarbonisation pathway. It does not. The GHG Protocol's territorial accounting boundary means that Scope 1 emissions from Australian coal combustion in an Indian steel plant appear in India's national inventory and in Indian steelmakers' Scope 1 disclosures — not in Australia's. This is not merely a moral asymmetry; it is a live regulatory and capital market risk for Indian industry. Under SEBI's BRSR Core framework, top 150 listed entities (by market capitalisation) are already required to disclose Scope 3 emissions on a reasonable assurance basis for FY2024-25 onward. For energy-intensive sectors — steel, cement, power, chemicals — Scope 3 Category 1 (purchased goods and services) and Category 3 (fuel and energy-related activities) will directly capture the embedded emissions of imported coal, LNG, and crude oil. The CAT analysis makes clear that the upstream supply of these fuels is not being decarbonised: Indian companies cannot rely on supplier-side action to reduce their Scope 3 numbers. The India-specific regulatory hook deepens further: India's Perform Achieve and Trade (PAT) scheme under the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) sets specific energy consumption reduction targets for designated consumers, many of whom are large importers of thermal coal and fuel oil. If the PAT scheme evolves — as expected under India's Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) notified in June 2023 — into a carbon price-linked offset mechanism, the emissions intensity of imported fuels will directly affect the cost of compliance credits. Companies importing high-emission-intensity coal from Australia or Indonesia will face structurally higher compliance costs compared to peers transitioning to domestic renewable energy or cleaner fuel mixes.
