Who Is Affected Indian steel manufacturers (primary steel via blast furnace route) importing coking and thermal coal from Australia and Indonesia — direct Scope 1 and Scope 3 Category 1 exposure Indian power generation companies (thermal IPPs and state DISCOMs) relying on imported coal from Indonesia, South Africa, and Australia for capacity balancing — exposed to fuel cost volatility and future carbon border adjustments Indian oil refiners and petrochemical companies importing Russian crude under current pricing arrangements — Scope 3 Category 3 exposure and potential EU CBAM indirect risk if refined products are exported to European markets Top 150 SEBI-listed companies by market cap required to disclose Scope 3 under BRSR Core from FY2024-25 — audit committees face assurance risk if upstream emission factors for imported fossil fuels are not accurately sourced CFOs and treasury heads at energy-intensive BSE 500 companies negotiating long-term fuel supply contracts — need to price in forward carbon cost of imported fossil fuel emissions CSOs and sustainability heads at Indian conglomerates with SBTi near-term or net-zero commitments — Scope 3 Category 1 and 3 targets will be unachievable without a credible imported fuel decarbonisation pathway ESG analysts and institutional investors at domestic mutual funds, foreign portfolio investors (FPIs), and development finance institutions (DFIs) with exposure to Indian energy and industrials — need to reassess Scope 3 transition risk embedded in portfolio companies Export-oriented Indian manufacturers (textiles, auto components, chemicals) supplying EU buyers subject to CSRD — upstream Scope 3 disclosure requirements from EU customers will cascade to Indian suppliers' own fuel and energy emission disclosures

Key Obligations & Deadlines SEBI BRSR Core — Scope 3 disclosure (all 15 GHG Protocol categories) required with reasonable assurance for top 150 listed companies from FY2024-25 reporting (annual reports due by September 2025) SEBI BRSR Core — Scope 3 disclosure extended to top 1,000 listed companies from FY2026-27; companies must begin baseline data collection and supply chain emission factor mapping now India Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS), notified June 2023 under Energy Conservation Act 2001 — BEE to operationalise compliance market by FY2025-26; emission intensity of imported fossil fuels will affect PAT cycle 7 and CCTS compliance obligations ISSB IFRS S2 — material Scope 3 emissions including Category 1 (purchased goods) and Category 3 (fuel and energy) required in climate disclosures; Indian subsidiaries of multinationals or Indian companies with international listings face ISSB-aligned reporting obligations from financial years beginning January 2024 onward in early-adopter jurisdictions EU CBAM — full phase-in from January 2026 applies to cement, steel, aluminium, fertilisers, hydrogen, and electricity; Indian exporters in these sectors must verify and disclose embedded carbon content including from imported fossil fuel inputs to avoid CBAM surcharges at EU border SBTi Corporate Net-Zero Standard — companies with validated near-term targets must reduce absolute Scope 3 emissions 42% by 2030 from base year; Indian companies with SBTi commitments that source significant energy from imported coal face a structural compliance gap if supply-side decarbonisation by exporting nations does not materialise CDP Supply Chain Programme — Indian suppliers to global multinationals responding to CDP Supply Chain questionnaire must disclose Scope 3 Category 1 and 3 data; non-disclosure increasingly results in supplier delisting from procurement shortlists

Climate Transition Angle The CAT analysis fundamentally reframes the physical and transition risk calculus for Indian industry. India sits at the intersection of two colliding forces: it is simultaneously the world's third-largest coal consumer and one of the most climate-vulnerable major economies. The continued — and in some cases growing — fossil fuel export pipelines from Australia, Indonesia, Russia, and the US mean that the feedstock of India's existing industrial base will remain available and price-competitive for at least another decade. This creates a classic carbon lock-in dynamic: Indian steel and power companies can continue to access imported coal cheaply, reducing the near-term economic incentive to accelerate the transition, while simultaneously accumulating transition risk in their balance sheets as global carbon pricing and border adjustment mechanisms tighten. Under ISSB IFRS S2 — which India's MCA and SEBI are watching closely as the foundation for future mandatory climate disclosure standards — companies are required to disclose climate-related risks across the full value chain, including the transition risks associated with carbon-intensive purchased inputs. For Indian steelmakers using Australian metallurgical coal, this means disclosing the scenario analysis implications of a world where Australian coal exports face carbon pricing, export levies, or shipping emission costs under IMO's 2023 GHG Strategy (targeting net-zero shipping by or around 2050). The physical risk dimension is equally acute: climate impacts on Australian and Indonesian coal export infrastructure — cyclones, flooding of mine access roads, port disruptions — are live supply chain risks that BRSR Core's value chain risk disclosures now require Indian companies to assess. India's Nationally Determined Contribution (NDC), updated in 2022, targets 45% reduction in emissions intensity of GDP by 2030 and 500 GW of non-fossil electricity capacity. These targets are achievable in principle, but the CAT analysis illustrates that exporting nations are not pricing the transition cost into their export economics. India's policymakers at MoEFCC and NITI Aayog must now grapple with the reality that the 'transition away' language of COP28 will not be operationalised by exporting nations voluntarily — meaning India's own industrial transition timeline cannot be calibrated on the assumption of supply-side tightening of imported fossil fuels. This has direct implications for BEE's energy efficiency roadmaps and MoEFCC's Long-Term Low Emission Development Strategy (LT-LEDS) submitted to UNFCCC in 2022.

What To Do Now CFO / Head of Sustainability — within 60 days: commission a Scope 3 Category 1 and 3 materiality assessment using GHG Protocol Scope 3 Calculation Guidance; map all imported fossil fuel volumes (coal, LNG, crude oil, fuel oil) to country-of-origin emission factors to build BRSR Core-ready disclosure datasets ahead of FY2024-25 annual report filing Procurement and supply chain teams — within 90 days: engage key fossil fuel commodity suppliers (coal traders, LNG off-takers, crude oil procurement desks) to obtain primary emission intensity data per GJ of fuel delivered; this is required for credible Scope 3 Category 1 supplier engagement under SBTi's Supplier Engagement Rating and CDP Supply Chain scoring Head of Strategy / CSO — within 6 months: conduct a scenario analysis stress-test under IEA Net Zero by 2050 and IEA Announced Pledges Scenario for the price and availability of imported coal and LNG through 2035; present findings to board risk committee as a climate transition risk disclosure for BRSR Core and any TCFD-aligned investor reports Company Secretary / Audit Committee — before FY2024-25 assurance engagement: brief statutory auditors and ESG assurance providers on the Scope 3 boundary, specifically the treatment of imported fossil fuel emission factors; confirm that the assurance methodology aligns with ISAE 3000 or equivalent and that country-of-origin emission intensity data sources are defensible under SEBI's BRSR Core reasonable assurance requirement Board Risk Committee — at next quarterly meeting: table the CAT analysis alongside India's CCTS roadmap and EU CBAM phase-in timeline as connected transition risk triggers; request management to disclose what percentage of current Scope 1 emissions are dependent on imported fossil fuels and what the forward carbon cost exposure is under a ₹1,000/tonne CO2 and ₹3,000/tonne CO2 scenario Investor Relations / ESG team — within 3 months: proactively update institutional investor and FPI engagement decks to address Scope 3 Category 1 and 3 exposure from imported fossil fuels; anticipate specific questions from UNPRI signatories and Climate Action 100+ members about alignment with SBTi and ISSB S2 disclosure on value chain emissions

Our Take The CAT analysis is important not because it reveals something unknown — the accounting blind spot for exported fossil fuel emissions has been a feature of the UNFCCC architecture since Kyoto — but because it arrives at a moment when the capital market and regulatory infrastructure to act on this knowledge is finally maturing. ISSB IFRS S2's Scope 3 requirements, SEBI's BRSR Core reasonable assurance mandate, EU CBAM's embedded carbon methodology, and SBTi's Scope 3 absolute reduction targets are collectively creating a regulatory pincer movement that will, within five years, make the 'exported emissions are not my problem' position untenable for Indian listed companies. The exporting nations — Australia, Indonesia, the US, Russia, Saudi Arabia — are not going to resolve this through voluntary action. That means the accountability will migrate downstream, to the importers, combustors, and disclosers: Indian industry. What boards should watch over the next 24 to 36 months: first, expect IOSCO and IESBA to push for mandatory Scope 3 assurance standards that close the current gap between Scope 1/2 (limited assurance becoming reasonable assurance) and Scope 3 (largely unassured today) — this will change the cost and risk profile of Scope 3 non-disclosure overnight. Second, watch for India's CCTS to evolve from a sectoral intensity-based scheme to an absolute cap mechanism as India approaches its 2070 net-zero commitment year; at that point, the carbon cost of imported fossil fuels will be explicitly priced into Indian compliance markets. Third, anticipate that the EU CBAM scope — currently covering six sectors — will expand to cover chemicals, paper, and glass by 2030; Indian exporters in these sectors who have not built imported fossil fuel emission tracking into their cost accounting will face sudden and severe competitive disadvantage. The companies that treat the CAT analysis as a procurement, disclosure, and strategy problem today will be structurally advantaged when these regulatory shifts materialise. Those that wait for regulatory compulsion will be managing crisis, not transition.

title: Framework for EPR Certificate Generation Under E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022: What Producers Must Know
summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has released a detailed operational framework for generating Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) certificates under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, tying certificate issuance directly to the recovery of specific key metals — Gold, Copper, Aluminium

sections.t: Executive Summary The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has released a detailed operational framework for generating Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) certificates under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, tying certificate issuance directly to the recovery of specific key metals — Gold, Copper, Aluminium, and Iron — from end-of-life electrical and electronic equipment. Producers of electronics must now track metal-specific recycling obligations tied to item codes, with a phased gold recovery ramp-up starting at 20% of total obligation in FY 2023-24 and scaling to 100% by FY 2028-29. Failure to meet these obligations triggers automatic liability carry-overs or compensatory obligations in non-ferrous metal certificates, making compliance strategy a financial and operational priority.

What Changed India's E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 established an EPR regime that requires producers, importers, and brand owners (PIBOs) of electrical and electronic equipment (EEE) to ensure their products are responsibly recycled at end-of-life. What this new CPCB framework adds is a granular, metal-by-metal certificate system — moving EPR from a broad weight-based recycling target to a chemically-specific accountability model. Under this framework, EPR certificates are no longer generic proof of recycling. They are now issued based on the actual recovery of three classified groups of metals: (1) Precious Metals — specifically Gold (Au); (2) Non-Ferrous Metals — Copper (Cu) and Aluminium (Al); and (3) Ferrous Metals — Iron (Fe), including steel and galvanized iron. Each category of electronic equipment — from mainframes and laptops to mobile phones, refrigerators, and solar panels — has been assigned a specific metal composition percentage in Annexure I, which forms the mathematical basis for calculating a producer's EPR obligation. Critically, because India's current gold recovery infrastructure is limited — CPCB verified that the combined annual gold recovery capacity of 11 certified e-waste recyclers falls short of total producer obligations — the framework introduces a phased ramp-up specifically for gold EPR targets. For copper, aluminium, and iron, the obligation is immediate and set at 100% from the outset. Additionally, the framework provides a sophisticated offset mechanism: producers who over-perform on gold can convert that surplus into reduced non-ferrous metal obligations, and those who under-perform on gold must either carry the deficit forward or purchase additional non-ferrous certificates — creating a dynamic, interconnected compliance calculus.

Who Is Affected Producers, Importers, and Brand Owners (PIBOs) of all 45+ categories of electrical and electronic equipment listed under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 — including IT equipment (ITEW codes), consumer electronics (CEEW codes), and solar panels Manufacturers of smartphones, laptops, tablets, and personal computers — these carry gold (Au) EPR obligations and face the phased ramp-up schedule Producers of telecom infrastructure equipment such as BTS towers (ITEW18), which carry one of the highest gold composition ratios at 0.0035% by weight Consumer electronics brands selling TVs, refrigerators, washing machines, and air conditioners — subject to copper, aluminium, and iron EPR obligations at 100% from day one E-waste recyclers and dismantlers registered with CPCB — they are the certificate-generating entities whose verified metal recovery capacity directly determines market supply of EPR certificates EPR compliance service providers and recycling aggregators who facilitate certificate trading between recyclers and producers Solar panel manufacturers and importers (CEEW14) — now formally within the EPR certificate framework for iron and aluminium recovery Corporate sustainability, legal, and procurement teams responsible for EPR registration, target-setting, and annual reporting to CPCB's centralized portal

Key Obligations & Deadlines IMMEDIATE — Register on CPCB's EPR portal and establish item-wise EPR liability calculations using the metal composition percentages in Annexure I for every EEE category you produce or import FY 2023-24 (Year 1) — Meet gold (Au) EPR obligation at 20% of total gold liability; meet 100% of copper (Cu), aluminium (Al), and iron (Fe) obligations for all applicable EEE items FY 2024-25 (Year 2) — Gold EPR target increases to 30% of total gold obligation; any gold deficit from FY 2023-24 carries forward as additional gold liability or triggers a proportional increase in non-ferrous certificate purchases FY 2025-26 (Year 3) — Gold EPR target increases to 45% of total gold obligation FY 2026-27 (Year 4) — Gold EPR target increases to 60% of total gold obligation FY 2027-28 (Year 5) — Gold EPR target increases to 80% of total gold obligation FY 2028-29 (Year 6) — Full 100% gold EPR obligation must be met; complete phase-in of gold recovery infrastructure expected by this date ONGOING — For each financial year, calculate and exercise offset options (surplus gold → reduce non-ferrous liability; deficit gold → increase non-ferrous liability or carry forward) before annual compliance filing ONGOING — Procure EPR certificates only from CPCB-verified recyclers with documented metal recovery capabilities; maintain item-code-level (ITEW/CEEW) certificate records for audit purposes ONGOING — Monitor CPCB's periodic updates on Rare Earth and other precious material EPR obligations, which are flagged for future inclusion in the certificate regime

Climate Transition Angle This EPR framework is far more than a domestic compliance exercise — it is India's entry point into the global circular economy transition for critical minerals, and its implications for climate strategy are substantial. Gold, copper, aluminium, and the rare earth elements flagged for future inclusion in this framework are all classified as critical minerals essential to the clean energy transition. Copper is the backbone of electric vehicle motors, wind turbines, and power grids. Aluminium is central to lightweight EV design and solar panel frames. Rare earths are indispensable for permanent magnets in wind turbines and EV drivetrains. By building a formal, traceable recycling certificate system for these metals, India is creating the infrastructure for a domestic secondary materials market that reduces dependence on virgin mining — itself a major source of Scope 3 emissions in electronics and clean energy supply chains. From a supply chain risk perspective, companies that master EPR compliance early will also be building the data systems needed to respond to emerging global disclosure requirements. The International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) under IFRS S2 requires companies to disclose climate-related risks across their value chains — and physical risks to mining regions (from water stress, extreme heat, and flooding) increasingly affect the availability and price of these exact metals. Similarly, the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and the EU's Critical Raw Materials Act will soon require Indian exporters supplying European markets to demonstrate responsible sourcing and circular material flows. The metal-specific EPR certificate system being built now will provide precisely the kind of granular, auditable data that these global frameworks demand. There is also a physical risk dimension domestically: as climate change intensifies monsoon variability and urban flooding in Indian cities, informal e-waste processing — which currently dominates — becomes both a toxic hazard and an untracked loss of recoverable materials. Formalizing the recycling chain through this certificate framework is therefore also a climate adaptation measure, reducing community exposure to hazardous substances while securing domestic material stocks.

What To Do Now CONDUCT A METAL LIABILITY AUDIT: Map every EEE product category you produce or import to its ITEW or CEEW code, apply the Annexure I metal composition percentages to your annual quantity placed on market, and calculate your exact gold, copper, aluminium, and iron EPR obligations for FY 2023-24 — this is the foundation of all compliance planning BUILD YOUR RECYCLER NETWORK STRATEGICALLY: Not all CPCB-registered recyclers can generate gold EPR certificates — only 11 have verified gold recovery capacity. Identify and contract with these verified facilities now, before certificate demand outstrips supply as obligations scale up; simultaneously, secure capacity with copper, aluminium, and iron recyclers for 100% obligation fulfillment MODEL YOUR OFFSET SCENARIOS: Build a financial model that simulates both over-performance and under-performance on gold obligations and calculates the downstream impact on non-ferrous certificate requirements — this is essential for procurement budgeting and should be reviewed quarterly, not just at year-end INTEGRATE EPR DATA INTO ESG REPORTING SYSTEMS: Establish item-code-level tracking of EPR certificate procurement and retirement in a format that can feed directly into your BRSR (Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report) disclosures, ISSB-aligned climate reports, and any future CSRD-compliant value chain disclosures — avoid siloing EPR compliance in a legal team spreadsheet MONITOR RARE EARTH AND PRECIOUS MATERIAL DEVELOPMENTS: CPCB has explicitly signaled that Rare Earth Elements and other precious materials will be brought into the EPR certificate regime; companies in sectors like EV batteries, wind energy, and advanced electronics should begin inventorying these materials in their products now, so they are not caught off-guard when obligations are formalized

Our Take This framework represents a meaningful maturation of India's e-waste governance — moving from aspirational recycling targets to a chemically-specific, auditable accountability system that mirrors the direction of global circular economy policy. The phased gold ramp-up is pragmatic and reflects an honest acknowledgment of where India's recycling infrastructure actually stands today, rather than where regulations wish it were. However, the compounding nature of the offset mechanism — where gold deficits automatically inflate non-ferrous obligations — means that producers who treat EPR as a year-end box-checking exercise will face escalating financial exposure. The smarter play is to treat this framework as a five-year transition program: invest in verified recycler relationships now, build internal data systems capable of tracking metal-level obligations by product category, and position your company to meet the 100% gold target by FY 2028-29 without scrambling for certificates in a supply-constrained market. Looking ahead, we expect CPCB to progressively tighten the framework — adding rare earth obligations, increasing scrutiny of recycler verification, and potentially linking EPR certificate compliance to product registration or import clearances. Companies that build robust systems today will not only avoid penalties; they will have a competitive advantage as global buyers and investors increasingly demand proof of responsible material stewardship throughout the electronics value chain.

title: Framework for Generation of EPR Certificates Under E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022: What Producers, Importers & Brand Owners Must Do Now
summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued a framework governing the generation of Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) certificates under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, formalising how producers, importers, and brand owners (PIBOs) must account for end-of-life electronics they p

sections.t: Executive Summary The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued a framework governing the generation of Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) certificates under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, formalising how producers, importers, and brand owners (PIBOs) must account for end-of-life electronics they place on the Indian market. This framework establishes a certificate-based compliance mechanism — where EPR certificates, generated through registered recyclers and dismantlers, serve as proof that a company has met its e-waste collection and channelisation targets. Companies that fail to acquire sufficient EPR certificates risk non-compliance penalties, restrictions on market operations, and increasing reputational exposure as ESG scrutiny of supply chains intensifies globally.

What Changed The E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 replaced the earlier 2016 rules and introduced a digitalised, market-based EPR system in India — a significant structural shift. Rather than simply filing collection reports, producers and importers are now required to meet annual EPR targets expressed as a percentage of the quantity of electrical and electronic equipment (EEE) they sell or import, and to demonstrate fulfilment of those targets through EPR certificates traded or acquired on the CPCB's centralised online portal. The CPCB's framework notice operationalises the certificate generation mechanism in detail. EPR certificates are generated when registered e-waste recyclers or dismantlers process e-waste and report it on the CPCB portal. These certificates are then available to PIBOs — either through direct agreements with recyclers or via the EPR exchange marketplace. The framework clarifies the chain of custody: from e-waste collection, through authorised dismantling or recycling, to certificate issuance and transfer. This brings India's e-waste compliance architecture closer in spirit to internationally recognised producer responsibility schemes and creates a verifiable, auditable paper trail for corporate sustainability reporting.

Who Is Affected Producers of Electrical and Electronic Equipment (EEE): Indian manufacturers of laptops, mobile phones, consumer electronics, IT equipment, white goods, and other Schedule I items listed under the 2022 Rules. Importers of EEE: Companies importing any scheduled electronic product for sale in India, regardless of their size or sector. Brand Owners (OBOs): Entities whose brand name appears on EEE sold in India, even if manufacturing is outsourced — including large FMCG-adjacent electronics brands. E-Waste Recyclers and Dismantlers: CPCB/State Pollution Control Board-authorised recyclers who generate EPR certificates and whose operational standards directly affect certificate validity. EPR Producer Responsibility Organisations (PROs): Intermediaries that aggregate EPR obligations for multiple PIBOs and coordinate certificate procurement — subject to their own registration and reporting obligations. Retail and E-Commerce Platforms: Increasingly in scope as facilitators of EEE sales, with potential obligation to ensure seller compliance on their platforms. Large Corporates with IT Asset Disposal (ITAD) Programmes: Enterprises disposing of bulk IT equipment who may act as bulk consumers under the Rules and have parallel channelisation duties.

Key Obligations & Deadlines REGISTRATION (Immediate): All producers, importers, and brand owners not yet registered on the CPCB EPR portal (https://eprnewportal.cpcb.gov.in) must register without delay — registration is a prerequisite to any certificate acquisition or target fulfilment. ANNUAL EPR TARGET SETTING: PIBOs must declare the quantity of EEE placed on the market in the previous financial year; CPCB assigns annual collection/recycling targets as a percentage of this quantity, scaling up year-on-year through 2023–2024 and beyond as per the Rules. EPR CERTIFICATE PROCUREMENT: PIBOs must procure EPR certificates equivalent to their assigned annual target by entering into agreements with CPCB-authorised recyclers/dismantlers or by purchasing certificates through the CPCB EPR marketplace. CERTIFICATE RECONCILIATION — FY END DEADLINE: EPR certificate targets must be reconciled against actual certificates held by the end of each financial year (March 31). PIBOs should not wait until Q4 — certificate procurement should be planned and executed across the year. ANNUAL RETURN FILING: Submit annual returns on the CPCB portal detailing EEE placed on market, EPR targets, certificates acquired, and any shortfall — non-filing attracts enforcement action. RECYCLER DUE DILIGENCE (Ongoing): PIBOs must verify that recyclers/dismantlers they contract with hold valid CPCB/SPCB authorisation; certificates from unauthorised processors are invalid for compliance purposes. SHORTFALL PENALTY: Failure to meet EPR targets or acquire sufficient certificates may attract Environmental Compensation (EC) as notified by CPCB, which is proportional to the uncovered tonnage — companies should model worst-case EC exposure as a financial risk item. RECORD KEEPING: Maintain documented records of all EPR certificate transactions, recycler agreements, and portal filings for a minimum of 5 years for audit and litigation purposes.

Climate Transition Angle E-waste EPR compliance is not merely a domestic regulatory box-tick — it sits at the intersection of circular economy principles, supply chain decarbonisation, and global sustainability disclosure standards, all of which are rapidly converging on Indian businesses. From a physical risk perspective, unmanaged e-waste contains hazardous materials — lead, mercury, cadmium — that contaminate soil and groundwater, compounding climate-driven water stress already acute in Indian manufacturing hubs. Formalising e-waste recycling chains through the EPR certificate framework directly mitigates these localised environmental liabilities. On the supply chain side, multinational buyers and investors are increasingly requiring Scope 3 emissions data and circularity metrics from Indian suppliers. The recovery of critical minerals — cobalt, lithium, copper, rare earths — from formalised e-waste recycling reduces dependence on virgin extraction, lowering embedded carbon in electronics supply chains. Indian producers who can demonstrate certified, traceable e-waste management will have a tangible advantage in ESG-screened procurement processes. From a disclosure standpoint, the ISSB's IFRS S2 (Climate-related Disclosures) and the EU's CSRD both require companies to assess and disclose material environmental impacts across their value chain. E-waste generation and end-of-life management are increasingly recognised as material topics under these frameworks, particularly for technology, consumer electronics, and industrial sectors. India's own BRSR Core (Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting), mandatory for the top 150 listed companies from FY2023-24, includes supply chain sustainability and waste management as reportable indicators — EPR certificate data provides exactly the kind of quantified, third-party-verifiable metric that strengthens BRSR disclosures.

What To Do Now AUDIT YOUR EPR STATUS TODAY: Confirm registration on the CPCB EPR portal, validate your declared EEE tonnage placed on market for the last completed financial year, and reconcile any pending certificate obligations. Engage your compliance or legal team to assess exposure for prior years. MAP YOUR RECYCLER NETWORK: Identify and shortlist CPCB/SPCB-authorised e-waste recyclers and dismantlers in your operational geography. Conduct due diligence on their authorisation validity, processing capacity, and ESG standards — a recycler losing its authorisation mid-year invalidates certificates already paid for. BUILD EPR CERTIFICATE PROCUREMENT INTO ANNUAL PLANNING: Treat EPR certificate acquisition as a line item in your annual compliance budget. Model your target tonnage, estimate certificate costs (which fluctuate based on marketplace supply-demand), and execute procurement agreements in Q1-Q2 rather than scrambling in Q3-Q4. INTEGRATE EPR DATA INTO SUSTAINABILITY REPORTING: Align your EPR certificate data — quantities, recycler details, material streams recovered — with your BRSR, GRI 306 (Waste), or ISSB disclosure workflows. This turns a compliance obligation into a reportable circular economy metric visible to ESG-rating agencies and investors. ENGAGE A PRODUCER RESPONSIBILITY ORGANISATION (PRO) IF NEEDED: Smaller producers or importers without dedicated compliance teams should evaluate empanelled PROs who can aggregate targets, manage recycler relationships, and handle portal filings — but retain contractual oversight and do not outsource accountability entirely.

Our Take India's shift to a certificate-based, digitally traceable EPR system for e-waste is a structural maturation of environmental regulation — and the CPCB's framework notice for certificate generation is a signal that enforcement rigour is increasing, not decreasing. For too long, EPR compliance in India was treated as a paperwork exercise; the marketplace-based certificate mechanism changes that calculus by creating a real financial cost for non-fulfilment and a verifiable audit trail that regulators, investors, and global buyers can scrutinise. Companies that move early — building robust recycler networks, integrating EPR data into ESG disclosures, and treating circularity as a strategic asset rather than a compliance liability — will be better positioned as India's regulatory architecture continues to tighten in line with global best practice. Expect CPCB to increase Environmental Compensation rates, expand the list of scheduled EEE categories, and introduce stricter recycler auditing in the next 12–24 months. The time to build compliance maturity is now, not at the next enforcement notice.

title: India's INDC to UNFCCC: What It Means for Indian Businesses and the Climate Transition
summary: India's Intended Nationally Determined Contribution (INDC) submitted to the UNFCCC is the foundational policy document that defines India's climate commitments to the global community — anchored in the principles of climate justice, equity, and Common But Differentiated Responsibilities (CBDR). It s

sections.t: Executive Summary India's Intended Nationally Determined Contribution (INDC) submitted to the UNFCCC is the foundational policy document that defines India's climate commitments to the global community — anchored in the principles of climate justice, equity, and Common But Differentiated Responsibilities (CBDR). It sets the strategic direction for India's low-carbon development pathway and directly informs domestic regulations, sectoral targets, and corporate sustainability obligations. For Indian businesses and investors, understanding this document is not optional — it is the upstream source of virtually every climate-related compliance requirement they will face in the years ahead.

What Changed Under the Paris Agreement (adopted in December 2015), every signatory nation was required to submit an Intended Nationally Determined Contribution (INDC) — a formal declaration of its climate ambitions, targets, and strategies. India submitted its INDC to the UNFCCC, a document that articulates the country's position on mitigation, adaptation, finance, technology transfer, and capacity building. India's INDC is built on a foundational argument: that India, with per capita emissions of just 1.56 metric tonnes of CO2 equivalent (as of 2010) — far below the 7–15 metric tonnes seen in many developed nations — is not historically responsible for the climate crisis, yet bears a disproportionate burden of its consequences. The document calls for a climate-just global framework that respects developing nations' right to grow while encouraging a fundamentally cleaner development model. Critically, the INDC does not merely make a moral argument. It lays out concrete targets across renewable energy capacity, emissions intensity reduction, and forest carbon sinks — targets that have since been updated and formalized in India's Nationally Determined Contribution (NDC) submitted in 2022. The INDC thus forms the policy backbone from which all subsequent domestic climate regulations — including the Energy Conservation (Amendment) Act, SEBI's BRSR Core disclosures, the Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS), and PAT (Perform, Achieve and Trade) — derive their strategic legitimacy.

Who Is Affected Large energy-intensive industries (steel, cement, aluminium, fertilisers, textiles, pulp & paper) covered under the PAT Scheme and proposed carbon market mechanisms Power sector companies — both public and private — with obligations under renewable purchase obligations (RPOs) and clean energy transition mandates Top 1,000 listed companies required to file Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reports (BRSR) under SEBI regulations Infrastructure developers, real estate companies, and construction firms impacted by green building norms and energy efficiency standards flowing from INDC commitments Financial institutions, banks, and asset managers financing projects in fossil fuel-heavy or climate-vulnerable sectors Export-oriented manufacturers facing scrutiny under the EU's Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) and supply chain due diligence regulations abroad Small and Medium Enterprises (SMEs) in the supply chains of large corporations that have made net-zero or science-based targets aligned with India's NDC trajectory Agricultural and forestry sector entities, given India's commitment to creating an additional carbon sink of 2.5 to 3 billion tonnes of CO2 equivalent through forest and tree cover

Key Obligations & Deadlines Emissions Intensity Reduction: India committed to reducing the emissions intensity of its GDP by 33–35% by 2030 compared to 2005 levels — updated in the 2022 NDC to 45%. Companies in energy-intensive sectors must align their decarbonisation roadmaps with this national trajectory. Renewable Energy Capacity: India's target of achieving approximately 40% of cumulative electric power installed capacity from non-fossil-fuel-based energy resources by 2030 (updated to 50% in the 2022 NDC) directly drives RPO compliance obligations for obligated entities under state electricity regulations — ongoing and annually reviewed. Carbon Sink Enhancement: The commitment to create an additional carbon sink of 2.5–3 billion tonnes of CO2 equivalent through additional forest and tree cover by 2030 has implications for land-use, afforestation-linked carbon credits, and REDD+ project developers. Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS): Operationalisation of India's domestic carbon market — notified under the Energy Conservation (Amendment) Act 2022 — is directly linked to INDC/NDC commitments. Obligated entities must prepare for mandatory carbon credit obligations; Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) is developing sector-specific baselines (timelines expected 2024–2026). BRSR Core Disclosures (FY2023-24 onwards, mandatory for top 150 listed companies by market cap, expanding thereafter): Companies must disclose Scope 1 and Scope 2 GHG emissions, energy consumption intensity, and transition risk assessments — all framed within India's INDC/NDC commitments. Technology Transfer & Finance: India's INDC explicitly calls for international climate finance and technology transfer. Companies seeking concessional green finance under mechanisms like Green Climate Fund (GCF), multilateral development banks, or sovereign green bonds must demonstrate alignment with India's NDC targets. Adaptation Planning: Sectors vulnerable to physical climate risks — agriculture, water, coastal infrastructure, health — are called out in the INDC for dedicated adaptation action. Companies in these sectors should treat adaptation planning as a regulatory expectation, not merely a voluntary exercise.

Climate Transition Angle India's INDC is far more than a diplomatic document — it is the strategic blueprint that connects India's domestic climate regulations to the global climate architecture. Every major compliance framework that Indian companies now navigate — from PAT cycles and RPOs to BRSR disclosures and the emerging carbon market — is a domestic operationalisation of commitments made in this document. From a physical risk perspective, the INDC acknowledges India's acute vulnerability to climate impacts: erratic monsoons, glacial retreat, sea-level rise, and extreme heat events. These are not abstract future risks. The Reserve Bank of India and SEBI have both flagged climate-related financial risks as systemic concerns, and the INDC's adaptation commitments signal that regulatory pressure on climate risk disclosure will only intensify. On the global standards front, the alignment with ISSB's IFRS S2 (Climate-related Disclosures) and the EU's CSRD is unmistakable. Both frameworks require companies to disclose how their strategies align with national and international climate trajectories — which, for Indian entities, means alignment with India's NDC. Supply chain due diligence requirements under CSRD and the EU Deforestation Regulation further mean that Indian exporters must demonstrate their operations are consistent with India's forest carbon sink and land-use commitments. The INDC's emphasis on 'climate justice' — India's argument that development space must be preserved for emerging economies — also has a direct commercial implication: Indian companies that proactively demonstrate low-carbon growth are positioned to attract international climate finance, green bonds, and sustainability-linked capital at favourable terms, precisely because India's emission intensity per unit of GDP compares favourably to historical benchmarks of developed economies at similar development stages.

What To Do Now Map your emissions baseline against India's NDC trajectory: Conduct or update a GHG inventory (Scope 1, 2, and material Scope 3) and benchmark your emissions intensity reduction pathway against India's national target of 45% reduction in GDP emissions intensity by 2030 (vs. 2005). This is the foundational step for both BRSR compliance and carbon market readiness. Assess carbon market exposure: Determine whether your facilities fall under sectors likely to be designated as 'obligated entities' under India's Carbon Credit Trading Scheme. Engage with the Bureau of Energy Efficiency's sectoral consultations and begin building internal capacity for carbon accounting, MRV (Measurement, Reporting and Verification), and credit trading. Integrate climate risk into enterprise risk management: Use the INDC's identification of India's climate vulnerabilities — water stress, extreme weather, agricultural disruption — to conduct a scenario-based physical risk assessment for your operations and supply chain. This is now an expectation under BRSR Core and increasingly under lender due diligence frameworks. Align your renewable energy strategy with RPO and NDC targets: Review your current renewable energy procurement against applicable Renewable Purchase Obligation thresholds. Develop a multi-year roadmap to transition to renewable energy — through open access, captive generation, or Power Purchase Agreements — that positions you ahead of tightening RPO trajectories linked to India's 50% non-fossil capacity target. Prepare for international supply chain scrutiny: If you export to the EU or supply to multinationals with CSRD obligations, urgently assess your alignment with India's NDC commitments on emissions intensity, forest cover, and biodiversity. Build documentation and disclosure systems that can respond to customer and investor ESG due diligence questionnaires referencing India's climate targets.

Our Take India's INDC to the UNFCCC is not a compliance document in the conventional sense — there are no penalty clauses, no audit triggers, no immediate filing deadlines. But that is precisely what makes it easy to underestimate, and dangerous to ignore. It is the source code of India's entire climate regulatory architecture. Every sectoral target, every carbon market mechanism, every green finance instrument, and every disclosure mandate that Indian companies will face over the next decade traces its authority back to the commitments made in this document. The language of climate justice and CBDR that permeates the INDC also signals India's negotiating posture in future global climate rounds — meaning that international frameworks like CBAM, ISSB, and CSRD will increasingly need to engage with India's development imperatives, creating both friction and opportunity for Indian businesses operating across borders. Companies that treat the INDC merely as geopolitics are missing the point: it is, in effect, the long-range strategic plan for the Indian economy's transition, and business leaders who align their capital allocation, risk management, and disclosure strategies with its trajectory will be far better positioned to navigate what is coming next.

title: GHG Protocol's Land Sector and Removals Standard: What Indian Businesses Need to Know About Nature-Based Carbon Accounting
summary: The GHG Protocol's Land Sector and Removals Standard establishes the first comprehensive global framework for how companies must account for greenhouse gas emissions and carbon removals from land use, land-use change, forestry, and nature-based solutions. For Indian businesses — particularly those i

sections.t: Executive Summary The GHG Protocol's Land Sector and Removals Standard establishes the first comprehensive global framework for how companies must account for greenhouse gas emissions and carbon removals from land use, land-use change, forestry, and nature-based solutions. For Indian businesses — particularly those in agriculture, food and beverages, plantations, real estate, and infrastructure — this standard fundamentally changes how carbon sequestration claims and land-related emissions must be measured, reported, and verified. Companies that fail to align with this standard risk having their net-zero commitments and carbon offset claims dismissed by investors, regulators, and global supply chain partners.

What Changed Until now, the GHG Protocol's existing corporate accounting standards — most notably the Corporate Standard (2001) and the Land Use, Land-Use Change and Forestry (LULUCF) guidance — provided only partial and inconsistent direction on how companies should account for carbon stored in, or released from, land. This left a significant gap: companies could make sweeping claims about forest conservation, agroforestry, or mangrove restoration as carbon 'removals' without a rigorous, standardised methodology underpinning those claims. The Land Sector and Removals Standard closes that gap. It provides detailed accounting rules for biogenic carbon (carbon stored in living biomass, soil, and organic matter), land-based carbon removal activities (such as afforestation, soil carbon sequestration, and wetland restoration), and the treatment of carbon dioxide removals (CDRs) in corporate inventories and net-zero targets. Critically, the standard distinguishes between emission reductions (doing less harm) and actual removals (actively drawing down CO2), a distinction that has enormous implications for corporate net-zero integrity. It also introduces rules on how to treat permanence risk — the possibility that stored carbon is re-released due to fire, disease, or land conversion — and how to handle reversals in corporate accounts.

Who Is Affected Agribusiness and food companies with supply chains involving crops, livestock, or forest commodities (e.g., tea, coffee, palm oil, timber, rubber) Plantation and forestry companies managing large tracts of land for commercial or conservation purposes Fast-moving consumer goods (FMCG) companies sourcing agricultural raw materials and making 'net-zero' or 'carbon neutral' product claims Real estate and infrastructure developers undertaking land-use change, especially in peri-urban or ecologically sensitive zones Energy companies investing in biomass, biogas, or biofuel feedstocks tied to land use Financial institutions and ESG-focused investors financing land-sector projects or companies with significant land-related emission profiles Companies purchasing voluntary carbon offsets from nature-based solutions (NbS) projects — such as REDD+, afforestation, or blue carbon — to meet climate targets Any company with a Science Based Target (SBTi) or net-zero commitment that relies on land-sector removals to achieve balance

Key Obligations & Deadlines Separate accounting for emissions and removals: Companies must now disaggregate land-sector emissions from removals in their GHG inventories rather than netting them out — ensuring transparency and preventing double-counting. Biogenic carbon boundary setting: Organisations must clearly define the boundary of biogenic carbon flows in their inventory, including CO2 absorbed by crops, forests, or soils within their operational or value chain scope. Permanence and reversal accounting: Companies must assess and disclose the permanence risk of any carbon stored through land-based activities and establish protocols for how reversals (e.g., a forest fire releasing stored carbon) will be reflected in future inventories. Distinction between CDR and emission reductions in net-zero targets: Net-zero claims must separately quantify how much of the 'balance' comes from genuine removals versus avoided emissions — aligning with the Science Based Targets initiative's (SBTi) Corporate Net-Zero Standard. Supply chain (Scope 3) land emissions: Companies must account for land-use change emissions embedded in their supply chains — for instance, deforestation linked to soy, palm oil, or cattle — under Scope 3, Category 1 (purchased goods and services). Voluntary carbon market alignment: Offsets from nature-based solutions used in corporate claims must meet the standard's requirements for additionality, permanence, and accurate quantification — raising the bar for which credits qualify. Formal deadline for adoption: The GHG Protocol has not yet mandated a universal compliance deadline; however, alignment is expected to be required by major frameworks (SBTi, ISSB, CDP) as they update their guidance. Companies should treat 2025–2026 as the de facto adoption window for leading practice.

Climate Transition Angle India's land sector is both a major source of emissions and one of the country's most significant potential carbon sinks. India's Nationally Determined Contribution (NDC) under the Paris Agreement explicitly commits to creating an additional carbon sink of 2.5 to 3 billion tonnes of CO2 equivalent through forest and tree cover by 2030. The Land Sector and Removals Standard directly intersects with this national ambition — and with the growing scrutiny that Indian companies face from global investors and supply chain partners on how they account for nature. From a physical risk perspective, Indian land assets are acutely vulnerable. Changing monsoon patterns, rising temperatures, and increasing frequency of extreme weather events threaten the carbon stored in forests, wetlands, and agricultural soils. A forest that sequesters carbon today may release it through drought-induced dieback or wildfire tomorrow. The standard's permanence and reversal accounting rules force companies to internalise this physical climate risk into their financial and non-financial reporting — a critical step toward honest climate resilience planning. Globally, this standard is highly complementary to the International Sustainability Standards Board's (ISSB) IFRS S2 Climate Disclosures standard and the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD), both of which require disclosure of Scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions including land-use change. Indian companies listed on global exchanges or supplying into European value chains will face direct pressure to apply GHG Protocol land sector accounting as CSRD due diligence requirements cascade through supply chains from 2025 onwards. The standard also underpins the integrity requirements being developed under Article 6 of the Paris Agreement for internationally transferred mitigation outcomes (ITMOs) — relevant for Indian companies participating in carbon credit markets.

What To Do Now Conduct a land-sector emissions materiality assessment: Map all operations and supply chain activities involving land use, land-use change, or biological carbon stocks. Prioritise sectors where land-related emissions or removals are material — even if they have previously been excluded from your GHG inventory. Audit existing carbon offset and NbS claims: Review any voluntary carbon credits or nature-based solution investments currently used in net-zero or carbon neutrality claims. Assess whether the underlying projects meet the standard's requirements for additionality, permanence, and reversal risk management. Retire or replace credits that do not qualify. Update your GHG inventory methodology: Work with your sustainability team or external consultants to integrate land-sector emission and removal categories into your corporate GHG inventory, ensuring biogenic carbon flows are separately disclosed and Scope 3 land-use change emissions from supply chains are captured. Engage your supply chain on deforestation and land-use data: Begin collecting primary or verified secondary data from Tier 1 and Tier 2 suppliers on land-use practices, particularly for high-risk commodities. This is essential both for accurate Scope 3 reporting and for meeting emerging EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR) requirements. Align net-zero target architecture with the new standard: If your company has an SBTi target or a self-declared net-zero commitment, review whether your land-sector removal claims are properly ring-fenced from emission reduction targets, and whether your accounting methodology will withstand scrutiny under the new GHG Protocol rules. Engage your SBTi account manager or target-validation body proactively.

Our Take The GHG Protocol's Land Sector and Removals Standard is arguably the most consequential update to corporate carbon accounting in two decades. For too long, the land sector has been a refuge for vague, unverifiable, and sometimes misleading sustainability claims — from 'carbon neutral' product labels backed by low-quality forestry offsets to net-zero commitments that paper over Scope 3 deforestation with tree-planting programmes of questionable permanence. This standard ends that era. For Indian companies, the timing is particularly significant: as SEBI's Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting (BRSR) framework matures, as the carbon credit market in India develops under the Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS), and as European supply chain due diligence laws begin to bite, having a robust, internationally recognised methodology for land-sector accounting is no longer optional — it is a competitive and reputational necessity. Companies that move early to align will be better positioned to defend their climate credentials, access green finance, and retain access to premium export markets. Those that wait will find themselves scrambling to retrofit their disclosures under investor and regulatory pressure. The land beneath your feet is now firmly on the balance sheet.

title: GHG Protocol Standards & Guidance: The Accounting Backbone Every Indian Company Needs to Understand
summary: The GHG Protocol provides the world's most widely used greenhouse gas accounting and reporting standards, forming the methodological foundation for virtually every major climate disclosure framework — including SEBI's BRSR Core, the ISSB's IFRS S2, and the EU's CSRD. Indian companies that fail to gr

sections.t: Executive Summary The GHG Protocol provides the world's most widely used greenhouse gas accounting and reporting standards, forming the methodological foundation for virtually every major climate disclosure framework — including SEBI's BRSR Core, the ISSB's IFRS S2, and the EU's CSRD. Indian companies that fail to ground their emissions reporting in GHG Protocol methodology risk producing disclosures that are inconsistent, non-comparable, and increasingly unacceptable to regulators, investors, and global supply chain partners. Understanding and correctly applying these standards is no longer optional — it is the baseline for credible climate action.

What Changed The GHG Protocol, jointly developed by the World Resources Institute (WRI) and the World Business Council for Sustainable Development (WBCSD), is not a single regulation but a suite of standards and guidance documents that define how organisations measure, manage, and report their greenhouse gas emissions. The core suite includes the Corporate Accounting and Reporting Standard (the foundational standard for Scope 1 and 2 emissions), the Scope 3 Standard (covering value chain emissions across 15 categories), the Market-Based Accounting Guidance (for Scope 2 electricity reporting), and the Land Sector and Removals Guidance, among others. While the GHG Protocol itself is not a regulatory mandate in India, it is the methodological engine that powers frameworks that are mandated — most notably SEBI's Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting (BRSR) framework, which explicitly references GHG Protocol definitions for Scope 1, Scope 2, and Scope 3 emissions. As India's regulatory expectations on climate disclosure intensify, and as global standard-setters converge on GHG Protocol methodology, fluency in these standards has become a core corporate competency. Critically, the GHG Protocol is currently undergoing its most significant update in over a decade. WRI and WBCSD have launched a formal review process to update the Corporate Standard and the Scope 3 Standard, with stakeholder consultations ongoing. The updated standards are expected to reflect advances in climate science, greater alignment with ISSB and TCFD frameworks, and stronger guidance on avoided emissions, removals, and net-zero target-setting. Indian companies should monitor these developments closely, as updated standards will cascade into BRSR and other domestic requirements.

Who Is Affected Top 1000 listed companies required to submit BRSR reports under SEBI's mandate — GHG Protocol methodology underpins all emissions disclosures Companies in the BRSR Core cohort (initially the top 150 by market capitalisation) subject to reasonable assurance on ESG disclosures, where methodological rigour is directly scrutinised Export-oriented manufacturers and suppliers in sectors such as textiles, chemicals, steel, cement, and automotive whose global buyers require GHG Protocol-aligned Scope 3 data Companies subject to the EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) — iron, steel, cement, aluminium, fertilisers, electricity, and hydrogen exporters to the EU Indian subsidiaries of multinational corporations whose parent companies report under CSRD, CDP, or ISSB frameworks Companies pursuing Science Based Targets (SBTi) validation, which mandates GHG Protocol-compliant emissions inventories Financial institutions and asset managers conducting portfolio emissions assessments under PCAF (Partnership for Carbon Accounting Financials), which is built on GHG Protocol principles

Key Obligations & Deadlines BRSR Core Compliance (FY 2023-24 onwards): Top 150 listed companies must disclose Scope 1, Scope 2, and material Scope 3 emissions using GHG Protocol-aligned methodology, subject to reasonable assurance by a third-party auditor BRSR Extended Rollout: SEBI is progressively expanding BRSR Core requirements to the top 1000 listed companies — companies outside the initial cohort should treat FY 2024-25 as a preparation year to build GHG accounting capability EU CBAM Transition Period (now live): Indian exporters of in-scope goods to the EU must report embedded emissions using methodologies consistent with GHG Protocol principles; full financial obligations under CBAM commence in 2026 CDP Disclosure Cycle (annual, typically April–July submission window): CDP questionnaires for Climate, Water, and Forests require GHG Protocol-aligned data; scores directly influence investor and customer assessments GHG Protocol Corporate Standard Update (expected 2025-2026): Monitor WRI/WBCSD stakeholder consultation outputs and prepare to transition to updated standards once finalised — establish a methodology review process now Science Based Targets (SBTi) Validation: Companies committing to net-zero targets must submit GHG Protocol-compliant base-year inventories; SBTi has tightened validation timelines and criteria in its Corporate Net-Zero Standard Ongoing: Establish or review organisational and operational boundaries, consolidation approaches (equity share vs. operational control vs. financial control), and Scope 3 materiality assessments in line with GHG Protocol guidance

Climate Transition Angle The GHG Protocol is not merely an accounting exercise — it is the infrastructure through which physical climate risks and transition risks become quantifiable, comparable, and actionable. Without a robust GHG inventory, companies cannot credibly assess their exposure to carbon pricing, stranded asset risk, or the carbon intensity demands of their customers and capital providers. From a supply chain perspective, Scope 3 accounting — the most complex and consequential part of the GHG Protocol suite — is rapidly becoming a competitive differentiator and a commercial requirement. Global buyers, particularly in Europe and North America, are embedding Scope 3 data requests into procurement contracts. Indian suppliers that cannot provide verified, GHG Protocol-aligned emissions data risk losing market access, regardless of the quality of their products. The convergence of global disclosure frameworks around GHG Protocol methodology is accelerating. IFRS S2 (the ISSB's climate disclosure standard) explicitly references GHG Protocol for emissions measurement. The EU's CSRD and its underlying European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS) do the same. The SEC's climate disclosure rule in the United States, though facing legal challenges, also draws on GHG Protocol concepts. For Indian companies with cross-border operations, listings, or investor bases, GHG Protocol fluency is the common language of global climate finance and trade. Looking ahead, the ongoing update to the GHG Protocol Corporate Standard is expected to introduce stronger guidance on the treatment of carbon removals, the distinction between gross and net emissions, and the integration of physical risk data into transition planning. Companies that build deep internal capability in GHG accounting now will be far better positioned to adapt to these evolving requirements — and to use their emissions data strategically for target-setting, capital allocation, and stakeholder communication.

What To Do Now Conduct a GHG Protocol Methodology Audit: Review your existing Scope 1, 2, and 3 inventory against the current GHG Protocol Corporate Standard. Identify gaps in boundary-setting, emission factor selection, data quality, and documentation — particularly if your current disclosures were prepared without specialist input Map Scope 3 Materiality: Use the GHG Protocol Scope 3 Standard's screening methodology to identify which of the 15 categories are material to your business. Prioritise upstream categories (purchased goods and services, capital goods, fuel and energy) and downstream categories (use of sold products, end-of-life treatment) based on sector relevance and data availability Establish an Emissions Data Management System: Move beyond spreadsheet-based tracking. Invest in a dedicated GHG accounting platform or ERP-integrated solution that supports audit trails, emission factor libraries, uncertainty assessments, and scenario modelling — all requirements for third-party assurance under BRSR Core Monitor the GHG Protocol Update Process: Assign a member of your sustainability team to track WRI/WBCSD consultation documents and draft standard releases. Participate in stakeholder consultations where possible. Begin assessing how proposed changes — particularly on Scope 3 recalculation triggers and removals accounting — may affect your reported figures Align Internal Capacity with External Assurance Requirements: Train finance, procurement, operations, and sustainability teams on GHG Protocol fundamentals. Engage your statutory auditor or a specialist ESG assurance provider early to understand what evidence and controls they will require for BRSR Core reasonable assurance — and build those systems before the reporting deadline

Our Take The GHG Protocol is the closest thing the climate disclosure world has to a universal accounting standard, and its influence on Indian corporate reporting is only going to deepen. As SEBI tightens BRSR requirements, as CBAM reshapes the economics of exports to Europe, and as institutional investors apply increasingly sophisticated climate lenses to portfolio analysis, the quality of a company's GHG accounting will be read as a proxy for the quality of its overall climate governance. Companies that treat GHG Protocol compliance as a checkbox exercise — producing numbers without understanding the methodology, boundaries, or uncertainty behind them — will find themselves exposed when assurance providers, regulators, or activist investors probe the detail. The upcoming update to the Corporate Standard is a pivotal moment: it will likely raise the bar on Scope 3 rigour and bring GHG accounting closer to financial accounting in terms of auditability and precision. Indian business leaders who invest now in building genuine internal capability — not just in reporting, but in using emissions data to drive strategic decisions — will be the ones setting the terms of the climate transition, rather than scrambling to keep up with it.

title: GRI Standards: The Global Baseline for Sustainability Reporting and What Indian Companies Must Know
summary: The Global Reporting Initiative (GRI) Standards remain the world's most widely adopted framework for sustainability disclosure, providing a universal baseline against which stakeholders — investors, regulators, and supply chain partners — assess corporate ESG performance. For Indian companies, volun

sections.t: Executive Summary The Global Reporting Initiative (GRI) Standards remain the world's most widely adopted framework for sustainability disclosure, providing a universal baseline against which stakeholders — investors, regulators, and supply chain partners — assess corporate ESG performance. For Indian companies, voluntary adoption of GRI is rapidly becoming a de facto requirement as SEBI's Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report (BRSR) framework converges with international norms and global customers demand transparent, comparable sustainability data. Understanding and aligning to the GRI Standards today is no longer optional — it is a strategic imperative for market access, investor confidence, and climate credibility.

What Changed The GRI Standards are a modular, interoperable set of disclosure requirements developed by the Global Reporting Initiative, an independent international standards body. The Standards are structured in three tiers: Universal Standards (GRI 1, 2, and 3), which apply to all reporting organizations regardless of size or sector; Sector Standards, which provide tailored guidance for high-impact industries such as oil and gas, mining, agriculture, and financial services; and Topic Standards, which cover specific economic, environmental, and social issues such as climate change (GRI 305 — Emissions), water (GRI 303), labor practices, and human rights. In 2021, GRI completed a comprehensive overhaul of its Universal Standards, replacing the previous GRI 101, 102, and 103 with the current GRI 1 (Foundation), GRI 2 (General Disclosures), and GRI 3 (Material Topics) — effective for reports published on or after January 1, 2023. This update strengthened the concept of 'double materiality,' requiring companies to assess both how sustainability issues affect the business (financial materiality) and how the business impacts people and the planet (impact materiality). GRI also introduced a formal alignment with the UN Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights, raising the bar on human rights due diligence disclosures. Critically, GRI Standards are now explicitly recognized by SEBI as a compatible framework for BRSR Core disclosures, making familiarity with GRI essential for any listed Indian company navigating the evolving mandatory reporting landscape.

Who Is Affected Listed Indian companies (especially NSE/BSE-listed entities in the top 1,000 by market capitalization) subject to SEBI's mandatory BRSR requirements, where GRI alignment supports Core disclosures Export-oriented manufacturers and suppliers — particularly in textiles, chemicals, auto components, pharmaceuticals, and IT services — whose European and North American customers require GRI-aligned ESG reports Indian subsidiaries of multinational corporations that must align with parent-company GRI reporting cycles and CSRD (Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive) obligations in the EU Financial institutions, banks, and asset managers responding to RBI and SEBI sustainability guidelines and investor ESG due diligence requirements Infrastructure, energy, and mining companies covered by GRI Sector Standards, where sector-specific disclosures are increasingly scrutinized by lenders and multilateral agencies SMEs in global supply chains where Tier-1 suppliers are being required to cascade GRI-aligned data requests to their own vendors

Key Obligations & Deadlines Adopt GRI Universal Standards (GRI 1, 2, and 3) as the foundation of any sustainability report — mandatory for 'in accordance' reporting claims as of January 1, 2023 Conduct a formal double materiality assessment (GRI 3) that identifies and prioritizes material topics based on both impact on stakeholders and financial significance to the business — this must be documented and disclosed Disclose against GRI 2: General Disclosures covering organizational profile, governance, strategy, stakeholder engagement, and reporting practices — these are non-negotiable for any GRI-aligned report Identify applicable GRI Sector Standards (e.g., GRI 11 for Oil and Gas, GRI 12 for Coal, GRI 13 for Agriculture, Aquaculture and Fishing) and incorporate sector-specific disclosures — sector standards progressively becoming mandatory for 'in accordance' claims Report Scope 1, 2, and 3 greenhouse gas emissions under GRI 305 (Emissions) using a recognized methodology such as the GHG Protocol — increasingly required by lenders, investors, and supply chain partners Ensure human rights due diligence disclosures align with GRI 2-23 through 2-26 and relevant Topic Standards (GRI 406–413), reflecting the UN Guiding Principles alignment built into the 2021 update For BRSR Core compliance (mandatory for top 150 listed companies from FY2023-24, expanding to top 1,000 by FY2026-27): cross-reference GRI disclosures to satisfy SEBI's ESG data requirements and minimize duplicative reporting burden Obtain third-party assurance on GRI disclosures — a growing expectation from institutional investors and required for BRSR Core from FY2023-24 onwards for the top 150 listed entities

Climate Transition Angle GRI Standards sit at the center of the global convergence of sustainability reporting frameworks, making alignment a critical bridge between India's domestic regulatory requirements and international climate expectations. The International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) — whose IFRS S1 and S2 standards are now being considered for adoption in India — has explicitly built upon GRI's concept of impact materiality and cross-references GRI Topic Standards for non-financial disclosures. Companies that have a strong GRI reporting foundation will find the transition to ISSB-aligned reporting significantly smoother. From a physical climate risk perspective, GRI 201-2 (Financial Implications of Climate Change) and GRI 305 (Emissions) directly support the kind of climate risk disclosure now expected under TCFD-aligned frameworks that underpin both ISSB S2 and SEBI's BRSR. Indian companies in water-stressed sectors — agriculture, textiles, beverages — will find GRI 303 (Water and Effluents) increasingly scrutinized as physical water risk becomes a material financial issue. Similarly, supply chain disruptions linked to extreme weather events demand disclosure under GRI 204 (Procurement Practices) and GRI 308 (Supplier Environmental Assessment). For companies with European customers or investors, the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and its European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS) are partially interoperable with GRI — meaning GRI disclosures can satisfy a significant portion of ESRS requirements, reducing the compliance burden for Indian exporters navigating the EU's value chain due diligence expectations.

What To Do Now Conduct a GRI Gap Analysis immediately: Map your current BRSR or sustainability disclosures against GRI Universal Standards (GRI 1, 2, and 3) to identify gaps in materiality assessment methodology, governance disclosures, and stakeholder engagement documentation Formalize your Double Materiality Assessment: Convene a cross-functional team including Finance, Legal, Operations, and Sustainability to conduct and document a GRI 3-aligned materiality assessment — this is the backbone of a credible GRI report and increasingly a prerequisite for investor engagement Build your GHG emissions inventory: If you have not already, initiate Scope 1 and Scope 2 measurement using the GHG Protocol, and begin mapping Scope 3 categories relevant to your sector — GRI 305 compliance and BRSR Core requirements both depend on this data Identify your applicable GRI Sector Standard: Review GRI's published Sector Standards and determine which apply to your primary business activity; begin incorporating sector-specific disclosures into your next reporting cycle to stay ahead of the 'in accordance' requirements Engage an assurance provider early: Third-party limited or reasonable assurance over GRI-aligned disclosures is becoming the market standard and is required for BRSR Core — identify a qualified assurance provider and integrate them into your reporting process from data collection, not just at the review stage

Our Take GRI Standards are no longer simply a voluntary best-practice framework for progressive companies — they are rapidly becoming the de facto infrastructure of mandatory sustainability disclosure in India and globally. SEBI's BRSR framework has effectively borrowed GRI's architecture, ISSB has built upon its foundation, and the EU's CSRD has formally recognized GRI as a compatible framework. For Indian companies, this convergence is both an opportunity and a warning signal: those who invest now in building robust GRI-aligned reporting systems will gain a significant competitive advantage in accessing global capital, winning export contracts, and demonstrating climate credibility. Those who delay risk being caught in a reactive compliance sprint — expensive, disruptive, and credibility-damaging. The direction of travel is unambiguous: comprehensive, assured, and comparable sustainability disclosure is the new cost of doing business in global markets, and GRI is the common language in which that disclosure must be spoken.

title: Guidelines for Determination of Processing Capacity of E-Waste Recycling Facilities by SPCBs/PCCs
summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued new guidelines directing State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs) and Pollution Control Committees (PCCs) on how to standardise the determination of processing capacity of e-waste recycling facilities. This move is designed to bring uniformity and

sections.t: Executive Summary The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued new guidelines directing State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs) and Pollution Control Committees (PCCs) on how to standardise the determination of processing capacity of e-waste recycling facilities. This move is designed to bring uniformity and credibility to capacity assessments across India's fragmented e-waste recycling ecosystem — a critical input for the functioning of the Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework. Companies registered under EPR obligations for electronics and electrical equipment must pay close attention, as recycler capacity directly governs the availability and validity of EPR certificates they rely upon for compliance.

What Changed Under India's E-Waste Management Rules, producers of electrical and electronic equipment (EEE) are required to meet annual EPR targets by channelling end-of-life products to registered recyclers and obtaining EPR certificates as proof of fulfilment. However, a persistent challenge has been the inconsistency with which different SPCBs and PCCs assess and certify the 'processing capacity' of recycling facilities — leading to over-reporting, certificate fraud, and mismatches between actual recycling throughput and claimed capacity. The CPCB's new guidelines establish a standardised methodology for SPCBs and PCCs to determine, verify, and formally approve the processing capacity of e-waste recyclers. This means capacity figures will now be based on defined parameters — likely including installed machinery throughput, operational hours, input material handling capability, and environmental compliance status — rather than self-reported or inconsistently audited numbers. The net effect is a tightening of the supply side of EPR certificates, raising the bar for what qualifies as a legitimate recycling facility.

Who Is Affected Producers and importers of electrical and electronic equipment (EEE) with EPR obligations under the E-Waste Management Rules, 2022 E-waste recyclers and dismantlers registered with SPCBs/PCCs seeking capacity authorisation or renewal Producer Responsibility Organisations (PROs) facilitating EPR certificate procurement on behalf of producers Brand owners in consumer electronics, IT hardware, telecom equipment, large and small home appliances, and lighting sectors E-commerce platforms and retailers with EPR take-back obligations State Pollution Control Boards and Pollution Control Committees responsible for authorising and auditing recycling facilities Investors and lenders with exposure to e-waste recycling infrastructure or EPR-linked businesses

Key Obligations & Deadlines SPCBs/PCCs must adopt CPCB's standardised methodology when evaluating and approving processing capacity claims of e-waste recycling facilities — new applications and renewals must comply with the updated guidelines Recycling facilities must ensure their capacity assessments are aligned with the new parameters — any mismatch between authorised capacity and actual throughput may result in suspension or cancellation of authorisation Producers and PROs should audit their existing EPR certificate portfolios to confirm they are sourced from recyclers whose capacity has been validated under the updated guidelines EPR-registered producers must verify that recyclers in their supply chain proactively seek re-assessment from the relevant SPCB/PCC if their previous capacity authorisation was based on older, non-standardised methodology Compliance teams should monitor their SPCB/PCC portals and CPCB's centralised EPR portal for updated recycler capacity listings and any notifications requiring re-submission of capacity documentation No specific deadline has been published with this notice — however, given the 'Urgent Notice' classification, immediate review and engagement with registered recyclers is strongly advised ahead of the next annual EPR target cycle

Climate Transition Angle E-waste is one of the fastest-growing waste streams globally, and its responsible management sits at the intersection of circular economy goals, toxic material containment, and critical mineral recovery — all of which are material to India's climate transition narrative. Devices contain recoverable quantities of gold, copper, cobalt, and lithium, materials essential for the clean energy transition (batteries, EVs, solar infrastructure). Informal or under-capacity recycling not only creates environmental and health hazards but also forfeits the opportunity to recover these transition-critical materials domestically. From a global standards perspective, the ISSB's IFRS S2 (climate-related disclosures) and the EU's CSRD both require companies to assess and disclose risks and opportunities across their value chains — including end-of-life management of products.
