Indian companies exporting to the EU or listed on global exchanges will increasingly face questions about the integrity of their EPR compliance and the environmental standards of their recycling partners. Standardised capacity determination by SPCBs/PCCs directly strengthens the evidential basis for such disclosures. Furthermore, as India develops its own sustainability reporting standards (aligned with ISSB), credible EPR data will become a key input for Scope 3 emissions and waste-related metrics under emerging disclosure frameworks.

What To Do Now Audit your recycler network immediately: Contact all registered e-waste recyclers in your EPR fulfilment chain and confirm whether their capacity authorisations have been — or will be — reviewed under the new CPCB guidelines. Do not wait for your annual EPR reconciliation to discover a capacity shortfall. Review your EPR certificate inventory: Cross-check the volume of EPR certificates held against the verified processing capacities of issuing recyclers. Certificates linked to recyclers with inflated or unverified capacity claims may face scrutiny from CPCB during target verification. Engage your PRO proactively: If you use a Producer Responsibility Organisation, request a written confirmation that their recycler panel is being updated to reflect the revised capacity standards, and ask for documentation of re-assessments as they occur. Build capacity risk into your EPR procurement strategy: Given that the new guidelines may reduce the effective certified capacity available in the market in the short term, begin planning for EPR certificate procurement earlier in the compliance year — do not rely on last-quarter sourcing. Update internal ESG disclosures and due diligence processes: Incorporate recycler capacity verification as a formal step in your EPR compliance SOP, and flag this regulatory development in your next sustainability report or ESG data submission as evidence of strengthened supply chain oversight.

Our Take This CPCB guideline is a quiet but consequential intervention in India's EPR ecosystem. For years, the credibility of e-waste EPR compliance has been undermined by the opacity around recycler capacity figures — creating a market where certificate availability did not always reflect genuine recycling activity. By standardising how SPCBs and PCCs determine processing capacity, CPCB is effectively cleaning up the supply side of the EPR certificate market, which should — over time — reduce fraud, increase actual recycling rates, and make EPR data more defensible to international scrutiny. The short-term disruption is real: producers and PROs may find that previously reliable recycling partners now hold lower certified capacities, tightening certificate supply. But this is the necessary medicine for a system that needs credibility more than it needs convenience. We expect this to be a precursor to broader digitalisation and real-time monitoring of recycler throughput on the CPCB EPR portal — and companies that build robust, verified recycler relationships now will be far better positioned when that next layer of oversight arrives.

title: How to Set Science-Based Targets: A Practical Guide for Indian Businesses
summary: Science-based targets (SBTs) are rapidly becoming the global benchmark for credible corporate climate commitments, with the Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi) providing the most widely recognised framework for setting emissions reduction goals aligned with limiting global warming to 1.5°C. For

sections.t: Executive Summary Science-based targets (SBTs) are rapidly becoming the global benchmark for credible corporate climate commitments, with the Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi) providing the most widely recognised framework for setting emissions reduction goals aligned with limiting global warming to 1.5°C. For Indian companies — whether responding to investor pressure, supply chain requirements from multinational customers, or upcoming domestic disclosure mandates — understanding how to set and validate science-based targets is no longer optional. This post walks sustainability and compliance teams through the SBTi process, its obligations, and why acting now positions your organisation ahead of the regulatory and market curve.

What Changed The Science Based Targets initiative is not a government regulation — it is a global standard-setting body backed by CDP, the United Nations Global Compact, World Resources Institute (WRI), and WWF. However, its Corporate Standard has effectively become the de facto international framework for corporate emissions target-setting, and its requirements are increasingly being referenced in mandatory regulations worldwide, including the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and the ISSB's IFRS S2 climate disclosure standard. The SBTi requires companies to set greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions reduction targets across Scope 1 (direct emissions), Scope 2 (purchased energy), and, critically, Scope 3 (value chain emissions) that are consistent with the level of decarbonisation required to keep global temperature rise to 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels. Targets are independently validated by SBTi's technical team and, once approved, must be publicly disclosed and progress reported annually. As of 2024, over 7,000 companies globally have committed to or validated targets through SBTi. In India, the number remains relatively small but is growing rapidly, driven by export-oriented sectors, listed companies with foreign institutional investors, and businesses embedded in the supply chains of global corporations that have made their own SBTi commitments.

Who Is Affected Large Indian listed companies subject to SEBI's Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report (BRSR) Core disclosure requirements, particularly those in the top 1,000 listed entities Export-oriented manufacturers — especially in textiles, pharmaceuticals, chemicals, auto components, and IT services — whose global customers require supply chain emissions disclosures or SBTi alignment Indian subsidiaries or affiliates of multinational corporations that have made SBTi commitments and must account for Scope 3 supply chain emissions Companies seeking foreign institutional investment or green financing, where SBTi validation is increasingly used as a credibility signal by ESG-focused investors SMEs and mid-cap companies that are tier-1 or tier-2 suppliers to large domestic or global corporations with SBTi commitments Financial institutions and banks that are setting their own financed emissions targets and may require portfolio companies to demonstrate credible climate commitments

Key Obligations & Deadlines COMMITMENT LETTER: Submit a commitment letter to SBTi via their online portal, publicly pledging to set science-based targets — companies then have 24 months to develop and submit validated targets GHG INVENTORY (PREREQUISITE): Complete a comprehensive GHG inventory covering Scope 1, Scope 2, and Scope 3 emissions using the GHG Protocol Corporate Standard — this is required before targets can be set and must use a base year no earlier than 2015 SCOPE 3 MATERIALITY SCREENING: Identify which of the 15 Scope 3 categories are material (typically those representing more than 40% of total emissions) — SBTi requires Scope 3 targets if Scope 3 emissions exceed 40% of total footprint TARGET SETTING: Use SBTi-approved methods — the Absolute Contraction Approach (ACA) requiring at minimum 4.2% absolute reduction per year for Scope 1 and 2, or sector-specific pathways where available (e.g., Steel, Cement, ICT, Financial Institutions) SUBMISSION & VALIDATION: Submit targets to SBTi for independent validation — as of 2024, SBTi charges a validation fee (USD 4,950 for companies with revenue above USD 250 million; lower tiers for SMEs) and the review process typically takes 10–14 weeks ANNUAL PROGRESS DISCLOSURE: Once validated, companies must report progress against targets annually through public channels (e.g., CDP disclosure, annual reports, sustainability reports) — failure to report can result in targets being delisted NET-ZERO STANDARD (OPTIONAL BUT RECOMMENDED): Companies may also set long-term net-zero targets under the SBTi Net-Zero Standard, requiring 90–95% absolute emissions reduction by 2050 across all scopes, with residual emissions addressed through carbon removal (not offsets)

Climate Transition Angle Science-based targets sit at the intersection of physical climate risk and transition risk management. On the transition side, as carbon pricing mechanisms, border carbon adjustments (such as the EU's Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism, or CBAM, which came into force in 2023), and mandatory climate disclosure rules proliferate globally, companies without credible decarbonisation roadmaps will face higher cost of capital, loss of market access, and reputational damage. SBTi validation provides a third-party verified signal that a company's climate strategy is grounded in science, not marketing. For Indian companies specifically, the connection to global standards is direct and urgent. The ISSB's IFRS S2 standard — which India's SEBI has signalled it will align with in future iterations of BRSR — requires companies to disclose their transition plans and emissions reduction targets. SBTi provides a ready-made, globally recognised methodology that satisfies the 'target-setting' component of these disclosure frameworks. Similarly, the EU's CSRD requires European companies to assess and disclose the climate ambition of their value chain partners — meaning Indian exporters will face questionnaires and contractual requirements from European customers that effectively mandate SBTi-aligned thinking. On physical risk, the process of setting science-based targets forces organisations to deeply understand their emissions hotspots — which are often also their exposure points to physical climate risk. A company that discovers 60% of its Scope 3 emissions come from agricultural raw material inputs, for example, is simultaneously identifying a major physical climate risk (crop yield disruption) and a decarbonisation priority. SBTi target-setting, done well, is therefore an integrated risk management exercise, not just a compliance checkbox.

What To Do Now CONDUCT A GHG INVENTORY IMMEDIATELY: If your organisation does not have a verified Scope 1, 2, and 3 GHG inventory, commission one now using the GHG Protocol standard — this is the non-negotiable foundation for everything that follows and typically takes 3–6 months for a first-time exercise ENGAGE YOUR BOARD AND CFO: Science-based targets require capital allocation commitments — renewable energy procurement, supply chain transformation, process efficiency investments. Secure board-level buy-in early and integrate target-setting into your capital expenditure planning cycle IDENTIFY YOUR SECTOR PATHWAY: Check whether SBTi has a sector-specific standard for your industry (available for Steel, Cement, Apparel, ICT, Financial Institutions, and others) — sector pathways provide more tailored and often more achievable glide paths than generic absolute contraction methods SUBMIT YOUR COMMITMENT LETTER WITHIN THE NEXT QUARTER: Submitting the commitment letter is free, signals climate ambition to investors and customers immediately, and starts the 24-month clock — giving your team structured time to develop robust targets without being rushed MAP YOUR SCOPE 3 VALUE CHAIN NOW: Begin engaging key suppliers, logistics partners, and raw material providers for emissions data — Scope 3 data collection is the most time-consuming part of the process, and early supplier engagement builds the relationships needed for credible supply chain decarbonisation

Our Take Setting science-based targets is quickly transitioning from a voluntary best practice to a de facto market requirement for any Indian company operating at scale in global markets or seeking international capital. What makes SBTi uniquely powerful — and uniquely demanding — is that it does not allow companies to set whatever target feels comfortable; it anchors ambition to what climate science actually requires. For Indian businesses accustomed to setting internal sustainability KPIs, this can feel like a significant shift in approach, and it is. But that rigour is also its value: an SBTi-validated target carries credibility with investors, regulators, and customers that self-declared targets simply do not. Looking ahead, as SEBI tightens BRSR assurance requirements and India moves toward mandatory climate risk disclosures aligned with ISSB standards, companies that have already embedded SBTi methodology into their strategy will be structurally better positioned — both to comply with less effort and to genuinely lead the transition in their sectors. The window to be an early mover in India is still open, but it is closing faster than most boards appreciate.

title: IMO MEPC83 Carbon Levy Negotiations: What India's Shipping Sector, Exporters, and ESG Investors Must Prepare For
summary: The IMO's 83rd Marine Environment Protection Committee (MEPC83) session in London is negotiating a global carbon levy and fuel standard for international shipping — a sector responsible for 3% of global GHG emissions and entirely excluded from national NDCs under the Paris Agreement. Climate Action

sections.t: Executive Summary The IMO's 83rd Marine Environment Protection Committee (MEPC83) session in London is negotiating a global carbon levy and fuel standard for international shipping — a sector responsible for 3% of global GHG emissions and entirely excluded from national NDCs under the Paris Agreement. Climate Action Tracker (CAT) rates IMO's current climate ambition as 'Highly Insufficient,' consistent with 4°C warming. For India — the world's seventh-largest maritime trading nation, with over 95% of its trade volume moving by sea — the outcome of MEPC83 will directly reshape freight costs, export competitiveness, green finance obligations, and the commercial viability of its nascent green shipping corridor ambitions. The central fork in the road is between a flat-rate universal carbon levy (backed by Small Island Developing States and LDCs, and analysed favourably by CAT) versus the Singapore-led 'J9' credit trading and two-tiered fuel standard mechanism. India's position — and the outcome it should strategically support — carries significant consequences for its own trade-exposed industries, port infrastructure financing, and long-term BRSR climate risk disclosures.

What Changed Reporting by Climate Action Tracker (https://climateactiontracker.org/blog/imo-at-a-crossroads-a-defining-juncture-for-climate-action-and-an-equitable-transition/) lays out the live negotiating dynamics at MEPC83 with unusual analytical clarity. The critical shift this session is the EU's reported retreat from its earlier support of a strong carbon levy aligned with SIDS and LDC positions, opening space for a compromise around the J9 mechanism. This is not a technocratic footnote — it is a structural inflection point in global maritime climate governance that will set shipping emission rules for the 2030–2050 arc. The IMO's 2023 GHG Reduction Strategy had already set a target of 20% emissions reduction by 2030 (aspirational: 30%), which CAT assessed as 'Insufficient' — consistent with 3°C warming, not 1.5°C. Even the most ambitious MEPC83 outcome, CAT warns, will not align shipping with a Paris-compatible pathway. This is material for Indian businesses because international shipping emissions are not counted in India's NDC — meaning they represent an unaccounted, unhedged carbon liability sitting entirely within the value chains of Indian exporters, importers, and logistics players. The J9 proposal — a credit trading mechanism with a two-tiered Global Fuel Standard — introduces structural ambiguity that CAT explicitly flags: it does not generate stable, predictable revenues for climate-vulnerable financing, it risks locking in LNG as a transition fuel (extending fossil fuel infrastructure by decades), and it sends weak price signals to shipowners and charterers. For Indian exporters negotiating long-term freight contracts and for Indian ports planning green infrastructure, this ambiguity is itself a planning risk. Conversely, a flat carbon levy would embed a predictable, rising carbon cost into freight economics — one that India's trade-exposed sectors (textiles, steel, chemicals, pharma, gems and jewellery) need to begin stress-testing in their financial models today.

Who Is Affected Indian merchandise exporters with high freight intensity: textiles (Tirupur, Surat), steel (JSW, Tata Steel, SAIL exports), chemicals (Gujarat export corridor), pharma API exporters, and gems and jewellery — all sectors where CIF/FOB freight cost differentials determine margin Major Indian ports and port trusts: JNPA, Mundra (Adani Ports), Hazira, Paradip, Ennore — particularly those planning green hydrogen bunkering or green shipping corridor infrastructure under India's Harit Sagar Guidelines 2023 Shipping Corporation of India (SCI) and private Indian fleet operators: fleet decarbonisation capex planning, fuel transition timelines, and charter contract renegotiations CFOs and Chief Risk Officers at BSE 500 companies with material import/export freight exposure — particularly those filing BRSR Core disclosures where Scope 3 Category 4 (upstream transportation) and Category 9 (downstream transportation) emissions are increasingly investor-scrutinised ESG analysts and institutional investors at funds with exposure to Indian logistics, commodities, and port infrastructure — freight carbon cost is a latent P&L risk not yet priced into most Indian equity models India's Ministry of Ports, Shipping and Waterways (MoPSW) and Ministry of Commerce trade negotiators — India's positioning at IMO will affect which levy revenue redistribution architecture it benefits from or contributes to

Key Obligations & Deadlines MEPC83 (April 2025, London): Outcome of carbon levy vs. J9 negotiations will define the legal and commercial framework for international shipping emissions regulation through 2030 and beyond — Indian delegations and industry bodies must engage actively with MoPSW's IMO negotiating team NOW IMO 2023 GHG Strategy — 2030 milestone: 20% absolute GHG reduction from international shipping vs. 2008 baseline; Indian-flagged vessels and Indian charterers of foreign-flagged vessels will face compliance obligations under whichever mechanism is adopted IMO 2050 net-zero target: All fleet operators, including Indian companies, will need credible decarbonisation roadmaps; SCI and private operators should initiate IMO DCS (Data Collection System) audit-readiness and EEXI/CII compliance tracking now — CII ratings already apply to vessels over 5,000 GT BRSR Core (mandatory for top 150 listed companies from FY2024-25): Scope 3 emissions disclosure now includes upstream and downstream transportation — companies must begin quantifying freight-related Scope 3 exposure before the carbon levy crystallises that liability into a hard cash cost EU ETS for Shipping (already in force from January 2024): Indian exporters shipping to/from EU ports on vessels above 5,000 GT face EU ETS compliance costs passed through freight rates — this is live, not hypothetical; CFOs should be verifying contractual freight pass-through clauses now India's Harit Sagar Green Port Guidelines 2023 (MoPSW): Port operators must align green bunkering and vessel emissions infrastructure investment plans with whichever IMO fuel standard emerges from MEPC83 — premature LNG infrastructure lock-in is a stranded asset risk if a strong fuel standard is adopted

Climate Transition Angle India sits at an uncomfortable intersection in the IMO negotiations: it is simultaneously a major emerging economy with legitimate concerns about freight cost increases on trade competitiveness, a climate-vulnerable nation that would benefit from levy revenues directed at adaptation finance, and an aspiring green shipping hub under its National Green Hydrogen Mission and Harit Sagar guidelines. These three roles pull in different directions at the negotiating table, and India's IMO delegation has historically leaned toward protecting near-term freight economics over locking in long-term decarbonisation architecture. That calculus is now strategically suboptimal. The EU ETS already applies a carbon cost to shipping into European ports — India's top export destination — meaning a global IMO levy would, perversely, reduce India's net disadvantage relative to EU-domestic competitors by levelling the playing field. More importantly, if levy revenues are directed toward climate-vulnerable nations and maritime transition financing, India — which qualifies in multiple dimensions — could be a net beneficiary of the redistribution architecture, not merely a net payer. Under ISSB IFRS S2 (now incorporated by reference in SEBI's BRSR Core guidance direction), companies must disclose climate-related risks that are material to enterprise value. Rising freight carbon costs — whether from an IMO levy, EU ETS pass-through, or green fuel premiums — qualify as transition risk under the ISSB framework. Indian CFOs filing BRSR Core or preparing for voluntary ISSB-aligned disclosure need to begin building freight decarbonisation scenarios into their climate risk quantification, something virtually no Indian listed company has done publicly to date. This is both a disclosure gap and a board governance failure waiting to be exposed by institutional investors.

What To Do Now CFOs and Corporate Treasury (immediate — within 30 days): Model three MEPC83 outcome scenarios — flat levy at $100/tonne CO2, J9 credit mechanism, and status quo — against your freight cost base for FY2026-2030; quantify P&L sensitivity and flag material thresholds to the audit committee Chief Sustainability Officers at BSE 500 exporters (within 60 days): Conduct a Scope 3 Category 4 and Category 9 emissions inventory specifically for international sea freight; use IMO DCS data from your freight forwarders and logistics partners — this is the baseline you will need for BRSR Core disclosure and for any SBTi FLAG or full-scope target-setting Supply Chain and Procurement Heads (within 90 days): Audit freight contracts for carbon cost pass-through clauses; identify which shipping lines serving your trade lanes are CII-rated A/B vs. D/E — poor CII-rated vessels will either be surcharging green premiums or face port state control restrictions; renegotiate accordingly Boards and Risk Committees (next board cycle): Ensure the IMO MEPC83 outcome is formally tabled as a climate transition risk item; link it to the company's BRSR Principal 6 disclosures on environmental risks and the ISSB S2 transition risk register — this is no longer a logistics issue; it is a board-level material financial risk India Industry Associations (FIEO, CII, FICCI maritime committees — immediate): Formally engage MoPSW's IMO negotiating team to articulate Indian industry's preference for a levy architecture that includes equitable revenue redistribution — India should be advocating for the SIDS/LDC position to ensure it participates in the revenue redistribution mechanism, not just bears the cost Port Operators and Infrastructure Investors (strategic planning horizon): Pause or conditional-gate LNG bunkering infrastructure commitments pending MEPC83 fuel standard outcome; accelerate feasibility work on green ammonia and green methanol bunkering aligned with IMO's Well-to-Wake emissions framework — these are the fuels that survive all regulatory scenarios

Our Take MEPC83 is the most consequential international climate negotiation for Indian trade and logistics since the Paris Agreement, and it is receiving a fraction of the board-level attention it deserves in Indian boardrooms. The EU's reported retreat from the ambitious levy position signals that the final architecture will likely be a politically diluted compromise — but even a watered-down levy at $50–75/tonne CO2 will add $15–30 per tonne to bulk cargo freight rates and $200–400 per TEU to container freight, depending on fuel mix and route. For margin-thin Indian export sectors like textiles and SME manufacturers, this is not an abstraction. More fundamentally, MEPC83 marks the beginning of the end of international shipping as a carbon-cost-free logistics medium. Whether the instrument is a levy, a credit mechanism, or a fuel standard — and whether it arrives in 2027 or 2030 — the direction is irreversible. India's strategic error would be to fight the instrument rather than shape the revenue architecture. A well-designed levy with ring-fenced revenue for climate-vulnerable nations and green shipping transition finance is, structurally, better for India than a market-based credit mechanism that will be captured by well-capitalised Northern shipowners and financial intermediaries. For ESG investors and lenders in India, the signal is clear: any Indian company with material freight exposure — and that includes virtually every large-cap manufacturer and commodity exporter — needs a credible freight decarbonisation narrative in its BRSR disclosures and investor presentations within the next two reporting cycles. Funds applying ISSB S2 or EU SFDR Article 8/9 screens will increasingly penalise companies that cannot demonstrate they have stress-tested shipping carbon costs. The companies that move first on Scope 3 freight quantification and green charter strategies will not only manage the risk better — they will communicate it better to capital markets. In India's ESG disclosure landscape, that is still a meaningful differentiator.

title: IMO MEPC83 Carbon Levy Negotiations: What India's Shipping Sector, Exporters, and ESG Investors Must Understand Now
summary: The IMO's MEPC83 negotiations in London are finalising the architecture of a global carbon levy and fuel standard for international shipping — a regulatory moment with direct and underappreciated consequences for India's export economy, its shipping and port sectors, and the ESG disclosure obligatio

sections.t: Executive Summary The IMO's MEPC83 negotiations in London are finalising the architecture of a global carbon levy and fuel standard for international shipping — a regulatory moment with direct and underappreciated consequences for India's export economy, its shipping and port sectors, and the ESG disclosure obligations of listed companies with material ocean freight exposure. Climate Action Tracker rates the IMO's current ambition as 'Highly Insufficient,' consistent with 4°C warming, but even the most ambitious MEPC83 outcome will not align with a 1.5°C pathway — meaning further tightening is structurally inevitable. For Indian CFOs and CSOs, the question is not whether a carbon cost on shipping arrives, but at what price, in what form, and how fast it will cascade into supply chain costs, Scope 3 disclosures, and export competitiveness.

What Changed Reporting by Climate Action Tracker (https://climateactiontracker.org/blog/imo-at-a-crossroads-a-defining-juncture-for-climate-action-and-an-equitable-transition/) details that MEPC83, the 83rd session of IMO's Marine Environment Protection Committee, is currently negotiating two competing frameworks: a universal flat-rate carbon levy — championed by Small Island Developing States (SIDS) and Least Developed Countries (LDCs) — and the 'J9' proposal put forward by Singapore, which offers a two-tiered Global Fuel Standard paired with a credit trading mechanism rather than a direct levy. The EU, previously aligned with the SIDS bloc, has reportedly softened its position and is open to compromise, materially weakening the prospects of the more ambitious levy architecture. The significance of this divergence cannot be overstated for Indian business. International shipping emissions — covering approximately 3% of global GHG emissions, comparable to Germany's entire national footprint — are explicitly excluded from countries' Nationally Determined Contributions under the Paris Agreement. India's own NDC, updated in 2022 and targeting a 45% emissions intensity reduction by 2030 against 2005 levels, does not cover these shipping emissions. This means that whatever the IMO decides at MEPC83 represents the only binding multilateral lever on a segment of India's trade-linked emissions that currently sits entirely outside BRSR Scope 3 reporting norms and India's domestic climate architecture. A carbon levy at IMO level would impose direct cost pressure on Indian importers and exporters — sectors ranging from iron ore, steel, and textiles to pharmaceuticals and chemicals — regardless of India's domestic carbon pricing trajectory under the Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) notified under the Energy Conservation (Amendment) Act, 2022. The J9 alternative introduces a two-tiered GHG intensity standard that, as CAT notes, could make LNG-fuelled vessels more commercially attractive — a serious lock-in risk given India's significant investments in LNG bunkering infrastructure at Paradip, Ennore, and Kochi. If LNG becomes the dominant transitional fuel under a weak IMO standard, Indian port infrastructure capex decisions made in 2025–2027 risk becoming stranded assets by the time the IMO tightens standards in its post-2030 review cycle.

Who Is Affected Indian exporters with high ocean freight dependency: textiles and apparel (Bangladesh-competitive), pharmaceuticals (US/EU export lanes), steel and iron ore (seaborne bulk), chemicals, and auto components — all face direct cost pass-through risk from a carbon levy on shipping Indian Shipping Corporation (SCI), Shipping Corporation of India Land and Assets Ltd, and private fleet operators: compliance cost exposure, fleet retrofit decisions, and green fuel procurement strategies Major Indian ports — JNPT, Mundra, Paradip, Vizag — with bunkering infrastructure investment pipelines that will be shaped by which fuel standard the IMO adopts BSE 500 listed companies with material international shipping in their Scope 3 Category 4 (upstream transportation) or Category 9 (downstream transportation) under GHG Protocol — increasingly relevant under BRSR Core's value chain disclosure requirements for the top 250 listed entities CFOs and treasury heads at export-oriented manufacturers who currently do not price carbon into freight cost modelling or long-term supply chain contracts CSOs and ESG heads at companies with SBTi-validated or BRSR-disclosed net zero targets who have not yet addressed Scope 3 shipping emissions in their decarbonisation roadmaps Indian institutional investors and ESG analysts covering shipping, logistics, and export-driven industrials within BSE 500 portfolios DFIs and green bond issuers financing port infrastructure or green shipping corridors — including India's proposed Green Hydrogen corridors where shipping is a key offtake pathway

Key Obligations & Deadlines MEPC83 negotiations concluding April 2025: outcome will define whether a flat-rate carbon levy or the J9 credit-trading mechanism forms the basis of IMO's mid-term measure — Indian shipping companies and exporters must track the final text immediately IMO 2023 GHG Reduction Strategy: indicative target of 20% emissions reduction (aspirational 30%) by 2030 from 2008 baseline — any measures agreed at MEPC83 are expected to operationalise this; Indian fleet operators must begin fleet efficiency audits against these benchmarks now IMO Net Zero by or around 2050 goal: sets the long-term compliance horizon — Indian shipping companies with vessels currently on order or under long-term charter must stress-test against zero-emission fuel compatibility by 2045 at the latest BRSR Core (applicable to top 1,000 listed entities from FY2024-25): while Scope 3 is not yet mandatorily assured under BRSR Core, SEBI's phased roadmap signals value chain emissions will be in scope — companies with material shipping Scope 3 should begin baselining now to avoid disclosure gaps when SEBI mandates it EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) — steel, aluminium, fertilisers, cement, hydrogen: live from October 2023 for reporting, levy from 2026 — embedded carbon in sea freight is not yet in CBAM scope but EU's shipping ETS (effective January 2024) already adds carbon cost to vessels calling EU ports, directly impacting Indian exporters to Europe EU Shipping ETS (EU ETS Phase 4, effective 1 January 2024): 100% of emissions from intra-EU voyages and 50% of emissions from voyages to/from non-EU ports now require ETS allowances — Indian exporters to Europe face freight rate increases of €50–€100/tonne CO2 equivalent depending on vessel type; this is operational today, not theoretical India's Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) under Energy Conservation (Amendment) Act 2022: currently covers energy-intensive domestic industries — international shipping is outside scope, but the Ministry of Power's roadmap for CCTS expansion could eventually intersect with IMO compliance mechanisms for Indian-flagged vessels

Climate Transition Angle India sits in a structurally complex position in IMO negotiations. As both a major maritime nation (ranking among the top 15 in flag state tonnage and a significant ship-owning nation through SCI and private operators) and a large developing economy with freight-intensive export growth aspirations, India's negotiating posture has historically leaned toward the J9-style flexibility architecture rather than the flat levy. This is commercially understandable but strategically shortsighted from a transition finance perspective. The CAT analysis makes clear that the flat levy — with revenues directed to climate-vulnerable nations — is both the most cost-effective long-term solution (per UNCTAD's own Comprehensive Impact Assessment) and the most equitable. India, as a country that repeatedly and rightly calls for climate finance flows from developed to developing nations, should recognise that a robust carbon levy on shipping is one of the only multilateral mechanisms currently on the table capable of generating dedicated, predictable climate finance outside the UNFCCC's perennially underfunded channels. From a transition pathway perspective, the J9's LNG-tolerance creates a direct contradiction with India's own Green Hydrogen Mission and its ambition to build green ammonia and green hydrogen export corridors — both of which require zero-emission shipping as the offtake pathway to be commercially viable by 2030. If IMO locks in an LNG-compatible standard at MEPC83, the commercial case for Indian green hydrogen exporters weakens materially, as their European and Japanese customers will face lower carbon cost penalties for using LNG carriers rather than switching to green hydrogen or ammonia-fuelled vessels. This is a $6–8 billion investment decision sitting inside India's National Green Hydrogen Mission that will be shaped, in part, by what happens in London this week. Indian companies with BRSR-disclosed net zero targets that include Scope 3 shipping emissions — or those pursuing SBTi FLAG or Corporate Net Zero Standard validation — will find their 2030 and 2035 Scope 3 trajectories increasingly difficult to defend to institutional investors if IMO's ambition remains at the 'Highly Insufficient' level that CAT currently rates it.

What To Do Now CFO / Treasury (immediate — within 30 days): commission a freight carbon cost scenario analysis modelling three IMO outcomes — no levy, J9 credit mechanism, flat carbon levy at $18–$150/tonne CO2 range — and stress-test against your top 10 export trade lanes; build this into FY2026 cost forecasting and supplier contract renegotiation calendars CSO / ESG team (within 60 days): audit your BRSR Core value chain disclosures and SBTi target boundary to determine whether Scope 3 Category 4 and Category 9 shipping emissions are baselined; if not, initiate data collection through your freight forwarders and 3PL partners using GLEC Framework-aligned methodology — this is the methodology most likely to align with future BRSR mandatory Scope 3 reporting Legal / Compliance (within 60 days): review exposure to EU Shipping ETS pass-through in existing freight contracts for Europe-bound shipments; confirm whether your logistics providers are absorbing or passing through ETS allowance costs, and ensure this is captured in your BRSR Core 'Scope 3 significant emission sources' disclosure Shipping and Port teams / Infrastructure investors (within 90 days): pause or condition any LNG bunkering infrastructure commitments pending MEPC83 outcome; if the J9 is adopted, reassess LNG lock-in risk against IMO's post-2030 tightening cycle — build a 2035 regulatory scenario into your infrastructure IRR models Board Risk Committee (next board meeting): table IMO MEPC83 outcome as a named climate transition risk under BRSR Principle 6 and ISSB IFRS S2 (Climate-related Disclosures) — both require disclosure of policy and legal transition risks; the IMO carbon levy is now a board-level material risk for any listed company with >5% of COGS linked to international freight Investor Relations / ESG communications: proactively brief institutional investors and ESG analysts on your shipping emissions exposure and transition strategy — foreign portfolio investors (FPIs) subject to EU SFDR or TCFD-aligned mandates will increasingly ask about Scope 3 shipping risks in engagement calls through 2025–2026

Our Take MEPC83 is the most consequential IMO session since the 2018 Initial GHG Strategy, and the outcome will define the commercial and regulatory landscape for international shipping through 2035. The EU's reported backsliding from the SIDS-led levy position is a significant warning signal: it mirrors the pattern seen in early CBAM negotiations, where political compromises produced a weaker initial instrument that was subsequently tightened faster than industry expected. Indian boards and CFOs who dismiss the IMO carbon levy as a distant diplomatic problem are repeating the same miscalculation that left Indian steel and cement exporters underprepared for CBAM's October 2023 reporting phase. The structural direction is unambiguous — international shipping will be carbon-priced, the only variable is the mechanism, price point, and timeline. What concerns us most about the J9 outcome — which now looks more likely given EU softening — is not the immediate cost exposure but the signal it sends on transition finance. A credit-trading mechanism without predictable revenue generation will not fund the green corridor infrastructure, the methanol and ammonia bunkering networks, or the vessel retrofit financing that Indian shipping and port sectors need to remain competitive in a decarbonising global trade system. India should be using its G20 legacy and its voice at IMO not just to protect short-term freight competitiveness, but to secure a seat in the revenue redistribution architecture of a flat levy — ensuring that Indian climate-vulnerable coastal communities and Indian green shipping infrastructure both benefit from the transition finance flows a robust levy would generate. In the next 12–24 months, watch for: SEBI expanding BRSR Core Scope 3 assurance requirements to include transportation categories; RBI's climate risk disclosure framework (expected 2025–2026) beginning to ask banks about shipping-linked transition risk in export credit portfolios; and EU CSRD double materiality assessments by European customers of Indian exporters flagging upstream Scope 3 shipping emissions as financially material — triggering supplier-level data requests that Indian companies are currently not equipped to answer.

title: IMO Net Zero Framework Postponed to April 2026: What the Shipping Sector's 4°C Risk Trajectory Means for Indian Exporters, Ports, and Green Fuel Strategy
summary: In October 2025, the IMO failed to adopt the Net Zero Framework — its most ambitious shipping decarbonisation mechanism to date — postponing the decision to MEPC 84 in April 2026 under pressure from the US and Saudi Arabia. Climate Action Tracker (CAT) warns this delay risks locking international sh

sections.t: Executive Summary In October 2025, the IMO failed to adopt the Net Zero Framework — its most ambitious shipping decarbonisation mechanism to date — postponing the decision to MEPC 84 in April 2026 under pressure from the US and Saudi Arabia. Climate Action Tracker (CAT) warns this delay risks locking international shipping onto a 4°C warming trajectory through 2050, compared to a near-2°C pathway if the NZF is adopted and strengthened. For India, the stakes are existential on multiple fronts: as the world's second-largest maritime nation by cargo volume, home to major shipbuilding ambitions under the Sagarmala programme, and as an exporter whose supply chain competitiveness is directly contingent on how quickly green shipping corridors, alternative fuel infrastructure, and carbon-adjusted freight costs become the global standard. Indian boards and CSOs must not treat MEPC 84 as a distant diplomatic event. The NZF's fuel emissions standard and market-based mechanism will directly determine freight cost structures, green corridor eligibility, EU FuelEU Maritime compliance exposure for Indian-flagged or Indian-chartered vessels, and the commercial viability of India's nascent green hydrogen and methanol export ambitions.

What Changed Reporting by Climate Action Tracker (https://climateactiontracker.org/blog/rescuing-shippings-net-zero-framework/), the NZF — agreed at MEPC 82 earlier in 2025 — was expected to be formally adopted at the IMO's October 2025 session. It was not. The US and Saudi Arabia applied sufficient pressure to defer the decision to MEPC 84, scheduled for April 2026. The NZF, in its current form, combines a global fuel emissions intensity standard (the GFS — Greenhouse Gas Fuel Standard) with a market-based mechanism (MBM) that would price carbon in shipping through a combination of compliance units and a dedicated IMO Fund designed to accelerate zero-emission fuel (ZEF) uptake and support a just transition for developing nations including Small Island Developing States (SIDS) and LDCs. CAT's modelling is unambiguous and alarming: without the NZF, shipping emissions will continue rising through 2050. With it, the sector moves from 'Highly Insufficient' (consistent with 3–4°C) to 'Insufficient' (consistent with 2–3°C) by 2030 — still far from Paris-aligned but a material step change. Critically, CAT finds that if stronger fuel standard targets are set post-2035, the NZF framework architecture could theoretically be made 1.5°C-compatible by 2050. The delay to April 2026 is therefore not merely procedural — every month of inaction extends the window during which LNG lock-in, biofuel dependency, and unpriced carbon in shipping freight rates continue to distort the green transition economics. For India, this matters in five specific ways. First, the Directorate General of Shipping (DGS) and the Ministry of Ports, Shipping and Waterways (MoPSW) are active IMO member-state participants — India's diplomatic position at MEPC 84 will signal its alignment with the SIDS/LDC bloc supporting adoption or with the obstructionist bloc. Second, India's National Green Hydrogen Mission (NGHM) — which targets 5 MMTPA of green hydrogen production by 2030 — is premised partly on export via green ammonia and methanol shipping routes; the NZF's fuel standard directly prices the competitiveness of these fuels versus LNG. Third, Indian exporters in sectors including textiles, chemicals, auto components, and pharmaceuticals face mounting Scope 3 freight emissions disclosure requirements under BRSR Core (for the top 150 listed companies from FY2024-25) and will face direct exposure if EU counterparties begin requiring FuelEU Maritime-compliant shipping as a supply chain condition. Fourth, India's major ports — JNPT, Mundra, Chennai, Vishakhapatnam — are negotiating green shipping corridor MOUs under the Clydebank Declaration framework; the NZF's adoption is the enabling policy instrument for those corridors to achieve commercial credibility. Fifth, Indian shipyards under the Sagarmala and Shipbuilding Financial Assistance Policy are positioning for alternative-fuel vessel orders — a weakened or delayed NZF materially reduces the order pipeline and technology investment rationale for those yards.

Who Is Affected Indian exporters in merchandise-heavy sectors: textiles, pharma, chemicals, engineering goods, auto components — whose Scope 3 Category 4 (upstream transportation) and Category 9 (downstream transportation) emissions are directly tied to the carbon intensity of the vessels carrying their goods BSE 500 and NSE-listed companies required to report BRSR Core disclosures including Scope 3 emissions — especially those with export revenues exceeding 20% of total revenue where shipping freight is a material emissions source India's major port authorities and private terminal operators (Adani Ports, JSW Infrastructure, DP World India operations) whose capex planning for onshore green fuel bunkering infrastructure (green methanol, green ammonia, LNG) is contingent on IMO fuel standard timelines Indian shipbuilding companies (Cochin Shipyard, Mazagon Dock, Garden Reach Shipbuilders) seeking alternative-fuel vessel contracts from domestic and international shipowners — the NZF delay reduces near-term order certainty Green hydrogen and green ammonia producers and developers targeting maritime export corridors — ACME, Greenko, L&T, NTPC Green Energy, ReNew Power — whose project economics rest partly on shipping fuel demand curves shaped by the NZF Institutional investors and ESG-focused funds with exposure to Indian shipping, port, logistics, and energy transition stocks — including foreign portfolio investors (FPIs) subject to SFDR Article 8/9 requirements who must assess Principal Adverse Impact (PAI) indicators including shipping emissions CFOs and treasury heads at Indian conglomerates with captive shipping fleets or long-term freight charter agreements, where carbon pricing in shipping will become a balance sheet liability post-NZF adoption Chief Sustainability Officers at Indian companies preparing CDP Climate disclosures or ISSB IFRS S2-aligned climate risk assessments where shipping sector transition risk must be quantified as a material financial risk

Key Obligations & Deadlines MEPC 84, April 2026: IMO member states vote on NZF adoption — India's DGS and MoPSW must finalise India's diplomatic position by Q1 2026; CSOs at Indian shipping-exposed companies should engage MoPSW on India's stance now EU FuelEU Maritime: enters into force January 2025 for vessels >5,000 GT calling at EU ports — Indian-flagged or Indian-chartered vessels on EU trade routes must already comply; non-compliance triggers surcharges that Indian exporters may absorb indirectly through freight cost pass-through BRSR Core Scope 3 disclosures: top 150 listed companies from FY2024-25, expanding to top 250 from FY2025-26 — Category 4 and 9 freight emissions must be disclosed; shipping carbon intensity of contracted carriers becomes a material data requirement IMO CII (Carbon Intensity Indicator) and EEXI (Energy Efficiency Existing Ship Index): already mandatory from January 2023 — Indian shipowners and operators must already be rated; CII ratings directly affect vessel charterability and insurance pricing ISSB IFRS S2 (effective for reporting periods beginning January 2024): Indian companies voluntarily adopting or those subject to investor demands must assess climate-related financial risks including shipping sector transition risk in their value chain — the NZF delay extends the period of regulatory uncertainty that must be disclosed as a risk India's National Green Hydrogen Mission milestones: 1 MMTPA production target by 2026-27 — project developers must model NZF adoption/non-adoption scenarios into offtake and export logistics assumptions by mid-2025 planning cycles SBTi FLAG and Transport sector guidance: companies with significant freight emissions seeking SBTi validation must account for Scope 3 shipping emissions reduction pathways — the NZF's trajectory affects the baseline and reduction curve used in target-setting

Climate Transition Angle CAT's central finding — that the NZF determines whether shipping drifts to 4°C or converges toward 2°C — has profound implications for Indian climate transition strategy, which cannot be understood in isolation from the sector. International shipping accounts for approximately 2.9% of global GHG emissions, but for India, maritime trade carries over 70% of the country's merchandise exports by volume. The embedded carbon in that freight is increasingly being scrutinised by European buyers under the EU's Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive (CSDDD) and CSRD supply chain emissions disclosure requirements — meaning the NZF is not just an IMO issue but a direct supply chain competitiveness and market access issue for Indian exporters. Under India's Updated NDC (submitted to UNFCCC in 2022), India committed to reducing emissions intensity of GDP by 45% by 2030 from 2005 levels and achieving 50% cumulative electric power capacity from non-fossil sources. Shipping decarbonisation, while not explicitly named, sits within India's broader industrial and export sector transition pathway. More concretely, India's NGHM positions green ammonia and green methanol as priority export commodities — and the IMO's fuel standard is the single most important demand-side policy instrument for those fuels. A weakened NZF, or one that locks in LNG (as CAT explicitly warns is a design risk), directly undermines the NGHM's export revenue assumptions and the capital mobilisation rationale for the $100 billion+ green hydrogen investment pipeline India is targeting. The just transition dimension — highlighted by SIDS and LDC support for the NZF's IMO Fund — resonates directly with India's G20 presidency legacy and its positioning at COP29 and beyond as a voice for climate equity. India voting against or abstaining on NZF adoption at MEPC 84 would create a diplomatic contradiction with its stated climate leadership posture. Conversely, India actively championing NZF adoption with strengthened fuel standards positions it as a beneficiary of the MBM's technology transfer and fund provisions, potentially directing IMO Fund resources toward Indian port green infrastructure. The BRSR and ISSB S2 frameworks increasingly require companies to disclose physical and transition climate risks across the value chain — for Indian manufacturers with maritime supply chains, the NZF's fate is a named, quantifiable transition risk that must appear in FY2026 disclosures.

What To Do Now Sustainability/Government Affairs teams at Indian exporters and port operators: engage MoPSW and DGS by January 2026 to understand India's planned position at MEPC 84 and assess alignment with your company's green corridor and BRSR Scope 3 commitments — this is a six-week window CFOs at BSE 500 companies with >15% export revenues: commission a freight carbon intensity audit of your top five shipping lanes by Q1 FY2026 to baseline Scope 3 Category 4/9 emissions ahead of BRSR Core expansion to top 250 companies — use IMO DCS (Data Collection System) records if available from carriers CSOs at companies preparing ISSB IFRS S2-aligned or CDP Climate disclosures: explicitly model two NZF scenarios (adopted April 2026 vs. further delayed/weakened) as transition risk scenarios in your climate risk assessment — quantify the freight cost delta and Scope 3 emissions trajectory difference; this is now material enough to require board-level disclosure Green hydrogen and green ammonia project developers (ACME, Greenko, NTPC Green Energy, L&T Hydrocarbon): update financial models and offtake term sheets to include NZF sensitivity analysis — if the NZF is weakened to permit LNG flexibility through 2035, green ammonia bunkering demand curves shift materially; stress-test IRR at both NZF-adopted and NZF-weakened scenarios before finalising project FIDs in 2025-26 Port authorities and private terminal operators (Adani Ports, JSW Infrastructure): accelerate green bunkering infrastructure feasibility studies under Clydebank Declaration corridor commitments — specifically for green methanol and green ammonia — independent of NZF timing, since EU FuelEU Maritime is already live and creating demand from vessels on Europe-India routes Investor relations and board risk committees at listed Indian companies: include IMO NZF delay as a named transition risk in the FY2025-26 Annual Report's Management Discussion & Analysis section under 'Climate and Sustainability Risks' — citing BRSR Principle 6 and ISSB S2 risk identification requirements; do not wait for MEPC 84 outcome to begin disclosure

Our Take The IMO NZF postponement is a microcosm of the broader geopolitical fracturing of multilateral climate governance — and Indian boards should read it as a structural signal, not a temporary hiccup. The US withdrawal from climate multilateralism under the current administration, combined with Saudi Arabia's strategic interest in protecting fossil-fuel shipping revenue streams, has now demonstrably delayed the single most consequential sectoral decarbonisation mechanism outside of power and transport. CAT's finding that the NZF in its current form only moves shipping from 'Highly Insufficient' to 'Insufficient' should itself be alarming — this is the best case, not the benchmark. What is most consequential for India in the next 12 to 36 months is not whether MEPC 84 adopts the NZF — it probably will, in some form, given the coalition of SIDS, LDCs, and EU member states in support — but whether the MBM and IMO Fund survive intact or are gutted through the 'flexibility' provisions that CAT explicitly identifies as risks for LNG lock-in and ZEF delay. India's strategic posture at MEPC 84 will be one of its most consequential climate diplomacy decisions of 2026. A India that votes for a strong NZF — with the IMO Fund intact and fuel standard targets escalating post-2035 — is an India that positions itself as a green fuel exporter, a Clydebank corridor leader, and a just transition champion consistent with its NDC and NGHM commitments. An India that hedges, abstains, or aligns with the weakening bloc to protect short-term LNG bunkering revenues at ports like JNPT and Mundra is an India that undermines its own green hydrogen investment narrative and hands a reputational gift to competitors like South Korea, Japan, and the Netherlands who are already positioning as green shipping leaders. For institutional investors, the NZF delay extends uncertainty over the shipping sector's carbon liability — which means Scope 3 freight emissions remain harder to price and harder to reduce in portfolio company value chains. ESG-focused FPIs with SFDR Article 8/9 mandates should be pressing Indian investee companies on their freight decarbonisation roadmaps now, not after MEPC 84. The companies that have already mapped their maritime Scope 3 exposure, engaged their freight forwarders and shipping lines on fuel transition timelines, and built NZF scenario analysis into their ISSB S2 disclosures will be materially better positioned for capital access, export market credibility, and audit committee scrutiny in the 2026-2030 window. The NZF's fate is not a shipping industry problem — it is a board-level ESG risk for every Indian company that moves goods across an ocean.

title: IMO's Net Zero Framework Postponement to April 2026: What India's Shipping-Dependent Exporters, Port Operators, and ESG Investors Must Prepare For Now
summary: In October 2025, the International Maritime Organization failed to adopt its Net Zero Framework — a global fuel emissions standard paired with a market-based pricing mechanism — following blocking pressure from the United States and Saudi Arabia, deferring the decision to MEPC 84 in April 2026. The

sections.t: Executive Summary In October 2025, the International Maritime Organization failed to adopt its Net Zero Framework — a global fuel emissions standard paired with a market-based pricing mechanism — following blocking pressure from the United States and Saudi Arabia, deferring the decision to MEPC 84 in April 2026. The Climate Action Tracker (CAT) warns this delay risks locking shipping onto a 4°C emissions trajectory rather than the 2°C pathway the NZF would have enabled (https://climateactiontracker.org/blog/rescuing-shippings-net-zero-framework/). For India, the consequences are immediate and commercially material: Indian exporters moving roughly 95% of trade by volume through international shipping, major port operators at JNPA, Mundra, and Vizag, and Indian shipbuilders competing for next-generation vessel contracts all face accelerating compliance cost uncertainty — and their European buyers are already pricing Scope 3 shipping emissions into CSRD-aligned supply chain disclosures. The April 2026 IMO vote is not a distant geopolitical event; it is a board-level capital allocation decision for every Indian company with material ocean freight exposure.

What Changed Reporting by Climate Action Tracker (climateactiontracker.org), authored by Michael Petroni and Finn Hossfeld, confirms that the IMO's Marine Environment Protection Committee failed at its October 2025 session to adopt the Net Zero Framework measures that had been agreed in principle earlier in the year. The postponement to MEPC 84 (April 2026) was driven by opposition from the United States — which remains categorically opposed to the framework — and Saudi Arabia, whose economy is structurally exposed to declining bunker fuel demand embedded in any credible shipping decarbonisation regime. The NZF, in its current form, would introduce two interlocking mechanisms: a Global Fuel Standard (GFS) that mandates a progressive reduction in the greenhouse gas intensity of marine fuels, and a market-based mechanism (MBM) — effectively a carbon levy or credit trading system — that prices excess emissions and channels revenues into an IMO Fund to support zero-emission fuel infrastructure and a just transition for developing maritime nations. CAT's modelling projects the NZF would reduce shipping emissions 14–22% below 2008 levels by 2030, improving the sector's current 'Highly Insufficient' rating to merely 'Insufficient' — still consistent with 3–4°C of warming — but critically establishing the regulatory architecture for steeper reductions post-2035 that could make the framework 1.5°C-compatible by 2050. What this overturns is the assumption, held widely in Indian shipping and port circles, that IMO decarbonisation rules would arrive on a predictable schedule tied to the 2023 GHG Strategy milestones. That strategy committed to 'at least 20%' emissions reduction by 2030 and net zero 'by or around 2050.' The NZF was the operational instrument to enforce those targets through MARPOL Annex VI amendments. Its postponement creates a 6-month regulatory vacuum — but more critically, it signals that the US under its current administration will use its IMO membership to actively obstruct binding global shipping emissions rules, a posture that may persist beyond April 2026. For Indian trade policy and port investment planning, this is a structural uncertainty, not a procedural delay. The India-specific implication is layered. First, India is among the world's top 10 flag states and a significant ship recycling nation under the Hong Kong Convention; any IMO framework that reshapes vessel economics directly affects Indian yards at Alang. Second, India's major exporters — textiles from Tirupur, pharmaceuticals from Hyderabad and Ahmedabad, auto components from Pune and Chennai, chemicals from Surat — ship predominantly on foreign-flagged vessels whose owners will pass through compliance costs via freight rates the moment the NZF is adopted. Third, the EU's own unilateral shipping inclusion under the EU ETS, which has been operational since January 2024 and covers 50% of emissions on voyages to/from EU ports, is already creating a two-tier freight market. Indian exporters sending containerised cargo to Rotterdam, Hamburg, or Antwerp are already absorbing EU ETS carbon costs embedded in freight invoices — whether or not their CFOs have mapped this as a Scope 3 cost or a procurement risk.

Who Is Affected Indian merchandise exporters with >15% revenue from EU, UK, or US ocean freight routes — particularly textiles, pharma, chemicals, auto components, and agri-commodities Indian port operators and port trusts: JNPA (Nhava Sheva), Adani Ports (Mundra, Hazira, Kattupalli), DP World India, Vizag Port — all facing infrastructure investment decisions tied to green fuel bunkering (methanol, ammonia, green hydrogen) whose economic case shifts materially with NZF adoption Indian shipbuilders and ship recyclers: Cochin Shipyard, Hindustan Shipyard, Alang yards — competing for new vessel orders where green propulsion specifications are being set by European and Japanese owners anticipating NZF-aligned fuel standards CFOs and Chief Procurement Officers at listed Indian companies with material ocean freight in their Scope 3 Category 4 (upstream transportation) inventory — relevant to BRSR Core disclosures and ISSB IFRS S2 alignment ESG and fixed income investors in Indian port infrastructure, logistics REITs, and shipping-adjacent companies — particularly those benchmarked against ESG indices that are beginning to price IMO transition risk Indian banks and DFIs (SBI, EXIM Bank, IIFCL) with project finance exposure to port terminals and vessel financing, where stranded asset risk rises if NZF adoption forces accelerated retrofitting or early vessel retirement

Key Obligations & Deadlines MEPC 84, April 2026: The next IMO vote on NZF adoption — Indian maritime and trade ministries (MoPSW, DGSHIP, MEA) must submit India's formal position before this session; India's vote matters given its status as a major flag state and developing economy beneficiary of the IMO Fund EU ETS for shipping — already in force since January 2024: Vessels of 5,000 GT+ on EU port calls must surrender EU Allowances (EUAs) for 50% of emissions in 2024, rising to 100% by 2026; Indian exporters on EU routes are absorbing this cost now through freight surcharges — CFOs must quantify and disclose this in FY25 annual reports EU FuelEU Maritime Regulation — effective January 2025: Sets GHG intensity limits for energy used by ships on EU routes; escalates 2% below 2020 baseline by 2025, 6% by 2030, 80% by 2050 — directly sets the fuel specification standard for vessels carrying Indian goods to Europe regardless of IMO NZF outcome BRSR Core (mandatory for top 150 listed entities, FY2023-24; top 250 from FY2024-25): Scope 3 emissions disclosure under Principle 6 — CFOs must now decide whether ocean freight emissions (Category 4) are material enough to require quantification; NZF uncertainty makes this a forward-looking risk, not just a historical accounting question ISSB IFRS S2 — India's roadmap to adoption: ICAI and SEBI are monitoring ISSB adoption timelines; IFRS S2 requires disclosure of climate-related risks including transition risks from sector decarbonisation regulations — IMO NZF trajectory is a named transition risk for shipping-dependent Indian companies Ship Recycling: India's Recycling of Ships Act 2019 (Hong Kong Convention aligned) — as older, non-NZF-compliant vessels face accelerated retirement under any post-2026 framework, Alang yards must be positioned to absorb the resulting scrapping volumes; DGSHIP must assess capacity and compliance readiness

Climate Transition Angle The CAT's core finding — that NZF adoption determines whether shipping settles on a 2°C or 4°C emissions pathway — carries direct implications for India's own Paris Agreement commitments and its Nationally Determined Contribution (NDC) update cycle. India's updated NDC targets 45% emissions intensity reduction of GDP by 2030 and 50% of cumulative electric power from non-fossil sources. Shipping is not currently disaggregated within India's NDC, but international shipping emissions — by IMO convention — sit outside national inventories. This accounting gap means Indian companies can simultaneously claim NDC alignment domestically while their ocean freight supply chains generate uncounted, unpriced Scope 3 emissions that are material to global temperature outcomes. The transition angle sharpens when you consider India's emerging green hydrogen and green ammonia ambitions. The National Green Hydrogen Mission, with its ₹19,744 crore outlay, explicitly targets maritime fuel exports as a demand driver. India's NTPC, Adani Green, and Greenko are all developing green hydrogen production capacity partly premised on shipping sector offtake as marine fuel. If the NZF is adopted — even in its current 'Insufficient' form — it creates binding demand pull for zero-emission fuels on global shipping routes from 2030, directly validating the investment thesis for Indian green hydrogen producers targeting maritime export markets. A delayed or weakened NZF is, paradoxically, a demand risk for India's green hydrogen ambitions, not just a global climate policy failure. On physical climate risk: shipping route disruption from climate impacts — particularly cyclone intensification in the Bay of Bengal and Arabian Sea affecting Indian coastal port operations — is already documented in the physical risk literature. The TNFD's LEAP framework and ISSB IFRS S2 both require assessment of physical climate risk to operations. Indian port operators have not yet systematically integrated IMO transition risk with physical climate risk in their board-level risk registers — this is a governance gap that institutional investors should flag in their stewardship engagement with port companies listed on BSE/NSE.

What To Do Now CFO / Treasury — within 60 days: Quantify EU ETS freight surcharge exposure for FY25 and FY26 across all EU-bound ocean freight contracts; treat this as a known, escalating cost line (rising to 100% EUA coverage in 2026), not a contingent liability — budget accordingly and disclose in BRSR Core Scope 3 commentary Chief Sustainability Officer / ESG team — within 90 days: Map Scope 3 Category 4 (upstream transportation) and Category 9 (downstream transportation) for ocean freight lanes; use GHG Protocol Corporate Value Chain (Scope 3) Standard methodology; flag IMO NZF as a named regulatory transition risk in the company's climate risk register under ISSB IFRS S2 / BRSR Core alignment Procurement / Supply Chain Head — immediately: Engage top 3–5 shipping line counterparties (Maersk, MSC, CMA CGM, Evergreen) to obtain carbon intensity ratings of allocated vessel capacity; require green freight data feeds compatible with CDP supply chain reporting — this protects against Scope 3 audit exposure as BRSR assurance requirements tighten post-FY2025 Board Risk Committee — before Q4 FY26 board meeting: Commission a scenario analysis under two pathways — (a) NZF adopted at MEPC 84 April 2026, and (b) NZF further delayed or weakened — assessing freight cost impact, export competitiveness risk, and green hydrogen demand implications; disclose material findings in FY26 Annual Report under BRSR Principle 6 Government affairs / policy team — before February 2026: Engage Ministry of Ports, Shipping and Waterways (MoPSW) and the Ministry of External Affairs on India's MEPC 84 submission; India should align with SIDS and LDCs in supporting NZF adoption to protect the IMO Fund mechanism that benefits developing nations — and to preserve the demand signal for India's green hydrogen export strategy Port operators and infrastructure investors — within 6 months: Commission a green bunkering infrastructure feasibility study for methanol and ammonia at JNPA, Mundra, and Paradip; NZF adoption or EU FuelEU Maritime alone will create near-term commercial demand — first-mover advantage in South Asian green bunkering is a real competitive position

Our Take The IMO NZF postponement is a preview of the fracturing multilateral climate governance architecture that Indian boards and investors must now price into their strategic planning. The US opposition to binding shipping emissions rules is not an aberration — it is consistent with a broader posture of withdrawing from multilateral climate frameworks, from Paris Agreement re-exit to IRA rollbacks. But here is what sophisticated Indian companies should understand: unilateral regulatory action by the EU does not wait for IMO consensus. The EU ETS for shipping is live. FuelEU Maritime is live. The EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM), while currently covering steel, cement, aluminium, and fertilisers, has an explicit review clause for potential expansion to shipping. India's exporters are already operating under a de facto European shipping emissions regime — the IMO NZF, if and when adopted, would merely globalise what the EU has already unilaterally imposed. The deeper strategic signal is this: the 'by or around 2050' ambiguity in the NZF's net zero formulation — flagged explicitly by CAT — is a red flag for long-dated infrastructure and vessel financing. Indian banks and DFIs financing port terminals with 20–30 year asset lives, or vessel acquisition loans with 15-year tenors, are implicitly underwriting a transition risk they have not yet quantified. The stranded asset risk from premature vessel retirement or bunkering infrastructure obsolescence (LNG being the canonical example, given CAT's concern about NZF locking in LNG) should be stress-tested against MEPC 84 outcomes in every credit committee presentation from Q1 2026 onwards. Looking 12–36 months out: if MEPC 84 adopts the NZF in April 2026 — even in its current insufficient form — it will trigger an immediate renegotiation of long-term shipping contracts, accelerate retrofitting decisions for Indian-flagged vessels, create a commercial bunkering investment race in major South Asian ports, and force every BRSR Core-reporting company with material ocean freight to upgrade its Scope 3 Category 4 methodology from estimate-based to activity-data-based calculation. India's SEBI will face pressure from global institutional investors — already aligned with ISSB S2 — to require shipping transition risk as a named disclosure item in BRSR. The companies that have done the work by April 2026 will be differentiated in capital market conversations. Those that have not will face the same audit scrutiny that drove the first wave of BRSR Core restatements. The window to act is now, not after the IMO vote.

title: India's 2nd Amendment to Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2022: What Producers, Importers & Brand Owners Must Know
summary: The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) notified the Plastic Waste Management (Second Amendment) Rules, 2022 on July 7, 2022, introducing critical definitional clarity and strengthening the Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework for plastic packaging. The amendment

sections.t: Executive Summary The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) notified the Plastic Waste Management (Second Amendment) Rules, 2022 on July 7, 2022, introducing critical definitional clarity and strengthening the Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework for plastic packaging. The amendment formally defines key concepts — including biodegradable plastics, plastic packaging, post-consumer and pre-consumer plastic packaging waste, waste-to-energy, and recycled plastic use — bringing India's regulatory vocabulary closer to global standards. Producers, importers, and brand owners (PIBOs) must take immediate note: EPR obligations under Schedule II are now explicitly mandatory, and registration requirements with the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) have been tightened for entities operating across multiple states.

What Changed The Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016 have been amended for the second time in 2022 (the first amendment came in January 2022). This amendment, effective from the date of its publication in the Gazette — July 7, 2022 — makes targeted but consequential changes across Rules 3, 4, and 9 of the parent rules. At its core, the amendment does three things. First, it dramatically expands the definitional framework in Rule 3 by adding or refining over a dozen terms. These include 'biodegradable plastic' (distinct from compostable plastic, certified by CPCB under BIS standards, and required to degrade without leaving microplastics or toxic residues), 'plastic packaging,' 'post-consumer plastic packaging waste,' 'pre-consumer plastic packaging waste,' 'plastic waste processor,' 'recycler,' 'reuse,' 'use of recycled plastic,' 'waste-to-energy,' and 'end-of-life disposal.' This definitional expansion is not merely academic — it creates clear legal categories that determine which obligations apply to which actors. Second, the amendment updates Rule 4 on prohibitions and restrictions: references to biodegradable plastics are now included alongside compostable plastics in provisions relating to carry bags and packaging, and the applicable Indian Standard is updated from IS 17088:2008 to IS/ISO 17088:2021, aligning with the latest international compostability standard. Third, and most critically for business compliance, Rule 9 on EPR is substantially revised. Sub-rule (1) is replaced to make explicit that producers, importers, and brand owners shall fulfil EPR obligations for plastic packaging as specified in Schedule II. References to collection plans and a two-year implementation window have been removed, signalling that the grace period is over — EPR is now a present, enforceable obligation. The amendment also clarifies that entities operating in more than two states or union territories must register with the CPCB (not just state pollution control boards), centralising oversight for larger national players.

Who Is Affected Producers: Manufacturers of plastic packaging materials, plastic carry bags, multilayer packaging, and plastic sheets — regardless of sector Importers: Any entity importing plastic packaging, products packaged in plastic, carry bags, multilayer packaging, or plastic sheets into India Brand Owners (including those with registered trademarks): Companies that sell products under their own brand in plastic packaging — this now explicitly includes trademark holders Plastic Waste Processors: Units engaged in recycling, co-processing (e.g., in cement or steel plants), waste-to-oil conversion, or waste-to-energy operations Recyclers: Units involved in the recycling of plastic waste, including industrial composting Fast-Moving Consumer Goods (FMCG) sector: High exposure due to large volumes of plastic packaging across product categories E-commerce companies: Significant users of plastic packaging for logistics and delivery — now more clearly captured under the brand owner definition Retail and organised trade: Entities using carry bags or packaging under their own brand or trademark Multi-state businesses: Companies operating across more than two states or union territories must now register with CPCB centrally, not just with state boards

Key Obligations & Deadlines Effective immediately (July 7, 2022): All provisions of the Second Amendment Rules came into force on the date of Gazette notification — there is no phased implementation grace period EPR compliance — mandatory now: Producers, importers, and brand owners must fulfil EPR targets for plastic packaging as per Schedule II of the PWM Rules; the earlier reference to a collection plan and two-year window has been deleted, confirming EPR is a live obligation CPCB registration — multi-state operators: Entities operating in more than two states or union territories must register with the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB); single-state operators continue to register with the relevant State Pollution Control Board (SPCB) or Pollution Control Committee (PCC) under Rule 13(2) Biodegradable plastic compliance: Producers or sellers of biodegradable plastics (as newly defined) must ensure CPCB certification and conformity with IS/ISO 17088:2021 (updated from IS 17088:2008); products must not generate microplastics or toxic residues under ambient environmental conditions Trademark holders: Companies whose products are sold under registered trademarks are now explicitly included in the definition of 'brand owner' — such entities must assess their EPR exposure and register accordingly if not already done Post-consumer and pre-consumer waste tracking: With formal definitions now in place, businesses should establish systems to separately account for and report pre-consumer (manufacturing stage) and post-consumer (end-user stage) plastic packaging waste — this data will be critical for EPR target calculations and CPCB audits End-of-life disposal pathways: Companies channelling plastic waste to co-processors, waste-to-energy plants, or waste-to-oil units must ensure these facilities fall within the newly defined categories and comply with applicable guidelines; feedstock chemical production for plastic remanufacturing is separately classified as recycling

Climate Transition Angle India's tightening of plastic waste regulation is not happening in isolation — it is part of a global regulatory convergence on plastic pollution that carries direct climate implications. Plastics are derived predominantly from fossil fuels; the production, incineration, and landfilling of plastic waste contributes to greenhouse gas emissions across the entire value chain. The amendment's formal recognition of waste-to-energy, co-processing, and waste-to-oil pathways — while practical transition mechanisms — must be read alongside India's net-zero commitments and the need to eventually phase down fossil-fuel-derived materials. For businesses reporting under frameworks such as the ISSB's IFRS S1/S2 standards or preparing for alignment with the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) — increasingly relevant for Indian exporters to European markets — plastic waste management is a material topic under both physical risk (pollution, ecosystem degradation) and transition risk (policy, regulatory, and market shifts toward circular economy models). The new definitions around recycled plastic use and the distinction between pre- and post-consumer waste directly mirror the language used in CSRD-aligned value chain disclosures and the EU Packaging and Packaging Waste Regulation (PPWR). The push for higher recycled content in plastic packaging, implicit in these rules, also connects to supply chain resilience: companies that develop domestic recycled plastic supply chains reduce their exposure to virgin polymer price volatility — itself linked to global oil markets and carbon pricing trends. Early movers who build robust EPR compliance systems will be better positioned as India inevitably tightens recycled content mandates and aligns with the global plastics treaty currently being negotiated under UNEA.

What To Do Now Audit your EPR registration status immediately: Confirm whether your organisation is registered under the PWM Rules as a producer, importer, or brand owner — and check whether you qualify as a 'multi-state operator' requiring CPCB-level (rather than SPCB-level) registration.
