If not registered, initiate the process without delay as non-compliance carries enforcement risk Map your plastic packaging footprint using the new definitions: Conduct an internal classification exercise using the newly defined categories — plastic packaging, post-consumer waste, pre-consumer waste, and recyclate use — to accurately quantify your EPR targets under Schedule II and prepare for CPCB reporting cycles Review product claims on biodegradable and compostable plastics: If your packaging carries biodegradable or compostable claims, verify that these materials are CPCB-certified and conform to IS/ISO 17088:2021 (not the older IS 17088:2008 standard); non-conforming claims now carry heightened legal exposure Engage your plastic waste processing partners: Verify that your recyclers, co-processors, and waste-to-energy partners meet the definitions and regulatory requirements under the amended rules; update contracts and due diligence checklists to reflect the new legal categories, and obtain documentation of their own registrations Build EPR data infrastructure for climate disclosure alignment: Begin tracking plastic packaging volumes, recycled content use, and waste recovery rates in a manner consistent with both CPCB reporting requirements and emerging global disclosure standards (ISSB IFRS S2, CSRD, Global Plastics Treaty obligations); companies that integrate EPR data into their sustainability reporting now will avoid costly retrofitting as disclosure mandates tighten

Our Take The Second Amendment to India's Plastic Waste Management Rules is, in substance, a regulatory maturation moment. The deletion of the two-year collection plan window and the explicit restatement of EPR as a mandatory obligation under Schedule II sends an unambiguous signal: the CPCB and MoEFCC are moving from framework-building to enforcement. The new definitional architecture — particularly the distinctions between biodegradable and compostable plastics, pre- and post-consumer waste, and the formal recognition of waste-to-energy and recycled content use — gives regulators the precise legal tools they need to hold businesses accountable in ways that were previously difficult to operationalise. For Indian companies with significant plastic packaging footprints, the window for a 'wait and watch' approach has firmly closed. More significantly, businesses should anticipate that these rules are a stepping stone: India is an active participant in the UN Global Plastics Treaty negotiations, and domestic rules will need to evolve to reflect whatever binding international obligations emerge — likely including mandatory recycled content targets, design-for-recyclability standards, and extended producer liability for transboundary plastic pollution. Companies that build adaptive, data-driven EPR compliance systems now will find themselves structurally ahead when the next wave of tightening arrives.

title: India's EPR Framework for Plastic Packaging: What Producers, Importers, and Brand Owners Must Do Now
summary: India's Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) has embedded legally binding Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) guidelines for plastic packaging directly into the Plastic Waste Management (Amendment) Rules, 2022, effective February 16, 2022. Producers, importers, and brand ow

sections.t: Executive Summary India's Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) has embedded legally binding Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) guidelines for plastic packaging directly into the Plastic Waste Management (Amendment) Rules, 2022, effective February 16, 2022. Producers, importers, and brand owners are now required to collect, channel, and ensure the recycling or end-of-life processing of both pre-consumer and post-consumer plastic packaging waste — with specific roles, targets, and accountability mechanisms enforced by the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB). Non-compliance carries significant regulatory, reputational, and supply-chain risk, particularly as global ESG disclosure standards increasingly demand transparency on plastic footprints.

What Changed Prior to this amendment, EPR obligations for plastic packaging under the Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016 were governed by guidelines issued on an ad hoc basis — meaning the rules could shift without formal gazette notification, creating ambiguity for businesses. The 2022 amendment changes this fundamentally: the EPR guidelines are now codified as Schedule II of the PWM Rules, giving them the full force of law. In plain terms, this means that any company that manufactures, imports, or sells products using plastic packaging — from carry bags to multi-layer packaging to plastic sheets — can no longer treat EPR as a voluntary or loosely monitored obligation. The guidelines define precisely who is responsible (producers, importers, brand owners), what they must do (collect and channel pre- and post-consumer plastic packaging waste for recycling or end-of-life processing), and which authority oversees compliance (CPCB at the national level, State Pollution Control Boards and Pollution Control Committees at the state level). The amendment also introduces critical definitions that determine who falls within scope. 'Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR)' is defined as a producer's responsibility for environmentally sound management of a product until its end of life. 'Brand Owner' covers any individual or company selling commodities under a registered brand, label, or trademark. 'Importer' includes anyone importing plastic packaging products, carry bags, multilayer packaging, or plastic sheets. Together, these definitions cast a wide net across India's manufacturing and consumer goods ecosystem.

Who Is Affected Producers: Manufacturers of carry bags, multilayer packaging, plastic sheets, and similar products — including industries that use plastic sheets or multilayer packaging to wrap or package goods for sale. Importers: Any entity importing plastic packaging products, carry bags, multilayer packaging, plastic sheets, or similar materials into India. Brand Owners: Any individual or company that sells commodities under a registered brand, label, or trademark — this includes FMCG, food & beverage, pharmaceuticals, personal care, e-commerce, and retail companies whose products reach consumers in plastic packaging. Recyclers and Plastic Waste Processors: Entities engaged in recycling plastic waste or converting it into energy (waste-to-energy), oil (waste-to-oil), or industrial composting — they are assigned specific roles in the EPR ecosystem. Sectors with highest exposure: Fast-Moving Consumer Goods (FMCG), food processing, beverages, pharmaceuticals, personal care and cosmetics, e-commerce and logistics, agriculture (plastic mulch films), and retail. Company size: The rules apply regardless of company size — small manufacturers and importers are not exempt, though compliance pathways (such as Producer Responsibility Organisations, or PROs) can ease the burden for smaller entities.

Key Obligations & Deadlines Immediate effect (February 16, 2022): The EPR guidelines under Schedule II came into force on the date of gazette notification. All ongoing EPR processes must be aligned with these guidelines without delay. EPR Registration: Producers, importers, and brand owners must register on the CPCB's centralised EPR portal to obtain EPR certificates. Operating without registration exposes companies to enforcement action. Collection and Channelisation Targets: Entities must meet prescribed annual targets for collection and channelisation of plastic packaging waste — both pre-consumer waste (manufacturing rejects and trimmings) and post-consumer waste (packaging discarded by end consumers). Targets are category-specific and increase progressively year on year. Plastic Packaging Categories: Companies must classify their plastic packaging under the prescribed categories (e.g., Category I: rigid plastic packaging; Category II: flexible plastic packaging including carry bags and multilayer packaging; Category III: multilayer plastic; Category IV: plastic sheets and like products) as targets and obligations vary by category. Use of Recycled Plastic Content: Companies are required to progressively incorporate recycled plastic content into their packaging as mandated — this is a supply-side obligation that requires early engagement with certified recyclers. EPR Certificate Procurement: Where companies cannot meet collection targets through their own systems, they must procure EPR certificates from registered recyclers or plastic waste processors through the CPCB portal — this is the compliance offset mechanism. Annual Reporting: Entities must file annual returns with CPCB/SPCBs detailing plastic packaging placed on the market, waste collected, recycled, and processed, along with EPR certificates held. End-of-Life Processing: Plastic waste that cannot be recycled must be directed to approved end-of-life processing routes — waste-to-energy, co-processing in cement kilns, waste-to-oil, or road construction as per Indian Roads Congress guidelines. Open burning and landfilling are not compliant disposal routes. Non-compliance: Failure to meet EPR targets or register attracts penalties under the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986, including fines, production stoppages, and public disclosure of non-compliance by CPCB.

Climate Transition Angle India's EPR framework for plastic packaging is not just a waste management regulation — it sits at the intersection of the circular economy, supply chain decarbonisation, and global climate disclosure requirements. From a physical risk perspective, plastic pollution degrades ecosystems, contaminates water bodies, and contributes to microplastic accumulation in soil and water — risks that directly impair agricultural productivity and water security, both of which are material climate adaptation concerns for India. By mandating collection and recycling, the EPR rules reduce the volume of plastic entering natural systems. On the transition risk and opportunity side, the shift to recycled plastic content in packaging aligns directly with the global push for circular economy principles embedded in frameworks like the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and the ISSB's IFRS S2 climate disclosure standard. Multinational brand owners sourcing from or selling into India are already subject to supply chain due diligence requirements that include plastic footprint disclosure. India's EPR rules create the domestic infrastructure — registered recyclers, certified EPR certificates, traceable collection chains — that makes this disclosure credible and auditable. For investors, EPR compliance is increasingly a proxy for broader ESG governance quality. Companies that build robust EPR systems now are better positioned to respond to Scope 3 value chain disclosures, ESG ratings scrutiny, and export market sustainability requirements — particularly as the EU's Green Deal tightens import standards on packaging sustainability. The SEBI Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report (BRSR) framework already requires India's top listed companies to disclose plastic waste management practices, making EPR compliance a direct input to mandatory ESG reporting.

What To Do Now Conduct a plastic packaging audit immediately: Map every SKU and product line to identify the type, category, and volume of plastic packaging placed on the market annually — this baseline is the foundation of your EPR target calculation and BRSR disclosure. Register on the CPCB EPR portal without delay: If your company has not already registered as a producer, importer, or brand owner, initiate registration now. Retroactive compliance gaps increase regulatory exposure, especially as CPCB ramps up enforcement. Engage certified recyclers and PROs: Build or formalise relationships with CPCB-registered recyclers or Producer Responsibility Organisations (PROs) who can help meet collection targets and supply EPR certificates. Do not wait until year-end — recycler capacity is finite and early movers secure better terms. Embed recycled content targets into procurement and packaging design: Work with packaging R&D and procurement teams to incorporate recycled plastic content into future packaging specifications. This is both a regulatory requirement and a competitive differentiator for export markets and sustainability-conscious buyers. Integrate EPR obligations into BRSR and ESG disclosures: Align your EPR data — tonnes of plastic placed on market, collected, recycled, EPR certificates held — with your SEBI BRSR reporting and any voluntary ISSB/GRI disclosures. Investors and ESG raters are increasingly scrutinising this data for accuracy and completeness.

Our Take The codification of EPR guidelines into Schedule II of the Plastic Waste Management Rules is a watershed moment for India's plastic governance landscape. By elevating what were previously administrative guidelines to statutory rules, the government has removed ambiguity and significantly raised the compliance bar. What this signals clearly is that India is moving from a framework of stated intent to one of measurable accountability — with CPCB's centralised digital portal creating a real-time audit trail that makes enforcement far more credible than it was under the pre-2022 regime. For business leaders, the message is unambiguous: EPR is no longer a checkbox exercise managed by the environment team in isolation. It demands cross-functional coordination across procurement, packaging design, supply chain, finance, and ESG reporting. Companies that invest now in building traceable, certified EPR systems will not only stay ahead of domestic enforcement but will also be better prepared for the next wave of obligations — which is likely to include category-specific recycled content mandates, EPR for categories beyond packaging, and integration with India's broader green taxonomy and sustainable finance frameworks. The question is not whether to comply, but how quickly and how strategically you build the capability to do so.

title: India's Fossil Fuel Import Trap: Why the Middle East Crisis Is a BRSR Materiality Event, Not Just an Energy Policy Story
summary: Climate Action Tracker's analysis by Dr. Nandini Das — India country lead — lays out a structural indictment of India's energy architecture: 85–90% crude oil import dependence, 45–50% fossil gas import exposure, and over 240 million tonnes of annual coal imports sit at the heart of every macro shock

sections.t: Executive Summary Climate Action Tracker's analysis by Dr. Nandini Das — India country lead — lays out a structural indictment of India's energy architecture: 85–90% crude oil import dependence, 45–50% fossil gas import exposure, and over 240 million tonnes of annual coal imports sit at the heart of every macro shock India has absorbed since 2003. For Indian CFOs and CSOs, this is not geopolitical noise — it is a BRSR Core materiality disclosure obligation, an ISSB IFRS S2 physical and transition risk event, and a direct threat to operating cost predictability across energy-intensive sectors. The renewable acceleration underway is real, but the simultaneous expansion of gas infrastructure risks locking India into a second fossil dependency trap — one that is arguably harder to exit than coal. The full analysis is available at: https://climateactiontracker.org/blog/breaking-the-cycle-of-energy-shocks-indias-renewable-opportunity/

What Changed Reporting by Climate Action Tracker (CAT), published at https://climateactiontracker.org/blog/breaking-the-cycle-of-energy-shocks-indias-renewable-opportunity/, frames the current Middle East crisis — which is disrupting approximately 20% of global energy supply — not as a temporary geopolitical disruption but as the latest iteration of a structurally recurring vulnerability in India's energy system. CAT's Dr. Nandini Das documents that fossil fuels constitute ~75% of India's primary energy, with import dependence spanning all three major fuels: crude oil (85–90% imported), fossil gas (45–50% imported), and high-grade coal (significant import share on top of one billion tonnes of domestic production). The analytical shift here is important: CAT is characterising coal's expanded utilisation not as a transition-era compromise but as a 'shock absorber' that provides short-term resilience while entrenching long-term vulnerability — a framing that has direct implications for how Indian companies classify and disclose energy-related financial risks. For Indian listed companies, this reframing has immediate regulatory consequence. SEBI's BRSR Core framework — mandatory for the top 150 listed companies by market capitalisation from FY2023-24 and extending to the top 1,000 from FY2024-25 — requires third-party assured disclosure on energy intensity, Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions, and transition risks. If imported fossil fuel price volatility constitutes a reasonably foreseeable material financial risk — which CAT's data strongly supports — then CFOs and audit committees of energy-intensive companies cannot treat it as a footnote disclosure. Under ISSB IFRS S2, which SEBI is progressively aligning BRSR toward, this class of risk requires scenario analysis, quantified financial impact ranges, and governance-level acknowledgment. The coexistence CAT identifies — accelerating renewable deployment alongside new gas infrastructure expansion — creates a specific stranded asset disclosure risk that Indian power sector companies, fertiliser manufacturers, and gas distribution companies must now assess explicitly. The policy contradiction CAT highlights — using emergency clauses to run imported-coal plants at full capacity while simultaneously fast-tracking wind and battery storage clearances — signals that India's energy transition is bifurcating into a public-sector infrastructure dilemma and a private-sector investment opportunity. For institutional investors with exposure to Indian power utilities, city gas distribution (CGD) networks, and LNG terminal developers, the CAT analysis constitutes new material information about the long-term asset life and revenue certainty of gas infrastructure investments.

Who Is Affected Power generation companies — thermal (coal and gas), IPPs, and state DISCOMs — facing stranded asset risk from long-life fossil fuel infrastructure as renewables achieve cost parity Energy-intensive industrials: steel (coking coal imports), cement, aluminium, chemicals, and fertilisers whose input cost structures are directly exposed to imported fossil fuel price volatility City Gas Distribution (CGD) companies and LNG terminal developers whose capital expenditure pipelines assume gas as a durable transition fuel — a thesis CAT explicitly challenges Top 1,000 BSE/NSE-listed companies subject to BRSR Core assured disclosures, particularly those with Scope 1 emissions from fossil fuel combustion or Scope 3 exposure through energy procurement CFOs and Audit Committees responsible for BRSR Core third-party assurance sign-off and materiality determination under SEBI's LODR framework Chief Sustainability Officers at companies with SBTi commitments or net zero targets who must now reconcile India's energy system trajectory with their own Scope 2 and Scope 3 decarbonisation pathways ESG-focused FIIs, domestic mutual funds with ESG mandates, and green bond investors holding exposure to Indian energy infrastructure — particularly those applying ISSB IFRS S2 or EU SFDR Article 8/9 screening criteria Export-oriented manufacturers supplying EU customers subject to CBAM (Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism) — where grid emission intensity driven by coal dependence directly affects CBAM liability calculations from 2026

Key Obligations & Deadlines BRSR Core — Top 150 listed companies (by market cap): Third-party assured ESG disclosures including energy intensity and GHG emissions mandatory from FY2023-24 annual reports (filing deadline typically September 2024 for FY24); top 1,000 companies phased in by FY2026-27 SEBI LODR Regulation 34(2)(f): Annual report must include BRSR with 'Leadership Indicators' covering transition risk assessment — fossil fuel import exposure now meets the threshold of a reasonably foreseeable material risk requiring named disclosure ISSB IFRS S2 (Climate-Related Disclosures): For companies voluntarily adopting or preparing for ISSB alignment (which SEBI's IOSCO membership signals as the convergence direction), physical risk (energy supply disruption) and transition risk (stranded fossil assets) must be quantified under at least two climate scenarios EU CBAM — Transitional phase data reporting: Indian exporters of steel, aluminium, cement, fertilisers, and electricity must report embedded carbon emissions quarterly from October 2023; financial CBAM liability commences January 2026 — grid emission intensity from coal-heavy dispatch directly increases CBAM cost exposure PAT Scheme Cycle VII (BEE): Designated consumers in power, steel, cement, aluminium, and other sectors must meet specific energy consumption targets — coal-heavy operations with low efficiency face PAT penalty risk as BEE tightens norms in line with India's NDC commitments SBTi Corporate Net Zero Standard: Companies with validated SBTi targets must demonstrate Scope 2 market-based emission reductions — in India's current grid, this requires active renewable energy procurement (PPAs, RECs) rather than reliance on grid average factors that embed coal-heavy dispatch RBI's Sustainable Finance Framework and Climate Risk Circular (2023): Banks and NBFCs must assess transition and physical climate risk in credit portfolios — fossil fuel-dependent borrowers in energy-intensive sectors face rising probability-weighted credit risk reclassification

Climate Transition Angle India's 2022 updated NDC commits to 50% non-fossil installed electricity capacity by 2030 and a 45% reduction in GDP emissions intensity versus 2005 levels. The National Electricity Plan 2023 projects 500 GW of non-fossil capacity by 2030. These are Paris Agreement-aligned anchors. But CAT's analysis exposes the gap between installed capacity targets and actual energy system transformation: adding renewable capacity while simultaneously running coal plants at higher capacity factors and expanding gas infrastructure does not reduce fossil fuel financial exposure — it layers new clean capacity on top of an unreformed fossil base, with the economic and carbon consequences determined by dispatch merit order, not installed megawatts. For Indian companies with BRSR Core or ISSB IFRS S2 disclosure obligations, this gap is the central transition risk. Under IFRS S2's scenario analysis requirement, companies must assess their financial position under a below-2°C scenario (where coal assets face accelerated retirement and carbon pricing escalates) and a 'current policies' scenario (where continued fossil dependence results in sustained import cost volatility and inflation pass-through). The CAT data — 240 million tonnes of annual coal imports, 85–90% crude oil import dependence — provides the quantitative baseline for that scenario analysis. Companies that do not perform this analysis face not just regulatory exposure under BRSR but credibility risk with ISSB-aligned institutional investors who are increasingly applying IFRS S2 screens to emerging market portfolios. The stranded asset dimension is particularly acute for gas infrastructure. CAT explicitly identifies the lock-in risk of capital-intensive gas pipelines, LNG terminals, and CGD networks. Under TNFD's nature-related financial disclosure framework — which India's large infrastructure developers and lenders are beginning to engage with — long-lived fossil infrastructure in climate-vulnerable geographies also carries physical risk from demand destruction as the energy transition accelerates. For Indian companies investing in gas infrastructure today with a 25–30-year asset life assumption, the ISSB S2 scenario analysis will need to model a credible early retirement scenario — and that has direct implications for impairment testing under Ind AS 36.

What To Do Now CFO / Finance teams (immediate — next 90 days): Commission a quantified energy import cost sensitivity analysis mapping your company's P&L exposure to a 20–30% fossil fuel price shock scenario; this analysis feeds directly into BRSR Core 'Leadership Indicators' on transition risk and satisfies the materiality threshold for SEBI LODR disclosure CSO / Sustainability teams (FY2024-25 reporting cycle): Map your Scope 2 emissions using both location-based (grid average) and market-based (contracted renewable energy) methodologies per GHG Protocol; if your company lacks active renewable PPAs or REC procurement, your BRSR and ISSB-aligned disclosure will show structurally high Scope 2 intensity — begin renewable PPA tendering or REC procurement to address this before the next assured BRSR filing Board Risk Committee (next board cycle): Add 'fossil fuel import price volatility' as a named principal risk in the risk register with quantified financial impact range; this is now a BRSR Core material topic and an ISSB IFRS S2 transition risk — failure to name it creates audit committee liability on BRSR assurance sign-off Capital expenditure and strategy teams (12-month horizon): Apply ISSB S2-aligned scenario analysis to any proposed investment in gas infrastructure (pipelines, LNG, CGD networks) — model asset life under a below-2°C trajectory and stress-test against a 2035 early retirement scenario; present findings to board before capital allocation approval Export-oriented manufacturers (immediate): Calculate your EU CBAM embedded carbon liability using India's current grid emission factor (approximately 0.71 kgCO2/kWh per CEA 2023 data) and stress-test against a higher factor scenario if coal dispatch intensity increases; begin tracking embedded emissions at product level to comply with CBAM reporting obligations that commenced October 2023 Investor relations teams (next quarterly investor update): Proactively brief FII and ESG-mandated investors on your company's energy supply risk management strategy, renewable procurement roadmap, and BRSR transition risk disclosures — do not wait for a proxy season challenge or an ESG rating downgrade to trigger this conversation

Our Take CAT's analysis should be read by Indian boards not as a climate advocacy document but as a financial risk map. The three-decade pattern of recurring fossil fuel price shocks hitting India's economy is now intersecting with a regulatory disclosure architecture — BRSR Core, ISSB IFRS S2, EU CBAM — that requires companies to quantify and govern exactly this class of risk. The window in which 'fossil fuel price volatility' could be treated as a generic macroeconomic disclaimer in an annual report is closing. Within 24 months, we expect SEBI to mandate scenario-based transition risk quantification in BRSR Core filings, likely drawing on ISSB IFRS S2 methodology, as part of India's commitments under its IOSCO membership and the G20 Sustainable Finance Working Group agenda. Companies that have not built this analytical capability internally will face a compressed, expensive scramble to achieve assurance-ready disclosures. The gas infrastructure question is the sleeper risk that Indian boards and infrastructure investors are underpricing. The political logic of gas-as-transition-fuel is seductive, but CAT's data on import dependence (45–50% of fossil gas imported) combined with Middle East supply disruption risk creates a scenario where India's gas infrastructure buildout — estimated at over USD 60 billion in pipeline and terminal investments planned through 2030 — faces dual obsolescence risk: geopolitical supply disruption in the near term and demand destruction from renewable-plus-storage cost curves in the medium term. Any Indian company or PE fund with capital in CGD networks, LNG terminals, or gas-to-power assets needs an honest ISSB S2-style scenario analysis on those assets before the next capital raise or refinancing. The risk is no longer hypothetical — it is the central question any sophisticated infrastructure debt or equity investor will ask. India's renewable acceleration is real, and the policy signals — fast-tracked wind and battery storage clearances even during the current energy crisis — show that the government understands the structural argument. But the transition will only de-risk Indian companies if it is accompanied by a parallel shift in how boards, CFOs, and investors account for the stranded asset exposure in the existing fossil infrastructure base. The companies that will win the next decade are those that treat energy transition not as a CSR narrative but as a balance sheet restructuring exercise — retiring fossil exposure, locking in long-term renewable PPAs, and building the disclosure infrastructure to prove it to international capital markets. BRSR Core and ISSB S2 are the instruments that will separate the leaders from the laggards, and enforcement is closer than most Indian boards currently assume.

title: India's Fossil Fuel Import Trap: Why the Middle East Energy Shock Is a Capital Allocation Wake-Up Call for Indian Boards
summary: India's structural dependence on imported fossil fuels — 85–90% of crude oil, 45–50% of gas, and over 240 million tonnes of imported coal annually — is no longer a geopolitical footnote but a balance sheet risk that Indian CFOs and board risk committees must price into long-term capital planning. Th

sections.t: Executive Summary India's structural dependence on imported fossil fuels — 85–90% of crude oil, 45–50% of gas, and over 240 million tonnes of imported coal annually — is no longer a geopolitical footnote but a balance sheet risk that Indian CFOs and board risk committees must price into long-term capital planning. The Middle East crisis, as documented by Climate Action Tracker (CAT) in its analysis 'Breaking the cycle of energy shocks: India's renewable opportunity' (https://climateactiontracker.org/blog/breaking-the-cycle-of-energy-shocks-indias-renewable-opportunity/), exposes a systemic contradiction: India is simultaneously accelerating renewable energy deployment and doubling down on imported coal and gas infrastructure, creating a stranded asset liability that will surface in BRSR Core disclosures and ISSB S2-aligned climate risk reporting within the next 24 months. For listed Indian companies — particularly energy-intensive sectors under BEE's PAT scheme, exporters facing EU CBAM scrutiny, and companies with international institutional investors applying ISSB or TCFD lenses — this is not an abstract energy policy debate. It is a live, quantifiable financial risk that is heading directly toward mandatory disclosure.

What Changed Reporting by Dr. Nandini Das, CAT's India country lead, published at https://climateactiontracker.org/blog/breaking-the-cycle-of-energy-shocks-indias-renewable-opportunity/, documents what three decades of energy shocks have repeatedly confirmed: India's energy system vulnerability is structural, not cyclical. The current Middle East disruption — affecting approximately 20% of global energy supply — has triggered two simultaneous and contradictory Indian policy responses: emergency clearances for wind and battery storage acceleration, and the invocation of emergency clauses to run imported-coal plants at full capacity. This is not a temporary tactical tension; it represents a fundamental strategic incoherence in India's energy transition pathway. What has materially changed is the speed at which these contradictions are becoming financially visible. India's coal import bill and crude oil import dependency create a persistent current account vulnerability — each oil price spike of USD 10/barrel adds approximately USD 12–15 billion to India's annual import bill, directly affecting the INR, inflation, and fiscal headroom. For Indian corporates, this translates into input cost volatility, working capital stress, and — critically — scope 2 and scope 3 emissions exposure that is now subject to mandatory disclosure under SEBI's BRSR Core framework for the top 150 listed companies (FY2023-24) and will extend to the top 1,000 listed companies by FY2026-27. The CAT analysis makes explicit what Indian boards have been slow to acknowledge: expanding gas infrastructure — LNG terminals, city gas distribution networks, cross-country pipelines — risks locking India into a second fossil fuel dependency trap at precisely the moment when solar and wind are cost-competitive and storage technology is scaling rapidly. The India-specific regulatory consequence is sharp. Under SEBI's BRSR Core (mandated for top 150 listed entities from FY2023-24), companies must now disclose Scope 1, 2, and 3 GHG emissions with reasonable assurance, and energy intensity metrics tied to their business activity. For companies in sectors such as steel, cement, aluminium, petrochemicals, and power — all of which sit within BEE's PAT Scheme Cycle III — exposure to imported fossil fuel price volatility is not just an operational risk; it is a disclosed, auditable financial risk. Simultaneously, Indian exporters to the EU face the Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM), which is now in its transitional reporting phase (October 2023–December 2025) and enters full financial liability from January 2026, with embedded carbon costs in steel, aluminium, cement, fertilisers, and hydrogen exports directly affecting trade competitiveness.

Who Is Affected Energy-intensive industrial sectors under BEE PAT Scheme Cycle III: steel, cement, aluminium, chlor-alkali, pulp and paper, textiles, fertilisers — all face rising input cost volatility from imported coal and gas price shocks Top 150 and progressively top 1,000 BSE/NSE-listed companies subject to SEBI BRSR Core mandatory GHG and energy disclosures — fossil fuel exposure is now an auditable line item Indian exporters to the EU in CBAM-covered sectors (steel, aluminium, cement, fertilisers, hydrogen) — embedded carbon from coal-based power and process heat is a direct financial liability from January 2026 CFOs and Treasury heads at energy-intensive corporates: fuel import cost volatility is a working capital and hedging strategy issue requiring board-level risk quantification Chief Sustainability Officers and ESG heads at listed companies: must now map fossil fuel dependency into BRSR Core KPIs, and anticipate ISSB IFRS S2 scenario analysis disclosure requirements Board Risk Committees: stranded asset risk from long-lived coal and gas infrastructure investments (20–30 year asset life vs. accelerating energy transition) needs explicit treatment in risk registers Institutional investors — domestic (LIC, SBI MF, HDFC AMC) and foreign (GIC, Norges, BlackRock) — applying TCFD or ISSB lenses to Indian energy and industrial sector holdings Project finance lenders and infrastructure funds exposed to Indian coal-based power or gas distribution capex — stranded asset timelines are compressing as renewables + storage cost curves fall

Key Obligations & Deadlines SEBI BRSR Core — Scope 1, 2, and 3 GHG emissions with reasonable assurance: mandatory for top 150 listed entities from FY2023-24 (reporting due in annual reports filed by September 2024); extends to top 1,000 listed entities by FY2026-27 BEE PAT Scheme Cycle III — energy consumption and intensity reduction targets for designated consumers (DCs) across 13 industrial sectors: compliance and trading period active; DCs must meet specific energy consumption targets or purchase Energy Saving Certificates (ESCerts) EU CBAM transitional phase — Indian exporters in covered sectors must submit quarterly embedded carbon reports to EU importers: reporting obligation active from October 2023 through December 2025; full financial carbon cost liability from January 1, 2026 ISSB IFRS S2 Climate Disclosures — scenario analysis and climate-related financial risk disclosure: adopted by IOSCO; SEBI is expected to align BRSR evolution with ISSB S1/S2 architecture — Indian listed companies should treat scenario analysis as a near-term disclosure expectation, likely mandatory within 2–3 years MoEFCC Environment Impact Assessment (EIA) Notification — any new coal or gas infrastructure requiring environmental clearance must demonstrate alignment with India's NDC pathways; boards approving such capex face increasing regulatory and investor scrutiny India's Updated NDC (2022) — 500 GW non-fossil capacity by 2030, 50% electricity from non-fossil sources by 2030: all corporate energy procurement strategies and PPAs must be stress-tested against this policy trajectory to assess stranded asset risk in existing fossil fuel offtake contracts

Climate Transition Angle The CAT analysis crystallises a transition risk that Indian boards have been underpricing: the physical and transition risk vectors are now converging simultaneously. On the physical side, Middle East supply disruptions — which CAT estimates are affecting ~20% of global energy supply — directly raise India's energy import costs and inflation, a macroeconomic transmission mechanism that ultimately affects corporate earnings, consumer demand, and sovereign fiscal capacity. On the transition risk side, India's accelerating renewable buildout (solar module prices have fallen over 90% in a decade; utility-scale solar is now the cheapest form of new electricity generation in India at INR 2–3/kWh) is rapidly making new coal and gas assets economically non-competitive, even before carbon pricing mechanisms fully activate. For Indian companies, the BRSR Core's Scope 3 disclosure requirement is particularly consequential in this context. Supply chain emissions — Category 1 (purchased goods and services) and Category 4 (upstream transportation) — are directly exposed to fossil fuel import price volatility and embedded carbon costs. A steel company using imported coking coal, or a cement company running captive coal-fired power, is carrying both a financial risk (fuel cost volatility) and a disclosure risk (rising Scope 1 and 2 emissions intensity) that will increasingly affect access to green finance, export market eligibility, and ESG ratings. The SBTi's requirement that corporate net-zero targets cover Scope 1, 2, and material Scope 3 emissions means that companies with heavy fossil fuel dependency in their value chains face a structural barrier to credible target-setting — which in turn affects their eligibility for green bonds, sustainability-linked loans, and institutional ESG mandates. India's carbon market, under development via the Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) notified by MoEFCC in June 2023, will over time put an explicit price on this embedded fossil fuel dependency, further compressing the economics of coal and gas-intensive operations. The renewable energy acceleration pathway — if executed with storage integration, grid flexibility, and demand-side management — represents India's most credible hedge against both physical energy security risk and transition-related capital market risk. For corporate energy buyers, this translates into a clear strategic imperative: accelerate long-term renewable PPAs, invest in captive solar and wind, and where feasible, pursue green hydrogen for hard-to-abate process heat. These are not just ESG commitments — they are balance sheet risk management decisions that CFOs should be driving alongside CSOs.

What To Do Now CFO / Treasury: Within Q3 FY2025, commission a quantitative stress test mapping the company's direct and indirect fossil fuel import cost exposure against three oil/gas price scenarios (base, +30%, +60%) — this feeds directly into BRSR Core's financial risk disclosure and ISSB S2 scenario analysis expectations CSO / Sustainability team: Map all energy inputs — grid electricity, captive generation, process fuels — against BRSR Core Scope 1/2/3 boundaries by December 2024; identify which Scope 3 categories carry fossil fuel import price risk and disclose accordingly in the FY2025 annual report Board Risk Committee: Add 'fossil fuel import dependency and stranded asset risk' as a named line item in the enterprise risk register, with quantified financial exposure and a transition timeline; link this to the company's capital allocation review for any planned coal or gas infrastructure investments over a 5-year horizon Strategy / Capex Committee: Any new capex proposals involving coal-based power, gas distribution, or LNG offtake contracts must include a stranded asset assessment against India's 2030 NDC targets and IEA's Net Zero 2050 scenario — board approval should require explicit sign-off on transition risk Procurement / Supply Chain: For EU-facing exporters, initiate CBAM embedded carbon calculation for all exported products in covered categories immediately — the transitional reporting period is live, and errors or gaps will create compliance liability from January 2026 Investor Relations: Proactively brief top 10–15 institutional investors (both domestic and FII) on the company's fossil fuel transition strategy, renewable energy procurement roadmap, and BRSR Core GHG disclosure trajectory — investors applying ISSB or TCFD frameworks are asking these questions now, and silence is being read as unpreparedness Legal / Compliance: Review all long-term fossil fuel offtake contracts and PPAs for force majeure, price re-opener, and termination provisions in light of accelerating energy transition policy; escalate any contracts with tenors beyond 2030 to board risk committee for stress-testing

Our Take The CAT analysis by Dr. Nandini Das is a rigorous, data-grounded articulation of what India's most sophisticated energy economists have been saying privately for years: India's energy transition is not a climate policy choice — it is an economic and national security imperative. What makes the current moment strategically different from previous energy shock cycles is the simultaneous maturation of three forces that did not exist together before: renewable energy is now cheaper than new fossil fuel capacity in India; mandatory ESG disclosure frameworks (BRSR Core, EU CBAM, incoming ISSB alignment) are making fossil fuel dependency financially legible to capital markets; and India's carbon market architecture (CCTS + PAT scheme convergence) is beginning to put a price signal on carbon intensity for the first time. For boards and institutional investors, the 12–36 month outlook is unambiguous: the cost of inaction on fossil fuel transition is rising faster than the cost of transition itself. We expect SEBI to progressively align BRSR with ISSB S1 and S2 over the next two annual reporting cycles, which will introduce mandatory climate scenario analysis for listed companies — a disclosure that will make stranded asset risk in coal and gas infrastructure directly visible to markets. We also expect MoEFCC's CCTS to begin its first compliance cycle for obligated entities by FY2026-27, creating an explicit carbon cost that will further erode the economics of coal-intensive operations. Companies that treat this as a compliance exercise will be perpetually behind; companies that treat it as a capital reallocation signal — accelerating renewable procurement, retiring or repurposing fossil fuel assets, and embedding carbon pricing into investment hurdle rates — will build a structural competitive advantage in export markets, green finance access, and institutional investor engagement. The renewable opportunity that CAT identifies is real. The question for Indian boards is whether they will capture it proactively or be forced into it reactively, at higher cost and with greater reputational and regulatory exposure.

title: India's INDC at 10: What the 2015 Climate Pledge Means for Indian Business in 2025 and Beyond
summary: In October 2015, India formally submitted its Intended Nationally Determined Contributions (INDC) to the UNFCCC ahead of the Paris COP21, committing to three landmark targets by 2030: reducing the emissions intensity of GDP by 33–35% from 2005 levels, achieving 40% of installed electricity capacity

sections.t: Executive Summary In October 2015, India formally submitted its Intended Nationally Determined Contributions (INDC) to the UNFCCC ahead of the Paris COP21, committing to three landmark targets by 2030: reducing the emissions intensity of GDP by 33–35% from 2005 levels, achieving 40% of installed electricity capacity from non-fossil fuel sources, and creating an additional carbon sink of 2.5–3 billion tonnes of CO₂ equivalent through forest and tree cover. These commitments — rooted in India's philosophy of 'Climate Justice' — have since become the architectural blueprint for every major climate regulation, energy transition policy, and ESG disclosure requirement that Indian businesses face today. Understanding the INDC is not a matter of historical interest; it is essential context for every corporate sustainability strategy operating in India through 2030 and beyond.

What Changed The INDC framework emerged from a decade-long evolution in international climate diplomacy. The 1997 Kyoto Protocol placed binding emission reduction targets only on developed (Annex I) nations. Developing countries, including India, were not legally obligated to cut emissions. This began to shift at the 2007 Bali COP with the introduction of Nationally Appropriate Mitigation Actions (NAMAs) for developing nations, followed by voluntary pledges at Copenhagen (2009) and Cancun (2010). India's pre-2015 pledge — a 20–25% reduction in the emissions intensity of GDP by 2020 compared to 2005 levels — was voluntary and widely recognised as achievable. By 2014, UNEP's Emissions Gap Report confirmed India was on track. The 2013 Warsaw and 2014 Lima COPs introduced the INDC concept: all countries, developed and developing alike, would now prepare and submit nationally determined climate plans ahead of the Paris Agreement. Crucially, the Lima decision clarified that INDCs need not be limited to mitigation — they could encompass adaptation, finance needs, technology transfer, and capacity building. India's INDC, submitted on 2 October 2015 and presented at a press conference by the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC), embraced this broader framing. India's submission explicitly acknowledged the country's development imperatives — poverty eradication, housing for all, universal electricity access — while simultaneously scaling up climate ambition by 75% over its 2020 pledge. This balance between equity and ambition defines India's climate posture to this day. In plain terms: India told the world it would grow, and it would grow cleaner. Per capita emissions in 2030 were projected to remain well below the current average of developed nations (8.98 metric tonnes of CO₂ equivalent). India's share of historical global emissions since 1850 stood at just 3%, compared to 29% for the USA and 45% for other developed countries — a central argument for 'Climate Justice' as the moral framing of the INDC.

Who Is Affected Energy & Power Sector: Utilities, independent power producers, and energy-intensive industries are directly affected by the 40% non-fossil fuel installed capacity target — implying a fundamental restructuring of India's energy mix by 2030. Heavy Industry & Manufacturing: Steel, cement, chemicals, aluminium, and other high-emission sectors face structural pressure from the GDP emissions intensity target (33–35% reduction by 2030), which rewards energy efficiency and low-carbon production. Automotive & Transport: The push for a sustainable green transportation network and solarisation of fuel infrastructure signals long-term demand shifts away from fossil-fuel vehicles. Real Estate & Urban Infrastructure: Smart Cities Mission, Housing for All, and climate-resilient urban centre commitments translate into green building codes, energy performance standards, and low-carbon construction mandates. Agriculture & Land Use: The carbon sink target (2.5–3 billion tonnes CO₂ equivalent via forests and tree cover) and Green India Mission affect agribusinesses, plantation companies, and land-intensive industries operating near forest land. Financial Services & Investors: Banks, insurers, and institutional investors financing Indian infrastructure and industry are exposed to stranded asset risk in fossil fuel-heavy portfolios and transition opportunity in renewables. All Listed Companies (Top 1000 by market capitalisation): SEBI's Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting (BRSR) framework — a direct downstream consequence of INDC commitments — requires disclosure of emissions, energy use, and transition plans. Export-Oriented Businesses: Companies supplying to EU markets face the Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM), which is calibrated against exactly the kind of emissions intensity metrics India committed to reducing in its INDC.

Key Obligations & Deadlines INDC Target 1 — Emissions Intensity: Reduce the emissions intensity of GDP by 33–35% by 2030 compared to 2005 levels. (India's Updated NDC, submitted 2022, raised this to 45%.) Baseline year: 2005. Compliance horizon: December 2030. INDC Target 2 — Non-Fossil Fuel Capacity: Achieve 40% of cumulative electric power installed capacity from non-fossil fuel-based energy sources by 2030. (Updated NDC raised this to 50%.) Current status: India crossed 40% ahead of schedule in 2023. INDC Target 3 — Carbon Sink: Create an additional carbon sink of 2.5–3 billion tonnes of CO₂ equivalent through additional forest and tree cover by 2030. Implementation vehicles: Green India Mission, Green Highways Policy (1% of project cost earmarked for plantation), Namami Gange Mission riverside plantation. Renewable Energy Milestone — 175 GW by 2022: Wind (60 GW), Solar (100 GW), Biomass (10 GW), Small Hydel (5 GW). This was subsequently revised upward to 500 GW of non-fossil capacity by 2030 under the Updated NDC. BRSR Core Disclosure (SEBI, FY2023-24 onwards): Top 150 listed companies by market cap must provide assured BRSR Core disclosures, including Scope 1 and Scope 2 greenhouse gas emissions, energy consumption intensity, and water use — all directly traceable to INDC metrics. Energy Conservation (Amendment) Act, 2022: Introduces carbon trading, energy conservation standards for non-energy sector industries, and obligations for designated consumers — all operationalising the INDC's emissions intensity trajectory. Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS), 2023: MoEFCC/BEE framework establishing India's domestic carbon market, with Perform Achieve and Trade (PAT) scheme as the foundation. Obligated entities must meet specific energy consumption norms or purchase carbon credits. Forest Conservation (Amendment) Act, 2023: Redefines forest land and affects carbon sink obligations — companies with land banks or supply chains near forest areas must reassess exposure.

Climate Transition Angle India's INDC is the foundational document of the country's entire climate transition architecture. Every regulation, standard, and incentive that has followed — from the BRSR framework to the Carbon Credit Trading Scheme to the Production Linked Incentive for solar manufacturing — is an operational instrument designed to deliver on these three 2030 targets. For business leaders, this means the INDC is not a government document to file away; it is the strategic map of where Indian policy, infrastructure investment, and regulatory pressure are headed. From a physical risk perspective, the INDC itself acknowledged India's acute vulnerability: the country has 2.4% of global land area but 17.5% of the world's human population. Climate impacts — heatwaves, erratic monsoons, flooding, and water stress — threaten agricultural output, labour productivity, and infrastructure stability. The adaptation components of the INDC (climate-resilient urban centres, river cleaning, sustainable agriculture) signal that companies with operations, suppliers, or customers in climate-vulnerable geographies must build physical risk assessments into their enterprise risk management frameworks. On global alignment: India's INDC targets map directly onto the metrics required under the International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) IFRS S2 standard (climate-related disclosures) and the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD), both of which require companies to disclose against nationally and scientifically derived transition pathways. Indian subsidiaries of multinationals and Indian companies with EU market exposure must demonstrate alignment between their Scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions trajectories and India's NDC pathway. The EU's Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM), now in its transitional phase, will impose carbon costs on Indian exports of steel, aluminium, cement, fertilisers, and electricity from 2026 — priced precisely against the kind of emissions intensity India committed to reducing. Companies that do not accelerate their own decarbonisation risk paying a carbon tariff that their low-carbon competitors will avoid. The International Solar Alliance, anchored by India as envisioned in the INDC, has since grown into a 120-nation body — signalling that India's climate commitments are not merely domestic but are reshaping global energy trade and technology cooperation in ways that will create new market opportunities for Indian clean-tech and renewable energy companies.

What To Do Now Map your emissions baseline to 2005: The INDC uses 2005 as its reference year. Compliance teams and sustainability officers should establish or verify their company's own 2005 emissions intensity baseline (Scope 1 and 2 at minimum), enabling you to demonstrate alignment — or flag divergence — with India's national trajectory. This is also required for BRSR Core disclosures and will be essential for future climate scenario analysis under ISSB IFRS S2. Conduct a stranded asset and transition risk audit: Identify all capital expenditure in fossil fuel-dependent assets (coal-based captive power, fossil fuel fleet, high-emission process equipment) with economic life extending beyond 2030. Model these against India's 45% emissions intensity reduction target and a 1.5°C-aligned scenario. Assets that become economically unviable under the transition are stranded assets — and investors and lenders are increasingly pricing this risk. Accelerate renewable energy procurement: The 40% (now 50%) non-fossil capacity target has already been met at the national grid level, but corporate power purchase agreements (PPAs), open access solar, and green hydrogen sourcing strategies must be formalised now. Waiting until 2028–2029 will mean competing for limited renewable capacity in an already constrained market. Engage your energy procurement team to develop a five-year renewable transition roadmap. Engage your supply chain on forest and land-use risk: The carbon sink target and the Forest Conservation Amendment Act create regulatory and reputational exposure for any company sourcing from or operating near forest land. Commission a supply chain deforestation risk assessment, align with Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi) forest, land, and agriculture (FLAG) guidance, and begin engaging first-tier suppliers on land-use practices. Integrate INDC/NDC pathways into your climate disclosure strategy: Whether you are preparing BRSR Core reports, responding to CDP questionnaires, or preparing for ISSB-aligned disclosures, your transition plan must be benchmarked against India's Updated NDC (45% emissions intensity reduction, 50% non-fossil capacity by 2030, 500 GW renewable by 2030). Work with your sustainability and legal teams to ensure disclosures are consistent, defensible, and forward-looking — not merely retrospective compliance tick-boxes.

Our Take India's 2015 INDC was, at the time, a carefully calibrated act of climate statesmanship — ambitious enough to demonstrate global leadership, grounded enough in development realities to be politically defensible at home. A decade on, it has proven to be something more consequential: the architectural blueprint for an economy-wide energy and industrial transition that is now well underway. India has already exceeded its non-fossil capacity milestone ahead of schedule, and the Updated NDC of 2022 raised the bar further. What this means for Indian business is that the transition is not a future scenario to be modelled — it is a present-tense competitive reality. Companies that treat the INDC and its downstream regulations as compliance burdens will be perpetually reactive. Those that read the INDC as a strategic signal — a 15-year map of where infrastructure investment, policy incentives, and regulatory pressure are converging — will find it one of the most useful documents in their boardroom. The next inflection point is COP30 in Belém, Brazil (2025), where countries will submit new NDCs for the post-2030 period. India's next NDC will almost certainly be more ambitious still. The window to get ahead of the curve is now.

title: India's Plastic Waste Management (Amendment) Rules, 2021: What Businesses Must Do Before the Deadlines Hit
summary: India's Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change notified the Plastic Waste Management (Amendment) Rules, 2021 on 12 August 2021, introducing a phased ban on identified single-use plastic (SUP) items effective 1 July 2022, raising minimum thickness thresholds for plastic carry bags, and ex

sections.t: Executive Summary India's Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change notified the Plastic Waste Management (Amendment) Rules, 2021 on 12 August 2021, introducing a phased ban on identified single-use plastic (SUP) items effective 1 July 2022, raising minimum thickness thresholds for plastic carry bags, and expanding Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) obligations to brand owners and plastic waste processors. Companies across manufacturing, retail, food and beverage, FMCG, e-commerce, and packaging supply chains face hard legal deadlines — non-compliance carries significant regulatory and reputational risk. Acting now to audit plastic use, redesign packaging, and register under EPR frameworks is no longer optional; it is a board-level imperative.

What Changed The Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016 were originally notified to regulate the manufacture, use, collection, and recycling of plastic waste in India. The 2018 amendment tightened certain provisions, but the landmark 2021 amendment — drafted in March 2021 and finalised in August 2021 after public consultation — is the most sweeping revision to date. The amendment does four major things. First, it prohibits the manufacture, import, stocking, distribution, sale, and use of a defined list of single-use plastic items from 1 July 2022. The banned items include plastic sticks in ear buds, balloons, and flags; candy and ice-cream sticks; polystyrene (thermocol) decoration; cutlery such as plates, cups, glasses, forks, spoons, knives, straws, and trays; wrapping films around sweet boxes; PVC banners below 100 microns; and stirrers. Compostable plastic alternatives are explicitly exempted from some of these restrictions. Second, the rules raise the minimum thickness of plastic carry bags in two steps: to 75 microns from 30 September 2021, and further to 120 microns from 31 December 2022. Non-woven plastic carry bags must meet a minimum weight of 60 grams per square metre (GSM) from 30 September 2021. Third, the amendment broadens the scope of Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) to explicitly include brand owners and plastic waste processors (recyclers, co-processors) alongside manufacturers and importers. This means brand owners — including FMCG companies and retailers who use plastic packaging — now carry direct legal accountability for end-of-life plastic management, not just the manufacturer of the packaging material. Fourth, the rules add new definitions for key terms: 'single-use plastic item,' 'non-woven plastic bag,' 'plastic waste processing,' 'thermoplastic,' and 'thermoset plastic.' These definitions matter because they set the legal boundaries of what is regulated.

Who Is Affected FMCG and Consumer Goods Companies: Any brand owner using plastic packaging — pouches, wrappers, multi-layer packaging — now falls directly under EPR obligations. Food & Beverage Industry: Restaurants, quick-service chains, packaged food manufacturers, and beverage companies that use cups, straws, cutlery, stirrers, or thermocol packaging face the SUP ban directly. Retail and E-Commerce: Businesses using plastic carry bags, wrapping films, or SUP items in customer-facing or last-mile operations must transition their packaging and logistics. Plastic Manufacturers and Importers: Must comply with revised thickness and GSM standards for carry bags and are now explicitly named in the EPR framework. Plastic Waste Processors — Recyclers and Co-Processors: Newly brought within the regulatory ambit; must ensure compliance with amended rules and EPR guidelines. Advertising and Event Management: PVC banners below 100 microns are banned; industries reliant on large-format outdoor advertising must switch materials. Pharmaceutical and Healthcare Packaging: While primary pharma packaging has specific exemptions, secondary packaging using affected SUP items must be reviewed. Importers of Any Category: Import of banned SUP items is prohibited alongside domestic manufacture, making customs compliance and supply chain sourcing a critical issue.

Key Obligations & Deadlines 30 September 2021 (PAST — remediation required if not done): Plastic carry bags must be at minimum 75 microns thick. Non-woven plastic carry bags must weigh at least 60 GSM. Companies still using sub-standard bags are in violation and must rectify immediately. 1 July 2022 (PAST — compliance audit required): Complete ban on manufacture, import, stocking, distribution, sale, and use of all identified SUP items (ear bud sticks, balloon sticks, plastic flags, candy sticks, ice-cream sticks, thermocol decoration, plastic cutlery, plates, cups, glasses, straws, trays, wrapping films around sweet boxes, invitation cards with plastic wrapping, cigarette packets with plastic wrapping, PVC banners below 100 microns, and stirrers). Companies must audit all operations to confirm no banned items remain in the supply chain. 31 December 2022 (PAST — compliance verification required): Plastic carry bags must be at minimum 120 microns thick. All non-compliant inventory must have been phased out by this date. Ongoing — EPR Registration and Compliance: Brand owners must register with the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) and relevant State Pollution Control Boards under the EPR framework, submit annual returns on plastic packaging used and waste collected, and demonstrate fulfilment of EPR targets as notified by CPCB. Ongoing — Labelling Requirements: Manufacturers, producers, and brand owners must ensure plastic carry bags and packaging materials bear the required markings including producer name, registration number, and thickness/GSM details as prescribed. Ongoing — Plastic Waste Processors: Recyclers and co-processors must register under the amended rules and comply with guidelines issued by CPCB from time to time. 10-Year Sunset Clause: Any future notifications banning additional categories of carry bags, plastic sheets, covers made from plastic sheets, multi-layer packaging, or polystyrene and expanded polystyrene items will come into force 10 years from the date of this notification (i.e., 12 August 2031), giving industry a long-term transition horizon for more complex packaging formats.

Climate Transition Angle Plastic waste is not merely a waste management issue — it is a climate issue. The production of virgin plastic is a fossil fuel-intensive process, and single-use plastics that escape waste streams become a source of microplastic pollution with cascading effects on ecosystems, agriculture, and water security, all of which are physical climate risks that affect business continuity. India generates approximately 3.5 million tonnes of plastic waste annually, and a significant portion remains uncollected and unprocessed, contributing to soil degradation and water contamination that undermine agricultural supply chains. For companies aligning with global reporting frameworks, this regulation is directly material. The International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) IFRS S1 and S2 standards require companies to disclose material sustainability and climate risks — plastic waste management practices, EPR compliance, and exposure to single-use plastic bans are increasingly considered material risks for consumer goods, retail, and packaging companies. Similarly, the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD), which affects Indian subsidiaries and supply chain partners of European companies, requires disclosure on pollution, resource use, and circular economy practices, all of which are implicated by plastic waste obligations. The 2021 amendment also accelerates India's alignment with global momentum: over 60 countries have enacted SUP bans or restrictions, and the UN Global Plastics Treaty — currently under negotiation with India as a signatory participant — is expected to impose even more stringent obligations on plastic producers and brand owners globally. Companies that treat the 2021 amendment as a floor, not a ceiling, and invest in alternative materials, circular packaging design, and robust EPR infrastructure will be better positioned to manage tightening global requirements and access international markets where plastic-free credentials are increasingly a trade prerequisite.

What To Do Now Conduct a Full Plastic Inventory Audit: Map every plastic item used across your operations — product packaging, in-store consumables, logistics packaging, and marketing materials. Classify each item against the SUP ban list and thickness/GSM requirements. Identify gaps and non-compliant items that require immediate substitution or elimination. Register Under the EPR Framework Immediately: If your company is a brand owner, manufacturer, importer, or plastic waste processor and has not yet registered with CPCB under the EPR portal (https://eprplastic.cpcb.gov.in), do so without delay. Assign a dedicated compliance officer responsible for annual EPR filings, target fulfilment, and documentation. Engage Your Supply Chain: Issue formal compliance notices to all packaging suppliers, contract manufacturers, and logistics partners requiring them to confirm adherence to the minimum thickness (120 microns for carry bags) and GSM standards (60 GSM for non-woven bags). Include compliance clauses in procurement contracts and conduct supplier audits. Accelerate Packaging Redesign and Alternative Sourcing: Work with your R&D and procurement teams to identify and qualify alternative materials for any banned SUP items still in use. Prioritise compostable, reusable, or recyclable alternatives. Build a 12–18 month transition roadmap with clear milestones, factoring in consumer communication and any necessary product reformulation. Integrate Plastic Compliance into ESG Reporting: Ensure your annual report, Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report (BRSR), and any ISSB- or GRI-aligned disclosures include quantitative data on plastic packaging used, EPR targets met, recycled content percentages, and progress toward eliminating SUP items. This positions your company credibly with investors, customers, and regulators as the UN Plastics Treaty tightens global norms.

Our Take The Plastic Waste Management (Amendment) Rules, 2021 represent a structural shift in how India regulates plastic — moving from a purely downstream, waste-collection model to upstream producer accountability through EPR, and from voluntary action to hard prohibitions on specific product categories. The SUP ban is not a symbolic gesture; enforcement actions by CPCB and State Pollution Control Boards have intensified since July 2022, and penalties under the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986 — including imprisonment and fines — apply to violations. What makes this amendment particularly significant for businesses is the explicit inclusion of brand owners in the EPR net: this means that even if your company does not manufacture plastic, if your brand appears on plastic packaging, you are legally responsible for its end-of-life management. Looking ahead, the 10-year sunset clause provides a transition window for complex packaging categories, but given the pace of the UN Plastics Treaty negotiations and India's stated ambition to eliminate single-use plastics entirely, companies that wait for the deadline before acting will face a costly, compressed transition. The businesses that will emerge strongest are those that treat this regulation as the catalyst for a circular economy transformation — redesigning products, building reverse logistics, and investing in recycled content — rather than a compliance checkbox to be minimally satisfied.

title: ISSB and IFRS Sustainability Standards: What Indian Companies Must Know Before the Global Disclosure Wave Hits
summary: The International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB), operating under the IFRS Foundation, has established two landmark global standards — IFRS S1 and IFRS S2 — that are rapidly becoming the baseline for sustainability and climate-related financial disclosures worldwide. Indian companies with int

sections.t: Executive Summary The International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB), operating under the IFRS Foundation, has established two landmark global standards — IFRS S1 and IFRS S2 — that are rapidly becoming the baseline for sustainability and climate-related financial disclosures worldwide. Indian companies with international operations, foreign investors, or global supply chain relationships are already feeling indirect pressure to align with these standards, even before a domestic mandate arrives. With SEBI's Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report (BRSR) framework increasingly converging toward ISSB principles, and major trading partners like the EU and UK adopting compatible regimes, the window for proactive preparation is narrowing fast.

What Changed The ISSB was established in 2021 at COP26 in Glasgow and released its first two standards — IFRS S1 and IFRS S2 — in June 2023, marking a historic consolidation of fragmented sustainability reporting frameworks (TCFD, SASB, CDSB, and others) into a single, coherent global baseline. IFRS S1 sets out general requirements for companies to disclose material information about sustainability-related risks and opportunities across their entire value chain. It requires companies to report on governance, strategy, risk management, and metrics and targets — the same four-pillar architecture that underpins TCFD. IFRS S2 goes further, specifically addressing climate-related disclosures, including both physical risks (floods, droughts, extreme heat) and transition risks (carbon pricing, stranded assets, shifting market demand). Crucially, IFRS S2 mandates Scope 1, 2, and 3 greenhouse gas emissions disclosure and scenario analysis — tools that many Indian companies are only beginning to adopt. The International Accounting Standards Board (IASB) works in parallel to ensure that sustainability disclosures under ISSB standards are connected to and consistent with financial statements prepared under IFRS Accounting Standards, creating an integrated view of a company's financial health and climate exposure. This 'connectivity' principle is a significant shift from treating sustainability as a separate, non-financial exercise — it places climate risk squarely inside the financial reporting infrastructure.

Who Is Affected NSE/BSE-listed companies in the top 1,000 by market capitalisation already subject to SEBI's BRSR Core disclosures — ISSB alignment is the logical next step Indian subsidiaries and joint ventures of multinational corporations headquartered in jurisdictions adopting ISSB-aligned standards (EU, UK, Australia, Japan, Singapore, Canada) Export-oriented manufacturers supplying to European or North American customers subject to CSRD or SEC climate disclosure rules — Scope 3 supplier data will be demanded Indian companies seeking international capital — foreign institutional investors (FIIs) and ESG-focused funds increasingly screen on ISSB-aligned metrics Financial institutions, banks, and asset managers whose loan books and portfolios carry climate-exposed assets — physical and transition risk assessment is increasingly expected Mid-size companies in high-emitting sectors: steel, cement, chemicals, textiles, automotive, and real estate, regardless of current listing status, as supply chain disclosure obligations cascade downward

Key Obligations & Deadlines IFRS S1 & S2 effective date: Annual reporting periods beginning on or after 1 January 2024 — jurisdictions adopting these standards are now in their first reporting cycle India (SEBI BRSR Core): Top 150 listed companies must provide reasonable assurance on BRSR Core KPIs from FY 2023-24; top 1,000 companies follow on a comply-or-explain basis — ISSB concepts are embedded in these KPIs Scope 1 & 2 GHG emissions disclosure: Required under IFRS S2 from Year 1 of adoption; Scope 3 disclosures permitted one-year relief in most jurisdictions but expected from Year 2 Climate scenario analysis: Companies must disclose how their business strategy performs under at least two climate scenarios, including a 1.5°C or 2°C pathway — many Indian companies have not yet conducted this exercise Industry-specific metrics: IFRS S2 references SASB industry standards for sector-specific disclosures; companies should identify their applicable SASB standard immediately Value chain disclosures: Companies must use 'reasonable and supportable' information available about their full value chain — not just owned operations — placing new data demands on procurement and supplier management teams Third-party assurance: While not universally mandated on Day 1, jurisdictions and investors are moving rapidly toward requiring limited and then reasonable assurance on sustainability disclosures — build internal controls now

Climate Transition Angle ISSB standards are not a reporting exercise in isolation — they are the financial architecture of the global climate transition. By requiring companies to disclose how climate risks affect their cash flows, asset values, and cost of capital, IFRS S1 and S2 create the conditions under which capital markets can price climate risk accurately and redirect investment toward resilient, low-carbon business models. For Indian companies, the physical risk dimension is particularly urgent. India faces some of the world's most severe climate hazards — extreme heat affecting labour productivity, erratic monsoons disrupting agricultural supply chains, coastal flooding threatening port and logistics infrastructure, and water scarcity constraining manufacturing. IFRS S2's requirement to assess and disclose physical risks means companies can no longer treat these as external, unquantifiable events — they must be integrated into financial planning and asset valuation. On the transition risk side, India's own Net Zero commitments, the carbon border adjustment mechanisms being introduced by the EU (CBAM), and the global shift in investor preferences are creating a new competitive landscape. Companies that can demonstrate credible transition plans — with science-based targets, capital expenditure aligned to decarbonisation, and transparent Scope 3 emissions data — will access cheaper capital and retain international customers. Those that cannot will face higher financing costs, supply chain exclusion, and reputational risk. ISSB standards are the language in which this competitive differentiation will be expressed. The convergence between ISSB, CSRD (EU), SEC climate rules (USA), and SEBI BRSR is not accidental — it reflects a coordinated global effort to make climate risk visible in financial markets. Indian companies that prepare for ISSB alignment are simultaneously positioning themselves for compliance across multiple regulatory regimes.

What To Do Now Conduct a gap assessment: Map your current BRSR, GRI, or TCFD disclosures against the four pillars of IFRS S1 and S2 (Governance, Strategy, Risk Management, Metrics & Targets) to identify disclosure gaps and data deficiencies — prioritise Scope 3 and scenario analysis as the highest-gap areas for most Indian companies Establish your GHG emissions baseline: If you have not yet completed a comprehensive GHG inventory covering Scope 1, 2, and material Scope 3 categories, commission one immediately using the GHG Protocol Corporate Standard — this is the non-negotiable foundation for IFRS S2 compliance and investor credibility Run at least one climate scenario: Engage a sustainability advisory firm or use publicly available tools (IEA, NGFS scenarios) to assess how your business strategy holds up under a 1.5°C transition scenario and a high-physical-risk scenario — document assumptions, data sources, and financial implications Engage your CFO and Board: ISSB standards explicitly require Board-level governance of climate risk — sustainability cannot remain siloed in CSR or EHS teams. Brief your Audit Committee on IFRS S2 requirements, assign a Board member with climate oversight responsibility, and integrate climate risk into your Enterprise Risk Management (ERM) framework Map your supply chain exposure: Identify Tier 1 and Tier 2 suppliers in high-emission or climate-vulnerable sectors, begin collecting activity data, and develop a supplier engagement programme — both because IFRS S2 requires it and because your multinational customers will demand it within 12–24 months

Our Take The ISSB standards represent the most significant development in corporate reporting since the introduction of mandatory financial auditing — and their arrival in India's disclosure ecosystem is a matter of 'when,' not 'if.' SEBI has made clear through successive iterations of BRSR that India's large listed companies will be held to globally comparable sustainability disclosure standards, and the ISSB framework is the international benchmark against which Indian regulation will continue to converge. More immediately, the pressure is coming not from domestic regulators but from global capital markets: foreign institutional investors managing trillions of dollars are embedding ISSB alignment into their due diligence checklists, and Indian companies that cannot provide credible, assured, ISSB-compatible data risk being screened out of ESG mandates and green bond markets. Our view is that the next 18 to 24 months are the critical preparation window — companies that invest now in GHG accounting infrastructure, scenario analysis capabilities, and Board-level climate governance will not only achieve compliance but will build a genuine competitive advantage as climate disclosure becomes as routine and expected as financial reporting.

title: Mastering GRI Standards: A Practical Guide for Indian Companies Navigating Sustainability Reporting
summary: The Global Reporting Initiative (GRI) Standards remain the world's most widely adopted sustainability reporting framework, and Indian companies — whether driven by SEBI's BRSR mandate, investor due diligence, or global supply chain requirements — increasingly need to understand how to use them corre
