sections.t: Executive Summary The Global Reporting Initiative (GRI) Standards remain the world's most widely adopted sustainability reporting framework, and Indian companies — whether driven by SEBI's BRSR mandate, investor due diligence, or global supply chain requirements — increasingly need to understand how to use them correctly. Misapplication of GRI Standards is one of the most common and costly compliance mistakes organisations make, leading to greenwashing allegations, audit failures, and lost credibility with stakeholders. This post breaks down how to use the GRI Standards effectively, who needs to act, and what your sustainability team should do right now.

What Changed The GRI Standards are a modular, interrelated set of disclosure requirements published by the Global Reporting Initiative, a globally recognised independent standards body headquartered in Amsterdam. The Standards were substantially updated in 2021, with the revised Universal Standards (GRI 1, 2, and 3) taking effect from 1 January 2023, replacing the previous GRI 101, 102, and 103 framework. This was one of the most significant overhauls in GRI's history. The updated framework introduces a clearer three-tier architecture: GRI 1 (Foundation) sets out the purpose, key concepts, and requirements for using the Standards; GRI 2 (General Disclosures) covers organisational profile, governance, strategy, and stakeholder engagement; and GRI 3 (Material Topics) provides a structured methodology for determining and reporting on material topics. These are then supplemented by Sector Standards and Topic Standards covering specific environmental, social, and governance issues such as emissions (GRI 305), water (GRI 303), occupational health and safety (GRI 403), and anti-corruption (GRI 205), among many others. A critical distinction that many organisations miss is the difference between reporting 'in accordance with' GRI Standards — which requires meeting all applicable requirements — versus 'with reference to' GRI Standards, which is a more flexible approach allowing selective use of disclosures. Both pathways are legitimate, but they carry different credibility signals to investors and regulators, and companies must clearly state which pathway they have followed.

Who Is Affected Top 1,000 listed companies in India required to file BRSR (Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report) with SEBI — GRI alignment strengthens BRSR disclosures significantly Indian subsidiaries and supply chain partners of European multinationals subject to CSRD (Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive), which references GRI as an accepted framework Export-oriented manufacturers in textiles, chemicals, automotive, and pharmaceuticals facing GRI-based supplier assessments from global buyers Private equity and venture-backed companies preparing for IPO or seeking ESG-focused institutional investment Public Sector Undertakings (PSUs) and CPSEs increasingly expected to align with GRI under DPE (Department of Public Enterprises) sustainability guidelines Mid-sized companies in the MSME segment that are part of listed company supply chains, as Scope 3 disclosure requirements push GRI expectations downstream Financial institutions, banks, and NBFCs responding to RBI's disclosure expectations and investor ESG scoring frameworks that use GRI as a benchmark

Key Obligations & Deadlines IMMEDIATE: Confirm whether your organisation is reporting 'in accordance with' GRI or 'with reference to' GRI — this must be explicitly stated in your sustainability report and GRI Content Index IMMEDIATE: Ensure your GRI Content Index is complete, accurate, and publicly accessible — this is the primary audit trail for any GRI-based disclosure and is scrutinised by ESG rating agencies such as MSCI, Sustainalytics, and CDP BY NEXT REPORTING CYCLE: Complete a formal materiality assessment aligned with GRI 3 requirements, including identification of actual and potential impacts across your value chain — the 2021 update requires 'impact materiality' not just financial materiality BY NEXT REPORTING CYCLE: Map your existing BRSR disclosures against the GRI Content Index to identify gaps — SEBI's BRSR Core indicators align closely with several GRI Topic Standards, enabling dual-use reporting efficiency ONGOING: Sector Standards (e.g., GRI 11 for Oil & Gas, GRI 13 for Agriculture) are being rolled out progressively — companies in covered sectors must integrate applicable Sector Standards into their reporting as they become effective ONGOING: Maintain documented evidence for all GRI disclosures — third-party assurance over GRI reports is increasingly expected by institutional investors and is mandatory for BRSR Core disclosures for the top 150 listed companies from FY2023-24 2024-2025: Watch for GRI's forthcoming Sector Standards covering financial services, mining, and infrastructure — begin gap assessments now if your sector is in the pipeline

Climate Transition Angle GRI Standards are not purely a reporting compliance exercise — they are a foundational tool for managing and communicating your company's exposure to climate transition risks and physical climate risks. GRI 201 (Economic Performance) now encourages disclosure of financial implications of climate risks, directly paralleling the recommendations of the Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures (TCFD) and the newly effective ISSB Standards (IFRS S2). For Indian companies, the stakes are particularly high. India's geographic exposure to physical climate risks — extreme heat, monsoon variability, flooding, and water stress — means that GRI disclosures on water (GRI 303), energy (GRI 302), and emissions (GRI 305) are no longer just reputational exercises. They are signals of operational resilience that sophisticated investors and lenders are actively pricing. Companies that cannot produce credible, GRI-aligned data on these metrics are increasingly disadvantaged in capital markets. The convergence of global frameworks is accelerating. The ISSB's IFRS S1 and S2 Standards, now adopted or under consideration by regulators in over 20 jurisdictions, explicitly acknowledge GRI as a complementary framework for impact-focused disclosures. The European Union's CSRD mandates the European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS), which were developed in collaboration with GRI to ensure interoperability. For Indian exporters and companies with European investors or customers, this means GRI compliance is increasingly a market access requirement, not just a voluntary best practice. Building GRI competency now positions your organisation at the intersection of every major global reporting standard that matters.

What To Do Now CONDUCT A GRI FRAMEWORK AUDIT: Map your current sustainability report against the 2021 GRI Universal Standards (GRI 1, 2, and 3) to identify structural gaps — many companies are still inadvertently using the old 2016 framework structure, which is no longer current INVEST IN MATERIALITY ASSESSMENT: Commission or refresh your materiality assessment using GRI 3's double materiality lens, covering both your impacts on people and planet AND the financial risks and opportunities that sustainability issues pose to your business — this is the backbone of credible GRI reporting and increasingly required by BRSR and CSRD-linked stakeholders BUILD YOUR GRI CONTENT INDEX: Create or update a structured GRI Content Index that maps each required disclosure to the relevant section of your report, indicates omissions with clear justifications, and is published as a standalone document on your website — this is the single most important credibility signal for ESG raters and investors ENGAGE YOUR DATA OWNERS: GRI reporting requires granular, verifiable data from operations, HR, procurement, legal, and finance — establish internal data governance protocols so that sustainability disclosures are treated with the same rigour as financial reporting, including clear ownership, collection timelines, and audit trails PLAN FOR ASSURANCE: Engage a third-party assurance provider familiar with GRI's assurance protocols to conduct limited or reasonable assurance over your key GRI disclosures — this is already mandatory for BRSR Core disclosures and will become a baseline expectation across your investor and customer base within 2-3 years

Our Take GRI Standards have quietly become the de facto common language of global sustainability disclosure — and Indian companies that treat them as a box-ticking exercise are making a strategic error they may not be able to correct quickly. The 2021 framework update was not cosmetic; it fundamentally shifted reporting from a communications function to an accountability function, embedding impact assessment, stakeholder engagement, and value chain thinking at the core of what disclosure means. As SEBI continues to strengthen BRSR requirements, as European customers demand CSRD-compatible supplier data, and as the ISSB framework gains regulatory traction across Asia, the companies that have invested in robust GRI infrastructure will find themselves with a significant competitive and reputational advantage. Those that have not will face a scramble to catch up under regulatory pressure and investor scrutiny simultaneously. The time to build this capability is now — not in response to the next enforcement notice.

title: MoEFCC Internal Appointment: What Executive Engineering Postings Signal for Environmental Infrastructure in India
summary: The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) issued an internal office order on 7 October 2025 appointing Shri Sanjay Kumar Meena as Executive Engineer (Civil) in the Civil Construction Unit. While administrative in nature, such postings within MoEFCC's engineering cadre are meani

sections.t: Executive Summary The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) issued an internal office order on 7 October 2025 appointing Shri Sanjay Kumar Meena as Executive Engineer (Civil) in the Civil Construction Unit. While administrative in nature, such postings within MoEFCC's engineering cadre are meaningful signals of the Ministry's operational capacity to execute physical infrastructure — including green buildings, research stations, eco-sensitive zone management facilities, and climate monitoring infrastructure. For companies and project proponents operating under MoEFCC's regulatory purview, understanding the Ministry's internal capacity helps anticipate timelines for approvals, inspections, and compliance verifications.

What Changed Through an official office order dated 7 October 2025, MoEFCC formally appointed Shri Sanjay Kumar Meena to the post of Executive Engineer (Civil) within the Ministry's Civil Construction Unit. Executive Engineers at MoEFCC oversee the planning, execution, and supervision of civil works undertaken by or on behalf of the Ministry — ranging from construction of field offices and research facilities to infrastructure within protected areas and forest divisions. This appointment fills a key technical role within the Ministry's internal engineering hierarchy. The Civil Construction Unit is responsible for translating the Ministry's physical infrastructure mandates into on-ground execution, and the appointment of a senior engineer signals continued investment in that capacity. For external stakeholders, this is a governance update — but one with downstream implications for how quickly MoEFCC can operationalize field-level environmental programmes and oversight mechanisms.

Who Is Affected Project proponents seeking Environmental Clearances (EC) or Forest Clearances (FC) under MoEFCC, where site inspections or physical verification may be required Infrastructure and construction companies working on projects in or near eco-sensitive zones, forest land, or coastal regulation zones Public Sector Undertakings (PSUs) and Central Government agencies coordinating with MoEFCC for joint infrastructure programmes Environmental consultants and legal advisors tracking MoEFCC's institutional capacity and decision-making velocity State Forest Departments and State Environment Impact Assessment Authorities (SEIAAs) that interface with MoEFCC's technical units Climate and biodiversity-focused NGOs and research institutions that rely on MoEFCC-managed field infrastructure

Key Obligations & Deadlines No direct compliance obligations arise for private entities from this internal appointment order Project proponents with pending site inspection requests under EIA Notification 2006 or Forest (Conservation) Act should note improved engineering capacity at MoEFCC, which may accelerate physical verification schedules — update internal project timelines accordingly Companies with active Environmental Clearance conditions requiring periodic MoEFCC inspections should ensure site readiness and documentation is current, as increased engineering staffing may lead to more frequent or thorough field visits There is no statutory deadline for external parties arising from this order; however, proponents should monitor MoEFCC's official website (moef.gov.in) for any subsequent orders linking this appointment to specific project oversight responsibilities Internal compliance teams should file this order as part of their regulatory change log for the MoEFCC category, maintaining a record of Ministry staffing changes that could affect approval and inspection pipelines

Climate Transition Angle India's climate transition is not purely a financial or policy exercise — it requires significant physical infrastructure. MoEFCC is responsible for maintaining and expanding India's network of environmental monitoring stations, biodiversity research facilities, coastal and wetland management infrastructure, and green corridor projects that form the backbone of the country's Nature-Based Solutions (NbS) commitments under the Paris Agreement and the National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC). Strengthening the Civil Construction Unit at MoEFCC directly supports India's ability to build and maintain the physical assets needed to measure, monitor, and report on climate and biodiversity outcomes. This is increasingly relevant as India aligns its national reporting frameworks with international standards. The ISSB's IFRS S2 (Climate-related Disclosures) and the EU's CSRD both emphasize the importance of physical risk data — data that depends on robust field infrastructure operated by institutions like MoEFCC. For corporate ESG teams, MoEFCC's institutional health is a material consideration: delays in environmental clearances or weakened field oversight can translate into stranded asset risks, reputational exposure, and supply chain disruptions — particularly for sectors like mining, power generation, real estate, and agribusiness that operate in or near ecologically sensitive landscapes.

What To Do Now Update your regulatory stakeholder map: Log this appointment in your MoEFCC engagement tracker. If your company has active projects requiring civil or site-level coordination with MoEFCC, identify whether the Civil Construction Unit is a relevant point of contact and update your correspondence records Review pending inspection and clearance timelines: With enhanced engineering capacity at MoEFCC, reassess the expected timelines for any site inspections tied to Environmental or Forest Clearance conditions — build contingency into project schedules for earlier-than-expected inspections Audit site compliance readiness: Ensure all project sites subject to MoEFCC oversight meet current EC/FC conditions. Prepare updated compliance documentation, including environmental monitoring reports, afforestation compliance records, and compensatory afforestation fund (CAMPA) status Engage your environmental consultant: Ask your legal and environmental advisory team to monitor whether this appointment leads to any changes in MoEFCC's civil works contracting or infrastructure development priorities that could affect your operating environment Integrate into ESG disclosure: For companies reporting under BRSR (Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting) or preparing for voluntary ISSB-aligned disclosures, document your engagement with MoEFCC's regulatory processes as part of your governance and physical risk management narrative

Our Take It would be easy to dismiss an internal MoEFCC appointment order as bureaucratic noise — but experienced ESG practitioners know that institutional capacity is a leading indicator of regulatory velocity. When India's premier environment ministry strengthens its technical engineering cadre, it signals intent: intent to build, inspect, monitor, and enforce more effectively. As India accelerates its commitments under the Global Biodiversity Framework, ramps up climate adaptation spending, and faces growing pressure from international investors demanding credible environmental governance, MoEFCC's operational depth matters more than ever. Companies that treat every Ministry order — however administrative — as a data point in their regulatory intelligence function will be better positioned to anticipate shifts in enforcement posture, approval timelines, and compliance expectations. The appointment of Shri Sanjay Kumar Meena is a small gear turning in a very large machine. Watch the machine.

title: NDC 3.0 and the 42% Emissions Gap: What the Climate Action Tracker's 2030 Benchmarks Mean for Indian Corporates, BRSR Disclosures, and Capital Access
summary: The Climate Action Tracker's latest sectoral analysis — published at https://climateactiontracker.org/blog/by-the-numbers-the-climate-action-we-need-this-decade/ — confirms that current global policies will deliver less than 1% emissions reduction by 2030, against the 42% cut needed to keep 1.5°C al

sections.t: Executive Summary The Climate Action Tracker's latest sectoral analysis — published at https://climateactiontracker.org/blog/by-the-numbers-the-climate-action-we-need-this-decade/ — confirms that current global policies will deliver less than 1% emissions reduction by 2030, against the 42% cut needed to keep 1.5°C alive, creating a structural climate credibility crisis that now has direct consequences for Indian corporates accessing international capital, export markets, and institutional ESG mandates. India's own NDC 3.0, due under the Paris Agreement by early 2025, will set the national emissions trajectory through 2035 — and the ambition level India signals in that submission will immediately filter into how global investors assess the stranded asset risk, transition readiness, and BRSR Climate-Related disclosures of every BSE 500 company operating in carbon-intensive sectors. For CFOs and CSOs, this is not a geopolitical abstraction: the gap between current policy trajectories and 1.5°C pathways is precisely the basis on which SBTi validates corporate targets, ISSB IFRS S2 requires scenario analysis, and EU CBAM prices carbon at the border — three mechanisms with direct, near-term financial exposure for Indian listed companies.

What Changed Reporting by Climate Action Tracker (original article: https://climateactiontracker.org/blog/by-the-numbers-the-climate-action-we-need-this-decade/), co-authored by Sophie Boehm, Clea Schumer, Kelly Levin and eleven other researchers under the Systems Change Lab project, provides the most granular sectoral breakdown yet of the emissions reductions needed by 2030 and 2035 to limit warming to 1.5°C. The core finding: current policies put the world on track for 2.6–2.8°C of warming, and the gap between current commitments and a 1.5°C pathway is now so wide that it cannot be closed without unprecedented deployment pace across every sector — power, transport, agriculture, industry, land use — simultaneously. What this builds on and what it overturns: previous NDC cycles allowed countries to set directional pledges without hard sectoral sub-targets. The new CAT analysis explicitly argues that NDC 3.0 submissions — due by early 2025 under UNFCCC rules — must embed quantitative, time-bound sectoral targets (EV sales percentages, renewable capacity additions, methane reduction rates) alongside economy-wide GHG targets for 2030 and 2035. This is a substantive shift from the architecture of India's current 2022 NDC, which commits to 45% emissions intensity reduction from 2005 levels and 50% non-fossil power capacity by 2030 — but lacks the granular sectoral sub-targets that international investors and standard-setters are now demanding as the credibility threshold. The India-specific consequence is immediate and multi-layered. First, India's NDC 3.0 ambition level will be scrutinised against the CAT's 1.5°C sectoral benchmarks by global rating agencies (MSCI, Sustainalytics, CDP), and any gap will be mapped back onto Indian corporates' transition risk scores. Second, SEBI's BRSR Core framework — mandatory for the top 150 listed companies from FY2024-25 and expanding to the top 1,000 by FY2026-27 — requires Scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions disclosure with third-party assurance; if India's NDC trajectory is rated 'Insufficient' or 'Critically Insufficient' by CAT, Indian companies face heightened scrutiny on the credibility of their own climate targets under BRSR. Third, ISSB IFRS S2 — now being assessed for adoption by ICAI and MCA's sustainability reporting roadmap — requires companies to disclose against 1.5°C and 2°C scenarios; the CAT data directly informs the physical and transition risk parameters used in those scenario models.

Who Is Affected Power sector: NTPC, Adani Green, Tata Power, JSW Energy — companies whose capital expenditure plans, renewable capacity additions, and coal retirement timelines are directly benchmarked against NDC sectoral targets and CAT's power sector decarbonisation pathway Steel, cement, aluminium, and chemicals: Tata Steel, JSW Steel, UltraTech Cement, Hindalco — high-emissions manufacturers facing EU CBAM pricing from October 2023 (transitional) and full implementation from 2026, where India's NDC ambition level affects the carbon price differential applied at the EU border Automotive OEMs and auto-ancillaries: Maruti Suzuki, Tata Motors, Mahindra — companies whose EV transition timelines and Scope 3 downstream emissions are directly exposed to NDC sectoral sub-targets for transport, which CAT now recommends should include quantitative EV penetration benchmarks Agri-food and FMCG: ITC, HUL, Dabur — companies with significant Scope 3 land-use and agriculture emissions, where CAT's land sector targets (including mangrove restoration, deforestation rates) will increasingly appear in CDP questionnaires and TNFD disclosures BSE 500 listed companies with international institutional shareholders (FPIs holding >5% in aggregate): subject to investor stewardship escalation if corporate climate targets are misaligned with updated NDC trajectories CFOs: responsible for BRSR Core assurance readiness, climate-related financial disclosure under ISSB S2 as it approaches India adoption, and EU CBAM compliance cost modelling Chief Sustainability Officers: must translate NDC 3.0 ambition levels into updated SBTi target submissions, BRSR Principal 6 disclosures, and CDP Climate response upgrades Board Risk Committees: required under SEBI's LODR amendments to oversee climate-related risks; the CAT's finding that current policies deliver <1% emissions reduction by 2030 constitutes a material transition risk that must be disclosed ESG-focused FPIs, domestic mutual funds with ESG mandates (SEBI's ESG fund circular, November 2023), and DFIs including SBI, NaBFID, and IREDA whose green bond frameworks reference NDC alignment

Key Obligations & Deadlines India NDC 3.0 submission to UNFCCC: due by early 2025 (February 10, 2025 is the working deadline referenced in UNFCCC COP29 decisions); Indian companies must engage with MoEFCC's NDC consultation process now, as sectoral targets will define regulatory expectations for 2030-2035 BRSR Core — Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions disclosure with reasonable assurance: mandatory for top 150 listed entities from FY2024-25 reporting cycle (annual report due by September 2025); Scope 3 disclosure mandatory from FY2025-26 BRSR Core expansion to top 1,000 listed companies: FY2026-27, with limited assurance on Scope 1/2; companies outside top 150 must begin baseline measurement now EU CBAM full implementation: January 1, 2026; Indian steel, cement, aluminium, fertiliser, and hydrogen exporters must have verified Scope 1 embedded emissions data for all EU-bound shipments, benchmarked against EU ETS default values SBTi near-term and net-zero target validation: SBTi's Corporate Net-Zero Standard requires targets aligned with 1.5°C pathways; the CAT sectoral benchmarks are the underlying reference data; companies with pending SBTi submissions must review alignment against updated 2030/2035 sectoral benchmarks ISSB IFRS S2 climate scenario disclosure: while not yet mandated in India, MCA's Companies (Accounts) Amendment Rules trajectory and ICAI's sustainability standards adoption roadmap suggest India-aligned standards by 2026-27; preparatory scenario analysis against 1.5°C and 2.6-2.8°C (current policy) pathways should begin in FY2025 CDP Climate questionnaire 2025 (submission window: April–July 2025): updated scoring methodology will reference NDC 3.0 ambition levels and CAT sectoral benchmarks; companies rated B or below face investor escalation under SEBI's ESG fund stewardship requirements

Climate Transition Angle The CAT's finding that current policies deliver less than 1% emissions reduction by 2030 — against a required 42% — is not merely a scientific alarm. It is the quantified basis for transition risk pricing across every asset class exposed to carbon-intensive sectors in India. For Indian corporates, this creates three compounding transition risk vectors that boards must now explicitly model. First, physical risk acceleration: a 2.6–2.8°C trajectory — the outcome of current policies — implies materially higher frequency of extreme weather events, monsoon disruption, water stress in key industrial belts (Vidarbha cotton, Odisha steel, Rajasthan cement), and coastal infrastructure risk that directly affects asset valuations, insurance costs, and working capital cycles. ISSB IFRS S2 Paragraph 10 requires disclosure of physical risks under both a low (1.5°C) and high (current policy) warming scenario — and the CAT's numbers are precisely what populates the high-warming scenario. Second, transition risk from policy discontinuity: if India's NDC 3.0 adopts more ambitious 2035 sectoral sub-targets — as CAT recommends — this will accelerate regulatory tightening under BEE's PAT Scheme cycles, CPCB's industrial emission standards revisions, and MoEFCC's environment clearance conditions for coal-based power and greenfield heavy industry. Companies that have not built transition capex into their 5-year plans on the assumption of policy continuity are carrying unpriced transition risk on their balance sheets. Third, capital access risk: India's green bond market (SEBI Green Bond Framework, 2023) and the RBI's evolving climate risk disclosure framework for banks will increasingly require borrowers to demonstrate NDC-aligned transition plans. The International Platform on Sustainable Finance (IPSF), of which India is a member, is moving toward taxonomy interoperability standards; Indian companies accessing green finance in EU, UK, or Singapore capital markets will face credibility challenges if their transition plans reference an NDC rated 'Insufficient' by CAT. This is not a future risk — it is already appearing in green bond eligibility assessments and bilateral DFI loan covenants in 2024-25.

What To Do Now Sustainability / Government Affairs teams — within 60 days: engage formally with MoEFCC's NDC 3.0 consultation process; submit sector-specific inputs on feasible 2030 and 2035 decarbonisation pathways, particularly on renewable energy deployment rates, EV transition timelines, and industrial energy efficiency targets under BEE PAT Scheme; the NDC sectoral targets India adopts will become the regulatory benchmarks against which BRSR disclosures are assessed CFO / Finance teams — within 90 days: commission a CAT-aligned transition risk scenario analysis for FY2025 financial planning; model the balance sheet impact of both a 1.5°C rapid-transition scenario and a 2.8°C current-policy scenario against key asset categories, energy costs, carbon cost exposure (EU CBAM, domestic carbon market), and insurance premiums; this is a board-level disclosure obligation under SEBI LODR Schedule V and an ISSB S2 preparedness imperative Chief Sustainability Officer — immediately: audit existing SBTi near-term targets against CAT's updated 2030 and 2035 sectoral benchmarks; if targets were validated before CAT's 2024 sectoral update, a voluntary re-assessment may be needed to maintain credibility with institutional investors applying Net Zero Investment Framework (NZIF) or CA100+ stewardship standards Company Secretary / Board Risk Committee — before next board meeting: table the CAT findings as a material transition risk update; ensure the board risk register explicitly captures the <1% vs. 42% emissions reduction gap as a macro risk driver affecting the company's regulatory environment, export eligibility, and cost of capital; document the board's oversight of this risk in the Annual Report's BRSR Management and Process Disclosures section Investor Relations — ahead of FY2025 annual report: proactively communicate to institutional investors how the company's climate targets align with (or plan to converge toward) NDC 3.0 sectoral benchmarks; ESG-focused FPIs operating under SEBI's ESG fund circular and UNPRI signatories will be filing stewardship escalation requests with companies whose climate plans are misaligned with updated CAT benchmarks through the 2025 AGM season Procurement / Supply Chain — within 6 months: initiate Scope 3 Category 1 (purchased goods and services) emissions mapping aligned with GHG Protocol Corporate Value Chain Standard; CAT's sectoral targets for agriculture, land use, and industry will cascade into supply chain procurement standards required by EU CSRD-reporting European customers from FY2025 onwards

Our Take The CAT's 2030 sectoral benchmarks represent a recalibration of the ambition floor — not just for governments, but for every corporate climate commitment that references national policy as its baseline. The gap between less than 1% actual reduction and the 42% required is so structurally large that it is now functioning as a credibility discount applied to all actors — governments and corporates alike — who claim alignment with Paris without demonstrating sector-specific, time-bound interim pathways. For India, this matters acutely because India's NDC 3.0 submission will arrive at a moment when three regulatory architectures are simultaneously tightening their scrutiny of national-to-corporate climate alignment: BRSR Core with mandatory assurance, ISSB S2 approaching domestic adoption, and EU CBAM in full implementation by 2026. The trajectory I expect over the next 12–36 months is as follows. India will submit an NDC 3.0 that modestly upgrades the 2030 intensity target and introduces a 2035 headline target, but will likely stop short of the granular sectoral sub-targets CAT recommends — this is both a political economy constraint and a data readiness issue. However, the gap between India's NDC ambition and the CAT 1.5°C benchmark will be used by rating agencies to justify continued 'Insufficient' or 'Almost Sufficient' national ratings for India, and this rating will feed directly into MSCI ESG Country Scores and Sustainalytics' sovereign risk overlays — which institutional investors apply when screening Indian equities and bonds. The companies most exposed are those in sectors where the CAT sectoral gap is widest: coal power, industrial steel and cement, and high-emission agriculture. The companies best positioned are those that have already decoupled their own climate targets from the national NDC trajectory — setting SBTi-validated absolute reduction targets referenced to global sectoral benchmarks rather than India's intensity-based NDC metric. Boards that are still using 'we are aligned with India's NDC' as their climate governance defence will find that argument increasingly untenable with international institutional shareholders by the 2026 AGM season. The smarter move, starting now, is to set targets that exceed NDC ambition and reference CAT sectoral benchmarks directly — because that is the language that preserves capital access in a world where the emissions gap is no longer deniable.

title: NDC Ambition Gap 2025: Why India's Next Climate Pledge Will Define Its Capital Market Credibility, Export Access, and Net Zero Trajectory
summary: Climate Action Tracker's latest analysis — published under the Systems Change Lab project and available at https://climateactiontracker.org/blog/by-the-numbers-the-climate-action-we-need-this-decade/ — delivers an unambiguous verdict: current global policies, including India's existing NDC commitmen

sections.t: Executive Summary Climate Action Tracker's latest analysis — published under the Systems Change Lab project and available at https://climateactiontracker.org/blog/by-the-numbers-the-climate-action-we-need-this-decade/ — delivers an unambiguous verdict: current global policies, including India's existing NDC commitments, will achieve less than 1% GHG emissions reduction by 2030 against 2023 baselines, against a scientifically required 42% cut to hold 1.5°C. With the next round of nationally determined contributions due by early 2025, India faces a structural fork in the road: submit a credible, sector-specific NDC that unlocks green capital and preserves export market access, or risk accelerating sovereign climate risk downgrades, carbon border exposure, and SBTi non-alignment penalties that are already beginning to cascade through institutional investor mandates. For Indian CFOs and CSOs, the immediate operational consequence is this: whatever sectoral targets India embeds in its 2025 NDC will become the regulatory scaffolding against which BRSR Core disclosures, BEE PAT cycle targets, CPCB compliance trajectories, and investor net zero alignment assessments are benchmarked over the next decade. This is not a distant policy event — it is the reference frame being set right now.

What Changed Reporting by Climate Action Tracker (https://climateactiontracker.org/blog/by-the-numbers-the-climate-action-we-need-this-decade/), authored by a 14-member team including Sophie Boehm, Kelly Levin, and Joel Jaeger under the Systems Change Lab project, provides the most granular public breakdown yet of what 1.5°C-aligned NDCs must contain — sector by sector, with quantified 2030 and 2035 benchmarks. The key numbers: a 42% global GHG reduction by 2030 and 56% by 2035, both relative to 2023 levels. Today's full policy implementation trajectory delivers less than 1% by either milestone year. Even full implementation of current NDCs puts the world on a 2.6–2.8°C warming pathway. What this overturns is the comfortable narrative that incremental NDC upgrades and long-term net zero pledges are sufficient. The analysis makes explicit that without a 'quantum leap in ambition' in the 2025 NDC round — backed by timebound, quantitative, sector-specific targets for power, transport, land use, industry, and buildings — the 1.5°C window closes irreversibly before 2035. For India, whose NDC submitted in 2022 committed to 45% emissions intensity reduction by 2030 (against 2005 levels) and 50% cumulative electric power installed capacity from non-fossil sources by 2030, the 2025 round demands a material step-up: absolute economy-wide targets for 2035, sector-specific EV sales benchmarks, renewable energy deployment floors, and methane reduction timelines aligned with the Global Methane Pledge India has not yet formally endorsed. The India-specific regulatory consequence is direct: SEBI's BRSR Core framework, mandatory for the top 150 listed companies from FY2023-24 and expanding to the top 1,000 by FY2026-27, already requires Scope 1 and Scope 2 GHG intensity disclosure with third-party assurance. ISSB IFRS S2 — which MCA's sustainability reporting roadmap is expected to reference — requires companies to disclose alignment with jurisdictional climate targets. If India's 2025 NDC raises the ambition bar with sector-specific 2035 targets, it automatically tightens the compliance and disclosure baseline for every listed Indian company operating in power, cement, steel, chemicals, automotive, and logistics sectors.

Who Is Affected Power sector: IPPs, coal-dependent utilities, and RE developers — NTPC, Adani Green, Tata Power, JSW Energy — whose capex plans will be benchmarked against any 2035 power sector decarbonisation target in the new NDC Hard-to-abate industries: Steel (Tata Steel, JSW Steel, SAIL), cement (UltraTech, ACC, Ambuja), and petrochemicals (Reliance, ONGC) facing SBTi Science-Based Target setting timelines and potential EU CBAM exposure if India's NDC fails to credibly reflect sectoral transition Automotive OEMs and component suppliers: Maruti Suzuki, Mahindra, Tata Motors, Bosch India — EV sales benchmarks in the NDC will directly influence CAFE norms, PLI scheme continuity, and EU EV export market access Export-oriented manufacturers in textiles, chemicals, leather, and agri-processing supplying EU buyers under CSRD/ESRS supply chain due diligence obligations, where India's NDC ambition signals sovereign transition credibility CFOs and Heads of Treasury at BSE 500 companies accessing green bonds, sustainability-linked loans, or foreign institutional investment — NDC credibility is a direct input to ESG ratings (MSCI, Sustainalytics, S&P CSA) which drive FII allocation decisions Chief Sustainability Officers and BRSR compliance leads responsible for FY2025-26 disclosures requiring Scope 3 ambition statements and transition plan outlines under evolving SEBI guidance Board Risk Committees with fiduciary exposure to stranded asset risk in fossil fuel-linked balance sheets and climate litigation risk if transition planning is demonstrably misaligned with science Institutional investors — domestic MFs, insurance funds under IRDAI's ESG disclosure framework, pension funds, and FIIs with Paris-aligned mandates — reassessing India sovereign and corporate climate risk premiums

Key Obligations & Deadlines India NDC 2025 submission: Due by early 2025 (UNFCCC deadline approximately February 2025) — MoEFCC and MoF coordination required; expected to include enhanced 2035 targets and sector-specific benchmarks following COP29 Baku signals BRSR Core third-party assured GHG disclosures: Mandatory for top 150 listed companies from FY2023-24 (already in effect); expanding to top 1,000 companies by FY2026-27 — Scope 1 and Scope 2 intensity metrics must be reported with limited assurance BEE PAT Cycle VIII targets (2023-2026): Designated Consumers in power, cement, steel, aluminium, fertiliser, pulp & paper, chlor-alkali, and textiles must meet specific energy consumption norms — NDC sectoral targets will inform Cycle IX design post-2026 EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM): Transition phase reporting live from October 2023; definitive pricing phase from January 2026 — Indian exporters of steel, aluminium, cement, fertilisers, and hydrogen must track embedded carbon against EU benchmarks now tied to NDC credibility signals SBTi Corporate Net Zero Standard: Companies with SBTi commitments must submit near-term targets (42-46% Scope 1+2 reduction by 2030 from a base year) within 24 months of commitment — India's NDC 2035 target provides the jurisdictional reference baseline SBTi uses for FLAG and ISIC-aligned target validation ISSB IFRS S2 adoption: MCA sustainability standards roadmap (expected consultation 2025) will align Indian reporting with ISSB S2 climate scenario analysis, transition plan, and physical risk disclosure requirements — NDC sector targets become the policy scenario anchor SEBI ESG rating provider framework (SEBI circular CIR/2023): ESG Rating Providers must incorporate sovereign climate commitment credibility into methodology — a weak India NDC 2025 directly depresses Indian corporate ESG ratings through the sovereign anchor effect

Climate Transition Angle The Climate Action Tracker analysis makes visible a structural problem Indian boards must internalise: the gap between current policy trajectories and 1.5°C is not a linear shortfall that time and incremental improvement will close. It is a compounding credibility deficit that, if unaddressed in the 2025 NDC round, will trigger a cascade of capital market consequences over the 2025-2030 window. Specifically, ISSB IFRS S2 requires companies to assess climate scenarios consistent with the goals of the Paris Agreement — if India's NDC signals a 2.6–2.8°C national pathway, multinational institutional investors using physical risk models (NGFS scenarios, IPCC AR6) will reprice Indian infrastructure, agriculture, and coastal real estate assets upward on physical risk and downward on transition readiness simultaneously. For Indian companies on SBTi pathways, the 2025 NDC round matters because SBTi's Country Pathway Tool and FLAG (Forest, Land, and Agriculture Guidance) use NDC sector benchmarks as policy context for target validation. A stronger NDC with explicit 2035 power sector and land-use targets would make it easier — not harder — for Indian steel, cement, and agri-business companies to set credible near-term SBTi targets without being penalised for sector-level ambiguity. Conversely, a weak NDC increases the probability that SBTi and CDP frameworks classify Indian sectoral pathways as misaligned, triggering disclosure flags that FIIs and ESG-screened funds use to underweight Indian equities. India's transition to a green industrial economy — via the PLI scheme for solar, battery storage, green hydrogen, and EV manufacturing — requires a credible NDC as its sovereign-level anchor to attract the USD 2.5 trillion in climate finance the country needs through 2030, per the International Energy Agency's India Energy Outlook.

What To Do Now CFO / Treasury (immediate, Q1 2025): Map your company's Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions intensity trajectory against both India's current 2022 NDC benchmarks AND the 1.5°C-aligned sector pathways quantified by Climate Action Tracker — identify the delta and stress-test your green bond covenants and SLL pricing step-downs against the more ambitious scenario Chief Sustainability Officer (within 60 days): Conduct a sector-specific NDC scenario analysis: if India submits a 2025 NDC with explicit 2035 power, transport, or industry targets, what does that mean for your BEE PAT obligations in Cycle IX, your BRSR Core intensity targets, and your SBTi validation timeline? Present the gap analysis to the board risk committee before FY2024-25 annual report sign-off Board Risk Committee (Q1 2025 board meeting): Commission a formal climate scenario analysis under ISSB IFRS S2 framework — use the NGFS 'Delayed Transition' and 'Net Zero 2050' scenarios with India-specific policy assumptions bracketed around the NDC 2025 outcome — this is both fiduciary prudence and pre-emptive preparation for MCA's forthcoming ISSB-aligned standards Export-oriented businesses (steel, aluminium, chemicals, textiles) — Head of Regulatory Affairs: Engage immediately with CBAM reporting infrastructure — the current CBAM transition phase requires quarterly embedded carbon reports; model the cost impact under both a weak and strong India NDC scenario, since EU benchmark evolution is tied to member state NDC credibility assessments IR and ESG Communications teams (before AGM season, April-May 2025): Proactively address the NDC ambition gap in investor engagement materials — institutional investors with Paris-aligned mandates will ask direct questions about your company's resilience to a 2.6°C versus 1.5°C policy pathway; have a quantified answer ready, not boilerplate language Procurement and supply chain heads (within 90 days): Initiate Scope 3 Category 1 and Category 11 (use of sold products) baseline assessments — as India's NDC raises sectoral ambition, downstream and upstream supply chain emissions will become material BRSR Core disclosures and CSRD-driven buyer requirements simultaneously; the window to establish baselines before these become hard obligations is closing

Our Take The 2025 NDC round is the single most consequential climate policy event of this decade for Indian business, and it is receiving a fraction of the boardroom attention it deserves. Here is what I expect to unfold over the next 12-36 months. First, India will submit an enhanced NDC in early 2025 that raises the absolute emissions reduction ambition modestly and includes sector-specific signals — particularly on renewable energy capacity, EV sales penetration, and potentially green hydrogen production targets — but will likely stop short of an absolute economy-wide 2035 GHG cap. This will be received by international climate finance bodies as 'improved but insufficient,' which will sustain India's 'Highly Insufficient' CAT rating and create a persistent sovereign climate credibility discount that institutional ESG frameworks will price into Indian corporate ratings. Second, the EU's CBAM definitive phase beginning January 2026 will accelerate a reckoning for Indian heavy industry: companies in steel, cement, and fertilisers that have not invested in transition credibility — measured partly through NDC alignment and SBTi commitments — will face compounding disadvantage in EU export markets just as domestic BEE PAT Cycle IX tightens energy norms post-2026. The companies that are already running 1.5°C scenario analysis, have third-party assured BRSR Core data, and have initiated SBTi target-setting will be able to demonstrate transition readiness to both regulators and capital providers. Those that have not will face the twin pressure of higher cost of capital and shrinking export market access simultaneously. Third, and most importantly for boards: MCA's sustainability reporting standards, expected for consultation in 2025 and aligned with ISSB IFRS S1/S2, will require Indian listed companies to disclose transition plans — which are, by definition, anchored to government climate policy. A company whose transition plan references India's NDC as its policy context will need that NDC to be credible, quantified, and sector-specific to withstand audit scrutiny and investor challenge. The boards and CFOs who are building their transition plans now, using the 1.5°C-aligned sector benchmarks that Climate Action Tracker has quantified, are not just doing good ESG — they are building audit-defensible, investor-grade climate disclosures ahead of a regulatory wave that will arrive whether India's NDC is bold or timid. The gap between action and ambition is not just a climate problem. For Indian business in 2025, it is a capital markets problem.

title: Notice to Producers Regarding Compliance of E-Waste Management Rules, 2022
summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent notice to producers covered under the E-Waste Management Rules, 2022, flagging non-compliance with Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) obligations. Producers who manufacture or import electrical and electronic equipment (EEE) are leg

sections.t: Executive Summary The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent notice to producers covered under the E-Waste Management Rules, 2022, flagging non-compliance with Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) obligations. Producers who manufacture or import electrical and electronic equipment (EEE) are legally required to register on the EPR portal, set annual collection targets, and ensure end-of-life management of their products through authorised recyclers. Failure to comply exposes companies to regulatory action, penalties, and reputational risk — making immediate remediation a business priority.

What Changed The E-Waste Management Rules, 2022 replaced the earlier 2016 framework and significantly strengthened India's approach to managing electronic waste. The 2022 Rules introduced a mandatory, market-based EPR mechanism under which producers must obtain EPR authorisation from the CPCB, register on the centralised EPR portal, set product-category-wise collection targets, and purchase EPR certificates from registered dismantlers and recyclers to demonstrate fulfilment of those targets. CPCB's latest urgent notice signals that a material number of producers have either failed to register on the EPR portal, have not filed required returns, or have not met their prescribed collection targets for the applicable financial year. This notice is a formal enforcement signal — not merely a reminder — and indicates that CPCB is moving from awareness-building to active compliance monitoring and potential prosecution. Companies that remain non-compliant risk having their EPR authorisation cancelled, facing show-cause notices, and being named publicly by the regulator.

Who Is Affected Producers of Electrical and Electronic Equipment (EEE): Any company that manufactures, imports, or sells EEE in India — including consumer electronics, IT hardware, telecom equipment, lighting, and large/small household appliances — falls under this notice. Brand Owners and OEMs: Companies that brand or market EEE products, even if manufacturing is outsourced, are classified as 'producers' under the Rules and bear EPR obligations. E-commerce platforms and importers: Online marketplaces facilitating sale of EEE, as well as importers, carry producer-equivalent responsibilities for products sold through their channels. Mid-sized and large enterprises: While smaller producers may have phased targets, medium-to-large companies with significant market volumes face the highest absolute collection obligations and are likely the primary audience for this enforcement notice. Registered Dismantlers and Recyclers: Although the notice targets producers, recyclers and dismantlers in the EPR certificate ecosystem must ensure their documentation and certificate issuance processes are audit-ready to support producer compliance.

Key Obligations & Deadlines EPR Portal Registration (Immediate): Producers not yet registered on the CPCB EPR portal (https://eprnewportal.cpcb.gov.in) must complete registration without delay. Operating without EPR authorisation is a direct violation of the 2022 Rules. Annual EPR Target Setting: Producers must log collection targets for each product category on the portal for the current financial year (FY 2024-25), calculated as a percentage of quantity of EEE placed on the market in the preceding years as per the Rules' prescribed trajectory. EPR Certificate Procurement: Producers must purchase EPR certificates from CPCB-authorised recyclers/dismantlers equivalent to their collection targets. Certificates must be linked to the producer's EPR account on the portal. Annual Return Filing: Producers are required to file annual returns on the EPR portal detailing EEE placed on the market, e-waste collected, and EPR certificates purchased. Returns for FY 2023-24 must be filed as per the prescribed schedule — companies should verify their specific filing deadline on the CPCB portal. Response to CPCB Notice (Urgent): Any producer who has received a direct notice from CPCB must respond within the stipulated timeframe mentioned in the notice — typically 15–30 days — with documentary evidence of compliance steps taken or an action plan for achieving compliance. Deposit to Environmental Compensation Fund: Producers unable to meet their EPR targets must deposit the prescribed Environmental Compensation amount, as notified by CPCB, to avoid further penal action.

Climate Transition Angle E-waste compliance is not merely a domestic regulatory checkbox — it sits at the intersection of India's circular economy ambitions, global supply chain due diligence requirements, and climate transition commitments. Electronic waste is one of the fastest-growing waste streams globally, and improper disposal releases toxic substances and greenhouse gases, while responsible recycling recovers critical minerals such as cobalt, lithium, copper, and rare earth elements that are indispensable for the clean energy transition — electric vehicles, wind turbines, and solar panels all depend on these recovered materials. For Indian companies with export operations or multinational investors, EPR compliance is increasingly a supply chain transparency requirement. The EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and the ISSB's IFRS S2 climate disclosure standards both require companies to account for material resource use and waste across the value chain. A failure to demonstrate responsible end-of-life product management could surface as a material gap in sustainability disclosures, affecting access to green finance and export market eligibility. India's own BRSR Core framework, mandated by SEBI for the top 150 listed companies, explicitly covers waste management and circular economy metrics — making EPR performance directly reportable to investors. Beyond compliance, producers who build robust EPR programmes gain early access to secondary raw material streams, reduce supply chain exposure to volatile virgin material prices, and align with the global narrative of responsible electronics stewardship that institutional ESG investors increasingly scrutinise.

What To Do Now Conduct an immediate EPR compliance audit: Assign your legal, sustainability, or EHS team to verify your company's registration status on the CPCB EPR portal, confirm all product categories are correctly listed, and identify any gaps in EPR certificate procurement versus targets for FY 2023-24 and FY 2024-25. Engage authorised recyclers and secure EPR certificates: If certificate shortfalls exist, initiate procurement immediately from CPCB-authorised dismantlers and recyclers. Maintain a diversified panel of recycler partners to avoid supply-side constraints and ensure audit-ready documentation for all transactions. File pending annual returns: Identify any unfiled or incorrectly filed annual returns and submit them on the EPR portal at the earliest. Consider appointing a dedicated compliance officer or engaging a specialist EPR consultant if internal capacity is limited. Integrate EPR metrics into BRSR and investor disclosures: Ensure that e-waste collection volumes, EPR certificate data, and recycler partner credentials are captured in your BRSR Core reporting under the 'Resource and Waste Management' principle, and flag material EPR liabilities in sustainability risk registers shared with your board and investors. Establish a forward-looking EPR governance structure: Embed EPR target-setting into your annual product planning and sales forecasting cycle. As EEE volumes grow, EPR obligations scale proportionally — proactive planning avoids the last-minute certificate scramble that is currently exposing many producers to regulatory risk.

Our Take CPCB's urgent notice is a clear sign that India's e-waste regulator has shifted gears from education to enforcement. The 2022 Rules were designed with intent — the market-based EPR certificate mechanism was crafted to create genuine economic incentives for formalising India's largely informal e-waste recycling sector, which currently handles an estimated 95% of the country's e-waste. Non-compliance by producers not only creates legal exposure for those companies but structurally undermines the entire ecosystem the Rules are trying to build. We expect CPCB to escalate enforcement over the next 12–18 months, including public disclosure of non-compliant producers, suspension of EPR authorisations, and potentially linking EPR compliance status to import licensing and BIS product approvals. For sustainability leaders and CFOs, the message is unambiguous: EPR compliance for e-waste is no longer a back-office environmental obligation — it is a business continuity, investor relations, and supply chain resilience issue that deserves board-level attention.

title: Notice to Recyclers: What the CPCB's E-Waste Management Rules 2022 Compliance Crackdown Means for Your Business
summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent compliance notice directed at registered e-waste recyclers, signalling a tightening of enforcement under India's E-Waste Management Rules, 2022. Recyclers who fail to meet registration, reporting, and Extended Producer Responsibility (E

sections.t: Executive Summary The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent compliance notice directed at registered e-waste recyclers, signalling a tightening of enforcement under India's E-Waste Management Rules, 2022. Recyclers who fail to meet registration, reporting, and Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) certificate issuance obligations face the risk of licence suspension, penalties, and removal from the CPCB's authorised recycler portal. This notice is a clear signal that India's e-waste regulatory framework is moving from policy articulation to active enforcement — and the entire EPR value chain, from producers to recyclers, must take notice.

What Changed India's E-Waste Management Rules, 2022 replaced the earlier 2016 rules and introduced a significantly revamped Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework. Under this system, Producers, Importers, and Brand Owners (PIBOs) of electrical and electronic equipment are required to meet annual e-waste collection and recycling targets. To fulfil these targets, PIBOs purchase EPR certificates from CPCB-authorised recyclers and refurbishers who process end-of-life electronics. The CPCB's latest notice to recyclers indicates that a substantial number of authorised recyclers are not meeting their core compliance obligations — including filing annual returns, updating processing capacity data on the centralised EPR portal, issuing EPR certificates accurately, and maintaining documentation of e-waste received and processed. The notice serves as a formal warning: non-compliant recyclers risk having their authorisation cancelled, which would directly disrupt the EPR certificate supply that producers depend upon to demonstrate their own compliance. In plain terms, this is the CPCB pulling on a critical thread in the e-waste compliance ecosystem. If recyclers are not compliant, the entire EPR market — including the producers who rely on them — is exposed to regulatory risk.

Who Is Affected CPCB-authorised e-waste recyclers and dismantlers across all states, particularly those operating in major e-waste clusters such as Delhi-NCR, Mumbai, Bengaluru, Chennai, Hyderabad, and Pune. Producers, Importers, and Brand Owners (PIBOs) of electrical and electronic equipment — including consumer electronics, IT equipment, telecom gear, and large/small appliances — who depend on recycler-issued EPR certificates to meet annual compliance targets. E-waste aggregators and collection point operators whose downstream recycling partners may be at risk of losing authorisation. Electronics manufacturers with extended supply chains who have entered into EPR fulfilment agreements with third-party recycling agencies. EPR compliance management platforms and intermediaries facilitating certificate trading on the CPCB portal. State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs) responsible for issuing and monitoring recycler authorisations at the state level.

Key Obligations & Deadlines RECYCLERS — Portal Registration & Profile Update: Ensure your facility profile on the CPCB Centralised EPR Portal (https://eprnewportal.cpcb.gov.in) is current, including actual processing capacity, infrastructure details, and valid authorisation certificates from the relevant SPCB. RECYCLERS — Annual Returns Filing: File annual returns for the Financial Year 2023-24 immediately if not already submitted. Returns must include total e-waste received (quantity and category), processed quantities, EPR certificates issued, and residual waste disposal records. RECYCLERS — EPR Certificate Issuance Integrity: Issue EPR certificates only against verified and documented quantities of e-waste actually processed. Inflated or fraudulent certificate issuance is a primary trigger for CPCB enforcement action and can attract criminal liability under the Environment Protection Act. RECYCLERS — Traceability Documentation: Maintain an unbroken chain of custody — from collection/receipt of e-waste through dismantling, shredding, and downstream processing or safe disposal of hazardous residues. This documentation must be available for CPCB/SPCB inspection at any time. PRODUCERS/PIBOs — Recycler Due Diligence: Audit the compliance status of all recyclers from whom you are purchasing EPR certificates. A recycler whose authorisation is cancelled retroactively invalidates certificates already purchased — creating a compliance gap for your organisation. ALL PARTIES — Respond to CPCB Show-Cause Notices: If your organisation has received a specific show-cause notice as part of this enforcement action, a written response must be submitted within the timeframe specified in the notice (typically 15–30 days from receipt). Failure to respond is treated as admission of non-compliance. ONGOING — Quarterly Reporting: Under the 2022 Rules, recyclers are expected to update processing data on the portal on a quarterly basis. Ensure Q3 and Q4 FY2024-25 data is uploaded on schedule.

Climate Transition Angle E-waste recycling sits at the intersection of the circular economy and climate transition — yet this compliance notice exposes a critical gap between India's regulatory ambitions and ground-level implementation. The formal e-waste recycling sector is a cornerstone of India's resource efficiency strategy: responsible recycling of lithium, cobalt, copper, and rare earth elements from end-of-life electronics is essential for the domestic supply chains that will power the energy transition — from electric vehicles to solar panels and grid storage batteries. From a physical risk and supply chain perspective, the failure of the recycler ecosystem has cascading consequences. If authorised recyclers are non-compliant and lose their licences, e-waste flows back into the informal sector, where hazardous materials such as lead, mercury, cadmium, and brominated flame retardants are processed using methods that contaminate soil and groundwater — creating long-term physical risks for communities and ecosystems near informal processing clusters. For companies reporting under global frameworks, this matters significantly. The International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) under IFRS S2 requires disclosure of climate-related risks and opportunities across the value chain. E-waste management is increasingly being flagged as a Scope 3 end-of-life treatment issue. Similarly, the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and its European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS) require disclosure on circular economy practices and waste. Indian companies with EU market exposure or EU-based investors will face questions about whether their EPR obligations are being met through genuinely compliant, traceable recycling — not certificate arbitrage with non-compliant processors. The CPCB's enforcement action is therefore not just a domestic compliance matter — it is directly relevant to the integrity of ESG disclosures that Indian businesses are making to global investors and regulators.

What To Do Now IMMEDIATE RECYCLER AUDIT: If you are a PIBO, conduct an emergency compliance audit of every recycler in your EPR fulfilment network. Verify their current authorisation status on the CPCB portal, check their annual return filing status, and request copies of their processing logs. Do not wait for a CPCB notice to discover that your EPR certificates are backed by a non-compliant recycler. PORTAL RECONCILIATION: Log into the CPCB Centralised EPR Portal and reconcile all EPR certificates purchased against the recycler's reported processing quantities. Any discrepancies between certificates purchased and quantities processed should be flagged to your legal and compliance team immediately — this is where regulatory exposure concentrates. CONTRACTUAL PROTECTIONS: Review and strengthen your EPR fulfilment agreements with recyclers to include compliance warranties, audit rights, and indemnification clauses in the event of recycler authorisation cancellation. Ensure contracts require recyclers to notify you within 48 hours of any CPCB/SPCB show-cause notice or enforcement action. INTERNAL ESG DISCLOSURE REVIEW: If your company reports under BRSR (Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting), GRI, or is preparing for ISSB-aligned disclosures, review how e-waste EPR compliance is being characterised in your sustainability reports. Ensure disclosures accurately reflect the compliance status of your recycler network — not just your own certificate procurement. ENGAGE A COMPLIANCE SPECIALIST: Given the complexity of the EPR portal, annual return requirements, and the risk of retroactive certificate invalidation, companies without dedicated e-waste compliance resources should immediately engage a specialist consultant or legal advisor with CPCB e-waste experience to conduct a gap assessment and remediation plan.

Our Take This CPCB notice is more significant than it may appear at first glance. For years, India's e-waste EPR framework has been criticised for creating a compliance-on-paper ecosystem — where certificates are traded without corresponding real-world recycling activity, and where the informal sector continues to process the vast majority of India's 1.6 million metric tonnes of annual e-waste generation. The CPCB's direct targeting of recyclers is a meaningful escalation: it attacks the supply side of the EPR certificate market, which, if enforced rigorously, will force a reckoning across the entire value chain. Producers who have been treating EPR as a certificate-buying exercise rather than a genuine material recovery programme will find themselves exposed. We expect this enforcement wave to intensify through 2025, with the CPCB likely publishing a list of non-compliant recyclers and potentially suspending portal access for defaulters. The companies that act now — by auditing their recycler networks, strengthening contracts, and building genuine traceability into their EPR programmes — will not only avoid regulatory risk but will be best positioned as India moves toward stricter e-waste targets and as global ESG reporting standards demand credible evidence of circular economy practices.

title: Notice to Registered Recyclers under E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022: What It Means for EPR Compliance in India
summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent notice directed at registered recyclers operating under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, signalling heightened regulatory scrutiny of India's formal e-waste recycling ecosystem. This notice is a clear indicator that CPCB is moving

sections.t: Executive Summary The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent notice directed at registered recyclers operating under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, signalling heightened regulatory scrutiny of India's formal e-waste recycling ecosystem. This notice is a clear indicator that CPCB is moving to tighten enforcement of Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) obligations across the e-waste value chain — from producers and importers to brand owners and their authorised recycling partners. Companies that have not yet aligned their EPR compliance programmes with the 2022 Rules should treat this as an urgent call to action.

What Changed The E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 replaced the earlier 2016 framework and introduced a significantly more structured EPR regime, including the creation of a centralised EPR portal managed by CPCB, mandatory annual EPR targets for producers and importers, and stricter registration and reporting requirements for recyclers and refurbishers. Under the 2022 Rules, registered recyclers are not passive participants — they are accountable entities required to maintain detailed records of e-waste collected, processed, and channelled, and to report this data accurately on the CPCB EPR portal. The issuance of this urgent notice to registered recyclers suggests that CPCB has identified compliance gaps — likely including underreporting, non-submission of quarterly or annual returns, discrepancies between declared processing capacities and actual throughput, or failure to renew registrations on time. While the full text of the notice was not publicly extractable at the time of writing, urgent notices of this nature from CPCB typically serve one or more of the following purposes: directing recyclers to update or verify registration details on the EPR portal, warning of cancellation of registration for non-compliant entities, demanding submission of overdue processing certificates or annual returns, or flagging audits of facility capacities and environmental compliance.

Who Is Affected Registered e-waste recyclers and dismantlers authorised under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 Producers, importers, and brand owners (PIBOs) of electrical and electronic equipment who have EPR obligations and rely on registered recyclers to meet their annual collection and recycling targets Producer Responsibility Organisations (PROs) that facilitate EPR compliance on behalf of multiple producers Refurbishers of electrical and electronic equipment registered under the 2022 Rules Original Equipment Manufacturers (OEMs) in sectors including consumer electronics, IT hardware, telecom equipment, white goods, and industrial electronics E-commerce platforms and retailers with obligations under the rules as importers or brand owners Environmental compliance and legal teams at any company with products listed under Schedule I of the E-Waste Rules

Key Obligations & Deadlines Registered recyclers must ensure their CPCB EPR portal registration is current, accurate, and reflects actual facility capacity — update any outdated information immediately Recyclers are required to submit quarterly processing reports on the CPCB EPR portal; verify that all pending submissions for FY 2023-24 and FY 2024-25 are completed without further delay Annual EPR target fulfilment certificates must be issued to PIBOs and PROs in a timely manner — recyclers should audit all outstanding certificates and issue them promptly Producers and importers (PIBOs) must verify that their empanelled recyclers remain validly registered; relying on a recycler whose registration has lapsed or is under scrutiny creates direct EPR compliance risk for the PIBO PROs should immediately cross-check the registration status of all recyclers in their network against the CPCB portal and flag any anomalies to their PIBO clients All recycling facilities should ensure they are audit-ready — physical infrastructure, pollution control equipment, and record-keeping must align with declared capacity and the conditions of their authorisation Watch for any individual show-cause notices or directions that may follow this broader urgent notice; response timelines in such cases are typically 15–30 days and must not be missed

Climate Transition Angle E-waste compliance is far more than a domestic regulatory checkbox — it sits at the intersection of India's circular economy ambitions, global supply chain transparency requirements, and climate transition strategy. The extraction of virgin metals and rare earth elements used in electronics is among the most carbon- and water-intensive industrial processes on earth. Effective e-waste recycling — when done through verified, formal channels — directly reduces demand for primary resource extraction, lowering lifecycle emissions of electronic products. For multinational companies operating in India or sourcing from Indian suppliers, the integrity of the domestic recycling ecosystem is increasingly relevant to their own climate and ESG disclosures. Frameworks such as the International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) IFRS S2 and the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) require companies to assess and disclose Scope 3 emissions and supply chain environmental risks — which explicitly includes end-of-life product management. If recyclers in a company's EPR network are found to be non-compliant or operating below environmental standards, this creates both a disclosure risk and a reputational liability in global markets. India's own SEBI Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report (BRSR) framework, now mandatory for the top 1,000 listed companies, requires disclosure of waste management practices including e-waste. The CPCB's tightening of recycler oversight directly supports the credibility of the data that companies will need to report under BRSR Core from FY 2024-25 onwards. Companies that have built their EPR compliance on informal or under-documented recycling relationships are exposed to data quality risks that will surface in sustainability reporting cycles.

What To Do Now Audit your recycler network immediately: If you are a PIBO or PRO, log into the CPCB EPR portal today and verify the registration status, capacity authorisation, and compliance history of every recycler in your network. Remove or replace any entity whose registration is lapsed, suspended, or flagged. Reconcile your EPR target fulfilment records: Cross-check the volume of e-waste channelled to recyclers against the EPR certificates received. Any gaps between claimed and documented recycling volumes must be resolved before they attract regulatory attention or invalidate your annual EPR filings. Engage your recycling partners directly: Contact your empanelled recyclers to confirm they have received and are responding to the CPCB notice. A proactive conversation now is far less costly than discovering mid-year that a key recycling partner has had their registration cancelled. Review your BRSR and sustainability disclosure pipeline: Ensure that e-waste data being prepared for BRSR, CDP, or CSRD disclosures is sourced from CPCB-verified, compliant recyclers. Sustainability reports that cite EPR fulfilment through recyclers later found to be non-compliant carry restatement and reputational risk. Consult legal and environmental counsel: If your organisation has received this notice directly as a registered recycler, do not delay in seeking expert guidance on the appropriate response. Frame your reply carefully, provide complete documentation, and treat the notice as an opportunity to demonstrate full compliance rather than as a routine administrative matter.

Our Take This notice is a meaningful signal that CPCB is graduating from rule-setting to rule-enforcing in the e-waste space — and that the era of passive registration without active compliance is over. India generates over 1.6 million metric tonnes of e-waste annually and ranks among the top five e-waste generating nations globally, yet the proportion channelled through formal, environmentally sound recycling remains stubbornly low. The 2022 Rules were designed to change this, and CPCB's escalating enforcement posture — of which this notice is one data point — reflects both the urgency of the problem and growing institutional capacity to act on it. For businesses, the message is clear: EPR compliance in e-waste can no longer be delegated and forgotten. It requires ongoing due diligence of recycling partners, real-time data management on the EPR portal, and integration with broader ESG reporting systems. Companies that invest in building robust, auditable EPR programmes now will be far better positioned as regulatory enforcement intensifies and as global customers and investors increasingly demand credible, verified sustainability data from their Indian counterparts.

title: Notice to Registered Recyclers under E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022: What It Means for Your EPR Compliance
summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent notice directed at registered recyclers operating under India's E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, signalling heightened regulatory scrutiny of the extended producer responsibility (EPR) ecosystem. This notice is a clear indicator that C

sections.t: Executive Summary The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent notice directed at registered recyclers operating under India's E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, signalling heightened regulatory scrutiny of the extended producer responsibility (EPR) ecosystem. This notice is a clear indicator that CPCB is actively monitoring compliance performance — including recycling volumes, certificate issuance, and data accuracy — across the e-waste value chain. Producers, importers, brand owners, and their recycling partners must treat this as an immediate prompt to audit their EPR obligations and recycler relationships.

What Changed India's E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 overhauled the previous 2016 framework by introducing a market-based EPR certificate trading system, mandatory online registration on the CPCB EPR portal, and clearly defined responsibilities for producers, dismantlers, and recyclers. Under this framework, registered recyclers are required to process e-waste collected through authorised channels, upload processing data on the centralised portal, and issue EPR certificates to producers as proof of fulfilment. The CPCB's urgent notice to registered recyclers suggests that the regulator has identified systemic issues — potentially including discrepancies in reported recycling quantities, non-submission of quarterly or annual returns, irregularities in EPR certificate generation, or failure to meet minimum infrastructure and environmental standards. While the full text of the notice was not publicly extracted, such urgent communications from CPCB typically serve as a final compliance warning before punitive action, which can include suspension or cancellation of recycler registration. For the broader EPR ecosystem, this matters enormously: if a recycler's registration is suspended, any EPR certificates previously purchased from them may be rendered invalid, directly exposing the producers and importers who relied on those certificates to non-compliance liability under the Rules.

Who Is Affected Registered e-waste recyclers and dismantlers holding CPCB authorisation under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 Producers of electrical and electronic equipment (EEE) — including consumer electronics, IT and telecom equipment, and large/small household appliances — who have EPR obligations and have procured certificates from recyclers Importers and brand owners of EEE who are registered on the CPCB EPR portal and rely on recycler certificates for annual compliance fulfilment Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) compliance managers and environmental officers within manufacturing companies Third-party EPR compliance aggregators and intermediaries who facilitate certificate trading between producers and recyclers Investors and lenders with exposure to e-waste recycling businesses or EEE manufacturers with significant EPR liabilities
