Key Obligations & Deadlines Registered recyclers must ensure all processing data — including quantities received, processed, and residue disposed — is accurately uploaded on the CPCB EPR portal without delay, as data gaps are a primary trigger for regulatory notices Recyclers must verify that EPR certificates issued to producers accurately reflect actual e-waste processed and are not inflated or duplicated — certificate fraud is treated as a serious violation under the Rules All recyclers must maintain valid consent-to-operate from their respective State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs) and ensure facility-level standards (storage, processing, effluent management) are audit-ready at all times Quarterly and annual returns must be filed on the CPCB portal within prescribed timelines — recyclers should immediately check for any overdue submissions and file pending reports Producers and importers should urgently verify the active registration status of all recyclers from whom they have procured EPR certificates in the current compliance year, using the public CPCB EPR portal database Any recycler that has received a direct notice from CPCB should seek legal and environmental compliance counsel immediately and respond within the stated notice period — typically 15 to 30 days — to avoid escalation to registration cancellation Producers whose recycler partners are under scrutiny should proactively engage alternate authorised recyclers to ensure their annual EPR targets remain on track ahead of the March 31 financial year-end deadline

Climate Transition Angle E-waste recycling is not merely a regulatory checkbox — it sits at the heart of India's circular economy and critical mineral security strategy. Electronic devices contain recoverable quantities of gold, silver, copper, cobalt, and rare earth elements, all of which are essential inputs for clean energy technologies including solar panels, EV batteries, and wind turbines. A well-functioning e-waste recycling sector directly reduces India's dependence on virgin mining and imported critical minerals, both of which carry substantial carbon and supply chain risk. From a global standards perspective, the ISSB's IFRS S2 climate disclosure standard and the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) increasingly require companies to account for Scope 3 emissions — which includes the end-of-life treatment of products sold. Companies that cannot demonstrate responsible, verified end-of-life recycling through credible partners risk both regulatory exposure under Indian law and reputational damage in export markets where customers and investors are demanding supply chain transparency. Physical climate risk is also relevant here: e-waste recycling facilities located in flood-prone or heat-stressed regions face operational disruptions that can cascade into compliance failures for the producers who depend on them. Companies should be mapping the climate vulnerability of their recycler networks as part of broader supply chain resilience planning — a practice that is becoming standard under TCFD-aligned disclosure frameworks increasingly referenced by SEBI's Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting (BRSR) requirements.

What To Do Now Audit your recycler roster immediately: Log into the CPCB EPR portal and verify the current registration status, certificate issuance history, and compliance standing of every recycler from whom your organisation has procured EPR certificates in FY 2024-25 — do not wait for official intimation of a problem Cross-check EPR certificate volumes against actual recycling capacity: If your recyclers have issued certificates significantly in excess of their reported processing capacity or installed throughput, this is a red flag — conduct a desk-based due diligence review and consider physical verification visits File all pending returns and close data gaps: Whether you are a recycler with overdue portal submissions or a producer with unreconciled EPR fulfilment data, use this notice as a trigger to bring all filings current before the financial year end Diversify your recycler base and build contractual safeguards: Dependence on a single recycler for large volumes of EPR fulfilment is a concentration risk — identify and onboard backup authorised recyclers, and include contractual clauses requiring recyclers to notify you of any regulatory actions against them Integrate e-waste EPR into your BRSR and ESG disclosures: Document your EPR compliance methodology, recycler due diligence process, and certificate verification practices — this strengthens your BRSR Core and Comprehensive disclosures and positions your organisation well for future alignment with ISSB S2 value chain disclosure expectations

Our Take This CPCB notice is a significant signal that India's e-waste EPR regime is maturing from a paper-compliance framework into one with real enforcement teeth. The regulator has built the digital infrastructure — the centralised EPR portal, certificate trading mechanisms, and data dashboards — and is now clearly moving into an active verification and enforcement phase. We expect this to be followed by more targeted show-cause notices, potential registration cancellations of non-compliant recyclers, and possibly sector-wide audits in the coming months. For producers and importers, the lesson is unambiguous: your EPR compliance is only as strong as the weakest link in your recycler network. Companies that have been treating EPR certificate procurement as a procurement transaction rather than a compliance obligation will find themselves exposed. The time to build rigorous recycler due diligence, real-time portal monitoring, and contingency planning into your EPR programme is now — before CPCB's scrutiny reaches your doorstep.

title: Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016 (Amended 2018): What Every Producer, Brand Owner, and Importer Must Know
summary: The Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016, as amended in 2018, represent India's most comprehensive regulatory framework for reducing, managing, and recycling plastic waste — placing legal obligations squarely on producers, brand owners, importers, manufacturers, local bodies, and waste generators al

sections.t: Executive Summary The Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016, as amended in 2018, represent India's most comprehensive regulatory framework for reducing, managing, and recycling plastic waste — placing legal obligations squarely on producers, brand owners, importers, manufacturers, local bodies, and waste generators alike. The rules enforce the Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) principle, mandating that those who profit from plastic products must bear the cost of managing them at end-of-life. For businesses operating in India, non-compliance is not merely a regulatory risk — it is increasingly a reputational, operational, and climate-transition risk that demands board-level attention.

What Changed The Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016, superseded the earlier Plastic Waste (Management and Handling) Rules, 2011, and were further strengthened by the 2018 amendment. The overhaul was driven by the need to move beyond compliance-on-paper toward a functioning circular economy for plastics in India. In plain terms, the rules did three significant things. First, they widened the net of who is responsible — from just manufacturers to include brand owners, importers, producers, local bodies, Gram Panchayats, and institutional waste generators such as hospitals, hotels, malls, schools, and government offices. Second, they embedded the 'polluter pays' principle by formalising Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR), making producers legally accountable for the environmentally sound management of their plastic products through their entire lifecycle — including post-consumer waste collection and recycling. Third, they introduced clearer definitions for critical terms — including multi-layered packaging (MLP), compostable plastics, energy recovery, and alternate use — to close loopholes and ensure that a wider range of plastic materials falls under regulatory oversight. The 2018 amendment further tightened these provisions, particularly around registration, EPR implementation timelines, and the inclusion of street vendors and waste pickers as recognised participants in the waste management value chain — a progressive move that formalises the role of India's informal recycling economy.

Who Is Affected Producers: Any entity manufacturing or importing carry bags, plastic sheets, multi-layered packaging (MLP), or similar plastic products for sale or use in India. Brand Owners: Companies selling any commodity under a registered brand label that uses plastic packaging — this includes FMCG, food & beverage, pharmaceuticals, e-commerce, and consumer durables sectors. Importers: Any entity holding an Importer-Exporter Code (IEC) that brings plastic products or plastic-packaged goods into India. Manufacturers of Plastic Raw Materials: Entities producing plastic polymers and resins used as raw materials by downstream producers. Institutional Waste Generators: Occupiers of offices (government and private), hospitals, schools, colleges, hotels, restaurants, malls, and shopping complexes. Local Bodies & Gram Panchayats: Municipal corporations, nagar panchayats, and village-level bodies responsible for waste collection, segregation, and processing infrastructure. Waste Generators (General): Households, commercial establishments, and industrial units that generate plastic waste. Note: Export-Oriented Units (EOUs) and Special Economic Zone (SEZ) units are partially exempt from Rule 4 — but this exemption does NOT apply to units packaging gutkha, tobacco, or pan masala, nor to surplus or rejected products.

Key Obligations & Deadlines EPR Registration: Producers, brand owners, and importers must register with the State Pollution Control Board (SPCB) or Pollution Control Committee (PCC) to obtain consent to establish and operate facilities handling plastic waste. Minimum Thickness Compliance: Carry bags must meet prescribed minimum thickness standards (as notified separately by MoEFCC/CPCB) to ensure recyclability — non-compliant carry bags are prohibited from manufacture, import, stocking, distribution, sale, and use. Source Segregation: Waste generators — including institutional generators such as offices, hotels, hospitals, and malls — are obligated to segregate plastic waste at the point of generation and hand it over to authorised waste collectors or recyclers. EPR Plan Implementation: Producers, brand owners, and importers must establish systems for collection, channelisation, and recycling of post-consumer plastic waste, in accordance with EPR guidelines issued by CPCB. This includes setting up or partnering with Producer Responsibility Organisations (PROs). Multi-Layered Packaging (MLP) Phase-Out: MLPs that are non-recyclable, non-energy-recoverable, or have no alternate use must be phased out. Producers using MLP must establish a collection-back system for used packaging. Local Body Obligations: Urban local bodies and Gram Panchayats must establish systems and infrastructure for collection, segregation, storage, transportation, processing, and disposal of plastic waste within their jurisdictions. Plastic Waste Processors: Recyclers, co-processors (cement kilns, waste-to-energy), and processors must register with the SPCB/PCC and operate only in accordance with prescribed environmental conditions. Labelling Requirements: Producers of plastic carry bags and multi-layered packaging must ensure products carry prescribed labels indicating material composition, recyclability status, and manufacturer details. Street Vendor Compliance: Street vendors using plastic carry bags must comply with prescribed standards on thickness and type of plastic used. Ongoing Reporting: Producers, importers, and brand owners must maintain records of plastic raw material used and plastic waste managed, and submit annual returns to the prescribed authority.

Climate Transition Angle Plastic waste management sits at the intersection of two of the most urgent dimensions of the climate crisis: the carbon footprint of virgin plastic production and the physical risk of plastic pollution to ecosystems that underpin economic activity. Globally, plastics are responsible for approximately 3.4% of annual greenhouse gas emissions — the majority of which come from the production of virgin fossil-fuel-based polymers. Every tonne of plastic that is collected and recycled rather than landfilled or incinerated represents a direct reduction in the demand for virgin plastic and, therefore, in Scope 3 emissions for brand owners and retailers. For Indian businesses, this has immediate relevance to global supply chain compliance. The European Union's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and the ISSB's IFRS S2 climate disclosure standards are accelerating requirements for multinational buyers to assess and report on the plastic and packaging footprint of their entire value chain — including Indian suppliers and contract manufacturers. Indian exporters who cannot demonstrate compliant plastic waste management practices, EPR registration, and measurable recycled content in their packaging risk losing access to European and North American markets. Furthermore, physical climate risks — including flooding amplified by choked waterways and drainage systems due to plastic waste — directly threaten industrial facilities, logistics infrastructure, and agricultural supply chains across India. The CPCB's own data consistently shows that uncollected plastic waste is a primary contributor to urban flooding in cities like Mumbai, Chennai, and Kolkata. For real estate, insurance, and infrastructure sectors, this is already a material financial risk. Robust compliance with the Plastic Waste Management Rules is therefore not merely a legal obligation — it is a forward-looking risk management strategy aligned with India's NDC commitments and the global net-zero transition.

What To Do Now Conduct a Plastic Footprint Audit: Map all plastic materials used across your products, packaging, and operations — including carry bags, MLP, plastic sheets, and single-use plastics. Quantify volumes by material type and assess current recycled content versus virgin plastic use. This audit forms the baseline for EPR compliance planning and voluntary sustainability disclosures under frameworks such as BRSR (Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting) and ISSB S2. Register Under EPR Immediately if Not Already Done: Producers, brand owners, and importers who have not yet registered on the CPCB's centralised EPR portal (https://eprnp.cpcb.gov.in) should do so without delay. Failure to register is a direct violation of the Rules and exposes companies to enforcement action by SPCBs, including closure notices and penalties under the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986. Establish or Join a Producer Responsibility Organisation (PRO): Rather than building an individual plastic waste collection system from scratch, evaluate partnerships with CPCB-registered PROs or industry-level plastic waste management consortia. PROs provide economies of scale for collection, aggregation, and recycling — particularly critical for mid-sized producers and brand owners who lack the operational infrastructure to manage EPR obligations independently. Phase Out Non-Recyclable Multi-Layered Packaging: Begin a time-bound packaging redesign process to eliminate MLPs that have no recycling or energy recovery pathway. Engage packaging suppliers and R&D teams to identify mono-material or easily recyclable alternatives. Align this with global packaging sustainability commitments (e.g., Ellen MacArthur Foundation's Global Commitment) to strengthen ESG positioning with international investors and buyers. Train Compliance and Operations Teams: Ensure that procurement, operations, and facility management teams — particularly at institutional waste generator sites such as offices, factories, warehouses, hotels, and canteens — are trained on source segregation obligations, authorised waste handover procedures, and record-keeping requirements. Designate a Plastic Waste Management Officer responsible for internal compliance monitoring and annual return filings with the SPCB.

Our Take The Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016 (as amended 2018) were a landmark step — but enforcement has historically been uneven, and many producers and brand owners have treated EPR as a checkbox exercise rather than a genuine operational commitment. That era is ending. CPCB and SPCBs have significantly stepped up scrutiny, the EPR portal has made non-compliance easier to identify, and global trade partners are embedding plastic accountability into procurement contracts and supplier codes of conduct. More importantly, the MoEFCC's subsequent notifications — including the Plastic Waste Management (Amendment) Rules, 2021 and 2022 — have dramatically expanded the scope of banned single-use plastics and raised EPR targets, building directly on the 2016/2018 framework. Companies that have not yet internalised these rules into their core business strategy — rather than treating them as a purely legal or government-affairs matter — are accumulating both regulatory and reputational liability. The direction of travel is unambiguous: India is tightening its plastic governance regime in lockstep with global standards, and businesses that move early on EPR compliance, packaging redesign, and plastic footprint disclosure will hold a meaningful competitive advantage in both domestic and international markets in the years ahead.

title: Recyclers & Refurbishers under E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022: What the Latest CPCB Notice Means for Your Business
summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent notice targeting recyclers and refurbishers operating under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, signalling heightened enforcement attention on Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) obligations across the electronics value chain. Recy

sections.t: Executive Summary The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent notice targeting recyclers and refurbishers operating under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, signalling heightened enforcement attention on Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) obligations across the electronics value chain. Recyclers and refurbishers who have not yet registered on the EPR portal, filed returns, or met channelisation targets face immediate compliance risk, including potential suspension of authorisations. Companies across the electronics, IT hardware, consumer durables, and telecom sectors — both as producers and as their downstream recycling partners — must treat this notice as a call to audit their EPR ecosystems without delay.

What Changed The E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 — which replaced the 2016 framework — significantly expanded the Extended Producer Responsibility architecture in India. Under the new rules, producers, manufacturers, refurbishers, and recyclers are each assigned distinct legal obligations: producers must meet annual EPR targets by procuring EPR certificates from registered recyclers; recyclers must obtain CPCB authorisation, operate within approved capacities, and generate EPR certificates only against verifiable e-waste processed; and refurbishers must register, report refurbished volumes, and channel residual waste to authorised recyclers. The CPCB's latest urgent notice is directed specifically at recyclers and refurbishers, suggesting that the Board has identified widespread non-compliance in this segment — whether through missed registration deadlines, underreporting of processed volumes, unauthorised capacity expansion, or failure to upload EPR certificates on the centralised portal. This type of urgent notice from CPCB typically precedes show-cause notices, cancellation of authorisations, and in serious cases, referral to State Pollution Control Boards for on-ground enforcement action. The message is unambiguous: the era of passive compliance in e-waste recycling is over.

Who Is Affected Authorised E-Waste Recyclers: Entities processing end-of-life electronics and generating EPR certificates for producers — directly in the crosshairs of this notice. Refurbishers: Companies that repair, refurbish, and resell used electronic and electrical equipment (EEE) and are required to register under the 2022 Rules and manage residual waste responsibly. Electronics & IT Hardware Producers: OEMs, importers, and brand owners whose EPR compliance depends on the validity and volume of certificates procured from recyclers — if your recycler is non-compliant, your EPR targets are at risk. Consumer Durables & Telecom Equipment Manufacturers: Companies selling products listed under Schedule I of the E-Waste Rules, obligated to channel e-waste through authorised recyclers. Bulk Consumers & Dismantlers: Large institutional users of EEE (IT companies, banks, government entities) and dismantlers in the informal-to-formal transition pipeline who interact with the recycling ecosystem. EPR Compliance Aggregators & PROs: Producer Responsibility Organisations facilitating EPR certificate procurement on behalf of multiple producers — whose entire service model depends on recycler authorisation status.

Key Obligations & Deadlines Registration on CPCB EPR Portal (Immediate): All recyclers and refurbishers must be registered on the centralised EPR portal (https://eprnewportal.cpcb.gov.in). Unregistered entities must apply immediately and halt operations pending authorisation. Annual Returns Filing: Recyclers and refurbishers are required to file annual returns on the portal detailing volumes of e-waste collected, processed, and EPR certificates generated. Entities that have missed filings for FY 2022-23 or FY 2023-24 must rectify this without delay. EPR Certificate Generation & Reconciliation: Recyclers must ensure EPR certificates are generated only against actual e-waste processed and reconciled with producer accounts on the portal. Mismatches or inflated certificate issuance are a key CPCB enforcement focus. Capacity Compliance: Recyclers must operate strictly within CPCB-authorised processing capacities. Any expansion requires prior amendment of authorisation — operating beyond approved capacity is a violation. Channelisation Documentation: Refurbishers must maintain records of all e-waste channelled to authorised recyclers and upload proof of disposal on the EPR portal. Residual waste cannot be handed to informal/unauthorised handlers. Response to CPCB Notice (Urgent): Any entity that has received a direct communication from CPCB must respond within the stipulated timeframe (typically 15–30 days) with documentary evidence of compliance. Failure to respond is treated as admission of non-compliance. State-Level Authorisation Renewal: Recyclers must also ensure their State Pollution Control Board (SPCB) authorisations under the Hazardous and Other Wastes Rules are current — CPCB and SPCB actions are often coordinated.

Climate Transition Angle E-waste regulation sits at the intersection of circular economy principles and climate transition strategy — and this is increasingly recognised by global investors and standard-setters. The ISSB's IFRS S2 standard and the EU's CSRD both require companies to disclose how their value chains — including end-of-life product management — contribute to or mitigate climate risk. India's own SEBI Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting (BRSR) framework, mandatory for the top 1,000 listed companies, requires disclosure of waste management practices including e-waste under Principle 2 (Sustainable & Safe Products and Processes). From a physical risk standpoint, the informal e-waste sector — which handles an estimated 80–90% of India's e-waste — releases toxic materials and greenhouse gases, contributing to localised environmental degradation and long-term supply chain risk for critical minerals like lithium, cobalt, and rare earths. Formalising the recycling ecosystem through rules like these is therefore not just a compliance exercise — it is foundational to building the domestic circular material flows that India's clean energy transition (batteries, solar panels, EVs) will depend on. For multinational companies sourcing from or selling into India, due diligence requirements under the EU Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive (CSDDD) will increasingly require evidence of responsible end-of-life management in supply chains. Indian recyclers and producers who cannot demonstrate CPCB-compliant operations risk being excluded from global supply chains as these requirements come into force between 2026 and 2028.

What To Do Now Audit Your Recycler & Refurbisher Network Immediately: Producers and PROs should pull the current authorisation status of every recycler in their EPR chain from the CPCB portal. Any recycler with lapsed, suspended, or missing authorisation puts your EPR certificate holdings at legal risk — act before CPCB does. Verify Portal Compliance End-to-End: Log into the CPCB EPR portal and confirm that all annual returns are filed, EPR certificates are reconciled, and no discrepancies exist between declared processing volumes and certificates issued. Engage your portal administrator or compliance consultant to conduct a full data audit. Respond to Any CPCB Communications Proactively: If your organisation has received any notice, advisory, or query from CPCB — even a routine one — treat it as urgent. Prepare a structured compliance response with documentary evidence of authorisation, processing records, and return filings. Legal counsel with environmental regulatory experience should review all responses. Assess BRSR & ESG Disclosure Implications: For listed companies, check whether your FY 2024-25 BRSR disclosures accurately reflect your e-waste EPR status. Gaps between reported compliance and actual portal status are a growing area of ESG investor scrutiny and could attract regulatory attention. Engage with Informal-to-Formal Transition Planning: If your supply chain relies on informal recyclers or dismantlers, begin a structured transition plan to channel e-waste exclusively through CPCB-authorised entities. Document this transition roadmap as part of your climate and circular economy commitments under ISSB/CSRD-aligned reporting.

Our Take This notice is a clear indicator that CPCB is moving from framework-building to active enforcement in India's e-waste sector — and the timing is deliberate. With EPR portals now operational, data on recycler performance is visible to regulators in near real-time, removing the ambiguity that allowed non-compliance to persist under the old paper-based system. What we are witnessing is the natural maturation of India's EPR architecture: the rules are in place, the digital infrastructure exists, and regulators are now using it. Companies — both producers dependent on recycler certificates and recyclers themselves — that treated EPR as a box-ticking exercise are now exposed. More importantly, this enforcement push is a preview of things to come: India is expected to progressively tighten e-waste targets, expand the product scope of the rules, and align its framework more closely with global circular economy standards as pressure from BRSR, ISSB, and trade partners intensifies. The companies that invest now in building genuinely compliant, transparent, and resilient EPR ecosystems will not just avoid regulatory penalties — they will be positioned to capture the commercial opportunity of a formalised, growing circular economy in one of the world's largest electronics markets.

title: Recyclers & Refurbishers under E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022: What the Latest CPCB Notice Means for Your Business
summary: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent notice targeting recyclers and refurbishers operating under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, signalling heightened regulatory scrutiny of downstream EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility) value chain actors. This notice reinforces

sections.t: Executive Summary The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) has issued an urgent notice targeting recyclers and refurbishers operating under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, signalling heightened regulatory scrutiny of downstream EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility) value chain actors. This notice reinforces that compliance obligations under India's e-waste framework extend well beyond producers and importers — recyclers and refurbishers are now firmly in the regulatory spotlight. Companies in the e-waste processing ecosystem must act immediately to audit their registrations, EPR certificates, and reporting obligations to avoid penalties, suspension, or cancellation of authorisations.

What Changed India's E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 — which replaced the earlier 2016 Rules — overhauled the country's approach to managing end-of-life electronics by introducing a formalised EPR framework administered through a centralised online portal managed by CPCB. Under this framework, producers, importers, and brand owners (PIBOs) are required to meet annual e-waste collection and recycling targets, but they can only fulfil these targets by channelling waste through CPCB-registered recyclers and refurbishers. The latest CPCB urgent notice appears to address non-compliance, irregular reporting, or operational irregularities among registered recyclers and refurbishers — entities that serve as the critical last mile in the EPR chain. This could encompass issues such as failure to update capacity declarations, discrepancies in EPR certificate issuance, non-submission of quarterly or annual returns, or operation beyond the scope of granted authorisations. The notice is a clear signal that CPCB is moving from framework-building to active enforcement, and that recyclers and refurbishers can no longer treat compliance as a back-office formality.

Who Is Affected CPCB-registered e-waste recyclers processing IT equipment, consumer electronics, large and small household appliances, lighting equipment, and other notified categories under the 2022 Rules Refurbishers of electrical and electronic equipment (EEE) who are registered or required to register under the 2022 Rules Collection centres, dismantlers, and aggregators that feed into the formal recycling chain and may hold ancillary authorisations Producers, Importers, and Brand Owners (PIBOs) who depend on registered recycler and refurbisher networks to fulfil their annual EPR targets — their compliance is directly tied to the legitimacy of their recycling partners EPR compliance service providers and intermediaries managing EPR certificate transactions on behalf of PIBOs State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs) and State Pollution Control Committees (SPCCs) that co-regulate authorisations at the state level

Key Obligations & Deadlines Registration & Authorisation Validity: Recyclers and refurbishers must ensure their CPCB registration and state-level authorisation (from SPCB/SPCC) are current, accurate, and reflect actual operational capacity — any lapse or mismatch must be rectified immediately EPR Certificate Issuance: Recyclers are obligated to generate and transfer EPR certificates to PIBOs through the CPCB EPR portal only for quantities actually processed and verified — fraudulent or inflated certificate issuance is a specific enforcement target Quarterly & Annual Returns: Submission of processing data, tonnage handled, and waste disposal records via the CPCB online portal must be completed within prescribed timelines — entities in default should file outstanding returns without delay Capacity Declaration Accuracy: Declared processing capacity must match on-ground infrastructure; CPCB inspections are increasingly being used to verify physical capacity against portal declarations Compliance with Notified E-Waste Categories: Recyclers must process only the categories of EEE for which they hold valid authorisation — processing unauthorised categories is a violation that can trigger cancellation of registration Response to CPCB Notices: Any entity that has received a show-cause notice or direction under this urgent notice must respond within the stipulated timeframe (typically 15–30 days) with documentary evidence of compliance or a corrective action plan Downstream Disposal Records: Recyclers must maintain auditable records of how residual waste, hazardous fractions, and recovered materials are disposed of or sold, in compliance with Hazardous Waste Management Rules

Climate Transition Angle E-waste regulation is not merely a domestic compliance matter — it sits at the intersection of India's climate commitments, circular economy transition, and global supply chain sustainability standards. The formal recycling of e-waste is essential for recovering critical minerals such as cobalt, lithium, copper, and rare earth elements that are foundational to clean energy technologies, including electric vehicles, solar panels, and energy storage systems. A well-functioning, compliant recycler ecosystem directly supports India's energy transition by reducing dependence on virgin mineral extraction, which carries significant carbon and ecological costs. From a physical climate risk perspective, informal or non-compliant e-waste recycling — which this notice implicitly targets by strengthening formal sector oversight — contributes to soil and groundwater contamination, particularly in climate-vulnerable geographies. As India faces increasing climate stress, protecting land and water resources through proper e-waste processing is a material environmental risk management imperative. On the global standards front, multinational companies sourcing from or operating in India are increasingly required under frameworks such as the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD), the ISSB's IFRS S2 climate disclosures, and the EU Battery Regulation to demonstrate responsible end-of-life management of products placed on global markets. Indian recyclers and refurbishers that achieve and maintain strong CPCB compliance will be better positioned to serve as verified partners in these global supply chains. Conversely, non-compliant recyclers risk being excluded from the formal EPR ecosystem entirely, losing access to the growing volume of e-waste being channelled through regulated routes.

What To Do Now Conduct an immediate compliance audit: Review your CPCB portal account, registration status, authorisation expiry dates, and all pending return filings — identify and close any gaps before a regulatory inspection or notice reaches you Reconcile EPR certificate data: Cross-check all EPR certificates issued against actual processing records and tonnage data; flag and investigate any discrepancies, and do not issue certificates for unprocessed or unverified quantities Engage your legal and environmental compliance team: If you have received a CPCB notice or show-cause order, engage qualified environmental law counsel immediately and prepare a factual, documented response within the prescribed timeline Upgrade your data management systems: Invest in digital record-keeping for intake, processing, residue disposal, and certificate issuance — CPCB's enforcement is increasingly data-driven, and paper-based systems will not withstand scrutiny If you are a PIBO, verify your recycling partners now: Audit the registration and compliance status of every recycler and refurbisher in your EPR network — your own EPR target fulfilment is only as strong as the legitimacy of the certificates your partners generate

Our Take This CPCB notice is a watershed moment for India's formal e-waste sector. Since the 2022 Rules were notified, the EPR certificate market has grown rapidly — but so have concerns about certificate fraud, capacity inflation, and shell recyclers issuing credits without genuine processing activity. CPCB's decision to issue an urgent notice specifically targeting recyclers and refurbishers signals that the regulator is no longer willing to allow the formal framework to be undermined by bad actors. We expect this to be the beginning of a sustained enforcement wave: inspections will increase, authorisations of non-compliant entities will be suspended or cancelled, and the knock-on effect will be felt by PIBOs whose EPR targets rest on certificates from now-invalidated recyclers. The medium-term outcome — a leaner, more credible formal recycling sector — is the right direction for India's circular economy ambitions. But the short-term disruption will be real, and only those recyclers and refurbishers who have invested in genuine compliance will emerge stronger. For producers and brands, this is also a wake-up call to treat recycler due diligence as a core procurement and ESG risk management function, not an afterthought.

title: SASB Oil & Gas Standards Revision: What Carbon Tracker's ISSB Submission Means for India's Upstream, Midstream, and Integrated Energy Majors
summary: Carbon Tracker, the London-based climate finance think tank, has formally submitted its response to the ISSB's exposure draft on SASB Standards for Oil and Gas — a development that signals the imminent tightening of sector-specific disclosure requirements for upstream, integrated, and midstream oil

sections.t: Executive Summary Carbon Tracker, the London-based climate finance think tank, has formally submitted its response to the ISSB's exposure draft on SASB Standards for Oil and Gas — a development that signals the imminent tightening of sector-specific disclosure requirements for upstream, integrated, and midstream oil and gas companies under the global IFRS Sustainability Disclosure Standards architecture. For Indian companies — ONGC, Oil India, Reliance Industries, BPCL, HPCL, and their listed subsidiaries — this is not a distant global standard: SEBI's BRSR Core framework already references ISSB alignment as its stated direction of travel, and any hardening of SASB oil and gas metrics will cascade directly into what Indian ESG auditors, institutional investors, and eventually SEBI will demand. The core implication is this: carbon intensity metrics, methane emissions disclosures, stranded asset risk quantification, and climate scenario analysis for oil and gas assets are moving from voluntary best practice to verifiable, investor-grade disclosure obligations — and Indian energy majors are structurally underprepared. Carbon Tracker's submission, published 26 November 2025 and available at https://carbontracker.org/reports/sasb-standards-on-oil-and-gas/, sets out the think tank's priority recommendations to the ISSB on strengthening the SASB oil and gas standards, with particular emphasis on closing reporting gaps that currently allow companies to obscure true carbon intensity of their portfolios.

What Changed Reporting by Carbon Tracker (carbontracker.org) confirms that the ISSB has issued an exposure draft proposing revisions to the SASB Standards specifically for the oil and gas sector — covering Exploration & Production, Midstream, Refining & Marketing, and Oil & Gas Services sub-sectors. Carbon Tracker's formal submission to this consultation sets out detailed recommendations on what the revised standards must capture to give capital markets a true picture of transition risk embedded in oil and gas assets. The SASB standards, now under ISSB stewardship since the consolidation of SASB into the IFRS Foundation in 2022, function as the industry-specific 'topical' layer beneath the cross-cutting IFRS S1 (general sustainability disclosures) and IFRS S2 (climate disclosures) standards. For oil and gas specifically, SASB metrics include Scope 1 and 2 GHG emissions intensity, methane emissions as a percentage of natural gas throughput, volume of produced water, hydrocarbon spills, and proven and probable reserves by resource type — metrics that directly feed into investors' assessment of stranded asset exposure. What Carbon Tracker is pushing for — based on their broader body of research — is a material strengthening of: (1) carbon intensity reporting across the full asset lifecycle, not just operational emissions; (2) disclosure of capex allocation toward high-cost, high-carbon reserves that are inconsistent with 1.5°C pathways; (3) explicit scenario analysis linking reserve portfolios to IEA Net Zero Emissions by 2050 and Announced Pledges Scenario outcomes; and (4) methane intensity disclosures aligned with the Global Methane Pledge thresholds. This matters enormously for India because SEBI's BRSR Core — mandatory for the top 150 listed companies by market capitalisation from FY2023-24, expanding to the top 250 from FY2024-25 — has been explicitly designed by SEBI to converge with ISSB standards over time. SEBI's Consultation Paper of July 2023 and the subsequent BRSR Core notification are unambiguous on this convergence intent. When ISSB tightens SASB oil and gas metrics, BRSR Core's 'Key Performance Indicators' for energy-intensive sectors will follow — and ONGC, Reliance, and BPCL will be first in the audit committee's line of fire. Beyond BRSR, Indian oil and gas companies with international bond issuances, GDR listings, or foreign institutional investor (FII) ownership above threshold levels are already subject to investor stewardship expectations aligned with ISSB S1/S2. Institutional investors including Norway's Government Pension Fund Global, BlackRock, and the major UK and European asset managers that hold significant positions in Indian energy sector equities are beginning to use SASB oil and gas metrics as a baseline for engagement letters and proxy voting decisions. A revision that raises the floor on what must be disclosed — particularly on methane, reserve carbon intensity, and capex alignment — will translate into direct investor pressure on Indian energy boards within 12-18 months of final standard publication.

Who Is Affected Upstream oil and gas producers: ONGC and Oil India — both BRSR Core-mandated disclosers — whose reserve portfolios and methane emission intensities from ageing fields in Assam, Rajasthan, and offshore basins will face new scrutiny under tightened SASB E&P metrics Integrated energy majors: Reliance Industries (refining, petrochemicals, upstream JVs) and GAIL (midstream gas transmission) — subject to SASB Midstream and Refining & Marketing sub-sector standards simultaneously Public sector oil marketing companies: BPCL and HPCL, whose refining Scope 1 intensity and transition capex allocation between fossil and clean energy assets will be a focal point of revised SASB Refining standards Listed oil field services companies: companies such as Deep Industries, Hindustan Oil Exploration (HOEC), and Selan Exploration, which fall under SASB Oil & Gas Services and are increasingly in the scope of supply chain due diligence by larger operators CFOs and treasury teams at Indian oil and gas companies with outstanding green bonds, sustainability-linked bonds, or syndicated loans with ESG-linked covenants — where SASB metric deterioration triggers ratchet clauses or covenant breaches Chief Sustainability Officers and BRSR reporting teams at BSE 500 energy sector companies who must anticipate ISSB-aligned metric additions in the next SEBI BRSR revision cycle ESG analysts and portfolio managers at domestic mutual funds (SBI MF, HDFC MF, Nippon India MF) and FII desks with energy sector exposure, who use SASB metrics as inputs to proprietary ESG scoring models Audit committees and statutory auditors (Big Four and Grant Thornton India) engaged in BRSR Core 'reasonable assurance' engagements, who will need to update their audit procedures to reflect any expanded SASB oil and gas metric set

Key Obligations & Deadlines ISSB Exposure Draft on SASB Oil & Gas Standards — consultation period open as of November 2025; final revised standards expected to be published within 12-18 months of consultation close, likely mid-to-late 2026; companies should track IFRS Foundation workplan at ifrs.org for precise timeline BRSR Core reasonable assurance — mandatory for top 150 NSE/BSE-listed companies by market cap from FY2023-24 reporting; expanding to top 250 companies from FY2024-25; energy sector companies in both cohorts must ensure their ESG KPIs are auditable and consistent with ISSB-aligned metrics as SEBI updates the BRSR framework SEBI BRSR framework update — SEBI has signalled intent to revise BRSR KPIs in line with ISSB S1/S2 adoption trajectory; Indian oil and gas companies should anticipate a SEBI circular or consultation paper on sector-specific BRSR enhancements within 18-24 months, likely referencing updated SASB oil and gas metrics directly Methane disclosure alignment — companies participating in India's upstream gas sector should note that the Global Methane Pledge (to which India is not a signatory but which influences investor expectations) sets a 30% methane reduction target by 2030 from 2020 levels; SASB revised standards are expected to sharpen methane intensity metric definitions to align with this benchmark Stranded asset scenario analysis — IFRS S2 paragraph 22 already requires climate-related scenario analysis; revised SASB oil and gas standards will likely mandate specific IEA scenario references (NZE 2050, APS) for reserve-level analysis; Indian companies with IND AS-compliant financial statements must assess whether impairment disclosures under IND AS 36 are consistent with disclosed climate scenarios — a gap that statutory auditors are beginning to probe EU CBAM and CSRD supply chain obligations — Indian oil and gas product exporters (particularly petroleum coke, LPG, naphtha) supplying EU-domiciled buyers face indirect exposure to CSRD value chain disclosure requirements and CBAM carbon pricing from 2026 full implementation; SASB metric alignment strengthens Indian exporters' ability to respond to EU customer data requests

Climate Transition Angle The revision of SASB oil and gas standards sits at the precise intersection of physical climate risk, energy transition capital allocation, and financial materiality — and for India, the stakes are asymmetric and underappreciated. India's oil and gas sector faces a dual transition pressure: domestically, the government's push for energy security through domestic upstream development (OALP bid rounds, deep-water exploration) sits in tension with the capital market's growing demand for credible transition plans; externally, India's refining exports — particularly from Reliance's Jamnagar complex, the world's largest refining hub — face increasing carbon intensity scrutiny from European and North American buyers operating under CSRD value chain disclosure obligations. Carbon Tracker's research trajectory — as evidenced by their concurrent work on 'Oil Companies in Disguise' (published June 2026, examining how automaker investments mask fossil fuel carbon intensity) — signals that the think tank is building an evidence base for systemic under-reporting of carbon intensity across fossil fuel value chains. Applied to the SASB oil and gas context, this means pressure for metrics that capture embedded carbon in products sold (Scope 3 Category 11 — use of sold products), not just operational emissions. For ONGC and Reliance, Scope 3 Category 11 dwarfs Scope 1 and 2 combined, and its inclusion in mandatory SASB disclosures would fundamentally alter how institutional investors price transition risk in these stocks. India's own carbon market — the Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) notified by MoEFCC in June 2023, and the PAT Scheme under BEE for energy-intensive sectors — creates a domestic price signal for carbon that, while nascent, is beginning to interact with global disclosure frameworks. As SASB oil and gas metrics tighten under ISSB oversight, the carbon intensity numbers that Indian companies report under BRSR Core will increasingly be used as inputs to both domestic CCTS baseline-setting and international investor ESG scoring — making accurate, assured, and ISSB-consistent reporting a financial asset, not merely a compliance cost. Companies that get ahead of the curve on methane intensity measurement, carbon intensity per barrel of oil equivalent, and capex alignment with transition scenarios will find access to green finance, sustainability-linked loans, and international capital markets materially easier than peers who treat SASB as a box-ticking exercise.

What To Do Now BRSR and sustainability reporting teams (immediate — within 30 days): Download and review Carbon Tracker's full submission to the ISSB SASB Oil & Gas exposure draft at https://carbontracker.org/reports/sasb-standards-on-oil-and-gas/ and map Carbon Tracker's priority recommendations against your current BRSR Core KPI disclosures to identify gaps — particularly on methane intensity, reserve carbon intensity, and transition capex ratios CFO and treasury (within 60 days): Commission a gap analysis comparing current BRSR Core energy and emissions disclosures against the full SASB Exploration & Production, Midstream, or Refining & Marketing standards (as applicable) — this analysis should also assess consistency between climate scenario disclosures and IND AS 36 impairment assumptions, a growing area of statutory audit scrutiny Chief Sustainability Officer (within 90 days): Initiate a Scope 3 Category 11 (use of sold products) quantification exercise if not already underway — given Carbon Tracker's clear direction of advocacy, ISSB will face pressure to include Scope 3 in revised SASB oil and gas standards; being prepared with a credible Scope 3 inventory positions the company ahead of the next BRSR revision cycle and strengthens responses to FII stewardship questionnaires Board Risk Committee (next scheduled meeting): Add 'SASB Oil & Gas Standards Revision and BRSR Convergence Risk' as a standing agenda item; request management to provide a 12-month regulatory watch brief covering ISSB exposure draft finalisation, anticipated SEBI BRSR update, and implications for the company's sustainability-linked financing covenants Investor Relations (within 45 days): Brief key institutional shareholders — particularly FIIs and ESG-mandated domestic funds — on the company's current SASB metric coverage and the roadmap for alignment with revised standards; proactive IR on SASB convergence reduces the risk of being caught off-guard by escalated engagement letters or negative ESG score revisions when the final standards are published Legal and compliance (within 60 days): Review all sustainability-linked bond documentation, ESG-linked loan covenants, and green finance frameworks for references to SASB metrics or ISSB standards as KPI anchors — identify any covenant language that could be triggered by metric redefinition under revised SASB standards and engage legal counsel on renegotiation options if necessary

Our Take Carbon Tracker's engagement with the ISSB on SASB oil and gas standards is not an isolated advocacy exercise — it is part of a coordinated, multi-year effort by the climate finance community to close the credibility gap between what oil and gas companies report and what their actual transition risk exposure looks like to a sophisticated capital markets investor. The pattern is now well established: Carbon Tracker publishes the analytical case for tighter disclosure (as they have done with their 'Oil Companies in Disguise' series on automotive-fossil fuel linkages), submits formal technical input to standard-setters, and then watches as those recommendations migrate — with a 2-4 year lag — into mandatory disclosure frameworks. Indian boards that treat this as a European or North American problem are making a strategic error of the first order. The trajectory for India is clear and accelerating. SEBI has already signalled ISSB convergence as BRSR's long-term destination. The MoEFCC-led CCTS will require carbon intensity baselines that overlap substantially with SASB oil and gas metrics. India's largest institutional investors — LIC, SBI Life, the National Pension System — are under growing pressure from IRDAI and PFRDA to integrate climate risk into portfolio management, which means they will begin demanding SASB-aligned disclosures from portfolio companies in the energy sector within the next 24-36 months. And as EU CSRD value chain obligations bite, Reliance's refining customers in Europe will begin passing SASB-derived data requests down the supply chain as early as 2026-27. The companies that will navigate this environment successfully are those that treat the SASB oil and gas revision not as a compliance burden but as a strategic disclosure opportunity: an invitation to tell a credible, investor-grade story about how they are managing the energy transition. For ONGC, that story must include a credible methane abatement roadmap and a candid assessment of which fields remain economic under a 2°C scenario. For Reliance, it requires a clear capex narrative distinguishing between high-carbon refining expansion and the new energy transition investments the market already knows are underway. The standard is tightening. The question is whether Indian energy majors lead the disclosure curve or are dragged across it.

title: SBTi Services Explained: What Indian Companies Need to Know About Science-Based Targets
summary: The Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi) provides the globally recognized framework for companies to set credible, Paris Agreement-aligned emissions reduction targets — and demand from Indian corporates, investors, and supply chain partners to adopt SBTi commitments is accelerating rapidly. As ES

sections.t: Executive Summary The Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi) provides the globally recognized framework for companies to set credible, Paris Agreement-aligned emissions reduction targets — and demand from Indian corporates, investors, and supply chain partners to adopt SBTi commitments is accelerating rapidly. As ESG disclosure requirements tighten under SEBI's BRSR Core framework and global customers apply Scope 3 pressure across supply chains, obtaining SBTi validation is shifting from a voluntary differentiator to a commercial and regulatory necessity. Indian companies that act now will be better positioned to access green finance, retain global customers, and meet emerging mandatory climate disclosure standards.

What Changed The Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi) is a partnership between CDP, the United Nations Global Compact (UNGC), World Resources Institute (WRI), and the World Wide Fund for Nature (WWF). It defines and promotes best practices in science-based target setting, independently assesses and approves companies' targets, and is widely regarded as the gold standard for corporate climate commitments globally. SBTi offers a suite of services and standards that allow companies to commit to reducing greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions in line with climate science — specifically, pathways consistent with limiting global warming to 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels as required by the Paris Agreement. The SBTi Corporate Standard covers Scope 1 (direct emissions), Scope 2 (purchased energy), and increasingly Scope 3 (value chain) emissions, requiring companies to set near-term targets (5–10 years) and long-term net-zero targets (by 2050 at the latest). Recent updates to the SBTi framework include the release of the Net-Zero Standard, the FLAG (Forest, Land and Agriculture) Guidance for land-intensive sectors, and ongoing development of sector-specific pathways for steel, cement, chemicals, apparel, and financial institutions — all of which are highly relevant to India's industrial base. The SBTi also updated its Corporate Manual in 2023–2024, tightening criteria around Scope 3 coverage thresholds and the use of carbon credits, signaling a continued move toward greater rigor and transparency.

Who Is Affected Large Indian exporters supplying to European, US, and UK markets where customers face CSRD or SEC climate disclosure obligations and are pushing Scope 3 targets down their supply chains Listed companies subject to SEBI's BRSR Core mandatory ESG disclosures, particularly the top 150 listed entities by market capitalization from FY2024–25 Manufacturing, steel, cement, textiles, chemicals, and agribusiness companies covered by SBTi's sector-specific standards including FLAG guidance Financial institutions — banks, NBFCs, and asset managers — as the SBTi Financial Institutions (SBTI-FI) standard requires financed emissions targets and portfolio alignment Indian subsidiaries of multinational corporations whose parent companies have existing SBTi commitments and require value chain alignment SMEs increasingly being asked by large buyers, investors, or global reporting frameworks to demonstrate emissions reduction commitments consistent with science-based pathways Companies seeking green bonds, sustainability-linked loans, or ESG-indexed investment, where SBTi validation provides credibility to climate claims

Key Obligations & Deadlines Commitment Letter: Companies must submit a commitment letter to SBTi pledging to set science-based targets; once submitted, targets must be developed and submitted for validation within 24 months Near-Term Targets: Companies must set targets covering at least 67% of Scope 1 and 2 emissions, and if Scope 3 exceeds 40% of total emissions, must set a Scope 3 target — targets must show emissions reductions consistent with a 1.5°C pathway Net-Zero Target (SBTi Net-Zero Standard): Companies committing under the Net-Zero Standard must set both a near-term and a long-term net-zero target, with net-zero no later than 2050; residual emissions must be neutralized through permanent carbon removal Annual Disclosure: Validated companies must disclose progress against their SBTi targets annually through CDP or equivalent public reporting platforms — non-disclosure can lead to removal from the SBTi's public Companies Taking Action list Scope 3 Coverage Threshold: From 2024 onward, SBTi requires companies in most sectors to address Scope 3 emissions if they constitute more than 40% of total GHG footprint — most Indian manufacturers and retailers will exceed this threshold FLAG Sector Deadline: Companies in forest, land, and agriculture sectors (including food & beverage, paper, palm oil) must align with FLAG guidance — SBTi has indicated these companies must include land-sector targets as part of their validated commitments Financial Institutions: Banks and asset managers must set portfolio-level financed emissions targets under the SBTi-FI framework, covering lending and investment portfolios aligned with sectoral decarbonization approaches (SDA) Carbon Credit Restriction: As of updated SBTi guidance, carbon offsets cannot be used to achieve near-term science-based targets — only genuine in-house or value chain emissions reductions count toward SBTi compliance

Climate Transition Angle SBTi sits at the intersection of physical climate risk management and transition risk strategy. Companies that set science-based targets are, by definition, building a roadmap to decarbonize their operations and value chains — which directly reduces their exposure to transition risks such as carbon pricing, stranded asset risk, and loss of market access as low-carbon product standards tighten globally. From a supply chain perspective, the ripple effect of SBTi adoption is profound. European companies subject to the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and its European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS) are legally required to disclose and manage their Scope 3 emissions — which means they will increasingly demand that Indian suppliers either have SBTi-validated targets or provide verified emissions data that allows the buyer to calculate their own Scope 3 footprint. Indian exporters who cannot demonstrate credible decarbonization plans risk being delisted from global supply chains. The alignment with ISSB standards (IFRS S2 — Climate-related Disclosures) is equally significant. ISSB S2 requires companies to disclose their transition plans, climate targets, and progress — and an SBTi-validated target is widely accepted as evidence of a credible, science-aligned transition plan. SEBI's BRSR Core framework is itself progressively converging with ISSB standards, meaning SBTi adoption today will reduce compliance burden tomorrow. India's domestic climate policy trajectory — including the Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) under the Energy Conservation (Amendment) Act 2022 — further reinforces the need for companies to establish a GHG baseline and reduction pathway now.

What To Do Now Establish Your GHG Baseline: Before committing to SBTi, companies must complete a robust GHG inventory across Scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions using the GHG Protocol Corporate Standard — engage a qualified third party to conduct or verify this inventory, as SBTi validation requires credible underlying data Submit Your SBTi Commitment Letter: Register on the SBTi portal and submit a commitment letter to formally signal your intent — this starts the 24-month clock and publicly demonstrates climate ambition to investors, customers, and regulators Identify Applicable SBTi Pathway and Sector Standard: Determine which SBTi sector-specific standard applies to your business (Corporate Standard, FLAG, Financial Institutions, or a sector pathway for steel/cement/apparel) and understand the specific decarbonization trajectory and coverage requirements for your industry Map Scope 3 Hotspots and Engage Your Supply Chain: Conduct a Scope 3 screening to identify the most material upstream and downstream emission categories — prioritize supplier engagement, procurement policy changes, and product design reviews that will be required to meet Scope 3 target thresholds Integrate SBTi into Board-Level Climate Governance: Ensure your Board or a dedicated ESG/Risk Committee formally owns the SBTi commitment — link executive remuneration to emissions reduction milestones, and embed SBTi progress into your annual BRSR and investor ESG reporting to demonstrate accountability

Our Take SBTi is no longer a nice-to-have badge for sustainability reports — it is rapidly becoming the baseline expectation for any Indian company with serious global ambitions, access to institutional capital, or exposure to European and US regulatory supply chain requirements. What makes the current moment particularly significant is the convergence of pressures: SEBI's BRSR Core is demanding verified climate data, CSRD is pushing Scope 3 accountability into Indian supply chains, the ISSB framework is being adopted by major trading partners, and India's own Carbon Credit Trading Scheme is creating domestic incentives for emissions accounting. Companies that invest in SBTi validation now are not just buying regulatory compliance — they are building the organizational capability in GHG accounting, transition planning, and stakeholder disclosure that will be table stakes for doing business by 2030. Our strong advice: treat SBTi not as a communications exercise but as a strategic transformation program, and start with the GHG inventory today.

title: SEBI's 2012 Compliance & Periodic Reporting Review: The Regulatory Foundation That Built India's ESG Disclosure Architecture
summary: SEBI's March 2012 circular on the Review of Regulatory Compliance and Periodic Reporting marked a pivotal early step in India's journey toward structured, mandatory corporate disclosure — laying the procedural and governance groundwork that would eventually evolve into today's Business Responsibilit

sections.t: Executive Summary SEBI's March 2012 circular on the Review of Regulatory Compliance and Periodic Reporting marked a pivotal early step in India's journey toward structured, mandatory corporate disclosure — laying the procedural and governance groundwork that would eventually evolve into today's Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting (BRSR) framework. By tightening how listed companies report compliance and periodic information to exchanges and regulators, SEBI signalled that transparency and accountability were non-negotiable pillars of market integrity. For sustainability and ESG professionals today, understanding this foundational circular is essential context for appreciating how far India's disclosure ecosystem has come — and where it is headed.

What Changed Issued on March 29, 2012, this SEBI circular directed listed entities to rationalise, streamline, and strengthen their periodic reporting obligations to stock exchanges. At its core, the circular was a housekeeping exercise — reviewing which compliance filings were redundant, which needed greater rigour, and how the overall architecture of regulatory reporting could be made more efficient and meaningful. In practical terms, SEBI was responding to a growing recognition that the volume of filings had increased significantly without a corresponding improvement in the quality or usability of disclosed information. The circular pushed companies and exchanges to move from a 'tick-box' compliance culture toward substantive, timely, and accurate reporting. While the circular predates formal ESG terminology in the Indian regulatory lexicon, it established the principle that periodic disclosures must serve investors and markets — not merely satisfy procedural checklists. This moment is historically significant: it represents SEBI beginning to treat disclosure as a market integrity tool rather than a bureaucratic formality. The logic embedded here — that regulators have a right to review, revise, and raise the bar on what companies must disclose — directly foreshadows the BRSR Core mandates of 2023 and the assurance requirements being phased in through 2026.

Who Is Affected All listed companies on Indian stock exchanges (BSE and NSE) at the time of issuance, with implications for all entities subsequently listed Company secretaries, compliance officers, and legal teams responsible for regulatory filings Stock exchanges acting as the first-level compliance monitors for SEBI Audit committees and boards overseeing the integrity of periodic disclosures Institutional investors and analysts who rely on periodic regulatory filings for investment decisions Sector-agnostic — applicable across all industries represented on Indian exchanges, from manufacturing and financial services to infrastructure and consumer goods

Key Obligations & Deadlines Listed companies were required to review existing periodic compliance submissions and ensure accuracy, completeness, and timeliness of all filings to stock exchanges Stock exchanges were directed to rationalise the compliance calendar and reduce duplication across reporting requirements, consolidating where possible Companies were expected to designate clear internal accountability for each category of periodic report, ensuring that compliance officers had the authority and resources to meet filing obligations Boards and audit committees were implicitly called upon to exercise greater oversight over the compliance reporting function, elevating it from a back-office task to a governance responsibility No single hard deadline was embedded in the circular itself, as it was a structural and procedural review directive — implementation timelines were to be communicated by exchanges in follow-up circulars Companies were advised to proactively audit their existing reporting workflows against the revised requirements and close any gaps identified by SEBI or the exchanges

Climate Transition Angle It may seem like a stretch to connect a 2012 procedural compliance circular to the global climate transition agenda — but the thread is direct and important. The International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB), whose IFRS S1 and S2 standards are now shaping mandatory climate disclosure in over 20 jurisdictions, is fundamentally built on the same principle SEBI articulated in 2012: that periodic, structured, investor-grade reporting is the foundation of accountability. India's own BRSR framework — which requires the top 1,000 listed companies to disclose Scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions, water usage, biodiversity impacts, and supply chain sustainability metrics — is the direct institutional descendant of the reporting rationalisation SEBI began in this era. From a physical risk perspective, the quality of periodic reporting directly determines whether investors and lenders can assess a company's exposure to climate-related risks such as water stress, extreme weather disruption, or stranded asset scenarios. Weak or inconsistent periodic reporting — the problem SEBI was trying to fix in 2012 — means that even today, climate risk data in company filings can be unreliable, inconsistent, or incomparable across peers. The discipline of rigorous periodic reporting, instilled at the compliance level, is therefore a prerequisite for credible ESG disclosure. As India aligns more closely with ISSB standards and as the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) creates extraterritorial obligations for Indian companies exporting to or operating in Europe, the compliance infrastructure that SEBI began building in 2012 becomes the backbone upon which climate transition disclosures must be layered. Companies that treated the 2012 circular as a genuine governance upgrade — rather than a box-ticking exercise — are better positioned today to meet the far more demanding requirements of BRSR Core and forthcoming climate risk disclosure norms.

What To Do Now Conduct a compliance lineage audit: Map your current BRSR, SEBI LODR (Listing Obligations and Disclosure Requirements), and ESG reporting obligations back to their foundational circulars to identify any structural gaps that have accumulated over time and may create regulatory risk today Strengthen your compliance calendar: Use this circular's original intent — rationalisation and accountability — as a prompt to review whether your organisation has clearly assigned ownership for every periodic regulatory filing, with board-level oversight confirmed in committee charters Integrate ESG data into your compliance reporting infrastructure: Do not treat BRSR disclosures as a separate 'sustainability team' function; embed Scope 1/2/3 data collection, water and waste metrics, and supply chain due diligence into the same periodic reporting workflows that govern financial compliance Prepare for assurance requirements: SEBI's phased introduction of reasonable assurance for BRSR Core metrics means that the quality of underlying data — collected through periodic reporting systems — will come under external scrutiny; begin internal readiness assessments now if not already underway Benchmark against ISSB and CSRD: If your company has international investors, supply chain relationships with European firms, or plans for overseas listings, assess your current periodic disclosure quality against IFRS S1/S2 and CSRD requirements to identify material gaps that will need to be closed by 2025–2027

Our Take SEBI's 2012 circular may appear, on the surface, to be a dated administrative document with little relevance to the sophisticated ESG and climate disclosure conversations happening in boardrooms and investor meetings today. We would argue the opposite. This circular represents the moment SEBI explicitly claimed its authority to continuously review, revise, and raise expectations around what listed companies must disclose — a principle it has exercised with increasing ambition in every subsequent decade. The companies that internalised the spirit of this directive built compliance cultures that are now far better equipped to handle the complexity of BRSR Core, ISSB alignment, and climate risk reporting. Those that treated it as paperwork are scrambling to catch up. As SEBI moves toward mandatory climate scenario analysis, Scope 3 assurance, and potentially an Indian version of the TCFD framework, the lesson of 2012 is timeless: disclosure is not a burden to be minimised — it is a strategic asset to be invested in.

title: SEBI's ESG Consultation Paper: What India's Capital Markets Are Signalling About the Future of Sustainable Finance
summary: In February 2023, SEBI released a landmark Consultation Paper on ESG Disclosures, Ratings and Investing, signalling a comprehensive effort to strengthen the architecture of sustainable finance in India's capital markets. The paper proposes reforms across three interconnected pillars — corporate ESG

sections.t: Executive Summary In February 2023, SEBI released a landmark Consultation Paper on ESG Disclosures, Ratings and Investing, signalling a comprehensive effort to strengthen the architecture of sustainable finance in India's capital markets. The paper proposes reforms across three interconnected pillars — corporate ESG disclosures under BRSR, the regulation of ESG Rating Providers (ERPs), and standards for ESG-themed investment products such as mutual funds. For listed companies, institutional investors, and rating agencies, this consultation marks the beginning of a more rigorous, accountable, and globally comparable ESG regime in India.

What Changed SEBI's February 2023 Consultation Paper does not itself enact new rules — rather, it invites public comment on a suite of proposed reforms that are expected to translate into binding regulations. This is an important distinction: companies and investors should treat it as a preview of the regulatory direction, not merely an academic exercise. The paper addresses three major areas. First, it proposes enhancements to the Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report (BRSR) framework, including the introduction of a 'BRSR Core' — a shorter, high-priority set of Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) subject to reasonable assurance by third parties. This would make a subset of ESG disclosures verifiable and auditable, raising the bar significantly from the current self-reported model. Second, the paper proposes a regulatory framework for ESG Rating Providers (ERPs) operating in India. Currently, ESG ratings are largely unregulated, creating risks of inconsistency, conflicts of interest, and opacity. SEBI proposes that ERPs be registered, follow defined methodologies, and ensure transparency in how ratings are derived — a move that mirrors regulatory trends in the EU and the UK. Third, the paper scrutinises ESG-themed mutual funds and other investment products, proposing guardrails to prevent greenwashing — the practice of marketing financial products as sustainable without substantive underlying criteria. This includes minimum thresholds for ESG-aligned investments within such funds and stronger disclosure obligations for asset managers.

Who Is Affected Top 1,000 listed companies (by market capitalisation) — already subject to BRSR; the proposed 'BRSR Core' with assured KPIs will directly impact their disclosure and audit processes All listed companies — broader BRSR enhancements may progressively extend to a wider universe of listed entities ESG Rating Providers (ERPs) — domestic and foreign agencies providing ESG ratings on Indian companies will face mandatory registration and conduct requirements Asset Management Companies (AMCs) — mutual funds offering ESG-labelled schemes will need to meet new portfolio composition, disclosure, and anti-greenwashing standards Foreign Portfolio Investors (FPIs) — those investing in Indian markets may face new ESG-related disclosure or engagement obligations Sustainability consultants and ESG assurance providers — demand for third-party assurance of BRSR Core KPIs will create both opportunity and responsibility for assurance professionals

Key Obligations & Deadlines BRSR Core KPIs: SEBI proposed a set of ~50 high-priority ESG indicators spanning environment, social, and governance themes, with mandatory reasonable assurance — companies should begin gap assessments against these indicators immediately Third-Party Assurance: The 'BRSR Core' framework proposes that disclosures be subject to third-party reasonable assurance, likely phased in starting with the top 150 listed companies before expanding further — identify and onboard qualified assurance providers now ESG Rating Provider Registration: ERPs will be required to register with SEBI, disclose their methodologies, manage conflicts of interest, and publish ratings with explanatory rationale — ERPs should prepare compliance frameworks ahead of formal rule notification ESG Mutual Fund Guardrails: AMCs offering ESG schemes must ensure a minimum proportion of assets are invested in companies meeting defined ESG criteria, with enhanced scheme-level disclosures — AMCs should review existing fund mandates for alignment Consultation Response Deadline: The public comment period for this consultation paper closed in early 2023; formal regulatory circulars translating these proposals into rules are expected to follow — monitor SEBI notifications closely for binding implementation dates Value Chain Disclosures: SEBI proposed that large listed companies disclose ESG performance of their top suppliers and customers (Scope 3 equivalent) — companies should begin supplier ESG data collection programmes

Climate Transition Angle SEBI's consultation paper is best understood not in isolation, but as India's contribution to a global convergence of sustainability disclosure standards. The proposed BRSR Core KPIs include climate-specific metrics — greenhouse gas emissions (Scope 1, 2, and progressively Scope 3), energy consumption, water intensity, and waste generation — that closely mirror the climate-related disclosure requirements under the ISSB's IFRS S2 standard and the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD). Indian companies with international operations, foreign investors, or global supply chain relationships are increasingly expected to report in a manner consistent with these frameworks. The push for assured disclosures is particularly significant from a climate transition perspective. Investors and lenders globally are using ESG data to make capital allocation decisions aligned with net-zero pathways. Unverified, inconsistent data undermines this process. By introducing assurance requirements, SEBI is laying the groundwork for Indian capital markets to support credible climate transition finance — including green bonds, sustainability-linked loans, and transition finance instruments. The value chain disclosure proposals also carry material implications for physical and transition climate risks. Companies that map their supplier ESG performance will, by extension, begin to understand where climate-related vulnerabilities sit in their supply chains — whether that is water-stressed agricultural suppliers, carbon-intensive raw material producers, or geographically exposed logistics partners. This is precisely the kind of systemic risk visibility that regulators, investors, and boards need as India's climate transition accelerates.

What To Do Now Conduct a BRSR Core Gap Assessment: Map your current disclosures against the proposed ~50 BRSR Core KPIs. Identify data gaps, especially for GHG emissions (Scope 1 & 2 as a minimum), water withdrawal, and employee safety metrics — these are most likely to be prioritised in the final rules Appoint an Assurance Provider Early: The market for BRSR assurance services is nascent and will become competitive quickly.
