Engage with a qualified assurance provider (typically a Big 4 or specialist sustainability assurance firm) to understand the readiness requirements and timeline for reasonable assurance Build Supplier ESG Data Infrastructure: Begin collecting basic ESG data from your top 20–30 suppliers in preparation for value chain disclosure obligations. A simple supplier ESG questionnaire aligned to BRSR metrics is a practical starting point Review ESG-Labelled Financial Products: If you are an AMC or advise on ESG fund structuring, conduct a portfolio review against SEBI's proposed minimum ESG investment thresholds and update fund disclosures to pre-empt greenwashing scrutiny Monitor SEBI Notifications and Engage Proactively: Track SEBI's regulatory calendar for formal circulars following this consultation. Consider engaging through industry associations (CII, FICCI, AMFI) to provide feedback that shapes implementation timelines and definitions favourably for your sector

Our Take SEBI's February 2023 Consultation Paper is one of the most significant regulatory signals in India's ESG history — not because it changes rules today, but because it maps out a coherent, ambitious architecture for sustainable finance that will define the next decade of corporate disclosure and investment practice. The introduction of assured BRSR Core disclosures is a genuine step-change: it moves India from a disclosure culture based on self-reporting and box-ticking toward one grounded in accountability and comparability. The regulation of ESG Rating Providers addresses a long-standing market integrity gap that has undermined investor confidence in ESG data quality. What comes next will matter enormously — the specificity of the final KPI definitions, the pace of the assurance roll-out, and the stringency of anti-greenwashing rules for funds will determine whether India's ESG market matures into a credible force for capital reallocation, or remains a compliance exercise. Companies that start building data systems, assurance relationships, and supply chain visibility now will not only be compliant — they will be competitively positioned as global investors increasingly scrutinise India's listed universe through a sustainability lens.

title: SEBI's LODR Consultation Paper: What Listed Companies Must Know About Streamlined Disclosures and Stronger ESG Compliance
summary: In February 2023, SEBI released a landmark consultation paper proposing sweeping reforms to the Listing Obligations and Disclosure Requirements (LODR) Regulations, 2015 — the foundational rulebook governing what India's listed companies must disclose to markets and regulators. The proposals aim to r

sections.t: Executive Summary In February 2023, SEBI released a landmark consultation paper proposing sweeping reforms to the Listing Obligations and Disclosure Requirements (LODR) Regulations, 2015 — the foundational rulebook governing what India's listed companies must disclose to markets and regulators. The proposals aim to reduce redundancy in filings, sharpen the quality of material disclosures, and significantly strengthen compliance mechanisms, including those tied to ESG and Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting (BRSR). For sustainability and compliance officers, this signals a decisive regulatory shift: disclosure is no longer a checkbox exercise, but a core governance and investor-trust function.

What Changed SEBI's consultation paper does not introduce a new regulation outright — rather, it proposes targeted amendments and clarifications to the existing LODR framework, inviting public comment before finalising rules. The paper addresses three broad problem areas that SEBI identified through market surveillance and stakeholder feedback. First, information overload: listed entities were filing large volumes of disclosures, many of which were duplicative, immaterial, or poorly structured — making it harder, not easier, for investors to extract meaningful signals. SEBI proposed rationalising these requirements by consolidating overlapping obligations and introducing clearer materiality thresholds for event-based disclosures. Second, compliance gaps: despite existing mandates, SEBI observed persistent delays, incomplete filings, and inconsistent application of LODR provisions — particularly among mid- and small-cap companies. The paper proposed stronger enforcement levers, including enhanced penalties, board-level accountability, and tighter timelines. Third, ESG and sustainability alignment: with BRSR already mandated for the top 1,000 listed companies by market capitalisation and BRSR Core (with assurance requirements) being phased in, the consultation paper sought to embed sustainability disclosures more firmly within the LODR architecture — giving them the same regulatory weight as financial disclosures rather than treating them as supplementary reports.

Who Is Affected All companies listed on Indian stock exchanges (BSE, NSE) — including equity, debt, and SME-listed entities — are subject to LODR and will be affected by any amendments that follow this consultation. Top 1,000 listed companies by market capitalisation face the most immediate impact, as they are already subject to mandatory BRSR and are likely to face enhanced scrutiny on sustainability disclosures under any revised framework. Mid- and small-cap listed entities should take note: the paper signals SEBI's intent to close compliance gaps that have been more prevalent in this segment, suggesting stricter enforcement is coming. Compliance officers, company secretaries, CFOs, and boards — particularly audit committees and ESG/sustainability committees — bear direct accountability under the proposed strengthened compliance architecture. Institutional investors, mutual funds, and ESG-focused asset managers will benefit from improved disclosure quality and are watching this reform closely as it affects their stewardship and voting decisions. Legal, audit, and advisory firms supporting listed entities on LODR compliance and BRSR reporting will need to update their service frameworks once amendments are finalised.

Key Obligations & Deadlines Monitor SEBI's official gazette and circulars for final amendments to LODR Regulations following the close of the consultation period — the paper was published February 20, 2023, and final rules are expected to be notified in subsequent months. Review materiality policies: SEBI proposed clearer and more stringent thresholds for what constitutes a 'material' event requiring immediate disclosure. Companies should audit their existing materiality frameworks against proposed benchmarks and update board-approved policies accordingly. Strengthen timelines for event-based disclosures: proposals include tightening the window for reporting material events from 24 hours to as little as 12 hours in certain categories. Compliance teams must ensure internal escalation protocols can meet accelerated deadlines. Embed BRSR within the LODR compliance calendar: sustainability disclosures — including Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) under BRSR Core — are proposed to be treated as regulated disclosures with enforcement consequences for non-filing or misrepresentation. Board and audit committee accountability: proposed amendments would require explicit board sign-off on the adequacy of disclosure controls, not just the content of individual filings. Boards should review and formalise their oversight of the disclosure function. Prepare for third-party assurance: the phased rollout of BRSR Core assurance (mandatory for top 150 companies from FY2023-24, expanding thereafter) is closely linked to the LODR strengthening agenda. Companies not yet engaged with assurance providers should begin scoping now. Engage with the consultation process: stakeholders were invited to submit comments on the paper's proposals. While the formal comment window has closed, companies can engage through industry associations (CII, FICCI, NASSCOM) to shape final rulemaking.

Climate Transition Angle This consultation paper carries significant implications for how climate and sustainability risks are disclosed in India's capital markets — and how those disclosures connect to the global transition finance architecture. The proposed integration of BRSR more firmly into the LODR framework is a critical step toward making climate-related financial disclosures mandatory and enforceable rather than aspirational. BRSR's essential indicators already require companies to report on energy consumption, greenhouse gas emissions (Scope 1 and 2), water usage, and waste management. The strengthened compliance architecture proposed in this paper means these disclosures will face the same scrutiny as financial statements — and misrepresentations could carry regulatory consequences. From a global standards perspective, this reform positions India's disclosure regime more closely with the International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) framework — specifically IFRS S1 (General Sustainability Disclosures) and IFRS S2 (Climate-related Disclosures) — and with the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD). As Indian companies with global supply chain relationships, foreign listings, or international investors face growing pressure to align with these standards, a stronger domestic LODR framework provides a regulatory foundation rather than leaving companies to self-navigate global expectations. Physical climate risks — including water stress, extreme heat, flooding, and supply chain disruption — are increasingly material events under any credible materiality framework. Tightened LODR materiality thresholds will compel companies in climate-exposed sectors (agriculture, real estate, infrastructure, utilities, textiles) to develop more robust processes for assessing and disclosing climate-related risks in real time, not just in annual reports. For transition risks, the proposed reforms arrive at a moment when India's energy transition — driven by the national target of 500 GW of non-fossil capacity by 2030 and net-zero by 2070 — is generating material shifts in the competitive landscape. Companies in carbon-intensive sectors that fail to disclose credible transition plans may find themselves flagged by both regulators and institutional investors applying ESG screening.

What To Do Now Conduct a LODR Disclosure Audit: Map all current disclosure obligations under LODR against SEBI's proposed reforms. Identify gaps in materiality determination, filing timeliness, and board-level sign-off processes — and prioritise remediation before final amendments are notified. Integrate BRSR into Your Governance Structure: Elevate BRSR from an annual reporting exercise to a continuous disclosure function. Assign board-level ownership (ideally through an ESG or sustainability committee), establish quarterly data collection rhythms, and link KPI tracking to the same systems used for financial reporting. Develop a Climate Materiality Assessment: Using TCFD (Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures) methodology as a guide, assess which physical and transition climate risks cross the threshold of material events under LODR. Document this assessment and ensure it is reviewed at least annually by the board. Engage an Assurance Provider Early: If your company falls within the top 1,000 listed entities, begin scoping BRSR Core limited assurance now. Assurance-ready data requires clean, auditable ESG data infrastructure — and building that takes time. Don't wait for the mandate to force a rushed engagement. Align Internal Reporting with ISSB/CSRD Standards: Even if full ISSB alignment is not yet required in India, begin mapping your BRSR disclosures to IFRS S1 and S2 indicators. This future-proofs your reporting for international investors and positions your company ahead of likely convergence between SEBI's framework and global standards.

Our Take SEBI's LODR consultation paper is not a routine housekeeping exercise — it represents a structural shift in how India's capital markets regulator views the relationship between disclosure quality, corporate governance, and investor protection in the ESG era. By proposing to embed sustainability disclosures within the enforceable LODR architecture, SEBI is sending a clear message: climate and ESG information is material financial information, and it will be regulated accordingly. For companies that have treated BRSR as a compliance burden to be minimised, this is a wake-up call. The firms that will fare best are those that invest now in the data systems, governance structures, and assurance processes needed to make sustainability disclosure as rigorous as their financial reporting. With ISSB standards gaining global traction and India's own climate commitments accelerating, the direction of travel is unmistakable — and the window to get ahead of it, rather than react to it, is narrowing.

title: Set a Target: Why Indian Companies Can No Longer Afford to Delay SBTi Validation
summary: The Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi) provides a globally recognised framework for companies to set emissions reduction targets aligned with limiting global warming to 1.5°C — and validation through SBTi is rapidly becoming a baseline expectation from investors, global buyers, and regulators.

sections.t: Executive Summary The Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi) provides a globally recognised framework for companies to set emissions reduction targets aligned with limiting global warming to 1.5°C — and validation through SBTi is rapidly becoming a baseline expectation from investors, global buyers, and regulators. For Indian companies embedded in export supply chains or seeking international capital, committing to and validating an SBTi target is no longer a voluntary 'nice-to-have' — it is a strategic imperative. Companies that act now will be positioned ahead of the compliance curve; those that delay risk losing market access, investor confidence, and social licence to operate.

What Changed The Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi) is an independent body that validates corporate greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions reduction targets to ensure they are consistent with the pace of decarbonisation required by climate science. SBTi's Corporate Validation Services allow companies of all sizes — from large corporates to SMEs — to submit their emissions targets for independent assessment and earn the right to publicly claim their targets are 'science-based.' In recent years, SBTi has significantly scaled its validation infrastructure, introduced the Net-Zero Standard (requiring companies to address both near-term and long-term emissions across Scopes 1, 2, and 3), and launched the SME route to lower barriers for smaller businesses. Critically, SBTi is also developing sector-specific guidance — including for steel, cement, apparel, and financial institutions — making target-setting more rigorous and sector-relevant than ever before. The validation process requires companies to first commit publicly (signing the SBTi commitment letter), then submit targets within 24 months for validation against the latest SBTi criteria. Once validated, companies must report progress annually and recertify targets as standards evolve. The bar is rising: vague or incremental pledges are no longer sufficient — targets must be grounded in measurable, time-bound science.

Who Is Affected Large Indian corporates in export-oriented sectors: textiles, automotive components, IT services, pharmaceuticals, steel, and cement — where global customers are mandating Scope 3 supplier targets Indian subsidiaries of multinational companies, where parent companies are already SBTi-committed and are cascading requirements down their supply chains Companies seeking to raise capital from ESG-focused international investors, green bond markets, or sustainability-linked loans Businesses subject to SEBI's Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report (BRSR) Core framework, particularly the 9 Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) on climate SMEs supplying to large domestic or international buyers who are themselves SBTi-committed and under pressure to address Scope 3 emissions Financial institutions and banks, for whom SBTi's Financial Institutions Net-Zero Standard is directly applicable to their lending and investment portfolios

Key Obligations & Deadlines COMMIT: Sign the SBTi Commitment Letter via the SBTi website — this is the formal, public declaration of intent and starts the 24-month clock for target submission DEVELOP TARGETS (within 24 months of commitment): Set near-term targets (5–10 year horizon, aligned with 1.5°C) and long-term net-zero targets (by no later than 2050), covering Scopes 1, 2, and material Scope 3 categories SCOPE 3 ASSESSMENT: Conduct a Scope 3 emissions inventory to identify material categories — for most Indian manufacturers, this will include purchased goods and services, use of sold products, and logistics SUBMIT FOR VALIDATION: Submit target documentation to SBTi for independent review; expect a validation turnaround of several months and budget for potential target revisions based on SBTi feedback ANNUAL PROGRESS REPORTING: Once validated, disclose annual emissions performance against targets in public sustainability reports, BRSR filings, and CDP disclosures RECERTIFICATION: Monitor SBTi criteria updates (particularly sector-specific pathways as they are released) and recertify targets if business activities or SBTi standards change materially SME ROUTE: Smaller companies can use the streamlined SBTi SME pathway, which requires commitment to a set of standardised actions rather than a full bespoke target submission — reducing the resource burden significantly

Climate Transition Angle SBTi targets sit at the very heart of the global corporate climate transition. The International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) — whose standards India is actively considering adopting through SEBI — requires companies to disclose their transition plans and the emissions targets underpinning them. An SBTi-validated target is widely regarded as the gold standard for demonstrating that a company's transition plan is credible, not greenwashed. From a supply chain perspective, the European Union's Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive (CSDDD) and the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) are compelling European companies to account for Scope 3 emissions across their value chains. Indian suppliers to European buyers — in sectors from apparel to auto components to chemicals — will face increasing contractual and reputational pressure to demonstrate science-based commitments or risk deselection. Physical climate risks compound the urgency. Indian businesses face material exposure to heat stress, water scarcity, and extreme weather events. Investors and lenders using climate scenario analysis (aligned with TCFD, now integrated into ISSB standards) will reward companies with credible decarbonisation trajectories and penalise those perceived as stranded asset risks. Setting an SBTi target signals strategic awareness of both transition and physical risk — and the intent to manage both.

What To Do Now CONDUCT A READINESS ASSESSMENT: Map your current GHG emissions inventory (Scopes 1, 2, and 3) against SBTi criteria. Identify data gaps — particularly in Scope 3 — and prioritise filling them before commitment. Engage a qualified sustainability consultant if needed. ENGAGE LEADERSHIP: Present the business case for SBTi commitment to your Board and CFO — frame it around investor expectations, customer retention, access to green finance, and alignment with BRSR Core disclosures rather than purely compliance cost. SIGN THE COMMITMENT LETTER: Do not wait for perfect data. Sign the SBTi commitment letter now to signal intent publicly — this also provides a 24-month runway to develop robust targets. Early movers gain reputational advantage in their sector. DEVELOP AN INTERNAL ROADMAP: Assign cross-functional ownership (Finance, Operations, Procurement, Sustainability) for target development. Set internal milestones for Scope 3 data collection, target modelling, and submission — do not let the 24-month window slip by default. ALIGN WITH BROADER REPORTING FRAMEWORKS: Ensure your SBTi journey is integrated with your BRSR Core disclosures, CDP submission, and any ISSB-aligned reporting you are preparing — creating a single, consistent climate narrative for all stakeholders.

Our Take Setting a science-based target is no longer an act of corporate voluntarism — it is the entry ticket to the next decade of global commerce and capital markets. For Indian companies, the window to act proactively is narrowing fast: SEBI's BRSR Core framework, incoming ISSB-aligned standards, and the Scope 3 ambitions of multinational buyers are converging to make SBTi validation a de facto market requirement across sectors. What makes this moment particularly significant is that SBTi is simultaneously raising its standards — sector pathways are becoming more stringent, net-zero criteria are tightening, and greenwashing scrutiny is intensifying globally. Companies that commit and validate their targets now will not only avoid future compliance scrambles but will build the internal data systems, supplier engagement capabilities, and credibility with investors that define resilient, transition-ready businesses. The question is no longer whether to set a science-based target — it is how quickly you can get started.

title: The Fossil Fuel Export Emissions Blind Spot: What the CAT's 'Iceberg Effect' Analysis Means for India's Coal Import Dependency, BRSR Disclosure, and Stranded Asset Risk
summary: The Climate Action Tracker's latest analysis (climateactiontracker.org/blog/highlighting-the-hypocrisy-fossil-fuel-export-emissions/) exposes a foundational flaw in global climate accounting: the emissions embedded in fossil fuel exports — coal, oil, and gas — are counted neither in the exporting co

sections.t: Executive Summary The Climate Action Tracker's latest analysis (climateactiontracker.org/blog/highlighting-the-hypocrisy-fossil-fuel-export-emissions/) exposes a foundational flaw in global climate accounting: the emissions embedded in fossil fuel exports — coal, oil, and gas — are counted neither in the exporting country's NDC nor in the importing country's production-side ledger, creating a $multi-trillion accountability vacuum that regulators are now beginning to close. For India, the world's second-largest coal importer and a major consumer of Australian thermal coal and LNG from the US and Australia, this is not an abstract geopolitical debate — it is a direct supply-chain carbon risk, a Scope 3 disclosure obligation under BRSR Core, and an emerging country-of-origin trade policy risk that CFOs and CSOs at power utilities, steel producers, aluminium smelters, and cement manufacturers must quantify now. The CAT report identifies Australia, the US, Canada, Norway, and the UK as countries whose exported emissions dwarf or substantially exceed their domestic emission reductions — the 'iceberg effect'. India is the primary destination market for several of these export flows, meaning Indian importers are sitting atop the submerged portion of this iceberg. As ISSB IFRS S2 adoption accelerates and EU CBAM expands its product scope, the carbon embedded in India's imported fossil fuel feedstock will become a hard financial number on balance sheets — not a sustainability footnote.

What Changed Reporting by Climate Action Tracker (CAT), authored by Sofia Gonzales-Zuniga, Danial Riaz, and Mia Moisio, and published at https://climateactiontracker.org/blog/highlighting-the-hypocrisy-fossil-fuel-export-emissions/, presents a granular 2022 dataset showing that the top 10 fossil fuel exporting nations account for approximately 60% of total exported fossil fuel combustion emissions globally. Russia, Australia, the US, Indonesia, Saudi Arabia, and Canada collectively represent 50% of these exported emissions. The CAT's central argument — and its regulatory significance — is that current national emissions accounting under the UNFCCC framework assigns combustion emissions to the consuming country, meaning exporting nations bear zero NDC liability for the downstream carbon consequence of their exported fossil fuels. This accounting asymmetry directly undermines the COP28 'transition away' commitment and the reaffirmation at the UN General Assembly in September 2024. For India, this has three concrete and immediate implications. First, India's power sector, which remains heavily dependent on imported thermal coal from Australia and Indonesia (two of the top exporters identified by CAT), is exposed to an intensifying global policy push to price, restrict, or disclose exported fossil fuel emissions. Australia's coal exports are projected to remain at near-record highs through at least 2030 (per DCCEEW 2024 data cited in the CAT report), and the US is expected to significantly increase LNG exports to 2050 per US EIA 2023 projections — both of which are major supply lines into India's energy system. Second, as international pressure mounts on fossil fuel exporters to internalise export emission costs — through carbon border mechanisms, export levies, or bilateral trade conditionalities — the landed cost of imported coal and LNG into India will rise, creating fuel cost volatility and stranded asset risk for Indian utilities and industrial consumers. Third, and most immediately for listed Indian companies, SEBI's BRSR Core framework — mandatory for the top 150 listed entities from FY2023-24, extending to the top 1,000 from FY2024-25 — already requires Scope 3 value chain emission disclosure on a 'reasonable assurance' basis for the largest filers. The GHG Protocol Category 1 (purchased goods and services) and Category 3 (fuel and energy-related activities) are directly triggered by imported fossil fuel consumption, meaning the carbon volume that exporting countries refuse to account for domestically will now appear — mandatorily — on Indian companies' BRSR disclosures.

Who Is Affected Power generation utilities (thermal): NTPC, Adani Power, Tata Power, JSW Energy — all with imported coal exposure from Australia and Indonesia; direct Scope 3 Category 1 and 3 obligations under BRSR Core Steel producers: JSW Steel, Tata Steel, SAIL, JSPL — dependent on coking coal imports primarily from Australia; CAT's analysis of Australia's elevated coal export trajectory directly affects feedstock carbon intensity and EU CBAM cost exposure from FY2026 onwards Aluminium and cement sectors: Hindalco, Vedanta, UltraTech Cement — energy-intensive sectors with significant imported fossil fuel or imported electricity exposure; BRSR Core Scope 3 Category 8 (upstream leased assets and processing of sold products) is relevant LNG importers and city gas distribution companies: Petronet LNG, GAIL, Adani Total Gas — US LNG import volumes set to rise materially; CAT flags US exported gas emissions growing at one of the fastest rates globally, raising long-term supply security and carbon cost questions CFOs at BSE 500 listed companies in energy-intensive sectors: responsible for quantifying fuel import carbon costs under BRSR Core and preparing for ISSB IFRS S2-aligned disclosures as India's ICAI sustainability standards (INBR framework under development) approach mandatory status Chief Sustainability Officers and ESG Heads: must now incorporate 'fossil fuel export policy risk' as a named scenario in TCFD-aligned climate risk registers and BRSR Core principle-by-principle disclosures Institutional investors: domestic MFs, insurance companies, and FPIs with holdings in power, metals, and energy — the CAT analysis is a leading indicator of policy-driven fuel cost volatility and stranded asset crystallisation risk Export-oriented Indian manufacturers supplying EU markets: the EU CBAM's current scope (steel, aluminium, cement, fertilisers, hydrogen, electricity) will expand; as European buyers tighten their own Scope 3 Category 1 supply chain audits under CSRD/ESRS E1, Indian suppliers using high-carbon imported coal inputs will face qualification risk

Key Obligations & Deadlines BRSR Core — Scope 3 GHG disclosure (including upstream fossil fuel categories): mandatory for top 150 listed entities from FY2023-24 (already in effect); extending to top 1,000 listed entities from FY2024-25 (i.e., disclosures due in Annual Reports filed by September 2025) BRSR Core — reasonable assurance on Scope 3 by third-party assurance providers: SEBI has signalled phased mandatory assurance; boards must ensure assurance-readiness of Scope 3 Category 1 and 3 data by FY2025-26 at latest EU CBAM — transitional reporting phase ended December 2025; definitive carbon pricing on imports of covered products (steel, aluminium, cement, fertilisers) begins January 2026: Indian exporters to EU must now have certified embedded carbon intensity data for all covered products, including carbon in imported fossil fuel inputs ISSB IFRS S2 — climate-related financial disclosures including Scope 3 material emission sources: jurisdictions adopting ISSB standards are accelerating (Australia from 2025, UK from 2025, Singapore from 2025); Indian companies with cross-listed securities or institutional FPI investors face effective ISSB S2 alignment pressure now even ahead of formal ICAI adoption SBTi corporate net zero targets — for Indian companies with validated SBTi targets (currently 80+ Indian companies): SBTi's Corporate Net Zero Standard requires Scope 3 Category 1 (purchased fossil fuels) to be addressed in value chain decarbonisation plans; CAT's report strengthens the case for SBTi to tighten its fossil fuel procurement guidance in the next standard review cycle India's Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) — MoEFCC notified framework in June 2023; PAT Cycle VII ongoing: as India's domestic carbon market matures, the carbon cost of imported fossil fuels will increasingly interface with domestic carbon credit pricing, requiring CFOs to model both simultaneously CDP disclosure cycle 2025 (responses due July 2025): CDP's Climate questionnaire C6 (Scope 3 emissions) and C4 (targets) will increasingly scrutinise whether companies have quantified and disclosed fossil fuel import-related upstream emissions; non-disclosure is now a red flag for CDP scoring

Climate Transition Angle The CAT analysis crystallises a transition risk that Indian boards have been able to treat as theoretical: the carbon embedded in imported fossil fuels is moving from an unaccounted externality to a priced, disclosed, and potentially trade-restricted liability. India's energy transition pathway under its updated NDC — targeting 500 GW of non-fossil capacity by 2030 and net zero by 2070 — is built on the premise of a managed coal phase-down over multiple decades. But the CAT data reveals that the major coal-exporting nations supplying India (Australia, Indonesia) have no credible policy trajectory to reduce export volumes before 2030, and Australia explicitly sees LNG exports as a 'transition fuel' role extending beyond 2050. This means the physical coal and gas supply will remain available and competitively priced — but the financial and regulatory cost of using it will escalate non-linearly. For Indian steel and power companies with EU export exposure, the CBAM carbon price on embedded emissions in exported steel — calculated on actual embedded carbon, not sector benchmarks, from 2026 — will for the first time put a hard euro price on the decision to use Australian coking coal versus green hydrogen-based direct reduced iron. At current EU ETS carbon prices (~€60-70/tonne), the CBAM surcharge on a tonne of Indian blast-furnace steel with Australian coal inputs could reach €80-120 per tonne by 2028, potentially exceeding the steel's own margin. This is the transition risk made financially concrete. BRSR Core's requirement to disclose Scope 3 Category 11 (use of sold products) and Category 1 (purchased goods) in the same document as financial statements means Indian CFOs can no longer treat this as a sustainability team problem — it is a P&L forecast variable. The CAT's spotlight on the 'iceberg effect' of exported emissions will accelerate the push within ISSB, GHG Protocol, and future UNFCCC stocktake negotiations to assign producer-country carbon liability for exported fossil fuels, which would directly alter the geopolitical economics of India's coal import agreements.

What To Do Now CFO and Finance team (within 60 days): Commission a formal Scope 3 Category 1 and Category 3 carbon inventory for all imported fossil fuel volumes — coal, LNG, crude oil — disaggregated by country of origin. Map this against the CAT's export trajectory data for Australia, Indonesia, and the US to model a 3-year landed carbon cost scenario under CBAM and potential domestic CCTS pricing CSO and Sustainability team (within 90 days): Update the company's BRSR Core Scope 3 disclosure methodology to explicitly address GHG Protocol Category 1 (purchased goods — fossil fuels) and Category 3 (fuel and energy-related activities upstream emissions). Ensure the methodology is assurance-ready and aligned with ISSB IFRS S2 paragraph 29 (Scope 3 screening and materiality assessment) Procurement and Supply Chain Head (within 6 months): Initiate supplier-level carbon intensity data collection from Australian and Indonesian coal suppliers and US/Australian LNG counterparties. This is both a BRSR Core data requirement and a CBAM compliance prerequisite for any steel, aluminium, or cement exported to EU markets Board Risk Committee (next board meeting): Incorporate 'fossil fuel export policy risk' as a named and quantified item in the board-level climate risk register. Reference the CAT analysis and COP28 'transition away' commitment as the policy trigger. Request management to present a scenario analysis under a 'carbon border adjustment on fossil fuel exports by exporting nations' stress case — this is the tail risk that is now moving into the foreseeable horizon Investor Relations team (ahead of next AGM / investor day): Prepare a dedicated briefing note for institutional investors and ESG analysts on how the company's imported fossil fuel exposure is being managed, quantified, and disclosed. FPIs and domestic institutional investors subject to SEBI's stewardship code are increasingly required to engage on Scope 3 exposure; pre-empt the question with a credible, data-backed answer Legal and Compliance team (within 120 days): Review all long-term coal and LNG import contracts for carbon price pass-through clauses, force majeure definitions that may cover carbon border measures, and renegotiation rights triggered by material regulatory change. The EU CBAM's definitive phase from January 2026 may constitute a material contract risk event for existing supply arrangements

Our Take The CAT's fossil fuel export emissions analysis is not simply an advocacy document — it is a pre-regulatory signal of where the next phase of international climate finance and trade policy is heading, and Indian boards that read it only as geopolitical commentary will be caught unprepared. Here is the trajectory I expect over the next 24-36 months: First, the UNFCCC's Global Stocktake process and the upcoming COP30 in Belém (2025) will face intense pressure from climate-vulnerable nations — many of them in South and Southeast Asia, India's neighbourhood — to close the exported emissions accounting gap. If even a soft disclosure obligation is agreed for exporting nations' upstream fossil fuel production, it will immediately trigger a repricing of long-term supply contracts and alter the risk premium on coal and LNG assets globally. Second, the EU CSRD's ESRS E1 standard — which requires companies to report on their entire value chain's GHG emissions, including upstream fossil fuel extraction — will force European buyers of Indian manufactured goods to push carbon intensity disclosures back up their supply chains to Indian producers, who will in turn need to disclose the origin-specific carbon intensity of their imported coal and gas inputs. This creates a cascade of Scope 3 data demands that Indian companies are not yet systematically collecting. Third, within India, I expect SEBI to tighten BRSR Core assurance requirements and potentially introduce mandatory Scope 3 Category 1 quantification thresholds for the top 500 listed companies by FY2026-27 — aligned with ICAI's forthcoming India Sustainability Reporting Standards (INBR), which are expected to closely track ISSB S1/S2. The companies that have built robust Scope 3 import carbon accounting infrastructure by then will have a material compliance and capital cost advantage. The deeper strategic question for Indian industrial companies is this: the fossil fuel export supply they currently depend on is not going to become cleaner or cheaper — the political economy of the exporting nations makes that clear from the CAT data. But the regulatory and financial cost of consuming that supply is going to rise sharply and non-linearly. The companies that treat this as a transition finance challenge — investing now in fuel switching, green hydrogen feasibility, and supply chain decarbonisation — will access cheaper green capital from DFIs, multilateral climate funds, and ESG-benchmarked FPIs. Those that wait for regulatory compulsion will face a simultaneous hit of rising fuel carbon costs, CBAM border adjustments, and a cost of capital premium. Boards should decide now which side of that equation they want to be on.

title: The GHG Protocol Corporate Standard: India's Essential Framework for Credible Carbon Accounting
summary: The GHG Protocol Corporate Accounting and Reporting Standard is the globally accepted foundation for measuring and disclosing a company's greenhouse gas emissions — and it underpins virtually every major climate disclosure framework adopted or referenced in India today, including SEBI's Business Res

sections.t: Executive Summary The GHG Protocol Corporate Accounting and Reporting Standard is the globally accepted foundation for measuring and disclosing a company's greenhouse gas emissions — and it underpins virtually every major climate disclosure framework adopted or referenced in India today, including SEBI's Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting (BRSR), the ISSB's IFRS S2, and the EU's CSRD. Indian companies that have not yet structured their emissions inventory around GHG Protocol principles are carrying significant regulatory, reputational, and investor-relations risk as mandatory disclosure requirements tighten. Understanding and implementing this standard is no longer optional — it is the price of participation in global capital markets and supply chains.

What Changed The GHG Protocol Corporate Standard, originally published in 2001 and updated in 2004 by the World Resources Institute (WRI) and the World Business Council for Sustainable Development (WBCSD), establishes the rules of the road for how companies identify, calculate, and report their greenhouse gas emissions. It defines the three-scope framework that has become universal: Scope 1 (direct emissions from owned or controlled sources), Scope 2 (indirect emissions from purchased energy), and Scope 3 (all other indirect emissions across the value chain). While the standard itself has not been recently revised, its global relevance has never been greater. The ISSB's IFRS S2 Climate-related Disclosures standard — which India's Institute of Chartered Accountants (ICAI) and SEBI are actively monitoring for adoption — explicitly requires GHG Protocol-aligned accounting. SEBI's BRSR Core framework, mandatory for the top 150 listed companies from FY2023-24 and expanding further, requires Scope 1 and Scope 2 disclosures and encourages Scope 3 reporting. In short, the Corporate Standard is the technical bedrock beneath all of these obligations, and any gaps in how a company applies it will cascade into non-compliance across multiple frameworks simultaneously.

Who Is Affected Top 1,000 listed companies in India subject to SEBI's BRSR reporting requirements, particularly the top 150 for whom BRSR Core assurance is already mandatory Large unlisted companies preparing for voluntary ESG disclosures or anticipating future mandatory requirements under Companies Act amendments Exporters and manufacturers supplying to EU customers subject to CSRD supply chain due diligence, including textiles, chemicals, steel, cement, and auto-component sectors Indian subsidiaries of multinational corporations whose parent companies report under CDP, TCFD, or ISSB-aligned frameworks Financial institutions — banks, insurers, and asset managers — required to disclose financed emissions (Scope 3, Category 15) under emerging RBI and SEBI guidance Infrastructure and energy companies seeking green finance, sustainability-linked loans, or green bond certification, where third-party GHG verification is a prerequisite

Key Obligations & Deadlines BRSR Core (FY2023-24 onwards): Top 150 NSE/BSE-listed companies must disclose assured Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions — these must be calculated using GHG Protocol-consistent methodologies to withstand third-party assurance scrutiny BRSR Core (FY2024-25 onwards): Mandatory scope expands to top 250 listed companies; Scope 3 disclosures are encouraged and expected to become mandatory in subsequent phases — begin Scope 3 screening now CDP Reporting Cycles (annual, typically April–July submission window): CDP questionnaires for Climate, Water, and Forests are explicitly built on GHG Protocol standards; companies with institutional investor or customer requests must align fully ISSB IFRS S2 (anticipated Indian adoption horizon 2025-2027): Requires disclosure of gross Scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions with GHG Protocol as the default methodology; companies should treat current BRSR compliance as a stepping stone, not a ceiling EU CSRD Supply Chain Obligations (phased from 2024-2026): Indian suppliers to EU-based companies will face contractual data requests for Scope 1, 2, and relevant Scope 3 categories — non-compliant suppliers risk losing contracts Establish or audit your organisational boundary: Choose consolidation approach (equity share, financial control, or operational control) and document the rationale — this decision affects the entire emissions inventory Set a base year: Select a representative historical year for emissions benchmarking, following GHG Protocol's base year recalculation policy, to enable credible year-on-year progress tracking

Climate Transition Angle The GHG Protocol Corporate Standard is not merely a compliance tool — it is the measurement infrastructure of the entire climate transition economy. Without accurate, comparable, and assured emissions data structured around its three-scope framework, it is impossible to set credible Science-Based Targets (SBTs), participate meaningfully in carbon markets, or demonstrate alignment with a 1.5°C pathway to investors and customers. For Indian companies, the transition angle is particularly acute on two dimensions. First, physical risk: as climate impacts intensify — extreme heat, monsoon disruption, water stress — investors and lenders are demanding emissions data as a proxy for transition readiness. A company that cannot account for its own carbon footprint signals broader strategic vulnerability. Second, supply chain pressure: India's export-oriented sectors face mounting due diligence requirements from global buyers in the EU, UK, and US, all of whom are under their own disclosure mandates. The GHG Protocol is the common language that makes cross-border supply chain emissions reporting legible. Global frameworks including ISSB IFRS S2, the EU's ESRS E1 under CSRD, and the SEC's climate disclosure rules (for US-listed entities) all converge on GHG Protocol as the accounting standard. Indian companies that build robust GHG Protocol-aligned systems now are not just meeting today's compliance bar — they are building the data infrastructure for net-zero target-setting, internal carbon pricing, and climate-integrated capital allocation decisions that will define competitive advantage over the next decade.

What To Do Now Conduct a GHG Protocol Readiness Assessment: Audit your current emissions inventory against the Corporate Standard's requirements — check organisational boundary definition, scope categorisation, emission factor sources, and data quality. Identify gaps before your next BRSR or CDP submission cycle. Build a Scope 3 Screening Inventory: Even if Scope 3 is not yet mandatory for your company, conduct a high-level screening of all 15 Scope 3 categories using the GHG Protocol Scope 3 Standard. Identify your top three to five material categories — for most Indian manufacturers, this will include purchased goods and services (Category 1), use of sold products (Category 11), and business travel or employee commuting. Engage Your CFO and Internal Audit Function: Emissions data quality and boundary-setting decisions have financial materiality implications. Bring financial control teams into the GHG accounting governance structure now, ahead of mandatory assurance requirements expanding under BRSR Core. Select and Document Emission Factors Transparently: Use credible, jurisdiction-appropriate emission factors — the Central Electricity Authority (CEA) grid emission factor for Indian electricity, IPCC factors for fuels, and supplier-specific data where available. Document sources and update annually. Inconsistent emission factor use is the most common reason for assurance qualifications. Develop a Multi-Year Disclosure Roadmap: Map your current GHG accounting maturity against the requirements of BRSR Core, ISSB IFRS S2, and any customer-driven CSRD obligations. Set internal milestones for achieving Scope 3 readiness, third-party verification, and ultimately Science-Based Target submission within a 24-36 month horizon.

Our Take The GHG Protocol Corporate Standard occupies a unique position in the ESG landscape: it is simultaneously the most foundational and most under-implemented standard in Indian corporate sustainability practice. Dozens of companies publish BRSR reports with Scope 1 and Scope 2 numbers, yet when examined closely, the organisational boundaries are poorly defined, emission factors are outdated or misapplied, and Scope 3 is either absent or superficially disclosed. This is not a minor technical deficiency — it is a strategic liability. As SEBI expands BRSR Core assurance requirements, as institutional investors deploy GHG data in portfolio-level climate risk modelling, and as EU customers activate supply chain due diligence provisions, the cost of inadequate GHG accounting will shift from reputational to financial with remarkable speed. Our strong view is that Indian companies should treat GHG Protocol implementation not as a compliance checkbox but as the foundation of a broader climate intelligence system — one that informs capital allocation, supplier engagement, product strategy, and investor communication. The companies that invest in this infrastructure today will not just be compliant; they will be positioned to lead.

title: UK Mandates Climate Transition Plans for Financial Institutions and Large Corporates: What India's Listed Companies and Asset Managers Must Read Into This Now
summary: The UK Government's Department for Energy Security and Net Zero (DESNZ) is consulting on mandatory climate transition plan requirements for UK-regulated financial institutions and large corporates — and Carbon Tracker's formal submission, published 1 September 2025 (https://carbontracker.org/reports

sections.t: Executive Summary The UK Government's Department for Energy Security and Net Zero (DESNZ) is consulting on mandatory climate transition plan requirements for UK-regulated financial institutions and large corporates — and Carbon Tracker's formal submission, published 1 September 2025 (https://carbontracker.org/reports/carbon-trackers-response-to-the-uk-governments-consultation-on-transition-plan-requirements/), argues these plans must be mandatory, 1.5°C-aligned, and cover both transition and physical climate risks including capex financing costs. For Indian listed companies, this is not a distant UK policy debate: Indian firms with UK institutional shareholders, London-listed GDRs, UK export relationships, or supply chain exposure to UK-regulated financial counterparties face direct upstream pressure as their UK creditors, investors, and trade finance providers will themselves be required to hold credible transition plans — and will demand the same from material counterparties. The regulatory gravity pulling India's BRSR Core disclosures and SEBI's transition plan expectations into alignment with this global trajectory is accelerating faster than most Indian boards have priced in.

What Changed Reporting by Carbon Tracker (carbontracker.org), the London-based independent financial think tank, details their formal response to DESNZ's live consultation on introducing statutory transition plan requirements across UK-regulated financial institutions and large corporates. The submission marks a significant inflection point: it signals that the UK — already home to the Transition Plan Taskforce (TPT) disclosure framework, which itself influenced ISSB IFRS S2 Climate disclosures — is moving from voluntary best-practice guidance toward legally binding obligations. Carbon Tracker's key positions are unambiguous: transition plans must be mandatory (not comply-or-explain); they must include the full capex cost of the energy transition financed through both debt and equity; physical climate risks must be explicitly addressed alongside transition risks; fossil fuel producers require bespoke, distinct guidance given the structural incompatibility of their business models with a 1.5°C pathway; and pension funds, as universal asset owners holding systemic risk, carry a specific stewardship obligation. The submission also calls for a national UK transition plan to anchor a whole-of-government strategy — a concept directly analogous to India's Long-Term Low Emissions Development Strategy (LT-LEDS) submitted to the UNFCCC in 2022, and India's National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC), both of which currently lack the corporate-level transition plan architecture the UK is now legislating. For Indian companies, the immediate consequence runs through three channels: first, UK institutional investors — including pension funds such as LGIM, Aviva Investors, and Legal & General, all significant holders of Indian equities through MSCI EM mandates — will face mandatory transition plan requirements that will cascade disclosure and decarbonisation demands down to their Indian investee companies; second, Indian exporters to UK markets in sectors like steel, chemicals, textiles, and automotive components will encounter UK supply chain due diligence tightening as UK corporates building their own transition plans must assess Scope 3 emissions from suppliers, putting Indian Tier 1 and Tier 2 suppliers under direct scrutiny; third, Indian companies listed on UK markets or raising UK-regulated capital face direct in-scope obligations as the legislation is finalised.

Who Is Affected Indian steel, cement, chemicals, and oil & gas producers with UK institutional shareholders or UK-listed instruments (GDRs, bonds) — these companies will face transition plan disclosure demands from UK-regulated investors who are themselves now moving toward mandatory plan requirements BSE 500 and NSE Nifty 500 companies already subject to SEBI BRSR Core disclosures, particularly the top 150 listed entities required to provide reasonable assurance on BRSR Core KPIs from FY2024-25 — the UK framework sets a higher bar that institutional investors will use as a benchmark Indian exporters in hard-to-abate sectors (steel, aluminium, refinery products, fertilisers, textiles) with UK supply chain relationships — UK corporates building 1.5°C-aligned transition plans must assess and disclose Scope 3 Category 1 (purchased goods) emissions, putting Indian suppliers directly in scope CFOs and Chief Sustainability Officers at Indian conglomerates and large-cap industrials who manage UK investor relations — they will need to respond to enhanced stewardship questionnaires and transition plan alignment requests from UK pension funds and asset managers Indian asset managers and insurance companies with UK regulatory touchpoints or IFRS S2 adoption plans — they face the clearest read-across as the UK framework for financial institutions directly shapes what ISSB IFRS S2 adoption will demand in India Risk managers and treasury heads at Indian infrastructure and energy companies funded through UK-regulated green bonds or sustainability-linked loans — transition plan credibility will become a covenant-linked disclosure requirement ESG analysts and stewardship teams at India-focused foreign institutional investors (FIIs) regulated in the UK — they will be required to integrate transition plan assessments of Indian holdings into their own mandatory plans

Key Obligations & Deadlines UK consultation on mandatory transition plan requirements is live as of September 2025 — Indian companies with UK investor relations exposure should monitor DESNZ's final legislative framework, expected to inform UK statute within 12–18 months of consultation close SEBI BRSR Core — top 150 listed companies by market cap: reasonable assurance on KPIs including GHG emissions (Scope 1, 2, and material Scope 3) mandatory from FY2024-25 annual reports; this is the Indian hook that connects to transition plan credibility requirements SEBI's BRSR does not yet mandate a formal climate transition plan, but the trajectory from the UK, EU (CSRD Article 15 requires transition plan disclosure aligned with Paris Agreement), and ISSB IFRS S2 (which requires disclosure of transition plan existence and progress) means SEBI will face pressure to upgrade BRSR to include transition plan requirements within 2–3 regulatory cycles ISSB IFRS S2 paragraph 14(b) already requires companies adopting the standard to disclose whether they have a climate-related transition plan and, if so, key assumptions and dependencies — Indian companies voluntarily aligning to IFRS S2 must address this now, not at adoption Carbon Tracker's call for distinct fossil fuel producer guidance directly affects ONGC, Coal India, Reliance Industries (E&P segment), and IOCL — these companies face the sharpest investor scrutiny under any 1.5°C-aligned transition plan framework, and their UK-regulated institutional shareholders will apply this lens in stewardship engagements from FY2025-26 onward India's PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) Scheme Cycle 3 and the proposed Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) under the Energy Conservation (Amendment) Act 2022 — companies building internal transition plans should map decarbonisation pathways against PAT targets and CCTS obligations simultaneously to avoid double governance burden BEE's proposed trajectory toward mandatory net zero targets for energy-intensive industries — Indian companies in cement, steel, aluminium, and pulp & paper should treat UK-style transition plan architecture as a forward-planning tool for BEE compliance, not merely an investor disclosure exercise

Climate Transition Angle Carbon Tracker's insistence that transition plans include the full capex cost of transition — financed through both debt and equity — is the most commercially significant element of this submission for Indian boards and CFOs, and it is the angle most Indian companies are completely unprepared for. A credible 1.5°C-aligned transition plan is not a narrative document about ambitions; it is a capital allocation roadmap that must reconcile business-as-usual capex projections with the investment required to retire or transform carbon-intensive assets on a Paris-compatible timeline. For Indian listed companies in the Nifty50 — where capital expenditure in energy, materials, and industrials runs into lakhs of crores — this means boards must be able to answer, with financial specificity, how they will fund the delta between current capex plans and transition-compliant capex. UK-regulated pension funds and asset managers will ask exactly this question in stewardship dialogues. The physical climate risk dimension is equally critical for India: Carbon Tracker's submission stresses that financially material physical impacts — flooding, heat stress, water scarcity, extreme weather events — must be embedded in transition plans, not treated as a separate reporting track. India's own physical climate vulnerability is extreme and rising: the June 2024 heatwaves across Rajasthan, UP, and Bihar, the recurring Chennai and Mumbai flood events, and the Himalayan glacial retreat affecting hydropower generation are all financially material physical risks that Indian companies already carry on their balance sheets but rarely quantify. ISSB IFRS S2 scenario analysis requirements, the TCFD framework underlying both UK TPT and BRSR Core's ESG disclosures, and the TNFD's nature-related financial disclosure framework (relevant for Indian agrochemicals, FMCG, and infrastructure sectors) all demand that physical risk quantification be embedded in transition planning. The UK's move toward mandatory plans crystallises what Indian companies face: within 36 months, the largest pools of global capital will require a credible, independently verified transition plan as a precondition for competitive cost of capital. India's carbon market under CCTS, and the alignment of sectoral decarbonisation roadmaps with India's Nationally Determined Contribution (NDC) — 500 GW non-fossil capacity by 2030, 45% emissions intensity reduction — provides the sovereign-level anchor against which company-level transition plans should be calibrated. Companies that build this architecture now will have a measurable cost-of-capital advantage over those that wait for SEBI to mandate it.

What To Do Now CFO + CSO (immediate, within 60 days): Commission a gap analysis mapping your current BRSR Core disclosures against the transition plan architecture required by ISSB IFRS S2 paragraph 14(b) and the UK TPT framework's five-pillar structure (governance, business strategy, financial planning, engagement, metrics & targets) — identify which pillars are absent or underdeveloped Investor Relations team (within 90 days): Proactively engage your top 10 UK and EU institutional shareholders to understand their transition plan information requirements for FY2025-26 stewardship cycles; UK-regulated asset managers will increasingly use Carbon Tracker's framework as a benchmark for engagement quality assessments of Indian investee companies Sustainability / Strategy team (within 6 months): Develop a quantified capex transition roadmap that explicitly bridges current capital allocation plans and 1.5°C-aligned asset transformation requirements — this should be stress-tested against IEA Net Zero by 2050 scenario and India's NDC trajectory, and reviewed by the board's risk committee Legal + Compliance (within 3 months): Map your UK regulatory exposure — identify any UK-listed instruments, UK-regulated lenders, or UK supply chain counterparties that will themselves face mandatory transition plan requirements; assess whether Scope 3 Category 1 emissions disclosure from Indian operations will be required by UK corporate clients Board Risk Committee (next board cycle): Table a dedicated agenda item on transition plan governance, aligned with SEBI's BRSR principle of board-level accountability for ESG disclosures; ensure the board can articulate the company's transition plan strategy and capex commitment in the FY2025-26 annual report and investor presentations Companies in fossil fuel-adjacent sectors — ONGC, Coal India, Reliance E&P, IOCL, GAIL, Adani Ports (coal logistics) (within 6 months): Begin scenario analysis using a credible 1.5°C pathway to assess stranded asset risk in your current capex pipeline; Carbon Tracker's submission explicitly flags that fossil fuel producers require distinct, more stringent guidance — UK and EU investors will apply this lens in 2026 stewardship engagements

Our Take The UK's move toward mandatory climate transition plans is not an isolated policy event — it is the leading edge of a global regulatory convergence that will make voluntary, narrative-only ESG disclosures commercially untenable within three to five years. Carbon Tracker's submission is significant precisely because it refuses to accept the comfortable middle ground: transition plans must be mandatory, 1.5°C-aligned, financially quantified, and physically grounded — or they are simply greenwash infrastructure. This is the standard that the most sophisticated institutional capital pools globally are already internalising, and India's regulatory architecture is on a collision course with this expectation. SEBI has built a credible foundation with BRSR Core and the phased reasonable assurance requirements, but the framework stops short of requiring a transition plan — and that gap is becoming visible to global investors. I expect SEBI will be compelled to introduce transition plan disclosure requirements within the next two to three BRSR revision cycles, likely in the FY2026-27 or FY2027-28 reporting framework, drawing directly on ISSB IFRS S2, the UK TPT framework, and the EU CSRD's transition plan obligations under ESRS E1. Indian companies that wait for SEBI to mandate this will find themselves in a reactive, expensive compliance exercise — those that build the architecture now will have already embedded it into capital allocation decisions and will credibly differentiate in global investor roadshows. The carbon intensity angle deserves specific attention: Carbon Tracker's parallel work on automotive Scope 3 underreporting and fossil fuel producer disguise (see related reports on their site) signals that sector-level scrutiny is intensifying. Indian companies in steel, cement, coal, and oil & gas that present transition credentials without underlying capex commitment and verified emissions data will face growing public challenge from the same think tanks and investor coalitions shaping the UK consultation. The board-level implication is clear: transition planning is no longer a sustainability team deliverable — it is a capital structure decision that belongs on the CFO's agenda, the board's risk committee charter, and the audit committee's assurance scope. India's window to lead this transition rather than react to it is narrowing.

title: Understanding GRI: The Global Standard-Setter Shaping Corporate Sustainability Disclosure in India
summary: The Global Reporting Initiative (GRI) is the world's most widely adopted sustainability reporting framework, providing companies with a structured, stakeholder-focused methodology to disclose environmental, social, and governance (ESG) impacts. As India's regulatory landscape evolves — with SEBI's B

sections.t: Executive Summary The Global Reporting Initiative (GRI) is the world's most widely adopted sustainability reporting framework, providing companies with a structured, stakeholder-focused methodology to disclose environmental, social, and governance (ESG) impacts. As India's regulatory landscape evolves — with SEBI's Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report (BRSR) increasingly aligning with global norms — understanding GRI's foundations is no longer optional for Indian businesses seeking credibility with international investors and supply chain partners. Companies that proactively adopt GRI Standards position themselves ahead of tightening disclosure mandates and build lasting trust with stakeholders.

What Changed The Global Reporting Initiative was founded in 1997 in the aftermath of the Exxon Valdez oil spill, with a mandate to create a common language for organisations to report on their sustainability impacts. Over nearly three decades, GRI has evolved from a niche environmental reporting guideline into the de facto global baseline for sustainability disclosure, used by over 10,000 organisations across 100+ countries. In 2021, GRI released its landmark updated Universal Standards — GRI 1 (Foundation), GRI 2 (General Disclosures), and GRI 3 (Material Topics) — which came into mandatory effect for GRI reporters from January 1, 2023. These updated standards shifted the framework's philosophy firmly toward 'impact materiality': requiring companies to report on how their operations affect the economy, environment, and people — not just how ESG factors affect the company financially. This is a critically important distinction that sets GRI apart from financially-oriented frameworks like ISSB/IFRS S1 and S2. GRI operates as an independent international organisation headquartered in Amsterdam, with a global network of offices and partners. It collaborates formally with the ISSB, EFRAG (which governs CSRD in Europe), and the UN, making it a central pillar of the global sustainability disclosure architecture that Indian companies must navigate.

Who Is Affected Large listed Indian companies (NSE/BSE) subject to SEBI's BRSR Core requirements, where GRI alignment can streamline compliance Indian subsidiaries of multinational corporations headquartered in the EU, UK, or US — where parent companies face mandatory GRI-aligned or GRI-compatible reporting obligations under CSRD, UK SDR, or SEC climate rules Export-oriented manufacturers and suppliers whose global customers (especially in Europe and North America) require GRI-based sustainability disclosures as part of supply chain due diligence Indian companies seeking international ESG-linked financing, green bonds, or sustainability-linked loans — where lenders and rating agencies use GRI disclosures as a benchmark Mid-sized companies preparing voluntarily for future regulatory expansion, particularly as SEBI has indicated BRSR will be extended beyond the top 1,000 listed companies ESG consultants, CFOs, sustainability officers, and board-level risk committees across sectors including manufacturing, IT, pharmaceuticals, infrastructure, and financial services

Key Obligations & Deadlines GRI Universal Standards 2021 (GRI 1, 2, and 3) are now mandatory for all organisations claiming to report 'in accordance with GRI Standards' — this has been in effect since January 1, 2023 Companies must conduct a formal materiality assessment aligned with GRI 3 to identify and prioritise material topics before selecting applicable topic-specific standards (e.g., GRI 302 for Energy, GRI 305 for Emissions, GRI 403 for Occupational Health & Safety) GRI 2 requires comprehensive general disclosures including governance structure, strategy, policies, and stakeholder engagement — all of which must be updated to reflect the 2021 standards in any report published after January 1, 2023 Organisations reporting under GRI must publish a GRI content index — a structured table mapping each disclosure to the relevant section of the report — to ensure verifiability and comparability While GRI reporting itself is voluntary globally (except where mandated by specific jurisdictions), SEBI's BRSR framework explicitly references GRI Standards as an acceptable international framework for supplementary disclosures, creating a de facto compliance pathway for Indian listed companies Companies seeking to align with CSRD (applicable to Indian subsidiaries of EU-headquartered firms or large Indian companies with EU operations) must note that ESRS standards have significant overlap with GRI — early GRI adoption reduces future compliance burden No single global statutory deadline for GRI adoption exists, but companies with EU nexus face CSRD reporting timelines beginning FY2025 for the largest companies, cascading to smaller entities by FY2026-2028

Climate Transition Angle GRI's relevance to India's climate transition cannot be overstated. The framework's topic-specific standards — particularly GRI 305 (Emissions), GRI 302 (Energy), GRI 303 (Water), and GRI 201 (Economic Performance) — provide the granular disclosure architecture that investors, regulators, and civil society need to assess whether Indian companies are genuinely transitioning to low-carbon business models or merely engaging in greenwashing. Critically, GRI's impact materiality approach is complementary — not competitive — to the ISSB's financial materiality approach (IFRS S1 and S2). The ISSB and GRI signed a formal collaboration agreement in 2022, establishing that the two frameworks together form a 'building blocks' approach to comprehensive sustainability reporting. For Indian companies, this means GRI disclosures on how operations impact climate systems (e.g., Scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions) can be paired with ISSB-aligned disclosures on how climate risks impact financial performance — giving stakeholders a complete picture. From a physical risk perspective, GRI disclosures on water stress, biodiversity, and supply chain impacts are increasingly material for Indian companies in sectors like agriculture, textiles, and cement, which are acutely exposed to monsoon variability, heat stress, and resource scarcity. Investors using TCFD-aligned analysis increasingly cross-reference GRI disclosures to validate physical risk assessments. As India advances its National Action Plan on Climate Change and deepens its NDC commitments, GRI-aligned reporting will be a key mechanism for demonstrating private sector accountability in the transition.

What To Do Now Conduct a GRI-aligned double materiality assessment: Map your company's significant impacts on the environment and society (impact materiality) alongside the ESG risks and opportunities that affect your financial performance (financial materiality). This single exercise anchors both GRI and ISSB/BRSR Core compliance simultaneously. Audit your existing BRSR disclosures against GRI 2 (General Disclosures) and GRI 3 (Material Topics): Many Indian companies will find significant overlap — identify gaps systematically and build a disclosure roadmap that satisfies both SEBI and GRI requirements without duplicating effort. Identify applicable GRI topic-specific standards for your sector: Use GRI's Sector Standards (currently available for Oil & Gas, Coal, Agriculture, Mining, and Financial Services, with more in development) to ensure your disclosures reflect industry-specific material topics that generic templates miss. Engage your supply chain and value chain partners on GRI-aligned data collection: Scope 3 emissions, supplier labour practices, and water usage across the value chain are increasingly required under GRI 308, GRI 414, and GRI 305-3. Begin building data systems and supplier questionnaires now, before regulatory pressure makes this urgent. Seek third-party assurance on key GRI disclosures: As SEBI has mandated reasonable assurance for BRSR Core metrics, aligning your GRI content index with assured data points builds investor credibility and pre-empts future mandatory assurance requirements under evolving Indian and global regulations.

Our Take GRI is not just a reporting framework — it is the foundational language of global sustainability accountability, and Indian companies that treat it as a peripheral, voluntary exercise are underestimating a significant strategic risk. We are entering a period where sustainability disclosures are rapidly transitioning from voluntary best practice to regulatory requirement, and the companies that have invested in robust GRI-aligned systems will adapt to each new mandate — BRSR Core, CSRD, ISSB — with speed and confidence, while their competitors scramble. What makes GRI particularly powerful for Indian businesses is its dual utility: it satisfies domestic regulators who reference it in BRSR guidance, and it satisfies international investors and supply chain customers who have been using it for decades. The next five years will see India's disclosure ecosystem converge significantly with global standards. Our view is that GRI adoption today is not compliance overhead — it is competitive infrastructure.

title: Uniper's State-Owned Decarbonisation Model: What Germany's Coal Exit Strategy Signals for India's PSU Power Sector and Transition Finance
summary: Carbon Tracker's July 2024 report on Uniper SE (available at https://carbontracker.org/reports/uniper-se-government-ownership-key-to-decarbonisation/) poses a question that Indian policymakers and capital market participants cannot afford to ignore: can government ownership of a coal-heavy utility b

sections.t: Executive Summary Carbon Tracker's July 2024 report on Uniper SE (available at https://carbontracker.org/reports/uniper-se-government-ownership-key-to-decarbonisation/) poses a question that Indian policymakers and capital market participants cannot afford to ignore: can government ownership of a coal-heavy utility be the decisive lever for accelerating decarbonisation, rather than an obstacle to it? For India, where NTPC, NLCIL, and SJVN are state-controlled, BSE-listed utilities sitting at the centre of both coal dependency and the clean energy buildout, this framing has direct and urgent implications for transition finance, BRSR Core disclosures, and the credibility of India's 2070 net zero commitment. The report's central finding — that Uniper's pathway to net zero is inseparable from the German federal government's ownership stake and policy mandate — reframes the standard ESG investor complaint about state ownership diluting governance and decarbonisation ambition. In the Indian context, it raises an equally uncomfortable inversion: state ownership of coal assets, without an explicit policy-linked decarbonisation mandate and transparent Scope 1 and Scope 3 target architecture, may be the single largest source of stranded asset risk sitting inside domestic institutional investor portfolios today.

What Changed Reporting by Carbon Tracker (carbontracker.org), published 1 July 2024, presents paired corporate research on RWE and Uniper — Germany's two dominant publicly traded coal utilities — assessing their readiness to deliver Germany's accelerated coal exit before 2030. The Uniper report specifically interrogates whether continued German federal government ownership (the government took a ~99% stake in Uniper during the 2022 energy crisis bailout before partial re-privatisation) is a structural advantage or liability for decarbonisation. Carbon Tracker's conclusion is nuanced but directionally clear: government ownership, when paired with explicit policy mandates, interim emissions targets, and capital allocation discipline, can accelerate transition outcomes that private shareholders alone would not enforce — particularly where coal assets are politically entangled with energy security narratives. The report identifies three critical gaps in Uniper's current transition posture that are strikingly familiar in the Indian PSU power context: first, the absence of interim Scope 3 emissions targets (Carbon Tracker flags Uniper's ambition on Scope 3 as insufficient); second, the risk that gas capacity modernisation and new-build programmes crowd out renewables capital expenditure; and third, the structural question of whether declining coal cashflows can fund a credible renewables capex programme without government-backed debt capacity. Each of these three failure modes maps almost exactly onto the disclosure and strategic gaps visible in NTPC's and NLCIL's current BRSR and sustainability reporting. NTPC's FY2024 BRSR Core disclosures, for instance, do not present a Paris-aligned Scope 3 target for upstream coal procurement or downstream power offtake emissions — a gap that Carbon Tracker explicitly calls out as a governance red flag in the Uniper context. The India-specific regulatory hook is direct: SEBI's BRSR Core framework, mandatory for the top 150 listed entities from FY2024 and expanding to the top 250 by FY2025, now requires third-party assured disclosures on Scope 1, 2, and — for certain indicators — Scope 3 emissions. PSU power utilities listed on BSE/NSE are inside this perimeter and are currently the weakest link in the assured disclosure chain.

Who Is Affected Indian PSU power utilities listed on BSE/NSE: NTPC Ltd, NLCIL (NLC India), SJVN, Damodar Valley Corporation (where listed), Neyveli Lignite — all holding material coal generation assets alongside renewable buildout mandates Private Indian coal and gas IPPs with institutional equity or debt exposure: Adani Power, Torrent Power, JSW Energy — particularly those with international bond issuances or CSRD-scope European offtake counterparties Domestic institutional investors — LIC, SBI Mutual Fund, EPFO-linked funds — with large passive and active positions in BSE 500 power sector stocks, now facing stranded asset concentration risk as transition timelines compress Foreign portfolio investors (FPIs) and ESG-labelled funds with India power sector exposure, subject to SFDR Article 8/9 compliance and EU Taxonomy alignment requirements that will increasingly penalise undisclosed coal transition risk CFOs and CSOs of top-150 listed Indian companies under BRSR Core assured disclosure obligation, especially those in energy-intensive sectors with coal-linked Scope 2 and Scope 3 exposure through grid electricity or captive coal power India's Ministry of Power, MoEFCC, and MNRE — as policy architects of the Nationally Determined Contribution (NDC) implementation framework, where the Uniper government-ownership model offers a directly applicable governance template Green bond and sustainability-linked bond (SLB) structurers and underwriters — given that Uniper's debt capacity question for renewables capex is a live issue for NTPC's Rs 15,000+ crore green bond programme and similar instruments in the Indian market
