Key Obligations & Deadlines BRSR Core assured disclosures — Scope 1 and Scope 2 GHG emissions with third-party assurance: mandatory for top 150 listed Indian companies from FY2023-24 reporting cycle (filed by September 2024); expanding to top 250 from FY2024-25 BRSR Core — Scope 3 emissions disclosure: currently a 'leadership indicator' (voluntary but disclosed) under SEBI's BRSR framework; Carbon Tracker's Uniper finding strengthens the case for SEBI to elevate Scope 3 to a core assured indicator in the next BRSR revision cycle, expected 2025-26 SBTi Corporate Net-Zero Standard: Indian companies with SBTi commitments (currently ~85 Indian companies have targets set or committed) must demonstrate Scope 3 coverage exceeding 67% of total emissions — a threshold most Indian power utilities with upstream coal procurement have not yet met ISSB IFRS S2 — Climate-related disclosures: while not yet mandated in India, SEBI has explicitly signalled alignment with ISSB as the interoperability baseline for future BRSR evolution; companies preparing voluntary ISSB S2 disclosures should treat Carbon Tracker's Scope 3 ambition critique as a direct indicator gap India's Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) under the Energy Conservation (Amendment) Act 2022: BEE's draft regulations for the compliance carbon market are expected to be notified in 2024-25; PSU coal utilities will be obligated participants, making transition capex planning and coal exit timelines financially material immediately PAT Scheme Cycle VII (BEE): Designated Consumers in the power sector face revised energy intensity reduction targets; coal plant efficiency improvements are increasingly cost-competitive with transition capex deferral, and Carbon Tracker's 'modernise existing before building new gas' recommendation applies directly here

Climate Transition Angle The Uniper report crystallises a transition finance dilemma that is arriving in Indian capital markets faster than most boards have modelled: as coal cashflows decline — driven by carbon pricing, renewable cost deflation, and policy-mandated capacity retirement — the internal capital generation capacity of coal utilities to self-fund renewables capex deteriorates precisely when the capex requirement is largest. Germany's answer for Uniper was a government-backed balance sheet and explicit policy ownership of the transition mandate. India's answer, as currently structured, is far less clear. NTPC's stated goal of 60 GW of renewable capacity by 2032 requires capital expenditure of approximately Rs 2.5-3 lakh crore over eight years — against a balance sheet that still derives the majority of its regulated equity returns from coal-based generation. Without an explicit government-backed debt facility or policy-linked transition finance mechanism (analogous to what the German government enabled for Uniper), NTPC's renewables capex programme faces the same crowding-out risk that Carbon Tracker identifies as structurally unresolved at Uniper. For Indian institutional investors, the BRSR and ISSB S2 lens makes this a disclosure and portfolio risk question, not merely a policy one. IFRS S2 requires companies to disclose climate-related transition risks that are reasonably expected to affect cash flows, financing, and strategy over the short, medium, and long term. A PSU power utility that discloses coal asset retirement schedules without modelling the capex funding gap — or that reports Scope 1 targets without Scope 3 coverage of coal procurement — is producing a materially incomplete climate risk disclosure under any credible international standard. As India's BRSR Core framework converges toward ISSB S2 interoperability (a direction SEBI has publicly endorsed in its 2023 consultation paper), this gap will become an audit and assurance exposure, not merely a best-practice gap. The physical risk angle is equally live: India's coal-producing regions in Jharkhand, Odisha, and Chhattisgarh face compound climate risk — extreme heat stress, water scarcity affecting thermal plant cooling, and monsoon variability impacting coal logistics — that is not currently modelled or disclosed in PSU utility BRSR filings. TNFD's LEAP methodology, now referenced in SEBI's proposed nature-related disclosure guidance, would require exactly this site-level physical risk assessment for coal asset portfolios.

What To Do Now CFOs of top-150 listed power and energy companies: commission an independent Scope 3 gap analysis against GHG Protocol Category 1 (purchased coal/fuel) and Category 11 (use of sold energy) before the FY2024-25 BRSR Core filing — Carbon Tracker's Uniper critique is a direct proxy for what SEBI assurance reviewers and FPI ESG teams will ask next CSOs and sustainability teams at NTPC, NLCIL, SJVN, and Adani Power: map your current emissions target architecture against SBTi Power Sector pathway requirements and ISSB S2 Appendix B scenario disclosure requirements; identify whether your 2030/2032 renewables targets are Paris-aligned or merely capacity-volume targets with no emissions intensity baseline Board risk committees of PSU utilities: table a formal agenda item on the 'coal cashflow-to-renewables capex funding gap' modelled under three carbon price scenarios (India CCTS baseline, EU CBAM shadow price, SBTi 1.5°C pathway) — this is the exact analytical frame Carbon Tracker applied to Uniper and it is the question your institutional investors will begin asking in AGM season 2025 Ministry of Power and MNRE policy teams: review Carbon Tracker's Uniper government-ownership model as a governance template for structuring a formal Just Transition Finance Facility for Indian coal utilities — including a policy-linked debt capacity enhancement mechanism that ring-fences renewables capex from coal asset depreciation cycles ESG analysts at domestic mutual funds and LIC: incorporate Carbon Tracker's three-flag framework (no interim targets, gas crowding out renewables, declining cashflow-capex mismatch) as a standard engagement checklist for power sector holdings in India; this directly maps to SEBI's stewardship code obligations for institutional investors Green bond and SLB structurers: ensure any new NTPC or PSU utility green bond framework explicitly addresses Scope 3 coal procurement emissions and includes a KPI for coal capacity retirement — without this, bonds face ICMA Green Bond Principles greenwashing scrutiny and potential FPI exclusion under SFDR Article 9 fund mandates

Our Take Carbon Tracker's Uniper report is, at its core, a warning about the gap between stated transition ambition and structurally funded transition delivery — and that warning lands in India with considerably more force than in Germany. Germany's coal exit is a 2030 political commitment backed by a government that nationalised its largest gas and coal utility to control the transition timeline. India's coal exit is a 2070 net zero commitment backed by NDC targets that have not been disaggregated into sector-level carbon budgets, transition finance mechanisms, or utility-level interim milestones. That asymmetry is not sustainable as global capital markets — including the FPIs that hold approximately 20-25% of BSE-listed power sector equity — begin pricing transition risk with the granularity that ISSB S2, CSRD, and SBTi demand. In the next 24-36 months, I expect three specific regulatory and market shifts to force this issue into Indian boardrooms: first, SEBI will elevate Scope 3 disclosures from leadership to core assured indicators in the next BRSR revision, creating an assurance liability for utilities that have not built Scope 3 data infrastructure; second, India's CCTS compliance carbon market will make coal asset retirement financially material in ways the current regulatory accounting framework does not capture, triggering impairment review obligations under Ind AS 36 for coal plant balance sheet valuations; and third, EU CBAM's expansion beyond its current five sectors will eventually reach electricity imports and energy-intensive industrial goods, creating a de facto Scope 2 and Scope 3 carbon price signal in Indian export supply chains that no listed company can ignore. Boards and investors that treat the Uniper question — 'is government ownership the best mechanism for getting to net zero?' — as a European curiosity will miss the fact that India has already answered it implicitly: the government owns the largest coal utilities and the largest renewable targets simultaneously. The missing piece is the explicit policy mandate, the transition finance architecture, and the disclosure framework to make that ownership actually deliver net zero. Building those three elements, with urgency, is the most consequential ESG governance task in India today.

title: Why Nature Matters: What Indian Business Leaders Need to Know About TNFD and the Coming Wave of Nature-Related Disclosure
summary: The Taskforce on Nature-related Financial Disclosures (TNFD) has established a global framework that compels companies and financial institutions to assess, disclose, and act on their dependencies and impacts on nature — including biodiversity, freshwater, land, and ocean systems. For Indian busines

sections.t: Executive Summary The Taskforce on Nature-related Financial Disclosures (TNFD) has established a global framework that compels companies and financial institutions to assess, disclose, and act on their dependencies and impacts on nature — including biodiversity, freshwater, land, and ocean systems. For Indian businesses, which are deeply embedded in nature-dependent sectors such as agriculture, textiles, pharmaceuticals, and infrastructure, this is not a distant voluntary exercise — it is the next frontier of mandatory ESG accountability. As global investors and trading partners increasingly align with TNFD, Indian companies that fail to understand their nature footprint risk losing capital access, supply chain contracts, and social licence to operate.

What Changed In September 2023, the TNFD released its final recommendations — a comprehensive, science-based framework for organisations to report on how their business activities affect and depend upon nature. Built on the architecture of the Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures (TCFD), the TNFD framework introduces the concept of 'nature-related risks and opportunities' across four pillars: Governance, Strategy, Risk & Impact Management, and Metrics & Targets. Unlike climate disclosures, which focus primarily on greenhouse gas emissions and temperature pathways, nature-related disclosures require companies to map their interactions with ecosystems — think water consumption from stressed river basins, deforestation embedded in commodity supply chains, soil degradation from intensive farming, or habitat destruction from real estate and mining operations. The TNFD also introduces the LEAP approach (Locate, Evaluate, Assess, Prepare) — a practical methodology for identifying where a company's operations and supply chains intersect with sensitive or degraded ecosystems. Globally, over 400 organisations have already signed up as early adopters, committing to begin TNFD-aligned reporting by financial year 2024–25. Regulators in the EU, UK, and Singapore are actively exploring how TNFD recommendations will be incorporated into mandatory disclosure regimes. India's own SEBI Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report (BRSR) framework is increasingly converging with global standards, making TNFD alignment a near-term inevitability for listed Indian companies.

Who Is Affected Large listed Indian companies (NSE/BSE) already subject to SEBI's BRSR Core disclosures, particularly those in high nature-impact sectors Agribusiness, food & beverage, and FMCG companies with commodity supply chains touching forests, freshwater, or marine ecosystems Textile and apparel manufacturers dependent on water-intensive cotton and synthetic fibre production Pharmaceutical and chemical companies sourcing botanical or marine-derived inputs Infrastructure developers, real estate firms, and mining companies operating near biodiversity-sensitive zones or protected areas Financial institutions — banks, asset managers, and insurers — with portfolio exposure to nature-dependent sectors Indian subsidiaries of multinational corporations whose parent companies are TNFD early adopters or subject to EU CSRD requirements Export-oriented businesses supplying to EU or UK markets, where nature-related due diligence obligations (e.g., EU Deforestation Regulation) are already in force

Key Obligations & Deadlines TNFD Early Adopter Commitment (Voluntary — FY2024-25 onwards): Companies that have signed up as TNFD early adopters are expected to begin producing TNFD-aligned disclosures in their annual or sustainability reports for financial years starting in 2024 EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR) — Enforcement from December 2025 (delayed from 2024): Indian exporters of soy, palm oil, cattle, wood, cocoa, coffee, rubber, and derived products must demonstrate deforestation-free supply chains with geolocation data EU Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) — Phased from 2025: Indian companies that are subsidiaries or significant suppliers of EU-regulated entities will face nature-related disclosure expectations flowing down their value chains SEBI BRSR Core — Mandatory for top 150 listed companies from FY2023-24, expanding to top 1,000 by FY2026-27: While not yet explicitly TNFD-aligned, BRSR's biodiversity, water, and land-use disclosures are foundational building blocks Immediate action (now): Conduct an initial screening of operations and supply chains using the TNFD LEAP methodology to identify high-priority nature-related interfaces Near-term (6–12 months): Establish internal governance ownership of nature risks at board or senior management level, mirroring existing climate risk governance structures

Climate Transition Angle Nature and climate are inseparable. Approximately 55% of global GDP is moderately or highly dependent on nature, and the degradation of ecosystems directly amplifies physical climate risks. Deforestation reduces carbon sinks, increasing warming trajectories. Wetland destruction removes natural flood buffers, exposing assets and communities to intensifying monsoon events. Soil degradation reduces agricultural resilience to droughts — a mounting concern for India's food systems as temperatures rise. The TNFD framework is explicitly designed to work in tandem with the ISSB's IFRS S2 climate standard and the TCFD framework. As the International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) signals its intention to develop a nature-related standard building on TNFD, Indian companies that invest in nature disclosure infrastructure now will be positioned ahead of the curve when ISSB biodiversity standards arrive — likely within the next two to three years. For Indian supply chains, the stakes are particularly high. India is one of the world's most biodiverse countries and simultaneously one of the most vulnerable to nature-related physical risks — water stress, land degradation, and biodiversity loss. Businesses that fail to account for these dependencies are carrying unpriced risk on their balance sheets, a fact that institutional investors and credit rating agencies are beginning to scrutinise with increasing rigour.

What To Do Now Conduct a TNFD LEAP screening: Use the TNFD's freely available LEAP (Locate, Evaluate, Assess, Prepare) methodology to map where your operations and tier-1 supply chain intersect with ecologically sensitive locations — start with IBAT (Integrated Biodiversity Assessment Tool) and global biodiversity footprint databases Assign board-level ownership: Designate a board committee or senior executive (Chief Sustainability Officer or equivalent) as accountable for nature-related risk oversight, and ensure it is documented in your governance disclosures Integrate nature into your existing ESG/BRSR reporting process: Audit your current BRSR disclosures for biodiversity, water, and land-use sections and identify gaps relative to TNFD's recommended metrics — treat this as a gap analysis, not a separate workstream Engage your supply chain: Issue supplier questionnaires or updated code-of-conduct requirements covering deforestation commitments, water stewardship certifications (e.g., AWS, FSC, RSPO), and land-use practices — particularly for tier-1 suppliers in agriculture, forestry, and raw material extraction Monitor regulatory developments actively: Track SEBI's evolving BRSR framework, the EU Deforestation Regulation's enforcement timeline, and ISSB's nature standard development — assign a team member to produce a quarterly regulatory watch brief so your compliance strategy stays ahead of mandatory deadlines

Our Take The TNFD framework represents a structural shift in how capital markets will price nature-related risk — and for India, the implications are profound and proximate. This is not a framework designed for European companies alone; it is the scaffolding upon which global investors, banks, and regulators are building a new vocabulary of accountability for any business that touches land, water, or biodiversity. Indian corporates have navigated the climate disclosure learning curve with TCFD and BRSR — and those that moved early gained a measurable advantage in investor confidence and export market access. The nature disclosure curve will be steeper and more complex, because ecosystems are local, data infrastructure is nascent, and supply chain traceability in sectors like agriculture and textiles remains largely underdeveloped. But that complexity is precisely why the time to act is now, not when SEBI mandates it or when a European buyer sends a non-compliance notice. Companies that embed TNFD thinking into their strategy today will find it is not just a compliance exercise — it is a risk management and competitive differentiation opportunity that will define which Indian businesses remain investable in the decade ahead.
